unit.name: Compounding Vessel (Jacketed Mixing Tank)
unit.cfj: Dissolve and blend active and excipient components into homogeneous bulk solution

meta.method: Ulwick ODI (Importance × Satisfaction → Opportunity), needs scoring v2

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that residual chemical, pharmaceutical, or biological contamination remains undetected during the final safety inspection
rows.plain: Make sure no leftover chemicals, medicines, or germs are missed during the last safety check. This helps ensure the equipment and product area are safe to use.
rows.imp_rat: Undetected chemical/pharma/biological contamination at final safety inspection is the last barrier before release; for liquid processing plants this is the core safety gate (relevance 10), and escaped contamination can cause poisoning, recalls, or fatalities (severity 10).
rows.sat_rat: Inspection and testing exist but detection coverage is inherently incomplete, so undetected residual contamination at final inspection remains a real recurring gap. Final contamination inspections and lab turnaround add time to project closeout. Detecting residual chemical, pharma or biological contamination relies on specialist sampling and analytical expertise. Sampling campaigns and lab analysis add cost, and missed contamination causes expensive rework.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of nonsterile or unintended material entering the batch through a manually established flow path
rows.plain: Make sure unwanted or unclean material cannot enter the product through a path set up by hand. This helps protect the product when the equipment is being operated manually.
rows.imp_rat: Nonsterile material entering the batch via a manual flow path strikes at the core of liquid batching's job of producing spec-compliant, sterile product; when it occurs the whole batch is contaminated and potentially harmful, so severity is catastrophic.
rows.sat_rat: Manually established flow paths are among the least controlled steps, and misconnections or non-sterile paths remain a recurring source of unintended material ingress. Manually setting up and sanitizing a flow path is time-consuming before transfer can proceed. Correctly establishing an aseptic manual flow path is highly technique-dependent and error-prone. Sterile hoses, connectors, and steam/SIP resources for each manual path add cost.

rows.source_job: Verify in-process integrity of single-use assembly
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a single-use assembly breach remains undetected before process material is committed
rows.plain: Make sure a tear or leak in the disposable assembly is found before product is put into it. This helps prevent product from being exposed or lost.
rows.imp_rat: An undetected single-use assembly breach before material is committed is central because it is the last checkpoint before irreversible product commitment; missing it commits an entire batch to a compromised path, a severe loss.
rows.sat_rat: Pre-use integrity testing of single-use assemblies is immature and often manual, so micro-breaches frequently remain undetected until contamination appears downstream. Pressure-decay or visual integrity checks add setup time, and an undetected breach discovered only after fill wastes the entire campaign's time. Reliable breach detection depends on operators correctly performing and interpreting integrity tests, which is inconsistent across shifts. Committing material into a breached single-use assembly loses the batch and product, a very high-cost failure mode.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that residual product, cleaning agents, or hazardous material remain in the batching system at handoff
rows.plain: Make sure no product, cleaning liquid, or dangerous material is left in the mixing equipment when it is handed over. This helps protect the next person who uses it.
rows.imp_rat: Residual product, cleaning agent, or hazardous material remaining at handoff is central to the clean-transfer purpose of the system and carries catastrophic cross-contamination and safety consequences for the next batch.
rows.sat_rat: Retained residues at handoff remain a common finding because complex batching geometry resists complete clearing. Achieving verified handoff cleanliness may require multiple flush cycles that extend the schedule. Confirming complete removal at handoff depends on skilled sampling and interpretation not always available on site. Repeated cleaning and lab verification consume solvents and testing budget.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of residual product or hazardous cleaning material remaining in the system at transfer
rows.plain: Make sure no product or dangerous cleaning material is left in the system when it is handed over. This helps protect the people who take responsibility for it next.
rows.imp_rat: Residual product or hazardous cleaning material remaining at transfer directly defeats the batching system's job of delivering a clean, correct-composition batch; central to the CFJ and catastrophic since it can poison a downstream batch or harm consumers.
rows.sat_rat: Residual product or cleaning chemical frequently lingers in dead legs and crevices, so clean-at-transfer is inconsistently achieved. Flush-and-verify cycles before transfer take real time and often require repetition. Judging whether a system is truly clean of product and cleaning agents requires process knowledge and sampling skill. Cleaning media, solvents, and verification sampling add cost but are standard.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of safety interlocks remaining bypassed or incorrectly configured after testing
rows.plain: Make sure the equipment’s safety locks are fully restored and set correctly after they are tested. Otherwise, the equipment could operate without the protection they are meant to provide.
rows.imp_rat: Interlocks left bypassed or misconfigured after testing removes the plant's primary protective layer, central to safe operation of the job (very high relevance); an undetected bypass can lead to explosions, releases, or fatalities (catastrophic severity).
rows.sat_rat: Bypass logs and force-management practices help, but left-in bypasses after testing remain a well-documented and recurring failure mode in industry incidents. Restoring and re-verifying every bypassed interlock after testing adds time that is often compressed under return-to-service pressure. Tracking which forces and bypasses were applied and confirming full restoration relies on disciplined attention rather than deep skill. The cost is largely diligence and paperwork, with little material expense.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of active ingredient concentration variation among vessel locations after mixing
rows.plain: Make sure the active ingredient is spread evenly throughout the vessel after mixing. This matters so that each part of the product has the intended strength.
rows.imp_rat: Active concentration variation among vessel locations directly measures blend uniformity; severe patient-dose consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Standard agitation designs achieve positional uniformity in most tanks, though geometry-driven low-flow pockets still cause occasional variation. Location-to-location uniformity is confirmed by sampling several points, adding wait time before proceeding. Identifying dead zones and interpreting positional variance requires seasoned process knowledge. Positional non-uniformity can scrap batches or cause underdosed product, driving material waste.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of sterile batch contamination during the interruption or resumption
rows.plain: Prevent the medicine from becoming contaminated when production is stopped and then started again. The process must be brought back safely without exposing the batch to unwanted material.
rows.imp_rat: Contamination during interruption/resumption of a sterile batch is highly relevant to maintaining continuous sterility and, when it happens, irreversibly compromises the batch with patient-safety consequences.
rows.sat_rat: There is little robust automated safeguard for maintaining sterility across interruption and resumption, leaving this a poorly served, deviation-prone situation. Interruptions force hold, re-sanitization, or re-verification that extend the batch timeline. Judging whether sterility survived an interruption and safely resuming demands expert assessment. Holds and potential re-sterilization or scrap on resumption are costly.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of active and excipient concentration variation across the target-volume batch
rows.plain: Keep the amounts of the medicine and its other ingredients consistent throughout a batch, even when the batch size changes.
rows.imp_rat: Concentration variation across the batch is the essence of the homogeneity job; high severity as it drives dose-uniformity failures.
rows.sat_rat: Well-designed impellers and validated mix times deliver homogeneity in most batches, but stratification in large tanks remains an intermittent failure. Confirming uniform concentration across the batch requires multi-point sampling that delays release. Interpreting whether the whole batch is uniform demands analytical and process expertise. Concentration variation risks rejecting the entire batch, a large material and energy loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of undissolved active or excipient material remaining when target-volume mixing ends
rows.plain: Make sure no undissolved medicine or other ingredient remains when mixing the planned amount is finished.
rows.imp_rat: Undissolved material at mixing end directly defeats the CFJ of dissolving components into homogeneous solution; residual undissolved active means out-of-spec potency, a serious patient-safety/regulatory failure.
rows.sat_rat: Validated recipes and endpoint checks catch most cases, but residual undissolved fines still slip through often enough to remain a recurring concern. Reaching a verified fully-dissolved endpoint still depends on visual checks and hold times that add unpredictable minutes to hours before completion is confirmed. Operators lean on experienced judgement to decide when the last solids have dissolved, since routine in-line dissolution sensing is rarely deployed. Undissolved material at target volume forces rework, filtration or batch loss, wasting expensive active and excipient.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of undissolved active ingredient remaining at the specified mixing endpoint
rows.plain: Leave as little undissolved medicine as possible when mixing is supposed to be complete. This helps make sure the mixture has the intended amount of medicine throughout.
rows.imp_rat: Undissolved active at endpoint is core to dissolution job; residual active undersolubilizes potency, a severe content-uniformity failure.
rows.sat_rat: Established mixing profiles reliably dissolve most actives, but poorly wetting or agglomerating actives still occasionally leave residue. Confirming the active is fully dissolved at the endpoint adds sampling and analysis delay that extends the batch. Detecting trace undissolved active depends on skilled visual inspection or off-line assay rather than an automated indicator. Any undissolved active is costly product loss given the high value of the active fraction.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that batch sterility is compromised during skid processing
rows.plain: Prevent germs from getting into the group of product while it is being processed. This helps keep the finished product safe to use.
rows.imp_rat: Batch sterility compromise during skid processing directly defeats the CFJ of producing safe biopharmaceutical product; a lost sterility event forces batch rejection and can endanger patients, making both centrality and consequences maximal.
rows.sat_rat: Closed-system design, validated sterilization, and integrity testing make gross sterility breaches uncommon in practice, though rare undetected excursions still occur. Sterility assurance relies on validated SIP cycles and hold-time monitoring that add process time but are routinely scheduled without major delay. Maintaining aseptic conditions demands trained operators and rigorous aseptic technique, so outcomes still hinge on experienced personnel and documented gowning/handling procedures. Sterility failures are catastrophically expensive as whole batches are scrapped, and the elaborate monitoring and media-fill programs to prevent them carry real ongoing cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of charging the wrong ingredient into a supply tank or feed line
rows.plain: Make sure the correct ingredient is put into the supply tank or feed line. Using the wrong ingredient could affect the product being made.
rows.imp_rat: Charging the wrong ingredient into a supply tank or line is a root-cause recipe integrity failure (relevance 10) that can adulterate every downstream batch, a severe outcome (severity 9).
rows.sat_rat: Where scan-to-confirm and dedicated lines exist wrong-ingredient events are rare, but many plants still rely on manual identity checks that occasionally fail. Verifying ingredient identity before charging adds a checking step to each addition. Correct ingredient routing still leans heavily on operator attention and label reading where manual additions occur. Barcode/RFID verification systems cost to install but prevent expensive wrong-ingredient batch losses.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of loading an ingredient that does not match the batch specification
rows.plain: Make sure the ingredient loaded into production matches the approved recipe for that product. The wrong ingredient could spoil the product or make it unsafe.
rows.imp_rat: Loading an ingredient that does not match batch specification directly defeats the core job of preparing a correct batch; the wrong ingredient can render the entire batch unsafe or unusable, causing product recalls or patient harm.
rows.sat_rat: Systems reliably match verified ingredients to spec, but grade, concentration, or lot mismatches that share identity codes still slip through occasionally. Specification-matching checks at load add minimal time when integrated into the dispensing workflow. Automated spec verification lessens the operator judgement needed to confirm a match. The verification infrastructure overlaps with identity checks so marginal cost is low.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of microorganisms surviving in skid fluid paths after the sterilization cycle
rows.plain: Make sure the heat treatment kills all germs left inside the fluid lines and equipment. This helps prevent the next product from becoming contaminated.
rows.imp_rat: Microorganisms surviving in fluid paths after the sterilization cycle is a direct failure of the skid's fundamental sterility barrier, so it is maximally central; the outcome is contaminated product and possible patient harm, making severity catastrophic.
rows.sat_rat: Thermal cycle validation with biological indicators and continuous temperature/pressure logging makes surviving organisms in wetted paths rare where practice is mature. Validated SIP cycles with defined F0 exposure run on schedule, adding predictable rather than problematic time. Once a sterilization cycle is validated the operator dependence is low, since automated cycle control removes most manual judgement. Steam, energy, and validation studies impose cost, but a failed cycle costing an entire batch dwarfs those and justifies the investment.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of safety interlocks that fail to initiate the required protective response during controlled testing
rows.plain: Make sure each safety device triggers the needed protective action when it is tested. This helps protect people and equipment when something goes wrong.
rows.imp_rat: Interlocks that fail to initiate their protective response during testing mean the plant's safety layer is non-functional, which is core to safely delivering the job (very high relevance); an undetected failure can result in catastrophic release or explosion (maximum severity).
rows.sat_rat: Proof-testing regimes catch many latent failures, yet some interlocks still fail to actuate on demand because coverage and test fidelity are imperfect. Full functional testing of protective responses is time-consuming and often batched into scheduled outages. Verifying that each interlock actually initiates its protective action needs instrumentation know-how to interpret responses correctly. Test equipment and downtime carry cost, but the routine test hardware is affordable.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of concentration nonuniformity across the batch at the required endpoint
rows.plain: Make sure the batch has the same concentration throughout when the process is finished. This matters so that different portions of the batch are not weaker or stronger than intended.
rows.imp_rat: Concentration nonuniformity at the required endpoint is the essence of the homogeneity job; severe dose-uniformity failure.
rows.sat_rat: Concentration uniformity is generally achievable with proper mixing but gradients recur in poorly mixed geometries. Multi-point concentration checks add sampling and assay time. Interpreting uniformity data requires standard analytical competence. Nonuniform batches risk rejection and reprocessing cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of residual contamination on product-contact surfaces escaping detection during sanitization validation
rows.plain: Make sure any leftover contamination on surfaces that touch the product is found during cleaning checks. This helps prevent unsafe product from being accepted.
rows.imp_rat: Residual contamination escaping sanitization validation undermines the hygiene assurance central to liquid product safety (relevance 9), and validated-but-dirty systems produce systematically contaminated product (severity 9).
rows.sat_rat: Validation sampling detects much residual contamination but limited swab coverage means some residue escapes detection, a well-known gap. Sanitization validation swabbing, sampling and lab turnaround add significant delay before release. Interpreting validation results and knowing where residues hide demands specialist microbiological and process knowledge. Sampling, lab testing and repeat cycles impose substantial recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of residual material from the previous formulation contaminating the next batch during the recipe change
rows.plain: Keep leftover material from the previous product out of the next batch when changing recipes. This prevents the new batch from being mixed with the wrong product.
rows.imp_rat: Carryover from the previous formulation during a recipe change is core to multi-product batching integrity, and cross-contamination severely compromises the next batch's identity, purity, and safety.
rows.sat_rat: Validated CIP cycles with rinse-sampling reliably control carryover for most products, though hard-to-clean geometries and potent residuals leave recurring risk. CIP/SIP cleaning between formulations is a lengthy changeover activity. Validated cleaning cycles reduce reliance on operator judgement once the recipe is established. Cleaning agents, water, energy, and downtime make changeover cleaning expensive.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of losing sterile containment while equipment transitions to the protective state
rows.plain: Avoid breaking the sealed, germ-free barrier while the equipment is being shut down in an emergency. This helps protect the product during the change to a safer state.
rows.imp_rat: Losing sterile containment during transition to protective state is central to a sterile liquid plant's CFJ (relevance high); a breach can contaminate entire batches with irreversible product loss and patient safety risk (severity high).
rows.sat_rat: Fail-closed/fail-safe design usually preserves containment on transition, but rapid trips can still momentarily breach sterile conditions. Transition to protective state is fast by design and not time-limited. Fail-safe valve positioning removes operator dependence during the transition. Fail-safe actuators and blanket systems are standard engineered costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of compromising sterile processing conditions while diagnosing and correcting a process fault
rows.plain: Avoid allowing contamination into the germ-free process while finding and fixing a problem. The process must stay protected during troubleshooting.
rows.imp_rat: Compromising sterile conditions while diagnosing a fault directly undermines sterile output (high relevance) and can ruin an entire batch and endanger patients (high severity).
rows.sat_rat: There is limited standardized practice for sterile-preserving fault correction, so outcomes vary considerably with who is intervening. Diagnosing a fault while preserving sterility often forces slow, cautious steps that extend fault duration. Correcting faults without breaching sterility demands scarce senior operator/engineer judgement under pressure, a chronic skill-dependent gap. Fault interventions can consume batches and utilities but the dominant loss is expertise, not money.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of adding an ingredient not specified for the current programmed step
rows.plain: Add only the ingredient called for in the current step. This prevents the wrong ingredient from being added to the batch.
rows.imp_rat: Adding an unspecified ingredient at a step is a fundamental recipe-integrity failure (relevance 10) and yields a cross-contaminated or adulterated batch requiring disposal (severity 9).
rows.sat_rat: Programmed step sequencing with valve interlocks reliably prevents out-of-step additions in automated lines, though manual add points remain a residual exposure. Sequenced recipe steps enforce order without pausing the operator. Step interlocks reduce reliance on the operator knowing the correct addition sequence. Interlock logic is inexpensive relative to the batch-loss cost it prevents.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that operator intervention needed to accommodate an ingredient property variation compromises the sterile boundary during batching
rows.plain: Let the operator handle differences in an ingredient without breaking the protected clean barrier around the batch. This helps keep the batch free from contamination.
rows.imp_rat: Operator intervention to handle ingredient variation breaching the sterile boundary is central to robust sterile batching, and any such breach severely compromises the batch with potential patient harm.
rows.sat_rat: Because the response is manual and ad hoc, each intervention is a fresh opportunity to compromise the boundary, keeping outcomes inconsistent. Improvising around an ingredient property variation mid-batch interrupts and lengthens the run. Accommodating variability without breaching sterility relies almost entirely on scarce operator expertise and judgement in the moment. Interventions and any resulting holds or scrap add unplanned cost.

rows.source_job: Set up and verify safety systems
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that safety interlock logic produces an incorrect protective response to a hazardous batch condition
rows.plain: Make sure the safety controls respond correctly when a dangerous batch condition occurs. This helps the equipment take the right protective action instead of creating another hazard.
rows.imp_rat: Incorrect protective response by safety interlock logic to a hazardous condition directly defeats the safety layer the job depends on (high relevance); a wrong response can cause explosions or injury (catastrophic severity).
rows.sat_rat: Mature safety-lifecycle practice, SIL assessments, and structured verification make protective-response failures uncommon where standards are enforced, though logic errors still occasionally slip through review. Safety interlock verification via FAT/SAT and logic testing adds significant lead time during preparation but is a well-scheduled activity. Correct interlock logic depends heavily on scarce functional-safety and process-hazard specialists whose availability limits how well this is served. Formal interlock design, IEC , and testing are costly but treated as non-negotiable investments in regulated batch plants.

rows.stk: Operators, sanitation/hygiene technicians, quality assurance personnel.
rows.source_job: Clean, sanitize, and decontaminate product-contact surfaces
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that microbial or endotoxin contamination remains on product-contact surfaces after sanitation
rows.plain: Make sure product-contact surfaces are free of germs and harmful substances after cleaning and treatment. This helps prevent contamination of the product.
rows.imp_rat: Residual microbial or endotoxin contamination on product-contact surfaces defeats the core purpose of producing a clean batch (high relevance) and can directly cause patient harm or recalls (catastrophic severity).
rows.sat_rat: Validated CIP/SIP plus rinse and endotoxin sampling is a mature, widely deployed practice that catches most residual contamination, yet dead legs and biofilm still cause occasional failures. CIP/SIP cycles are time-validated and run automatically, so cleaning duration is predictable but adds fixed downtime to every service window. Validated automated sanitation sequences reduce operator dependence, though correct swab sampling and endotoxin testing still require trained microbiology staff. Chemicals, WFI, and lab testing consume real resources each cycle but are budgeted and routine.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of loading an incorrect ingredient component or lot into the new batch
rows.plain: Make sure the new batch receives the correct ingredient and batch lot. This prevents the wrong material from being used in the product.
rows.imp_rat: Loading an incorrect ingredient or lot directly defeats the CFJ of preparing a correct batch (relevance 10), and the consequences—an entire adulterated or mislabeled batch, potential recall or patient harm—are severe (severity 9).
rows.sat_rat: Scan-to-confirm against the recipe with weigh-verify is mature and widely deployed, so wrong-ingredient loads are uncommon though manual override and unscanned totes remain residual gaps. Barcode/RFID scanning at material staging adds only seconds and rarely delays the batching start. Verification is largely automated so it no longer depends on an operator memorizing lot numbers, though attentive staging discipline is still required. Scanner infrastructure and MES integration carry moderate cost but prevent expensive scrap of misformulated batches.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an incorrect processing input is introduced into the liquid stream
rows.plain: Make sure the correct processing material is added to the liquid flow. This prevents the wrong material from entering the product.
rows.imp_rat: Introducing an incorrect processing input corrupts the liquid stream at its source, central to the job (relevance very high); it can render an entire batch unsafe or unusable (severity very high).
rows.sat_rat: Material verification systems and segregated storage reduce misadditions, but manual charging and mislabeling still cause occasional wrong-input incidents. Barcode/verification checks add little time and catch wrong inputs quickly at the point of addition. Correct input identification still leans on operator attention and labeling discipline, which are fallible. Wrong-input events can scrap entire batches, so the failure cost is high when it occurs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of hygiene-critical plant locations omitted from sanitization validation
rows.plain: Make sure every plant area that must be especially clean is included when the cleaning process is checked. This helps prevent contamination from being missed in an overlooked area.
rows.imp_rat: Omitting hygiene-critical locations from sanitization validation leaves blind spots central to hygienic operation (relevance 8), and undetected harboring sites can contaminate every batch (severity 9).
rows.sat_rat: Riboflavin coverage tests and sampling plans help, but omitted locations remain a common validation gap because complex circuits hide many points. Building complete validation location maps is time-consuming and often rushed. Identifying every hygiene-critical location, including dead legs and worst-case points, requires deep process and hygienic-design expertise. Comprehensive location mapping and sampling drives added labor and lab cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of initiating the batching cycle with parameters that differ from the confirmed recipe or quantity
rows.plain: Reduce the chance of starting a mixing or production run with settings or an amount different from the approved instructions. This helps ensure the run makes the intended product in the intended quantity.
rows.imp_rat: Starting the cycle with off-recipe parameters produces an out-of-spec batch, which is core to the batching job (relevance 10); a fully misbatched lot is costly and potentially unsafe (severity 9).
rows.sat_rat: MES-to-controller recipe locking with electronic signatures is standard and dependable, though manual parameter edits at the HMI still leave an occasional deviation path. Recipe download and electronic confirmation take moments and do not meaningfully slow cycle initiation. The control system holds the confirmed recipe so the operator needs little expertise to align parameters, only to acknowledge prompts. Recipe management software is a fixed overhead that avoids costly off-spec batches from parameter mismatch.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that released liquid contaminates a sterile production area
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that spilled liquid spreads into an area where germ-free production takes place. This helps protect the area and the product from contamination.
rows.imp_rat: Contamination of a sterile production area by released liquid directly defeats the CFJ of producing sterile liquid product (very high relevance), and the consequence is a compromised, potentially recalled batch with patient-safety implications (very high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Closed systems and containment barriers usually prevent released liquid reaching sterile areas, but breaches and spillover during upsets still occur. Contained release handling is not primarily time-driven. Containment relies on engineered barriers more than continuous operator skill, reducing human dependence. Bunding, drains and containment design are standard capital costs already in place.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a parameter adjustment compromises the sterile state of the batch
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that changing mixing settings makes the batch no longer germ-free. This helps protect the batch while achieving the desired mixing.
rows.imp_rat: A parameter adjustment breaching sterility is central to safely operating the batching process and carries severe consequences since a compromised sterile state means batch loss or unsafe product.
rows.sat_rat: Adjustments are typically managed procedurally rather than interlocked against sterility, so a change occasionally compromises the sterile state. Making a parameter adjustment safely often requires pausing and re-confirming boundary status, adding time. Knowing which adjustments threaten sterility requires deep process knowledge, a scarce expertise. Adjustments that trigger re-verification or holds consume time and material resources.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of active and excipient concentration variation within batches operated inside the proposed ranges
rows.plain: Reduce how much the amounts of the main ingredients and added materials vary from one part of a batch to another when operating within the proposed settings. This matters so the product is made consistently.
rows.imp_rat: Concentration variation within batches operated inside ranges is central to blend uniformity; severe potency-distribution consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Validated ranges generally hold variation acceptable, but not every in-range corner is proven, leaving residual risk. Confirming low variation across the full operating range needs repeated sampling that adds time. Assessing variation robustness inside ranges requires statistical and formulation expertise. Excess variation in-range risks batch rejection and material loss.

rows.stk: Instrumentation technicians, maintenance engineers, quality personnel.
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of introducing contamination into sterile product-contact areas during calibration
rows.plain: Prevent germs from entering areas that touch the product while measuring equipment is being adjusted. This keeps the product protected during the adjustment.
rows.imp_rat: Introducing contamination into sterile product-contact areas during calibration directly undermines the core job of producing clean batches (high relevance) and carries catastrophic consequences—contaminated sterile product can cause recalls or patient harm (high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Introducing contamination during sterile calibration remains a recognized risk that current gowning and wipe-down practice only partly controls. Aseptic calibration under controlled conditions is slow because of gowning, staging, and post-work re-sanitization. Calibrating sensors in sterile product-contact zones without breaching asepsis demands high aseptic-technique skill that is scarce. Sterile consumables, cleanroom time, and potential re-sterilization make each calibration expensive.

rows.source_job: Install single-use assemblies onto the skid
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of breaching a sterile barrier while handling or mounting product-contact assemblies
rows.plain: Avoid damaging the protective germ-free covering while attaching the parts that will touch the product. This helps keep the product clean and safe.
rows.imp_rat: Breaching a sterile barrier while mounting product-contact assemblies directly negates the aseptic setup essential to the job; a breach exposes the product path to contamination with severe batch-integrity consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Pre-sterilized single-use assemblies and aseptic connectors help, but tearing or compromising a barrier during physical handling still happens and often goes unnoticed. Careful barrier handling and mounting is slow and deliberate, and a suspected breach forces re-fetching and re-staging sterile components. Avoiding punctures, snags, and mis-seated seals when mounting sterile assemblies rests almost entirely on trained manual dexterity and attention. A breached sterile barrier means scrapping costly single-use or re-sterilizing hardware, a real recurring materials cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that system KPI status indicates acceptable control while blend homogeneity or GMP control is deteriorating
rows.plain: Make it less likely that general system indicators say everything is under control while the mixture is becoming uneven or quality controls are weakening. This matters because a problem could be missed until the product is affected.
rows.imp_rat: KPI indicating acceptable control while homogeneity/GMP deteriorates masks a failing batch; relevant with very high severity as an undetected escape.
rows.sat_rat: KPIs frequently reflect lagging or aggregated indicators that can show acceptable status while underlying homogeneity control drifts, a well-recognized and poorly solved gap. KPI dashboards update on reporting cycles that can lag real deterioration in blend control. Interpreting whether green KPIs mask true drift requires seasoned quality judgment that is scarce. KPI/monitoring systems are already funded, so cost is not the barrier.

rows.stmt: Minimize the time between recognition that continued operation is unsafe and initiation of emergency shutdown
rows.plain: Make the time as short as possible between noticing that it is unsafe to continue and starting the emergency stop. A faster stop can reduce harm.
rows.imp_rat: Delay between recognizing unsafe operation and initiating emergency shutdown is central to protecting a processing plant from escalation; when it occurs the consequences can be catastrophic — explosion, fire, mass release — making both relevance and severity extreme.
rows.sat_rat: SIL- shutdown systems with periodic proof testing make timely trip initiation highly consistent, with residual gaps mainly in operator-recognized rather than sensor-detected hazards. SIS/ESD systems with hardwired trips and SIL-rated logic solvers execute shutdown within seconds of a validated demand, so the recognition-to-initiation gap is short where instrumentation is proper. Automated interlocks remove most reliance on operator judgement to initiate shutdown, though manual E-stop paths still depend on operators noticing and acting. SIS/ESD infrastructure is expensive to install and maintain but is standard capital in liquid processing plants, so the cost is largely already absorbed.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of concentration variation across vessel locations at the homogeneity decision
rows.plain: At the time the mixing result is judged, the amount of ingredient should be nearly the same throughout the container. This helps show that the whole mixture is even.
rows.imp_rat: Concentration variation at the homogeneity decision point is core to CFJ; severe because a nonuniform batch can be wrongly released.
rows.sat_rat: Established multi-location sampling protocols reliably characterize spatial uniformity in validated vessels, though dead zones can still be missed. Multi-point sampling adds handling time but is scheduled into the batch, limiting delay. Interpreting spatial concentration variance requires analytical support and some judgement on sampling locations. Detecting and correcting spatial variation avoids costly rejects but multi-point testing consumes labor and assay resources.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of an actual fluid-path breach remaining undetected after integrity testing
rows.plain: Make it less likely that a real break or leak in the liquid path goes unnoticed after testing. Detecting such a problem helps prevent contamination or loss of product.
rows.imp_rat: An undetected fluid-path breach after integrity testing allows contaminated or leaked material to reach product unnoticed, undermining the core assurance function of the skid; consequences include compromised sterile batches and delayed detection, both highly severe.
rows.sat_rat: Standardized pressure-hold and bubble-point methods catch most gross breaches, yet micro-leaks and marginal failures near acceptance thresholds still slip through, leaving a persistent residual risk. Pressure-decay and diffusion integrity tests run in reasonable cycle times but are held tightly to production windows. Automated integrity test units reduce reliance on expert judgement, though interpreting borderline results and setup errors still demand trained technicians. Test rigs and single-use filter consumables carry real cost, but the cost of an undetected breach dwarfs the test spend.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of product-contact surfaces retaining microbial contamination after sanitization
rows.plain: Reduce the amount of harmful living contamination left on surfaces that touch the product after cleaning and sanitizing. Cleaner surfaces help protect the product from contamination.
rows.imp_rat: Microbial contamination retained on product-contact surfaces after sanitization seeds every subsequent batch, so controlling it is central to the job; the consequence is cross-batch contamination and product loss, which is severe though somewhat detectable via monitoring.
rows.sat_rat: Validated CIP/SIP with routine swab and rinse verification catches most contamination, yet dead-legs, shadowed surfaces, and biofilm mean residual positives still recur intermittently. CIP/SIP cycles are time-validated and automated, but requalifying and re-running cycles after a failed swab adds real delay before the skid is released. Effective sanitization depends heavily on validation engineers designing correct spray coverage and drainage, and residual dead-legs still defeat even skilled crews. Steam, WFI, and cleaning agent consumption plus swab/rapid-micro testing are significant but budgeted recurring costs of the practice.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of component concentration variation across the batch during mixing and hold
rows.plain: The amount of each ingredient should stay as even as possible throughout the group of products while it is mixed and held. This helps ensure the products have the intended strength.
rows.imp_rat: Concentration variation across the batch during mixing and hold is core to CFJ; severe because non-uniformity carries to fill.
rows.sat_rat: Continued agitation during hold usually maintains uniformity, though settling or stratification during static holds remains a recurring gap. Monitoring concentration through mixing and hold adds sampling delays to the process. Judging when transient variation has settled during hold takes experienced interpretation. Variation during hold can force remixing, wasting energy and time-linked cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a candidate vessel cannot achieve blend homogeneity across specified fill volumes and fluid properties
rows.plain: Avoid choosing a vessel that cannot mix the contents evenly for the required amounts and types of liquid. Even mixing helps ensure each filled unit has the right contents.
rows.imp_rat: A vessel that cannot achieve homogeneity across fill volumes fails the core job outright; severe because unfit equipment jeopardizes every batch.
rows.sat_rat: Standard blend-time correlations and vendor sizing handle common cases, but non-Newtonian or wide fill-range fluids still spring surprises at spec time. Mixing studies and CFD/scale-down trials take time to run but are established steps in the specification cycle. Predicting homogeneity across varied fluid properties still leans heavily on scarce mixing-engineering judgement and vendor expertise. Trial batches and pilot studies to confirm homogeneity consume material and engineering resources.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of required component mass left undissolved within permitted batch constraints
rows.plain: Reduce how much of an ingredient remains unmixed or undissolved in the batch. This helps ensure the finished batch contains the intended amount throughout.
rows.imp_rat: Undissolved mass within permitted constraints is core dissolution shortfall; severe potency consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Achieving full dissolution within constrained batch windows is usually attainable but not guaranteed for difficult components. Verifying dissolution within batch time constraints adds bounded effort. Balancing dissolution completeness against batch limits needs process judgement. Undissolved material within constraints means lost yield.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of finished liquid falling outside required quality specifications when feedstock properties change
rows.plain: Make it less likely that the finished liquid fails its required quality checks when the starting materials change. This helps keep the final product within the expected requirements.
rows.imp_rat: Finished liquid falling out of spec when feedstock varies is directly the CFJ of producing conforming product (relevance very high); off-spec finished product means rejection and lost value (severity high).
rows.sat_rat: Feed-forward control and inline analyzers exist but are unevenly deployed, so quality slips remain a recurring problem when feedstock shifts. Lab QC turnaround introduces lag, so feedstock property changes are often detected after some off-spec product is already made. Compensating for feedstock variability requires experienced operators to adjust recipes, a skill-intensive and error-prone task. Off-spec product and rework represent real material and energy losses when compensation fails.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of falsely accepting an off-target ingredient dose as on target
rows.plain: Make it very unlikely that an ingredient amount outside the required range is wrongly accepted as correct. This helps ensure the product has the right recipe.
rows.imp_rat: Falsely accepting an off-target dose as on-target is central to batch accuracy, and the consequence is an out-of-spec batch that passes undetected, potentially reaching customers with severe quality or safety impact.
rows.sat_rat: Gravimetric verification reliably rejects off-target doses, though calibration drift and static or flow effects can still cause an off-target dose to read as on target. Automated in-line weighing gives immediate pass/fail on dose without slowing the load step. Load cells and tolerance bands remove most operator estimation from confirming on-target dosing. Calibrated weigh systems are standard, well-priced equipment in batch dispensing.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of batches containing undissolved active or excipient material when operated within the proposed ranges
rows.plain: Make sure fewer production batches contain solid ingredients that have not dissolved, when the process is run within the planned settings. This helps ensure each batch is made evenly and works as intended.
rows.imp_rat: Batches with undissolved material within proposed ranges strike at the core dissolution job; each such batch is a serious potency failure.
rows.sat_rat: Validated ranges usually keep failures rare, but edge combinations within range still occasionally yield undissolved solids. Discovering undissolved material within approved ranges typically surfaces late, extending investigation time. Setting robust ranges that avoid failures requires substantial development expertise. Batches with undissolved material within nominally valid ranges are direct scrap or rework losses.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of component mass left undissolved at the required batch endpoint
rows.plain: Leave as little of the ingredient undissolved as possible when the batch is supposed to be finished. This helps ensure the mixture has the intended makeup.
rows.imp_rat: Component mass left undissolved at the required endpoint is a direct CFJ failure; severe potency/uniformity impact.
rows.sat_rat: Standard endpoint checks work well for typical formulations but miss slow-dissolving edge cases. Endpoint sampling and analysis add manageable time to batch confirmation. Confirming endpoint dissolution uses routine but attention-dependent technique. Residual undissolved mass represents wasted active material.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percent of required component mass undissolved at the defined blend endpoint
rows.plain: Leave as little of the required ingredient undissolved as possible when mixing is complete. This helps ensure the finished mixture is evenly made throughout.
rows.imp_rat: Required component mass undissolved at blend endpoint is a direct dissolution failure and potency shortfall; core with high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Dissolution endpoint verification is fairly mature, though sampling limitations leave occasional undetected undissolved fractions. Dissolution endpoint studies add defined but bounded time. Judging complete dissolution requires moderate analytical skill and sampling technique. Undissolved mass wastes active and can fail the batch.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of introducing contamination into sterile product-contact areas during fault repair
rows.plain: Make it very unlikely that contamination enters clean areas touching the product while a problem is being repaired. This helps keep the product safe after the repair.
rows.imp_rat: Introducing contamination into sterile product-contact areas during fault repair directly defeats the core sterility objective (high relevance) and can result in contaminated, unusable, or recalled product harming patients (high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Fault repairs are less controlled than planned maintenance, so introducing contamination remains a persistent risk even with procedures in place. Contamination controls slow every step of fault repair inside product-contact zones. Avoiding contamination during an unplanned repair depends heavily on the technician's aseptic discipline under pressure. Sterile parts, cleaning agents and post-repair re-sanitization add cost each event.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that offsetting ingredient deviations conceal an out-of-tolerance dose during end-to-end verification
rows.plain: Make it very unlikely that one ingredient being too high hides another ingredient being too low during a full batch check. Each ingredient amount should be correct on its own.
rows.imp_rat: Offsetting ingredient deviations concealing an out-of-tolerance dose defeats end-to-end verification (high relevance) and is severe—an undetected out-of-spec batch can be released undetected (high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Standard component-total checks rarely isolate individual doses that cancel out, so this failure mode routinely slips through end-to-end verification. End-to-end mass-balance verification can be run quickly by software, but reconciling offsetting deviations often triggers slow manual investigation. Detecting that two compensating errors mask an out-of-tolerance dose demands experienced review that few operators reliably apply during routine batching. Undetected offsetting deviations that surface later cause costly rejected or reworked batches.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of wetted surface area retaining hazardous residues after decontamination
rows.plain: After cleaning, as little as possible of the wet surface should still have dangerous residue on it. This helps protect people and prevent the next use from being affected.
rows.imp_rat: Hazardous residues retained on wetted surfaces after decontamination compromise the safe-handoff aspect of the job; severity is high due to toxic exposure and cross-contamination risk, though it is a decontamination-specific concern.
rows.sat_rat: Complex internal geometries and crevices mean measurable residue commonly persists after decontamination, a persistent gap. Measuring wetted-surface residue via swabbing or rinse sampling is slow and iterative. Quantifying surface residue and interpreting decontamination adequacy requires specialized analytical skill. Swab testing, rinse analysis, and repeat decon add meaningful cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the bulk solution departs from its homogeneity specification before fill completion
rows.plain: Keep the bulk mixture evenly mixed until filling is finished. This helps ensure that each filled container receives the intended mixture.
rows.imp_rat: Loss of homogeneity before fill completion means non-uniform dose delivery downstream, central to the blend job; severity is high because it produces out-of-spec product.
rows.sat_rat: Validated mixing parameters and in-process sampling make sustained homogeneity common, but sampling gaps and settling of some formulations leave residual excursions. In-process homogeneity checks and validated mix times keep the solution within spec through most of the fill window, though checks occur at intervals rather than continuously. Validated recipes reduce reliance on operator judgement, but detecting slow drift before fill completion still depends on experienced oversight. Occasional out-of-spec drift forces reblend or rejection, imposing material and time costs on the batch.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a tank interlock fails to establish a safe state under a hazardous condition
rows.plain: When a tank enters a dangerous condition, its safety controls should put it into a safe state. This helps limit harm to people and equipment.
rows.imp_rat: An interlock failing to establish a safe state under a hazardous condition removes the last line of protection central to safe operation (relevance 9); the result is an uncontrolled hazardous event (severity 9).
rows.sat_rat: Standard functional-safety practice (IEC substantially reduces interlock failures, yet real recurring gaps remain from proof-test errors, misconfiguration and untested failure modes. Interlock verification and SIL testing extend commissioning schedules but are scheduled activities. Functional safety validation demands specialized instrumentation and safety engineers who are not always available on site. SIS design, testing and documentation carry meaningful cost but are justified against hazard consequences.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of an unexpected release of pressure, energy, or process material from disconnected plant systems
rows.plain: When plant equipment is taken apart, pressure, stored energy, or process material should not be released unexpectedly. This helps prevent injury, damage, or harmful exposure.
rows.imp_rat: Unexpected release of pressure, energy, or process material from disconnected systems directly undermines safe isolation, a core enabler of the job; the consequences include severe or fatal worker injury, giving very high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Mature LOTO and permit-to-work regimes catch most releases, but unexpected trapped energy in disused decommissioned systems still causes periodic surprises. Lock-out/tag-out and line-breaking permits add time to the disposal sequence but the delay is predictable and accepted. Safe disconnection depends heavily on experienced isolation engineers correctly identifying every stored-energy source, and skill gaps are a recurring cause of incidents. Permit systems and blind-flange kits are inexpensive relative to the plant, so cost is not the binding constraint.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of initiating liquid product processing with a compromised sterile process boundary
rows.plain: Production should not start if the barrier keeping the product clean and protected is damaged or not properly closed. This helps prevent the liquid product from being contaminated.
rows.imp_rat: Starting processing with a compromised sterile boundary is central to producing acceptable liquid product; the error can render entire batches non-sterile and unsafe for use, driving both high relevance and severity.
rows.sat_rat: Pre-use bubble-point/pressure-hold filter tests and documented SIP make undetected boundary compromise uncommon, yet human procedural gaps still allow occasional compromised starts. Pre-startup integrity checks and SIP verification take real time and can pressure schedules, occasionally tempting shortcuts before processing begins. Confirming boundary integrity depends on trained operators correctly interpreting pressure-hold and filter-integrity tests, leaving room for judgement error. Integrity-test equipment and validated SIP cycles are established costs but consume utilities and time each batch.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of process output becoming contaminated during discharge from the skid
rows.plain: The product leaving the equipment should not become contaminated during discharge. This helps keep the finished output safe and usable.
rows.imp_rat: Contamination during discharge occurs at the point product leaves the skid and defeats prior sterility efforts, so it is highly central; the consequence is a spoiled finished batch entering downstream, which is severe.
rows.sat_rat: Sterile connectors, tube welders, and closed filling substantially reduce discharge contamination, but the open moments at the point of transfer remain a recurring vulnerability. Closed discharge and sterile connection setup add setup time but do not typically bottleneck the operation. Aseptic discharge connections and drum/bag filling still require careful manual technique that depends on operator training and attention. Single-use connectors and sterile transfer sets add consumable cost, and any discharge contamination loses the finished output.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that previous-formulation residue contaminates the next batch
rows.plain: Leftover material from the previous product should not mix into the next batch. This helps ensure the new batch has the intended ingredients and quality.
rows.imp_rat: Previous-formulation residue contaminating the next batch is core to producing correct, uncontaminated batches, and the consequence is a spoiled or unsafe batch requiring disposal and investigation.
rows.sat_rat: Validated cleaning protocols control most formulation carryover, but hard-to-clean geometries and validation gaps allow residue contamination to recur. Preventing carryover depends on cleaning cycles whose duration is a recognized throughput cost between formulations. Judging cleaning adequacy for a given formulation transition still involves significant process and validation expertise. Cleaning validation, solvents, and downtime make contamination avoidance a substantial recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of an unintended hazardous or contamination event during controlled safety interlock testing
rows.plain: Testing safety devices should not accidentally cause a dangerous release, movement, or contamination. The test must be controlled so people and the process remain safe.
rows.imp_rat: An unintended hazardous or contamination event during safety interlock testing strikes at both the safety and product-integrity core of liquid processing (high relevance); the consequences can be catastrophic, including harm to personnel and loss of an entire production batch (high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Test procedures and simulation exist, but induced testing states are inherently risky and unintended events during testing remain a real recurring concern. Interlock testing must be scheduled and sequenced carefully, extending outage windows. Safely forcing and testing interlocks without triggering a real hazardous event demands considerable control and process expertise. Testing consumes engineering time and may require test media, but hardware costs are modest.

rows.stmt: Reduce the rate of nonhomogeneous batches approved as conforming
rows.plain: Prevent batches that are not evenly mixed from being approved as acceptable. This helps ensure that every part of the batch has the intended composition.
rows.imp_rat: Nonhomogeneous batches approved as conforming is a direct escape of defective product; severity is very high as patients receive out-of-spec doses.
rows.sat_rat: Defined acceptance criteria and dual review make erroneous conforming approvals uncommon, though sampling bias can still let borderline batches through. Approval decisions wait on assay results, adding some lag but not chronic delay. Disposition depends on QA judgement and correct interpretation of homogeneity data, a skill-sensitive step. A nonhomogeneous batch released as conforming risks costly recall and rework, a significant downside.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which the dosing tolerance permits batch composition to deviate from its specified range
rows.plain: Make sure the amount of each ingredient added keeps the final mixture within its approved recipe range. This helps ensure every batch has the intended makeup.
rows.imp_rat: Dosing tolerance directly governs whether the batch composition lands in spec, which is the defining functional job of batching; an out-of-range deviation ruins the batch and can cause underdose/overdose harm, though it is often detectable before release.
rows.sat_rat: Modern dosing systems hold composition within specified ranges reliably, though tolerance stack-up at extreme ratios remains a gap. Tight dosing tolerances are achieved without extra time once dosing systems are tuned. Automated dosing control reduces dependence on skilled manual metering to stay in spec. High-accuracy dosing pumps and metering hardware carry a cost premium.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of batch contamination during transfer to the intermediate vessel
rows.plain: Prevent germs or other unwanted material from getting into the batch while it is moved into another container. This protects the batch from being spoiled or made unsafe.
rows.imp_rat: Contamination during transfer to the intermediate vessel is highly relevant since transfer is a core batching step, and contamination there irreversibly compromises product safety.
rows.sat_rat: Closed sterile transfer to intermediate vessels is a maturing practice that controls most contamination, though connection integrity remains a residual failure point. Transfer through sterile connections is reasonably quick once the path is set up. Validated transfer assemblies reduce but do not remove aseptic-technique dependence. Single-use transfer sets and validated connectors are recurring costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of deviation from approved pressure, flow, and thermal limits during shutdown
rows.plain: Keep pressure, liquid movement, and temperature close to their approved limits while production is being stopped. This helps prevent unsafe or damaging shutdown conditions.
rows.imp_rat: Deviating from approved pressure, flow, and thermal limits during shutdown is central to safe operation of a liquid processing plant (high relevance); breaching these limits can cause equipment rupture, overpressure, or thermal runaway with catastrophic consequences (high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Programmed shutdown logic keeps most planned shutdowns within limits, but abnormal or emergency shutdowns still overshoot approved bounds. Automated shutdown sequences step down pressure, flow and temperature at controlled rates. Non-routine or emergency shutdowns still lean on operator judgement to stay within limits. Limit violations during shutdown risk equipment damage, but controlled sequences limit that cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of breaching the sterile boundary during ingredient addition
rows.plain: Keep the germ-free area sealed while ingredients are added. This prevents outside contamination from entering the product.
rows.imp_rat: Breaching the sterile boundary during addition is critical in sterile applications (relevance 7); a breach compromises the entire sterile batch with severe safety and compliance consequences (severity 9).
rows.sat_rat: Sterile connectors and RTP ports improve outcomes but manual breaches at the addition boundary remain a recurring, hard-to-eliminate failure mode. Establishing and verifying sterile connections during addition slows the operation considerably. Maintaining the sterile boundary at addition points demands high aseptic skill and is easily compromised by lapses. Sterile connectors, single-use assemblies, and validated transfer methods are costly consumables.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a setpoint adjustment moves a critical parameter outside validated operating limits during an active batch
rows.plain: Lower the chance that changing a machine setting during production pushes an important condition outside its tested safe range. This helps keep the batch within safe and approved conditions.
rows.imp_rat: A setpoint adjustment pushing a critical parameter outside validated limits during an active batch strikes at the heart of controlled preparation and can cause immediate off-spec, unsafe, or non-compliant product.
rows.sat_rat: Enforced setpoint clamping and alarm limits in modern control systems reliably block excursions, though limits that are mis-configured or absent still let some through. Software range checks validate entries instantly without slowing the operator. Configured high/low limits offload the burden of remembering validated ranges from the operator. Preventing out-of-range moves avoids the cost of deviations and lost batches at little added expense.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of microbial or particulate contamination entering the liquid feedstock while it passes through active processing equipment
rows.plain: Prevent germs or particles from getting into the liquid material while it moves through operating equipment.
rows.imp_rat: Microbial or particulate contamination entering feedstock through active equipment strikes at the core quality objective of the plant; contaminated product can cause recalls or patient harm, so severity is very high alongside high relevance.
rows.sat_rat: Validated closed processing with terminal or inline sterilizing filtration makes in-process ingress rare, though seal, gasket and filter failures remain a recurring low-frequency risk. Closed-system processing and inline sterile design avoid added handling time during the pass through equipment. Maintaining contamination-free flow relies on validated closed systems more than on continuous operator skill during the run. Sterile-grade filters, closed piping and CIP/SIP regimes are costly consumables but standard operating expense.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of contaminating the skid fluid path during single-use assembly replacement
rows.plain: Prevent germs or other unwanted material from entering the equipment’s liquid path while disposable parts are replaced.
rows.imp_rat: Contaminating the fluid path during single-use assembly replacement is central to modern disposable-based skid operation; a contamination event here forces batch loss and rework, making severity high.
rows.sat_rat: Pre-sterilized gamma-irradiated assemblies with aseptic connectors reduce risk, but installation errors and connection breaches during replacement remain a real recurring failure mode. Single-use assembly changeover is faster than cleaning stainless systems, so time is generally favorable. Correct aseptic installation of single-use assemblies is heavily manual and error-prone, depending strongly on operator technique and concentration during connection. Single-use assemblies are recurring consumable expense, and a mis-installed assembly can contaminate and scrap the batch.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that preceding-batch residue contaminates a subsequent batch because of sequence incompatibility
rows.plain: Lower the chance that leftovers from one batch spoil the next batch because the two batches cannot safely follow each other. This helps prevent unwanted material from carrying over.
rows.imp_rat: Preceding-batch residue cross-contaminating a subsequent batch through sequence incompatibility is central to correct batch preparation, and the consequence is a compromised batch with potential patient harm and recall.
rows.sat_rat: Validated production sequencing prevents many cross-contamination cases, but incomplete compatibility matrices and manual schedule changes leave notable residual risk. Sequence-compatibility checks are automated in scheduling systems and add no run-time delay. Managing incompatible sequences still relies on well-maintained changeover matrices and planner expertise. Sequencing logic and validated changeover rules are established but require ongoing maintenance effort.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of in-range parameter combinations that yield unacceptable dissolution, homogeneity, or stability
rows.plain: Avoid operating settings that appear acceptable but still produce products that do not dissolve properly, are not evenly mixed, or do not remain stable. This helps ensure the product works as intended and stays usable.
rows.imp_rat: In-range parameter combinations yielding unacceptable results undermine the validated operating space; severe because they cause hidden batch failures.
rows.sat_rat: Ranges are typically validated at set points rather than exhaustively, so unacceptable in-range combinations can persist undetected. Finding which in-range combinations fail requires extended DOE studies that take significant development time. Mapping the failure envelope inside approved ranges is a high-expertise development activity. Undetected bad combinations cause failed batches that waste materials.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the recommended vessel fails dissolution or blending criteria at intended minimum or maximum batch volumes
rows.plain: Choose a mixing container that works properly both when it is filled as little as intended and when it is filled as much as intended. The contents should dissolve and mix well at both amounts.
rows.imp_rat: Vessel failing dissolution/blend at min or max batch volume means it is unfit at the range extremes; central with high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Min/max volume qualification is standard intent but bracketing is often incomplete, so failures at extremes still occur. Testing at both volume extremes lengthens PQ campaigns considerably. Predicting min/max volume performance requires mixing and scale-up expertise that is scarce. Full-range batch testing consumes significant material and vessel time.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of batches with composition outside target because incoming ingredient concentration differs from its expected value
rows.plain: Reduce the number of batches whose mixture is off target because an ingredient arrives stronger or weaker than expected. This helps keep each batch’s composition consistent.
rows.imp_rat: Composition outside target from incoming ingredient concentration variance strikes at the core job of producing a correctly formulated batch; consequences are serious rejected batches though usually caught and reworkable rather than catastrophic.
rows.sat_rat: Most sites trust supplier CoA values without in-house verification, so real lot-to-lot concentration variation still pushes batches off target with some regularity. Certificate-of-analysis review is fast, but true at-line concentration confirmation adds testing time many sites skip. Correcting a batch for incoming concentration variation requires analytical and formulation skill that is unevenly available on the floor. Off-target composition from concentration drift wastes ingredient and forces rework, a recurring material cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of losing blend homogeneity at intended fill-volume or viscosity extremes
rows.plain: Lower the chance that a mixture becomes uneven when the amount being filled or the mixture’s thickness is at either end of the intended range. This helps ensure each filled unit is made the same way.
rows.imp_rat: Losing homogeneity at fill-volume/viscosity extremes is a core blend-robustness failure; relevant with high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Extremes of fill and viscosity are the conditions most likely to break homogeneity and are inconsistently covered. Testing homogeneity at fill and viscosity extremes lengthens evaluation. Anticipating extreme-condition mixing behavior requires specialized knowledge. Lost homogeneity at extremes drives batch waste.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of component precipitation during planned vessel holds or thermal transitions
rows.plain: Prevent ingredients from separating out or forming unwanted solid material while they are held in a container or moved between temperatures. This helps keep the product evenly mixed and consistent.
rows.imp_rat: Precipitation during holds or thermal transitions destroys homogeneity and potency; relevant with high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Precipitation during holds and thermal transitions is a real risk imperfectly predicted from solubility data. Evaluating hold-stability adds thermal-cycle study time. Predicting precipitation risk requires solubility and formulation expertise. Precipitation ruins batches, causing significant loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of tank pressure exceeding the upper process limit during use
rows.plain: Prevent the pressure inside the tank from rising above the highest safe operating level. This helps protect the tank, the product, and nearby people.
rows.imp_rat: Overpressure exceeding the upper process limit is central to safe tank operation (relevance 9); when it occurs the consequences are catastrophic rupture, explosion, and loss of life (severity 10).
rows.sat_rat: Layered protection (relief devices plus high-pressure interlocks) is a mature, code-mandated practice that makes uncontrolled overpressure a rare event. Pressure relief valves and rupture discs act instantly on overpressure, so time-to-mitigate is essentially immediate once the setpoint is reached. Sizing and setting relief devices is a codified engineering task (ASME, API), removing most operator judgement from the runtime protection. PSVs, rupture discs and interlocks are standard, inexpensive fixtures relative to the catastrophic loss they prevent.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of undissolved active ingredient remaining after completion of the addition sequence
rows.plain: Reduce the amount of the active medicine that remains undissolved after all additions are finished. This helps ensure the mixture is properly prepared before the next step.
rows.imp_rat: Undissolved active after addition sequence is central to dissolution; severe because affected batch fails potency specs.
rows.sat_rat: Sequenced addition with validated agitation dissolves most material, yet late-added or clumped active can persist. Verifying no undissolved active remains after the addition sequence requires a hold and check that consumes batch time. Judging completeness after charging relies on operator experience with the specific formulation. Undissolved active after addition means reprocessing costs and possible loss of high-value material.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of closing the disruption while the bulk solution remains nonhomogeneous
rows.plain: Lower the chance that a production problem is declared resolved while the main mixture is still uneven. This helps ensure the product is properly mixed before work continues.
rows.imp_rat: Closing the process while still nonhomogeneous releases a defective batch; central to job completion with high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Requirement for confirming results before closure makes premature closure uncommon, though pressure to proceed occasionally overrides verification. Confirmatory sampling before closing a disruption adds verification time but is procedurally expected. Judging whether homogeneity is truly restored requires competent interpretation of confirming data. Prematurely closing on a nonhomogeneous batch risks downstream reject costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that tank-wall degradation remains undetected until integrity is lost
rows.plain: Find signs that the tank wall is weakening before it develops a hole or loses its ability to contain the contents. This helps prevent leaks and possible exposure.
rows.imp_rat: Undetected wall degradation until integrity is lost strikes at the core containment job of the tank (relevance 8); sudden failure yields catastrophic release and injury (severity 9).
rows.sat_rat: Point-based gauging leaves gaps between measurement locations, so localized degradation can and does slip through until a leak forms. UT thickness mapping and periodic gauging campaigns take significant downtime but are schedulable during planned outages. Reliable detection depends heavily on certified NDT technicians whose probe placement and interpretation drive whether thinning is caught. Full-coverage inspection and instrumentation are costly, so practice often samples rather than covers the whole wall.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that intervention on an alarmed condition compromises the sterile processing boundary
rows.plain: Make sure responding to a warning does not let germs into the protected product area. This keeps the product and its surroundings clean and safe.
rows.imp_rat: Intervention on an alarmed condition that breaches the sterile boundary directly threatens sterility while responding to an already-abnormal state (high relevance), risking batch loss and contamination (high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Procedures exist but ad-hoc alarm interventions frequently threaten the boundary, so results are inconsistent. Responding to an alarm quickly and aseptically pulls in opposite directions, adding time. Intervening on an alarmed condition without breaching the sterile boundary relies heavily on trained-operator restraint and technique. Interventions risk product loss but the primary constraint is skilled response, not cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of false-pass results concealing malfunctioning protective devices
rows.plain: Make sure safety devices cannot appear to pass a test when they are actually not working. This helps ensure that faulty protection is discovered.
rows.imp_rat: False-pass results concealing malfunctioning protective devices defeats the plant's safety verification job (relevance 7); an undetected failed guard can lead to serious injury when it is later needed (severity 9).
rows.sat_rat: Simple pass/fail checks frequently mask a dangerously degraded device, and this false-pass blind spot is a well-known, poorly-closed gap. Proof-testing safety devices is time-boxed into shutdowns, but thorough testing to expose latent faults takes longer than most schedules allow. Detecting a false pass requires skilled technicians who understand failure modes rather than just observing a nominal response. Comprehensive functional testing costs instrument time and spares, so testing is often minimized.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of introducing microbial, particulate, or cross-product contamination while loading ingredient components
rows.plain: Prevent germs, dust, particles, or material from another product from getting into the ingredients while they are being loaded. This helps keep the ingredients clean and prevents products from being mixed accidentally.
rows.imp_rat: Contamination during ingredient loading is a direct pathway into the batch (relevance 8); cross-product contamination can trigger recalls and harm (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Even with SOPs and monitoring, manual component loading remains a leading contamination-ingress point where failures still recur despite best practice. Aseptic loading procedures, gowning, and line clearance add substantial time to each ingredient addition. Contamination-free loading depends heavily on trained aseptic technique and constant operator vigilance, a scarce and error-prone dependency. Cleanroom operation, disposables, and environmental monitoring make contamination control expensive.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of deviation between programmed recipe parameters and the approved specification
rows.plain: Make the programmed production settings match the approved instructions as closely as possible. This helps the process produce the intended result safely and consistently.
rows.imp_rat: Deviation between programmed recipe parameters and the approved specification is core to producing a compliant batch, and the consequence is a batch that fails specification and regulatory scrutiny.
rows.sat_rat: Electronic recipe transfer keeps most parameters aligned to spec, but small unmanaged deviations persist where manual configuration is involved. Parameter-by-parameter comparison against specification is time-consuming when done manually. Judging the significance of a parameter deviation requires recipe and process understanding. Verification labor is moderate; deviations caught late cost rework.

rows.stmt: Reduce the rate of actual interlock faults incorrectly recorded as passing results during periodic verification
rows.plain: Reduce cases where a failed safety lock is mistakenly recorded as working during checks. This helps prevent unsafe equipment from being treated as safe.
rows.imp_rat: Interlock faults mis-recorded as passing during verification undermines the integrity of safety systems (relevance 7); a latent faulty interlock can cause catastrophic incidents when relied upon (severity 9).
rows.sat_rat: Manual test methods and incomplete stimulus coverage allow real interlock faults to be logged as passes with uncomfortable frequency.
