Periodic interlock verification is scheduled, but full-coverage testing of every fault path is time-consuming and often abbreviated. Correctly forcing and observing interlock trips demands experienced controls technicians to avoid misreading a fault as a pass. Rigorous interlock testing consumes downtime and specialist labor, pressuring shortcuts.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of incorrect recipe parameters being activated for the next batch
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that the next batch starts with the wrong instructions or settings. This helps prevent the batch from being made incorrectly.
rows.imp_rat: Activating incorrect recipe parameters for the next batch strikes the heart of batching (very high relevance) and produces an entire out-of-spec batch with potential safety and scrap consequences (high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Recipe-bound parameter activation with change control is a mature, dependable practice, though manual overrides leave an occasional path to incorrect parameters. Approved parameters download automatically with the recipe, adding no meaningful delay. Automated parameter activation removes reliance on operators entering values correctly. Parameter management is a low incremental cost within existing recipe systems.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that critical tank-shell or weld deterioration remains undetected during inspection
rows.plain: Make sure serious damage or weakening of the tank walls or joined seams is found during inspection. Finding it early helps prevent leaks or failure.
rows.imp_rat: Critical shell or weld deterioration missed during inspection is central to verifying containment integrity (relevance 8); an undetected weld defect can rupture catastrophically under pressure (severity 9).
rows.sat_rat: Mature codified weld inspection methods catch most consequential defects, though subsurface and root flaws still occasionally escape. Weld and shell inspection during a turnaround is time-consuming, especially with surface prep and access setup. Detecting weld deterioration relies on skilled RT/UT/PAUT operators whose judgement governs flaw sizing and acceptance. Advanced weld NDT and access scaffolding are expensive but broadly budgeted for critical vessels.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of critical process parameters leaving validated limits during throughput adjustment
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that important production conditions move outside the tested safe range when the production rate is changed. This helps keep the process under control as output changes.
rows.imp_rat: Critical parameters leaving validated limits during throughput adjustment strikes at the heart of controlled processing (relevance 9), producing non-compliant or unsafe product batches (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Sequenced/automated throughput changes keep parameters within limits most of the time, with occasional slips during large or fast adjustments. Ramping controls execute throughput changes gradually to keep parameters within limits, adding controlled time but preventing excursions. Well-designed ramp logic reduces reliance on operator finesse, though manual throughput changes still need care. Excursions during ramping can cause off-spec material, but automated ramping largely contains that cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of programmed ingredient quantities differing from approved recipe values
rows.plain: Reduce mistakes in the amounts of ingredients entered into the recipe compared with the approved amounts. This helps each batch use the intended ingredients and quantities.
rows.imp_rat: Programmed ingredient quantities differing from approved recipe values directly undermine the correct-batch job, and the consequence is a systematically out-of-spec batch across the whole run.
rows.sat_rat: Master-recipe download and formula management reduce manual quantity errors, but transcription and version mismatches still let some through. Comparing programmed quantities to approved recipe values adds verification steps during setup. Detecting a wrong ingredient quantity needs someone who knows the approved recipe, a moderate skill dependency. Review effort is modest but wrong quantities that reach batching waste material and product.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of composition variation between vessel sampling locations at the blend endpoint
rows.plain: Make sure samples taken from different places in the vessel have nearly the same mixture at the end of blending. This shows that the product is mixed evenly throughout.
rows.imp_rat: Composition variation between sampling locations at blend endpoint is core homogeneity failure; severe uniformity consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Spatial composition uniformity is reasonably managed with good mixing but sampling coverage gaps leave residual uncertainty. Multi-location sampling at endpoint adds test time. Correct sampling location selection depends on mixing knowledge. Location-to-location variation risks costly nonconformance.

rows.source_job: Synchronize timing of connected processing stages
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of material exceeding its validated sterile hold limit while waiting between connected stages
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that material stays waiting so long between connected steps that it passes the longest tested time it can remain clean. This helps preserve its cleanliness before the next step.
rows.imp_rat: Exceeding validated sterile hold limits between stages compromises sterility assurance (relevance 7); breaching it can render product unsafe and non-releasable, a severe compliance and safety failure (severity 9).
rows.sat_rat: MES timers and alarms exist in advanced plants, but many facilities still track holds manually, so limit breaches during unplanned waits recur. Sterile hold limits are inherently time-based and interstage delays from upstream/downstream mismatches frequently push material toward the limit. Tracking cumulative hold time across connected stages relies on operator vigilance and manual timers where automation is incomplete. Exceeding a validated hold means discarding sterile-critical material, a costly loss that plants absorb when scheduling slips.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of intended batch conditions in which blend uniformity fails acceptance criteria
rows.plain: Reduce the chances that the ingredients are unevenly mixed during normal production. An even mixture helps ensure each part of the batch has the intended strength.
rows.imp_rat: Intended conditions where blend uniformity fails acceptance is a direct CFJ failure across the operating range; relevant with high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Blend uniformity acceptance testing is a mature practice, so failures within intended conditions are relatively uncommon. Blend-uniformity sampling and assay across conditions extends qualification timelines. Designing valid sampling plans for uniformity needs experienced formulation and statistics staff. Extensive sampling and assay drive analytical cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of process or cleaning residue remaining in the batching system after draining and flushing
rows.plain: Remove as much leftover processing material and cleaning liquid as possible from the mixing system before storing it for a long time. This helps prevent buildup, damage, and contamination.
rows.imp_rat: Residual process or cleaning residue after draining and flushing carries directly into the next batch, undermining the prep job's core purpose; consequences include adulterated product and potential chemical contamination of consumers.
rows.sat_rat: Draining and flushing remove the bulk of residue but consistently leave films and low-point pockets behind. Draining and flushing to reduce residue is time-consuming and often iterative. Optimizing flush sequences to reach low points needs process familiarity. Flush media and disposal of spent flush add recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that homogeneity demonstrated at evaluation scale fails at production scale
rows.plain: Make sure a mixture that is uniform in small-scale testing stays uniform when made in full production. This helps ensure every part of the product has the intended contents.
rows.imp_rat: Homogeneity passing at eval scale but failing at production is a scale-up risk that undermines every commercial batch; relevant with high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Bench-to-production homogeneity translation remains a classic failure mode that standard practice only partially mitigates. Scale-up confirmation trials add significant time before capability is trusted. Reliable scale-up prediction is a specialized mixing science few practitioners command well. Homogeneity failures at production scale trigger expensive batch losses and rework.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of initiating the batch cycle before required sterile conditions are established
rows.plain: Do not start the batch until the required clean, germ-free conditions are in place. Starting too soon could contaminate the batch.
rows.imp_rat: Initiating a batch before sterile conditions are established is central to the CFJ of correct batch preparation (high relevance), and the consequence is a contaminated batch that harms patients and forces destruction (high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Software permissives and sterilization-hold interlocks reliably block cycle start when sterile conditions are unverified, though sensor drift or bypassed permissives remain occasional gaps. Automated pre-start sterility gating adds little delay because sterilization state is confirmed as part of the normal recipe sequence. Modern batch control systems with recipe-enforced permissives remove most reliance on operator judgement to confirm sterile conditions. Interlocked sterility checks are built into existing PLC/DCS logic so incremental cost per batch is negligible.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of active-ingredient concentration variation among vessel locations at the blend endpoint
rows.plain: Make the active ingredient equally concentrated throughout the mixing container at the end of mixing. This helps ensure each portion has the intended amount of medicine.
rows.imp_rat: Active-concentration variation among locations at blend endpoint is core homogeneity failure; severe dose-uniformity consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Validated mixing plus endpoint sampling makes uniform solutions reliably achievable for most formulations. Confirming uniformity requires blend time and sampling turnaround. Judging homogeneity depends on validated parameters more than moment-to-moment skill. Concentration variation risks batch rejection, a high-value loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of a hazardous atmosphere remaining in the tank after purging
rows.plain: Make sure no dangerous vapors or gases are left in the tank after it is cleared out. This protects people who open or enter the tank and prevents unsafe conditions.
rows.imp_rat: A hazardous atmosphere remaining after purging directly defeats the tank's safe-processing job (relevance 8); the consequence is ignition or asphyxiation during subsequent work, causing severe injury (severity 9).
rows.sat_rat: Continuous multi-gas monitoring and lock-out permits make residual hazardous atmospheres uncommon under mature practice, though stratified pockets still occasionally slip through. Purging and gas-testing cycles add hours to the disposal timeline but are well-established sequential steps. Confined-space entry standards and gas-testing protocols exist but demand a competent, certified attendant whose judgement remains the failure point. Purge gases, monitors and standby crews add modest cost that is routinely budgeted for.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of finished liquid becoming contaminated during transfer to the sterile filling stage
rows.plain: Prevent the finished liquid from becoming contaminated while it is moved to the clean filling stage. This helps preserve its safety and quality.
rows.imp_rat: Contamination of finished liquid during transfer to sterile filling occurs at the last quality-critical step; a failure here wastes fully processed product and risks unsafe finished goods, driving high relevance and severity.
rows.sat_rat: SIP'd transfer lines and closed connections make transfer-stage contamination uncommon, though connection points remain the residual weak link. Direct sterile transfer lines to filling minimize handling time between stages. Hard-piped or single-use transfer reduces reliance on operator finesse during the handoff. Validated transfer manifolds and single-use assemblies are established, budgeted costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of contamination entering the opened product-contact path during sealing-component replacement
rows.plain: Keep dirt or germs from entering the opened product path while replacing seals and gaskets. This protects the product from contamination during the repair.
rows.imp_rat: Contamination entering the product-contact path during seal replacement is a maintenance-time error, so somewhat less central to routine batching, but consequences are severe — an undetected contaminated batch causing recall or patient harm.
rows.sat_rat: The opened path during sealing-component work is an established contamination risk that manual practice controls only partially. Seal and gasket replacement is fast mechanically but the surrounding clean-open-reclean sequence dominates the time. Fitting seals into an opened product-contact path cleanly relies on careful hygienic handling that is technician-dependent. Seals are cheap but the associated recleaning and revalidation drive the real cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of initiating the batching cycle while sterile boundary conditions are compromised
rows.plain: Do not start mixing and batching while the barriers that keep the process clean are damaged, open, or otherwise not working properly. This helps prevent contamination of the batch.
rows.imp_rat: Initiating a cycle with compromised sterile boundary conditions is central to sterile batching (relevance 7) and would sterility-fail the whole batch with high safety stakes (severity 9).
rows.sat_rat: Filter integrity tests and pressure interlocks catch many compromised-boundary starts, but undetected micro-breaches still occasionally allow a cycle to begin compromised. Pre-batch integrity checks and pressure holds add setup time before the cycle can begin. Interpreting integrity-test results and confirming boundary status requires trained judgement. Integrity-test instrumentation and the batches held for confirmation carry real cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that candidate capability conclusions fail at intended batch scale
rows.plain: Make sure conclusions about what a possible solution can do still hold when it is used at the size of a real production batch. This helps avoid choosing something that works only in small tests.
rows.imp_rat: Capability conclusions failing at intended scale means the selected vessel underperforms in production; relevant with high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Conclusions drawn at evaluation scale often fail to hold at batch scale, a persistent and imperfectly managed risk. Validating conclusions at intended batch scale prolongs qualification. Extrapolating capability conclusions to full scale requires scarce scale-up judgement. Wrong scale conclusions mean costly late-stage equipment changes.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree to which liquid feedstock is exposed to temperature, mixing, or reaction conditions outside validated limits
rows.plain: Keep the liquid ingredients from being exposed to temperatures, mixing, or chemical changes outside the tested safe ranges. This helps preserve the intended quality of the material.
rows.imp_rat: Exposing feedstock to conditions outside validated limits strikes at the heart of producing conforming liquid product (relevance very high); it directly yields defective batches (severity high).
rows.sat_rat: Validated operating envelopes with automated control keep most feedstock within limits, though transient upsets and unmeasured variables still push conditions outside bounds occasionally. Real-time monitoring flags out-of-validated-limit conditions quickly enough to correct within a run. Defining and defending validated limits demands process knowledge, and interpreting borderline conditions still relies on skilled operators. The instrumentation and validation programs required carry meaningful ongoing cost but are standard in regulated plants.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the finished batch becomes contaminated during discharge
rows.plain: Keep the completed batch from becoming contaminated while it is being emptied or transferred. This protects the quality of the finished product.
rows.imp_rat: Contamination of the finished batch during discharge occurs at the final gate before product leaves the system, so it is highly central; the consequence is a fully contaminated finished batch that may reach customers unmitigated.
rows.sat_rat: Closed discharge into validated containers keeps finished-batch contamination uncommon, though connection points and filling steps remain residual risk. Closed-transfer discharge adds little time versus open handling. Closed and automated discharge routes reduce the operator finesse needed to avoid contamination. Closed-system hardware carries capital cost but prevents expensive contamination losses.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of previous-batch material remaining in wetted components after cleaning
rows.plain: Remove as much material from the previous batch as possible from parts that contact the product during cleaning. This helps prevent one batch from contaminating the next.
rows.imp_rat: Residual previous-batch material in wetted components after cleaning is central to cross-contamination control, and the consequence is a tainted subsequent batch with quality and compliance failures.
rows.sat_rat: CIP with validated limits gets residue low most of the time, but dead legs, gaskets, and complex wetted parts leave consistent residual carryover concerns. Achieving low residual material relies on extended CIP cycles that lengthen changeover time. Reaching validated residue limits in wetted components depends on cleaning-process design skill and swab/rinse interpretation. Cleaning agents, water, energy, and analytical testing to confirm low residue are significant ongoing costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of previous-batch material remaining on wetted components after cleaning
rows.plain: Remove as much material from the previous batch as possible from parts that contact the product during cleaning. This helps prevent one batch from contaminating the next.
rows.imp_rat: Residual material remaining on wetted component surfaces after cleaning is core to preventing carryover, and the consequence is cross-contamination of the following batch with serious quality impact.
rows.sat_rat: Standard cleaning reliably reduces surface residue in accessible areas, but shadowed surfaces and inconsistent coverage keep residual contamination a persistent issue. Surface residue removal on wetted components hinges on cleaning-cycle duration that constrains turnaround. Confirming component surfaces meet residue limits still relies on skilled sampling and cleaning-process knowledge. Repeated cleaning, verification swabbing, and rework carry meaningful recurring resource cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that plant-specific protective infrastructure is disabled while residual process hazards remain
rows.plain: Do not remove the facility’s safety protections while leftover dangers from the process are still present. This keeps people protected until the area is safe.
rows.imp_rat: Disabling protective infrastructure while hazards remain undermines the safety layer around the plant (relevance moderate-high); it exposes personnel to uncontrolled hazards with catastrophic potential (severity very high).
rows.sat_rat: Permit sequencing helps but premature disabling of protective infrastructure while hazards remain is a recognized and error-prone failure mode. Sequencing protection removal after hazard clearance adds scheduling constraints. The central difficulty is expert judgement confirming residual hazards are gone before disabling the infrastructure that guards against them. Maintaining protective systems longer costs little extra.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of target-liquid degradation under the candidate plant's shear, thermal, and residence conditions
rows.plain: Make sure the liquid will not be damaged by the candidate plant's mixing force, heat, or time spent in the equipment. This helps determine whether the process is suitable for the liquid.
rows.imp_rat: Target-liquid degradation under the plant's shear, thermal, and residence conditions directly affects product quality — the core deliverable; degraded product means yield/quality loss though rarely an acute safety catastrophe, so severity is moderately high.
rows.sat_rat: Without product-specific testing, degradation risk is estimated from generic assumptions, so the outcome is often uncertain and only discovered after commissioning. Predicting degradation typically requires trial runs or pilot testing that add significant time to the acquisition decision. Judging shear/thermal/residence effects on a specific liquid demands scarce process-chemistry expertise that most buyers lack during selection. Pilot trials or lab studies to confirm no degradation are expensive and often skipped, leaving the risk unpriced.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of malfunctioning emergency stops, guards, alarms, limit switches, or shutdown interlocks incorrectly recorded as passing the functional test
rows.plain: Avoid marking safety devices as passing when they are actually not working correctly. This includes emergency shutoffs, guards, warnings, position switches, and automatic shutdowns.
rows.imp_rat: Falsely recording a malfunctioning e-stop, guard, or interlock as passing is a testing-integrity issue peripheral to the batching process itself, but severity is maximal since a defeated safety device can cause serious injury.
rows.sat_rat: False-pass recording of a malfunctioning safety device is a serious, recurring failure that manual checklist testing does not reliably catch. Verifying each interlock, e-stop, and alarm actually trips takes time that pressured schedules erode. Correctly confirming a protective device truly functions rather than assuming it passed requires diligent, knowledgeable testing. Thorough functional verification adds labor and downtime cost that encourages shortcutting.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of a critical alarm being obscured by simultaneous lower-severity alarms
rows.plain: Make sure the most serious warning cannot be hidden by several less serious warnings appearing at the same time. This helps workers notice and respond to the greatest danger first.
rows.imp_rat: A critical alarm obscured by lower-severity alarms is highly relevant to operators detecting hazards in time (relevance); a missed critical alarm can allow an excursion to escalate to a catastrophic event (high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Alarm flooding during major upsets is a well-known chronic problem where standard prioritization often fails to reliably surface the critical alarm. Alarm floods appear instantly during upsets, giving operators almost no time to sort critical from trivial. Discriminating the critical alarm amid dozens demands high operator experience and cognitive load, and alarm rationalization projects are inconsistently applied. Poor alarm management leads to costly missed events, but the alarm systems themselves are already installed.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of internal fluid pathways retaining microbial or chemical residue after the cycle
rows.plain: Remove germs and chemical residue from the inside passages that carry liquid. Leftover residue could contaminate the next product.
rows.imp_rat: Microbial or chemical residue retained in internal pathways after the cleaning cycle directly undermines the sanitary preparation job and can cause severe contamination or sterility failures downstream.
rows.sat_rat: Validated CIP with conductivity/rinse verification reliably removes most residue, though dead legs and shadowed geometry occasionally retain residual, keeping it well but not perfectly served. Automated CIP cycles run on validated time programs, so cleaning duration is predictable and largely hands-off. Validated CIP recipes offload most cleaning judgement from technicians, though troubleshooting incomplete cleaning still needs expertise. CIP consumes water, chemicals, and energy but at controlled, budgeted rates.

rows.source_job: Confirm batch system readiness before start
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of accepting an unverified cleaning or sterilization prerequisite as satisfied before batch start
rows.plain: Do not accept cleaning or germ-killing steps as complete unless they have been checked. This helps ensure the batch starts in a properly prepared system.
rows.imp_rat: Accepting an unverified cleaning/sterilization prerequisite as satisfied is a key gating error for the CFJ (high relevance); it lets a contaminated batch proceed with severe downstream harm (high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Where electronic verification of CIP/SIP status ties into start permissives it works well, but manual acceptance of unverified prerequisites remains a common real-world weak point. Requiring verified cleaning records before start can add hold time when documentation lags behind physical readiness. Confirming a cleaning/sterilization prerequisite still often relies on operator sign-off and judgement about what counts as verified. Electronic batch records and CIP verification systems are established but carry meaningful implementation and maintenance cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that cleaning solution bypasses required piping or vessels during the cycle
rows.plain: Make sure the cleaning liquid flows through every required pipe and container instead of skipping any part of the equipment.
rows.imp_rat: Cleaning solution bypassing required piping or vessels leaves uncleaned surfaces that directly defeat the preparation job's hygiene requirement; the consequence is hidden contamination that can carry into subsequent batches undetected.
rows.sat_rat: Automated valve sequencing and flow verification help, yet undetected bypass of a branch or vessel still occurs on complex systems, keeping this only acceptably served. Verifying correct flow-path valve alignment before a cycle adds setup time that is usually built into the routine. Confirming every vessel and line is actually swept requires understanding of the piping and valve matrix that leans on experienced staff. Undetected bypass wastes a full cycle's chemicals and time, but such events are periodic rather than constant.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of contamination entering adjacent sterile process lines through opened interfaces
rows.plain: Keep germs or other unwanted material from entering nearby clean process lines through open connections. This protects the cleanliness of the connected systems.
rows.imp_rat: Contamination entering adjacent sterile process lines through opened interfaces threatens the sterile integrity the CFJ relies on; a breach can compromise multiple downstream sterile products, an irreversible and costly outcome.
rows.sat_rat: Interface capping and isolation practices generally protect adjacent lines, though contamination breaches still occur when discipline lapses. Blanking and capping opened interfaces to protect adjacent sterile lines is a quick standard step. Maintaining sterile-boundary integrity during decommissioning requires cleanroom-aware discipline not universal on demo crews. Caps, blanks, and covers are cheap protective items.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of routing a liquid stream to the wrong vessel or processing stage during concurrent processing
rows.plain: Make sure each liquid goes to the correct container or step in the process when several activities happen at the same time.
rows.imp_rat: Misrouting a stream to the wrong vessel during concurrent processing is a high-consequence error in multi-product plants (relevance 8), risking cross-contamination and full-batch loss (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Matrix interlocks help, but concurrent multi-stream routing raises the chance of cross-connections that current systems don't fully eliminate. Concurrent processing routing is automated but complex, and verification adds coordination time. Managing multiple simultaneous streams demands high operator attention and situational awareness. A misroute during concurrent processing can spoil multiple batches, a high-cost failure.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of viscous product produced outside critical quality specifications at peak throughput
rows.plain: Keep thick product within the required quality range while making it at the highest production rate. This prevents large amounts of unusable product.
rows.imp_rat: Producing viscous product outside quality specs at peak throughput strikes at the plant's core output job (relevance 9); the consequence is rejected/reworked product and lost yield, costly but usually recoverable (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Inline analytics and PID control catch much drift, but viscous, non-Newtonian behaviour at throughput limits still yields recurring spec excursions that standard practice only partly contains. At peak throughput operators have little slack to react before off-spec viscous product accumulates, so timing pressure erodes quality control. Holding spec at maximum rate leans heavily on experienced operators tuning around the plant's limits, a skill-intensive balancing act. Off-spec viscous material at peak rate is costly to rework or scrap, and running below peak to stay safe sacrifices throughput value.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of contaminating a liquid stream while it passes through the required plant flow path
rows.plain: Keep the liquid clean while it travels through the required parts of the facility. Contamination could make the liquid unsafe or unusable.
rows.imp_rat: Contaminating a liquid stream as it passes through the required flow path is core to delivering acceptable product; contamination generally means batch loss and possible safety risk, giving high relevance and substantial severity.
rows.sat_rat: Validated flow paths with CIP/SIP and automated interlocks make cross-contamination uncommon, though shared lines and misrouting remain occasional failure modes. Fixed validated flow paths and automated valve sequencing avoid time-consuming manual routing during transfers. Automated routing reduces dependence on operator skill, but manual valve line-ups where they persist are error-prone. Dedicated piping and automated valve manifolds are capital-intensive but standard plant infrastructure.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of temperature, pressure, or flow excursions beyond specified bounds during the production run
rows.plain: Keep temperature, pressure, and liquid or gas movement within the allowed range during production. If they go outside that range, bring them back as quickly as possible.
rows.imp_rat: Temperature/pressure/flow excursions during the production run are core to keeping the process within validated bounds (relevance very high); prolonged excursions cause off-spec product and possible equipment damage (severity high).
rows.sat_rat: Closed-loop control with alarms and trips keeps most excursions short, but sensor drift, valve stiction and cascade upsets still cause recurring brief deviations. Automated control loops and trips react to excursions in seconds, far faster than manual intervention could. Tuning and setpoint management require experienced process engineers, and poorly tuned loops still let excursions persist. DCS/PLC control infrastructure is a large but sunk investment that most processing plants already run.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that adjusted settings exceed validated operating limits during the batch
rows.plain: Keep changed mixing settings within the tested and approved operating range during production. Settings outside that range could affect the product.
rows.imp_rat: Adjusted settings exceeding validated operating limits during the batch directly threatens the controlled-preparation core job; severity is high due to off-spec and compliance breach, close to item 11's concern.
rows.sat_rat: This closely overlaps need is handled by the same limit-enforcement logic, so excursions are uncommon where those interlocks are properly configured. Automated limit enforcement adds no meaningful delay to setpoint changes. Validated-range interlocks reduce reliance on operator knowledge of acceptable bounds. Blocking out-of-limit settings averts costly deviation and rework at negligible marginal cost.

rows.source_job: Set up and verify safety systems
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of hazardous batch sequences permitted by safety interlock logic
rows.plain: Prevent the safety system from allowing a dangerous sequence of steps. This helps stop equipment from operating in an unsafe way.
rows.imp_rat: Hazardous sequences permitted by interlock logic reflect a defect in the safety design central to safe operation (high relevance); each permitted hazardous sequence can produce severe injury or equipment loss (high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Interlock matrices and sequence testing catch most hazardous permissive paths, yet combinatorial edge cases sometimes remain permitted, keeping this short of solved. Enumerating and blocking hazardous sequences requires methodical HAZOP-driven testing that lengthens commissioning but is routine. Identifying all hazardous permissible sequences leans on experienced process-safety engineers, a real dependency on scarce judgement. Sequence hazard analysis and interlock coding consume engineering budget but are standard in capital projects.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of critical batch deviations not recognized before affecting the batch
rows.plain: Catch serious problems in a batch before they can affect the product. This helps prevent a damaged or unusable batch from continuing unnoticed.
rows.imp_rat: Failing to recognize critical deviations before they affect the batch is central to protecting batch quality (high relevance); once the batch is affected the loss is severe (high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Standard monitoring recognizes some deviations before impact but a meaningful fraction are only caught after the batch is already affected. Recognizing a deviation before it affects the batch demands detection faster than the process dynamics, which current polling and review cadence sometimes miss. Anticipating which deviations will harm the batch requires process knowledge that varies widely among operators. Advanced predictive or model-based detection carries cost that many facilities have not funded.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of ingredient sources mapped to an incorrect tank, line, pump, valve, or dosing channel
rows.plain: Make sure each ingredient source is connected to the correct equipment. This prevents an ingredient from being sent to the wrong place.
rows.imp_rat: Mapping an ingredient source to the wrong tank, line, pump, or valve directly causes wrong-ingredient batches (high relevance) with severe consequences—cross-dosing errors can render product unsafe (high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Commissioning checks catch most mismapping, but undocumented changes and manual hose connections leave recurring misroute risk. Verifying source-to-destination mapping is a one-time commissioning task rather than a per-batch delay. Correct mapping depends on engineering knowledge and careful P&ID interpretation, making it skill-sensitive. Mapping verification is low material cost but errors cause expensive downstream rework.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required unsafe equipment states omitted from functional testing
rows.plain: Make sure testing includes every situation in which the equipment could become unsafe. This helps confirm that the safety shutoffs work when they are needed.
rows.imp_rat: Omitting required unsafe equipment states from functional testing is a validation/testing gap somewhat peripheral to routine batching, but the severity is very high because an untested unsafe state can lead to injury or equipment failure.
rows.sat_rat: Omission of required unsafe-state tests is a recognized chronic gap since manual test coverage frequently falls short of the full required set. Functionally testing every unsafe state and fault condition is time-consuming and often abbreviated under schedule pressure. Knowing which unsafe states must be provoked and how to force them safely demands high controls/safety expertise that is scarce. Comprehensive fault-state testing costs downtime and specialist labor, tempting omission.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percent of batch material remaining undissolved at homogeneity assessment
rows.plain: Reduce the amount of material left undissolved when the mixture is checked for evenness. This helps ensure that the batch is properly dissolved and mixed.
rows.imp_rat: Percent undissolved at homogeneity assessment is a direct measure of CFJ success; consequences are serious potency/uniformity nonconformance.
rows.sat_rat: Validated dissolution parameters make full dissolution the norm for well-characterized formulations, with residual failures on difficult actives. Dissolution endpoints are checked at set times, so undissolved material is usually caught before proceeding but not instantly. Visual and analytical checks for undissolved solids are routine and require moderate operator competence. Undissolved fractions trigger extended mixing or rejection, wasting material and energy.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of microbial, particulate, or cross-product contamination during dissolution and blending
rows.plain: Prevent germs, unwanted particles, or another product from getting into the medicine while it is being dissolved and mixed. This helps keep the product safe and consistent.
rows.imp_rat: Microbial/particulate/cross-product contamination during compounding is catastrophic for a pharmaceutical bulk solution, though tangential to the dissolve-and-blend mechanics; consequences are severe patient harm.
rows.sat_rat: Mature cleaning validation, closed vessels, and environmental monitoring substantially reduce contamination, though residual excursions still occur and are hard to eliminate. Environmental and bioburden monitoring results lag the blending step, so contamination is often confirmed after the fact rather than prevented in real time. Aseptic technique, cleaning validation, and contamination-control expertise are demanding and depend heavily on well-trained personnel. Cleanroom controls, cleaning validation, and monitoring are expensive but treated as non-negotiable GMP infrastructure.

rows.source_job: Feed viscous material into processing path
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that incorrect feedstock enters the active processing path during a production run
rows.plain: Make sure only the correct material enters the part of the machine currently processing the product. This prevents the wrong material from mixing with or damaging the product.
rows.imp_rat: Incorrect feedstock entering the active path corrupts the entire run (relevance 8) and can create hazardous or non-compliant product requiring full batch destruction (severity 9).
rows.sat_rat: Scan-to-confirm dispensing and recipe interlocks are mature and widely deployed, though manual overrides and unbarcoded materials leave a residual misfeed risk. Feedstock verification adds a short confirmation step at charging that rarely bottlenecks the run. Barcode/RFID material verification and interlocked charging reduce reliance on operator memory to pick the right feedstock. A wrong-feedstock event ruins an entire run, so verification systems are cheap insurance against high-value loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of active or excipient material settled in lower vessel regions during the pre-fill hold
rows.plain: Keep the active ingredient and other ingredients from collecting at the bottom of the container while the mixture waits to be filled. This helps ensure every filled product has the right mixture.
rows.imp_rat: Material settling during pre-fill hold destroys the achieved homogeneity before fill; relevant with high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Minimizing pre-fill hold and agitation practice limits settling, but it still occurs during interruptions. Re-suspending settled material before mixing adds handling time. Managing pre-fill holds to avoid settling takes operational awareness. Settled material can be lost or cause off-spec, wasting product.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of active or excipient material degraded by mixing exposure at the target volume
rows.plain: Reduce the amount of medicine ingredient or other added material damaged by mixing at the intended batch size. This helps preserve the product’s quality and effectiveness.
rows.imp_rat: Material degraded by mixing exposure reduces potency and creates impurities; strongly relevant with high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Validated conditions usually limit degradation, but scale-up thermal and shear effects occasionally push it up. Quantifying degradation at target volume needs analytical turnaround that adds time. Predicting scale-dependent degradation requires specialized process knowledge. Material degraded at scale is costly lost product.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the corrective response fails to restore dissolution and blend homogeneity
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that the fix for a production problem fails to fully dissolve the material and mix it evenly. This helps restore the mixture to the required condition.
rows.imp_rat: A corrective response that fails to restore homogeneity leaves a defective batch unrecoverable; central to the job's robustness with high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Standard reblend procedures usually restore homogeneity, but success is not guaranteed for degraded or heavily deviating batches. Corrective reblending consumes additional mixing cycles that extend the batch timeline. Choosing an effective corrective response leans heavily on experienced operators and process knowledge. A failed corrective attempt wastes further material, energy and time before the batch is salvaged or lost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of lubricant entering the sterile processing environment during component lubrication
rows.plain: Keep oil or grease from getting into the germ-free processing area while moving parts are being lubricated. This protects the product and the clean working environment.
rows.imp_rat: Lubricant entering a sterile environment during lubrication is fairly central to maintaining product integrity, and contamination consequences are severe, risking product safety and regulatory sanction.
rows.sat_rat: Sealed bearings and food-grade lubricants reduce ingress, yet lubricant reaching the sterile environment during application remains an intermittent, not fully solved problem. Careful lubrication near sterile zones slows the task as it demands deliberate, controlled application. Correct dosing and containment during lubrication rely on experienced technicians rather than foolproof methods. Sterile-compatible lubricants and any resulting rework add cost but remain manageable.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of deviation between reported and actual batch quality attributes
rows.plain: Make sure the reported product quality matches the product’s actual quality. This matters because decisions should be based on accurate results.
rows.imp_rat: Deviation between reported and actual batch quality attributes corrupts the verification that the job was done (high relevance) and is severe because bad batches pass as good, risking release of defective product (high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Validated assays and electronic batch records keep reported-versus-actual deviation modest, though sampling representativeness and manual data entry leave recurring gaps. Lab QC testing to confirm actual attributes introduces significant reporting lag. Interpreting analytical results and reconciling them to reported values needs trained QC personnel. Analytical testing, sampling, and documentation are ongoing cost drivers.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of microbial or particulate contamination entering the liquid stream during input addition
rows.plain: Keep germs, dust, and other unwanted material out of the liquid while ingredients or other materials are added. This helps keep the liquid clean and suitable for processing.
rows.imp_rat: Microbial or particulate contamination entering during input addition is central to maintaining stream integrity during processing; such ingress can compromise the batch and downstream safety, giving both proxies high marks.
rows.sat_rat: Aseptic connectors and closed charging reduce ingress, but manual additions and open transfers remain a common contamination entry point. Input addition through validated ports is reasonably quick but manual charging steps slow it. Sterile addition depends on operator technique at transfer connections, a persistent skill-sensitive step. Single-use aseptic connectors and closed transfer sets add cost but are widely adopted.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of diverted liquid released into the sterile processing area
rows.plain: Release less unwanted liquid into the clean work area when separating out material that does not meet requirements.
rows.imp_rat: Diverted liquid released into the sterile processing area directly threatens sterility, a core function (relevance high); such release can force batch rejection and area re-sterilization, a serious consequence (severity high).
rows.sat_rat: Diversion systems reliably route away from sterile areas, but the volume released still varies with detection speed. Diversion routing is automatic and quick, not a time issue. Automated diversion to drain reduces operator involvement. Diverted liquid is lost product plus cleanup, a recurring material cost with only partial recovery practice.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of product-contact surfaces and vessel areas retaining prior-product residue after sanitation
rows.plain: Remove more of the previous product from surfaces that touch the product and from inside containers before switching to a new product.
rows.imp_rat: Product-contact surfaces retaining residue after sanitation undermines every subsequent batch (relevance 8) and risks microbial or cross-contamination with recall-level consequences (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Validated CIP recipes cover most cases but viscous products leave recurring residue in complex geometries that periodic sampling only samples, not guarantees. Verifying residue-free surfaces through swabbing and rinse sampling extends turnaround but is a standard step. Judging adequate cleaning and interpreting residue results relies on trained sanitation and QC personnel. Repeated cleaning cycles and analytical testing consume water, chemicals and labor when residue is found.

rows.stk: Process engineers, system operators, batch technicians.
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of ingredient quantity values distorted by unit or scaling errors
rows.plain: Avoid entering ingredient amounts incorrectly because of confusing measurement units or changes in scale, so each batch has the right ingredients.
rows.imp_rat: Ingredient quantity values distorted by unit or scaling errors strike the heart of accurate batching; the consequence is a mis-formulated batch that may be unsafe or unusable, directly failing the core job.
rows.sat_rat: Recipe management systems with unit fields reduce these errors, but manual transcription still allows them to slip through. Unit or scaling errors are typically caught late, adding rework time when they surface. Preventing unit/scaling distortion depends on careful data entry and reviewer attention, a skill-dependent step. Data validation costs little but an undetected scaling error can scrap a whole batch.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that collected samples do not represent component distribution throughout the compounding vessel
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that collected samples fail to show how ingredients are spread throughout the mixing container. This helps confirm that the whole mixture is evenly mixed.
rows.imp_rat: Non-representative samples misjudge distribution, allowing nonuniform batches to pass; relevant with high severity as an escape risk.
rows.sat_rat: Sampling that fails to capture true spatial distribution in a large tank is a well-known blind spot, and validation of sampling representativeness is often weaker than assay reliability itself. Representative sampling plans are executed within the normal batch cycle, so timing is not the primary concern. Designing and executing spatially representative sampling of a large vessel demands real sampling expertise and is error-prone. Multiple sampling points and analyses increase cost modestly but acceptably.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percent of vessel working volume outside composition acceptance limits under worst-case intended conditions
rows.plain: Keep more of the mixture within the required ingredient ranges throughout the container, even under the most difficult expected conditions. This helps ensure the product is evenly mixed and has the right composition.
rows.imp_rat: Working volume outside composition limits under worst case is direct non-uniformity; relevant to CFJ with high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Dead zones and stratification under worst-case conditions are inconsistently predicted, leaving real recurring gaps. Assessing worst-case composition uniformity across working volume takes added sampling time. Identifying worst-case volume conditions needs experienced process interpretation. Out-of-spec volume fractions cause batch rejection costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of leaving a tested protective control bypassed or incorrectly reset after functional testing
rows.plain: Make sure a safety feature that was tested is turned back on and set correctly afterward. This helps ensure the machine will respond safely if a dangerous condition occurs.
rows.imp_rat: Leaving a protective control bypassed or wrongly reset after testing is a maintenance-state error somewhat peripheral to batching itself, but severity is very high as an unguarded system can cause injury during subsequent operation.
rows.sat_rat: Left-bypassed or mis-reset safety controls remain a well-documented recurring incident cause that current permit/tag practice only partly prevents. Restoring and re-verifying every bypass after testing is a final step often rushed at job close-out. Tracking which protective controls were bypassed and confirming correct reset depends on technician memory and discipline. Reset verification adds little material cost but the consequences of a missed reset are severe.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the candidate plant loses process control at the extremes of the target liquid's viscosity and temperature ranges
rows.plain: Make sure the plant can keep the process under control when the liquid is at the thickest, thinnest, hottest, or coolest expected condition. This helps show that the process will work reliably across its intended range.
rows.imp_rat: Loss of process control at viscosity and temperature extremes is central to reliably processing the target liquid; excursions typically cause off-spec product or trips, with serious but usually recoverable consequences, so severity is moderately high.
rows.sat_rat: Suppliers typically warrant nominal operating points, so behaviour at the true extremes is frequently unproven until operation, a recurring surprise. Verifying control at viscosity/temperature extremes usually needs vendor simulations or trials that stretch the evaluation timeline. Assessing control-loop stability across a wide operating envelope requires specialist control and rheology knowledge rarely resident in the acquisition team. Demonstrating extreme-condition control may require test rigs or extra instrumentation quotes, adding cost that is often avoided.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the operator response worsens the out-of-limit process condition
rows.plain: Make sure the operator’s actions do not make a process problem worse when an alarm shows that conditions are outside the allowed limits. This helps bring the process back to a safe and acceptable state.
rows.imp_rat: An operator response that worsens an out-of-limit condition is somewhat peripheral to routine processing (relevance 7), but escalation can trigger runaway conditions, equipment damage, or safety incidents (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Procedures and alarm guidance help, but human decision error during upsets is a well-known, poorly mitigated failure mode. Under time pressure operators must act fast, and hasty responses frequently worsen the upset. Correct response depends squarely on operator diagnostic skill and experience, which vary widely and degrade under stress. A wrong response can escalate into batch loss or equipment damage, a costly failure mode.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of introducing microbial or particulate contaminants to product-contact surfaces during rinsing
rows.plain: Keep germs and bits of unwanted material from getting onto product-touching surfaces while they are rinsed between batches.
rows.imp_rat: Introducing microbial or particulate contaminants during rinsing directly corrupts product-contact surfaces, central to the CFJ of preparing safe liquid batches; consequences can render entire batches unsafe and trigger recalls or health harm.
rows.sat_rat: Qualified CIP and rinse validation make rinse-introduced contamination rare in well-maintained systems, though poor drainage and rinse-water quality still cause occasional excursions. Validated CIP/rinse cycles run on fixed timed sequences without adding operator effort. Automated cleaning cycles largely remove reliance on operator technique for rinse integrity. Rinse water, cleaning agents and utilities are ongoing costs, but far cheaper than a contamination event.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of unsafe or out-of-spec process control behavior after the updated control system returns to service
rows.plain: After new control software is put back into use, prevent the process from behaving unsafely or producing results outside the required limits.
rows.imp_rat: Out-of-spec control behavior after a control-system update directly governs process integrity on return to service (relevance high); unsafe behavior can produce defective product or hazardous conditions (severity high).
rows.sat_rat: Factory/site acceptance testing and staged startup catch many issues, but latent control errors after updates still produce out-of-spec behavior on return to service. Post-update verification and controlled restart of the control system extend the return-to-service window. Confirming safe control behavior after an update requires scarce controls-engineering expertise. Software validation and staged commissioning consume engineering effort but little material cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of technician exposure to hazardous energy while servicing pumps, valves, agitators, or drives
rows.plain: Technicians should have as little contact as possible with dangerous stored or active power while servicing pumps, valves, mixing equipment, or motors. This helps protect them from electric, pressure, or movement-related injuries.
rows.imp_rat: Technician exposure to hazardous energy while servicing pumps, valves, agitators, or drives is highly central to safe plant operation and maintenance; the error produces potentially fatal injuries, so severity is maximal even if servicing is periodic rather than continuous.
rows.sat_rat: OSHA-mandated energy-isolation practice, verification steps and permit systems make hazardous-energy exposure rare where the procedure is followed, though human lapses still occur. Lockout/tagout procedures add time to every job but the delay is well-understood and built into maintenance planning. LOTO is a mature, codified discipline that most trained technicians execute competently with minimal specialist judgement required. Padlocks, tags and isolation hardware are cheap and the main cost is the labor time of proper isolation.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that hazardous plant equipment continues operating after emergency-control activation
rows.plain: Make sure dangerous factory equipment stops after the emergency control is activated. This helps protect people from continued movement or operation.
rows.imp_rat: Equipment continuing to run after emergency-stop is central to safe plant operation (high relevance) and can cause fatal injury or catastrophic asset loss when it occurs (severity 10).
rows.sat_rat: Functional-safety standards (IEC , redundancy and mandated proof-testing make residual failure to stop after activation rare, though stored energy and slow-coasting mixers occasionally keep hazardous motion briefly alive. Hardwired E-stop and safety-instrumented systems cut power to hazardous equipment within milliseconds of activation, so time is essentially not the constraint here. SIL-rated emergency-stop circuits are engineered to act without any operator judgement once triggered, so little skill is demanded at the moment of activation. Certified safety relays, redundant sensors and periodic proof-testing carry real capital and maintenance cost but are standard and budgeted in modern plants.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of cleaning-agent residue remaining on product-contact surfaces after cleaning
rows.plain: As little cleaning liquid as possible should remain on surfaces that touch the product after cleaning. This helps prevent the product from being exposed to cleaning chemicals.
rows.imp_rat: Cleaning-agent residue on product-contact surfaces is central to producing safe, uncontaminated liquid product (high relevance); residual chemicals can adulterate product and harm consumers, a severe regulatory and safety failure (high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Mature CIP validation with conductivity, TOC and swab testing makes residual carryover on contact surfaces reliably detectable and controllable, though occasional dead-leg misses occur. Validated CIP cycles and conductivity monitoring add rinse time but keep the process on a predictable schedule. Cleaning validation protocols and automated CIP skids reduce operator judgement, though swab-test interpretation still needs trained QC staff. Rinse water, cleaning agents, and analytical testing consume resources, but these are budgeted routine costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a critical process alarm fails to reach the control room operator during active liquid processing
rows.plain: Make sure an important process warning reaches the person watching the operation while liquid is being handled. This allows that person to respond quickly to a problem.
rows.imp_rat: A critical alarm failing to reach the control room operator during active processing defeats the plant's safety response chain; undetected excursions can escalate to loss of containment or product loss, making both proxies high.
rows.sat_rat: Redundant DCS annunciation with alarm rationalization per ISA- a critical alarm rarely failing to reach the operator, though nuisance-alarm masking is a real residual gap. DCS/SCADA alarms annunciate to the control room essentially instantly once configured correctly. Well-designed alarm management reduces reliance on operator vigilance, but alarm floods still demand skilled prioritization to avoid missing critical ones. Alarm-management systems and rationalization projects carry cost but are standard in modern control rooms.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that ingredient proportions are incorrect after scaling the batch quantity
rows.plain: Make sure the ingredient amounts stay in the correct proportions when the batch size is changed. This helps produce the intended mixture.
rows.imp_rat: Incorrect ingredient proportions after scaling directly defeats the preparation job (very high relevance); the whole batch is off-formula, causing waste or unsafe product (high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Recipe-management scaling reliably preserves proportions for standard formulations, with rare errors mostly from non-linear or edge-case ingredients. Automated scaling computes proportions instantly at batch definition. Recipe scaling logic removes the manual arithmetic that once required careful operator attention. Scaling is a built-in software function with negligible marginal cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of contaminants entering the sterile process boundary during purging or flushing
rows.plain: The chance of unwanted dirt or germs entering the clean processing area should be as low as possible while the pipes are cleared and rinsed. This helps keep the process clean.
rows.imp_rat: Contaminants entering the sterile boundary during purging or flushing undermines the very step meant to clean the system (high relevance) and can seed contamination into subsequent batches (high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Validated flush procedures work well when followed, but misdirected flow or incomplete purging can still admit contaminants at boundary transitions. Proper purge and flush sequences take time to complete and verify before the boundary is considered clean. Correctly setting up flow paths and directions to keep the sterile boundary intact requires trained judgement. Purge media, water and flushing agents are consumed and disposed of at recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that prescribed cleaning parameters leave residues above acceptance limits on product-contact surfaces
rows.plain: Make sure the required cleaning process removes enough leftover material from surfaces that touch the product. This helps prevent residue from getting into the product.
rows.imp_rat: Cleaning parameters leaving residues above acceptance limits directly compromises product-contact cleanliness central to the job (high relevance); cross-contamination causes rejected batches and patient risk (high severity).
rows.sat_rat: CIP cycle development and validation handle typical cases, but residue excursions above limits still recur with difficult geometries or new products, leaving a real gap. Cleaning-parameter development and validation swabbing extend preparation timelines considerably. Setting effective cleaning parameters requires cleaning-validation expertise and knowledge of soil chemistry that many sites lack in depth. Recovery studies, swab analysis, and solvent use make residue-limit assurance moderately costly.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a critical batch deviation remains unrecognized during the cycle
rows.plain: Make sure a serious problem with a production batch is noticed while the batch is still being made. Early notice allows people to respond before the problem gets worse.
rows.imp_rat: A critical batch deviation going unrecognized during the cycle undermines the monitoring central to producing a conforming batch (high relevance); undetected deviations yield defective or unsafe product (high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Alarm and trend systems catch many deviations but alarm floods, poor thresholds, and normalized alerts allow critical deviations to go unrecognized more often than desired. Continuous monitoring flags deviations in near real time, but recognition can lag when alarms are reviewed rather than acted on immediately. Distinguishing critical deviations from noise still leans heavily on operator experience and alarm interpretation. Deviation-monitoring instrumentation and historians are established investments already in place at most facilities.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of prior-formulation residue contaminating the first batch after a formulation switch
rows.plain: As little leftover material from the previous product as possible should get into the first batch of the new product. This helps prevent the new batch from being mixed with the old one.
rows.imp_rat: Prior-formulation residue contaminating the first post-switch batch is central to changeover integrity (relevance 8); cross-contamination can force recalls or endanger consumers (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Validated CIP and swab/rinse verification handle most switches, yet clingy high-viscosity residues in dead legs cause recurring first-batch contamination risk. Validated cleaning between formulation switches consumes significant changeover time that plants tolerate to avoid carryover. CIP recipes standardize the procedure, but verifying complete removal of viscous residue still needs skilled sampling and judgement. Cleaning chemicals, water, energy and lost production time make residue avoidance expensive, and a failed first batch adds scrap cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of active or excipient degradation produced at the proposed operating boundaries
rows.plain: Limit damage to the medicine ingredients and other ingredients when choosing the equipment settings. This helps keep the product effective and consistent.
rows.imp_rat: Degradation at operating boundaries threatens product quality across the range; relevant with high severity for impurity/potency impacts.
rows.sat_rat: Boundaries are often less thoroughly characterized for degradation, leaving meaningful residual uncertainty. Assessing degradation at operating boundaries requires additional stability testing time. Understanding degradation at range edges is a demanding development task. Boundary degradation wastes active material.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of cleaning-agent residue remaining in the process circuit after the final rinse
rows.plain: Leave as little cleaning liquid as possible in the pipes and equipment after the final rinse. This helps prevent the cleaning liquid from affecting the next process.
rows.imp_rat: Cleaning-agent residue remaining in the process circuit after final rinse directly threatens product safety (high relevance); it introduces chemical adulteration into product that reaches consumers, a severe consequence (high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Inline conductivity and TOC endpoint verification of final rinse is a mature, widely deployed practice that reliably confirms residue clearance in the circuit. Automated final-rinse monitoring adds cycle time but is well integrated into standard CIP programs. Inline conductivity/TOC sensors offload most judgement from operators onto validated setpoints. Extra rinse water and testing are consumed but represent modest recurring cost against product safety.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of active processing equipment units that remain operating after emergency shutdown is initiated
rows.plain: Stop as much of the operating equipment as possible once an emergency shutdown begins. This helps limit danger and prevent the situation from getting worse.
rows.imp_rat: Equipment continuing to operate after emergency shutdown is initiated defeats the purpose of the shutdown and is central to controlling an unfolding hazard; residual operation can drive escalation to catastrophic release, so severity is high.
rows.sat_rat: Cause-and-effect matrices drive coordinated equipment shutdown reliably, though residual units on independent supplies or requiring manual isolation can linger. ESD logic de-energizes designated equipment groups near-simultaneously on trip, leaving little lag. Sequenced shutdown logic removes operator dependence on remembering which units to stop. Shutdown-group engineering is embedded in existing control design at modest incremental cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of composition nonuniformity within the scaled batch
rows.plain: Make the materials in the larger batch as evenly mixed as possible. This helps ensure that each portion has the intended composition.
rows.imp_rat: Composition nonuniformity within the scaled batch is the definitional core of the batching/preparation job; severity is high because non-uniform product fails spec, though it is typically scrapped rather than causing irreversible harm.
rows.sat_rat: Well-characterized agitation and blend-time studies make in-spec uniformity the routine outcome, though dead zones and scale-up effects still occasionally cause stratification. Validated mixing recipes with defined agitation times deliver uniform batches without extra operator time. Once mixing parameters are qualified the operator needs little judgement, reducing skill dependence substantially. Properly specified agitation adds modest energy cost while preventing costly non-uniform rejects.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of sterile product-contact boundaries being compromised during reconfiguration
rows.plain: Keep the sterile surfaces and connections protected while changing from one product mixture to another. This helps prevent contamination during the changeover.
rows.imp_rat: Compromising sterile product-contact boundaries during reconfiguration threatens sterility whenever the line is changed over (moderately high relevance) and can contaminate the next production run (high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Post-reconfiguration integrity depends on manual make-and-break connections, a recurring source of boundary compromise. Reconfiguration requires re-sterilization steps that add significant time. Reconnecting product-contact boundaries aseptically depends heavily on technician technique and correct re-SIP. Re-sterilization consumes utilities and time each reconfiguration.

rows.source_job: Confirm batch system readiness before start
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an unsatisfied safety interlock condition remains undetected before batch start
rows.plain: Make sure no required safety condition is missing or unnoticed before the batch starts. This helps prevent the machine from starting when it is not safe or ready.
rows.imp_rat: An undetected unsatisfied interlock before start undermines the pre-start safety gate central to safe batch preparation (high relevance), and starting into a hazardous state risks severe harm (high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Validated safety interlocks per ISA/IEC standards catch most unsatisfied conditions before start, but undetected sensor faults or defeated interlocks are the residual failure mode. Interlock status is polled continuously so unsatisfied conditions surface instantly without added wait. Standard safety-PLC interlock logic checks conditions automatically, sharply reducing dependence on operator vigilance. Interlock hardware and logic are already standard equipment cost with minimal marginal expense.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of charging the wrong component because its identity is ambiguous in the addition sequence
rows.plain: Make the order for adding ingredients clear enough that workers do not add the wrong one. This helps prevent mistakes in the product.
rows.imp_rat: Charging the wrong component from ambiguous identity is a catastrophic wrong-material error; lower mixing-mechanics relevance but severe patient-safety consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Barcoding and dispensing checks reduce mix-ups, but ambiguous labeling still leaves residual human-error risk. Resolving ambiguous component identity during charging can stall the sequence. Avoiding wrong-component charges depends heavily on operator attention and clear labeling rather than automated verification. A wrong component ruins the batch, a large material loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of active ingredient degradation caused by the established mixing conditions
rows.plain: Keep the active medicine from breaking down because of how it is mixed. This helps the finished product remain effective.
rows.imp_rat: Active degradation from mixing conditions defeats the goal of a usable homogeneous solution; severe potency loss and impurity generation.
rows.sat_rat: Validated gentle mixing conditions keep degradation low in most batches, with rare excursions. Confirming degradation stays low requires stability assays that delay disposition. Setting mixing conditions that avoid degradation demands formulation and thermal expertise. Degraded active is unrecoverable product loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of loading an ingredient that differs from the batch specification
rows.plain: Make sure the ingredients loaded match the recipe for that batch. This helps prevent the wrong materials from being used.
rows.imp_rat: Loading an ingredient differing from specification is a core execution error for correct batch preparation (high relevance); a wrong ingredient can render the batch unsafe or unusable (high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Barcode-gated dispensing catches most wrong-ingredient events, but manual overrides, unscanned materials, and label errors leave a real residual failure rate. Barcode or weigh-station verification at loading adds only seconds while confirming the ingredient identity. Scan-to-verify systems reduce dependence on operator recognition of correct ingredients. Dispensing verification and labeling systems are moderate, widely adopted investments.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of recontaminating cleaned product-contact surfaces before batch materials are introduced
rows.plain: Keep cleaned surfaces that touch the product from getting dirty again before the materials are added. This helps keep the product free from unwanted contamination.
rows.imp_rat: Recontamination after cleaning but before charging negates the cleaning step central to a safe batch (relevance 8), leading to contaminated product with high consequence (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Clean-hold-time limits and closed systems help, but recontamination during the gap before batching remains a real recurring vulnerability. Keeping circuits closed and pressurized after cleaning until batching adds hold-time management overhead. Maintaining post-clean integrity depends on operators respecting clean-hold windows and closed-system discipline. Nitrogen blanketing or clean-hold consumables add modest cost to protect cleaned surfaces.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of introducing a liquid other than the designated liquid into a required circuit during priming
rows.plain: Put only the intended liquid into the required pipes and equipment when filling them for use. This helps prevent the process from being contaminated or disrupted by the wrong liquid.
rows.imp_rat: Introducing the wrong liquid into a circuit during priming directly undermines batch identity and purity, a central concern, with severe consequences of a mis-formulated or unsafe batch.
rows.sat_rat: Dedicated circuits and interlocks prevent most wrong-liquid priming, but shared manifolds still leave a real risk of cross-introduction. Confirming the correct priming liquid adds a verification step before flow. Avoiding cross-liquid priming depends on operator line identification and valve alignment knowledge. Dedicated lines and verification hardware cost more than shared manifolds.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that mixing requirements omit production-scale blending behavior
rows.plain: Make sure the mixing instructions reflect how the process behaves when it is run at full production size. This helps prevent a mix from turning out differently when production increases.
rows.imp_rat: Requirements omitting production-scale blending behavior risk scale-up failure; relevant upstream with high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Requirements frequently reflect bench behavior and omit production-scale mixing dynamics, a recurring source of downstream failure. Adding production-scale blending analysis extends the evaluation timeline. Anticipating full-scale blending behavior from lab data is a scarce scale-up specialty. Ignoring scale effects risks costly capacity or capability shortfalls after purchase.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of purge discharge entering an active or clean process path
rows.plain: Keep material flushed out of pipes from entering a pipe route that is being used or kept clean. This prevents unwanted material from reaching the process.
rows.imp_rat: Purge discharge entering an active or clean process path directly undermines product integrity, central to the CFJ (relevance high); cross-contamination can ruin batches and force recalls (severity high).
rows.sat_rat: Valve line-up procedures and physical drain segregation handle most cases, but cross-connection errors still occasionally route discharge into active paths. Segregating purge discharge to drain rather than active paths is a quick routing step within the procedure. Correct valve line-up to keep discharge out of clean paths relies on procedural discipline more than rare skill. Dedicated drain routing is inexpensive infrastructure with low operating cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration that a critical process excursion remains unnoticed while the operator observes concurrent readings
rows.plain: Notice unusual process changes as quickly as possible while watching several readings at once. Early warning gives the operator more time to respond.
rows.imp_rat: Duration a critical excursion goes unnoticed is central to timely intervention (relevance); prolonged undetected excursions can cause equipment damage, releases, or unsafe conditions (high severity).
rows.sat_rat: DCS trending and alarms catch most excursions eventually, but the unnoticed interval remains variable and operator-dependent. The lag between an excursion occurring and an operator noticing it while scanning multiple screens is a recognized, recurring delay. Timely detection depends on operator vigilance and pattern recognition across many concurrent readings, a demanding skill. Missed excursions cause off-spec product and energy waste, though monitoring infrastructure is already in place.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of cleaned product-contact surfaces becoming recontaminated before processing resumes
rows.plain: Keep cleaned surfaces that touch the product from becoming dirty again before work starts. This helps keep the product safe.
rows.imp_rat: Recontamination of cleaned product-contact surfaces before restart defeats the cleaning job central to hygienic liquid processing (relevance 8), and can contaminate a full batch requiring disposal (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Clean-hold time limits and sanitized-hold practice reduce recontamination well, but breaches during extended holds or restarts still occur. Maintaining cleaned surfaces under hold until restart adds monitoring time but no major delay. Preserving cleaned surfaces relies on following hold SOPs rather than expert judgement. Sterile hold conditions and re-flush before restart consume utilities at modest recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of difference between indicated and actual batch conditions during the cycle
rows.plain: Keep the displayed process conditions as close as possible to the real conditions in the batch. Accurate readings help workers control the process safely and correctly.
rows.imp_rat: A gap between indicated and actual batch conditions is central because operators act on the indications; the consequence is undetected process deviations producing defective batches.
rows.sat_rat: Calibrated sensors keep indicated close to actual for most parameters, but sensor drift, placement, and fouling still introduce discrepancies between reading and reality. Live sensor readouts present indicated conditions in real time with negligible lag from actual state.
