Calibrated instrumentation removes most need for operators to infer true conditions from indirect signs. Field instrumentation and calibration programs are standard, established costs in batch plants.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of prior-recipe residue contaminating the new formulation during changeover
rows.plain: Prevent leftover material from the previous product from getting into the new product when changing formulas.
rows.imp_rat: Prior-recipe residue contaminating a new formulation during changeover is central to producing correct, safe product (high relevance); cross-contamination causes batch rejection, recalls, and consumer harm (high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Validated CIP and changeover protocols are mature and largely prevent residue carryover, yet dead legs and complex geometries still occasionally allow contamination. Automated CIP sequences add cleaning time between batches, but they run on established timed cycles that are well understood. Validated cleaning recipes and automated changeover reduce reliance on operator finesse, though verification still needs trained oversight. Cleaning consumes water, chemicals and downtime, and rework from cross-contamination is costly when it occurs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that updated process logic drives critical process parameters outside validated limits
rows.plain: Make sure software changes do not make important process conditions go outside their approved safe ranges. This helps keep the process working safely and correctly.
rows.imp_rat: Updated process logic pushing parameters outside validated limits is core to controlled processing (relevance 8) and can cause scrapped batches, safety excursions, or regulatory deviations (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Established change-management and validation protocols catch most excursions, yet human review gaps still occasionally let logic drive parameters out of limits. Change validation and requalification of updated process logic takes time but is routinely scheduled into maintenance windows. Verifying that logic changes keep parameters inside validated limits demands scarce controls-and-validation engineers who understand both the process envelope and the automation. Formal change control and revalidation carry real overhead but are budgeted as normal GMP practice.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a tank opening remains unsealed before pressure is introduced
rows.plain: Make sure every tank opening is properly closed before pressure is added, so material or pressure cannot escape unexpectedly.
rows.imp_rat: An unsealed opening before pressurization directly undermines containment integrity needed for the job (relevance 7); pressurizing an open tank causes ejection of material or blowout injuries (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Open-port detection is only partially automated on typical tanks, so an unsealed opening before pressurization remains a recurring human-error gap. Verifying every opening is sealed adds pre-pressurization checklist time that is often manual and non-trivial. Confirming all ports, manways and drains are closed relies heavily on operator attention and procedural discipline rather than automated confirmation. Closure verification uses cheap position switches or visual checks, but a missed opening causes expensive spills or releases.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of the finished batch outside composition-uniformity limits
rows.plain: Make sure the ingredients are spread evenly throughout the finished batch. This helps ensure that each portion has the intended mixture.
rows.imp_rat: Composition-uniformity failure in the finished batch is central to product acceptability (relevance 8); non-uniform batches are typically rejected or reprocessed, a significant but bounded loss (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Validated mixing protocols and end-point checks handle most batches, but dead zones and viscosity-driven poor mixing in high-viscosity product produce recurring uniformity failures. Achieving uniformity in viscous batches requires extended mixing times, and rushing to meet schedule commonly leaves gradients unresolved. Judging when a high-viscosity batch is truly homogeneous still depends on operator experience and validated recipes rather than direct measurement. Non-uniform batches drive rework, blending or disposal costs, and over-mixing wastes energy and time as a hedge.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of technician injury from hazardous plant conditions during fault repair
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that a technician is hurt by dangerous conditions in the facility while fixing a problem.
rows.imp_rat: Technician injury from hazardous plant conditions during fault repair is a maintenance-phase concern central to operating safely; the injuries can be disabling or fatal, keeping severity high while relevance is somewhat narrower.
rows.sat_rat: Permit-to-work and JSA practices reduce injury frequency but fault conditions are inherently unpredictable and less controlled than planned work, leaving recurring gaps. Fault repairs are often urgent and time pressure works against careful hazard assessment before entering the work area. Diagnosing and safely repairing an unexpected fault depends heavily on the technician's experience reading unfamiliar failure conditions. PPE and permit-to-work systems are inexpensive relative to the value of avoiding injury during repair.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of uncontrolled movement or collapse of heavy plant assemblies during dismantling
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that heavy parts move unexpectedly or fall while the equipment is being taken apart. This helps protect people and nearby equipment.
rows.imp_rat: Uncontrolled movement of heavy assemblies during dismantling is a maintenance-phase concern rather than core production (relevance 6), but a collapse can kill or maim workers (severity 10).
rows.sat_rat: Method statements and lift plans control most cases, but unexpected residual loads, corroded fixings, and shifting centers of gravity still cause uncontrolled movement more often than for pure energy isolation. Rigging studies and staged dismantling sequencing add meaningful time but are standard in planned demolition. Safe dismantling of heavy, top-loaded plant assemblies depends heavily on experienced riggers and structural judgement that is not uniformly available. Cranes, temporary supports, and specialist rigging crews are costly line items in decommissioning budgets.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of microbial or particulate contamination entering the system during storage
rows.plain: Keep germs and particles from getting into the system while it is stored. Proper storage helps keep the system clean and ready for use.
rows.imp_rat: Microbial or particulate contamination entering during storage is important to batch integrity, and the consequence is a contaminated batch that can harm patients or trigger recalls.
rows.sat_rat: Standard closures and filtered venting help, but microbial and particulate ingress during storage is inconsistently controlled and remains a recurring gap. Sealing and monitoring stored systems adds minor upfront effort but little ongoing time burden. Effective microbial and particulate exclusion during storage depends on correct barrier setup, a moderately skill-dependent step. Filters, seals, and inert purges are low-cost measures.

rows.source_job: Assemble shipped-loose components onto the skid
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of contamination entering exposed product-contact openings during skid assembly
rows.plain: Keep dirt and germs out of openings that will touch the product while parts are being attached. This helps protect the product during later operation.
rows.imp_rat: Contamination entering exposed openings during assembly is a setup-phase risk that is important for establishing a clean starting state; an ingress here compromises the whole subsequent batch, giving high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Grade-A protection and aseptic connectors reduce ingress substantially, but open-connection moments during assembly remain a recurring source of undetected contamination. Assembling under a cleanroom or LAF hood with grade-A protection follows established timing but rushing connections during exposed windows is where contamination slips in. Correct aseptic technique at open connections is highly operator-dependent and remains one of the hardest manual skills to sustain consistently. Cleanroom gowning, environmental monitoring, and consumables add ongoing cost, and a contamination event forces expensive batch loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of selecting an unapproved or obsolete recipe version for the next batch
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that someone chooses a recipe that has not been approved or is no longer current for the next batch. This helps ensure the batch is made using the correct instructions.
rows.imp_rat: Selecting an unapproved or obsolete recipe version is central to producing the correct batch, and using the wrong recipe severely compromises product identity, compliance, and safety.
rows.sat_rat: Electronic recipe version control with approval workflows and obsolete-version lockout is mature and reliable, so selecting an obsolete recipe rarely occurs. Selecting the current recipe from a managed system is immediate and non-blocking. Version control removes the need for the operator to know which revision is approved. Recipe-management software is a fixed overhead that prevents costly wrong-version batches.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of pressure exceeding allowable limits while routing a liquid stream
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that pressure becomes dangerously high while a liquid is being sent through the system. This helps protect the equipment and the people nearby.
rows.imp_rat: Pressure exceeding allowable limits while routing liquid is central to safe process operation (relevance high); overpressure can cause rupture, release, and injury (severity very high).
rows.sat_rat: Mature, code-enforced overpressure protection makes uncontrolled pressure exceedance rare, which is why this is among the best-served needs. Relief valves and rupture disks actuate essentially instantly when pressure exceeds limits. Sizing and setting relief devices requires engineering expertise, but day-to-day operation needs little operator skill. Relief systems are mandatory code-driven investments already built into every pressure system.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of component precipitation caused by an incompatible addition order in the compounding vessel
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that an ingredient forms unwanted solid particles because ingredients were added in the wrong order. The correct order helps keep the mixture usable and consistent.
rows.imp_rat: Precipitation from incompatible addition order directly destroys homogeneity and dissolution; severe because it can ruin the whole batch.
rows.sat_rat: Validated addition sequences prevent precipitation reliably when followed, so this failure is uncommon in mature processes. Precipitation from bad addition order forces investigation and reprocessing that consume batch time. Preventing incompatibility precipitation relies heavily on formulator knowledge of addition sequence. A precipitation event typically scraps the batch, a major material loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of batch setpoints configured outside approved ranges
rows.plain: Reduce the number of batch settings entered outside the allowed ranges. This helps the batch run under the conditions that were approved for it.
rows.imp_rat: Setpoints configured outside approved ranges are central to running a compliant batch, and the consequence is an out-of-spec cycle producing defective product and deviation reports.
rows.sat_rat: Configured range limits in modern batch systems reliably block most out-of-range values, making this fairly well served versus critical-only checks. Range-checking setpoints is quick where automated bounds exist but slow where done manually. Automated range enforcement reduces the skill needed, though defining correct ranges still needs process knowledge. Software range limits are cheap to configure once approved ranges are established.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of introducing microbial or particulate contamination into the ingredient feed lines and supply tanks during priming and charging
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that germs or unwanted particles enter the ingredient pipes and supply tanks while they are being filled and prepared for use. This helps keep the ingredients clean.
rows.imp_rat: Contaminating feed lines and supply tanks during priming/charging propagates into every batch drawn from them (relevance 8), making the consequence widespread contamination and scrap (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Closed charging systems and sterile filtration handle most cases reliably, but open-manway ingredient additions remain a recurring contamination gap. Sterile charging of feed lines and supply tanks adds handling time at each ingredient introduction. Operators must correctly execute aseptic tank charging and line priming, so outcomes remain dependent on careful technique. Sanitary fittings, filters and disposable transfer sets add material cost that plants already absorb.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of persistent low-circulation regions within the compounding vessel
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that parts of the mixing container have very little movement. Good movement throughout the container helps keep the mixture evenly blended.
rows.imp_rat: Persistent low-circulation dead zones cause localized non-uniformity, central to blend job; severe as they yield undetected out-of-spec regions.
rows.sat_rat: Baffles and validated impeller placement reduce dead zones, but tank geometry still leaves occasional stubborn low-flow regions. Low-circulation zones prolong dissolution and homogenization, extending mix time. Recognizing and mitigating dead zones requires mixing-engineering expertise not always on hand. Persistent dead zones cause off-spec batches and wasted material.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that homogeneity samples omit concentration extremes within the vessel
rows.plain: Take samples from enough different places in the vessel to include areas with both lower and higher concentrations. This helps show whether the contents are mixed evenly.
rows.imp_rat: Samples that omit concentration extremes can miss non-uniformity, letting defective batches pass; relevant with high severity as an undetected escape.
rows.sat_rat: Validated sampling plans target representative and worst-case locations, but manual sampling can still systematically miss true extremes. Following a defined sampling plan takes fixed time and does not add much delay. Ensuring samples capture concentration extremes depends heavily on the sampling-plan design skill and disciplined execution. Missing extremes leads to undetected defects with high downstream cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of contamination being introduced into product-contact areas during service
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that dirt, germs, or other unwanted material gets into areas that touch the product during servicing. This helps protect the product from contamination.
rows.imp_rat: Contamination introduced during service is relevant to maintaining product-contact integrity (moderate-high relevance) and can contaminate subsequent batches with serious consequences (high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Human intervention is the single largest ingress risk and even with SOPs and EM, contamination introduced during service remains a recurring, hard-to-eliminate deviation source. Aseptic re-assembly and post-service sanitization extend service windows considerably as line clearance and re-sterilization are required. Contamination control during service leans heavily on the discipline and gowning technique of maintenance staff, a persistent human-factor vulnerability. Gowning, environmental monitoring, and re-validation after intervention add meaningful cost each event.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of losing sterile conditions in product-contact paths during shutdown
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that product-contact paths stop being clean and germ-free when production is shut down. This helps keep the equipment ready for safe use later.
rows.imp_rat: Losing sterile conditions in product-contact paths during shutdown risks the sterility that must be re-established on restart (moderately high relevance), potentially requiring costly re-sterilization or scrapping product (high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Automated shutdown sequences with sterile holds help, but abnormal or emergency shutdowns frequently risk loss of product-contact sterility. Controlled shutdown sequences take time to preserve sterile conditions in product-contact paths. Maintaining sterility through shutdown depends on operators executing sequenced isolation correctly. SIP-hold and blanket-gas provisions add utility cost but are standard.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percent of the intended fill-volume, viscosity, thermal, and batch-size envelope absent from capability evaluation
rows.plain: Test the process across as much of the planned range of fill amount, thickness, temperature, and batch size as possible. This shows whether the process can work reliably under different expected conditions.
rows.imp_rat: Envelope gaps in capability evaluation risk deploying an unfit vessel; relevant upstream with high severity across all batches.
rows.sat_rat: Evaluations typically cover nominal and a few extremes, leaving portions of the operating envelope untested and prone to failure. Mapping the full envelope of fill, viscosity, thermal and batch-size cases is time-consuming and often truncated. Defining the worst-case corners of a multi-variable envelope takes experienced process judgement. Unevaluated envelope regions become costly capability gaps discovered in production.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of ambiguity in formulation homogeneity endpoints and acceptance criteria
rows.plain: Make the expected point for an evenly mixed product and the rules for accepting it clearer. Clear requirements help people know when mixing is complete and acceptable.
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguous homogeneity endpoints and acceptance criteria undermine consistent release decisions; relevant with high severity for compliance.
rows.sat_rat: Homogeneity endpoints and acceptance criteria are frequently vague at specification time, a recurring source of error. Clarifying homogeneity criteria upfront saves later rework time but is often skipped. Defining unambiguous acceptance criteria requires cross-functional formulation and QA expertise. Ambiguous criteria cause costly disputes and re-testing.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of connected electrical, pneumatic, hydraulic, or thermal energy sources remaining active after isolation
rows.plain: Make sure all connected sources of electrical, air, water-powered, or heat energy are turned off after isolation. This helps prevent unexpected movement, release of energy, or injury.
rows.imp_rat: Residual live energy sources after isolation directly defeats safe maintenance/dismantling of the plant (high relevance); consequences include electrocution, burns, or crush injuries during service (severity 9).
rows.sat_rat: Mature LOTO standards, permit-to-work systems, and zero-energy verification make missed live sources uncommon where the practice is enforced, though stored hydraulic/pneumatic energy is occasionally overlooked. Lockout-tagout and energy isolation procedures are routine and add only modest time to the shutdown sequence. Certified electricians and competent persons following established LOTO protocols make this a well-drilled competency in most industrial decommissioning teams. Isolation hardware and verification labor are inexpensive relative to the consequences avoided.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of deviation between the specified and actual ingredient quantity added at a programmed step
rows.plain: Make the amount of ingredient added at each planned step match the required amount as closely as possible. This helps ensure the final product has the intended recipe.
rows.imp_rat: Deviation between specified and actual quantity at a step is the essence of accurate batching (relevance 9); moderate deviations may be caught or corrected, but larger ones spoil the batch (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Load-cell and Coriolis-based step dosing is a mature practice holding tight tolerances, but drift, in-flight material, and viscous residuals produce recurring small deviations. Automated dosing at each step runs to setpoint without operator waiting beyond normal fill time. Closed-loop dosing removes most manual finesse, though calibrating flow and weigh instruments still needs trained technicians. Metering and load-cell hardware plus recalibration add cost, offset by tighter yield and less rework.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of the wetted process circuit retaining product or microbial residue after cycle completion
rows.plain: Leave as little product or germ residue as possible inside the product-carrying pipes and parts after cleaning. This helps keep the next batch clean.
rows.imp_rat: Wetted circuit retaining product or microbial residue after cycle completion is central to cleaning effectiveness (relevance 8), and residual microbial load can spoil or contaminate subsequent product (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Validated CIP coverage is generally reliable, but dead legs and low-flow zones can retain residue, making full-circuit assurance imperfect. Achieving full circuit coverage requires adequate cycle and drain time that is built into validated recipes. Ensuring full wetted-circuit contact depends on hygienic design and CIP setup expertise more than routine operator skill. Complete circuit flushing consumes utilities and chemicals at a predictable recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which live flow, temperature, pressure, and level readings differ from actual process conditions
rows.plain: Make the live readings for flow, temperature, pressure, and liquid level match the actual conditions as closely as possible. Reliable readings help workers control the process safely.
rows.imp_rat: Live readings diverging from actual flow, temperature, pressure, and level conditions is highly central to controlling the process (relevance); false readings mislead operators and automation into unsafe actions with severe consequences (high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Well-calibrated flow, temperature, pressure and level instruments track actual conditions closely, with drift caught by periodic calibration, making this well served. Modern instruments update readings in near real time, so temporal lag is rarely the issue. Interpreting whether a reading is drifting requires trained instrument technicians for calibration and diagnostics. Calibration programs and instrument maintenance are routine budgeted costs that keep divergence low.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a critical vessel KPI exceeds its approved threshold without detection before bulk-solution transfer
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that an important vessel measurement goes above its allowed limit without anyone noticing before the solution is moved out. Early warning helps prevent an unsuitable batch from being transferred.
rows.imp_rat: An undetected KPI breach before transfer allows a defective batch to proceed; relevant to control with high safety severity.
rows.sat_rat: Alarms and pre-transfer checks catch most excursions, but manual or gapped monitoring leaves detection windows. KPI review before transfer is often periodic, so a threshold breach may persist briefly before it is caught. Interpreting whether a KPI truly exceeds an approved limit requires trained oversight, especially with manual logging. An undetected excursion transferred downstream risks costly batch loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of contamination entering sterile product-contact areas during the component upgrade
rows.plain: Prevent harmful contamination from entering areas that touch the sterile product while equipment parts are being replaced or improved. This helps keep the product safe and clean.
rows.imp_rat: Contamination entering sterile product-contact areas during a component upgrade directly threatens sterility during a planned improvement activity (moderately high relevance), and a breach can spoil subsequent product (high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Change-control and aseptic install practices mitigate risk, but any physical intrusion for an upgrade still leaves a real contamination-ingress chance. Component upgrades under sterile conditions require lengthy controlled entry and re-qualification. Installing new components without contaminating product-contact surfaces demands both mechanical and aseptic expertise. Upgrades carry material cost plus contamination-control and revalidation overhead.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that specified wetted materials are incompatible with the formulation or sanitation requirements
rows.plain: Make sure the materials that touch the product work safely with the product and can meet cleaning and sanitation needs. This helps prevent damage, contamination, or ineffective cleaning.
rows.imp_rat: Specifying wetted materials incompatible with the formulation or sanitation undermines both product quality and cleanability (high relevance) and is severe—systemic contamination or corrosion across many batches (high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Standard material spec sheets and sanitation compatibility guidance handle typical cases, yet gaps between formulation and cleaning-agent exposure are a known recurring failure. Cross-checking wetted materials against formulation and sanitation regimes lengthens specification review modestly. Requires combined knowledge of formulation chemistry and CIP/SIP sanitation effects, a rare skill combination. Wrong material selection drives rework and requalification costs, though the check itself is low-cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that evaluation sampling misses localized composition gradients within the vessel
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that samples miss areas where the solution is unevenly mixed. This helps confirm that the whole vessel has the expected composition.
rows.imp_rat: Evaluation sampling missing localized gradients can validate an inhomogeneous process; relevant with high severity as a systemic escape.
rows.sat_rat: Sampling plans routinely miss localized composition pockets, a persistent detection gap. Comprehensive gradient sampling adds substantial test time often shortcut. Designing sampling that catches localized gradients requires specialized statistical and mixing knowledge. Missed gradients cause escaped nonconformances and recalls.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that material properties used to specify mixing capability are inaccurate across expected operating conditions
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that incorrect information about the solution is used when deciding how well it must be mixed. This helps ensure the mixing equipment is suitable under all expected conditions.
rows.imp_rat: Inaccurate material properties used to specify capability can cause systemic mis-sizing; relevant upstream with high severity if unfit vessel is chosen.
rows.sat_rat: Property data is often taken at nominal conditions and viscosity/density shifts with temperature and concentration routinely undermine specification accuracy. Verifying material property data across conditions delays finalizing the spec. Judging whether literature or single-point property data extrapolates correctly requires specialized material knowledge. Inaccurate properties lead to mis-sized equipment and expensive corrections.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that anticipated batch scales, solution properties, or operating extremes fall outside qualification coverage
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that testing does not cover the expected batch sizes, solution types, or unusual operating conditions. This helps show that the process will work safely in the situations where it is expected to run.
rows.imp_rat: Operating extremes falling outside qualification coverage leaves unvalidated conditions in use; relevant upstream with high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Qualification coverage often reflects current known conditions, so future scales or extremes routinely fall outside it. Anticipating full operating ranges early is time-consuming when future batch scales are uncertain. Forecasting operating extremes and solution properties demands deep process knowledge that few possess. Under-scoped coverage forces costly re-qualification when ranges are exceeded.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of charged active and excipient quantities deviating from batch targets
rows.plain: The amounts of active ingredients and other added materials should stay closer to the amounts planned for each batch. This helps keep the finished product consistent.
rows.imp_rat: Charged quantities deviating from targets directly corrupt formulation composition; central to correct concentration with high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Weigh-and-verify procedures and dispensing controls make charging deviations uncommon in mature operations. Verifying charged quantities against targets adds weigh-check steps. Accurate charging depends on operator diligence and dispensing skill. Charging errors waste material and can scrap batches.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of hazardous motion, pressure, or energy after emergency shutdown activation
rows.plain: After an emergency stop, quickly end any remaining dangerous movement, pressure, or stored power.
rows.imp_rat: Duration of hazardous motion/pressure/energy after e-stop is important to the protective function tied to the job (moderate-high relevance); lingering energy after shutdown can cause severe injury (high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Stored mechanical, pneumatic, and thermal energy predictably dissipates but not instantly, leaving a residual hazard window that current practice only partly closes. Residual motion, pressure, or energy still bleeds down over a finite interval after shutdown despite fast trip circuits. Shutdown is automatic once activated, so no operator skill governs the decay of stored energy. Dump valves, brakes, and bleed systems that shorten decay add real cost and are not always fully specified.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of hazardous pressure remaining in process equipment before dismantling
rows.plain: Make sure dangerous pressure has been released before taking the equipment apart, so no one is injured.
rows.imp_rat: Hazardous pressure remaining before dismantling is important to safe maintenance but is a specific isolation step rather than the whole job; when it occurs it can cause explosive release and severe injury, so severity remains very high.
rows.sat_rat: Standard depressurize-and-verify practice with double-block-and-bleed is highly effective, though blocked vents or hidden dead-legs occasionally retain pressure. Depressurization and venting steps are routine and add modest, well-understood time to teardown. Verifying zero-energy state before breaking joints requires trained technicians reading gauges and confirming isolation, a well-drilled competency. Pressure-relief and depressurization procedures use standard low-cost fittings and vents.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of deviation between the ingredient quantity loaded and the batch target
rows.plain: Load an ingredient amount that is as close as possible to the amount required for the batch. This helps keep the finished product consistent.
rows.imp_rat: Deviation between loaded quantity and batch target directly determines batch correctness (relevance 9); consequences range from rework to scrap depending on magnitude (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Total-batch weigh control is standard and generally accurate, though cumulative dosing error, tank tare drift, and clingage keep small target deviations occurring. Gravimetric batch totalizing reaches target automatically without adding wait time. The system tracks cumulative mass so operators need little skill to hit the batch target. Weigh-vessel instrumentation is capital-intensive but reduces giveaway and out-of-spec loss.

rows.stk: Environmental health and safety personnel, waste management contractors.
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of incompatible retained chemical residues being combined during segregation and packaging
rows.plain: Keep leftover chemicals that could react badly from being mixed while they are separated and packed for disposal.
rows.imp_rat: Combining incompatible retained chemical residues during segregation and packaging is a waste-handling concern peripheral to batch preparation, but severity is catastrophic due to potential for violent reactions or toxic release.
rows.sat_rat: Chemical-compatibility segregation is well-established practice, so incompatible mixing during packaging is relatively uncommon. Segregating and packaging by compatibility adds sorting time that is planned into waste handling. Preventing dangerous combinations relies on trained personnel reading SDS and applying compatibility knowledge. Separate containers and segregation add modest cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of compromising sterile processing conditions while servicing plant equipment
rows.plain: Avoid letting servicing work make the clean conditions unsafe for sterile production.
rows.imp_rat: Compromising sterile conditions while servicing equipment is important to maintaining the sterile job across maintenance cycles (moderately high relevance), and a lapse can contaminate product-contact surfaces and spoil production (high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Aseptic maintenance protocols exist but any intervention breaches the sterile envelope, so compromise risk persists despite best practice. Maintaining sterility during service forces slow gowning, controlled entry and re-sanitization steps that extend the job. Aseptic servicing technique is a specialized skill that many general maintenance technicians lack. Sterile consumables, barriers and re-validation drive meaningful recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of incompatible hazardous residues being combined during segregation or temporary storage
rows.plain: Keep dangerous leftover materials that could react badly from being mixed while they are separated or stored temporarily.
rows.imp_rat: Combining incompatible hazardous residues during segregation is somewhat peripheral to routine processing (relevance moderate); however incompatible mixing can cause violent reactions, fire, or toxic release (severity very high).
rows.sat_rat: Compatibility matrices and segregation rules are well established, but mislabeling and mixed legacy residues still cause occasional dangerous combinations. Segregated storage and compatibility checks add handling and labeling time. Correct compatibility segregation depends on chemical knowledge and diligent labeling by trained staff. Segregated containers and compliant temporary storage add cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unresolved conflicts between mixing requirements and sterile-boundary or contamination-control constraints
rows.plain: Fewer unresolved disagreements should remain between mixing needs and the rules for keeping products and clean areas protected from contamination. Resolving these issues helps the mixing process work without compromising cleanliness.
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved conflicts with sterile-boundary/contamination constraints can undermine sterility; moderate mixing relevance but high contamination severity.
rows.sat_rat: Conflicts between mixing requirements and sterile-boundary constraints are commonly unresolved until late. Reconciling mixing needs with sterile/contamination constraints adds coordination time. Resolving these conflicts needs combined sterile-process and mixing expertise. Unresolved conflicts drive costly redesign or contamination events.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of cleaned product-contact surfaces with product residue or deposits remaining
rows.plain: Leave as little product buildup as possible on surfaces that touch the product after cleaning. This helps prevent old material from affecting the next batch.
rows.imp_rat: Residue remaining on cleaned product-contact surfaces is a direct failure of the cleaning job core to hygienic liquid processing (relevance 8), risking cross-contamination and microbial growth (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Validated CIP is a mature, widely deployed practice that reliably removes residue on well-designed circuits, with residual deposits mainly in poorly-designed areas. Validated CIP cycles run on established durations that are already optimized into the schedule. Automated CIP removes most manual technique dependence for residue removal. CIP chemicals, heat and water are recurring costs but efficiently dosed in mature systems.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of personnel exposure to hazardous pressure, liquid, or equipment movement during shutdown
rows.plain: Prevent people from being exposed to dangerous pressure, liquids, or moving equipment while the machine is being stopped in an emergency. This helps protect them from serious injury.
rows.imp_rat: Personnel exposure to hazardous pressure/liquid/movement during shutdown is important to safe plant operation (relevance moderate-high); the consequence is potential serious injury or death (severity very high).
rows.sat_rat: Mature LOTO, pressure-relief and de-energization practice makes shutdown personnel exposure uncommon, though residual stored-energy incidents persist. Lockout/tagout and depressurization add deliberate time before personnel approach. LOTO and stored-energy procedures reduce reliance on individual judgement about when equipment is safe. PPE and isolation hardware are modest standard costs.

rows.stk: Environmental health and safety personnel, waste management contractors.
rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of hazardous or regulated chemical residue left in the batching system after disposal activities
rows.plain: Leave as little dangerous or legally controlled chemical residue as possible in the mixing system after it is emptied for disposal. This helps protect people and the environment.
rows.imp_rat: Hazardous or regulated chemical residue left after disposal activities is a downstream/end-of-life concern less central to producing a batch, but consequences are severe: regulatory violation and environmental or personnel harm.
rows.sat_rat: Residue reduction is achievable but inconsistently reaches low regulatory levels in aged batching systems. Reducing regulated residue often means added flush cycles that lengthen the job. Driving residue down to regulatory thresholds needs knowledgeable selection of cleaning chemistry and methods. Every increment of residue removed adds solvent, labor, and hazardous-disposal cost, and leftover residue drives up regulated waste tonnage.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of heat-transfer fluid entering the batch through a compromised jacket boundary during mixing
rows.plain: Prevent the liquid used to control temperature from leaking through a damaged outer wall into the product during mixing. This protects the product from being contaminated.
rows.imp_rat: Heat-transfer fluid entering the batch through a compromised jacket is direct product contamination/adulteration; moderate mixing relevance but catastrophic severity.
rows.sat_rat: Jacket integrity testing and pressure monitoring make breaches rare, though not impossible. A jacket breach forces immediate stop and investigation, a major time loss. Detecting fluid ingress requires monitoring and maintenance expertise. Heat-transfer fluid contamination scraps the batch, a severe loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of residual liquid backflow into product-contact areas during routing
rows.plain: Prevent leftover liquid from flowing backward into areas that touch the product. This helps keep those areas clean and prevents unwanted liquid from entering the product.
rows.imp_rat: Liquid backflow into product-contact areas during routing is a recognized contamination pathway central to hygienic design (relevance 8), and can introduce cross-contamination into finished product (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Check valves, backflow preventers and hygienic design make backflow into product-contact areas uncommon, though failed valves occasionally allow it. Check valves and air breaks act passively and instantly to prevent backflow. Backflow prevention is engineered into piping design, requiring minimal operator attention. Backflow devices are inexpensive standard hardware relative to contamination cost avoided.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of introducing microbial or particulate contamination into product-contact circuits during priming
rows.plain: Prevent germs and small particles from getting into the parts that carry the product while they are being filled with liquid. This helps keep the product clean and safe.
rows.imp_rat: Introducing microbial/particulate contamination into product-contact circuits during priming is central to a preparation system whose output must be safe (relevance 8), and contaminated product can cause serious harm and batch loss (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Mature CIP/SIP and closed-transfer practice makes contamination during priming uncommon in well-run plants, though occasional breaches still occur. Priming with sterile-grade filtration and aseptic connections adds setup time but is a routine part of established start-up sequences. Aseptic priming demands trained operators and adherence to SOPs, so residual contamination events still trace back to skill and vigilance lapses. Single-use aseptic connectors and sterile filters raise consumable spend but are standard budgeted items in regulated facilities.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that updated control logic produces out-of-spec batching conditions
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that changed control software causes the production mixture to fall outside its required limits. This helps keep the finished product consistent and acceptable.
rows.imp_rat: Updated control logic producing out-of-spec batching conditions directly governs whether batches meet specification, central to the CFJ; a bad logic change can silently ruin many batches before detection, a high-severity outcome.
rows.sat_rat: Formal change-control and factory-acceptance/pre-release testing catch most out-of-spec logic, yet edge-case interactions still slip through into production periodically. Regression testing and simulated batch runs before release consume days but are budgeted into planned maintenance windows. Verifying control logic against spec demands scarce controls engineers who understand both PLC code and process chemistry, a persistent bottleneck. Test batches, wasted materials, and engineering time make validation runs expensive but broadly accepted as necessary.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of selecting the wrong batch phase, parameter, or target value when adjusting an active process setpoint
rows.plain: Avoid choosing the wrong step, setting, or desired value when changing an active process. This helps prevent the process from running incorrectly.
rows.imp_rat: Selecting the wrong batch phase, parameter, or target when adjusting a setpoint is central to correct process control; an operator error here can ruin a batch or breach limits, making severity high though it is preventable operator ambiguity.
rows.sat_rat: Structured recipe phases and confirmation prompts reduce mis-selection, yet ambiguous HMI layouts leave a real residual error rate. HMI navigation to the correct phase and parameter is quick once operators are familiar with the interface. Selecting the right setpoint among many phases still relies on operator attention, and cluttered HMIs invite the wrong pick under pressure. A wrong setpoint selection can spoil a batch, but such errors are individually infrequent so aggregate cost is moderate.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of introducing contamination into the sterile process stream during sampling
rows.plain: Prevent germs and other unwanted material from getting into the product while a sample is being taken. This helps ensure the sample and the product remain trustworthy and safe.
rows.imp_rat: Introducing contamination into the sterile stream during sampling is a specific procedural error but can invalidate an entire sterile batch; the quality and safety consequences make severity high while relevance is narrower than whole-path contamination.
rows.sat_rat: Closed and steam-lock sampling systems reduce risk substantially, yet manual sampling ports remain a recurring contamination-entry point. Aseptic sampling adds deliberate slow steps but is not primarily a time problem. Contamination-free sampling still leans heavily on technician aseptic technique, a well-known error source even with closed samplers. Closed/single-use sampling devices add per-sample cost but are increasingly standard.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of liquid feedstock released from the processing path during operation
rows.plain: Reduce the amount of liquid material that escapes from the process while the equipment is running. This helps prevent waste, spills, and possible exposure.
rows.imp_rat: Feedstock released from the processing path during operation is highly relevant to maintaining process integrity and containment (relevance); active leaks during operation risk fire, exposure, and continuous loss (high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Sealed piping, welded joints and routine integrity checks make major releases uncommon, but small chronic leaks from the processing path are a persistent reality. Leak detection and flow-balance monitoring flag releases reasonably fast, but slow seepage from seals and gaskets can persist unnoticed for hours. Preventing releases leans on skilled maintenance of seals, flanges and pumps, and inconsistent workmanship remains a common source of leaks. Fugitive feedstock losses through minor leaks are a chronic, hard-to-eliminate expense despite preventive maintenance programs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that specified agitation conditions damage shear-sensitive formulation components
rows.plain: Make sure the required mixing does not harm ingredients that can be damaged by strong movement. This protects the quality of the finished mixture.
rows.imp_rat: Agitation damaging shear-sensitive components degrades product quality; relevant with high severity for such formulations.
rows.sat_rat: Shear-sensitivity limits are often poorly characterized at spec time, so damaging conditions can be specified unknowingly. Determining safe shear windows for sensitive components adds study time. Predicting shear damage thresholds requires specialized formulation-sensitivity expertise. Shear damage degrades expensive actives, driving batch loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of hazardous residue remaining inside the tank after decontamination
rows.plain: Reduce the amount of dangerous residue left inside the tank after cleaning. This helps make the tank safer for the next use.
rows.imp_rat: Hazardous residue after decontamination affects batch purity and worker safety, important to the processing job (relevance 7); consequences include contamination and exposure but are often bounded (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Verification is often visual or sampled rather than exhaustive, so residue trapped in baffles, welds and dead legs frequently remains understated. Repeated wash-and-swab cycles to hit a clean endpoint can stretch decontamination over days. Judging when residue is acceptably removed relies heavily on experienced personnel and interpretation of swab results. Solvents, water, disposal of rinsate and labour make thorough decontamination expensive.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of product-contact components omitted from cleaning
rows.plain: Clean every surface and part that touches the product, without leaving any out. This helps prevent leftover material or contamination from affecting the next product.
rows.imp_rat: Product-contact components omitted from cleaning leave uncleaned surfaces central to the hygiene job (relevance 8), acting as contamination reservoirs affecting product safety (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Validated cleaning scope and change-part tracking make omission of product-contact components uncommon, with occasional gaps only for manually-cleaned or newly-added parts. Cleaning scope is defined once in validated procedures, so no per-cycle time penalty for coverage. Automated CIP loops and documented cleaning boundaries reduce reliance on operator memory to include all components. Ensuring all components are cleaned adds little marginal cost over the base cycle.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of component degradation caused by candidate agitation across specified operating conditions
rows.plain: Limit damage to parts caused by the movement used by the proposed equipment under the required operating conditions. This helps show whether the equipment can handle the parts safely.
rows.imp_rat: Component degradation from candidate agitation across conditions affects vessel fitness and quality; relevant with high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Degradation across the full range of agitation conditions is inconsistently characterized, leaving residual risk. Characterizing degradation across the operating range extends testing. Assessing cumulative shear degradation demands specialized analytical judgement. Degradation wastes active material and batches.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of shear-sensitive component degradation caused by agitation during compounding
rows.plain: Reduce damage to components that can be harmed by strong mixing during preparation. This helps the process work properly and protects product quality.
rows.imp_rat: Shear-sensitive component degradation from agitation reduces potency/quality; relevant with high severity for affected products.
rows.sat_rat: Actual degradation from agitation is hard to predict and only partially controlled by conservative specifications. Confirming low-shear performance requires added agitation trials. Interpreting degradation-versus-shear behavior needs scarce formulation knowledge. Degraded shear-sensitive material is costly product loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that contamination enters the process through the sampling interface during withdrawal
rows.plain: Taking a sample should not allow dirt or germs to enter the process through the sampling opening. This helps keep the material being made clean.
rows.imp_rat: Contamination entering through the sampling interface directly threatens both the sample and the bulk process, moderately central since sampling is a recurring routine operation; a breach can contaminate the live batch, making consequences severe.
rows.sat_rat: Closed sampling systems and steamable sample valves help, but the sampling interface remains one of the more frequent points where adventitious contamination slips in. Aseptic sampling adds small increments of time per sample that are manageable within batch schedules. Manual sampling through septa or valves is highly technique-dependent, so contamination risk tracks closely with operator skill and vigilance. Sampling assemblies and any invalidated results from a contaminated sample cost material and analytical rework.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that evidence used for batch disposition is inaccurate or unverifiable
rows.plain: Make sure the information used to decide whether a batch can be released is correct and can be checked. Unreliable information could lead to releasing a batch that should not be used.
rows.imp_rat: Inaccurate or unverifiable disposition evidence can release a defective batch; low mixing relevance but severe compliance/safety consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Audit trails and review-by-second-person practices help, but data-integrity lapses remain a leading cause of regulatory findings, so evidence trustworthiness is only partially assured. Verifying evidence integrity adds review time but is not primarily a delay problem. Data-integrity review demands trained reviewers who can spot unverifiable or altered records. Audit-trail systems and review labor add cost but are standard GMP spend.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the compounding cycle exceeds the allowable sterile hold window
rows.plain: Make sure the mixing process finishes before the allowed time for safely holding the sterile material runs out. Going beyond that time could make the material unsuitable for use.
rows.imp_rat: Exceeding the allowable sterile hold window compromises sterility of the whole batch; moderate mixing relevance but severe consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Hold-window compliance is manageable when cycles are well characterized but risky when cycle times vary unexpectedly. Comparing cycle time against sterile hold window is a defined scheduling check. Estimating total cycle against hold limits needs process planning skill. Exceeding hold windows forces batch discard, a major cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of introducing the wrong material or lot into the tank
rows.plain: Put only the intended material and batch into the tank. This prevents the wrong material from entering the process and affecting the product.
rows.imp_rat: Introducing the wrong material or lot corrupts the batch the tank exists to process (relevance 7); it causes off-spec product, possible dangerous reactions, and rework (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Barcode/MES-enforced material verification is available and strong where deployed, but many operations still rely on manual checks, leaving a real error rate. Barcode/label verification adds only seconds when used, but manual lot checks slow charging. Correct material/lot identification often depends on operator reading labels and following batch records, a vigilance-heavy task. Scanning systems are inexpensive, but a wrong-material charge can scrap an entire batch at high cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of sterile-boundary breaches caused by overlapping operator activity
rows.plain: Two or more operators working at the same time should not cause a break in the clean boundary around the process. This helps keep the product protected from contamination.
rows.imp_rat: Sterile-boundary breaches from overlapping operator activity are a procedural contributor to contamination rather than a core process step; when they occur they can breach sterility and spoil batches, a severe consequence.
rows.sat_rat: SOPs and interlocks help, but sterile-boundary breaches from concurrent human activity remain a persistent, behavior-driven failure mode. Coordinating operator activity to avoid sterile-boundary overlap can slow work through sequencing and waiting. Avoiding overlapping intrusions depends heavily on trained aseptic technique and disciplined behavior. A single boundary breach can scrap a costly batch, making occasional breaches expensive.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of process instability during abrupt changes in feedstock viscosity, composition, or temperature
rows.plain: The process should continue working steadily when the incoming material suddenly changes in thickness, makeup, or temperature. This helps prevent interruptions and poor results.
rows.imp_rat: Process instability during abrupt feedstock changes directly threatens producing on-spec liquid product (relevance 8), and consequences are typically off-spec batches or transient upsets that are usually recoverable (severity 6).
rows.sat_rat: Standard PID control handles gradual changes but struggles with abrupt viscosity or composition swings, so process instability remains a recurring problem. Abrupt feed changes can outpace control loop response, causing instability before correction takes hold. Stabilizing the process during sharp transients relies heavily on experienced operator intervention and judgement. Instability wastes energy and off-spec material until steady state is restored.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that equipment retains product or hazardous material at handover
rows.plain: Make sure no product or dangerous material is left inside the equipment when it is handed over or sold. This protects the people who receive or work on it.
rows.imp_rat: Equipment retaining product or hazardous material at handover affects safe transition between operations (relevance moderate-high); retained hazardous residue endangers workers and cross-contaminates next runs (severity high).
rows.sat_rat: Handover clearance procedures with sign-off are established, yet retained material in dead-legs and complex geometries occasionally slips through. Verification cleaning and swabbing before handover extends the closeout timeline. Confirming equipment is residue-free at handover needs trained inspectors and validated sampling. Testing, swabbing and rework of failed handovers carry cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of heat-sensitive component degradation caused by localized thermal exposure during compounding
rows.plain: Reduce damage to ingredients or parts that can be harmed by heat during mixing. This helps preserve their quality and effectiveness.
rows.imp_rat: Localized thermal exposure degrading heat-sensitive components harms quality; relevant with high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Wall hot spots causing degradation are a known risk that scraped agitation and control partly but not fully mitigate. Assessing localized thermal exposure requires added characterization. Identifying hot-spot degradation risk needs specialized thermal-sensitivity knowledge. Heat-degraded material is wasted active.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of introducing microbial contamination onto product-contact surfaces during cleaning
rows.plain: Prevent germs from getting onto surfaces that touch the product while those surfaces are being cleaned. This helps keep the product safe.
rows.imp_rat: Microbial contamination onto product-contact surfaces during cleaning undermines the hygienic foundation of preparation (relevance 8) and risks a contaminated batch and patient safety (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Validated SIP and sanitization is a mature, widely deployed practice that reliably delivers clean surfaces with rare excursions. Automated CIP/SIP cycles run to validated schedules, consuming predictable cleaning time between batches. Validated automated cleaning removes most operator judgement, lowering the skill needed to achieve sterile surfaces. Cleaning chemicals, water and energy are significant but well-controlled recurring costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an entered setpoint fails to take effect in the active batch
rows.plain: Make sure a new target setting actually takes effect in the batch that is running. Otherwise, the process may continue using the old setting.
rows.imp_rat: An entered setpoint failing to take effect in the active batch undermines the reliability of process control central to the job; the batch drifts off target though the failure is often detectable and correctable.
rows.sat_rat: Closed-loop feedback and setpoint-echo confirmation catch most non-applied entries, though comms glitches and unconfirmed manual writes still occasionally fail silently. Control systems apply confirmed setpoints essentially instantly, and failures to take effect surface fast via feedback. Operators must still confirm the change registered, requiring some attentiveness where feedback is weak. A setpoint silently not applying can ruin a batch, but robust confirmation loops keep such losses infrequent.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that wetted-material incompatibility with the target liquid goes undetected
rows.plain: Make sure the parts that touch the liquid are suitable for that liquid. Problems caused by an unsuitable material should be found before the process begins.
rows.imp_rat: Undetected wetted-material incompatibility with the target liquid can silently corrode or leach into product; it is a selection/validation error, moderately central, but its consequences (contamination, equipment failure) are serious.
rows.sat_rat: Established compatibility charts and supplier datasheets catch most incompatibilities, but edge cases with mixtures, trace additives, or unusual temperatures still slip through, leaving recurring gaps. Material compatibility checks against supplier wetted-material lists take modest time but are rarely the bottleneck during acquisition. Reliable detection depends on a materials/process engineer cross-referencing chemical compatibility charts, and that expertise is not always on the buying team. Compatibility data is largely free from suppliers and standard references, so cost is not the limiting factor.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required dissolution and blending conditions lacking evidence of reproducible results
rows.plain: Make sure there is evidence that the required mixing and dissolving conditions produce the same reliable results each time. This helps show that the process works consistently.
rows.imp_rat: Conditions lacking reproducibility evidence weaken validation confidence; relevant to reliable job delivery with high compliance severity.
rows.sat_rat: Established DQ/IQ/OQ/PQ frameworks and vendor test packages cover most reproducibility conditions, yet gaps in specific edge conditions routinely persist. Assembling reproducibility evidence across conditions takes weeks of protocol review, though templates from prior qualifications shorten this. Judging whether replicate runs constitute adequate evidence demands experienced validation engineers who are in short supply. Repeat qualification runs consume material and vendor time, but standard budgets absorb this expense.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of contaminants entering controlled production areas while batching-system electrical infrastructure is accessed
rows.plain: Keep dirt and other unwanted material out of the controlled production area while electrical parts of the mixing system are being accessed. This protects the cleanliness of the area.
rows.imp_rat: Contaminants entering controlled areas during electrical-infrastructure access moderately relevant to preserving the aseptic environment around batching, with serious contamination consequences for concurrent production.
rows.sat_rat: Preventing particulate or personnel-borne contamination during electrical access relies on ad hoc containment that frequently leaves gaps, so protection is only partial. Erecting containment or isolating an electrical enclosure area from clean production space adds meaningful setup time before work can begin. Balancing electrical access with contamination control requires cross-disciplinary judgement that few maintenance techs hold alone. Temporary barriers, gowning, and area re-cleaning after panel access impose real recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of contamination entering product-contact areas during idle storage
rows.plain: Keep unwanted material from reaching the parts that touch the product while the system is stored and not in use. This helps keep the system ready for safe production.
rows.imp_rat: Contamination entering product-contact areas during idle storage moderately affects batch readiness, and the consequence is a contaminated first batch after startup with meaningful quality risk.
rows.sat_rat: Idle-storage contamination is often under-controlled with informal practices, so ingress over extended idle periods remains a common and frequently underestimated failure. Preparing systems for idle storage (drying, purging, capping) adds time upfront and re-qualification before restart. Proper layup and integrity of protective barriers depend on staff knowledge that is unevenly applied. Nitrogen blanketing, desiccants, and covers are inexpensive relative to a contaminated restart.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of critical sterility, containment, or product-contact requirements omitted from the specification
rows.plain: Make sure the equipment requirements do not leave out important needs for preventing contamination, containing hazards, or protecting the product. Missing requirements can lead to unsafe or unsuitable equipment.
rows.imp_rat: Omitting critical sterility/containment requirements from the specification is an upstream design gap somewhat removed from execution (moderate relevance), but such omissions propagate into unsafe batches (high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Established URS templates and GMP checklists catch most common contact requirements, yet novel formulations and site-specific needs regularly slip through, leaving recurring gaps. URS development for batch prep systems takes weeks of cross-functional effort, and time pressure often forces spec freeze before all contact requirements are captured. Complete sterility and product-contact specification depends heavily on scarce senior process and QA experts whose judgement determines what gets included. Omissions surface as costly change orders or requalification later, but the specification effort itself consumes modest resources.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of residual viscous product left on product-contact surfaces and inside vessels after cleaning
rows.plain: Remove as much thick leftover product as possible from surfaces and containers after cleaning. This helps prevent old product from remaining in the equipment.
rows.imp_rat: Residual viscous product on surfaces after cleaning drives both contamination and yield concerns (relevance 7); it can taint following batches or require re-cleaning, a moderate recurring loss (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Viscous product notoriously clings in low-flow zones, so standard CIP leaves recurring residue that is a chronic, only-partly-controlled problem. Extended flush and soak cycles are needed to shift clingy viscous product, lengthening cleaning time. Operators must know product-specific cleaning parameters and dead-leg locations to remove tenacious residue. High cleaning-agent, solvent and energy consumption is inherent to removing sticky viscous material.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time from halt initiation until hazardous batch activity ceases
rows.plain: Make sure dangerous activity in the batch stops quickly after the stop command is given. This helps limit the risk to people, equipment, and product.
rows.imp_rat: The time from halt initiation to cessation of hazardous activity is important for containing an emergency (moderate-high relevance); prolonged hazardous activity can escalate into catastrophic injury or loss (high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Validated E-stop and halt logic reliably ceases hazardous activity quickly, with residual delay only from stored energy or slow-acting final elements. Emergency halt systems are engineered to cut hazardous activity within defined short response times, which is their core purpose. A single operator action triggers the halt sequence, requiring no special skill to invoke. Fast-acting stop circuits and valves are standard safety spend, well amortized across equipment life.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that system contact materials introduce contaminants into the formulation
rows.plain: Make sure the parts that touch the product do not add dirt or other unwanted substances to it. This helps keep the finished product safe and suitable for use.
rows.imp_rat: Contact materials leaching contaminants into the formulation compromises product purity central to the job (moderate-high relevance) and can cause contaminated product reaching patients (high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Widely adopted USP Class VI, 316L, and validated polymer standards make contaminant introduction from contact materials well controlled in mature practice, with rare surprises. Extractables/leachables and material compatibility reviews add lead time but are routinely scheduled into acquisition. Interpreting material-contaminant risk requires specialized materials-science and toxicology expertise that not every buying team has in-house. Selecting proven contact materials adds upfront cost that is far cheaper than a contaminated batch, and standard materials are widely available.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of deviation between measured component concentrations and actual batch concentrations
rows.plain: Make sure the measured amounts of ingredients are as close as possible to the amounts actually present in the production mixture. This helps confirm that the mixture has the intended makeup.
rows.imp_rat: Deviation between measured and actual concentrations undermines the reliability of disposition data; severe because it can mask an out-of-spec batch.
rows.sat_rat: Validated assays, calibrated instruments, and control charts make measured-versus-actual concentration agreement one of the best-controlled aspects of compounding today. Assay turnaround for concentration verification runs hours to days, but the governance concern is accuracy of the result rather than its speed. Validated analytical methods and trained QC analysts make concentration measurement a mature, repeatable competency for the overseer to rely on. Reference standards and instrument time cost money, but this is a routine budgeted expense rather than a pain point.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the batch cycle continues after the operator initiates the halt
rows.plain: Make sure the production run stops promptly after the operator tells it to stop because of a problem. This limits the effects of the fault.
rows.imp_rat: A batch cycle continuing after the operator commands halt defeats operator control central to safe preparation (moderate-high relevance) and can lead to injury or uncontained hazard (high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Well-designed control logic reliably honors operator halt commands, with continuation failures being rare and typically traced to logic or actuator faults. Halt commands execute near-instantly so the cycle stops without meaningful lag once initiated. The operator only needs to trigger the halt; ensuring the cycle actually stops is handled automatically. Halt-confirmation logic is inherent in existing control systems at negligible added cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of applying incorrect production parameters during line startup
rows.plain: Prevent the wrong operating settings from being used when the production line starts. This helps avoid producing defective or unsafe product.
rows.imp_rat: Applying incorrect parameters at line startup directly compromises product quality from the run's outset (relevance 8); it typically yields off-spec product needing rework or scrap, a significant but recoverable loss (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Validated recipe download and startup interlocks reliably apply correct parameters, though manual setups and legacy lines still allow occasional errors. Recipe-driven startup loads parameters quickly, avoiding lengthy manual entry that would delay the line. Electronic recipe management removes most manual parameter entry, cutting reliance on operator memory at startup. A mis-parameterized startup wastes early production and energy, but recipe automation makes this a rare, contained cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of product-contact areas becoming contaminated during pump or valve servicing
rows.plain: Keep the areas that touch the product clean while pumps or flow-control parts are being repaired or maintained. This helps prevent the product from becoming contaminated.
rows.imp_rat: Contamination during pump or valve servicing is an intermittent maintenance-driven risk, less central than routine processing steps; but when it occurs it introduces contamination into product-contact areas requiring re-sanitization or batch loss, so severity is high.
rows.sat_rat: SOPs, controlled environments, and post-service cleaning reduce contamination events, but manual intervention on open product-contact parts remains a recurring source of excursions. Servicing pumps and valves aseptically adds significant time for gowning, staging, and controlled disassembly. Contamination-free servicing depends heavily on the operator's aseptic technique and vigilance, which varies person to person. Requires cleanroom consumables, gowning, and often re-cleaning if a lapse occurs, adding recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of the active batch cycle spent outside specified temperature, pressure, or timing limits
rows.plain: Reduce the amount of time a production run spends outside its required temperature, pressure, or timing limits. This helps keep the process consistent and within safe operating conditions.
rows.imp_rat: Time spent outside specified temperature, pressure, or timing limits is central to batch quality, and the consequence is degraded or out-of-spec product proportional to the excursion.
rows.sat_rat: Automated control keeps most of the cycle within specified limits, with brief excursions during transitions, upsets, or tuning limits being the main residual. Closed-loop control keeps the cycle within limits continuously, minimizing time spent out of spec. PID and recipe-driven control reduce reliance on operators to hold parameters in band. Standard control loops deliver this with well-understood, amortized instrumentation cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of deviation from target consistency after a parameter adjustment
rows.plain: Keep the mixture as close as possible to the desired evenness after changing the mixing settings. This helps maintain consistent product quality.
rows.imp_rat: Deviation from target consistency after a parameter adjustment is central to achieving the target product quality of the preparation job; consequences are off-spec batches that are typically correctable rather than harmful.
rows.sat_rat: Without in-line consistency feedback and model-based control, post-adjustment deviation from target recurs and is corrected reactively. Re-attaining target consistency after an adjustment costs settling and re-test time. Predicting how a parameter change moves consistency toward target leans on operator experience. Overshoot and corrective re-work consume ingredient and time, a recurring moderate cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of worker exposure from trapped pressure or residual product released during maintenance access
rows.plain: Prevent trapped pressure or leftover product from reaching the worker when the process lines are opened for maintenance. The lines should be emptied and rinsed safely first.
rows.imp_rat: Worker exposure from trapped pressure or residual product during maintenance access is central to safe servicing; the release can cause chemical burns or serious injury, so severity is high while relevance is maintenance-scoped.
rows.sat_rat: Depressurize-and-verify procedures are standard practice, but trapped pressure in isolated sections still causes occasional releases where isolation points are misjudged. Depressurization and drain-down steps add measurable time before access but are routine in the work sequence. Recognizing trapped pressure or product in dead legs requires system knowledge that less-experienced technicians can miss. Bleed valves and pressure gauges are low-cost, and product loss from proper draining is minor.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of product-contact surface area retaining previous-batch residue after cleaning
rows.plain: Remove as much leftover material from product-touching surfaces as possible, so residue from the previous batch does not affect the next one.
rows.imp_rat: Residual previous-batch residue after cleaning risks cross-contamination and is important to batch integrity (relevance 8); consequences depend on residue toxicity but generally require rework or scrap (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Riboflavin and swab-validated CIP reliably clears residue on accessible surfaces, though shadowed geometries and deadlegs still retain traces. Adequate CIP flow, time and temperature to remove residue extends the cleaning window. Validated cleaning recipes reduce reliance on operator skill to reach residue targets. Reaching low residue levels drives water, chemical and energy consumption upward.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of formulation temperature excursion caused by the order or pace of exothermic additions
rows.plain: Keep the mixture from getting too hot or too cold because ingredients are added too quickly or in the wrong order. This helps protect the mixture and keep the process under control.
rows.imp_rat: Temperature excursion from exothermic addition order can degrade product or cause boil-off; relevant with high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Controlled dosing rates and jacket cooling usually contain excursions, but fast or manual additions still occasionally spike temperature. Managing exothermic-addition pace to avoid excursions may require slowed, longer charging. Pacing exothermic additions safely depends on operator knowledge of the reaction. An uncontrolled exotherm can degrade material and waste a batch.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that translated mixing parameters exceed vessel operating limits at the target volume
rows.plain: Make sure the adjusted mixing settings stay within what the vessel can safely handle when it contains the intended amount. This helps prevent unsafe operation or poor mixing.
rows.imp_rat: Translated parameters exceeding vessel limits at target volume risks equipment/process failure; relevant with high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Engineering review usually catches limit violations before execution, though scale-up translation errors do slip through. Catching parameter translations that exceed vessel limits at scale is a design-time check, not a runtime delay. Correctly translating parameters to the target volume demands scale-up engineering expertise. Parameters exceeding limits can damage equipment or scrap batches, an expensive failure.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of hazardous residues escaping containment before final disposal
rows.plain: Keep dangerous leftover materials contained until they are finally disposed of. This protects people and the surrounding area from exposure.
rows.imp_rat: Hazardous residues escaping containment before disposal is a waste-handling concern less central to the primary processing job, but escaped hazardous material can harm personnel and environment, giving elevated severity.
rows.sat_rat: Containment practice is established but aging equipment, unmapped residues and transfer steps leave real recurring escape risk before final disposal. Containment setup and controlled decanting extend the disposal timeline noticeably. Judging residue behavior and appropriate containment demands experienced hazmat handlers, and errors trace to gaps here. Secondary containment, bunding and licensed disposal routes impose meaningful recurring cost.

rows.stk: Process engineers, materials engineers, procurement leads.
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of judging chemically incompatible wetted materials suitable for the intended application
rows.plain: Make sure the materials that touch the process liquid will not react badly with it or make the process unsafe. This helps ensure the equipment is suitable for its intended use.
rows.imp_rat: Judging chemically incompatible wetted materials suitable is a design-selection error, moderately central to batching operation (relevance) but severe—material degradation can leach contaminants or cause failures (high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Compatibility charts, USP Class VI data and supplier declarations cover common cases, but nonstandard chemistries still produce recurring misjudgements.
