Material compatibility review adds review cycles but is typically done in parallel with other evaluation tasks. Correctly judging chemical compatibility of wetted materials against a specific formulation requires specialized materials-science and process expertise that is not always on hand. Compatibility errors surface late and are costly to remediate, but upfront review itself is inexpensive.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of hazardous equipment activity after shutdown activation
rows.plain: Stop dangerous equipment activity as quickly as possible after the emergency stop is activated, so people and equipment are protected.
rows.imp_rat: Hazardous equipment activity persisting after shutdown weakens the emergency-stop reliability the job depends on (moderate-high relevance) and can cause serious harm before it stops (high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Equipment reliably de-energizes on shutdown, though coast-down of rotating or pressurized components leaves a short residual activity period. Post-shutdown equipment activity is minimized by de-energization circuits acting within standard trip times. Automatic shutdown sequences remove operator involvement in halting equipment motion. Standard safety relays and contactors handle this at modest incremental cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an electrical, pressure, steam, gas, or liquid utility remains energized or connected at handover
rows.plain: Make sure all electricity, pressure, steam, gas, and liquid connections are shut off and disconnected before the equipment is handed over, so no hidden danger remains.
rows.imp_rat: Energized utilities at handover is a maintenance/lockout error tangential to the processing job itself (relevance 6), yet failure can cause electrocution, burns, or fatal injury to personnel (severity 9).
rows.sat_rat: Mature isolation registers, LOTO and verification make stranded live utilities at handover relatively uncommon, though missed hidden connections still occur. Systematic utility isolation and de-energization is a routine, well-scheduled closeout step. Confirming all utilities are dead requires competent technicians and verification testing that is well practiced. Isolation, capping and verification use standard low-cost methods.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of losing sterile conditions during a throughput change
rows.plain: Keep the work area sterile when the production speed or amount changes, so the product remains protected from contamination.
rows.imp_rat: Losing sterile conditions during a throughput change threatens continuity of sterile production during a routine operational adjustment (moderately high relevance), with batch loss consequences (notable severity).
rows.sat_rat: Validated turndown ranges keep sterility stable within design limits, though excursions outside range can compromise conditions. Throughput changes are ramped gradually, adding some time but not the core issue. Ramping while holding sterility relies on operator control-loop tuning and attention. Turndown-capable equipment carries capital cost but is standard design.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of batch temperature deviation from the validated mixing range
rows.plain: Keep the mixture close to the approved temperature range while it is being mixed. This helps the batch turn out as expected.
rows.imp_rat: Temperature deviation from validated range risks dissolution failure and degradation; relevant with high severity for product quality.
rows.sat_rat: Automated jacket temperature control keeps batches within validated range reliably in most facilities. Bringing and holding the batch in the validated range can add ramp and equilibration time. Tuning jacket control to stay in range needs modest operator skill supported by automation. Out-of-range temperature risks batch loss and energy overuse.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that product or cleaning residues remain on vessel product-contact surfaces after cleaning
rows.plain: Make sure as little product or cleaning material as possible is left on the surfaces that touch the product after cleaning. This helps prevent unwanted substances from getting into the product.
rows.imp_rat: Residues remaining after cleaning risk cross-contamination of the next batch; low direct mixing relevance but catastrophic contamination severity.
rows.sat_rat: Validated CIP with defined surface finishes reliably clears residues for most well-characterized products, yet tenacious actives and worst-case residues still produce occasional failures that keep this from being fully solved. Automated CIP cycles run in defined timeframes, but confirming true residue removal still requires swab and rinse sampling turnaround that adds hours to days. Selecting electropolished 316L surfaces and validated CIP recipes is well understood by qualified engineers, but interpreting residue-limit adequacy across new formulations still demands specialist cleaning-validation judgement. Meeting stringent residue limits drives investment in high-finish surfaces and analytical testing, but this is a normalized, budgeted cost in pharma capital projects.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an existing containment breach remains undetected during tank testing
rows.plain: Make sure any existing leak or opening in the tank is found during testing. This helps prevent unsafe leaks or contamination later.
rows.imp_rat: An existing containment breach missed during testing defeats the purpose of the test that qualifies the tank (relevance 7); a leak released into service can cause hazardous exposure and product loss (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Established pressure/leak testing is a mature, sensitive practice that reliably reveals most existing breaches. Hydrostatic or pneumatic leak testing is a well-defined procedure that fits within planned maintenance windows. Standardized test protocols reduce dependence on rare expertise, though pressure-decay interpretation still needs trained eyes. Test media handling, fill/drain and disposal add cost but are routine and predictable.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of technician exposure to hazardous pressure, stored energy, or process liquid during diagnosis and repair
rows.plain: Protect technicians from dangerous pressure, trapped energy, or process liquid while they find and fix a problem.
rows.imp_rat: Technician exposure to stored energy or hazardous liquid is a maintenance-side error, less central to the batching job itself (relevance 5), but its consequences—serious injury or death—are catastrophic (severity 10).
rows.sat_rat: Mature OSHA/regulated LOTO and permit-to-work practice reliably prevents most exposure incidents, though residual failures occur when a hidden energy source or accumulator is overlooked. LOTO and de-energization procedures add time to every job but are routinely built into maintenance schedules, so the time cost is predictable rather than a chronic pain. Executing a proper energy-isolation sequence on a multi-utility batching skid demands trained, authorized technicians who understand every stored-energy source, which remains a real skill dependency. Lockout hardware, permits, and downtime carry modest recurring cost that is broadly accepted as the price of safe work.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a hazardous skid condition fails to trigger a protective interlock
rows.plain: Make sure a dangerous equipment condition automatically starts the needed protective action. This helps protect people and equipment when something goes wrong.
rows.imp_rat: Failure of a protective interlock to trigger on a hazardous condition is a safety-system reliability issue somewhat aside from routine processing, so moderate relevance; the consequence is unmitigated equipment or personnel hazard, which is severe.
rows.sat_rat: Safety-rated interlocks with documented FAT/SAT proof-testing are mature practice and fail-to-trigger events are uncommon when properly commissioned. Interlock verification is scripted into commissioning tests, adding bounded time but not chronic delay. Designing and testing safety interlocks requires competent controls engineers, but the logic itself removes the need for operator vigilance in operation. Safety PLC hardware and validation testing carry cost, offset by avoided incident and downtime losses.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a candidate vessel cannot meet required heating and cooling profiles under maximum thermal load
rows.plain: Make sure a proposed vessel can heat and cool the material as required even when it is carrying the greatest expected heat load. This helps confirm that the equipment can handle the process in demanding conditions.
rows.imp_rat: Meeting heating/cooling profiles under max thermal load supports dissolution but is a thermal-capability adjunct; severe for exothermic or heat-sensitive formulations.
rows.sat_rat: Established heat-transfer engineering usually predicts profiles well, though worst-case thermal load fouling and viscosity effects create residual misses. Heat-transfer calculations from jacket area and coolant data are quick once inputs are known. Thermal duty sizing is a well-codified engineering discipline requiring moderate but common expertise. Oversizing jackets or utilities to hedge uncertainty adds capital cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of incompatible residual process liquids mixing during drainage
rows.plain: Keep different leftover process liquids from mixing if they should not be combined while they are drained away.
rows.imp_rat: Mixing incompatible residual liquids during drainage is a transitional-state concern rather than core throughput (relevance moderate); the consequence can be hazardous reactions and equipment damage (severity high).
rows.sat_rat: Line-by-line segregated drainage is standard, though shared drains and misidentified contents create recurring mixing risk. Sequenced drainage of incompatible lines into separate collection adds time to the drain-down. Knowing which residual liquids must not meet requires process knowledge held by experienced staff. Separate collection vessels and routing add modest cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of lubricant entering sterile or product-contact areas during maintenance
rows.plain: Keep oil or grease out of germ-free areas and places that touch the product while moving parts are being serviced.
rows.imp_rat: Lubricant entering sterile or product-contact areas during maintenance is a maintenance-adjacent risk (moderate relevance), but such contamination causes serious product adulteration and recalls (high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Design isolation and food-grade lubes mitigate the risk, but lubricant migration into product zones still occurs occasionally and is a known failure mode. Applying food-grade lubricants carefully and containing them adds modest time to maintenance tasks. Preventing migration depends on technician judgement about quantity and placement, a skill-sensitive manual task. Food-grade/sterile-compatible lubricants cost more than standard ones but usage volumes are small.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of liquid spread from the release point onto adjacent equipment or walkways
rows.plain: Keep a liquid spill close to where it starts, so it does not spread onto nearby equipment or places where people walk.
rows.imp_rat: Liquid spreading from a release point onto adjacent equipment and walkways is moderately central to the core job but very consequential when it occurs (high severity), creating slip hazards, corrosion, and chemical exposure to personnel.
rows.sat_rat: Curbing and drainage reliably limit spread onto walkways in most releases, though large or high-pressure releases can overwhelm containment. Passive containment (bunds, sloped floors, drains) confines spread immediately without response delay. Spread control is designed-in and needs little operator skill once containment is built. Containment infrastructure is standard and cleanup cost is bounded when it works.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of batch-to-batch variation in reaching the specified dissolution and homogeneity endpoint
rows.plain: Make sure every batch reaches the required level of dissolving and even mixing in about the same way. This makes the process more predictable and the results more consistent.
rows.imp_rat: Batch-to-batch variation in reaching dissolution/homogeneity endpoint reflects process robustness; relevant with moderate-high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Batch-to-batch endpoint variation persists without tight process analytical control. Confirming endpoint consistency across batches takes repeated cycles. Diagnosing endpoint variation causes needs experienced judgement. Endpoint variability drives inconsistent cycle times and losses.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of critical dissolution, blend-uniformity, and temperature-control outputs lacking reproducibility limits
rows.plain: Make sure important test results for mixing, dissolving, and temperature control can be repeated reliably and have clear limits for acceptable variation. This helps people know when the process is working properly.
rows.imp_rat: Missing reproducibility limits for critical outputs is a specification-completeness gap; moderately relevant with notable compliance severity.
rows.sat_rat: Critical outputs are usually specified but reproducibility limits are inconsistently applied, leaving recurring undefined bounds. Defining reproducibility limits for each critical output extends protocol development but follows known templates. Setting statistically defensible limits for dissolution, blend uniformity, and temperature control requires scarce validation and statistics expertise. Deriving limits mainly costs analyst hours rather than materials.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of transferring batch material into a tank with incompatible readiness status
rows.plain: Make sure material is not moved into a tank that is not ready or suitable to receive it. This helps prevent mixing, processing, or equipment problems.
rows.imp_rat: Transferring batch material into a tank of incompatible readiness status undermines correct process staging (relevance 6); it risks contamination, reaction, or lost batch (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Tank-readiness interlocks exist in advanced MES/DCS setups but are inconsistently applied, so wrong-status transfers remain a recurring risk. Confirming a receiving tank's readiness status can require manual checks that delay transfer initiation. Judging whether a tank is clean, empty and released relies on operator tracking and shift handover unless enforced by automation. Status tracking uses cheap logs or DCS states, but transferring into an unready tank causes costly cross-contamination or rework.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of introducing contamination into exposed product-contact areas during repair
rows.plain: Keep exposed areas that touch the product free from contamination while workers fix a problem.
rows.imp_rat: Contamination introduced into exposed product-contact areas during repair moderately affects batch integrity (relevance) and is severe because it can taint subsequent product (high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Contamination ingress during open repair is a chronic risk poorly contained by manual practice, so it is only partially served. Repairing an opened product-contact component under contamination control extends job duration through cleaning and clearance steps. Keeping an exposed wetted surface clean during hands-on repair depends heavily on disciplined aseptic technique that varies by technician. Repair-then-reclean-and-revalidate cycles consume significant chemicals, labor, and lost production.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of mismatch between the demonstrated configuration and conditions and the intended sterile fill-finish application
rows.plain: Make sure the demonstration setup and conditions closely match how the system is meant to be used to fill and finish germ-free products.
rows.imp_rat: Mismatch between demonstrated configuration and the intended sterile fill-finish application is a qualification/validation alignment concern moderately relevant to fitness-for-use, with serious consequences if a system is misapplied to sterile duty.
rows.sat_rat: Demos and reference installations rarely match the exact intended conditions, so residual mismatch is a common and under-controlled failure. Verifying demonstrated configurations against intended conditions often requires site visits or FAT observation that extend schedules. Judging whether a vendor demonstration truly represents the intended sterile application takes experienced process engineers who can spot subtle mismatches. Travel, witnessed testing and demo setups add cost, and undetected mismatches trigger expensive change orders later.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of residual pressure, chemical, electrical, or mechanical hazards left unidentified before decommissioning
rows.plain: Before taking equipment out of service, find any remaining pressure, chemical, electrical, or moving-part dangers. This helps prevent injuries during removal or shutdown.
rows.imp_rat: Leaving residual pressure, chemical, electrical, or mechanical hazards unidentified before decommissioning is peripheral to routine batching output (moderate relevance) but severe when it occurs, since undetected stored energy can cause serious injury during teardown.
rows.sat_rat: Pre-decommissioning hazard assessments exist as practice but consistently miss hazards on aged or poorly documented systems. A thorough pre-decommissioning hazard survey takes significant time that projects frequently underbudget. Comprehensively enumerating pressure, chemical, electrical, and mechanical hazards requires broad multidisciplinary expertise rarely held by one person. The assessment labor is a modest fraction of overall project cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of batch nonuniformity caused by variation in incoming ingredient viscosity
rows.plain: Keep each production run as consistent as possible when the incoming ingredient is thicker or thinner than usual. This helps the finished product remain uniform.
rows.imp_rat: Batch nonuniformity from incoming viscosity variation is a real cause of off-spec product relevant to mixing quality, but it is one contributing factor among several and usually yields correctable batch defects rather than severe harm.
rows.sat_rat: Fixed mixing recipes seldom adapt to lot-to-lot viscosity swings, so non-uniformity from this source recurs without dedicated in-line rheology feedback. Compensating for incoming viscosity variation typically requires added mixing or heating time that extends the batch. Judging how to adapt agitation to a more viscous incoming lot leans on operator experience that is not consistently codified. Extra energy and occasional rework from viscosity-driven non-uniformity add recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of technician exposure to stored mechanical or process energy during service
rows.plain: Protect technicians from energy that remains stored in pumps and mixing equipment while they are being serviced. This includes pressure, moving parts, and other forces that could suddenly be released.
rows.imp_rat: Technician exposure to stored mechanical/process energy during service is peripheral to the batching function (relevance 5) but can cause fatal or disabling injury (severity 10).
rows.sat_rat: Standardized energy-dissipation and zero-energy verification is well established and consistently effective, with rare lapses on infrequently serviced components. Verifying stored mechanical/process energy is discharged (springs, accumulators, actuators) is a defined step that adds bounded time to service. Recognizing all stored-energy points on pumps, actuated valves, and pressurized vessels still relies on technician training and experience. Bleed-down and verification tooling is inexpensive relative to the injury and downtime it prevents.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of directing a liquid stream to the wrong processing stage
rows.plain: Make sure each liquid is sent to the correct step in the process. This helps prevent incorrect mixing, wasted material, or damage to equipment.
rows.imp_rat: Directing a stream to the wrong processing stage disrupts correct process sequence (relevance 7), causing mis-processed or cross-mixed product that may need disposal (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Interlocked routing and valve position feedback prevent most misrouting, but manual line-ups remain an error source. Automated valve routing directs streams quickly without manual line-up delay. Manual valve line-ups still depend on operator accuracy where automation is absent. Misrouting can cross-contaminate or lose a batch, a costly error.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of batch-to-batch variation in undissolved component mass at the specified endpoint
rows.plain: Keep the amount of material that remains undissolved at the chosen stopping point nearly the same from one batch to the next. This makes the process and its results more dependable.
rows.imp_rat: Batch-to-batch variation in undissolved mass reflects dissolution inconsistency; relevant with moderate-high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Batch-to-batch dissolution variation persists where endpoint control is not tightly instrumented. Tracking batch-to-batch undissolved mass requires ongoing sampling over cycles. Distinguishing variation causes needs experienced process interpretation. Variable undissolved mass causes inconsistent yield losses.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of tank deformation when pressure falls below the safe operating limit
rows.plain: Prevent the tank from bending or collapsing when the pressure inside drops too low. This protects the tank from damage.
rows.imp_rat: Vacuum-induced deformation below the safe operating limit compromises tank integrity, central to structural function (relevance 7); a collapsed tank means major asset loss and possible release (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Vacuum breakers and low-pressure interlocks are well-established, but underpressure protection is more often overlooked than overpressure, so failures still occur. Vacuum/breaker valves relieve internal underpressure essentially instantly as external pressure builds. Vacuum-relief sizing is standard practice but implosion protection is less universally scrutinized than overpressure, leaving more room for design oversight. Vacuum breakers and pressure-vacuum vents are low-cost fittings, though a collapsed tank is a costly loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of formulation component degradation caused by charging before suitable vessel conditions are established
rows.plain: Ingredients should not be added until the container is in the right condition. This helps prevent the ingredients from being damaged or becoming less effective.
rows.imp_rat: Degradation from charging before suitable conditions harms product quality; relevant with high severity for sensitive components.
rows.sat_rat: Recipe entry conditions and interlocks usually prevent premature charging, with occasional lapses. Waiting for suitable vessel conditions before charging can delay the batch. Knowing when conditions are suitable relies on operator judgement and interlocks. Premature charging degrades material, causing loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of residual process liquid being released outside the intended collection boundary during drainage
rows.plain: Keep leftover liquid inside the intended collection area while it drains. This prevents spills and unwanted release into the surrounding area.
rows.imp_rat: Residual liquid released outside the collection boundary during drainage affects containment during transitions (relevance moderate-high); spills create environmental and cleanup burdens plus potential contamination (severity moderate-high).
rows.sat_rat: Containment-boundary practice is mature and effective, but overflows, splash and unmapped drain paths still let liquid escape occasionally. Setting up bunds and collection boundaries before drainage adds preparation time. Managing controlled drainage within a boundary is routine competence rather than a scarce skill. Bunding, drip trays and portable containment are relatively low cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of variation in dissolution endpoint achievement across repeat batches
rows.plain: Repeated batches should reach the point where ingredients have fully dissolved to the same extent each time. This helps make the product consistent.
rows.imp_rat: Variation in dissolution endpoint across repeat batches undermines process consistency; relevant with moderate-high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Repeat-batch dissolution data is routinely collected in PQ, giving moderate confidence though variation still appears. Running repeat batches to characterize endpoint variation adds substantial campaign duration. Interpreting endpoint variation across runs requires analytical and process judgement. Replicate batches consume active material, a real recurring expense.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that diverted liquid exceeds the available holding capacity during a process deviation
rows.plain: Make sure the container receiving rejected liquid does not fill beyond its available space during a problem. This helps prevent spills and keeps the diverted liquid contained.
rows.imp_rat: Diverted liquid exceeding holding capacity during a deviation is moderately central to safe deviation handling (relevance); overflow can cause secondary spills and loss of containment (notable severity).
rows.sat_rat: Fixed holding volumes handle typical deviations but a sustained or large deviation can overwhelm capacity, a real and recurring gap. Diversion to holding tanks happens quickly once triggered, but capacity fills fast during prolonged deviations leaving little buffer time. Sizing and managing surge capacity requires experienced process engineering, and operators must judge when overflow risk is imminent. Extra holding capacity is expensive to build and maintain, so plants often run with marginal buffer that can be exceeded.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of introducing contamination into the sterile batch during sampling
rows.plain: Prevent germs or other unwanted material from getting into the sterile batch while a sample is taken. This helps keep the batch safe and usable.
rows.imp_rat: Contamination during sampling is a discrete step less central to the whole batching job (relevance 6), but can invalidate results or contaminate the sterile batch (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Closed aseptic sampling valves reduce ingress but sampling remains a well-known contamination route where failures still happen. Aseptic sampling with sanitized valves and flush volumes adds handling time per sample. Contamination-free sampling relies on careful aseptic technique, making it operator-skill dependent. Sterile sample assemblies and discarded flush material add per-sample cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration that cleaning temperature, flow, or chemical concentration remains outside validated limits during the cycle
rows.plain: Keep cleaning heat, liquid flow, and cleaning chemical strength within their approved ranges throughout the cleaning cycle. This helps ensure the equipment is cleaned properly.
rows.imp_rat: Cleaning temperature, flow, or chemical concentration drifting outside validated limits compromises cleaning efficacy that the batching system depends on; sustained excursions leave contamination risk and validation failures, though a brief excursion may be recoverable.
rows.sat_rat: Instrumented CIP with logged parameters keeps cycles within validated limits most of the time, with brief excursions during ramp or upset the main residual gap. CIP controllers hold temperature, flow, and concentration to setpoint automatically, minimizing time spent out of limits. Automated closed-loop control removes most manual parameter management, though deviation diagnosis needs skill. Sensor calibration and utility supply carry ongoing cost but excursions are relatively cheap to correct.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of introducing contaminants into product-contact areas during service
rows.plain: Prevent dirt or other unwanted material from getting into areas that touch the product while equipment is being repaired or maintained. This helps protect the product from contamination.
rows.imp_rat: Introducing contaminants during service moderately affects the clean-batch objective (relevance) and is severe since post-service contamination can compromise multiple downstream batches (high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Established hygienic-service SOPs cover most cases but recurring contamination gaps persist, keeping this acceptably rather than well served. General service in product-contact areas is bounded but slowed by cleaning and clearance protocols. Avoiding contamination during service requires consistent hygienic discipline that remains technician-dependent. Cleaning consumables and re-verification add ongoing cost to routine service.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of heat from dissolution or agitation exceeding available jacket heat removal
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that mixing or dissolving the material creates more heat than the equipment can remove. Keeping the temperature under control helps protect the process and its product.
rows.imp_rat: Dissolution/agitation heat exceeding jacket removal causes runaway temperature and degradation; relevant to thermal control with high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Jacket capacity is typically sized with margin, so exceeding removal capability is uncommon but possible with high loads. If dissolution heat outpaces jacket removal, cooling holds extend the batch. Anticipating heat-load-versus-capacity mismatch requires engineering knowledge of the system. Insufficient heat removal can degrade material and force scrap.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of approving a prospective supplier whose quality management system fails sterile fill-finish requirements
rows.plain: Avoid approving a new supplier whose quality controls do not meet the needs for making sterile products. This helps ensure that supplied materials and services are dependable.
rows.imp_rat: Approving a supplier failing sterile fill-finish QMS is an upstream procurement error moderately connected to the on-line batching job, but the downstream consequence of systemic contamination is severe and far-reaching.
rows.sat_rat: Established supplier qualification programs, ISO/GMP certification checks and audit protocols catch most deficient QMS cases, though undetected gaps still occasionally slip through. Supplier quality audits and QMS reviews take weeks to schedule and complete, adding real delay to acquisition timelines. Assessing whether a QMS truly meets sterile fill-finish requirements demands scarce senior QA auditors whose judgement varies. Formal audits and third-party certifications carry meaningful expense but are a routine, budgeted line in pharma procurement.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of technician exposure to hazardous product residues during purging and cleaning
rows.plain: Keep technicians from touching or breathing in harmful product leftovers while they flush and clean the equipment. This helps protect their health during cleaning.
rows.imp_rat: Technician exposure to hazardous residues during purging is a maintenance-adjacent error rather than central to running production (moderate relevance), but when it occurs it can cause serious chemical injury to workers (high severity).
rows.sat_rat: CIP and purge procedures are mature and largely protect technicians, though residual hot-spots and incomplete purges still cause occasional exposure. Multi-cycle purging and cleaning validation is time-consuming but routine. Effective cleaning-in-place and purge verification relies on trained operators following validated protocols. Cleaning agents, solvents and effluent handling carry ongoing cost during purge cycles.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of operator exposure to the released liquid during containment
rows.plain: Prevent workers from coming into contact with liquid released during a spill. This helps protect them from possible harmful effects of the liquid.
rows.imp_rat: Operator exposure to released liquid during containment relates to safe fault handling (relevance moderate); such exposure risks acute injury or chemical burns (severity high).
rows.sat_rat: Layered protection (bunding, interlocks, remote isolation, PPE) makes serious operator exposure uncommon in modern plants, though residual splash and vapor exposures during response still occur. Emergency containment responses (dikes, bunds, drainage) act passively and immediately, but manual isolation steps can still lag during an active release. Trained operators follow PPE and lockout protocols, yet exposure avoidance still depends heavily on individual discipline and correct hazard judgement under stress. Secondary containment, PPE and safety systems are standard capital already provisioned, so incremental cost per event is low.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of critical setpoints configured outside approved batch values
rows.plain: Fewer important machine settings should be entered outside the approved ranges for the batch. This helps the batch be made correctly and safely.
rows.imp_rat: Critical setpoints configured outside approved values directly affects whether the batch meets specification (moderate-high relevance) and can produce out-of-spec or unsafe product (high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Recipe-management systems with approved parameter libraries and dual review catch most deviations, though manual entry errors on critical setpoints still occasionally pass. Verifying critical setpoints against approved values adds review time but is a defined checklist step. Recognizing an out-of-approval critical setpoint requires trained operators or engineers familiar with the recipe intent. Configuration review labor is modest relative to the cost of a bad critical batch.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of personnel exposure to hazardous residues while removing plant-specific safety infrastructure
rows.plain: Protect workers from harmful leftover materials while removing the site’s safety equipment. This helps prevent exposure during the removal work.
rows.imp_rat: Personnel exposure to hazardous residues while removing plant-specific safety infrastructure is a decommissioning-stage error peripheral to routine processing, but exposure can cause serious harm, so severity stays elevated.
rows.sat_rat: Sequencing procedures exist but the paradox of dismantling protection while hazards may remain leaves an error-prone gap that standard practice only partly closes. Removing safety infrastructure last-in-first-out adds sequencing time to the job. The core difficulty is judging when it is safe to strip out the very systems that protect workers, requiring scarce senior decommissioning expertise. PPE and monitoring during this phase are modest costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a collected sample does not represent the batch as a whole
rows.plain: Make sure the collected sample accurately reflects the entire lot, rather than just one part of it. This makes the quality decision more reliable.
rows.imp_rat: A non-representative sample defeats the purpose of in-process testing that gates batch quality; the consequence is accepting an out-of-spec batch based on false assurance, a serious quality-escape risk.
rows.sat_rat: Grab sampling from a single point often misrepresents an incompletely mixed batch, and this remains a common weakness without in-line or multi-point sampling. Drawing a representative sample after adequate mixing costs some wait time. Knowing when a batch is homogeneous enough for a valid grab sample relies on operator judgement. Unrepresentative samples cause false decisions and reprocessing, a recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of consequential vessel geometry and working-volume effects omitted from mixing capability requirements
rows.plain: Make sure mixing requirements account for how the container’s shape and fill level affect the result. This helps ensure the contents are mixed properly.
rows.imp_rat: Omitted geometry/working-volume effects in requirements risk dead zones and poor mixing; relevant upstream with moderate-high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Consequential baffle, aspect-ratio and low-fill effects are commonly omitted, producing unreliable capability requirements. Accounting for geometry and working-volume effects adds analysis steps to the spec process. Recognizing which geometry and impeller-to-volume effects matter demands deep mixing expertise that most influencers lack. Overlooked geometry effects cause redesign or dead-zone remediation costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of technician exposure to hazardous residual process liquid during drainage
rows.plain: The technician should not come into contact with leftover process liquid while it is being drained. This helps prevent exposure to a potentially harmful liquid.
rows.imp_rat: Exposure to hazardous residual liquid during drainage is a peripheral servicing task relative to core production (moderate relevance), yet direct contact with hazardous process liquid can cause severe injury (high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Established drain-down and PPE practice reliably limits exposure, with splash and residual pooling remaining the usual failure modes. Controlled gravity drainage into collection systems takes time but is a well-scheduled step. Drainage requires competent technicians managing valves and collection but is not among the most expert-dependent tasks. Drainage uses existing plant piping and low-cost collection vessels.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of cleaning-agent residue remaining in tanks, lines, pumps, mixers, valves, and dispensing points after cleaning
rows.plain: As little cleaning liquid as possible should remain in the tanks, pipes, pumps, mixing equipment, valves, and points where product is dispensed. This helps prevent the cleaning liquid from getting into the product.
rows.imp_rat: Cleaning-agent residue left in the system contaminates the next batch chemically (relevance 7); consequences are meaningful adulteration but often detectable and correctable (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Conductivity and TOC endpoint verification make cleaning-agent carryover uncommon, but low-flow points still occasionally retain residue. Final rinse and conductivity-verified flush steps add measurable time to each cleaning cycle. Automated rinse-to-conductivity endpoints reduce the judgement needed to confirm chemical removal. Extra rinse water and analytical checks add cost aimed at eliminating chemical carryover.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of utility interruptions that stop vessel mixing or thermal control
rows.plain: Reduce interruptions to the services that keep the vessel mixing and at the right temperature. This helps prevent delays and keeps the production schedule on track.
rows.imp_rat: Utility interruptions stopping mixing/thermal control can abort or ruin a batch; relevant with moderate-high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Redundant utilities and preventive maintenance reduce frequency, but interruptions still occur and are largely outside overseer control. Utility interruptions halt mixing and thermal control, causing unplanned stoppages that extend the batch. Restoring utilities is a facilities task not dependent on compounding operator skill. Interruptions risk batch loss and idle capacity, a meaningful cost exposure.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of contaminated liquid released into the work area or waste stream during decontamination
rows.plain: As little contaminated liquid as possible should spill into the work area or be sent into waste during cleaning. This helps protect people and the surrounding area.
rows.imp_rat: Contaminated liquid released into the work area or waste stream during decontamination is a decontamination-phase environmental/safety issue less central to producing a batch, with severe exposure and compliance consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Bunding, mats, and capture systems reliably contain most decon liquid, with occasional escapes during high-volume flushing. Setting up containment before decontamination adds setup time that is routine. Effective containment placement requires modest, widely held skill. Uncontained contaminated liquid entering the waste stream increases hazardous-disposal volume and cost, which containment directly limits.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of contamination entering the open fluid path during element replacement
rows.plain: Replacing a filter or similar part should not let dirt or germs enter the open path carrying the liquid or material. This helps protect the product from contamination.
rows.imp_rat: Contamination entering the open fluid path during element (filter) replacement moderately affects batch integrity (relevance) and is severe since a compromised fluid path taints product directly (high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Open-path exposure during element change is a known contamination window only partly mitigated by technique and local flushing. Filter-element replacement is a defined quick task, but flushing and clearance afterward add time. Swapping an element without contaminating the open fluid path requires standard hygienic handling most techs can perform. Elements, gaskets, and post-change flush chemicals are recurring but modest costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of introducing microbial or particulate contamination into the batch during the sample draw
rows.plain: Taking a sample should not introduce germs or particles into the batch. This helps keep the batch clean and trustworthy.
rows.imp_rat: Contamination introduced during the sample draw is a localized event (relevance 6); it can compromise the batch or yield false readings with notable consequence (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Closed single-use sampling systems substantially cut particulate and microbial ingress during the draw, though connection points still present residual risk. Closed sampling devices draw quickly once connected, adding modest time. Single-use closed samplers lower the aseptic skill needed versus open draws. Single-use sampling manifolds are recurring consumable costs but modest per draw.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of anchoring dust and debris entering sterile processing areas
rows.plain: As little dust and debris as possible should enter the clean work areas while equipment is being fixed in place. This helps keep those areas clean.
rows.imp_rat: Anchoring dust and debris entering sterile processing areas is relevant where sterility is part of the job (relevance); particulate ingress compromises sterility and can contaminate product batches (notable severity).
rows.sat_rat: Despite dust barriers and drilling shrouds, migration into sterile zones during anchoring still recurs and is hard to guarantee against. Temporary containment and phased anchoring add setup time but are routine schedule items during preparation. Cleanroom protocols exist but rely heavily on trained crews maintaining discipline during dusty anchoring work. Temporary enclosures, HEPA-filtered negative pressure and cleanup labor are recurring but budgeted costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of liquid product unintentionally released during shutdown
rows.plain: As little product as possible should accidentally spill when production is stopped. This reduces waste and helps keep the work area safe and clean.
rows.imp_rat: Unintentional liquid product release during shutdown moderately affects the plant's job of processing product safely (relevance); the consequences include product loss, environmental release, and cleanup burden (notable severity).
rows.sat_rat: Drain-down procedures and isolation reduce loss, but residual holdup and imperfect isolation mean some unintended release during shutdown persists. Quick-closing isolation valves limit release duration during shutdown but cannot recover liquid already in transit. Minimizing loss during shutdown depends on operators executing drain and isolation steps correctly. Unintentional release during shutdown is direct product and disposal loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of critical formulation and process inputs missing from the mixing requirements basis
rows.plain: Make sure the mixing plan includes all important product and process information. This helps ensure the equipment and mixing method are suitable for making the product correctly.
rows.imp_rat: Missing critical formulation/process inputs in the requirements basis is an upstream ambiguity that can misdirect the whole design; moderately relevant with meaningful severity.
rows.sat_rat: Requirements baskets are chronically incomplete because early-stage formulation data is immature and no standard checklist reliably captures every critical input. Chasing missing formulation inputs from R&D lengthens the requirements-gathering phase considerably. Knowing which inputs are critical demands cross-functional formulation and process knowledge that is unevenly distributed. Missing inputs surface late and drive costly rework or requoting.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that established mixing parameters exceed compounding vessel operating limits
rows.plain: Choose mixing settings that stay within what the mixing tank can safely handle. This helps prevent equipment damage or an unsafe process.
rows.imp_rat: Established parameters exceeding vessel operating limits risk mechanical failure or off-spec mixing; relevant with high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Documented operating limits and reviews reliably prevent most limit exceedances. Verifying parameters stay within vessel limits is an up-front check with little runtime impact. Comparing parameters to vessel limits requires equipment knowledge but is well-supported by documentation. Exceeding limits risks equipment damage and lost batches.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of product-contact surfaces that become contaminated while the sterile liquid-processing plant is idle
rows.plain: Keep surfaces that touch the product clean while the processing equipment is not being used. This helps prevent the next product from becoming contaminated.
rows.imp_rat: Contamination of product-contact surfaces during idle periods threatens sterility readiness (relevance moderate-high); contaminated surfaces can compromise the next batch and require re-cleaning (severity moderate-high).
rows.sat_rat: Clean-hold and blanketing practices are well established, though extended idle periods still allow some surface contamination to develop. Idle-state protection like holding cleaning solution or nitrogen blankets is set up quickly and then passive. Managing idle-hold conditions follows standard SOPs requiring modest operator attention. Hold media, blanketing gas and periodic recirculation add ongoing but modest cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of temperature variation across the batch during heating and cooling
rows.plain: Keep the temperature as even as possible throughout the batch while it is heated or cooled. This helps the whole batch have the same quality.
rows.imp_rat: Temperature variation during heating/cooling affects uniform dissolution and local degradation; moderately central with moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Jacketed vessels with agitation give fairly uniform temperatures, though localized gradients remain in viscous or large batches. Thermal uniformity mapping adds bounded measurement time. Interpreting temperature gradients uses standard thermal engineering. Thermal nonuniformity can degrade material and waste energy.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of hazardous residual fluid remaining in skid piping or vessels after draining and purging
rows.plain: Remove as much dangerous leftover liquid as possible from the connected pipes and vessels after draining and flushing. This helps reduce the chance of exposure or accidental release.
rows.imp_rat: Hazardous residual fluid left after draining and purging is largely a cleaning/changeover and safety issue rather than core processing, so relevance is moderate; residue drives cross-contamination and exposure risk, a meaningfully severe consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Draining and purging work well on accessible lines but residual fluid persistently hides in low points, dead-legs and single-use assemblies, so complete removal is inconsistent. Multiple drain-flush-purge cycles are time-consuming and often repeated when clearance fails. Operators know standard CIP/drain sequences, but locating trapped fluid in complex piping demands experienced judgement. Flush media, solvents and disposal of rinsate add meaningful spend.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of worker exposure to hazardous residues or stored energy during component recovery
rows.plain: Prevent workers from coming into contact with dangerous leftover material or trapped power while recovering parts. This helps them remove usable components safely.
rows.imp_rat: Worker exposure to residues or stored energy during component recovery is a maintenance-phase concern peripheral to normal production (moderate relevance), but stored energy release or hazardous contact can cause severe or fatal injury (high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Practice varies widely between contractors, so residual-hazard and stored-energy surprises during recovery remain a real recurring risk. Component recovery for reuse or salvage adds careful handling time versus simple demolition. Safely recovering components while confirming residues are gone and energy is dissipated demands experienced judgement that is often in short supply. Recovery can offset cost through salvage value but requires investment in careful teardown.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of the technician misidentifying the root cause of a process upset
rows.plain: Help the technician correctly identify the real reason a process went wrong. This allows the right problem to be fixed instead of only treating its effects.
rows.imp_rat: Misidentifying the root cause of an upset affects troubleshooting efficiency rather than core throughput (relevance 6), leading to repeated upsets and lost time but rarely irreversible harm (severity 6).
rows.sat_rat: Alarm floods and ambiguous symptoms frequently mislead diagnosis, and diagnostic tooling rarely pinpoints root cause reliably. Root-cause misdiagnosis prolongs troubleshooting and extends downtime. Correctly identifying the true cause of an upset is a deeply expert-dependent task that few technicians master. Chasing the wrong cause wastes labor, spare parts and lost production.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of the technician encountering residual pressure or hazardous liquid during inspection
rows.plain: Prevent trapped pressure or dangerous liquid from surprising or reaching the technician during inspection. This helps keep the inspection safe.
rows.imp_rat: A technician encountering residual pressure or hazardous liquid during inspection is an inspection-phase safety error, moderately central; the resulting injury can be severe, keeping severity high.
rows.sat_rat: Standard verification-before-access practice handles most cases, but inspection access to poorly-drained or complex assemblies still yields surprises. Verifying zero pressure and drained lines before inspection adds a short, planned delay. Knowing where residual pressure or liquid can hide during an inspection relies on familiarity with the specific plant layout. The instruments and drain provisions needed are inexpensive and widely available.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the compounding vessel cannot hold a parameter within its proposed range under normal load
rows.plain: Make sure the mixing container can keep an important setting within its intended range during normal operation. This helps the process stay under control.
rows.imp_rat: A vessel that cannot hold a parameter in range under normal load is fundamentally unfit; relevant with high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Vessels are usually qualified against their ranges, so inability to hold a parameter under normal load is uncommon. If the vessel cannot hold a parameter, batches stall or fail, adding recovery time. Anticipating vessel capability shortfalls needs engineering assessment. Capability shortfalls cause failed batches and material waste.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required safety interlocks omitted from the periodic test scope
rows.plain: Make sure every required safety safeguard is included in the regular testing, so none are left unchecked.
rows.imp_rat: Omitting required safety interlocks from the periodic test scope is relevant to verified safety-system readiness (relevance); an untested interlock may silently fail when needed, allowing hazardous escalation (notable severity).
rows.sat_rat: Formal preventive maintenance schedules and SIS management systems capture most interlocks, but incomplete registers still let some fall out of the periodic test scope. Building complete interlock test scopes takes time but is done during planned shutdowns. Identifying every required safety interlock relies on engineering expertise and complete documentation that varies by site. Comprehensive testing adds labor and downtime cost, tempting scope reduction.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which processing input introduction occurs outside the required process window
rows.plain: Make sure materials are added during the required part of the process, not too early or too late. This helps the process produce the intended result.
rows.imp_rat: Introducing input outside the required process window affects product conformance (relevance 7), typically yielding off-spec batches that can often be reworked or rejected (severity 6).
rows.sat_rat: Sequenced dosing and phase interlocks handle most cases, but window violations still occur where addition is manual or poorly instrumented. Timing of input introduction is often interlocked to process phase, minimizing mistimed additions. Manual additions still depend on operator timing judgement when automation is absent. Out-of-window additions can spoil a batch, incurring material loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of the same vessel disruption recurring before batch completion
rows.plain: Prevent the same equipment or process problem from happening again before the batch is finished. This helps avoid repeated interruptions and delays.
rows.imp_rat: Recurring vessel disruptions before batch completion threaten completion and consistency; relevant with moderate-high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Without strong in-batch corrective closure, the same disruption recurring before completion remains a real, poorly-controlled risk. Recurring disruptions repeatedly interrupt the batch, cumulatively costing time. Preventing recurrence depends on effective root-cause skill that is not always applied mid-batch. Repeat disruptions waste resources and can jeopardize the batch.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of deviation from the required ingredient charge level in the feed lines and supply tanks
rows.plain: Keep the amount of ingredient entering the supply lines and tanks as close as possible to the required amount, so the process starts with the right quantity.
rows.imp_rat: Deviation from required charge level in feed lines/tanks affects batch accuracy (relevance 8); moderate charge errors typically cause rework rather than catastrophe (severity 6).
rows.sat_rat: Calibrated flow/level instrumentation holds charge deviation tight in most systems, with drift and manual top-ups as residual sources of error. Metered charging to target level is fast once flow and level instruments are set up. Automated level and flow control reduces the operator finesse previously needed to hit charge targets. Flow meters and level transmitters are a standard capital cost that pays back in charge accuracy.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of disagreement among homogeneity results for the same batch
rows.plain: Tests of the same batch should give results that are more alike. This helps show whether the batch is mixed evenly throughout.
rows.imp_rat: Disagreement among homogeneity results creates uncertainty in the release decision; relevant with high severity if a bad result is discounted.
rows.sat_rat: Standardized sampling and assay methods keep result agreement usually acceptable, but method and sampling variability still produce occasional discordance. Resolving disagreements among results requires resampling or retesting, adding delay. Interpreting discordant homogeneity results demands analytical judgement to distinguish real variation from assay error. Repeat testing to reconcile disagreement consumes assay and labor resources.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of drawing the in-process sample from the wrong batch or sampling point
rows.plain: Make sure the sample comes from the correct batch and the correct place, so its test results describe the product being made.
rows.imp_rat: Sampling the wrong batch or point undermines the quality verification tied to batch release; a mis-sourced sample can pass a bad batch or reject a good one, causing significant quality and disposition errors.
rows.sat_rat: Sample labeling and defined sample ports cut wrong-source errors, but manual sampling still allows mix-ups where identification is not enforced. Barcode or dedicated sampling-point identification adds negligible time. Labeling and scanning reduce reliance on operator memory for which batch or port to sample. A mis-sourced sample can invalidate testing and force resampling, a modest recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of liquid feedstock being routed through an incorrect sequence of unit operations
rows.plain: Make sure the liquid material follows the correct series of processing steps, so it is made properly and safely.
rows.imp_rat: Routing feedstock through an incorrect sequence of unit operations degrades product processing (relevance 7), yielding off-spec or improperly treated product usually caught in QC (severity 6).
rows.sat_rat: Batch control systems (e.g., S88 recipes) reliably enforce sequence, though manual or hybrid plants still see occasional sequence errors. Recipe-driven sequencing executes unit-operation order automatically without manual delay. Automated batch sequencing reduces reliance on operators remembering the correct sequence. Wrong sequences can ruin batches, but sequence control largely prevents this.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of bulk-solution degradation from blending beyond the approved endpoint
rows.plain: Avoid mixing the bulk product longer than approved, since this could cause it to break down or lose quality. This protects the product from damage during preparation.
rows.imp_rat: Degradation from over-blending past endpoint reduces quality; relevant with high severity for potency/impurity.
rows.sat_rat: Validated blend endpoints make degradation from overblending rare for characterized products. Defined mix endpoints prevent excessive blending that would otherwise waste time. Recognizing overblending risk relies on validated endpoints rather than operator vigilance, easing skill demand. Overblending degradation would waste an entire batch, but endpoints largely prevent it.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of production-run intervals with material temperature outside the specified processing range
rows.plain: Keep the material within the required temperature range throughout production. This helps the process work correctly and protects the quality of the product.
rows.imp_rat: Material temperature drifting outside the processing range is central to viscous-product quality and flow (relevance 8); excursions degrade quality or throughput but are often correctable within a run (severity 6).
rows.sat_rat: Closed-loop temperature control with logged trends keeps most intervals in spec, with residual excursions during transitions and in poorly-mixed viscous zones. Jacketed vessels and thermal loops respond with lag, but continuous control keeps most intervals in range without waiting on manual action. Automated temperature control removes most operator judgement, though tuning loops for viscous, low-conductivity product still needs expertise. Heating/cooling energy is a major operating cost and out-of-range intervals waste product, but the control equipment itself is standard.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of stored pressure, electrical energy, or agitator motion being released during tank disconnection
rows.plain: Prevent trapped pressure, stored electricity, or movement of the mixer from being released while the tank is disconnected, so workers are not hurt.
rows.imp_rat: Release of stored pressure, electrical, or agitator energy during disconnection is a maintenance-phase hazard, somewhat peripheral to core processing (relevance 6); when it occurs it causes severe crush, burn, or electrocution injuries (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: LOTO is a mature, widely enforced practice that reliably prevents most uncontrolled energy release, with residual failures tied to overlooked sources. Energy-isolation steps add time but are compact compared to the overall disconnection effort. Lock-out/tag-out is a codified competency, though correctly identifying all stored-energy sources still needs a trained eye. Isolation hardware and blinding materials are cheap relative to the job.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of technician exposure to hazardous liquid during fault investigation
rows.plain: Prevent technicians from coming into contact with dangerous liquid while they investigate why the process went wrong.
rows.imp_rat: Technician exposure during fault investigation is a peripheral maintenance situation (relevance moderate); direct hazardous liquid contact carries serious injury consequences (severity high).
rows.sat_rat: LOTO and drain-down before fault access usually prevent exposure, but live troubleshooting under pressure remains a recurring hazard. Isolating and draining before investigation adds time to fault response. Avoiding exposure during investigation depends on technician following isolation and PPE discipline. PPE and containment for investigation are low incremental costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that loss of required utilities interrupts the cycle before completion
rows.plain: Make it less likely that the cleaning cycle stops before it is finished because an essential supply, such as power, water, air, or another service, is lost. This helps ensure the equipment is properly cleaned.
rows.imp_rat: Loss of utilities interrupting the cycle before completion directly halts the batching process central to the CFJ; consequences are aborted or reprocessed batches, typically recoverable but wasteful and disruptive.
rows.sat_rat: Utility monitoring and controller hold/resume logic handle most interruptions gracefully, though a genuine utility loss still commonly voids and repeats the cycle. Utility loss mid-cycle forces a restart, and detecting and recovering adds unplanned time. Diagnosing an interrupted cycle and deciding on safe restart requires operator judgement about cleaning state. An aborted cycle wastes chemicals and water and demands a repeat, imposing real but occasional cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of variation in component dissolution behavior between material lots
rows.plain: Make the way the components dissolve more consistent from one batch of material to another. This helps the product behave predictably each time it is made.
rows.imp_rat: Variation in dissolution behavior between lots challenges endpoint consistency; relevant with moderate-high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Incoming material specs limit but do not eliminate lot-driven dissolution differences, a recurring source of variation. Lot-to-lot dissolution variation forces extended or repeated mixing, adding time. Compensating for material-lot variability relies on experienced adjustment. Lot variability causing failures wastes material and forces rework.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of temperature nonuniformity among regions of the batch during mixing
rows.plain: Keep the temperature as even as possible throughout the mixture while it is being mixed. This helps ensure that all parts of the product are treated the same way.
rows.imp_rat: Temperature nonuniformity during mixing affects dissolution rate and local degradation; moderately central with moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Jacketed heating with agitation gives fairly uniform temperature, but gradients near the wall and center still occur. Verifying thermal uniformity across regions takes multi-probe monitoring and settling time. Diagnosing regional temperature gradients requires thermal and mixing expertise. Hot or cold spots can degrade material or impair dissolution, wasting product.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of personnel exposure incidents while handling hazardous residues and contaminated materials
rows.plain: Reduce the number of incidents in which workers are exposed to dangerous leftover substances or contaminated materials. This helps protect people while waste is being handled and discarded.
rows.imp_rat: Exposure incidents while handling hazardous residues and contaminated materials are a support/handling concern rather than central to producing product (moderate relevance), but they carry serious injury and compliance consequences (high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Mature waste-handling and PPE regimes limit incidents, but repeated manual handling of varied contaminated materials leaves a steady residual exposure rate. Handling and segregating contaminated materials adds handling time throughout teardown. Correct classification and handling of hazardous residues requires trained hazmat personnel. Licensed waste handling, packaging and disposal impose significant recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of mismatch between mixing and thermal requirements across compounding stages
rows.plain: Make the mixing and heating or cooling needs fit together across all preparation steps. This helps the product be made consistently without one step interfering with another.
rows.imp_rat: Mismatch between mixing and thermal requirements across stages can leave a stage under-served; relevant with moderate-high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Stage-to-stage mismatches are common where multi-step processes are specified with single-condition assumptions. Reconciling mixing and thermal needs across stages adds coordination time. Balancing competing stage-by-stage requirements demands integrated process expertise. Mismatches force compromise equipment or added utilities cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that foundation bearing capacity is below the fully loaded tank requirement
rows.plain: The floor support should be strong enough to hold the tank when it is full and fully equipped. A weak support could crack, sink, or fail.
rows.imp_rat: Inadequate foundation bearing capacity is a precondition for safe loaded operation but is a one-time siting concern (relevance 6); if it fails the tank tips or ruptures with severe consequences (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Mature codified practice (API B, geotechnical standards) makes bearing-capacity failures uncommon where proper investigation is done, though undetected subsurface variation still causes occasional settlement problems. Geotechnical investigation and foundation design add weeks to project schedules but are routinely planned into preparation timelines. Requires licensed geotechnical and structural engineers, whose expertise is standard on tank projects of any scale. Soil borings, load calculations and foundation engineering are established budgeted line items, not surprise costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of temperature variation across the target-volume batch during mixing
rows.plain: Keep the temperature as even as possible throughout the full amount being mixed. This helps the mixture turn out consistently.
rows.imp_rat: Temperature variation across the batch affects uniform dissolution and degradation; moderately central with moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Circulating agitation with jacket control usually holds uniform temperature, though large volumes retain minor gradients. Confirming batch-wide temperature uniformity adds monitoring time. Judging acceptable uniformity across the batch takes process experience. Temperature spread risks off-spec dissolution and material loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that mechanical or utility failure makes the vessel unavailable during a scheduled batch
rows.plain: Prevent equipment or service failures from making the vessel unavailable during a planned batch. This helps avoid delays and keeps production capacity available.
rows.imp_rat: Mechanical/utility failure making the vessel unavailable disrupts batch execution and hold windows; relevant with moderate-high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Preventive maintenance programs make scheduled-batch vessel availability generally dependable, with occasional unexpected failures. An unavailable vessel forces rescheduling that consumes shift time. Preventing failure depends on maintenance expertise rather than overseer action. Vessel unavailability idles capacity and disrupts throughput, a significant cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of contamination entering the skid product-contact path during storage or shutdown
rows.plain: Make it less likely that dirt or other harmful substances enter the product-contact path while the processing unit is stored or shut down. This helps keep the path clean before production resumes.
rows.imp_rat: Contamination entering the product-contact path during storage or shutdown affects readiness rather than active processing, so relevance is moderate; undetected ingress during idle periods can spoil the next batch, a serious but often catchable consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Validated storage and shutdown procedures with clean-hold time limits handle most cases, though breaches from expired hold times or seal degradation still occur. Establishing hold, blanketing, and dry-state storage adds setup time but is routine and scheduled. Following storage and shutdown protocols is procedurally driven and needs moderate rather than expert skill. Nitrogen blanketing, desiccants, and clean hold consumables cost money but are modest against contamination losses.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of introducing an incorrect additive into the tank
rows.plain: The person should make sure the correct substance is added to the tank. Adding the wrong one could affect the process or damage the equipment.
rows.imp_rat: An incorrect additive corrupts the formulation the tank is processing, moderately central to the job (relevance 6); it usually results in an off-spec or reactive batch requiring disposal (severity 6).
rows.sat_rat: Recipe-enforced dispensing and barcode checks reduce errors where present, but manual additive handling still leaves a persistent error gap. Additive verification adds minimal time when scanning is used but slows manual weigh-and-charge steps. Correct additive selection leans on operator diligence and recipe-following where no automated dispensing exists. Verification tools are cheap, but a wrong additive can ruin a batch and waste material.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of an unplanned compounding-vessel failure stopping batch execution
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that the mixing vessel fails unexpectedly and stops the batch. This helps the batch finish on schedule.
rows.imp_rat: Unplanned vessel failure stopping execution can abort or spoil the batch; relevant with moderate-high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Maintained jacketed vessels are generally robust, so mid-batch failures are uncommon but not eliminated. An unplanned failure stops execution and forces time-consuming recovery. Diagnosis and repair fall to maintenance specialists, not the overseer. Batch stoppage from equipment failure risks material loss and lost capacity.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of personnel exposure to residual hazardous material during skid decommissioning
rows.plain: Prevent workers from coming into contact with leftover dangerous material while the processing equipment is taken out of service. This protects their health during dismantling.
rows.imp_rat: Personnel exposure to residual hazardous material during decommissioning is a safety concern peripheral to the primary processing job, so relevance is moderate; however the consequence of worker chemical/biological exposure is severe, warranting a high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Mature industrial-hygiene practice, air monitoring and swabbing substantially reduce exposure events yet unmapped dead-legs and surprise residuals still cause occasional incidents. Established LOTO and decommissioning permit workflows add time but are routine and rarely the bottleneck. Competent EHS staff and hazmat-trained technicians are standard on decommissioning crews, though judgement on unexpected residuals still varies. PPE, monitoring and containment consume real budget but are absorbed as normal project cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of injury from stored energy or pressurized liquid released during decommissioning
rows.plain: Prevent injury from trapped pressure or stored force being released while the equipment is taken out of service. This makes dismantling safer.
rows.imp_rat: Stored-energy or pressurized-liquid release during decommissioning is peripheral to the day-to-day batching job but can cause serious operator injury when it occurs, giving low relevance yet high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Mature LOTO and stored-energy-release standards make most incidents avoidable, though surprise residual pressure in dead legs still causes uncommon but serious failures. Lockout/tagout and energy-isolation procedures add scheduled time but are a routine part of decommissioning workflows. Executing a stored-energy isolation reliably still depends on trained, certified technicians who understand the specific system's hydraulics and pneumatics. The gauges, blanks, and isolation hardware are inexpensive relative to the injury and downtime an incident would cause.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of personnel or environmental exposure to hazardous batch residues during decommissioning
rows.plain: Prevent people or the environment from being exposed to dangerous leftover material from a production batch while the system is taken out of service. This supports safe cleanup and disposal.
rows.imp_rat: Personnel/environmental exposure during decommissioning is peripheral to the ongoing batch-preparation job (moderate relevance), but exposure to hazardous residues can cause serious health harm (high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Mature permit-to-work, cleaning-verification, and containment practices substantially reduce exposure events, yet residual pockets and unexpected residues still cause occasional incidents. Decommissioning plans allocate substantial flush-and-purge time and this is generally scheduled adequately, though rushed teardowns still cut corners. Established HAZWOPER-trained crews and residue-handling procedures exist but competent decommissioning specialists are not always on hand at every plant. PPE, containment, and disposal of hazardous residues add real cost but are budgeted as a routine part of any regulated shutdown.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of air entrained in the bulk solution during blending
rows.plain: Keep as much air as possible out of the liquid while it is being mixed. Too much trapped air can affect the quality of the mixture.
rows.imp_rat: Air entrainment affects blend quality and downstream fill accuracy; moderately relevant, with moderate severity as it can be corrected by hold/deaeration.
rows.sat_rat: Air entrainment is a chronic, hard-to-see problem with only partial mitigation via baffles and speed limits, so outcomes vary. Slow addition and speed control add time but the operator can pace charging to limit air pull-in. Avoiding vortex entrainment relies heavily on operator experience in judging speed and blade submergence. Entrained air rarely destroys batch material but can force re-blend or extended holds that waste time and energy.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of quality technician exposure to hazardous process liquid during sampling
rows.plain: Protect the quality worker from touching or being splashed by harmful liquid while taking a sample. The sampling method should keep the person safely separated from the liquid.
rows.imp_rat: Quality technician exposure during sampling is a supporting activity to the CFJ (relevance moderate); exposure to hazardous liquid can cause serious personal harm (severity high).
rows.sat_rat: Closed sampling systems and PPE make technician exposure to process liquid uncommon, with open ports the residual risk. Closed sampling adds minor time but is not the constraint. Closed and needleless samplers reduce reliance on technician technique for exposure protection. Closed sampling devices and PPE are established per-sample costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of batch-to-batch variation in the product temperature trajectory under equivalent conditions
rows.plain: Keep the product's temperature changing in the same way from one batch to the next when conditions are the same. This makes the process more predictable and consistent.
rows.imp_rat: Batch-to-batch variation in temperature trajectory affects dissolution/degradation consistency; moderately relevant with moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Modern jacket control gives repeatable trajectories, though fouling and load changes cause some drift. Verifying temperature-trajectory repeatability adds monitoring time. Interpreting trajectory variation uses standard control-engineering skill. Trajectory variation risks inconsistent quality and energy waste.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an interrupted update leaves plant control components on inconsistent versions
rows.plain: Make sure all parts of the plant control system end up with matching software if an update is interrupted. Mismatched software can make the parts work together incorrectly.
rows.imp_rat: An interrupted update leaving control components on inconsistent versions is a control-integrity risk (moderate relevance) that can cause unpredictable plant behavior and downtime when it occurs (moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Few plants have robust transactional update or automatic rollback, so an interruption often leaves components on mismatched versions with no clean recovery. Recovering from an aborted update and reconciling versions can consume a large unplanned chunk of the outage. Diagnosing and safely rolling back inconsistent controller versions requires deep firmware and system expertise rarely held on-site. A half-completed update can leave the plant unable to restart, incurring major lost-production cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of deviation in blend temperature from the specified profile
rows.plain: Keep the mixture's temperature close to the required temperature pattern over time. This helps keep the product consistent from one production group to the next.
rows.imp_rat: Blend temperature deviation from profile affects dissolution and degradation; moderately relevant with moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Programmed jacket control follows the profile reliably in most modern systems. Correcting deviations from the temperature profile adds control settling time. Following a specified thermal profile requires modest skill with automated support. Profile deviations can impair dissolution and waste material.
