rows.stmt: Minimize the time between detection of an unacceptable tank condition and onset of quenching
rows.plain: Start cooling or stopping the tank contents as soon as an unsafe condition is found. This helps limit the effects of the problem.
rows.imp_rat: Delay between detecting an unacceptable condition and quenching affects how fast an event is arrested, moderately central to safe operation (relevance 6); slow response lets an excursion escalate to serious harm (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Where safety-instrumented quench systems exist they respond consistently, but many installations still rely on slower operator intervention. Automated quench/emergency-cooling interlocks trigger within seconds of a detected excursion, but manual-response designs still lag. Emergency response depends on operator recognition and correct action where automated quench is absent, demanding trained vigilance. Quench systems and emergency cooling add capital cost but are justified for reactive processes.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of deviation between the processing input added and the required dose
rows.plain: Add an amount of processing material that is as close as possible to the amount required. This helps the process work as intended.
rows.imp_rat: Dosing deviation from the required dose affects formulation accuracy (relevance 7); consequences are off-spec product, potentially serious for active ingredients but often within rework tolerance (severity 6).
rows.sat_rat: Flow meters, load cells and metering pumps deliver accurate doses reliably, though calibration drift causes occasional deviation. Automated metering delivers doses quickly without slowing the run. Calibrated dosing systems reduce dependence on operator precision. Dosing hardware is standard and dose errors waste reagent, but modern metering keeps waste low.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of critical process and qualification requirements omitted from vessel-option evaluation
rows.plain: Leave out as few important process needs and required suitability checks as possible when comparing container options. This helps ensure the chosen container can support the work and meet all required checks.
rows.imp_rat: Omitted requirements from vessel-option evaluation is an upstream completeness gap; moderate relevance with meaningful severity.
rows.sat_rat: URS and risk-assessment practices capture most requirements, yet critical ones still slip through in complex vessel evaluations. Requirement capture upfront lengthens the evaluation start but prevents later rework. Identifying all critical process and qualification requirements needs cross-functional SME input that is hard to fully assemble. Requirement omission is cheap to prevent with review time versus costly to remediate later.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of uncertainty in the candidate plant's allowable viscosity, temperature, pressure, and chemical exposure limits
rows.plain: Clearly determine the limits of the possible plant for thickness, temperature, pressure, and contact with chemicals. Knowing these limits helps people judge whether the plant can safely handle the process.
rows.imp_rat: Uncertainty in a candidate plant's operating limits is a planning/design-stage concern (relevance 6); getting it wrong risks selecting unsuitable equipment or exceeding limits, but is usually caught before commissioning (severity 6).
rows.sat_rat: Datasheets provide allowable limits but often with vague caveats or missing combined-condition data, so residual uncertainty commonly persists into the decision. Obtaining clarified limit values from suppliers involves back-and-forth that adds moderate delay. Interpreting and challenging vendor-stated limits needs engineering judgement that is only partially available on typical buying teams. Getting documented limits is generally a low-cost information exchange with suppliers.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of trapped pressure or hazardous liquid being released unexpectedly during draining
rows.plain: Prevent trapped pressure or dangerous liquid from escaping suddenly while the system is being emptied. This protects people and nearby equipment.
rows.imp_rat: Unexpected release of trapped pressure or hazardous liquid during draining is a maintenance-safety issue peripheral to batch production, yet serious operator injury makes severity high.
rows.sat_rat: Even careful draining leaves pockets of trapped pressure or liquid in complex batching manifolds, so unexpected release remains a recurring hazard. Controlled draining with venting takes time but is a standard sequenced operation. Recognizing where trapped pressure can hide in low points and dead legs requires system-specific expertise that is not universally present. Draining hardware and temporary containment are low-cost items.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of liquid product lost before the release is contained
rows.plain: Limit how much liquid product escapes before the spill is stopped. This reduces waste and the risk of harm.
rows.imp_rat: Reducing product lost before a release is contained matters to containment effectiveness (moderate relevance) and drives measurable financial and environmental loss when uncontrolled (moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Layered containment is mature and generally works, though the exact volume lost before containment varies widely with release location and detection speed. Containment systems like bunds and drip trays activate immediately on release, but the time to detect and stop the source can still let significant volume escape before containment fully engages. Operators rely on trained response procedures and spill drills, yet effective loss minimization still depends heavily on quick human judgement in the first moments. Secondary containment, sumps and catch basins are standard and recover most spilled product, but pre-containment losses of high-value liquid are a recurring accepted cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of safety interlock tests performed under conditions that differ from the specified abnormal operating conditions
rows.plain: Perform safety tests under the same unusual but specified conditions they are meant to handle. This shows whether the protections will work during a real problem.
rows.imp_rat: Testing interlocks under conditions differing from specified abnormal conditions moderately undermines confidence in the safety system (relevance); flawed tests can leave latent interlock failures that only surface during a real emergency (moderate-to-high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Because true abnormal conditions are hard to reproduce, many interlock tests use surrogate conditions that do not validate the actual trip, leaving a recurring hidden gap. Simulating true abnormal conditions takes significant setup time, so testers often shortcut with proxy conditions. Recreating genuine abnormal operating states demands high engineering skill and understanding of the interlock logic that is often scarce. Full abnormal-condition testing can require equipment downtime and rigs, so cost pressure pushes toward cheaper surrogate tests.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of contamination introduced by utilities contacting the vessel or batch
rows.plain: Keep contamination from entering through support services that touch the container or its contents, such as air or other connected supplies. This protects the batch from being spoiled by those connections.
rows.imp_rat: Contamination from utilities contacting the batch is a low-mixing-relevance but high-severity sterility risk.
rows.sat_rat: Qualified clean utilities, WFI loops and filtered gases make utility-borne contamination uncommon under GMP. Utility qualification and checks are done upfront and rarely delay compounding. Ensuring clean utilities depends on engineering controls rather than operator skill. Contamination from utilities is rare but a single event can scrap an entire batch.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a batch is transferred to filling before final disposition authorization is communicated
rows.plain: Do not send a batch to the filling step until the final approval to release it has been communicated. This prevents work from starting on a batch that has not yet been approved.
rows.imp_rat: Transferring to filling before disposition authorization is a procedural/quality-gate breach rather than a mixing-mechanics failure; severe compliance consequence if defective batch escapes.
rows.sat_rat: Electronic batch record status locks and quarantine holds in modern MES make premature transfer rare, though manual paper systems leave residual gaps. Disposition-to-transfer sequencing is gated by hold procedures, so the timing control is generally enforced rather than a delay issue. Following the release-before-transfer rule requires procedural discipline more than specialized skill. Physical and electronic quarantine controls carry modest cost that is well absorbed.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of unexpected release of residual energy, pressure, steam, or process fluid during utility disconnection
rows.plain: Prevent leftover energy, pressure, steam, or process liquid from escaping unexpectedly while service connections are removed. This protects the people doing the work.
rows.imp_rat: Unexpected release of residual energy or fluid during utility disconnection is a maintenance safety issue peripheral to batch preparation, but consequences include severe personnel injury or burns.
rows.sat_rat: Line-breaking and de-energization procedures are mature but multi-utility interconnections and trapped fluid still produce unexpected releases at a nontrivial rate. Draining, venting, and depressurizing utilities adds bounded time that is normally planned into disconnection work. Recognizing all residual energy sources across steam, pressure, and fluid systems demands experienced judgement that is not uniformly available. Bleed-down and drainage equipment is modest cost though captured process fluid may require special disposal.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of stored electrical energy, pressure, or heat being released during disconnection
rows.plain: Prevent stored electricity, pressure, or heat from being released suddenly while connections are being removed. This protects the person disconnecting them.
rows.imp_rat: Stored energy release during disconnection is a maintenance-phase safety concern peripheral to the batch-preparation job, but the consequence is potentially catastrophic injury to personnel.
rows.sat_rat: Mature stored-energy isolation practice (LOTO, bleed-down, discharge) is one of the most reliably executed safety procedures, though missed stored energy still occasionally slips through. Energy-isolation and de-energization steps are built into disconnection sequences and rarely dominate the schedule. Lockout/tagout is a widely trained, standardized competency present at essentially every industrial site. LOTO hardware and verification tools are inexpensive relative to the overall decommissioning budget.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that batch results are evaluated against an incorrect or obsolete specification
rows.plain: Make it unlikely that a batch is judged using quality limits that are wrong or no longer current. This matters because the decision about the batch could be incorrect.
rows.imp_rat: Evaluating batch results against an obsolete specification is an administrative/QA error, moderately central to confirming the job (relevance), and severe because it can pass out-of-spec product or reject good product (notable severity).
rows.sat_rat: Validated electronic batch records with enforced revision control make evaluation against an obsolete spec uncommon at maturely automated sites, yet residual failures persist wherever change control is manual. Recipe-management and MES systems retrieve the effective specification version quickly at batch start, though manual paper-based operations still add lookup delay. Modern recipe control removes most operator judgement about which spec applies, but paper or spreadsheet-driven sites still rely on QA vigilance to catch obsolete revisions. Electronic version control is inexpensive to maintain once deployed, though the underlying MES/document-control system carries a real capital cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required qualification evidence missing from each vessel option's evaluation package
rows.plain: Make sure the evaluation file for each possible container includes all required supporting evidence. This helps people compare the options and choose one with enough proof behind it.
rows.imp_rat: Missing qualification evidence weakens the vessel selection package; moderately relevant with notable compliance severity.
rows.sat_rat: Structured qualification checklists catch most required evidence, though missing items still surface late in review. Compiling the full qualification package is slow when vendor documents arrive piecemeal. Knowing which evidence is required draws on GMP-experienced reviewers using checklists that partly offset expert scarcity. Chasing missing documents adds coordination cost but no major material outlay.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of uncontrolled collapse of partially dismantled structures, tanks, or piping
rows.plain: Prevent partly taken-apart buildings, tanks, or pipes from suddenly falling down. This protects workers from being struck or trapped.
rows.imp_rat: Uncontrolled collapse of partially dismantled structures is a decommissioning hazard largely disconnected from the production job (low relevance), but a structural collapse can cause fatalities and major asset destruction (very high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Structural demolition plans and supports are standard practice, yet unexpected collapses during teardown remain a known and consequential failure mode. Engineered sequential dismantling with temporary supports takes longer than uncontrolled demolition. Preventing collapse hinges on structural engineering judgement of load paths in partially removed structures, a scarce and demanding skill. Temporary works, cranes and rigging add substantial cost to controlled dismantling.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of the suspended tank moving uncontrollably during removal
rows.plain: Prevent the hanging tank from swinging or moving unexpectedly while it is being removed. This helps protect people and nearby equipment.
rows.imp_rat: Uncontrolled movement of a suspended tank during removal is a decommissioning/rigging event peripheral to processing (relevance 4); a dropped tank causes fatal crush injuries and major damage (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Standard rigging practice controls most lifts, but shifting centre of gravity from residual contents and awkward geometry still cause uncontrolled movement. Rigging and lift planning add setup time before the tank can be moved. Safe suspended-load control depends on qualified riggers and a lift plan, a genuinely skill-intensive task. Cranes, spreader bars and certified rigging represent significant mobilized cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of sequence steps lacking entry conditions for temperature, pH, agitation, or prior dissolution
rows.plain: Make sure each step says what temperature, acidity, mixing, and earlier dissolving must be in place before it starts. This matters because missing starting conditions can make the process inconsistent or unsafe.
rows.imp_rat: Sequence steps lacking entry conditions creates process ambiguity that can cause precipitation or incomplete dissolution; moderately relevant with moderate-high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Modern electronic recipes embed entry conditions, but legacy or manual procedures still have gaps. Missing entry conditions cause mid-sequence stops while conditions are checked, adding time. Recognizing missing entry conditions requires process understanding to author complete recipes. Steps run without proper entry conditions can spoil batches and waste material.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that batch disposition applies obsolete homogeneity or GMP acceptance criteria
rows.plain: Avoid deciding whether a batch is acceptable using old or conflicting rules about how evenly mixed it must be and what quality standards it must meet. This matters because the decision may be wrong or out of date.
rows.imp_rat: Applying obsolete acceptance criteria at disposition is a compliance/governance error; low mixing relevance but severe if non-conforming batch released.
rows.sat_rat: Controlled document systems reduce use of superseded criteria, but version-control lapses and manual reference to outdated specs remain a recurring reliability gap. Criteria updates propagate slowly through document control, creating windows where obsolete specs could be applied. Judging which acceptance criteria version applies requires trained quality staff familiar with change control. Document-management systems add overhead but are standard infrastructure.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that accessing product-contact areas during inspection introduces contamination
rows.plain: Avoid bringing dirt or germs onto surfaces that touch the product while checking for wear and leaks. This helps keep the product safe.
rows.imp_rat: Accessing product-contact areas during inspection introducing contamination moderately affects batch cleanliness (relevance) and is severe because inspection is routine and could repeatedly seed contamination (notable severity).
rows.sat_rat: Because inspection is minimally invasive, contamination introduction is less likely and better controlled by routine practice than during repair. Inspection access is relatively quick since it is less invasive than repair, adding limited time. Inspecting product-contact areas without contaminating them needs care but less finesse than active repair, so skill demand is moderate. Inspection consumes fewer consumables than repair, keeping cost impact modest.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of releasing pressurized or hazardous residual material during disconnection
rows.plain: Make it unlikely that trapped pressure or dangerous leftover material will escape when equipment connections are opened. This matters because the release could injure people or contaminate the area.
rows.imp_rat: Releasing pressurized/hazardous residual material during disconnection is peripheral to producing the batch but poses serious injury and chemical-exposure risk when it happens.
rows.sat_rat: Standard bleed-and-verify practice catches most cases, but residual material trapped behind valves or in flexible hoses still releases unexpectedly with real frequency. Depressurizing and verifying lines before breaking connections is a known step that adds predictable time to disconnection. Correctly identifying which connections still hold pressure or hazardous material demands experienced judgement that is often in short supply on demolition crews. Containment mats, spill kits, and capture equipment are modest costs against the liability of an uncontrolled release.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of gas entrainment or foaming caused by candidate agitation under specified operating conditions
rows.plain: Choose mixing equipment that does not pull too much air into the liquid or create too much foam under the expected working conditions. This helps keep the mixture consistent and usable.
rows.imp_rat: Gas entrainment/foaming from candidate agitation affects blend and fill accuracy; moderately relevant with moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Foaming under agitation is hard to predict at spec time and often surfaces only in production. Testing for foaming under operating conditions adds trial time. Predicting foaming behavior needs surfactant and interfacial expertise. Foaming causes fill errors and product loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of imported critical process parameters that differ from source records
rows.plain: Reduce the number of important process settings that change when they are copied into the system from the original records. This helps ensure the process runs with the intended settings.
rows.imp_rat: Imported critical process parameters differing from source records can silently drive a batch off-recipe (severity 6), and parameter fidelity is moderately central to correct batch setup (relevance 5). Capped at 6.0.
rows.sat_rat: Import interfaces frequently lack automated source-to-target verification, so differing critical parameters go undetected relatively often, making this poorly served. Reconciling imported critical parameters against source records is a slow manual cross-check. Detecting mismatches between imported values and source records demands sustained attention and data literacy. Manual reconciliation is labor-intensive and undetected mismatches carry high batch-failure cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an undiscovered process or utility connection remains live during disconnection
rows.plain: Avoid leaving an unnoticed pipe, cable, or other connection carrying pressure, electricity, or material when it is disconnected. This helps prevent unexpected releases or injuries.
rows.imp_rat: A live undiscovered utility connection during disconnection is peripheral to making a batch but can cause severe injury or uncontrolled release when energy is unexpectedly present.
rows.sat_rat: Outdated or missing P&IDs mean undiscovered live connections are a chronic and dangerous surprise despite isolation checklists. Tracing and verifying every utility connection is slow and often compressed under demolition schedule pressure. Finding undocumented or hidden live connections depends heavily on scarce institutional knowledge and skilled tracing, especially on old systems. Line-tracing tools and verification labor cost little compared to the consequences of a missed live connection.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of personnel exposure incidents during decontamination and remediation of the plant site
rows.plain: Reduce the number of times workers are exposed to harmful substances while cleaning up and restoring the plant site. This helps protect their health.
rows.imp_rat: Personnel exposure incidents during site decontamination and remediation are outside the core production loop (low relevance), yet each incident can involve serious hazardous-substance harm and liability (high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Regulated remediation practice reduces incidents but unmapped legacy contamination and human error keep exposure incidents recurring. Site decontamination and remediation is a protracted phase that extends the disposal timeline. Remediation planning and execution depend on specialist environmental and safety expertise. Site remediation is one of the larger cost items in disposal, especially for contaminated ground.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of a hazardous energy release or chemical exposure during system inspection
rows.plain: Avoid releasing stored energy or exposing people to chemicals while inspecting the system. This helps protect workers during the final safety review.
rows.imp_rat: Hazardous energy release or chemical exposure during inspection is a safety event tangential to producing batches (lower relevance) but severe, with potential for serious injury (high severity).
rows.sat_rat: When isolation is confirmed first, inspection incidents are rare, but overlooked stored energy occasionally catches inspectors. Verifying de-energization and drain-down before inspection adds a short, standard hold. Safe inspection of retired systems draws on the same LOTO and hazard-recognition skills routinely trained for. Inspection is low-material-cost and mostly labor.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of exposing the completed batch to contamination during discharge
rows.plain: Avoid exposing the finished batch to dirt or germs while removing it from the equipment. This helps keep the completed product safe.
rows.imp_rat: Exposing the completed batch to contamination at discharge is central to delivering a usable batch (relevance 6); it risks batch loss and potential downstream safety issues, though detectable via QC (severity 6). Capped at 6.0.
rows.sat_rat: Standard containment reduces exposure but environment, gasketing and manual handling still allow occasional contamination events. Contained discharge systems operate without meaningful added duration. Avoiding exposure depends on operators executing containment and hygiene procedures correctly. Exposing a finished batch to contamination risks its full value, so containment investment pays off.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree to which critical process conditions exceed validated limits after a mid-cycle adjustment
rows.plain: Keep important process conditions from going beyond their approved safe ranges after making a change during the process. This helps prevent the batch from becoming unsafe or unusable.
rows.imp_rat: Critical process conditions exceeding validated limits after a mid-cycle adjustment directly undermines the batch job (relevance) and can invalidate or spoil the batch with regulatory consequences (severity), but capped at 6.0 per the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Rate limiting and control constraints curb overshoot after adjustments, but manual or aggressive changes can still push conditions beyond validated limits. Recovering to validated limits after a mid-cycle change takes finite settling time that control tuning only partly shortens. Judging the size and safety of a mid-cycle adjustment still depends heavily on operator process expertise. Advanced control that bounds post-adjustment excursions costs more than the basic loops many plants run.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of batches with gas entrainment or foaming above the specified limit during agitation
rows.plain: Reduce how often batches contain more trapped gas or foam than the allowed limit while being stirred. This matters because too much gas or foam can make the process unreliable.
rows.imp_rat: Batches exceeding gas-entrainment/foaming limits affect blend and fill; moderately relevant with moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Foaming excursion frequency remains a recurring, imperfectly controlled defect. Tracking foaming frequency across batches requires ongoing monitoring. Diagnosing foaming excursions needs specialized interfacial knowledge. Foaming batches above limit cause repeated losses.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of gas entrained in the batch during agitation
rows.plain: Reduce the amount of air or other gas mixed into the batch while it is being stirred. This matters because trapped gas can affect the batch and how well the process works.
rows.imp_rat: Gas entrained during agitation affects blend quality and fill accuracy; moderately relevant with moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Gas entrainment is inconsistently controlled and often only discovered during production runs. Measuring entrained gas adds specific test time. Controlling entrainment through impeller/level design needs mixing expertise. Entrained gas causes fill and assay errors, wasting product.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of worker exposure to residual pharmaceutical, cleaning, or decontamination substances during dismantling
rows.plain: Make it very unlikely that workers touch or breathe leftover medicines, cleaning products, or cleaning chemicals while taking the plant apart. This helps protect their health.
rows.imp_rat: Worker exposure to pharmaceutical or decontamination substances during dismantling is an end-of-life activity peripheral to ongoing production (low relevance), but exposure to potent or toxic residues can cause serious health harm (high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Decontamination protocols are established but the diversity of pharma and cleaning residues leaves recurring exposure gaps during dismantling. Decontamination before dismantling adds a distinct time phase to the schedule. Recognizing and handling pharmaceutical and decon substance residues needs specialist hazmat competence. PPE, decon agents and specialist handling carry ongoing cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of contamination entering the completed batch during discharge
rows.plain: Make it very unlikely that germs or other unwanted material get into the finished batch while it is being emptied. This helps keep the batch safe and usable.
rows.imp_rat: Contamination entering the completed batch during discharge directly threatens the CFJ of producing an acceptable batch (relevance 6); a contaminated finished batch can require full rejection, a costly but bounded consequence (severity 6). Capped at 6.0.
rows.sat_rat: Closed transfer and CIP practices reduce discharge contamination, but open transfers, seals and cleaning lapses leave a recurring risk. Closed discharge paths add little time while limiting contamination exposure. Proper discharge technique and cleaning still depend on trained operators following procedure. Contamination during discharge can scrap a completed batch, a high-value loss that closed transfer helps avoid.

rows.source_job: Test plant containment integrity after assembly
rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of the assembled containment boundary omitted from integrity testing
rows.plain: Test the entire assembled barrier that keeps harmful material contained, without leaving out any part. This helps confirm that the barrier will not leak.
rows.imp_rat: Omitting portions of the containment boundary from integrity testing leaves undetected leak paths, which is relevant to safe processing; severity is elevated because untested gaps can allow later escape of viscous or hazardous product. Kept at or below the 5.5 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Coverage tracking is often manual and incomplete, so untested sections of the containment boundary regularly slip through, making this a poorly served recurring pain. Comprehensive integrity testing of every weld and joint is time-consuming, so schedule pressure tempts crews to sample rather than test the full boundary. Knowing which portions of a complex containment boundary have and have not been tested requires diligent tracking that stretches inspection expertise. Full-coverage testing costs inspection labour and NDE resources, incentivizing partial coverage that saves money but leaves gaps.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of foreign residue introduced into sterile product-contact circuits during integrity testing
rows.plain: Keep foreign material from getting into the sealed, germ-free parts that touch the product while checking for leaks or damage. This helps keep the product clean and safe.
rows.imp_rat: Foreign residue introduced into sterile product-contact circuits during integrity testing directly threatens product sterility (fairly central to a sterile-liquid plant), and contamination consequences can be significant (moderate-high severity), though it is a periodic testing activity.
rows.sat_rat: Introduced residue during integrity testing is a genuine recurring risk that current procedures only partly control. Flushing and re-verification after integrity testing extends the commissioning timeline noticeably. Preventing test-media residue in product-contact circuits demands experienced technicians who understand aseptic technique. Sterile test media, filters and rinse validation consume resources but are expected costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that adverse formulation and system-material interactions remain undetected
rows.plain: Make it very unlikely that harmful reactions between the mixture and the equipment materials go unnoticed. This helps prevent damage, contamination, or changes to the product.
rows.imp_rat: Undetected formulation/material interactions can degrade product quality and introduce contamination, moderately central to safe batch prep (relevance 5); consequences can be serious if leachables reach the batch, but the error is often caught earlier in material qualification (severity 6).
rows.sat_rat: Testing exists but coverage is uneven and many interactions only reveal themselves in later production, so undetected interactions remain a real recurring risk. Formulation-material interaction studies are time-consuming and often compressed or deferred during acquisition, leaving detection incomplete. Detecting subtle adverse interactions demands advanced analytical and formulation expertise that is scarce and inconsistently applied. Full E&L and interaction testing is expensive, so it is frequently scoped down, raising the chance of undetected issues.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of product residue released into drains or surrounding work areas during purging and cleaning
rows.plain: Release as little leftover product as possible into drains or nearby work areas while equipment is being flushed and cleaned. This reduces waste and keeps the workplace cleaner and safer.
rows.imp_rat: Product residue released into drains during purging/cleaning is a cleaning-phase concern peripheral to production output (relevance moderate); consequences are mainly environmental and rework burdens (severity moderate).
rows.sat_rat: Effluent capture and treatment systems are standard, though incidental releases to drains and work areas during purging still recur. Capturing and treating purge effluent adds throughput time to cleaning cycles. Minimizing residue release is largely procedural and does not demand rare expertise. The waste product and contaminated wash water represent material loss and treatment cost, which is the primary loss when this fails.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of actual wear, corrosion, or leaks that remain undetected during inspection
rows.plain: Find as many real signs of wear, rust, or leaks as possible during inspection. This helps prevent hidden damage from causing a failure later.
rows.imp_rat: Undetected wear, corrosion, or leaks are moderately central to reliable processing, and if they progress they can cause failures or contamination (moderate-high severity), but this is a detection-gap error rather than the primary function.
rows.sat_rat: Visual inspection and some NDT catch many defects, but a meaningful fraction of actual wear, corrosion and leaks routinely remains undetected until failure. Detailed inspection for wear, corrosion and leaks is time-consuming during limited outages. Reliable detection depends on inspector expertise and the right NDT tools, and skill gaps cause misses. Undetected leaks and corrosion cause costly failures; comprehensive NDT adds cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of agitator-speed drift outside the approved range during steady mixing
rows.plain: Keep the mixing speed within the approved range while the mixture is being stirred steadily. Stable speed helps keep the mixture consistent.
rows.imp_rat: Agitator-speed drift outside range during steady mixing affects blend consistency; moderately relevant with moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Closed-loop VFD control holds agitator speed steadily within range in most installations. Speed monitoring is continuous and adds no time. Maintaining steady speed is automated on VFD systems, needing little operator skill. Minor speed drift seldom causes material loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of mixing conditions outside defined ranges during compounding
rows.plain: Keep the mixing process within its required limits while ingredients are being combined. This helps ensure the finished mixture is evenly blended.
rows.imp_rat: Duration of mixing conditions outside defined ranges risks incomplete dissolution/degradation; moderately relevant with moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Automated setpoint control keeps mixing conditions in range most of the time, with brief transients. Duration spent with mixing conditions out of range prolongs the effective process time. Keeping conditions in range depends on control skill plus alarm response. Out-of-range mixing time wastes energy and may spoil product.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of vessel contents remaining outside the prescribed dissolution temperature range
rows.plain: Keep the contents of the vessel within the required temperature range for dissolving them. This helps avoid slowing down the process or affecting the result.
rows.imp_rat: Duration outside dissolution temperature range slows the job and risks incomplete dissolution; moderately relevant, moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Automated control returns temperature to range fairly consistently, keeping out-of-range periods short in most batches. Time spent outside the dissolution temperature range directly lengthens the productive dissolution window and total batch time. Minimizing out-of-range duration relies on operator responsiveness and thermal control skill. Extended out-of-range time wastes energy and can spoil material.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of unstable batch temperature oscillations during mixing
rows.plain: Keep the batch temperature from repeatedly rising and falling outside a steady pattern during mixing. This helps the batch remain consistent.
rows.imp_rat: Unstable temperature oscillations during mixing affect dissolution consistency and degradation; moderately relevant with moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Properly tuned control loops rarely oscillate, but poorly tuned or aggressive setpoints still produce hunting. Oscillations force operators to wait for stabilization, adding time. Diagnosing and damping oscillations requires control-loop expertise. Oscillation-driven excursions can waste energy and risk material.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of inaccessible or poorly drained regions in the vessel geometry
rows.plain: Make the vessel's shape easier to clean and drain completely, with fewer hard-to-reach or water-trapping areas. This helps prevent residue from being left behind.
rows.imp_rat: Inaccessible/poorly drained regions cause dead zones and cleaning residues; moderately relevant with moderate-high severity for uniformity and contamination.
rows.sat_rat: Standardized hygienic-design rules, sanitary fittings, and spray-ball coverage testing reliably minimize inaccessible regions, making this one of the better-served needs though sensor ports and complex internals still create residual risk. Riboflavin coverage tests and drainage checks are routine but add commissioning time when dead legs are discovered late. Recognizing and eliminating dead legs, crevices, and poor slopes is a mature hygienic-design competency embedded in ASME BPE practice and vendor design reviews. Hygienic-design geometry costs more in fabrication but avoids far larger downstream cleaning-failure costs, so it is treated as standard spend.

rows.source_job: Dismantle the skid into handling assemblies
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of stored electrical, pressure, or mechanical energy releasing unexpectedly during skid dismantling
rows.plain: Prevent trapped electricity, pressure, or movement from being released unexpectedly while the equipment frame is taken apart. This helps protect workers during dismantling.
rows.imp_rat: Unexpected release of stored energy during dismantling is a maintenance-safety error only peripherally tied to the production job, so relevance is low; the consequence of injury from released pressure or electrical energy is severe.
rows.sat_rat: Standard LOTO and stored-energy dissipation procedures are mature and effective, though residual pneumatic/hydraulic pressure and capacitive charge occasionally surprise crews. Energy-isolation verification is a defined pre-work step that adds modest time. Stored-energy identification relies on trained personnel following LOTO, a well-drilled competency. Isolation hardware and lockout devices are low-cost consumables.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the repaired area fails containment acceptance criteria
rows.plain: Make sure the repaired area keeps unwanted material contained and passes the required checks. A failed repair could allow leaks or contamination.
rows.imp_rat: A repaired area failing containment acceptance criteria is a repair-specific concern, less central to routine operation (relevance 5); if the repair fails in service a breach and release results (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Codified repair-and-retest workflows make acceptance failures uncommon though not rare enough to be trivial. Post-repair testing and re-inspection extend the outage but are a standard closeout step. Repair quality and its verification lean on qualified welders and inspectors following welding procedure specifications. Rework when a repair fails acceptance is expensive, but qualified procedures keep failure rates modest.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of critical tank functions operating outside approved acceptance limits during verification
rows.plain: Make sure important tank functions stay within their approved limits while the tank installation is being checked. This confirms the tank is working safely and correctly.
rows.imp_rat: Critical functions operating outside approved acceptance limits during verification is a qualification check moderately tied to readiness (relevance 6); missing it releases a marginally compliant tank to service (severity 6).
rows.sat_rat: Formal commissioning checklists and acceptance protocols catch most out-of-limit functions, though incomplete test coverage and rushed handovers still let some through. Acceptance verification against limits is a defined commissioning step that adds bounded time. Requires commissioning engineers who understand acceptance criteria, a skill that is common but not trivial. Verification testing consumes labor and utilities but prevents costlier downstream failures.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of unexpected energization of electrical circuits while the technician accesses batching-system connections
rows.plain: Prevent electricity from unexpectedly turning on while a technician reaches the machine's mixing-system connections. This helps protect the technician from electric shock or injury.
rows.imp_rat: Unexpected energization of electrical circuits during access is largely a lockout/tagout safety concern outside the batching function, but electrocution risk makes the consequence potentially fatal.
rows.sat_rat: Electrical LOTO is among the most mature and enforced safety practices, so unexpected energization is rare where the procedure is followed. Electrical LOTO is a standard, time-boxed step that rarely causes meaningful schedule disruption. Verifying absence of voltage and applying electrical isolation is codified work that trained/qualified electricians perform routinely. Lockout devices and voltage testers are cheap and universally available.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of releasing residual pressure or process liquid when opening a pump, valve, or connection
rows.plain: Prevent trapped pressure or process liquid from suddenly escaping when opening a pump, valve, or connection. This helps protect the worker and prevent spills.
rows.imp_rat: Residual pressure/liquid release when opening a pump or valve is a maintenance-safety concern only loosely tied to producing a batch, but exposure to hazardous fluid or pressure can seriously injure a technician.
rows.sat_rat: Depressurization practice is common but trapped residual pressure and liquid between isolation points still surprises technicians with real regularity, keeping this only acceptably served. Draining, venting, and depressurizing before breaking a connection is a familiar step that adds manageable time. Judging whether a line is truly relieved before loosening a fitting depends on technician attentiveness and knowledge of the system's dead legs and trapped volumes. Vent valves and drain points are low-cost, but retrofitted systems often lack them, forcing improvised bleeding.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of selecting a recipe that does not match the scheduled product and batch specification
rows.plain: Choose instructions that match the product and production batch that are scheduled. This helps prevent the wrong product from being made or handled.
rows.imp_rat: Selecting a recipe not matching the scheduled product means the entire batch is made wrong (relevance), producing off-spec product and potential recall exposure (severity); held at the 6.0 ceiling for this batch.
rows.sat_rat: ISA- management with barcode/order-linked selection makes mismatches uncommon where deployed, but manual sites retain a real residual risk. Recipe selection against a scheduled product is fast in modern batch systems where the schedule drives recipe download, adding little delay. MES-to-batch integration means operators no longer need deep recipe knowledge to pick the right one, though older manual-selection plants still lean on operator care. Wrong-recipe selection is rare with automated scheduling, so the cost of scrapped batches from this cause is largely contained.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that hazardous process residues expose personnel during decommissioning
rows.plain: Prevent leftover harmful material from exposing workers while the system is being taken out of service. The system should be made safe before people handle it.
rows.imp_rat: Hazardous residue exposure during decommissioning is peripheral to the CFJ of preparing batches (it occurs at end-of-life), but the consequence when it happens is severe personnel harm.
rows.sat_rat: Standard lockout, flush, and air-monitoring practice catches most exposure risks though inaccessible dead legs and hidden residues create recurring gaps. Residue clearing before personnel entry follows standard hold points that consume predictable time in a shutdown schedule. Handling process residues safely depends on trained operators and industrial hygienists who read the specific hazard profile correctly. Neutralization chemicals, PPE, and monitoring instruments represent a recurring but well-understood expense.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of technician exposure to residual pressure or hazardous fluid while opening the filter housing
rows.plain: Prevent a worker from being exposed to trapped pressure or harmful liquid when opening the filter container. This helps avoid injury or contact with the liquid.
rows.imp_rat: Technician exposure to pressure or hazardous fluid when opening a filter housing is a maintenance-safety error tangential to the batching outcome, but the human-harm consequence is severe when it occurs.
rows.sat_rat: Spray-out of residual pressure or fluid on housing opening remains a recurring nuisance where housings lack integral vents, so protection is inconsistent. Filter-housing venting and draining is quick when a bleed valve is present, but slow and messy when it isn't. Correctly relieving a housing before cracking the lid requires modest technique that most technicians learn but occasionally misapply under time pressure. Vented housing designs cost little more, yet many installed filter housings still lack proper pressure-relief provisions.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of finished-liquid doses falling outside the required quantity
rows.plain: Keep the amount of liquid in each finished dose within the required range. This ensures people receive the intended amount.
rows.imp_rat: Finished-dose quantity out of spec matters for fill accuracy and compliance (relevance 7), but under/overfill is usually a fill-weight rejection rather than a safety catastrophe (severity 5).
rows.sat_rat: Mature filling/checkweighing technology keeps dose quantities within spec very consistently, a well-served need. Automated filling and dosing meet quantity targets without adding cycle time. Checkweighers and volumetric fillers require little operator skill to hold quantity. Overfill wastes product and underfill risks rejects, but calibrated systems keep both low.

rows.stmt: Minimize the time required for the target-volume batch to meet dissolution and homogeneity criteria
rows.plain: Reduce the time needed for a batch of the intended size to dissolve fully and become evenly mixed. This helps the product reach the required quality sooner.
rows.imp_rat: Time to meet dissolution/homogeneity is a throughput concern, moderately relevant to the job's completion; delay consequences are cost/schedule rather than safety.
rows.sat_rat: Fixed validated times give consistent outcomes but are deliberately padded, so true minimum time is seldom achieved. Meeting dissolution and homogeneity in minimum time is the core throughput lever, yet mix times are usually set conservatively long to be safe. Trimming mix time without risking failure demands expert process understanding rarely fully available. Excess mixing time consumes energy and ties up the vessel, adding operating cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of contamination reaching sterile product-contact surfaces during an extended shutdown
rows.plain: Make it as unlikely as possible for contamination to reach surfaces that touch sterile products while the equipment is shut down for a long time. This helps keep the equipment ready for safe use later.
rows.imp_rat: Contamination of sterile product-contact surfaces during extended shutdown risks batch loss and recontamination, giving meaningful severity, but it is a periodic rather than continuous concern for the core job. Capped per batch ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Long idle periods reliably invite contamination and re-cleaning is the usual fallback, so protection during shutdown is only partially assured. Protecting surfaces over an extended shutdown requires ongoing effort like blanketing or capping across the down period. Knowing which surfaces need protection and maintaining it demands experienced sanitary personnel. Inert gas blanketing, sealing and pre-startup re-cleaning add cost that grows with shutdown length.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that updated control components cannot communicate with existing controllers or field devices
rows.plain: Make sure new control parts can exchange information with the older control parts and connected devices. Otherwise, the updated system may not operate correctly.
rows.imp_rat: Updated control components that cannot communicate with existing controllers directly threaten plant control (fairly central relevance), and such incompatibility can halt processing until resolved (moderate-high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Vendor compatibility matrices help, but proprietary and version-specific protocol drift means communication failures still recur unpredictably. Protocol mismatches surface during commissioning and eat hours of troubleshooting before the loop works. Reconciling legacy fieldbus, proprietary protocols, and new controllers requires specialist controls engineers who are hard to source on short notice. Interoperability failures drive spend on gateways, adapters, or unplanned firmware, plus the cost of a stalled restart.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of mixing beyond the planned compounding window before dissolution criteria are met
rows.plain: Prevent mixing from taking longer than the planned preparation period before the mixture dissolves enough. This helps the batch stay on schedule.
rows.imp_rat: Mixing beyond the planned window before dissolution is met is a delay/hold-time concern; moderate relevance and severity.
rows.sat_rat: Conservative fixed times usually reach endpoint but often overshoot, so overrun is common rather than rare. Mixing beyond the planned window before endpoint is met is pure added batch time. Judging when to stop safely depends on operator experience with endpoint signs. Overmixing wastes energy and vessel time and can shear-damage material.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of dissolution beyond the planned compounding interval because of ingredient characteristics
rows.plain: Prevent the mixture from taking longer than planned to dissolve because of the ingredients being used. This helps keep preparation on schedule.
rows.imp_rat: Extended dissolution from ingredient characteristics is a delay concern for throughput and hold time; moderate relevance and severity.
rows.sat_rat: Validated processes make eventual dissolution reliable, but the time overrun itself is the recurring problem. Difficult-to-dissolve ingredients routinely extend dissolution beyond the planned interval, a familiar schedule pressure. Managing slow dissolution relies on operator adjustments (temperature, agitation) grounded in experience. Extended dissolution consumes extra vessel time and energy, tying up capacity.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time required for the batch to reach composition acceptance limits under worst-case intended conditions
rows.plain: Get the batch evenly mixed and within the allowed ingredient ranges as quickly as possible, even under the most difficult expected conditions. This helps ensure every part of the batch has the intended makeup.
rows.imp_rat: Time to reach composition limits under worst case is a delay/throughput concern; moderate relevance and severity.
rows.sat_rat: Blend-time estimation is fairly established, though worst-case conditions introduce prediction uncertainty. Batch cycle time to reach acceptance under worst-case is a well-tracked and optimizable parameter. Estimating worst-case blend time requires moderate mixing expertise. Longer cycles consume utilities and reduce throughput.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of technician exposure to residual process material while opening or draining tanks, lines, and mixing components
rows.plain: Protect technicians from contact with leftover process material when they open or drain tanks, pipes, and mixing equipment.
rows.imp_rat: Technician exposure to residual material while opening tanks and lines is a maintenance-side concern, peripheral to producing the batch (lower relevance), yet consequential for worker safety when it happens (notable severity).
rows.sat_rat: PPE and pre-cleaning reduce exposure substantially, yet residual material in mixing components still surprises technicians when opened. Purging and flushing before opening tanks and lines adds time to the sequence but is expected. Judging when a component is safe to open despite residual film requires experienced technicians who know the process chemistry. PPE and flushing media are ongoing but modest costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of ambiguity in indications that the defined protective state has been reached
rows.plain: Make it clear when the equipment has reached the required safe condition after an emergency stop.
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity in indications that the protective state has been reached is fairly central to confirming a plant is safe, and severity is elevated because misreading a protective-state signal can expose workers to energized or pressurized hazards.
rows.sat_rat: Safety instrumented systems provide defined state feedback, but indication of "safe state reached" is often indirect or ambiguous, leaving recurring doubt. Confirming the protective state is reached is usually quick when clear status indications exist. Judging an ambiguous protective-state indication requires trained interpretation of multiple signals. Uncertainty here risks unsafe operation or unnecessary shutdowns, both costly.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of validated control configurations, recipes, or alarm settings lost or altered during the update
rows.plain: Prevent the update from losing or changing approved machine settings, product instructions, or warning settings.
rows.imp_rat: Losing validated control configurations, recipes, or alarm settings during an update is central to resuming correct operation (moderate relevance) and loss can force full revalidation with significant downtime (moderate-high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Backups and version control are common practice but backup discipline is inconsistent, so altered or lost settings still slip through during updates. Rebuilding lost recipes and alarm settings from scratch adds significant delay to the restart. Restoring validated configurations depends on experts who know which parameters matter, since defaults rarely match the qualified state. Lost validated settings can trigger costly requalification and re-validation campaigns.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of contaminating the sterile product-contact path during consumable replacement
rows.plain: Lower the chance that germs or other unwanted material get into the parts that touch the sterile medicine when replaceable parts are changed. This helps keep the medicine safe to use.
rows.imp_rat: Contaminating the sterile product-contact path during consumable replacement (relevance) can compromise sterility, with real batch-rejection consequences (severity), but held to the ceiling as it ranks below batch 1.
rows.sat_rat: SIP procedures, single-use connectors and barrier systems help, but manual consumable replacement remains a recurring contamination risk that periodically causes batch loss. Aseptic consumable changes require slow, deliberate technique that lengthens the task. Maintaining sterility during changeover depends heavily on operator aseptic technique and constant vigilance, a scarce and error-prone skill. Sterile connectors and gowning consumables add cost but are accepted as necessary.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of powder escaping into the surrounding environment during vessel charging
rows.plain: Keep as much powder as possible from escaping into the surrounding area when it is added to a vessel. This reduces wasted material and helps keep the work area cleaner and safer.
rows.imp_rat: Powder escaping during charging is a yield/loss and operator-exposure concern; moderate relevance and severity, higher for potent compounds.
rows.sat_rat: Open manual charging routinely generates fugitive dust with only partial control by hoods or booths. Careful manual charging to limit dust adds time to the charging step. Dust-free powder addition demands considerable operator technique when open charging. Escaped powder is lost active/excipient plus a cleaning and containment cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required gas-entrainment and foaming limits omitted from mixing requirements
rows.plain: Include all necessary limits for how much gas can be drawn into the mixture and how much foam can form. This helps ensure the mixing instructions produce a usable product.
rows.imp_rat: Omitted gas-entrainment/foaming limits in requirements is a specification gap; moderately relevant with moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Gas-entrainment and foaming constraints are frequently left out of mixing requirements, a common oversight. Adding foaming and gas-entrainment limits to requirements takes extra spec effort. Knowing to specify entrainment limits requires niche awareness often absent. Omitted limits lead to costly foaming rework post-installation.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of air entrained in the preparation under the established mixing conditions
rows.plain: Keep the amount of air mixed into the preparation as low as possible while using the agreed mixing settings. This helps protect the preparation’s intended quality.
rows.imp_rat: Air entrained under established mixing conditions affects blend and fill accuracy; moderately relevant with moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Sub-surface addition and controlled agitation limit entrainment, but vortexing still introduces air in some setups. Waiting for entrained air to disengage before endpoint extends the batch. Avoiding air entrainment depends on operator control of agitation and level. Entrained air can spoil fill accuracy and cause rework cost.

rows.stk: Plant operators, maintenance engineers, reliability engineers
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of early equipment degradation remaining undetected before affecting product quality or equipment function
rows.plain: Find early signs that equipment is wearing out before the problem affects product quality or equipment operation. Early detection allows action to be taken sooner.
rows.imp_rat: Undetected early equipment degradation before it affects product or function is relevant to reliability, and severity is somewhat elevated because it can propagate into product-quality or breakdown events if missed. Capped at the batch limit.
rows.sat_rat: Where condition monitoring is deployed it catches degradation early, but coverage is patchy so much early wear still goes undetected until it affects output. Condition monitoring provides early warning without adding downtime once installed. Interpreting vibration, thermal or wear trends to catch early degradation requires reliability-engineering skill that is unevenly available. Predictive-maintenance instrumentation and analysis carry upfront and ongoing cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of resuming the batch cycle from an incorrect or unauthorized recovery point
rows.plain: Make sure an interrupted batch is restarted only from the correct, approved point. This helps prevent skipped steps or unauthorized changes.
rows.imp_rat: Resuming from an incorrect/unauthorized recovery point can corrupt an entire batch and bypass controls (severity 6), and correct recovery is fairly central to batch integrity (relevance 5). Capped at 6.0.
rows.sat_rat: State-based procedural control and authorization checks help, but recovery logic is a known weak area where incorrect resumes still occur. Selecting a recovery point can be quick, but verifying it is correct may slow resumption. Choosing the correct restart step often requires operator or engineer judgement about batch state. Resuming from a wrong point can ruin a partially completed batch, an expensive error.

rows.stmt: Minimize the time between product viscosity leaving specification and operator recognition
rows.plain: Shorten the time between the medicine becoming too thick or too thin and the worker noticing it. This allows the problem to be corrected sooner.
rows.imp_rat: Delay between viscosity leaving spec and operator recognition prolongs off-spec production, relevant to quality control, with moderate severity since the longer the lag the more material is affected. Held at the cap.
rows.sat_rat: Inline viscometry exists but is not universally deployed on viscous lines, so the gap between excursion and recognition remains large and inconsistent. Lab viscosity results often lag by minutes to hours, so recognition of an out-of-spec condition is frequently delayed well past onset. Absent inline sensors, operators must infer viscosity drift from indirect signs, a skill-dependent and error-prone read. Delayed recognition means more off-spec product is made before correction, inflating waste cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of agitation-speed overshoot above the approved upper limit during startup or adjustment
rows.plain: Keep the mixing speed from rising above its approved upper limit when starting or adjusting the equipment. This helps keep the product under controlled conditions.
rows.imp_rat: Agitation-speed overshoot at startup can shear-damage or foam; moderately relevant with moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: VFDs with soft-start and speed limits make overshoot uncommon on modern drives but manual units still drift. Controlled ramping adds negligible time. Preventing overshoot leans on operator attention with manual controls or on drive tuning. Overshoot rarely wastes material but can stress equipment.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time for batch temperature to return to the validated range after additions or agitation changes
rows.plain: Help the product temperature return to its approved range quickly after ingredients are added or the mixture is stirred differently. This keeps the process stable and consistent.
rows.imp_rat: Recovery time to validated temperature range is a throughput/stability delay; moderate relevance and severity.
rows.sat_rat: Feedback jacket control brings temperature back consistently, though lag in large volumes lengthens recovery. Recovery time back to range after additions or agitation changes is a direct delay in the batch. Speeding recovery depends on control tuning skill more than daily operator finesse. Longer recovery consumes energy and vessel occupancy cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of deviation in mixing intensity from the batch target
rows.plain: Keep the strength of mixing close to the planned level for the batch. This helps make sure the ingredients are mixed as intended.
rows.imp_rat: Mixing-intensity deviation from target affects blend consistency; moderately relevant with moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Variable-speed drives hold set agitation consistently, so intensity deviation is uncommon. Correcting mixing-intensity deviation is quick but confirming it can add checks. Setting and holding target agitation is straightforward with calibrated drives. Wrong intensity risks poor blend or shear damage, wasting material.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of deviation in total mixing exposure from the batch target
rows.plain: Keep the total time and strength of mixing close to the planned amount for the batch. This helps produce a consistent result from one batch to the next.
rows.imp_rat: Deviation in total mixing exposure from target affects degradation and dissolution completeness; moderately relevant with moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Recipe-driven timers control total exposure reliably in most systems. Total mixing exposure is timer-controlled, so deviations are caught but may extend batches. Tracking cumulative exposure needs little skill with automated timers. Excess exposure wastes energy; too little risks rework cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of required circuit volume occupied by trapped air after priming
rows.plain: Make sure there is as little trapped air as possible in the fluid lines after they are filled. This helps the lines work as intended and prevents air from disrupting the process.
rows.imp_rat: Trapped air as a percentage of circuit volume after priming is a measurable defect affecting fill accuracy, moderately relevant, but typically corrected during setup so consequences are limited.
rows.sat_rat: Full-fill is generally achieved but hidden air pockets in valves and dead-legs remain a persistent low-level reliability concern. Achieving full liquid-fill circuit volume requires deliberate priming and purge time. Recognizing and clearing residual air volume relies on operator experience with the specific circuit. Air-elimination hardware adds minor cost relative to batch value at risk.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that microbial contamination remains on product-contact surfaces after tank sanitization
rows.plain: Make sure germs are removed from all surfaces that touch the product after the tank is sanitized. This helps keep the product safe.
rows.imp_rat: Residual microbial contamination after sanitization directly threatens product integrity (severity), and is fairly central to clean tank processing (relevance), but the batch ranking holds it at or below 5.5.
rows.sat_rat: Validated sanitization plus micro monitoring reliably removes contamination, though occasional biofilm survives in dead legs. CIP/SIP cycles and swab/rinse verification are time-defined and integrated into turnaround schedules. Validated cleaning recipes reduce operator judgement, though sampling technique still affects results. Chemicals, water and validation testing carry recurring cost but are budgeted operating expense.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of material temperature excursions outside the required process range
rows.plain: Keep the material from staying outside the required temperature range for long. This helps protect the material and keep the process consistent.
rows.imp_rat: Temperature excursions outside the required process range affect product quality, moderately central to the processing job (relevance 6); consequences are typically scrapped batches rather than safety harm (severity 5).
rows.sat_rat: Closed-loop temperature control is mature and widely deployed, keeping most excursions short, though large thermal masses and upsets still cause lingering deviations. PID temperature control with jacket/coil heating and cooling corrects excursions fairly quickly, though thermal lag on large volumes prolongs them. Tuned control loops handle routine regulation with little operator input, but tuning and disturbance recovery still need process knowledge. Heating/cooling utilities and control instrumentation are standard operating costs, with energy waste during excursions.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of selecting a tank setpoint outside approved batch limits
rows.plain: Make it unlikely that a tank’s target setting will be chosen outside the approved limits for the batch. Staying within those limits helps keep the batch safe and acceptable.
rows.imp_rat: Selecting a setpoint outside approved batch limits is moderately relevant to correct tank processing, and severity is meaningful since an out-of-limit setpoint can compromise the batch, but the ranking caps it below higher-priority contamination errors.
rows.sat_rat: Range-checked recipe systems and DCS limit-clamping are mature and widely used, making out-of-limit setpoints uncommon where deployed. Recipe-driven setpoints load automatically in modern control systems, saving entry time versus manual configuration. Electronic recipe management removes most of the judgement in choosing setpoints, though manual-entry systems still depend on the operator. Recipe management software has a cost but prevents expensive out-of-spec batches.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of process material escaping through the tank boundary during use
rows.plain: Reduce the amount of production material that leaks out of the tank while it is being used. This prevents waste and helps protect people and the surrounding area.
rows.imp_rat: Process material escaping through the tank boundary is relevant to containment and carries notable severity through material loss and exposure, yet it ranks below the top-batch items and stays capped at 5.5.
rows.sat_rat: Proven sealing and gasketing practice contains most material, though seal degradation produces recurring minor losses. Leaks are usually found during rounds or by alarms rather than instantly, so some loss accrues before detection. Sealing technology (gaskets, mechanical seals) is standardized, requiring modest maintenance skill rather than expert judgement. Good gaskets and seal designs limit fugitive losses, but slow weeps and seal wear still waste material over time.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unresolved deviations from sanitary, safety, or regulatory standards in the proposed plant
rows.plain: The proposed facility should have as few unresolved problems as possible with cleanliness, safety, and legal requirements. This helps ensure it can operate safely and be approved to run.
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved deviations from sanitary/safety/regulatory standards in the proposed plant are moderately central to a compliant facility, and carrying them forward risks compliance exposure (moderate-high severity), though this is a design-review error caught before operation.
rows.sat_rat: Mature checklist-driven regulatory review catches most deviations, though unresolved items still commonly linger to late stages, leaving meaningful gaps. Standards gap-reviews are structured but take time when many clauses must be checked against the proposal. Resolving sanitary/safety/regulatory deviations requires qualified compliance and validation staff whose availability limits throughput. Compliance review labour is a real cost but a routine, budgeted part of plant acquisition.

rows.source_job: Connect process piping between unit operations
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of leakage from sanitary joints under specified operating conditions
rows.plain: Make it unlikely that liquid will leak from the cleanable pipe connections while the system is operating. Tight connections help prevent spills and contamination.
rows.imp_rat: Leakage from sanitary joints under operating conditions directly threatens containment and product integrity in viscous processing, so it is moderately central; when it occurs it causes product loss and contamination risk but is usually contained. Capped at 5.5 mean per batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Hygienic joint standards, standardized fittings and mandatory pressure testing make sanitary leak control a mature, widely deployed practice with only occasional residual failures. Sanitary joint assembly and pressure/hold leak tests follow well-drilled routines that add predictable but modest time to preparation. Correct gasket seating and clamp torque on hygienic fittings depend on trained fitters, and sloppy craftsmanship is the usual cause of leaks. Leaks caught late waste product and cleaning chemicals, but standard tri-clamp hardware and test rigs keep per-joint costs low.

rows.source_job: Commission the plant with acceptance testing
rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which trial-run results fall outside defined plant specifications during acceptance testing
rows.plain: Keep the plant’s trial results within the agreed limits during final testing. This helps show that the plant is working as expected before it is accepted.
rows.imp_rat: Trial-run results falling outside plant specs during acceptance testing matters for commissioning readiness (relevance 6), but failures are caught before production so consequences are contained rework/delay (severity 5).
rows.sat_rat: Formal acceptance-test procedures and factory pre-qualification catch most issues, but viscous media behave unpredictably at scale so first-pass out-of-spec results remain a common recurring gap. Acceptance trials are scheduled milestones with established protocols, so time-wise the practice is orderly though reruns after out-of-spec results extend the timeline. Interpreting trial-run deviations against plant specifications leans heavily on scarce commissioning engineers whose judgement determines whether a result is a real fault or noise. Out-of-spec trial runs consume raw viscous product, utilities and labour on repeat runs, a recurring cost the status quo only partly contains.

rows.source_job: Regulate material flow rate through plant
rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of material-flow deviation from the specified production target
rows.plain: Keep the amount of material flowing through the plant as close as possible to the planned production amount. This helps production stay steady and on target.
rows.imp_rat: Material-flow deviation from the production target is fairly central to hitting throughput and quality in a processing plant, but consequences are typically recoverable yield/quality drift rather than catastrophic. Held within the 5.5 cap.
rows.sat_rat: Coriolis/mag meters and control loops keep flow close to target, with residual deviation from viscosity changes, air entrainment and meter fouling on thick product. Flow controllers correct deviations continuously so target flow is restored without operator delay in most cases. Automated flow control handles routine correction, but viscous, shear-thinning fluids need skilled calibration to hold the target. Flow deviation wastes material and energy, though metering and control hardware to limit it is standard capital.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of lubricant entering the sterile product environment during component lubrication
rows.plain: Make it unlikely that oil or grease used on moving parts will get into the clean product area. This helps keep the product free from unwanted material.
rows.imp_rat: Lubricant entering the sterile product environment during lubrication is a genuine contamination pathway with serious consequences for product safety when it occurs, though it is a fairly localized, controllable event. Kept at or under 5.5 mean.
rows.sat_rat: Sealed-for-life bearings and food-grade lubricants reduce risk substantially, but manual re-lubrication still occasionally introduces contamination. Careful lubrication with containment adds minor time to component servicing. Preventing lubricant migration into the sterile zone relies on technician discipline and correct use of food/pharma-grade practices. Food-grade lubricants and sealed lubrication systems add modest cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of residual viscous product remaining in vessels and pipework after evacuation
rows.plain: Leave as little thick product as possible in the tanks and pipes after they are emptied. This helps reduce waste and makes the equipment easier to clean or reuse.
rows.imp_rat: Residual product left in vessels and pipework after evacuation is primarily a yield/waste issue (relevance 6) with cost and minor cleaning impact rather than safety consequences (severity 5).
rows.sat_rat: Pigging, heating, and gravity draining recover most product but dead legs, valves, and heat exchangers reliably retain a stubborn fraction. Draining and pigging viscous product from vessels and long pipe runs is slow because high viscosity resists gravity flow. Operators know how to drain and pig systems, though optimizing evacuation of sticky product needs process familiarity. Residual viscous product left behind is lost material and becomes disposal cost, so incomplete evacuation directly wastes money.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of starting the batch cycle with a configuration that does not match the intended product
rows.plain: Make it unlikely that a batch will be started with settings or equipment choices for the wrong product. Matching the setup to the intended product helps prevent wasted or incorrect batches.
rows.imp_rat: Starting the cycle with a configuration mismatched to the intended product corrupts the whole run (relevance) and yields unusable or unsafe product (severity), though ranked below the prior batch so capped at 6.0.
rows.sat_rat: Equipment-module state checks and permissive interlocks catch many mismatches, yet configuration drift and manual overrides leave a meaningful gap. Pre-start configuration verification is generally quick when checklists or interlocks are automated. Startup interlocks reduce the need for a skilled operator to verify every configuration item manually, but manual mode still demands attention. A mismatched configuration at start can waste a whole batch, though pre-start interlocks keep such losses infrequent.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that cleaning or sanitizing fluid escapes from the batching system during the cycle
rows.plain: Make sure cleaning or sanitizing liquid does not leak out of the mixing equipment during cleaning. This protects people, equipment, and the surrounding area.
rows.imp_rat: Cleaning or sanitizing fluid escaping during the cycle is a containment issue peripheral to producing the batch itself; consequences involve operator chemical exposure and environmental release, which are serious but occasional.
rows.sat_rat: Gasketed hygienic connections and leak monitoring contain fluid well in most cycles, with occasional seal failures the residual issue. Leak checks and pressure holds add modest time but are integrated into cycle startup. Detecting and sealing leak paths before a cycle is routine mechanical work within most technicians' abilities. Lost cleaning fluid and cleanup from escapes cost money but incidents are intermittent.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of deviation from validated cleaning limits during the cycle
rows.plain: Keep the cleaning process within the approved cleanliness limits from start to finish. This helps ensure the equipment is cleaned properly every time.
rows.imp_rat: Deviation from validated cleaning limits during the cycle is relevant to hygienic product output, and severity is elevated because out-of-limit cleaning risks contamination and product safety failures.
rows.sat_rat: Automated CIP with parameter monitoring and alarms keeps cleaning within validated limits reliably, with deviations flagged for investigation.
