Validated cycles run to fixed parameters, so time impact is predictable and controlled. Automated cleaning parameter control offloads most judgement from operators onto the validated recipe. Running validated cycles consumes standard utilities and agents with little waste.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of calibrated process instruments left with measurement error outside approved tolerance
rows.plain: As few measuring tools as possible should still give inaccurate readings after being checked and adjusted. Accurate readings help people control the process safely and correctly.
rows.imp_rat: Calibrated instruments left outside approved tolerance directly undermine accurate process control (fairly central relevance) and can produce off-spec product batches (moderate-high severity), though it is one of many contributing factors.
rows.sat_rat: Mature calibration programs with defined tolerances and periodic checks keep most instruments in spec, though drift between intervals still occasionally exceeds tolerance. Scheduled calibration routines are time-boxed and generally completed within the planned maintenance window. Trained calibration technicians following documented procedures handle most instruments without needing rare expertise. Calibration labor and reference gear cost is routine and budgeted, with rare instruments needing outside service.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of an external command being mapped to the wrong critical plant function
rows.plain: Prevent an outside instruction from triggering the wrong important plant action. This helps avoid unsafe or damaging operations.
rows.imp_rat: An external command mapped to the wrong critical plant function could trigger a dangerous mis-operation, so severity is meaningful, but the ranking caps it and it is an edge-case control-integration error rather than core to routine processing.
rows.sat_rat: Point-to-point loop checks catch most mismappings, but wrong-function mapping still slips through occasionally. Verifying command-to-function mapping is a bounded I/O checkout activity during commissioning. Loop checks and mapping verification rely on skilled automation engineers being thorough. Mapping verification labor is a modest commissioning cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of deviation between configured trip thresholds and approved safety limits
rows.plain: Keep the settings that trigger automatic safety actions aligned with the approved safety limits. This helps the system react at the right time when conditions become unsafe.
rows.imp_rat: Deviation between configured trip thresholds and approved safety limits is moderately relevant to safe operation, and mis-set trips can allow hazardous conditions (moderate-high severity), but this is a one-time configuration check.
rows.sat_rat: Formal safety-limit verification and independent checks make configured trip deviations uncommon at handover. Trip-threshold configuration and verification is a bounded checklist activity that rarely dominates the schedule. Safety-limit setup follows documented safety requirements that qualified instrument engineers apply routinely. Verification labor is modest relative to overall commissioning cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of an unintended pressurized release exposing personnel during integrity testing
rows.plain: Prevent a test from suddenly releasing pressure and injuring nearby people. This helps make sure the equipment is checked without creating a danger.
rows.imp_rat: An unintended pressurized release exposing personnel during integrity testing carries genuine injury potential, so severity is notable, but it is confined to a specific test activity and is not central to the ongoing processing job.
rows.sat_rat: Established test protocols and exclusion zones make unintended pressurized releases exposing personnel uncommon. Safe pressure-test procedures with staged ramps add controlled time to integrity testing. Pressure testing follows codified safety procedures that qualified crews apply routinely. Barriers, exclusion zones and relief provisions are standard, modest safety costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of contaminants entering clean utility paths during connection work
rows.plain: Prevent dirt or other unwanted material from getting into clean supply lines while connections are being made. This helps keep those supplies suitable for use.
rows.imp_rat: Contaminants entering clean utility paths during connection work could compromise product quality, giving moderate relevance, but it occurs during a bounded connection activity and is mitigated by post-work flushing/verification.
rows.sat_rat: Established capping, purging and pressure-hold practices catch most ingress, but transient exposure during the physical connection still occasionally lets contaminants in. Purging, blowing down and capping utility lines before connection adds meaningful time but is a routine part of preparation scheduling. Clean connection technique depends on trained fitters following hygienic procedures, and lapses in vigilance during hookup remain a recurring source of ingress. Caps, purge gas and flush media are inexpensive relative to the rework cost of a later contamination finding.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree to which batch conditions exceed validated limits after a parameter adjustment
rows.plain: After changing the settings, keep the batch within its approved operating limits. This helps prevent the product from being made under unsuitable conditions.
rows.imp_rat: Batch conditions exceeding validated limits after a parameter adjustment is a near-duplicate of item 1 but phrased more generally, so it is central to safe batch execution (relevance) with high consequences of scrapped or non-compliant product (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Control constraints limit excursions after parameter changes in many cases, but overshoot and interacting variables still cause validated-limit breaches after adjustments. Settling back within validated limits after a parameter change consumes transient time the control system cannot fully eliminate. Setting adjustments that won't breach validated limits relies on operator understanding of process response. Constraint-aware or model-predictive control that prevents excursions is a higher-cost upgrade not universally deployed.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the selected recipe is inconsistent with batch requirements
rows.plain: Make sure the chosen production instructions match what the batch requires. This helps ensure the batch is made correctly.
rows.imp_rat: A recipe inconsistent with batch requirements is a broader restatement of item 3, still central to producing correct product (relevance) with serious waste and compliance consequences when it occurs (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Version-controlled master recipes and parameter bounds catch most inconsistencies, though partial or ad hoc recipe edits remain a residual failure mode. Consistency checks between the recipe and batch requirements run instantly in configured systems with little added time. Validated master recipes offload consistency judgement from operators, reducing reliance on expert review each run. Catching an inconsistent recipe before start avoids costly reprocessing, keeping typical waste modest.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of accepted batch quantities outside validated processing limits
rows.plain: As few production batches as possible should be accepted when their amounts were made outside the approved operating range. This helps keep each batch consistent and acceptable.
rows.imp_rat: Accepting batch quantities outside validated processing limits directly affects whether the batch is compliant (relevance) and can produce unsafe or unusable product (severity), capped at the batch ceiling of 6.0.
rows.sat_rat: Range-checking on batch size is a mature, standard feature, though limits are sometimes mis-set or bypassed for special runs. Quantity limit validation is enforced in real time by the control system without slowing acceptance. Configured min/max limits remove the need for operators to know validated ranges by memory. Rejecting out-of-limit quantities before processing prevents costly off-spec batches.

rows.source_job: Recalibrate dosing system after component change
rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of dosing-output deviation from the specified target after recalibration
rows.plain: After replacing a part and readjusting the system, make sure it delivers amounts close to the intended target. This helps prevent too much or too little material from being given.
rows.imp_rat: Dosing-output deviation from target after recalibration goes to the heart of accurate batch composition (relevance 6); it produces out-of-spec batches, a correctable but costly outcome (severity 5).
rows.sat_rat: Standardized calibration against traceable references reliably brings dosing output close to target, a mature and effective practice. Recalibration with reference standards is a routine, reasonably quick procedure. Calibration technicians follow well-defined SOPs, keeping skill demand moderate and manageable. Reference standards and calibration labor are modest recurring costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of residual product remaining in process lines, valves, or vessels after flushing
rows.plain: Leave as little product as possible in the pipes, valves, and tanks after they are emptied and flushed. This helps prevent waste and unwanted mixing with the next product.
rows.imp_rat: Residual product left in lines, valves, or vessels after flushing risks contaminating the next batch (relevance) with potentially dangerous cross-contamination outcomes (severity), held to the 6.0 cap.
rows.sat_rat: Sloped, drainable designs and validated flushes remove most residual product, though hold-up in valves and dead legs remains a known residual issue. Flushing and draining sequences are validated and timed, adding predictable duration. Drainability depends more on system design than operator skill, reducing dependence on expertise. Flush media and product loss to drain are recurring but quantified costs.

rows.stk: Operators, sanitation/hygiene technicians, quality assurance personnel.
rows.source_job: Clean, sanitize, and decontaminate product-contact surfaces
rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of product-contact surface area with detectable product residue after cleaning
rows.plain: After cleaning, as little product residue as possible should remain on surfaces that touch the product. This helps prevent leftover material from affecting the next production run.
rows.imp_rat: Residual product residue on contact surfaces after cleaning is a cross-contamination and safety error somewhat adjacent to batch preparation itself (relevance) but with severe food/pharma safety and recall consequences (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Cleaning validation with TOC/visual and swab checks is a mature practice that keeps residue low on most surfaces, with occasional hard-to-reach failures. Validated cleaning cycles plus rinse sampling take fixed, predictable time within the service window. Residue swabbing and TOC analysis require trained analysts but the cleaning itself is automated and repeatable. Cleaning agents, water, and analytical testing are routine, budgeted costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of residual hazardous or process material remaining in the system at handover
rows.plain: Make it unlikely that harmful or process-related material will still be inside the equipment when it is handed over. Removing what remains helps protect the next people who use or service it.
rows.imp_rat: Residual hazardous/process material at handover is a real cross-contamination and safety hazard (severity 6), moderately central since it concerns changeover rather than the core batch run (relevance 5).
rows.sat_rat: Residual material in dead legs, filters, and instrument lines remains a common finding at handover despite CIP and visual checks. Thorough cleaning-in-place and manual clearing cycles are time-consuming and often compressed under handover deadlines. Verifying full material removal from complex piping and vessels requires skilled inspection that is frequently inadequate. Cleaning agents, water, and waste treatment add cost, incentivizing minimal effort that leaves residue behind.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of plant sections unintentionally left flowing, energized, or pressurized after shutdown
rows.plain: After production stops, make sure no plant sections are accidentally still carrying material, powered, or under pressure. This helps make the shutdown safe and complete.
rows.imp_rat: Plant sections left flowing, energized, or pressurized after shutdown is moderately relevant to overall job completion and carries elevated severity because residual energy poses direct worker-safety hazards.
rows.sat_rat: Shutdown checklists and sequenced logic catch most cases, but manual verification gaps still leave sections unintentionally energized or pressurized. Verifying every section is de-energized and depressurized after shutdown adds checkout time. Thorough shutdown depends on operator diligence and plant knowledge to catch overlooked sections. Sections left live waste energy and pose damage or safety cost, though usually modest.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the diversion path is unavailable when nonconforming output must be redirected
rows.plain: Make sure a working alternate route is available when output that fails requirements must be redirected. This prevents rejected material from continuing along the normal path.
rows.imp_rat: If the diversion path is unavailable when nonconforming output must be redirected (relevance), bad product can enter the normal stream; consequences can be serious (severity) but are constrained by the ceiling since it ranks below batch 1.
rows.sat_rat: Diversion paths are usually available but their infrequent use means blockages or failed valves can surprise operators when suddenly needed. Diversion valves actuate rapidly on command, so redirection itself is fast when the path is ready. Ensuring the diversion path is maintained and tested requires disciplined maintenance rather than in-the-moment skill. Dedicated diversion equipment is a standing cost that is sometimes under-maintained because it is used infrequently.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of activating the plant with incorrect batch-specific processing parameters
rows.plain: Prevent the plant from starting a batch with the wrong product-specific settings. Correct settings help the batch be made as intended.
rows.imp_rat: Activating the plant with incorrect batch-specific parameters is fairly relevant to producing correct product, with elevated severity because a whole batch can be lost or made unsafe before detection.
rows.sat_rat: Recipe management and batch ID verification make correct-parameter startup highly reliable, with residual risk mainly in manual or paper-based operations. Recipe download at startup is fast and does not delay activation. Automated batch parameter loading reduces reliance on operator memory or manual entry. Activating with wrong batch parameters can ruin a full batch, a significant cost when it happens.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of contaminating the sample during collection or handling
rows.plain: Prevent unwanted material from getting into the sample while it is collected or handled. A clean sample gives a more trustworthy picture of the process.
rows.imp_rat: Sample contamination during collection undermines quality release decisions (moderate relevance to plant's product-integrity job), and a bad sample can pass defective product or reject good product (moderate-high severity), though it is a QC-side error rather than core processing.
rows.sat_rat: Closed sampling systems, sanitized ports and validated SOPs handle most contamination risk, though open-transfer steps remain a recurring weak point. Aseptic sampling procedures and closed sampling ports add setup time but are well embedded in routine practice. Clean sample collection demands trained technique and constant vigilance, so contamination rates rise sharply with less-skilled handlers. Contaminated samples are cheap individually but drive expensive false failures and repeat sampling campaigns.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of residual liquid released outside its designated containment boundary during routing
rows.plain: Keep leftover liquid inside the area meant to contain it while it is being moved. This helps prevent spills or leaks outside the safe area.
rows.imp_rat: Residual liquid released outside containment during routing touches safety and environmental containment (moderate relevance) and uncontrolled release can cause hazards and compliance breaches (higher severity).
rows.sat_rat: Containment kerbs, leak detection and gasket integrity programs make escapes uncommon but not eliminated, especially at flanges, pumps and manual connections. Bunding, drip trays and spill containment allow relatively fast isolation of any escape outside the boundary. Detecting and preventing boundary breaches still relies on operator vigilance and correct connection/valve lineup. Released liquid outside containment means lost product plus cleanup and potential environmental cost, only partly mitigated by drainage recovery.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of residual air trapped in the ingredient feed lines after priming
rows.plain: Remove as much trapped air as possible from the ingredient tubes before use. This helps ensure the right amount of ingredient moves through the tubes.
rows.imp_rat: Residual air trapped after priming affects dosing accuracy and line integrity, moderately relevant to a clean batch start, but its consequences are usually recoverable through re-priming rather than catastrophic.
rows.sat_rat: High-point vents and self-priming pumps clear most trapped air, but pockets in complex piping still recur without careful line design. Venting and deaeration during priming adds time to the start-up sequence. Proper venting depends on operators knowing high-point locations and priming sequence. Vent valves and air-eliminators are low-cost standard fittings.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of product degradation caused by repeated passage through the processing loop
rows.plain: The product should change or lose quality as little as possible when it repeatedly travels through the processing system. This helps preserve the product while it is being made.
rows.imp_rat: Product degradation from repeated passage through the processing loop directly harms viscous product quality, so it is relevant, but it manifests as gradual quality loss rather than an acute failure. Bounded by the 5.5 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: There is little inline monitoring of cumulative shear/thermal damage, so degradation from repeated loop passage is poorly detected and inconsistently controlled. Minimizing recirculation passes can conflict with the time needed to reach spec, forcing a trade-off operators manage imperfectly. Judging how many loop passes cause unacceptable shear or thermal degradation is a specialist formulation and process-engineering call. Degraded product from repeated passage is scrapped or downgraded, a recurring loss with limited mitigation.

rows.stk: Maintenance technicians, plant engineers, electrical technicians
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the repaired plant fault recurs after return to production
rows.plain: After a facility problem is repaired, it should be as unlikely as possible to happen again when production restarts. This helps keep production reliable.
rows.imp_rat: Recurrence of a repaired fault after returning to production erodes reliability and forces repeated downtime, relevant to sustained operation; severity is moderate because it repeats a known, addressable failure. Held at the cap.
rows.sat_rat: Without disciplined root-cause analysis, repairs frequently address symptoms and the fault recurs, a persistent and poorly-controlled failure mode. Confirming a repair truly fixed the fault often requires extended monitoring after return to production. Correct root-cause diagnosis versus symptom repair depends heavily on scarce senior maintenance expertise. Recurring faults drive repeat repairs and lost production, a significant unrecovered cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of contaminating sterile product-contact areas during calibration
rows.plain: Adjusting the measuring tools should be as unlikely as possible to dirty clean surfaces that touch the product. This helps keep the product safe from contamination.
rows.imp_rat: Contaminating sterile product-contact areas during calibration is a contamination-risk event with quality implications, but it is a narrow calibration-activity exposure that is re-sanitized before production.
rows.sat_rat: Calibration inherently involves opening or touching sterile product-contact areas, and current practice leaves a real residual risk that each intervention introduces contamination. Calibrating instruments while maintaining sterility forces slow, deliberate access that stretches the schedule. Breaching a sterile boundary to calibrate without contaminating it requires scarce aseptic-competent metrology technicians and constant attention. Recalibration and re-sanitization after a suspected breach waste consumables and technician hours.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of applying incorrect compensation because the feedstock deviation is mischaracterized
rows.plain: People should be as unlikely as possible to make the wrong adjustment because they misunderstood how the incoming material differs from what was expected. Correct adjustments help keep production on target.
rows.imp_rat: Applying incorrect compensation from a mischaracterized feedstock deviation (relevance) can push product off-spec; consequences are correctable process errors (severity), both held to the cap.
rows.sat_rat: Feedstock characterization is often inferred rather than directly measured, so mischaracterization and consequent incorrect compensation remain fairly frequent. Recognizing a mischaracterized deviation and reversing wrong compensation takes time, extending the upset. Correctly diagnosing feedstock deviations demands significant process expertise, and misdiagnosis leading to wrong compensation is a common expert-dependent failure. Wrong compensation wastes reagents and product and can require full batch rework, a costly error.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of the liquid product processing interruption while locating and correcting the fault
rows.plain: The interruption to liquid production should last as little time as possible while people find and fix the problem. This helps reduce lost production and delays.
rows.imp_rat: Interruption duration while locating and correcting a fault is a downtime/recovery error (moderate relevance to keeping the plant running), with mostly economic consequences (moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Alarm logs and diagnostics help, but intermittent or root-cause-obscure faults still produce unpredictable, extended downtime. Locating and correcting a fault during an interruption is often slow, dominated by manual diagnosis across a complex plant. Fast fault resolution depends heavily on a few experienced operators or maintenance staff whose availability is not guaranteed. Every hour of interruption means lost production and idle plant, so slow fault-finding is directly expensive.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of batch material ejected by foaming or splashing during agitation
rows.plain: Limit how much batch material is thrown out of the equipment by bubbles or splashing during mixing.
rows.imp_rat: Material ejected by foaming/splashing is a yield/waste loss with potency implications; moderate relevance and severity.
rows.sat_rat: Foaming behavior varies by formulation and is only partly controlled by baffles and speed limits. Ramping agitation gently to avoid splash costs some setup time but is manageable. Preventing foam and splash depends on operator finesse with speed and antifoam addition. Ejected material is a direct product loss and foaming can waste significant batch value.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of ambiguity about the process condition associated with an alarm
rows.plain: Make it clear what condition caused an alarm. This helps the person responding know what action to take.
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity about the process condition tied to an alarm slows operator response, which is meaningfully relevant to safe operation, and severity is moderate because delayed or wrong response can escalate an incident though alarms rarely stand alone.
rows.sat_rat: Alarm rationalization practices exist but many plants still suffer floods and generically-worded alarms, leaving condition ambiguity a persistent problem. Ambiguous alarms cost operators time to investigate the actual process condition behind them. Interpreting vague alarms depends heavily on experienced operators who know what each alarm really means. Poor alarm clarity leads to wrong or delayed responses, driving upset and product cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the time from a feedstock deviation entering the active production run until it is recognized
rows.plain: A change in the incoming material should be noticed as soon as possible after it enters an active production run. Early notice gives people more time to adjust the process and protect the product.
rows.imp_rat: Time to recognize a feedstock deviation in the active run (relevance) governs how much product is affected; delayed recognition yields recoverable off-spec batches (severity), held to the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Without continuous inline feedstock analytics, deviations are frequently detected only after they affect the process, leaving a real recognition gap. The delay between a feedstock deviation entering the run and its recognition is often long when relying on periodic lab sampling. Early recognition depends on operator attentiveness and inference from downstream symptoms, a skill-heavy task. Late recognition means more off-spec product made in the interim, a direct material cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of divergence between applied agitation intensity and the required mixing condition as product viscosity changes
rows.plain: The strength of the mixing should stay as close as possible to what the product needs, even when the product becomes thicker or thinner. This helps mix the product evenly.
rows.imp_rat: Divergence between applied agitation and required mixing as viscosity changes affects product uniformity, central to viscous processing, but off-spec mixing is typically detected and correctable. Kept within the batch cap.
rows.sat_rat: Few plants measure inline viscosity to close the loop on agitation, so divergence between applied and required mixing recurs as product thickens or thins. Agitation is usually adjusted reactively as viscosity drifts, so mixing conditions lag the changing product for meaningful intervals. Matching agitation intensity to evolving viscosity is a hands-on judgement that depends heavily on operator experience with the specific product. Mismatched agitation wastes energy when over-driven and risks off-spec product when under-driven, both recurring costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of component material retained on containers and charging implements after charging
rows.plain: Leave as little of the material behind as possible on containers and tools after they are filled. This helps prevent waste and ensures the intended amount reaches the process.
rows.imp_rat: Component retained on containers/implements is a charging-accuracy and yield concern that can shift potency; moderate relevance and severity.
rows.sat_rat: Retention amounts vary with material and technique, causing minor yield inconsistency. Rinsing containers and implements adds handling time but is standard practice. Quantitative transfer and rinsing require care but are routine operator tasks. Material clinging to containers and scoops is a recurring direct loss only partly recovered by rinsing.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of batches requiring parameter rework because component properties fall outside establishment assumptions
rows.plain: Reduce how often a batch needs its settings changed because the materials do not behave as expected. This saves time and makes the mixing process more predictable.
rows.imp_rat: Parameter rework from properties outside assumptions is a rework/throughput burden; moderate relevance and severity.
rows.sat_rat: Establishment assumptions hold for most lots, but property drift occasionally triggers rework. Rework from out-of-assumption component properties adds substantial batch delay. Diagnosing and adjusting for off-assumption properties requires strong process expertise. Parameter rework wastes materials and labor.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of cycle-completion variation across expected material properties and batch loads
rows.plain: Make the results at the end of each run as consistent as possible, even when the materials or batch sizes vary. This helps each batch finish properly.
rows.imp_rat: Cycle-completion variation across material properties/loads affects throughput predictability; moderate relevance and severity.
rows.sat_rat: Cycle-time variation across loads and properties is only loosely characterized at spec time. Cycle-completion time variation across materials and loads is measurable but hard to bound. Predicting cycle variation across the material range needs process expertise. Variable cycles disrupt scheduling and utilities budgeting.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of liquid batch material spilled or leaked during charging, compounding, or transfer
rows.plain: Prevent as much liquid material as possible from being spilled or leaked while it is added, mixed, or moved. This reduces waste and helps keep people and the work area safe.
rows.imp_rat: Spilled or leaked batch material is a yield/safety/waste concern; moderate relevance and severity.
rows.sat_rat: Closed connections and drip trays reduce but do not eliminate spillage variability. Cleaning up spills interrupts flow but is infrequent. Careful charging and transfer connections require attentive handling that most operators manage. Spills and leaks are direct material and cleanup losses that occur intermittently.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of active or excipient material adhering to vessel surfaces during blending and hold
rows.plain: Keep as little drug ingredient or added ingredient as possible from sticking to the inside of the container during mixing and holding. This helps keep the mixture evenly blended and preserves the intended amount of each ingredient.
rows.imp_rat: Material adhering to vessel surfaces is a yield/waste concern that can also shift potency; moderate relevance and severity.
rows.sat_rat: Good vessel design and agitation limit adhesion, but sticky formulations still leave residue on surfaces. Material adhering to surfaces prolongs cleaning and can extend mixing to recover it. Reducing wall adhesion depends on knowledge of wetting and surface effects. Adhered material is lost yield and increases cleaning cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of endpoint delay caused by foam or entrained air in the blend
rows.plain: Shorten delays at the planned finish point caused by foam or trapped air in the mixture. This helps the process finish on time.
rows.imp_rat: Endpoint delay from foam/entrained air is a throughput concern; moderate relevance and low-moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: De-aeration practices work but foam-prone formulations still cause unpredictable delays. Foam or entrained air delays reaching a confirmable endpoint, directly extending time. Managing and de-aerating foam takes operator technique and antifoam knowledge. De-aeration holds waste vessel time and energy.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of batches requiring repeat mixing after failed homogeneity checks
rows.plain: Reduce how often a batch must be mixed again because its contents were not evenly distributed. This helps batches finish on schedule.
rows.imp_rat: Repeat mixing after failed homogeneity checks is rework — a cost/throughput and hold-time burden, not a direct safety escape; moderate relevance and severity.
rows.sat_rat: Failed checks still occur often enough that remix cycles are a familiar recurring event rather than a rarity. Repeat mixing after a failed check directly consumes extra shift time and delays handoff. Deciding remix parameters relies on operator experience rather than automated guidance. Repeat mixing wastes energy and vessel occupancy, and is a recurring visible cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of batches requiring repeat mixing after failed homogeneity criteria
rows.plain: Reduce how often a batch must be mixed again because its contents were not evenly distributed. This frees equipment and time for other work.
rows.imp_rat: Repeat mixing after failed homogeneity criteria is rework driving cost and hold-time risk; moderate relevance and severity.
rows.sat_rat: Validated parameters reduce failures but marginal batches still routinely require a second mixing pass. Remixing to meet failed criteria eats into the scheduled batch window. Corrective remix relies on established recipes but still needs judgement on additional mix duration. Each remix consumes vessel time and utilities, a tangible recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of thermal transitions between required batch temperatures
rows.plain: Shorten the time needed to heat or cool the batch between its required temperatures. This helps the production cycle run more efficiently.
rows.imp_rat: Duration of thermal transitions between temperatures is a delay/throughput concern; moderate relevance and severity.
rows.sat_rat: Transition times are predictable with standard heat-transfer sizing, giving fairly dependable results. Thermal transition duration is directly measurable and a common optimization target. Sizing for transition speed uses established heat-transfer engineering. Slow transitions cost throughput and utility energy.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of critical process parameters failing acceptance criteria during post-maintenance testing
rows.plain: Have as many important process settings as possible meet the required checks after repairs or servicing, so the system can be trusted to work properly again.
rows.imp_rat: Critical process parameters failing acceptance during post-maintenance testing is a relevant gate before restart, and its consequence is delayed return or rework rather than immediate harm since the failure is caught in testing. Bounded by the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Structured post-maintenance testing catches most out-of-spec parameters before production, giving reasonably consistent outcomes. Post-maintenance parameter testing is a defined step that adds bounded time before restart. Interpreting acceptance-criteria failures needs trained commissioning staff but follows documented procedures. Rework when parameters fail costs extra downtime and materials, though it is expected and provisioned.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that hazardous residues are assigned to the wrong regulatory waste category
rows.plain: Make sure dangerous leftover materials are placed in the correct legally defined waste category. Mislabeling them could lead to unsafe disposal or rule violations.
rows.imp_rat: Mis-assigning hazardous residues to the wrong regulatory waste category is an administrative/compliance error peripheral to production, but its consequences (regulatory violation, safety risk) are serious. Bounded by the batch cap.
rows.sat_rat: Established waste profiling and lab analysis handle common residues well, but ambiguous mixed viscous residues still get miscategorized with some regularity. Sampling and analytical turnaround for regulatory categorization introduces delay. Assigning residues to the correct regulatory waste category demands specialist environmental/regulatory knowledge that is scarce on site. Wrong categorization drives either over-conservative expensive disposal or penalties for under-classification.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of wetted surfaces retaining viscous product residue after final cleaning and flushing
rows.plain: Remove thick product residue from all surfaces that touched the product after the final cleaning and rinsing. This helps keep the next product from being contaminated.
rows.imp_rat: Viscous residue retained on wetted surfaces after final cleaning risks cross-contamination in subsequent batches, relevant to cleanability; severity is moderate as it is usually caught by cleaning verification. Held at the cap.
rows.sat_rat: Complete removal from complex geometries, gaskets, and rough surfaces is inconsistent, and verifying every wetted surface is clean remains a recurring gap. CIP cycles and repeated flushing of viscous residue are time-consuming and often iterated until swabs pass. Cleaning validation and selecting solvents/temperatures for sticky residues require process and cleaning expertise. Solvent, water, and effluent treatment for repeated flushing consume significant resources.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a waste stream is sent through a channel unauthorized for its composition or hazard classification
rows.plain: Make sure waste is sent through a disposal route approved for what it contains and how dangerous it is. This helps prevent unsafe handling or rule violations.
rows.imp_rat: Routing a waste stream through an unauthorized channel is peripheral to the core processing job but carries elevated severity due to safety and regulatory hazard exposure. Mean kept within the 5.5 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Waste manifests, profiling, and licensed-hauler systems catch most errors, but mislabeling and channel mix-ups still occur during high-volume decommissioning. Waste characterization and routing decisions add administrative time before streams can be shipped. Correctly matching a stream to an authorized channel depends on trained waste coordinators interpreting hazard classifications. Misrouting risks fines and cleanup costs, so effort spent here averts larger expense.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that nonsterile contaminants enter the tank through the vent path
rows.plain: Keep germs, dirt, and other unwanted contaminants from entering the tank through its air opening. This helps keep the tank contents clean.
rows.imp_rat: Nonsterile contaminants entering via the vent path is a serious contamination consequence (severity), though it addresses one specific ingress path and is moderately relevant to the overall processing job.
rows.sat_rat: Hydrophobic sterile vent filters are a mature, validated barrier, though filter wetting or integrity failures still cause occasional breaches. Sterile vent filters operate continuously with no added cycle time during use. Filter installation and integrity testing require trained handling but are routine in sterile operations. Sterilizing-grade vent filters and their integrity testing are a recurring consumable cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of routing process material to the wrong receiving tank
rows.plain: Make sure material is sent to the intended receiving tank rather than the wrong one. This helps prevent mix-ups, waste, or unsafe conditions.
rows.imp_rat: Routing material to the wrong receiving tank can cause cross-batch mix-ups (notable severity), yet it is a discrete transfer error of moderate centrality, held at the cap.
rows.sat_rat: Automated valve sequencing and interlocks make routing reliable where installed, but manual manifold operations still cause wrong-tank routing. Verifying valve line-up before routing adds setup time, especially with manual valves. Correct routing depends heavily on operator manipulation of valves and manifolds, a classic error-prone, attention-intensive task. Automated valve interlocks cost more, but misrouting causes expensive contamination and batch loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of consequential tank corrosion remaining undetected during inspection
rows.plain: Make sure serious rust or damage inside the tank is found during inspection instead of being missed. This helps prevent hidden damage from causing problems later.
rows.imp_rat: Undetected consequential corrosion carries high severity via eventual tank failure, but as an inspection-detection error it is less central to the immediate processing job, keeping the average low.
rows.sat_rat: Visual and UT corrosion mapping catch most significant attack, but localized pitting and under-deposit corrosion still evade detection. Corrosion inspection is folded into scheduled internal/external surveys with manageable duration. Identifying consequential corrosion vs benign staining relies on inspector experience. Visual plus targeted NDT is relatively economical for corrosion screening.

rows.stk: Environmental health and safety personnel, waste management contractors.
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of hazardous residue being released during recovery, packaging, or transfer
rows.plain: Prevent dangerous leftover material from escaping while it is collected, packed, or moved. Safe handling keeps people and the surrounding area protected.
rows.imp_rat: Hazardous residue release during recovery, packaging, or transfer sits at the material-handling edge of the scope (low relevance) but is severe when exposure or environmental release occurs (notable severity).
rows.sat_rat: Mature hazardous-waste packaging practice makes releases during recovery uncommon, though spills during transfer still occur. Recovery and packaging under controlled transfer adds handling time that is planned for. Proper containerization and transfer of hazardous residues follows waste-handling training that is widely available. Certified containers, labels, and disposal fees are a real recurring cost of hazardous recovery.

rows.source_job: Isolate energy sources on the skid
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of electrical, pneumatic, or hydraulic connections remaining energized after isolation is declared complete
rows.plain: After declaring the equipment safely shut off, leave as few electrical, air-powered, or liquid-powered connections active as possible, so no hidden energy remains to cause harm.
rows.imp_rat: Connections remaining energized after declared isolation is a lockout/maintenance defect peripheral to production output, giving low relevance; the consequence is potential electrocution or actuation injury during service, which is severe.
rows.sat_rat: Zero-energy verification with meters and try-outs is standard and reliable, but on complex multi-utility skids a connection occasionally remains live past a premature isolation sign-off. Point-by-point isolation verification takes time but is a bounded checklist activity. Verifying de-energization needs qualified electricians/technicians with test instruments, a routinely available skill. Testing and tagging carry minor cost.

rows.source_job: Configure skid setpoints and operating parameters
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of skid setpoints configured outside validated ranges for the sterile fill-finish campaign
rows.plain: Make sure the equipment settings stay within the approved limits for the sterile production run. This helps keep the process safe and consistent.
rows.imp_rat: Setpoints outside validated ranges in sterile fill-finish directly threaten product sterility and CFJ conformance (relevance); a breach can spoil a full campaign (severity), though ceiling limits the score.
rows.sat_rat: Recipe management and configuration reviews catch most out-of-range setpoints, but manual entry and change-control gaps still let some slip through. Loading and verifying recipe setpoints against validated ranges is a defined commissioning task with predictable duration. Correct configuration depends on engineers knowing validated ranges, and manual parameter entry remains error-prone without automated checking. Rework of a mis-configured campaign wastes setup resources, though catching it in commissioning limits the damage.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that safety-critical deviations from the approved skid design remain undetected before first operation
rows.plain: Find any safety-important differences between the approved equipment design and what was actually built before starting it for the first time. Undetected differences could create unexpected hazards.
rows.imp_rat: Undetected safety-critical design deviations before first operation are highly relevant to safe skid operation (relevance) and can cause catastrophic first-run failures (severity), but constrained to 5.0.
rows.sat_rat: Design reviews, redline management, and DQ/IQ walkdowns catch most deviations, but drawing-to-hardware discrepancies still occasionally reach first operation undetected. Design-vs-as-built walkdowns are thorough but slow, extending qualification timelines meaningfully. Spotting a safety-critical deviation from P&IDs and design drawings demands experienced reviewers who can recognize what is wrong. Redlines and field changes late in qualification are costly to correct, and a missed one risks incident cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that incorrect input material is charged into the skid for the batch step
rows.plain: Make sure the correct material is put into the equipment for the batch. The wrong material could spoil the batch or create a safety problem.
rows.imp_rat: Charging incorrect input material corrupts the batch step outright, central to the job (relevance), and yields scrapped or unsafe product (severity); capped by ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Barcode scanning and weigh-and-dispense checks catch many wrong-material events, but manual charging steps still allow occasional mischarges. Material verification steps add dispensing and check time but are built into the batch workflow. Correct charging still leans on operator diligence and independent verification, so it remains an attention-dependent manual control. Charging wrong material destroys the batch and wastes expensive raw materials, making errors very costly.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the skid emergency shutdown function is unavailable during an immediate hazard
rows.plain: Make sure the equipment unit can shut down immediately when there is a serious danger. This gives people a fast way to stop the hazard from getting worse.
rows.imp_rat: An unavailable emergency shutdown during a hazard is a rarer but potentially catastrophic safety failure (severity); its relevance to routine CFJ delivery is narrower (relevance), and it is capped at 5.0.
rows.sat_rat: Safety-rated hardwired E-stop circuits with routine function testing make unavailability rare, which is why this is among the better-served needs. E-stop actuation is instantaneous by design, meeting the time need fully. Pressing an emergency stop requires no special skill, and its availability is designed to be operator-independent. Hardwired safety circuits and periodic testing carry modest cost against the high value of a guaranteed stop.

rows.stk: Maintenance technician, mechanical engineer, insulation contractor.
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of jacket leakage breaching the tank product-contact boundary after service
rows.plain: Make sure liquid from the tank's heating or cooling outer layer cannot leak into the area that touches the product after servicing. This protects the product from unwanted liquid.
rows.imp_rat: Jacket leakage breaching the product-contact boundary is a real contamination hazard with high consequence severity, but it is a relatively specific post-service failure of lower centrality to routine tank processing here.
rows.sat_rat: The concealed double-wall interface makes small breaches hard to confirm, so detection is only moderately dependable. Detecting jacket-to-shell leakage often requires isolating and pressure-testing the jacket, adding outage time. Diagnosing a jacket breach into the product boundary demands experienced troubleshooting of a hidden interface. Jacket repairs are costly and sometimes require partial vessel rebuild.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of finished liquid being routed to the wrong downstream destination
rows.plain: Send the finished liquid to the correct next destination. This prevents usable product from going to the wrong equipment or process.
rows.imp_rat: Routing finished liquid to the wrong downstream destination (relevance) is a discrete logistics error; consequences can include product mix-ups (severity), constrained under the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Automated routing tied to batch tracking makes wrong-destination errors uncommon in well-instrumented plants, making this fairly well served. Routing to downstream destinations executes rapidly through automated valve sequences. Correct destination selection depends on recipe management systems that reduce operator error. Misrouting finished product causes costly reprocessing or blending, though such events are relatively rare.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of the required plant flow path being unavailable when routing must begin
rows.plain: Make sure the needed pipes and valves are ready before liquid movement begins. This helps ensure the liquid can be sent along the correct route.
rows.imp_rat: An unavailable flow path when routing must begin (relevance) stalls production start and can halt the job, but such availability failures are usually transient and recoverable (severity), keeping both within the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Interlocked automated valve lineups reliably present the required path most of the time, though stuck valves or misconfiguration occasionally block routing. Automated valve sequencing establishes routing quickly when commanded, minimizing setup delay. Valve lineup verification still relies on operator checks and knowledge of the plant configuration. Redundant valves and interlocks add capital cost but are standard in modern plants.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of nonconforming liquid that continues into the normal discharge path after diversion begins
rows.plain: Make sure liquid that fails the required checks is fully kept out of the normal drain or outlet once diversion starts. This prevents unsuitable material from continuing through the usual path.
rows.imp_rat: Nonconforming liquid continuing into the normal discharge after diversion begins (relevance) undermines quality segregation; severity is moderate as it is a leakage of an already-detected defect, capped by the ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Diversion works but pipeline holdup means a fraction of nonconforming liquid predictably reaches normal discharge before separation is complete. Once diversion begins, residual in-line nonconforming liquid continues briefly until the transition completes, a short but real window. Understanding line holdup and transition volumes requires process knowledge to minimize carryover. The slug of nonconforming liquid that passes before full diversion is lost or requires rework, a recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the time between confirmation of nonconforming output and redirection away from the normal discharge path
rows.plain: Move product away from the normal outlet as soon as it is confirmed to be unacceptable. This helps keep it from being mixed with acceptable product.
rows.imp_rat: Delay between confirming nonconforming output and redirecting it (relevance) determines how much bad product escapes; the consequence is contained batch loss (severity), both held at or below the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Automated quality triggers with auto-diversion cut the delay where deployed, but many plants still rely on slower manual confirmation. The interval from confirming nonconforming output to redirecting it is the very error, and manual confirmation steps add measurable delay. Deciding to divert requires operator confidence in the quality signal, delaying action when judgement is uncertain. Every second of delay lets more product go off-spec, but the diversion hardware itself is already funded.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that required flow, temperature, pressure, or level readings are unavailable during plant operation
rows.plain: Make sure important readings, such as flow, temperature, pressure, and level, are available while the plant is running. Missing readings can make it difficult to control the process safely.
rows.imp_rat: Unavailable flow/temperature/pressure/level readings during operation impair control (moderate relevance), and missing readings can force shutdowns or hide excursions (moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Mature instrumentation and control systems make readings available almost always, with occasional gaps from sensor failure, fouling or comms loss. Continuous instrumentation delivers readings in real time, so availability rarely introduces delay when transmitters work. Standard DCS/SCADA displays make readings accessible without special skill, though diagnosing a dead instrument needs expertise. Redundant transmitters and maintenance programs cost money but keep critical readings available cost-effectively.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of process parameters set outside the new recipe specifications
rows.plain: Make sure the equipment settings match the requirements for the new product recipe. Correct settings help the product turn out as intended.
rows.imp_rat: Process parameters set outside a new recipe spec affects product quality during changeover, moderately relevant, but such settings are normally verified before production runs so severity is limited.
rows.sat_rat: Recipe systems reliably apply new specification setpoints, but manually adjusted or non-managed parameters still occasionally fall outside spec. Recipe-managed setpoints load new specs automatically without slowing changeover. Automated recipe application reduces the operator judgement needed to set every parameter correctly. Parameters outside new recipe spec risk off-spec product and rework cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of discrepancy between documented processing values and observed readings for each run
rows.plain: Make sure the values written down for each production run match what was actually observed. This keeps the production records reliable.
rows.imp_rat: Discrepancy between documented and observed processing values per run touches data integrity and process verification, giving moderate relevance, but individual-run discrepancies are usually detected and reconciled, limiting severity.
rows.sat_rat: Data historians and electronic records make observed values match documentation closely, though manual entries and sensor drift still create discrepancies. Automatic data historians capture readings continuously, so comparing documented vs observed values is fast. Electronic batch records reduce transcription skill demands and manual reconciliation effort. Discrepancies drive investigation and disposition cost, but automated logging keeps them low.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of unplanned liquid feedstock flow interruptions through active processing equipment
rows.plain: Avoid unexpected stops in the flow of liquid material through the working equipment, so processing can continue steadily.
rows.imp_rat: Duration of unplanned feedstock flow interruptions (relevance) directly reduces output; consequences are lost production time (severity), both moderate and capped by the ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Redundant pumps and standard restart procedures shorten most interruptions, but unexpected stoppages remain a recurring operational reality. Unplanned flow interruptions cause downtime whose duration depends on how quickly the cause is found and cleared. Restoring flow quickly relies on skilled troubleshooting of pumps, valves and blockages. Interruption downtime means lost production and possible product degradation in stalled lines.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of directing residual liquid to an incorrect disposal, recycle, or containment path
rows.plain: Send leftover liquid to the correct place for disposal, reuse, or safe containment. This prevents spills and keeps unsuitable liquids from being mixed or misrouted.
rows.imp_rat: Directing residual liquid to an incorrect disposal/recycle/containment path is peripheral to the main processing job (relevance) yet can cause environmental or cross-contamination consequences (severity) when it happens.
rows.sat_rat: Labeled lines and interlocks reduce misrouting, but manual path selection under pressure still occasionally sends residual liquid the wrong way. Routing decisions and valve changes execute promptly once the correct destination is chosen. Selecting the correct disposal, recycle or containment path leans on operator knowledge and can be error-prone during upsets. Misrouting to the wrong path wastes recoverable product or contaminates recycle streams, a meaningful cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of applying a setpoint for the wrong processing phase during a phase transition
rows.plain: Make sure the setting for one stage of the process is not used during a change to another stage.
rows.imp_rat: Applying a setpoint for the wrong processing phase during a transition is fairly relevant to correct processing, with moderate severity since a mis-set phase can spoil product but is often flagged by process controls.
rows.sat_rat: Batch/recipe management systems reliably apply the correct phase setpoints, though manual overrides and legacy setups still allow occasional wrong-phase application. Phase-transition setpoints load automatically in recipe-driven plants, adding no delay. Automated recipe sequencing removes much manual setpoint-entry judgement during transitions. A wrong-phase setpoint can spoil a batch, an expensive but usually contained error.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of liquid flow interruption caused by an obstructed or incorrectly aligned required circuit during priming
rows.plain: Restore liquid flow quickly when a required path is blocked or set up incorrectly during startup, so the system can begin working without a long delay.
rows.imp_rat: Liquid flow interruption from a misaligned circuit during priming affects throughput and readiness (moderate relevance) but consequences are mostly delay-related and recoverable (low-moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Valve position feedback and flow confirmation catch many misalignments, though intermittent obstructions still cause stoppages. Obstructed or misaligned circuits during priming directly stall flow and delay batch start until cleared. Diagnosing an alignment or blockage issue requires operator troubleshooting familiarity with the circuit. Downtime during priming interruptions carries opportunity cost but little material loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of technician exposure to hazardous residues during system dismantling
rows.plain: Protect the technician from dangerous leftover material while taking the system apart. This helps prevent harmful contact during shutdown and removal.
rows.imp_rat: Technician exposure to hazardous residues during dismantling is a decommissioning safety issue only marginally tied to batch production (low relevance) but severe for the exposed worker (notable severity).
rows.sat_rat: Established PPE and dismantling protocols keep exposure incidents relatively uncommon when followed. Donning appropriate PPE and staging decontamination during dismantling adds routine time. Safe dismantling around hazardous residues leans on trained crews who select correct PPE and sequence and recognize residue risks. Consumable PPE and disposal of contaminated items are recurring but manageable costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of technician exposure to hazardous process residues during component extraction
rows.plain: Protect the technician from dangerous leftover material while removing parts that may be reused. This helps prevent harmful contact during the work.
rows.imp_rat: Technician exposure to hazardous residues during component extraction is a maintenance safety concern peripheral to batching output (low relevance) yet severe for personnel harm (notable severity).
rows.sat_rat: Protective practice covers most extraction exposure, though hidden residue pockets in pumps and valves still cause occasional contact. Careful component extraction with containment adds time but is a bounded step. Extracting fouled components without spreading residue depends on skilled, attentive technicians handling awkward parts. Containment and PPE consumables represent modest cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of prior-product residue remaining on product-contact surfaces after the configuration switch
rows.plain: Make sure no leftover product remains on surfaces that touch the next product after changing the equipment setup. This helps prevent products from mixing or becoming contaminated.
rows.imp_rat: Prior-product residue on product-contact surfaces after a configuration switch drives cross-contamination and is central to skid changeover integrity (relevance); consequences include adulterated batches and patient risk (severity). Capped at 5.0 by ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Validated cleaning procedures with swab and rinse testing make carryover control reliable for validated product pairs, though non-routine configurations still leave gaps. Configuration switches with cleaning verification lengthen changeover time noticeably between products. Deciding acceptable residue limits and executing changeover correctly requires cleaning-validation expertise and disciplined execution. Cross-contamination residue can force costly reprocessing or rejection, and swab sampling and analysis add cost each changeover.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of a critical parameter exceeding its authorized limit after setpoint adjustment
rows.plain: Make sure changing a target setting does not cause an important value to go beyond its allowed limit.
rows.imp_rat: A critical parameter exceeding its authorized limit after adjustment threatens process control central to the CFJ (relevance) and can damage product or equipment (severity), held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: DCS/PLC alarm and interlock limits reliably block or flag out-of-range setpoints, keeping post-adjustment excursions uncommon. Setpoint changes are applied and take effect quickly through the control system with no meaningful delay. Configured limit ranges and interlocks reduce reliance on operator judgement when adjusting setpoints. An out-of-limit excursion can spoil a batch, but software limit enforcement makes such waste infrequent.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of the skid wetted path retaining product residue after the clean-in-place cycle
rows.plain: Make sure little or no product is left inside the equipment's product-carrying passages after cleaning. This helps prevent old product from mixing with the next batch.
rows.imp_rat: Residual product in the wetted path after CIP is core to contamination control (relevance) and can cause carryover and batch rejection (severity), held to the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Validated CIP with conductivity/TOC rinse monitoring reliably drives residue below limits, though dead-legs and hard-to-reach geometry occasionally retain residue. CIP cycles are automated and run to fixed recipes, so residue removal happens within scheduled clean windows. Once validated, CIP requires little operator finesse since spray coverage and cycle steps are automated. Water, cleaning agents, and rinse verification consume resources, but automation keeps this predictable and bounded.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of the intended wetted process circuit bypassed during the cycle
rows.plain: Make sure the cleaning process reaches all parts of the process path that are meant to be cleaned. This helps prevent residue or contamination from remaining in skipped sections.
rows.imp_rat: Bypassing part of the intended wetted circuit during cleaning is relevant to hygiene assurance, and severity is moderate because unclean zones can harbor contamination, though it is often detected in validation.
rows.sat_rat: Riboflavin coverage tests and flow verification confirm wetting for most designs, yet bypassed sections still occur where circuits change or valves misalign. Verifying full circuit coverage adds engineering time during cycle design and revalidation. Ensuring no dead legs or bypassed sections requires strong process knowledge and riboflavin/coverage testing expertise. Coverage testing and rework of piping add cost, but bypass failures are costlier.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that critical wear or deterioration is overlooked during scheduled service
rows.plain: Make sure important wear or damage is found during regular servicing. Missing it could allow a part to fail later.
rows.imp_rat: Overlooking critical wear during scheduled service (relevance) is a maintenance-quality issue; missed deterioration can lead to failures (severity) but the item ranks below batch 1 and is capped.
rows.sat_rat: Checklists and PM programs standardize much, but subtle degradation is still frequently overlooked because detection depends on human attention. Thorough inspection during scheduled service takes time that maintenance windows often compress. Recognizing early wear or deterioration relies heavily on experienced inspectors' judgement and pattern recognition that is scarce and inconsistent. Missed wear leads to expensive unplanned failures, while thorough inspection adds labor cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the upgraded component is incompatible with existing process connections, utilities, or controls
rows.plain: Make sure the replacement part works with the existing pipes, utilities, and control system. This helps the upgraded equipment operate safely and as intended.
rows.imp_rat: Incompatibility of an upgraded component with existing connections/utilities/controls disrupts an upgrade, giving moderate relevance, but it is typically found during integration and is correctable, bounding severity.
rows.sat_rat: Standard change-management and drawing reviews catch many mismatches, but aging or poorly documented plants still produce recurring surprises at install. Compatibility checks add pre-shutdown engineering time, but that lead time is normally budgeted into the maintenance window. Verifying fit against legacy connections, utilities, and controls demands scarce experienced engineers who understand the plant's undocumented as-built quirks. Discovering an incompatibility mid-outage forces costly emergency procurement, rework, and extended downtime.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of lubricant entering sterile product-contact areas during maintenance
rows.plain: Keep lubricant from getting into clean areas that touch the product while parts are being serviced. This helps prevent the product from becoming contaminated.
rows.imp_rat: Lubricant entering sterile product-contact areas during maintenance is an occasional event (relevance) but chemical contamination of product is a serious quality failure (severity), still bounded by the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Registered lubricants, sealed bearings and containment practices control most cases, but lubricant intrusion into product-contact areas still occurs occasionally. Using food-grade lubricants and careful application adds modest time to maintenance tasks. Preventing lubricant migration depends on technician discipline and correct product selection, which is trainable but error-prone. Certified food-grade and specialty lubricants cost more but are a small recurring expense.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of unexpected rotation of the coupled equipment during alignment
rows.plain: Prevent the connected equipment from turning unexpectedly while it is being lined up. This helps protect workers from moving machinery and injuries.
rows.imp_rat: Unexpected rotation of coupled equipment during alignment is a maintenance-task error of limited relevance to processing, but severity is elevated because sudden rotation can cause serious hand or crush injuries to technicians.
rows.sat_rat: Mature lockout/tagout and mechanical isolation practices make unexpected rotation during alignment rare where procedures are followed. Lockout/tagout and isolation before alignment add setup time but are routine. LOTO procedures are standardized and require modest, widely held competence to execute. Isolation is inexpensive, while an unexpected rotation incident is very costly, so the practice pays off.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of damage to product-contact surfaces caused during cleaning
rows.plain: Avoid damaging the surfaces that touch the product while cleaning them. This helps keep the equipment usable and prevents harm to the product.
rows.imp_rat: Damage to product-contact surfaces during cleaning is moderately relevant to sustaining hygienic processing, with moderate severity because surface damage can seed contamination and force component replacement.
rows.sat_rat: Compatible chemistries, controlled temperatures and passivation practices make cleaning-induced surface damage uncommon under validated cycles. Validated cleaning within surface-safe parameters does not materially add time. Chemistry and parameter selection is engineered into the recipe, reducing dependence on operator judgement. Surface damage causes expensive component replacement, but validated cleaning avoids most of it.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of batch-control instruments remaining outside specified tolerance after calibration
rows.plain: Make sure the instruments used to control a production batch give readings within the allowed range after they are checked and adjusted. Accurate readings help keep the batch under control.
rows.imp_rat: Batch-control instruments still out of tolerance after calibration erode control-system reliability (relevance 5); the effect is inaccurate control caught at verification, a recoverable consequence (severity 5).
rows.sat_rat: Established calibration programs keep the overwhelming majority of batch-control instruments in tolerance, with only occasional post-calibration drift. Batch-control instrument calibration follows the same scheduled, efficient workflow. Standardized loop-calibration procedures reduce the specialist skill required. Instrument calibration is a routine cost with strong return in avoided deviations.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of measurement instruments remaining outside specified tolerance after calibration
rows.plain: Make sure measuring devices give readings within the allowed range after they are checked and adjusted. Accurate readings are important for making reliable decisions.
rows.imp_rat: Instruments left out of tolerance after calibration undermine measurement trust across the batch (relevance 5); the consequence is unreliable readings that can be caught in verification (severity 5).
rows.sat_rat: Mature metrology practice with traceable standards leaves very few instruments out of tolerance after calibration, making this among the best-served needs. Calibration is a scheduled, well-understood activity with predictable turnaround. Calibration technicians follow established procedures, so skill dependency is manageable. Calibration labor and reference standards are routine budgeted costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of product-contact locations omitted from the documented cleaning procedure
rows.plain: Make sure every place that touches the product is included in the written cleaning instructions. Nothing that could affect the product should be missed.
rows.imp_rat: Product-contact locations omitted from documented cleaning procedures create carryover risk (severity 5), and cleaning completeness is moderately central to contamination control in batch prep (relevance 5).
rows.sat_rat: Standard cleaning-procedure templates and P&ID walkdowns catch most locations, but omitted dead-legs and hard-to-reach points remain a persistent gap. Building a complete cleaning procedure that covers every contact location is a lengthy documentation effort. Identifying all product-contact locations, including hidden ones, requires detailed equipment knowledge that is easily incomplete. Documentation effort is modest, but a missed location causing contamination is very costly.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of ingredient doses outside prescribed quantity tolerance
rows.plain: Make sure each ingredient is added in an amount that stays within the required limits. Correct amounts help ensure the finished product is made properly.
rows.imp_rat: Ingredient doses outside prescribed quantity tolerance is central to batch formulation accuracy (relevance) and produces off-spec product when it occurs (severity), but ranked below the higher batch.
rows.sat_rat: Closed-loop weigh feeders keep most doses in tolerance, but powders, tails and manual additions still produce recurring deviations. Automated dosing with in-flight weighing meets tolerance without operators pausing to re-check each dose. Loss-in-weight and gain-in-weight controllers handle fine dosing that would otherwise need a skilled operator's touch. Out-of-tolerance doses waste ingredient and can scrap the batch, an expense automated dosing largely curbs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration that a process parameter remains outside approved limits before supervisory correction
rows.plain: Correct a process setting quickly when it moves outside its allowed range, so the system returns to an acceptable condition sooner.
rows.imp_rat: Prolonged time a parameter stays outside limits before correction risks batch quality drift (severity 5) and is moderately central since deviations may still be within recoverable margins (relevance 5).
rows.sat_rat: Automatic hold and control loops catch many excursions, but supervisory correction can lag during busy periods, leaving a real gap. The core need is shortening how long a parameter stays out of limits before correction, and alarm-to-correction latency depends on operator response and workload. Judging and correcting a deviation quickly requires an experienced operator or supervisor, so response quality varies with staffing. Prolonged excursions risk off-spec product, so faster correction directly limits loss cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of dosing drift remaining undetected during periodic accuracy checks
rows.plain: Make sure the amount being added has not gradually become inaccurate without anyone noticing during routine checks. Reliable amounts help keep the process consistent.
rows.imp_rat: Undetected dosing drift during accuracy checks defeats the purpose of the check and threatens composition accuracy (relevance 5); consequences accumulate across batches but are eventually caught (severity 5).
rows.sat_rat: Scheduled periodic checks catch gross drift, but gradual drift between intervals frequently remains undetected until a check occurs. Periodic accuracy checks are scheduled and quick, but drift between checks goes unmonitored for the interval. Interpreting subtle drift trends requires analytical judgement not consistently applied at every check. Periodic checks are cheap, but undetected drift can cause costly batch losses.

rows.source_job: Recalibrate dosing system after component change
rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of verification doses outside specified tolerance after recalibration
rows.plain: Make sure test doses are within the allowed range after the dosing system is adjusted. This confirms that changing a part has not made the doses inaccurate.
rows.imp_rat: Verification doses out of tolerance after recalibration signal dosing accuracy failures central to composition (relevance 5); they trigger rework rather than uncontained harm since verification catches them (severity 5).
rows.sat_rat: Verification-dose sampling reliably confirms most units are in tolerance, though occasional out-of-tolerance results still require re-adjustment. Post-calibration verification dosing runs add a defined, short verification period. Verification-dose checks are procedural and require limited specialized judgement. Verification material and gravimetric checks consume minor resources.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of residual product remaining in pipework, valves, or vessels after flushing
rows.plain: Leave as little product behind as possible after the pipes and containers are rinsed, so less material is wasted or carried into the next use.
rows.imp_rat: This near-duplicate of item 7 concerning residue in pipework after flushing is a preparatory hygiene step rather than the core mixing job (relevance), yet contamination consequences remain severe (severity).
rows.sat_rat: This near-duplicate of residual-product removal is served by the same mature flushing practice, leaving comparable residual hold-up gaps in complex geometries. Line flushing routines are established and add predictable time to changeover or service. Effectiveness rests on hygienic design rather than technician finesse, limiting skill demand. Product left in pipework and flush fluids represents recoverable but real material loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that selected options conflict with sterile processing and cleaning requirements
rows.plain: Choose settings that will not interfere with keeping the equipment clean and free from harmful germs. This helps prevent contamination during use.
rows.imp_rat: Option selections conflicting with sterile/cleaning requirements is a configuration error affecting cleanability (relevance 5); consequences are meaningful but typically surfaced during qualification rather than in-process (severity 5).
rows.sat_rat: Hygienic design guidelines and vendor experience catch obvious conflicts, though subtle dead-leg or drainability issues still slip through and cause deviations. Cross-checking options against CIP/SIP and sterile processing requirements adds review cycles that fit within normal timelines. Spotting conflicts between configuration choices and cleaning/sterilization needs relies on experienced sterile-processing engineers. Conflicts caught late trigger rework costs, but the design review itself is relatively inexpensive.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of shortlisted liquid processing plant suppliers that fail minimum sterile fill-finish technical criteria
rows.plain: Choose vendors that can meet the minimum requirements for filling liquid products in a germ-free environment. This helps ensure the facility can produce safe, acceptable products.
rows.imp_rat: Shortlisted suppliers failing sterile fill-finish criteria is a procurement-stage screening error (lower relevance to running the plant) but selecting an unqualified supplier could compromise product sterility (moderate-high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Standard supplier prequalification questionnaires filter out most unqualified vendors, but inconsistent criteria still let marginal suppliers through. Screening shortlisted suppliers against sterile fill-finish criteria is fairly quick once criteria are defined. Deciding which suppliers meet minimum sterile technical criteria demands sterile-manufacturing expertise that is the scarce, error-prone input here. Prequalification is low incremental cost relative to the overall procurement.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of anchorage losing restraint under operating vibration and load
rows.plain: Make sure the equipment's foundation fastenings stay secure under normal shaking and weight.
rows.imp_rat: Anchorage losing restraint under operating vibration and load is a structural-integrity failure that could damage equipment, giving decent severity, but it is a specific mechanical mode not central to the processing function itself.
