rows.sat_rat: Engineered anchorage design and torque checks make loss of restraint under vibration rare. Torqueing and anchor verification adds routine time to installation completion. Anchoring to engineered specs uses standard rigging and torque-verification skills. Engineered anchors and torque tooling are modest costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of cracking the foundation during anchor installation
rows.plain: Install the anchors without cracking the foundation that supports the equipment. A sound foundation keeps the equipment secure.
rows.imp_rat: Cracking the foundation during anchor installation is a one-time installation defect (moderate relevance to ongoing liquid processing), but a cracked foundation can cause structural instability and expensive remediation, giving it moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Established anchor-design, edge-distance and drilling practice makes foundation cracking uncommon, though hitting rebar or thin slabs still occasionally causes damage. Core drilling, layout and epoxy-cure waits add time, but these are standard planned civil steps. Anchor installation follows well-known rigging and drilling practice that competent millwrights routinely execute without specialist judgement. Correct drilling and adhesive anchors cost little versus repairing a cracked foundation.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of residual deviation between instrument readings and reference-standard values after calibration
rows.plain: After adjustment, make sure the instrument's readings are as close as possible to trusted correct values.
rows.imp_rat: Residual deviation between instrument readings and reference standards after calibration is moderately relevant to accurate process control, with moderate severity because drift can bias batches but is typically caught and rechecked.
rows.sat_rat: Mature calibration practice with reference standards keeps residual deviation reliably within tolerance. Calibration routines are quick, well-scheduled activities during commissioning. Instrument calibration is a highly standardized procedure that trained technicians perform routinely. Reference standards and calibration labor are established, predictable costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of calibrating an instrument against an expired or untraceable reference standard
rows.plain: Make sure the instrument is checked against a trusted reference that is still valid and can be traced to its source.
rows.imp_rat: Calibrating against an expired or untraceable reference undermines measurement traceability and could propagate errors, giving moderate relevance, but it is usually caught in QA review and confined to instrumentation activities.
rows.sat_rat: Calibration-management practice with traceability records makes use of expired or untraceable standards rare. Checking standard traceability and expiry is a fast pre-calibration step. Verifying reference-standard traceability is a well-defined quality routine requiring little rare skill. Maintaining traceable, in-date standards is a predictable metrology cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of deviation between configured phase settings and approved processing requirements
rows.plain: Make sure the settings for each processing step match the approved requirements. This helps the process run as intended.
rows.imp_rat: Deviation between configured phase settings and approved requirements affects process correctness (moderate relevance), and misconfiguration can produce out-of-spec product (moderate severity), though typically caught during validation.
rows.sat_rat: Parameter verification against approved recipes is systematic today, so deviations are usually caught before production. Configuring and verifying phase parameters against recipes is a defined FAT/SAT step that fits the schedule. Automation engineers configure phase settings competently, though translating approved requirements still needs domain judgement. Configuration effort is labor cost already covered by commissioning budgets.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of equipment tipping or shifting while being moved into its designated operating location
rows.plain: Move the equipment into place without letting it tip over or slide. Keeping it stable protects people, equipment, and the surrounding area.
rows.imp_rat: Equipment tipping or shifting during a move is peripheral to the processing job (low relevance) but a tipping event risks worker injury and major equipment damage (higher severity).
rows.sat_rat: Standard load-securing and slow-move discipline make tipping or shifting rare when followed, though top-heavy loads on uneven floors remain a residual risk. Using proper jacks, skates and tie-downs makes moves slower than dragging equipment freehand. Safe load handling is codified in rigging standards and executed by trained movers as routine practice. Skates, cribbing and restraints are low cost against the loss from a tipped skid or injury.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of damage to the processing plant equipment during positioning
rows.plain: Move the equipment into place without damaging it or other parts of the processing plant. Careful positioning helps keep the plant working safely.
rows.imp_rat: Damage to processing equipment during positioning is a one-time installation defect (lower relevance to ongoing operation) but can require costly repair or replacement of critical assets (moderate-high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Documented lift plans, spreader bars and trained riggers make damage-free positioning the usual outcome, but tight spaces and improvisation still produce occasional knocks. Careful rigging and slow controlled placement extend the positioning window versus a quick set-down. Skilled riggers and lift plans are the norm, though quality varies with crew experience. Rigging gear and protective padding are cheap against the cost of denting or damaging expensive plant skids.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which supplied utility conditions deviate from plant interface requirements
rows.plain: Make sure the supplied services, such as power or air, meet the conditions required by the plant equipment.
rows.imp_rat: Supplied utilities deviating from interface requirements can disrupt processing, giving moderate relevance, but such mismatches are typically identified at interface check and severity is bounded because the plant will not run product on out-of-spec utilities.
rows.sat_rat: Supplied utility conditions deviating from plant requirements is a common and disruptive commissioning surprise. Verifying supplied utilities against interface requirements can slip when utility readiness lags. Utility interface checks are documented but require coordination judgement across trades. Correcting out-of-spec utilities can trigger unplanned rework costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of manufacturing residue remaining on product-contact surfaces after initial cleaning
rows.plain: Remove as much manufacturing residue as possible from surfaces that touch the product during the first cleaning. This helps prepare the surfaces for sanitizing.
rows.imp_rat: Manufacturing residue left on product-contact surfaces after initial cleaning is a commissioning-phase error (moderate relevance), and residual contaminants can compromise early product batches (moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Cleaning validation and swab testing catch most residue, though initial-clean failures and repeats still happen regularly. Initial cleaning cycles and rinse-sampling turnaround add real duration before qualification can proceed. Cleaning validation is a mature discipline but still relies on skilled analysts interpreting swab and rinse results. Detergents, WFI and analytical testing are significant but well-understood cleaning costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time required for the operator to identify and isolate the release source
rows.plain: Help the operator quickly find where a spill is coming from and stop or contain it. This can limit the spread of the spilled material.
rows.imp_rat: Time to identify and isolate a release source affects response speed (limited relevance to the core processing job), but a prolonged release can escalate safety/environmental harm (moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Gas/leak detection and sectional isolation valves exist but coverage is patchy, so source identification remains inconsistent. Identifying and isolating a release source is often a slow, walk-the-plant activity where leaks are hard to pinpoint quickly. It relies on operator experience to interpret sparse indications and know where releases typically originate. Delayed isolation increases product loss, cleanup and potential safety cost, making speed economically important.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of contamination of exposed product-contact surfaces during module assembly
rows.plain: Prevent exposed surfaces that will touch the product from becoming dirty or contaminated while the parts are being put together. This helps keep the product safe and clean.
rows.imp_rat: Contamination of exposed product-contact surfaces during module assembly is relevant to eventual product safety, but assembly is followed by cleaning/sterilization steps that bound the severity of a contamination event.
rows.sat_rat: Open assembly of product-contact parts is one of the hardest points to keep clean, and even with covers and gloving, contamination during handling remains a persistent problem. Assembling modules under controlled conditions with intermediate cleaning steps slows the build appreciably. Keeping exposed product-contact surfaces clean during hands-on assembly relies heavily on disciplined, well-trained assemblers, and finesse lapses are frequent. Protective covers and re-cleaning after exposure add modest cost, but a contamination-driven reassembly is expensive.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which mounting surfaces and load-bearing provisions deviate from the plant installation specifications
rows.imp_rat: Mounting-surface and load-bearing deviations from installation specs are a mechanical setup concern; relevant to safe stable operation but not central to the plant's core processing job, and consequences are usually caught and corrected during installation before running product.
rows.sat_rat: Foundation-versus-spec verification is routine, though deviations discovered late still occur occasionally. Verifying mounting surfaces against installation specs is a defined pre-installation check. Surveying foundations and load provisions uses standard civil/mechanical competencies. Survey effort and any grouting corrections are moderate costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of construction debris or contamination remaining in the installation area at handover
rows.imp_rat: Construction debris left at handover is a one-time commissioning error (limited relevance to ongoing operation), yet residual debris in a liquid-product plant can contaminate first batches (moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Punch-list walkdowns catch most debris, yet contamination found after handover is a common recurring dispute. Final cleaning and inspection walkdowns add days to handover but are planned into the schedule. Handover cleaning follows checklists that any competent contractor can execute without rare expertise. Cleanup crews and re-inspection cycles cost money but are standard line items.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of unintended mechanical load transferred from misaligned piping to plant-component connections
rows.imp_rat: Unintended mechanical load from misaligned piping transferred to component connections is an installation-integrity error of limited relevance to running the job, but severity is moderate as stressed joints can leak or fail over time.
rows.sat_rat: Pipe-strain transfer to connections is a known but only partially controlled recurring installation problem. Checking and correcting piping-induced load through cold alignment adds time to mechanical completion. Detecting pipe strain requires experienced technicians interpreting flange and alignment checks. Rework to relieve piping strain can consume unplanned labor and support materials.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of pump cavitation caused by retained air during production startup
rows.imp_rat: Pump cavitation from retained air at startup can damage equipment and disrupt flow, making it moderately relevant, but consequences are usually a delayed or degraded startup that is correctable. Kept below the 5.5 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: High-point vents, priming routines and startup checklists mitigate cavitation, but retained air in high-viscosity systems is stubborn so events still recur despite standard practice. Venting and priming procedures during startup take time, and trapped-air cavitation events force restart delays. Recognizing and purging retained air in viscous-service pump lines relies on operator experience with venting sequences that novices often mishandle. Cavitation damages pump internals and seals, and viscous product losses during purging add material cost.

rows.source_job: Connect utility services to the plant
rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of deviation between utility supply conditions and plant interface requirements
rows.imp_rat: Deviation between utility supply and plant interface requirements can disrupt operation, but utilities are a supporting input rather than the core processing job, so relevance is lower; consequences vary from minor to a forced stop. Under the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Interface specification sheets and utility surveys make deviations largely detectable before startup, giving mature coverage with only intermittent surprises. Utility interface checks are part of pre-commissioning and are usually completed on schedule against documented requirements. Matching utility supply to interface specs requires competent multi-discipline engineers but is a routine, well-understood task. Late-discovered utility mismatches force rework or temporary supplies at real cost, though most are caught during design coordination.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of connecting process lines to incorrect tank ports
rows.imp_rat: Connecting lines to incorrect tank ports is a preventable procedural defect with moderate severity if misrouting occurs, but its relevance to the core processing outcome is secondary.
rows.sat_rat: Tagging, P&ID checks and line lists reduce misconnections but crossed process lines remain a recurring commissioning defect where labeling is ambiguous. Verifying port connections adds walkdown time but does not dominate the schedule. Correct connection relies on fitters reading P&IDs and tags, an error-prone task dependent on attention. Mislabeled or crossed lines force rework that wastes labor and materials.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of material residence beyond the validated hold limit
rows.imp_rat: Residence beyond the validated hold limit has moderate quality/compliance severity and modest relevance to the core tank job, keeping the average within the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: MES hold-timers and alarms enforce limits well where deployed, but manual tracking and production pressure leave recurring excursions. Hold timers and scheduling systems flag validated hold limits, but downstream constraints still force material to sit longer. Hold-time tracking is procedural and requires operator/scheduler attention rather than specialist skill. Exceeding hold limits can force costly retesting or disposal, while tracking itself is cheap.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of deviation between the actual input-addition point and the specified process stage
rows.imp_rat: Deviation between actual and specified input-addition point can affect reaction consistency (moderate severity) with moderate relevance to processing accuracy, staying within the cap.
rows.sat_rat: Sequenced recipe control keeps addition points accurate where automated, but manual-stage judgement leaves deviation risk. Recipe sequencing times additions to process stages automatically, though manual staging can drift. Adding inputs at the correct process stage relies on operator timing and recipe adherence where not automated. Mistimed additions can degrade yield and waste material, while control tools are inexpensive.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration that material remains in the tank beyond the prescribed reaction endpoint
rows.imp_rat: Material remaining beyond the reaction endpoint can degrade batch quality (moderate severity) and is somewhat relevant to consistent processing, but the ranking constrains it below 5.5.
rows.sat_rat: Timed protocols and endpoint testing keep most batches near endpoint, but sampling lag and manual scheduling cause recurring overshoot. Endpoint detection via in-line analytics or timed sampling controls overrun, but lab-turnaround delays extend residence past the endpoint. Judging reaction endpoint often needs analytical interpretation and process expertise unless automated PAT is installed. Overrun wastes utility time and can degrade product, though the direct cost per event is moderate.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that residual oxygen or process gas remains above the specified limit after purging
rows.imp_rat: Residual oxygen or process gas above limit after purging affects certain oxygen-sensitive processes with moderate severity, but its relevance to the general tank CFJ is limited and stays capped.
rows.sat_rat: Oxygen analyzers and validated purge cycles reliably confirm residual gas limits where instrumented, though non-monitored purges rely on assumption. Purge cycles take defined time and oxygen analyzers confirm endpoint fairly quickly. Inert purging is a standardized procedure, but interpreting purge adequacy still needs some process understanding. Inert gas consumption is a real recurring cost, and analyzers add instrumentation expense.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percent of hazard-bearing areas of the batching system that remain uninspected during the safety review
rows.imp_rat: Uninspected hazard-bearing areas during the safety review is an administrative assurance gap peripheral to producing the batch; when a missed hazard later manifests the consequences can be serious for operator safety, but the review error itself is indirect.
rows.sat_rat: Concealed or inaccessible areas routinely go uninspected, leaving coverage gaps in the safety review. Fully covering every hazard-bearing area in a safety review lengthens the review substantially. Ensuring no hazard zone goes uninspected demands thorough, experienced reviewers who know where hazards hide. The review is labor cost with little material spend.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that deterioration in critical structural, mechanical, or fluid-path components remains undetected during inspection
rows.imp_rat: Undetected deterioration in structural, mechanical, or fluid-path components is moderately central to reliable operation (relevance) and can cause failures or leaks with real harm (severity); held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Periodic inspection and checklists catch overt wear, but subtle or hidden degradation frequently goes unnoticed until failure, making this an inconsistent outcome. Thorough inspection of structural, mechanical, and fluid-path components is time-consuming, especially where disassembly is required. Detecting incipient deterioration relies heavily on experienced inspectors' judgement and is only partially aided by instruments. Visual and NDT inspection labor is costly, and missed deterioration can cause expensive failures.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of residual skid contamination remaining undetected before removal clearance
rows.imp_rat: Undetected residual contamination before removal clearance risks release of a compromised skid (relevance) with serious downstream contamination consequences (severity), held below 5.0 average.
rows.sat_rat: Sampling covers representative surfaces but undetected contamination in inaccessible geometry remains a recurring gap that testing regimes don't fully close. Swab sampling and lab turnaround for contamination clearance introduce significant delay. Interpreting where and how to sample for hidden contamination depends heavily on scarce QA and cleaning-validation expertise. Analytical testing and repeat sampling add cost per clearance cycle.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of deviation between indicated and actual temperature, pressure, and flow conditions during operation
rows.imp_rat: Deviation between indicated and actual temperature/pressure/flow undermines control confidence central to the CFJ (relevance) and can silently spoil batches (severity); held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Scheduled calibration and redundant sensing keep indicated-versus-actual deviation small, though drift between calibrations still introduces occasional error. Instrument readings are continuous and real-time, so no time penalty arises from indication. Calibration and verification require metrology skill, but routine operation needs little operator finesse to trust readings. Calibration programs and reference standards cost money, and drifted instruments causing bad batches are costly.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of product-contact surfaces affected by microbial growth during nonuse
rows.imp_rat: Microbial growth on surfaces during nonuse has moderate severity and only intermittent relevance since it concerns idle periods rather than active processing, so it stays well within the cap.
rows.sat_rat: Layup practices reduce but do not eliminate growth in low-flow zones during extended idle periods. Preventing microbial growth during idle periods requires ongoing purging or drying regimens that consume standby time. Effective layup practice needs knowledge of stagnation risks but is procedurally guidable. Nitrogen blanketing, recirculation or drying add utility cost during nonuse.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a direct command outside authorized limits is applied during manual override
rows.imp_rat: An out-of-limit manual override command is a narrower operational event (relevance) but can cause acute process or safety excursions when it occurs (severity); capped.
rows.sat_rat: Some overrides retain hard interlocks but many bypass software limits by design, leaving a genuine recurring exposure during manual intervention. Manual override is invoked quickly when needed, so timing is not the concern here. Manual override deliberately hands control to operator judgement, so safe outcomes depend heavily on operator skill and situational awareness. An unsafe override command can damage equipment or spoil product, incurring significant cost when it happens.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of high-viscosity feedstock obstructing movement through required processing stages
rows.imp_rat: High-viscosity feedstock obstructing movement through stages (relevance) affects throughput; the consequence is a slowdown or blockage (severity) that is generally recoverable, kept under ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Standard viscosity-management measures usually keep flow moving, though seasonal or off-spec viscous batches still cause occasional obstructions. Viscosity-related obstructions slow or halt throughput until heating or flushing clears them, adding downtime. Managing high-viscosity handling relies on operator experience with heating, dilution and pump selection. Heat tracing, jacketing and higher-power pumps address viscosity but at ongoing energy and equipment cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of leaks from disturbed process joints after the upgraded module returns to service
rows.imp_rat: Leaks from disturbed joints after an upgraded module returns to service are a maintenance/re-commissioning error (limited relevance to steady operation), though leaks can cause loss or contamination (moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Standard reinstatement checks, pressure tests and joint integrity verification make post-upgrade joint leaks reasonably rare though not eliminated. Post-upgrade leak testing and re-torquing add commissioning time before return to service. Reassembling disturbed joints correctly needs competent fitters, a widely available skill. Leaks after restart cause rework cost, but re-verification is a modest expense.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of introducing contaminants into the sterile fill-finish area during alignment
rows.imp_rat: Introducing contaminants during alignment in the fill-finish area is a narrow situational error (relevance); it can affect final product sterility (severity) but stays within the imposed cap.
rows.sat_rat: Isolators and RABS reduce exposure, but alignment still involves manual intervention in the most critical zone, leaving a real recurring contamination risk. Alignment work in the fill-finish area is slow because every movement must respect aseptic constraints. Precise mechanical alignment inside a sterile zone without introducing contaminants relies on highly skilled technicians balancing two demanding goals. Extended downtime of fill lines and re-sterilization drive high cost when alignment intrudes.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an installed consumable has incompatible material, dimensions, or operating limits
rows.imp_rat: An installed consumable with incompatible material or limits is an installation error (limited relevance to ongoing operation), but incompatibility can lead to failure or contamination downstream (moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Approved-parts lists, material certificates and spare-part cataloging make incompatible installs uncommon, though substitution errors still slip through. Verifying consumable specs adds procurement and check time that is usually manageable. Confirming material and dimensional compatibility requires documentation literacy and part knowledge, somewhat error-prone. Wrong consumables cause scrap and rework, while spec verification and certified parts add modest cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of leakage from an improperly seated or damaged gasket or seal after replacement
rows.imp_rat: Leakage from an improperly seated gasket after replacement is a maintenance-task error (limited relevance to core processing), though a leak can cause product loss or contamination (moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Torque specs, new-gasket policies and pressure/leak testing after reassembly make post-replacement leaks fairly uncommon, though seating errors still occur. Proper seating and leak testing after seal replacement adds time but fits normal maintenance flow. Correct gasket seating relies on technician skill and torque discipline, which is trainable and moderately reliable. Seals are cheap, but a post-replacement leak causes costly rework and downtime.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of introducing contamination into sterile processing areas during inspection
rows.imp_rat: Introducing contamination during inspection is a comparatively infrequent, procedural event (relevance); its consequences to sterility are meaningful (severity) but constrained under the batch cap.
rows.sat_rat: Access controls and monitoring catch many breaches, but human-borne contamination during inspection is a persistent, incompletely controlled risk. Gowning and aseptic entry protocols slow every inspection into a cleanroom. Contamination avoidance during inspection depends almost entirely on trained personnel behavior and disciplined technique that is hard to sustain. Gowning, environmental monitoring and lost time add cost but are routine.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of contaminating sterile processing areas during instrument access and calibration
rows.imp_rat: Contaminating sterile areas during instrument access and calibration is a peripheral maintenance-adjacent error (relevance) with moderate sterility consequences (severity), kept at or below ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: In-place calibration provisions help, but instrument access remains a manual, judgement-heavy step where sterility breaches recur. Calibration under aseptic conditions requires slow, careful entry and instrument handling. Accessing and calibrating instruments without breaching sterility demands specialized technicians with aseptic training in short supply. Downtime and specialized calibration services add cost, and contamination events are expensive.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percent of sampled plant areas that remain above contamination clearance thresholds after remediation
rows.imp_rat: Sampled areas remaining above clearance thresholds after remediation indicates incomplete decontamination, relevant to release of the site but a remediation-specific measure with recoverable consequences via further cleaning.
rows.sat_rat: Confirmatory sampling against thresholds is standard, but residual exceedances after treatment remain common enough to require re-work. Re-remediation of failed areas adds significant rework time. Achieving clearance depends on skilled selection of remediation methods matched to the contaminant. Areas that fail verification force repeat treatment, driving up cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of contaminants spreading beyond the remediation boundary during work on the plant site
rows.imp_rat: Contaminants spreading beyond a remediation boundary is a site-safety/environmental event with real consequences, but it is peripheral to the plant's routine processing job and occurs only during remediation work.
rows.sat_rat: Bunding, dust suppression and boundary monitoring are mature but migration through soil, groundwater or air still occasionally escapes control. Containment setup and monitoring add time, but delay is not the core loss when a breach occurs. Effective containment relies on experienced remediation contractors who understand pathways for the specific contaminants. Breaches beyond the boundary trigger expensive off-site cleanup and liability.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of hazardous residues and contaminated materials assigned an incorrect regulatory classification
rows.imp_rat: Incorrect regulatory classification of hazardous residues is peripheral to liquid processing itself (low relevance) but misclassification risks regulatory violations and environmental harm (higher severity).
rows.sat_rat: Established coding frameworks and labs help, but ambiguous mixed residues are still misclassified with some regularity. Reclassifying misassigned residues delays downstream shipment. Correct regulatory classification requires waste-code expertise that is genuinely scarce and error-prone. Misclassification risks fines and higher disposal-route costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of damaging shared facility utilities while disconnecting plant-specific safety infrastructure
rows.imp_rat: Damaging shared facility utilities while disconnecting plant-specific safety infrastructure is a decommissioning-phase error, so relevance to the running processing job is limited, though severity is moderate since collateral utility damage can disrupt neighboring operations.
rows.sat_rat: Isolation drawings and marking help, but poor documentation of shared infrastructure means accidental damage during disconnection recurs. Careful separation of plant-specific from shared utilities slows disconnection work. Avoiding damage to shared services requires accurate as-built knowledge and skilled tracing, which is often incomplete on older plants. Damaging shared utilities causes costly disruption to remaining operations and repairs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of contaminating product-contact surfaces or trial media during wet trials
rows.imp_rat: Contaminating product-contact surfaces or trial media during wet trials affects trial validity rather than saleable product, so relevance and severity are moderate and confined to the trial phase.
rows.sat_rat: Wet trials expose surfaces to open handling and temporary connections, so despite cleaning protocols recontamination during the trial is a common recurring gap. Pre-trial CIP/flush cycles and hold checks lengthen the trial preparation window considerably. Correctly sequencing cleaning, sampling and trial media handling demands experienced commissioning staff who are not always available. Trial media and repeated flushes consume real material and utilities, and a contaminated trial means discarding a batch of media.

rows.stk: Plant operators, shift supervisors, training coordinators.
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of contamination of sterile product-contact areas during practical operator training
rows.imp_rat: Contamination of sterile product-contact areas during operator training is a bounded, training-context exposure that is corrected by re-cleaning before production; moderate severity but low centrality to the core job.
rows.sat_rat: Because trainees are by definition error-prone, current supervised-training practice reduces but does not reliably prevent contamination of sterile areas during practical drills. Hands-on training in sterile areas consumes both trainer and line time and lengthens qualification schedules. The whole point is that trainees lack skill, so putting inexperienced operators in contact with sterile surfaces is intrinsically the highest-risk moment for aseptic error. Sacrificial media, gowning and re-sanitization for training runs consume resources that are effectively written off.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of finished liquid lost during routing, dosing, or transfer
rows.imp_rat: Finished liquid lost during routing, dosing, or transfer directly reduces salable yield (moderate relevance), but the loss is typically incremental and recoverable financially (moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Pigging, line-blowing and mass-balance metering recover some product, but hold-up and transfer losses remain a persistent recurring leak. Routing and transfer losses occur during normal timed operations without adding delay. Reducing line-drain and dosing loss depends on careful operator practice and line design familiarity. Product left in lines, over-dosing and transfer holdup represent a steady, accepted material loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of product-contact equipment being damaged during shutdown
rows.imp_rat: Product-contact equipment damaged during shutdown is a transient-phase error with limited relevance to steady-state processing, though severity is moderate because damaged wetted surfaces can compromise later hygiene and require costly repair.
rows.sat_rat: Sequenced shutdown logic and procedures protect equipment reliably in most cases, but abnormal or emergency shutdowns still cause occasional damage. Careful controlled shutdown to protect product-contact equipment takes deliberate, non-rushed time. Avoiding shutdown damage relies on operators knowing correct sequences and thermal/pressure limits. Damaged product-contact equipment is expensive to repair or replace and idles the plant.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of liquid lost from the plant flow path during routing
rows.imp_rat: Liquid lost from the flow path during routing reduces yield and is fairly relevant to efficient processing, but the loss is generally incremental and financially absorbable (moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Established seal, gasket and joint practice keeps in-path losses consistent and rare, with occasional losses from wear or connection errors. Sealed piping and CIP-integrated flow paths route liquid quickly with little time lost to leakage recovery in modern plants. Standard hygienic pipe design and welded/clamped joints minimize the operator judgement needed to prevent routine flow losses. Product loss during routing is a well-understood yield concern managed by closed piping, pigging systems, and drainage recovery, though hold-up and line residues still cost real product.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of nonuniformity in processing-input concentration throughout the liquid stream
rows.imp_rat: Nonuniformity of input concentration through the stream (relevance) affects product consistency; the consequence is variable quality (severity) that is often correctable, held to the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Inline mixers and agitated vessels usually deliver acceptable uniformity, but concentration gradients and stratification still occur with difficult fluids. Achieving uniform concentration through mixing takes blending time that is usually built into the process. Proper mixer design and operation reduce reliance on operator skill to achieve homogeneity. Mixing consumes energy and agitator maintenance, a modest recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of liquid product discarded because of the process upset or corrective action
rows.imp_rat: Liquid product discarded due to process upset is a yield-loss symptom rather than a core control error (moderate relevance), and while costly, the loss is usually financially absorbable (moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Some plants divert off-spec to rework or dump tanks, but much upset product is still routinely written off. Deciding to discard upset product is quick, but the loss is realized regardless of speed. Judging what can be reworked versus discarded relies on operator and quality experience. Product discarded from upsets or corrective actions is a direct, recurring material loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percent of processed liquid outside the prescribed flow balance across parallel streams
rows.imp_rat: Flow imbalance across parallel streams affects throughput consistency (moderately relevant to processing job), but excess variability usually degrades yield/quality gradually rather than causing acute harm (modest severity).
rows.sat_rat: Distributed control with flow measurement on each stream keeps most plants within balance, yet fouling, drift and unmetered streams still cause recurring imbalance. Flow-balancing across parallel streams is largely automated by control loops, so corrective response is fast once deviation is detected. Modern flow controllers reduce reliance on operator finesse, though tuning and troubleshooting still need process expertise. Out-of-balance streams waste product and energy, but automated balancing keeps the give-away modest in well-instrumented plants.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time from detecting an out-of-bound process variable to completing the required setpoint adjustment
rows.imp_rat: Time to complete setpoint adjustment after detecting an out-of-bound variable (relevance) affects control tightness; the consequence is usually minor transient off-spec (severity), kept low and within cap.
rows.sat_rat: Closed-loop control reliably completes routine corrections quickly, though poorly tuned loops or manual interventions can still lag. Automated PID and advanced control loops adjust setpoints within seconds of detecting an out-of-bound variable. Well-tuned control loops reduce reliance on manual operator intervention for routine corrections. Control system tuning and maintenance are standing costs, but fast correction limits off-spec waste.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of imported recipes containing incorrect parameter values or units
rows.imp_rat: Imported recipes containing incorrect parameter values or units affects batch correctness (relevance), and can drive an entire batch off-spec (severity), but it is a narrower data-import edge case ranked below the prior batch.
rows.sat_rat: Import validation is often weak, with limited automated unit/value checking, so incorrect imported parameters remain a recurring and poorly caught problem. Checking imported recipes for wrong values or units is a slow manual reconciliation task. Spotting incorrect units or values in imports demands careful attention and domain knowledge that is easy to lapse. Manual import verification consumes labor, and undetected unit errors can ruin batches at high cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of raw ingredient lost through leaks while charging the feed lines and supply tanks
rows.imp_rat: Raw ingredient lost through leaks while charging feed lines is mainly an efficiency and yield concern rather than a job-blocking one; consequences are cost and minor formulation drift, usually absorbed without endangering the batch.
rows.sat_rat: Standardized sanitary connections make charging leaks infrequent, with occasional gasket failures the residual issue. Leak-driven cleanups and reconnections add incidental time during charging. Proper sanitary connection technique limits leaks and is a well-established operator competency. Sanitary gaskets and clamped fittings largely contain ingredient loss, keeping leak-related material waste low.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of ingredient doses outside specified quantity tolerance
rows.imp_rat: This restatement of item 13 on doses outside specified tolerance is important to correct formulation (relevance) with off-spec consequences (severity), slightly lower as a near-duplicate.
rows.sat_rat: Standard weigh-and-dose control holds specified tolerances well for liquids but manual and minor additions remain a persistent source of deviation. Specified-tolerance dosing runs at line speed through automated feeders with negligible added time. Automated dosing reduces dependence on operator dexterity for hitting specified tolerances. Tight dosing avoids both ingredient overuse and rejected batches, containing material cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of difference between the indicated and delivered ingredient quantity
rows.imp_rat: Difference between indicated and delivered ingredient quantity is a dosing-accuracy error affecting formulation fidelity (relevance) that can shift product quality out of spec (severity), a narrower metrological concern.
rows.sat_rat: Calibrated load cells and flow meters keep indicated and delivered quantities close, though calibration drift and material buildup cause recurring small errors. Feedback weighing corrects indicated-versus-delivered gaps during dosing without operator delay. Calibrated instruments and automatic tare handle accuracy that would otherwise need skilled manual correction. Minimizing the indicated-delivered gap prevents both giveaway and rework, saving material.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a software update introduces control behavior outside the validated state
rows.imp_rat: A software update introducing control behavior outside the validated state is an infrequent change-management event (relevance), but loss of validated control can cause unsafe or non-compliant batches (severity).
rows.sat_rat: GAMP-style change control and regression testing constrain most software regressions, yet unvalidated behavior occasionally emerges after updates. Software validation and regression testing before release add considerable time to update cycles. Verifying control behavior stays within validated state requires specialized automation and validation engineers. Test-environment maintenance and validation effort make software updates expensive.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that upgraded components cause unsafe or unintended system behavior
rows.imp_rat: Upgraded components causing unsafe or unintended behavior is a rare maintenance-lifecycle event (relevance), but unsafe system behavior carries serious safety consequences (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Formal change control and requalification catch most issues, but emergent unsafe behavior from upgrades still slips through and is a recognized risk. Change control, re-qualification, and testing of upgraded components significantly extend project timelines. Assessing upgrade impact on system behavior demands cross-disciplinary engineering judgement that is scarce. Requalification, testing, and documentation for upgrades are substantial cost drivers.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time from activation of a batch-system alarm to operator recognition
rows.imp_rat: Delay from alarm activation to operator recognition matters when fast intervention is needed (severity 5), but alarms are one safeguard among several, keeping it moderately central (relevance 4).
rows.sat_rat: Audible/visual alarms make recognition reliable for single events, but alarm floods and complacency delay recognition in practice. The need is squarely about latency from alarm activation to operator recognition, which annunciators and HMI alerts shorten to seconds. Recognizing an alarm needs little skill once it annunciates clearly, though prioritizing among many does. Faster recognition limits excursion duration and associated loss at low added cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that rated throughput overstates achievable output under target-product and sterile-processing conditions
rows.imp_rat: Overstated rated throughput leads to planning shortfalls (moderate relevance) with commercial rather than catastrophic consequences (moderate severity); held at 4.0.
rows.sat_rat: Vendor ratings are typically taken under ideal or low-viscosity conditions, so achievable sterile output is frequently overstated and rarely validated pre-purchase. Verifying that rated throughput holds under actual viscous-product sterile conditions requires trials that lengthen the acquisition timeline. Interpreting vendor throughput claims against real aseptic-viscous behaviour demands rare process-engineering judgement most purchasers lack. Overstated throughput surfaces as costly capacity shortfalls or duplicate capex after purchase.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of commissioning delay caused by required plant utilities being unavailable or outside specification during scheduled testing
rows.imp_rat: Commissioning delay from unavailable/out-of-spec utilities is a schedule error affecting time-to-startup; moderately relevant to project delivery but consequences are recoverable and confined to the commissioning window.
rows.sat_rat: Utility-driven delays are frequent and hard to prevent, making schedule outcomes unpredictable. Utility unavailability or out-of-spec conditions during scheduled testing is a leading cause of commissioning delay with weak mitigation. Sequencing utilities to be ready needs experienced planning that is often lacking on complex projects. Idle crews and extended commissioning windows from utility delays waste significant money.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of batch material remaining as an unrecoverable vessel heel after transfer
rows.plain: Leave as little material as possible stuck in the container after the batch is moved out, so less product is wasted.
rows.imp_rat: Unrecoverable vessel heel is a yield-loss/waste concern; low-moderate relevance and severity.
rows.sat_rat: Heel volume is fairly predictable per vessel, so it is consistent even if wasteful. Draining and heel recovery add transfer time that is largely accepted as routine. Minimizing heel depends on vessel design and operator drainage technique more than special skill. The unrecoverable heel is a persistent direct material loss on every batch with limited remedy.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of product-contact surfaces that fail release criteria after decontamination
rows.imp_rat: Product-contact surfaces failing release criteria after decontamination is a detected-failure metric (relevance) that triggers rework rather than escape (severity); scored modestly under the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Validated cleaning methods give reasonably consistent release pass rates, but hard-to-clean surfaces and legacy fouling still cause recurring failures. Failed release criteria trigger reclean-and-retest loops that extend the timeline. Decontamination effectiveness leans on validated cleaning knowledge and analytical judgement held by specialists. Cleaning agents, labor and repeat analytics drive up spend when surfaces fail.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of critical aseptic controls omitted from skid operating procedures
rows.imp_rat: Omitting critical aseptic controls from procedures undermines contamination defense (relevance) and can lead to sterility failures (severity), but it is procedural and capped by ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Structured SOP templates and cross-functional review capture most critical controls, yet subtle aseptic steps are still occasionally left out. SOP authoring and review cycles are lengthy but do not gate operation directly once complete. Whether critical aseptic controls end up in procedures depends on the author's process knowledge and the reviewers' aseptic expertise. An omitted control drives deviations and reprocessing, but the SOP effort itself is low-cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of entering incorrect setpoints, limits, or sequence values for the current batch
rows.imp_rat: Entering incorrect setpoints/limits/sequence values impairs correct batch execution (relevance) and can yield off-spec product (severity), but the ceiling holds it modest.
rows.sat_rat: Electronic recipe management and range checks catch many bad entries, though manual configuration steps remain a recurring source of setpoint errors. Recipe-driven entry with stored parameters speeds setup versus manual keying of every value. Where recipes are downloaded automatically operator burden is low, but manual limit/sequence entry still depends on careful, knowledgeable input. A wrong entered value can waste a batch, and rework of misconfigured runs consumes resources.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of equipment misalignment after anchors are secured
rows.imp_rat: Equipment misalignment after anchoring is an installation-quality error (limited relevance to processing function), causing wear or vibration over time (low-moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Precision alignment after anchoring is a mature practice, so residual misalignment is usually within tolerance. Post-anchoring alignment checks and shimming add time but are standard installation steps. Millwrights use established laser and dial-gauge alignment methods requiring solid but common trade skill. Alignment tooling and shims are inexpensive relative to the equipment.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of misalignment between mating module interfaces during assembly
rows.imp_rat: Misalignment between mating module interfaces during assembly is an installation-quality error that only marginally affects the ongoing job of processing liquid product (moderate relevance), and consequences are usually reworkable rather than catastrophic (moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Modular skids arrive with pre-defined interfaces, so mating misalignment is generally well controlled. Fitting mating interfaces during assembly is routine and rarely a schedule bottleneck. Module interface alignment relies on fitters following drawings with widely available skills. Gaskets, fasteners and fit-up labor are modest costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of trained personnel unable to recognize and respond correctly to hazardous process deviations
rows.imp_rat: Personnel failing to recognize hazardous deviations can escalate into safety incidents (high severity) and matters to safe operation (moderate relevance), but the batch ceiling limits it to 4.0.
rows.sat_rat: Standard training and competency assessments exist but verifying real-world response capability is inconsistent, so a meaningful fraction of personnel remain unprepared for actual deviations. Training programs consume real preparation time yet are routinely scheduled before handover. This need is fundamentally about building operator competence to recognize hazardous deviations, and status-quo classroom training only partly transfers that judgement. Training and simulator time carry cost, and undertrained staff cause incidents that are far costlier.

rows.source_job: Establish startup and shutdown operating procedures
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of startup and shutdown steps ordered contrary to plant dependencies
rows.imp_rat: Startup/shutdown steps ordered against dependencies risk equipment damage or upsets (moderate severity) and matter to safe operation (moderate relevance), yet remain below the batch ceiling of 4.0.
rows.sat_rat: Written sequences and some interlocks exist, but reliance on manual adherence means dependency-order errors still occur with regularity during preparation. Sequencing startup and shutdown steps against dependencies is deliberate and can be slow when done manually from procedures. Correct ordering depends heavily on experienced commissioning operators who understand plant interlocks, and mistakes trace directly to that scarce judgement. Out-of-sequence steps can damage equipment or spoil viscous batches, imposing avoidable rework and material costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of in-process material produced outside acceptance criteria while plant settings are adjusted
rows.imp_rat: In-process material made outside acceptance during setting adjustments is expected transitional waste; it is only modestly relevant to the core job and its cost is a bounded, recoverable yield loss. Well under the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Model-based and gradual ramping reduce transition off-spec, but standard practice still yields recurring transient material outside acceptance. Setpoint adjustments settle over a transition period during which off-spec material accumulates before the plant stabilizes. Skilled operators can shorten transitions with anticipatory moves, but the transient material loss still depends on their finesse. In-process material made during adjustment transients is a well-known and largely accepted waste stream with only partial recovery.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of mismatch between transfer flow and receiving-stage demand during continuous processing
rows.imp_rat: Mismatch between transfer flow and receiving-stage demand directly disrupts continuous processing (moderate-to-high relevance), though consequences are usually recoverable throttling issues (moderate severity); held to the 4.0 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Continuous level and flow control keeps stages balanced most of the time, with residual mismatch during upsets and viscous-fluid transfer variability. Buffer tanks and level control absorb short-term flow mismatches without immediate operator intervention. Cascade level/flow control handles routine balancing, reducing the operator judgement needed to match transfer to demand. Mismatch causes overflow loss or starvation-induced downtime, costs that surge tanks partly but not fully mitigate.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of installing a sealing element with incompatible material or dimensions
rows.imp_rat: Installing an incompatible sealing element can cause leaks or seal failure (moderate-high severity) and affects containment (moderate relevance), but ranking caps it at 4.0.
rows.sat_rat: Standardized spares kits and BOMs reduce mismatches, yet incorrect-material or size seal installs still occur when documentation is weak. Verifying seal material and dimensions before install adds little time when references are at hand. Avoiding an incompatible seal hinges on the technician's knowledge or accurate spare-part documentation, a recurring skill-dependent error. Wrong seals cause scrapped parts and repeat downtime, but correct spares are inexpensive.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of residual viscous material remaining in pipes, pumps, and transfer lines after flushing
rows.imp_rat: Residual viscous material after flushing is a real cross-contamination and yield concern (moderate-high severity) tied to cleaning effectiveness (moderate relevance), but capped at 4.0 per ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Viscous residue clings in pumps and dead legs, so line clearing is a recurring, imperfectly-solved challenge despite standard flush routines. Flushing pipes, pumps and transfer lines of viscous material demands prolonged flush cycles. Effective flushing depends on operators knowing hold-up points and correct flush sequencing. Large flush volumes of solvent or product-displacing fluid are consumed and often wasted.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of incompatible process chemicals being combined in the same waste stream during segregation
rows.imp_rat: Combining incompatible process chemicals in a waste stream can cause dangerous reactions (high severity), but waste segregation is somewhat peripheral to the plant's core processing job (moderate relevance); capped at 4.0.
rows.sat_rat: Compatibility charts, segregation protocols, and labeling make dangerous combinations uncommon, though errors during rushed cleanout remain a known hazard. Segregating incompatible chemicals into separate containers slows the waste consolidation process. Chemical compatibility judgement relies on trained personnel and safety data sheet interpretation. Extra containers and segregated handling add modest cost that prevents catastrophic reaction expense.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of concentration variation across tank contents during agitation
rows.imp_rat: Concentration variation across tank contents during agitation affects batch homogeneity (modest severity) and is moderately relevant, but the ranking keeps it low in this batch.
rows.sat_rat: Validated agitation designs achieve uniform concentration reliably for most fluids, though viscous or stratified contents still show variation. Achieving homogeneity takes defined mixing time that is generally well characterized. Agitator selection and mixing scale-up require engineering expertise to avoid dead zones in large or viscous batches. Agitation consumes energy and mixer capital, and poor mixing can waste batches.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of circulation with material flow outside specified limits
rows.imp_rat: Circulation flow outside specified limits affects process uniformity with modest severity and moderate relevance, placing it comfortably below the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Metered, closed-loop circulation control is mature and keeps flow within spec consistently where instrumented. Flow meters and pump control adjust circulation rates promptly to stay within limits. Flow control loops handle regulation automatically, needing little operator finesse once configured. Flow instrumentation and pump control are standard costs; out-of-limit flow wastes energy and can affect quality.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a relief device is accepted outside its specified activation condition
rows.imp_rat: Accepting a relief device outside its activation condition carries safety severity, yet as a specific acceptance-check error it has limited relevance to core processing outcomes.
rows.sat_rat: Documented pop/set testing before return-to-service makes acceptance outside spec rare. Set-pressure verification on a test bench is a quick, defined step in the servicing flow. Standard pop-testing reduces reliance on rare expertise for verifying activation condition. Bench test gear and gas are inexpensive relative to the risk they mitigate.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of lubricant entering product-contact surfaces during maintenance
rows.imp_rat: Lubricant entering product-contact surfaces during maintenance is a genuine contamination severity concern but occurs only in maintenance windows, giving it low relevance to routine processing.
rows.sat_rat: Procedures and barrier practices usually keep lubricant out, but human error occasionally introduces contamination. Careful lubricant control during agitator/valve service adds modest task time. Preventing lube ingress depends on technician discipline in using food/pharma-grade lube and containment. Food-grade lubricants and cleanup add minor cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of serviced relief devices returned to service outside operability requirements
rows.imp_rat: Serviced relief devices returned outside operability is a safety-relevant defect with moderate severity, but it is a maintenance-QA error of low centrality to the tank's processing job.
rows.sat_rat: Certified reconditioning and bench verification make out-of-spec returns fairly uncommon. Relief-device bench servicing and reset is a standardized, schedulable turnaround activity. Valve shops and certified technicians handle set-pressure adjustment with well-established methods. Bench testing and recertification are routine, moderate recurring costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a required plant utility is unavailable at scheduled startup
rows.imp_rat: A required utility unavailable at scheduled startup delays production (limited relevance to the core processing job), with mainly schedule/economic consequences (low-moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Pre-start checklists and utility monitoring make availability reliable in most plants, though occasional unnoticed utility shortfalls still stall startups. Utility readiness checks add lead time but are routine and scheduled ahead of startup. Confirming utilities (steam, air, water, power) is a checklist task requiring modest skill. A missing utility at startup wastes crew time and delays production, a moderate recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of startup sequences halted by unresolved safety or equipment interlock conditions
rows.imp_rat: Startup sequences halted by unresolved interlocks are a startup-availability nuisance (limited relevance to steady processing), with mostly delay-based consequences (low-moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Well-defined interlock systems reliably enforce safe start, and sequenced startup logic reduces surprise halts, though unresolved permissives still stall sequences. Interlock-halted startups cost time to clear, but modern systems flag the offending condition quickly. Resolving interlock trips often needs operators who understand the permissive logic, creating dependence on experienced staff. Halted startups waste labour and utility warm-up, but interlocks prevent far costlier equipment damage.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the operator misinterprets a process reading because its identifier, unit, or status indication is ambiguous
rows.imp_rat: Operator misinterpreting an ambiguous reading identifier/unit is an interface-clarity error (limited relevance to core processing), and while it could prompt a wrong action, most are caught (low-moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: HMI standards, tag conventions and engineering units on displays reduce ambiguity substantially, but legacy screens and inconsistent labelling still cause recurring misreads. Ambiguous tags or units rarely slow reading directly but cause time-consuming confusion when misread. Correct interpretation of ambiguous identifiers leans on operator experience and plant-specific knowledge. Misinterpreted readings can trigger costly wrong actions or off-spec product.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of difference between the collected sample and the process stream at the designated sampling point
rows.imp_rat: Difference between collected sample and actual process stream (relevance) affects measurement accuracy; consequences are indirect quality-decision errors (severity), both modest and capped.
rows.sat_rat: Standard sampling points and procedures give reasonable representativeness, yet stratification and dead-leg sampling still produce non-representative samples. Grab sampling and lab turnaround introduce delay, but the representativeness error itself is the concern here. Obtaining a representative sample requires proper technique and sampling-point design, and poor practice biases results. Unrepresentative samples cause wrong disposition decisions that waste product, but sampling hardware is inexpensive.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required sterile-processing and contamination-control criteria omitted from the plant specification
rows.imp_rat: Omitting sterile-processing criteria from the specification can allow contamination to reach product (notable severity for viscous/aseptic runs) and is fairly central to the processing job (moderate relevance), but ranking caps it at 4.0.
rows.sat_rat: Even with URS templates and GMP checklists, viscous sterile requirements are non-standard enough that criteria are routinely omitted, leaving the outcome inconsistent. Assembling a complete sterile-processing and contamination-control specification takes lengthy iterative review cycles today, and omissions surface late. Getting the full set of cGMP/aseptic criteria into a spec relies on scarce sterile-processing subject-matter experts whose availability gates completeness. Missed contamination-control criteria drive expensive requalification and retrofit downstream, but that cost is invisible at spec time.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of clean-in-place cycles stopping before completion
rows.imp_rat: Clean-in-place cycles stopping before completion is a reliability nuisance with moderate relevance, and severity is limited because an incomplete cycle can be restarted before product runs, though it costs downtime.
rows.sat_rat: Modern CIP automation with robust sequencing and alarm handling completes most cycles, though sensor faults and utility interruptions still cause occasional aborts. Aborted cycles waste time and force restarts, but automation limits how often this happens. Diagnosing and preventing premature stops requires some troubleshooting skill that is generally available. Incomplete cycles waste water, chemicals and energy on restarts, a recurring but bounded cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of damaging product-contact housings or mating surfaces while removing or installing consumables
rows.imp_rat: Damaging product-contact housings or mating surfaces while changing consumables is a maintenance-phase error with modest relevance, and severity is moderate because damaged surfaces impair sealing and hygiene until repaired.
rows.sat_rat: Proper tooling, procedures and trained fitters keep handling damage fairly infrequent, though occasional scoring or scratches still occur. Careful removal and installation of consumables adds modest time to maintenance tasks. Avoiding damage to housings and mating surfaces depends on technician care and technique, a common but attention-dependent skill. Damaged product-contact housings are costly to replace, while careful handling costs little.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of remediation waste loads rejected because their contents do not meet disposal acceptance criteria
rows.imp_rat: Rejected remediation waste loads that fail disposal acceptance criteria are peripheral to the core processing job (low relevance) but cause rework, delays, and potential compliance exposure when they occur (moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Waste characterization and pre-acceptance profiling are mature practice, so rejections are uncommon but still happen with mischaracterized loads. Rejected loads mean truck turnaround and reprocessing delays. Meeting acceptance criteria needs staff who understand facility-specific waste profiles. Each rejected load incurs return transport and reprocessing charges.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of concentration variation across the batch after mixing
rows.imp_rat: Concentration variation across the batch after mixing directly undermines batch uniformity, the essence of the CFJ, and out-of-tolerance concentration causes potent/subpotent product with severe consequences. Capped at mean 4.0 by ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Standard mixing and PAT/in-line monitoring provide fairly consistent concentration, yet localized gradients and segregation still occur, keeping this only acceptably served. Concentration uniformity is achieved through fixed mixing durations that add limited time. Ensuring uniform concentration leans on established mixing know-how, partly codified in validated procedures. Concentration variation risks out-of-spec batches and material waste when segments differ.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of composition variation across the batch after mixing
rows.imp_rat: Composition variation across the batch after mixing is central to the CFJ of batch preparation — an inhomogeneous batch fails its core purpose, and the consequence (out-of-spec product, rejected lot) is severe. Ceiling applies, so capped at mean 4.0.
rows.sat_rat: Validated mixing profiles and equipment qualification deliver consistent composition in most cases, but blend-uniformity failures remain a recognized recurring risk. Achieving homogeneity requires defined mix cycles, and adequate blend time is generally built into the recipe. Mixing to a validated endpoint depends partly on operator judgement, though validated cycle parameters reduce this dependence. Composition variation can lead to rejected or reworked batches, a notable resource loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of ambiguity in identifying the abnormal condition associated with an active alarm
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity in identifying the abnormal condition behind an alarm slows diagnosis (relevance 4) but rarely causes direct harm since operators can investigate before acting (severity 4).
rows.sat_rat: Alarm rationalization and grouping help, but poorly configured or flooding alarms still leave operators guessing at the real fault. Diagnosing the true abnormal condition behind an alarm often takes time when messages are cryptic. Resolving ambiguous alarms leans heavily on experienced operators who know the plant, a persistent expertise dependence. Misdiagnosis prolongs excursions and wastes product, an ongoing cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of deviation in batch conditions from acceptable limits while paused
rows.imp_rat: Deviation of batch conditions while paused matters to holding integrity (relevance moderate) and can spoil a batch (severity moderate), but held to the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Continued control of key parameters during pause is standard, but excursions during power events or long holds remain a recurring vulnerability. Holding conditions are maintained without ongoing time input once a pause is initiated. Judging acceptable deviation during a pause draws on process knowledge, especially for long holds. Drift beyond limits while paused can scrap batch material, a significant cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of connecting a batch preparation system inlet or outlet to the wrong process line
rows.imp_rat: Connecting a system inlet/outlet to the wrong process line is moderately central to correct batch routing and could cross-contaminate or misdirect product, a severe outcome. Capped by ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Tagged lines, P&IDs, and pre-startup safety reviews catch most mistakes, though a residual rate of misconnections persists where labeling is ambiguous or missing. Verifying line assignments during hookup adds hours but is a standard part of commissioning walkdowns so it rarely stalls the schedule badly. Correct connection relies heavily on experienced pipefitters and commissioning engineers reading P&IDs, so misreads still occur when skilled staff are stretched. A wrong-line connection means rework and possible product loss, but P&ID checks and labeling keep this expense occasional rather than routine.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of connecting inlet or outlet piping to the wrong plant line
rows.imp_rat: Mis-connecting inlet/outlet piping to the wrong plant line is a rework/setup error somewhat central to correct routing, and wrong connections can cross-contaminate with fairly severe consequence. Capped by ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Line lists, color coding, and isometric drawings make correct plant-line connection a mature practice with only occasional slips at ambiguous tie-in points. Plant-line identification is embedded in standard walkdown procedures and seldom becomes a schedule bottleneck. It depends on drawing literacy and plant familiarity, so newcomers to a site are more prone to routing piping to the wrong header. Reworking mis-routed piping is costly in labor and downtime, but is contained by established verification steps.

rows.source_job: Integrate control system with plant network
rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of batch records transferred to plant systems with missing or incorrect values
rows.imp_rat: Batch records transferred with missing or incorrect values affects traceability and compliance (relevance moderate) with moderate severity, kept within the 4.0 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Structured record transfer catches gross failures, but missing or incorrect field values on transfer remain a recurring data-integrity issue. Verifying transferred batch records for completeness adds reconciliation time. Spotting missing or incorrect transferred values requires familiarity with expected record content. Manual record verification consumes effort; bad records trigger costly investigations.

rows.source_job: Integrate control system with plant network
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that required batch data fail to reach plant control, historian, or MES destinations
rows.imp_rat: Batch data failing to reach control/historian/MES affects record completeness and traceability (relevance moderate) with moderate consequences (severity), held at the 4.0 mean.
rows.sat_rat: Modern MES/historian integrations with buffering deliver most data reliably, but interface drops and misconfigurations still cause intermittent losses. Confirming data reached historian/MES destinations can require manual checks that add delay. Diagnosing why data failed to arrive needs integration and IT skills not always on hand. Interface middleware and monitoring have real cost but are commonly deployed.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of mixing, temperature, dosing, or control results outside approved specifications in the trial batch
rows.imp_rat: Trial-batch results outside specification for mixing, temperature, or dosing are caught during trials rather than production (relevance), limiting damage to rework and delay rather than shipped defects (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Trial batching is a standard practice that surfaces most out-of-spec results before production, though it does not prevent them and some slip to first production. Trial batches themselves take time to run and to analyze results against spec. Interpreting mixing, temperature, and dosing results requires experienced process staff. Trial batches consume material and equipment time, a meaningful cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of critical trial-batch results falling outside specification
rows.imp_rat: Critical trial-batch results falling outside specification is a duplicate framing of item 10; being a trial-stage catch it is moderately relevant (relevance) with contained, non-shipping consequences (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Focused monitoring of critical trial results catches most excursions, yet variability in the first trials still yields recurring out-of-spec critical points. Measuring critical trial-batch results against spec is a defined but time-bounded activity. Assessing critical-parameter conformance needs quality and process expertise. Testing critical results adds analytical cost but prevents costlier production failures.

rows.source_job: Map ingredient sources to system inputs
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of ingredient sources mapped to incorrect system inputs or storage locations
rows.imp_rat: Ingredients mapped to incorrect system inputs/storage duplicates the wrong-inlet concern; it is moderately central to correct batch formulation and carries severe wrong-material consequences. Capped by ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Configuration checks and physical verification reduce mapping errors, but incorrect source-to-input mappings remain a recurring residual failure. Confirming ingredient-source mappings to system inputs and storage adds verification effort. Mapping sources to correct inputs and storage relies on layout knowledge and careful configuration. Incorrect mappings waste material and risk contamination, a real cost exposure.

rows.source_job: Map ingredient sources to system inputs
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of ingredient sources assigned to the wrong system inlet or storage location
rows.imp_rat: Assigning ingredient sources to the wrong inlet or storage location is fairly central since wrong-ingredient loading corrupts the batch, and the consequence of a wrong-material batch is severe waste or harm. Capped by ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Line-verification procedures and source addressing catch most misassignments, but wrong-inlet errors still occur where checks are manual. Verifying ingredient-source-to-inlet assignments requires physical and configuration checks that add time. Correctly assigning sources to inlets needs operators who know the plant piping and layout. A wrong inlet assignment can cause cross-contamination or waste at high cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of batch, material, or status fields mapped incorrectly between designated systems
rows.imp_rat: Incorrectly mapped batch/material/status fields between systems is a data-integrity error somewhat peripheral to physically preparing a batch, though a mis-mapped status can cause release of wrong material with moderate consequence. Capped by ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Interface mapping tools help, but incorrect field mappings between systems still recur, especially with schema changes, keeping reliability middling. Configuring and checking field mappings between designated systems takes appreciable setup time. Mapping batch/material/status fields correctly requires cross-system domain knowledge. Mapping and testing labor is moderate, with rework cost when mappings are wrong.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of critical batch, material, or quality data mapped to incorrect external-system fields
rows.imp_rat: Critical data mapped to incorrect external-system fields harms data integrity across systems (relevance moderate) with moderate consequences (severity), held at the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Field-mapping errors to external systems are a well-known, frequently under-tested failure mode, so this need is only weakly served today. Validating field mappings to external systems is a slow, one-by-one configuration task. Correct mapping of critical data to external fields demands integration specialists who understand both systems. Integration mapping effort is significant and remapping after errors is expensive.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that batch material leaves validated holding conditions during suspension
rows.imp_rat: Material leaving validated holding conditions during suspension threatens product integrity (severity notable) and is relevant to job reliability, but the ordering caps it below higher batches.
rows.sat_rat: Automatic hold logic maintains agitation and temperature during pauses fairly well, but extended or unexpected suspensions still drift out of range. Maintaining holding conditions during suspension is passive once set, adding no active time burden. Keeping material within holding conditions relies on operators and supervisors monitoring the paused state. Material leaving validated conditions during a pause can force disposal, a real cost risk.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of formulation parameter values entered incorrectly for the scheduled batch
rows.imp_rat: Incorrect formulation parameter entry directly corrupts the batch recipe (relevance moderate-high) with moderate consequences, held at the 4.0 ceiling despite its importance.
rows.sat_rat: Recipe-driven parameter loading with range checks makes entry errors uncommon, though manual overrides and setpoint changes remain an occasional source. Parameter entry via recipe download is quick and avoids manual keying delays. Pulling values from validated recipes rather than manual entry sharply reduces the operator skill and attention needed to avoid entry errors. An incorrectly entered parameter can spoil a batch, a cost that automated download largely prevents.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of formulation parameters entered with incorrect values
rows.imp_rat: Formulation parameters entered with incorrect values is near-duplicate of the entry-error need; central to correct preparation (relevance) with moderate severity, capped at 4.0.
