rows.sat_rat: Recipe management with configured limits, forced-value checks and e-signatures catches most bad entries, making this a mature, well-served need though occasional out-of-range values still pass. Recipe entry itself is quick, but time is not the primary concern for parameter-value correctness. Modern MES/electronic batch record systems reduce reliance on operator expertise via dropdown selection of approved formulas rather than free-typed values. Incorrect parameters that slip through can scrap an entire batch, so the cost stakes are high though usually contained by validation.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which the withdrawn sample differs from batch material at the specified sampling point and stage
rows.imp_rat: A withdrawn sample that differs from actual batch material at the sampling point moderately undermines the job by misrepresenting batch quality, and the consequence is release decisions on false data of moderate-to-high severity. Capped by ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Sample representativeness is a known weak spot where manual withdrawal introduces variability that standard practice only partly controls. Manual sampling at defined points takes little time but must be timed to the correct process stage. Representative sampling depends heavily on operator technique and adherence to sampling SOPs, a persistent skill dependency. An unrepresentative sample can misjudge a good batch or pass a bad one, with costly release-decision consequences.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which the withdrawn sample differs from the batch at the specified process point
rows.imp_rat: Sample non-representativeness at the process point is relevant to verifying batch correctness but is a near-duplicate measurement-error concern; consequences are erroneous quality judgments of moderate severity. Capped by ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: This is among the worst-served needs because manual/point sampling routinely fails to capture true batch state and few robust automated alternatives are widely deployed. Grabbing a sample at a process point is fast, so time is not the limiting factor. Getting a sample that truly reflects the batch at that point relies on operator placement and technique that resist standardization. Non-representative samples drive wrong disposition decisions that can scrap or wrongly release material.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a prepared batch exceeds its approved holding limit before filling
rows.imp_rat: A prepared batch exceeding its holding limit before filling can force disposal (moderate severity) and is relevant to timely prep, but the ranking caps the mean at 4.0.
rows.sat_rat: Hold timers and alerts exist but cross-department scheduling gaps mean expired holds before filling still occur with some regularity. Tracking elapsed hold time is straightforward, but downstream filling delays that breach limits are often outside batch-system control. Managing hold-time limits depends on operators and schedulers coordinating handoffs, a coordination burden more than a system feature. Exceeding an approved holding limit can force disposal of a finished batch, a high-cost outcome.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of batch quantity entries differing from the approved target
rows.imp_rat: Batch quantity entries differing from the approved target affects yield and compliance (relevance moderate) with moderate severity, constrained to the ceiling by ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Automated dosing with tolerance checks and reconciliation against approved targets makes this reliably handled by standard practice, with residual manual-add errors. Load-cell dispensing and automated weighing are fast, so time seldom drives quantity errors. Guided weigh-and-dispense workflows lower the skill needed to hit approved targets without deep operator judgement. Quantity deviations waste raw material and can force reprocessing, a meaningful but usually bounded cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of actuating the wrong batch preparation function in manual mode
rows.imp_rat: Actuating the wrong batch function in manual mode is an edge-case operator error not central to normal automated batching (relevance), but could cause equipment or product harm when it happens (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Confirmation dialogs and permissive interlocks help, but manual mode by design removes many safeguards, so consistency depends heavily on the operator. Manual-mode actuation itself is quick, but recovering from a wrong action costs time. Manual mode inherently shifts the burden onto the operator's attention and training, and mislabeled or crowded HMIs make wrong actuations a live risk. A wrong manual function can damage product or equipment, an occasional but expensive loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an unintended function is actuated in manual mode
rows.imp_rat: An unintended function actuated in manual mode duplicates item 16 and is peripheral to routine batch preparation (relevance), though it can damage product or equipment when triggered (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Two-step confirmations and access levels reduce but do not eliminate unintended manual actions, which remain a recognized weak spot. Unintended actuation is instantaneous to trigger and slow to undo. Preventing unintended actuation in manual mode rests largely on operator vigilance and familiarity with the HMI layout. An unintended function can spoil a batch or trip equipment, incurring real but sporadic cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the batch cycle resumes from an incorrect or unauthorized recovery point
rows.imp_rat: Resuming from an incorrect/unauthorized recovery point is fairly central to safe batch continuation and can produce a corrupted batch, but capped per batch-3 ceiling. Relevance is moderate-high and severity notable, yet held to a 4.0 mean.
rows.sat_rat: ISA- models and access control reduce wrong resumes, but complex holds and abnormal states leave residual uncertainty. Authorized recovery-point resumption is fast where procedural logic guides it, slower where operators must decide. Correct and authorized resumption depends on operator understanding of the interrupted state, a skill-heavy task. An unauthorized or wrong recovery point risks batch loss, incurring notable cost when it happens.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that critical wear or damage remains undetected during scheduled service
rows.imp_rat: Undetected wear or damage during scheduled service can cause later failures (severity moderate) but is a maintenance concern peripheral to the core prep job (relevance low), constrained by ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Preventive service intervals catch much wear, but subtle or early-stage damage between intervals is regularly missed. Scheduled service inspections are time-boxed, which can limit how thoroughly wear is examined. Recognizing early wear or incipient damage relies heavily on the inspector's experience. Missed wear leads to unplanned failures whose cost far exceeds inspection effort.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of wear, corrosion, cracks, or leaks remaining undetected in inaccessible system areas
rows.imp_rat: Undetected corrosion or leaks in inaccessible areas is an integrity/maintenance risk (severity moderate) but peripheral to executing a batch (relevance low), kept under ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Wear, corrosion, and leaks in inaccessible areas routinely go undetected because standard inspection cannot reach them, making this a chronic, poorly served pain. Accessing inaccessible areas requires disassembly that greatly lengthens inspection or is skipped entirely. Detecting hidden corrosion or cracks demands specialized NDT skills often unavailable on site. Disassembly or advanced NDT for hidden areas is expensive, so it is done sparingly.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that corrosion, cracks, leaks, or wear remain undetected during inspection
rows.imp_rat: Undetected corrosion/cracks/leaks during inspection carries moderate failure consequences (severity) but is a maintenance-inspection concern only loosely central to batch prep (relevance low).
rows.sat_rat: Routine visual inspection catches obvious defects but subtle corrosion, hairline cracks, and slow leaks are frequently missed. Visual and routine inspections are quick but shallow, trading thoroughness for schedule adherence. Reliable detection of corrosion, cracks, and leaks depends on trained inspector judgement and consistency. Basic inspection is cheap, but missed defects cause costly failures and rework.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that intermittent dosing drift remains undetected during verification
rows.imp_rat: Undetected intermittent dosing drift directly affects batch accuracy (relevance) and can quietly degrade quality (severity), but ranking constrains the mean to 4.0.
rows.sat_rat: Intermittent drift is notoriously hard to reproduce during scheduled verification, so it frequently escapes detection and remains a chronic pain. Detecting intermittent drift often requires extended or repeated observation that periodic verification rarely provides. Catching transient, non-repeatable drift demands high vigilance and diagnostic skill that is scarce. Intermittent faults drive repeated troubleshooting and wasted verification runs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of an interlock falsely passing under conditions that do not represent its actual trip condition
rows.imp_rat: An interlock falsely passing hides a real safety exposure (severity moderate) but this test-integrity issue is peripheral to core batch execution (relevance low), within the cap.
rows.sat_rat: Simulated or forced-input testing frequently confirms only that a signal path works, not that the actual physical trip condition activates the interlock, leaving latent false-pass failures. Forcing an interlock into a genuine trip condition takes extra setup time that crews often shortcut with simulated signals. Distinguishing a true functional trip from a bypassed or simulated pass requires deep understanding of the loop and is a common source of expert-dependent error. Creating real trip conditions can require process disruption or test rigs, and the cost pressure pushes technicians toward cheaper simulated checks that mask false passes.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of installed safety interlocks omitted from functional testing
rows.imp_rat: Omitting safety interlocks from functional testing risks undetected safety gaps (severity moderate) but is a commissioning/verification activity peripheral to routine prep (relevance low).
rows.sat_rat: Paper or CMMS-based safety-loop test checklists standardize coverage in regulated batch plants, but reliance on an accurate interlock inventory means additions or modifications still slip through untested. Interlock testing follows periodic maintenance windows, and today's checklist-driven procedures generally allocate adequate time though full testing can be skipped under production pressure. Verifying every installed interlock demands experienced technicians who know the plant's full safety architecture, and undocumented or added interlocks are easily overlooked by less-seasoned staff. Comprehensive interlock testing consumes downtime and labor, creating incentives to abbreviate coverage that raise the risk of omissions.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of validated control configurations or batch recipes altered during the software update
rows.imp_rat: Validated configurations or recipes altered during a software update is a maintenance-integrity concern outside normal batch running (relevance), which can silently corrupt subsequent batches if undetected (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Version control and configuration management typically preserve validated recipes, though inadvertent alterations during updates still occur. Reviewing which recipes and configurations an update touches adds review time but is bounded. Identifying affected validated configurations needs configuration-management expertise that not all sites maintain well. Configuration audits and re-approval add moderate documentation cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that upgraded components are incompatible with existing mechanical, electrical, or control interfaces
rows.imp_rat: Incompatible upgraded components against existing interfaces is largely an integration/maintenance concern peripheral to running a given batch (relevance 4); it causes downtime and rework rather than product harm (severity 4).
rows.sat_rat: Documented interface specs and pre-installation checks catch most incompatibilities, but legacy-system mismatches still cause occasional integration failures. Compatibility checks and interface testing before installation add lead time but are plannable. Assessing mechanical/electrical/control interface fit requires experienced integration engineers. Interface adapters, rework, and testing add cost when incompatibilities surface late.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that displayed parameters misrepresent the batch preparation system's actual condition
rows.imp_rat: Displayed parameters misrepresenting actual condition can mislead operators (severity moderate) and is relevant to safe control, but the ranking holds the mean at or below 4.0.
rows.sat_rat: Scheduled instrument calibration and validation practices catch most drift, yet sensor faults, wiring issues, or stale HMI values can still misrepresent actual condition between checks. Cross-checking displayed values against field readings adds time but is a routine part of calibration rounds. Recognizing that a plausible-looking reading is actually wrong requires process familiarity and instrument knowledge that not all maintenance staff possess. Calibration equipment and periodic verification carry recurring cost but are broadly budgeted in maintained batch facilities.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of displayed parameter readings that misrepresent actual system condition
rows.imp_rat: A near-duplicate display-misrepresentation need; moderate relevance to correct operation and moderate severity from bad decisions, capped by batch position.
rows.sat_rat: Established calibration intervals bound the percentage of inaccurate readings, but between-interval drift and undetected failures keep a residual fraction misrepresenting condition. Periodic calibration sweeps consume planned time and typically keep the fraction of bad readings low. Quantifying and reducing the share of misleading readings requires metrology skill that varies across sites. Maintaining low error rates across all instruments means recurring calibration and replacement spending that facilities generally accept.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of a structural or process assembly shifting or falling during disassembly and rigging
rows.imp_rat: An assembly shifting or falling during disassembly is a safety hazard (severity high) but is peripheral to routine batch preparation execution (low relevance), so kept under the cap.
rows.sat_rat: Engineered lift plans and certified rigging substantially control fall/shift risk, but unexpected loads and corroded fixings still cause incidents. Rigging plans and lift sequencing add planning time but are standard for any major disassembly. Certified riggers and lift planners are a recognized trade, though availability of experienced crews varies by region. Cranes, rigging gear, and certified personnel are significant costs that are nonetheless routinely budgeted.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of structural or process assemblies shifting or falling during removal
rows.imp_rat: Assemblies shifting or falling during removal is a maintenance-safety error largely outside the core preparation job, but the consequence—personnel injury—can be severe. Kept low on relevance and capped by ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Standard demolition sequencing and bracing reduce collapse risk, yet unpredicted structural behavior during removal remains a recurring hazard. Controlled removal sequences take time that is normally accounted for in demolition scheduling. Safe removal of heavy assemblies relies on experienced rigging and demolition judgement not always present. Temporary bracing, cranes, and skilled labor drive cost but are standard demolition line items.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required sterile manufacturing and contamination-control evidence that is missing or unverifiable
rows.imp_rat: Missing or unverifiable sterile/contamination-control evidence is a documentation-adjacent error less central to the mechanical act of batch prep, yet missing contamination evidence carries severe compliance and safety risk. Capped by ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Supplier qualification and audit practices retrieve much evidence, yet portions remain missing or unverifiable, leaving a meaningful gap. Collecting and verifying contamination-control evidence from vendors takes considerable back-and-forth time during due diligence. Verifying whether evidence is adequate and authentic requires GMP audit and quality expertise that is in limited supply. Audits and evidence reviews consume audit resources, but they are standard budgeted acquisition activities.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of critical sterile fill-finish requirements omitted from evaluation criteria
rows.imp_rat: Omitting sterile fill-finish requirements from evaluation criteria is downstream of preparation and only moderately central to mixing a batch, but omission can lead to contaminated product with high patient-safety consequence. Capped by ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Scoring matrices exist but critical sterile requirements are commonly omitted from criteria, so selection reliability suffers recurring gaps. Building evaluation criteria takes deliberate effort that is often shortchanged near award decisions. Defining critical fill-finish evaluation criteria requires expert judgement that inexperienced teams lack. Weak criteria lead to poor supplier selection and expensive downstream consequences, though creating criteria is cheap.

rows.stk: Maintenance technicians and engineering staff responsible for skid upkeep.
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of installing a replacement component that fails skid material, dimensional, or qualification specifications
rows.imp_rat: Installing a non-conforming replacement component is a maintenance-stage error (relevance) that can degrade skid integrity if undetected (severity), but it is peripheral and capped.
rows.sat_rat: Approved-vendor lists, certificates of conformance, and receiving inspection make non-conforming installs uncommon, though counterfeit or mislabeled parts occasionally get through. Verifying spare part specs adds procurement and receiving-inspection lead time before installation. Material and dimensional verification is well-proceduralized, needing document-check discipline more than deep expertise. Qualified parts and incoming inspection cost more than commodity parts, but prevent costly rework and deviations.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of critical sterility and containment conditions omitted from skid requirements before vendor engagement
rows.imp_rat: Omitting sterility/containment requirements before vendor engagement is upstream and correctable later (relevance), though it can propagate design gaps with meaningful cost (severity); scored low.
rows.sat_rat: URS templates and cGMP checklists help, but omitted critical conditions before vendor engagement remain a recurring, high-consequence failure mode. Assembling a complete set of sterility and containment conditions early adds front-loaded effort, but templates and URS boilerplate keep the timeline manageable. Capturing every critical sterility/containment condition depends heavily on scarce SME judgement (sterility assurance, containment engineers) that many acquiring teams lack in depth. Omissions surface as expensive change orders and requalification later, so the status quo carries real hidden cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that sterile-boundary and cleanability vulnerabilities remain unidentified before purchase commitment
rows.imp_rat: Unidentified sterile-boundary and cleanability vulnerabilities before purchase is a procurement-phase error; relevance to daily job is low though missing them can be costly, hence slightly higher severity. Capped at 3.5 mean.
rows.sat_rat: Hygienic design guides (ASME BPE) exist but pre-commitment identification is inconsistent, leaving many vulnerabilities undetected until FAT or operation. Design reviews to surface sterile-boundary and cleanability weaknesses take time and often compete with schedule pressure before purchase. Spotting cleanability dead-legs and sterile-boundary gaps demands rare hygienic-design expertise that is inconsistently available on acquisition teams. Vulnerabilities missed pre-purchase become costly retrofits or rejected skids, a frequent and painful expense.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of incorrect safety-critical command and status exchanges between skid controls and facility automation
rows.imp_rat: Incorrect safety-critical command exchanges between skid and facility automation is relevant to safe integration, yet the ranking caps this batch and severity is contained. Capped at 3.5 mean.
rows.sat_rat: Point-to-point I/O checks and integration test protocols catch most mismatches, but edge-case and timing-related handshake errors still surface after startup. Integration testing of skid-to-facility signal exchanges is time-consuming during SAT/commissioning. Verifying safety-critical command/status handshakes requires skilled controls and integration engineers who understand both systems. Integration rework and prolonged commissioning carry cost, but the exchanges themselves consume no material.

rows.source_job: Perform installation and operational qualification tests
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of installed skid attributes that deviate from approved specifications during qualification testing
rows.imp_rat: Installed attributes deviating from approved specs during qualification is caught in testing (relevance), limiting escape, though deviations can require rework and delay (severity); scored low.
rows.sat_rat: Formal IQ protocols against approved specs are mature and reliably surface most installation deviations before qualification proceeds. IQ attribute verification is a systematic checklist activity with predictable, bounded duration. Comparing installed attributes to specs is largely procedural and needs only moderately trained technicians following protocols. Finding a deviating attribute at IQ is cheaper than field discovery later, keeping cost impact modest.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a critical alarm is overlooked when multiple skid alarms occur together
rows.imp_rat: Overlooking a critical alarm amid alarm floods is relevant to safe operation, but per the ranking this batch is bounded and the error's realized severity is contained. Capped at 3.5 mean.
rows.sat_rat: Basic alarm prioritization and grouping exist but true rationalized alarm management is inconsistently deployed, so critical alarms still get buried in nuisance floods. Alarms annunciate instantly, so the problem is not timing but sorting priority under load. During alarm floods operators must rapidly triage many signals, placing heavy demand on experience and attention to catch the critical one. A missed critical alarm can cascade into batch loss or equipment damage with substantial cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of encountering an obstruction or insufficient clearance while moving the equipment to its designated location
rows.imp_rat: Encountering an obstruction or insufficient clearance while moving equipment is a logistics delay (low relevance to the processing job) with mostly schedule consequences that are absorbable (low-moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Drawing-based route checks catch most obstructions, yet as-built discrepancies and forgotten overhead services still cause surprise clearance problems. Route surveys and clearance checks before moving equipment add up-front time that is usually well spent. Path planning uses ordinary logistics judgement and tape-measure checks that most site teams can handle. A missed clearance can mean costly dismantling or door modification, but pre-checks themselves are inexpensive.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of deviation of plant skids and vessels from the specified level tolerance
rows.imp_rat: Deviation of skids and vessels from level tolerance affects drainage and stability but is a commissioning-level defect (moderate relevance) with mostly correctable consequences (moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Documented level tolerances checked with laser instruments make this a mature, repeatable practice where deviations are reliably caught and corrected before sign-off. Laser leveling and shimming of skids and vessels is a fast, standardized step within the installation sequence. Precision leveling depends on trained millwrights and surveyors, but modern laser and digital level tools substantially reduce the finesse required. Leveling hardware and survey time are modest costs, and correcting a poorly leveled skid later is far more expensive.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of critical plant-status details omitted during shift handover
rows.imp_rat: Omitting critical plant-status details at shift handover can allow deviations to go unaddressed (moderate severity) and affects continuity (moderate relevance), a communication error below the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Structured shift-handover checklists exist but adherence varies, so critical plant-status details are still omitted with troubling frequency. Handovers are time-boxed and rushed changeovers routinely cut short the transfer of critical status details. Complete handover depends on the outgoing operator's judgement about what matters, an inconsistent and experience-dependent filter. Omitted status details cause downstream errors and rework, a recurring hidden cost of imperfect handover.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree to which the withdrawn sample differs from material at the defined sampling location
rows.imp_rat: A withdrawn sample differing from actual material at the sampling point can mislead quality decisions (moderate severity) but is a measurement-integrity issue of moderate centrality (moderate relevance).
rows.sat_rat: Validated sampling procedures and defined ports help, but viscosity-driven stratification and settling mean samples still diverge from true bulk material. Sampling and analysis are periodic, so a withdrawn sample reflects a point in time that may not represent the bulk at analysis. Obtaining a representative sample from viscous, potentially stratified material requires careful technique and validated sampling points. Unrepresentative samples cause wrong disposition decisions, driving unnecessary scrap or costly quality escapes.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required processing parameters and results omitted from each production-run record
rows.imp_rat: Omitted parameters in production-run records undermine traceability and batch release (moderate severity) but are a documentation error somewhat removed from physical processing (moderate relevance).
rows.sat_rat: EBR/historian systems with enforced mandatory entries make omissions uncommon where deployed, though paper-based or hybrid plants still lose data. Automated data historians capture parameters continuously so record completeness does not depend on timely manual logging. Electronic batch records with mandatory fields reduce reliance on operator diligence to record every value. Missing record fields trigger costly investigations or batch rejection, which automated capture largely prevents.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of heat-transfer and jacketed surface area retaining buildup after descaling
rows.imp_rat: Buildup retained on heat-transfer surfaces after descaling reduces thermal efficiency (moderate severity) and affects throughput (moderate relevance), a maintenance-quality issue below the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Jacketed and heat-transfer surfaces are hard to access and inspect, so residual buildup after descaling is a common, poorly-verified outcome. Descaling heat-transfer and jacketed surfaces is a slow chemical or mechanical process. Judging descaling completeness on hidden jacketed areas requires experienced maintenance judgement. Descaling chemicals, energy losses from residual buildup, and rework represent notable cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required safety and regulatory closure obligations unresolved at decommissioning closeout
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved regulatory closure obligations at decommissioning carry compliance exposure (moderate-high severity) but are peripheral to running the processing job (low relevance).
rows.sat_rat: Closeout checklists and permits exist, but obligations are frequently left dangling because no single system reliably tracks all of them to completion. Closing out regulatory and safety obligations is a long tail of paperwork that often extends past physical decommissioning. Tracking every closure obligation requires regulatory expertise and diligent project management. Unresolved obligations carry ongoing compliance and legal cost exposure.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of tank setpoints configured outside approved process ranges
rows.imp_rat: Setpoints outside approved ranges risk process excursions (moderate severity), but relevance is limited as automated interlocks often intercept, and the ranking caps importance here.
rows.sat_rat: Setpoint review against approved ranges is common but manual entry and change-control lapses still let out-of-range values persist. Setpoint configuration and review is a bounded configuration task within commissioning. Correct setpoints depend on process engineers translating ranges into control parameters, a knowledge-dependent step. Configuration itself costs little but wrong setpoints can cause product loss or trips.

rows.stk: Installation crew, rigging contractor, mechanical engineer.
rows.source_job: Level and anchor tank to foundation
rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of tank deviation from specified level tolerance after anchoring
rows.imp_rat: Tank level-tolerance deviation after anchoring is a largely installation-related error with limited relevance to ongoing processing and modest severity, warranting a lower score.
rows.sat_rat: Precision leveling with laser/optical survey and defined tolerance checks is mature and consistently achieves specified flatness/plumb tolerances. Leveling and shimming during anchoring is a well-understood task with predictable duration. Millwrights and surveyors performing tank leveling use routine, well-established techniques. Survey equipment and shim materials are inexpensive relative to overall installation.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of discrepancy between indicated tank level and actual material level
rows.imp_rat: Level-indication discrepancy affects fill accuracy and overflow avoidance (moderate relevance), but consequences are usually recoverable and caught by redundant checks (limited severity), keeping it within the 3.5 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Radar/DP level instruments are standard and reasonably accurate, yet foaming, agitation and density changes still cause recurring level-reading errors. Level readings are continuous, but reconciling indicated versus actual level requires periodic manual verification. Level instrument selection and correction for foam, agitation and density need some process expertise to trust. Level transmitters are inexpensive, but discrepancies cause costly over/underfilling and reconciliation effort.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree to which the collected sample differs from the tank contents
rows.imp_rat: A sample differing from actual contents misinforms decisions (moderate relevance), but severity is bounded since confirmatory testing often catches gross deviations.
rows.sat_rat: Stratification, settling, dead legs and single-point sampling ports mean the drawn sample routinely diverges from true tank contents, a chronic and poorly mitigated source of error today. Grab sampling through a valve or hatch is quick, but obtaining a genuinely representative sample often requires waiting for adequate mixing or repeated draws. Getting a representative sample depends heavily on operator judgement about port location, mixing state and flushing dead legs, making outcomes inconsistent between people. Sampling hardware and flush volumes are inexpensive, so cost is not the binding constraint on representativeness.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of difference between instrument indication and the actual tank condition
rows.imp_rat: Discrepancy between instrument indication and actual tank condition undermines process control, but batch-ranking caps it below prior items; relevance is moderate since bad readings can misdirect operations, severity is limited as many checks catch gross errors.
rows.sat_rat: Routine calibration and redundant sensing keep indications trustworthy, but sensor drift and fouling still create recurring indication-vs-reality gaps. Instrument calibration and verification are periodic, so drift can persist between checks. Calibration and instrument troubleshooting require trained metrology/technician skill. Calibration programs and reference standards are a steady maintenance cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of disagreement among commissioning records about which product-contact surfaces require sanitation
rows.imp_rat: Disagreement among commissioning records about which surfaces need sanitation is a documentation-ambiguity error (low relevance to running processing), with modest consequences typically resolved during commissioning (low-moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Ambiguous and inconsistent commissioning records about product-contact surfaces are a persistent, poorly-controlled problem. Reconciling conflicting commissioning records about which surfaces need sanitation causes avoidable delays. Resolving documentation disagreement requires experienced QA judgement across multiple document sets. Rework and re-sanitation driven by record disputes waste labor and materials.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of collecting a sample from the wrong sampling point or production lot
rows.imp_rat: Collecting a sample from the wrong point or lot (relevance) is a peripheral procedural error; it can mislead quality decisions (severity) but is low-frequency and administrative, scored low.
rows.sat_rat: Established SOPs, labelled valves and sample-point tagging cover most cases, but wrong-point or wrong-lot errors still recur where identification relies on manual judgement. Verifying a sampling point against the lot record adds only minor time when barcode or tag systems are used, but manual cross-checking still slows sampling rounds. Correct sampling still depends heavily on operator familiarity with plant layout and lot tracking, making it vulnerable to inexperienced or fatigued staff. A mis-sampled lot is inexpensive to draw but can trigger costly re-testing, re-work, or disposition delays when the error surfaces later.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of consequential support or anchor deterioration left undetected during inspection
rows.imp_rat: Undetected support or anchor deterioration could eventually threaten structural integrity (moderate severity), but it is peripheral to the daily tank job and ranks below the prior batch.
rows.sat_rat: Hidden or coated support corrosion and settlement can be under-appreciated, so some consequential deterioration is missed. Inspecting supports, saddles and anchors is a quick add-on to external surveys. Judging consequential structural deterioration needs some experience but is not highly specialized. Visual support inspection is low-cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of vulnerable tank substrate left without restored barrier coverage
rows.imp_rat: Vulnerable substrate left without restored barrier invites future corrosion (moderate severity), but relevance to immediate tank operation is secondary and it ranks below the prior batch.
rows.sat_rat: Coverage gaps at edges, welds and hard-to-reach areas commonly leave some substrate under-protected. Surface prep, application and cure of restored barrier coatings extend the outage substantially. Achieving full coverage on complex substrate needs skilled applicators controlling prep and thickness. Coating materials, containment and cure conditions are notable costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of interior tank surfaces retaining product residue after cleaning
rows.imp_rat: Residual product on interior surfaces after cleaning risks cross-contamination (moderate relevance), yet the severity is bounded since subsequent inspection often catches it before batches are affected.
rows.sat_rat: Validated cleaning with residue analytics reliably clears most surfaces, with residual risk in shadow zones. Cleaning plus rinse-sample verification adds defined cycle time within the maintenance window. Validated cleaning recipes limit operator judgement, though visual final checks still matter. Water, solvents and analytical testing are recurring but budgeted costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of protective-lining defects left undetected during inspection
rows.imp_rat: Undetected protective-lining defects can progress to substrate corrosion (moderate severity), but relevance to immediate tank function is secondary and this sits below the prior batch.
rows.sat_rat: Manual holiday detection over complex geometry leaves pinholes and thin spots that escape detection. Holiday/spark testing and visual lining survey take meaningful time over large internal areas. Lining defect detection relies heavily on experienced coating inspectors and correct test-voltage judgement. Spark testers are cheap, but full-area inspection labor is significant.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of consequential tank-connection defects remaining undetected during inspection
rows.imp_rat: Undetected consequential connection defects could lead to leaks (moderate severity), but relevance to the core tank job is secondary and this item ranks below the prior batch, holding under the cap.
rows.sat_rat: Connections are numerous and geometrically awkward, so consequential defects are moderately likely to be missed. Nozzle and connection inspection adds access and disassembly time. Detecting connection defects at flanges and penetrations depends on inspector thoroughness. Inspection of many small connections is labor-intensive but not equipment-heavy.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of contaminating product-contact surfaces during agitator service
rows.imp_rat: Contaminating product-contact surfaces during agitator service risks batch contamination (moderate severity), but it is a maintenance-event risk peripheral to the core job, held under the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Post-service cleaning usually restores integrity, but the intrusive work still risks introducing contaminants. Agitator seal/bearing service plus post-service cleaning adds contained downtime. Avoiding contamination during agitator work depends on technician cleanliness discipline. Clean-work provisions and re-sanitization add moderate cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of hazardous tank residues being assigned an incorrect waste classification
rows.imp_rat: Incorrect waste classification of hazardous residues carries compliance risk (moderate severity), but it is a downstream administrative-type error peripheral to the tank's core function, capped by ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Standard waste-profiling procedures exist, but reliance on generator knowledge and limited sampling still produces misclassifications. Waste characterization and profiling introduce lead time before disposal can proceed. Correct waste classification requires regulatory expertise that many disposal crews lack in-house. Misclassification risks costly rework or penalties, and full analytical profiling is itself expensive.

rows.source_job: Assess tank condition for end-of-life routing
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that hazardous tank contamination is understated during condition assessment
rows.imp_rat: Understating hazardous contamination during assessment could expose workers (moderate severity), but relevance is limited to assessment accuracy rather than the core tank job, held by the ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Assessments rely on incomplete process history and limited sampling, so understated hazardous contamination is a recurring and consequential failure. Condition assessment is a discrete inspection step that does not dominate the timeline. Accurately characterizing contamination demands scarce specialist knowledge of process history and sampling. Sampling and lab analysis cost money, tempting shortcuts that understate contamination.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that sterile product is contaminated during the operational handover
rows.imp_rat: Contamination during operational handover threatens sterile product (severity), and handover is relevant, but the ranking places this below the prior batch, capping importance.
rows.sat_rat: Mature aseptic transfer SOPs handle most handovers, though residual contamination risk during transition remains a real recurring gap. Handover procedures add coordination time but are scheduled around production. Aseptic handover of sterile product requires trained operators following validated SOPs. Any contamination event during handover risks losing an entire sterile batch, a large cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that validated recipes, calibration parameters, or audit records are altered during the update
rows.imp_rat: Alteration of validated recipes, calibration, or audit records during an update (item 8) has moderate severity for data integrity, but it is an update-maintenance event peripheral to routine batching. Bounded by the 3.5 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Audit trails, backups, and validated data-integrity controls make unintended alteration of recipes or calibration data rare in mature systems. Backup and verification of records before updates adds modest overhead within the maintenance window. Following data-integrity procedures needs trained personnel but is largely procedural rather than deeply expert. Version control and audit-trail systems are already in place, so incremental cost is low.

rows.stk: Instrumentation technicians, maintenance engineers, quality personnel.
rows.stmt: Reduce the time the measurement device remains in service beyond its approved calibration interval
rows.imp_rat: Running a measurement device past its calibration interval (delay error) is moderately relevant to batching accuracy, but the ceiling caps it; severity is real since out-of-cal data can compromise batch quality yet is often caught in review.
rows.sat_rat: CMMS/calibration-management due-date tracking keeps most devices within interval, though occasional overdue instruments slip through. Automated scheduling minimizes delay in pulling overdue devices from service. Following recall schedules is procedural and requires little judgement. The recall process itself is inexpensive relative to the risk of out-of-cal operation.

rows.stk: Instrumentation technicians, maintenance engineers, quality personnel.
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of calibration records assigned to the wrong measurement device or reference standard
rows.imp_rat: Calibration records assigned to the wrong device (ambiguity) is a records-integrity error with limited centrality to physical batching, though misattribution could allow a bad instrument to run and affect a batch.
rows.sat_rat: Asset tagging and barcode-linked records make wrong-device record assignment uncommon in electronic systems, though manual entry still errs. Record entry and linking is quick within digital calibration systems. Assigning records correctly is a low-skill data-entry task aided by scanned IDs. Misassignment cost is mostly rework and audit findings rather than material loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of failing to detect wear, leakage, or lubricant degradation during mechanical inspection
rows.imp_rat: Failing to detect wear, leakage, or lubricant degradation during inspection can lead to failures, but as a preventive-maintenance detection gap it is indirect to the batching job and held under the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Manual visual/tactile inspection is inconsistent and misses early-stage degradation, making undetected wear a common outcome. Thorough inspection takes time and is often compressed within tight maintenance windows. Detecting subtle wear, incipient leakage, or lubricant degradation depends heavily on inspector experience and judgement. Missed early wear leads to unplanned failures that are far costlier than the inspection itself.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of existing leaks, corrosion, deterioration, and component wear not detected during the inspection
rows.imp_rat: Missing existing leaks, corrosion, or wear during inspection risks later breakdowns; severity is moderate but relevance to the core batching act is secondary and bounded by the ordering.
rows.sat_rat: A meaningful fraction of existing defects routinely goes undetected because manual inspection coverage and consistency are inherently limited. Comprehensive inspection of all surfaces is time-consuming and often abbreviated. Reliably spotting all corrosion, deterioration, and wear across a complex system demands high vigilance and expertise that varies by inspector. Undetected deterioration escalates into larger failures, wasting far more than the inspection cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of installing a sealing component incompatible with the process fluid, operating conditions, or hygienic requirements
rows.imp_rat: Installing an incompatible sealing component can cause failure or contamination, which is fairly severe, but as an installation/materials decision it is upstream of and less central than core batching execution.
rows.sat_rat: BOM/spare-part specifications and compatibility charts prevent most mismatches, yet substitution errors still occur under time pressure. Verifying seal material compatibility adds lookup time but is a discrete pre-task step. Correct seal selection requires materials/process knowledge, though reference charts reduce the expertise burden. Wrong seals cause failures and contamination, but correct parts are inexpensive to specify upfront.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of environmental contaminants entering sterile fluid paths during extended storage
rows.imp_rat: Environmental contaminants entering sterile paths during extended storage has real severity for sterility, but this storage-phase error is less central to the active batching job and is capped by ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Standard sealing practice reduces ingress but extended storage of decommissioned systems still sees contaminant entry when seals degrade unmonitored. Capping and sealing fluid paths for storage is quick with standard closures. Proper sealing and desiccant use is routine technician work needing modest skill. Protective closures and controlled storage add modest cost that is usually accepted.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of designated liquid released from circuit connections during priming
rows.imp_rat: Designated liquid released from circuit connections during priming is a minor waste and housekeeping issue largely peripheral to completing the batch; consequences are small volume loss and cleanup, easily absorbed.
rows.sat_rat: Clamped and welded sanitary joints reliably contain priming release, with rare seal failures remaining. Minor releases at connections during priming cause small cleanup delays. Making tight aseptic connections is routine trained work that limits release. Leak-tight sanitary connectors contain most designated-liquid release, limiting product waste.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of a contaminated utility supply entering the batching system after hook-up
rows.imp_rat: A contaminated utility supply entering after hook-up carries real sterility severity and is relevant to system integrity, but the ranking places this item below the top batch.
rows.sat_rat: Utility qualification and point-of-use filters help, but post-hookup contamination still occurs and is detected inconsistently, leaving this only partially served. Confirming utility supply integrity after hook-up requires sampling or checks that take time. Judging utility cleanliness needs trained utilities/quality personnel. A contaminated utility entering the system can spoil batches and force cleaning, a substantial cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of contamination entering the product-contact path during the validation batch
rows.imp_rat: Contamination entering the product-contact path during validation is serious for product safety, yet as a validation-batch-specific event the ranking holds its combined importance at or below 3.5.
rows.sat_rat: Aseptic design, cleaning validation and environmental monitoring provide solid safeguards, but contamination risk during validation is never fully eliminated, giving acceptable coverage. Confirming contamination control in the product-contact path during validation requires sampling that takes time. Assessing product-contact contamination needs microbiological and aseptic-process expertise. Contamination in the validation batch scraps the batch and delays qualification at high cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of critical batch parameters outside approved limits during the validation batch
rows.imp_rat: Critical batch parameters outside approved limits during a validation batch harm compliance, but this is a validation-phase concern rather than routine production, limiting relevance to the core job.
rows.sat_rat: Validation protocols with automated data capture and alarms make parameter monitoring fairly robust, so this is well served. Monitoring and confirming batch parameters during the validation batch takes dedicated time. Interpreting whether parameters stay within approved limits requires validation and process expertise. Out-of-limit parameters fail the validation batch, wasting materials and repeat runs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of cleaning and reset exercises completed with contamination hazards
rows.imp_rat: Cleaning and reset completed with contamination hazards has meaningful severity for downstream batches and is relevant to preparation readiness, yet it is capped below the prior batch.
rows.sat_rat: CIP procedures and cleaning validation are established, but residual contamination hazards during resets still recur, giving acceptable but imperfect coverage. Completing and verifying cleaning/reset exercises without hazards is time-intensive. Proper cleaning execution and hazard recognition depend on trained operators following validated procedures. Cleaning failures waste chemicals, water, and force re-cleans and lost batches.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of process piping runs connected to incorrect system or plant ports
rows.imp_rat: Incorrect piping runs to wrong plant ports risk misrouting, but this is largely a one-time installation/setup concern rather than a recurring batch-execution failure, keeping relevance modest.
rows.sat_rat: Tagging, line-check procedures and mechanical keying give solid but not foolproof protection against wrong-port connections. Confirming piping runs match the correct system/plant ports adds verification time. Correct routing relies on personnel interpreting drawings and tags accurately. Wrong port connections can cause cross-contamination and costly batch loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of completed piping connections containing unintended crevices or stagnant pockets
rows.imp_rat: Crevices or stagnant pockets in piping connections foster contamination (severity) and are relevant to sterile integrity, but the ranking caps this item below the higher-priority batch.
rows.sat_rat: Sanitary fittings and design standards reduce crevices, but manual assembly still introduces them, leaving recurring gaps. Inspecting each connection for crevices or stagnant pockets slows commissioning. Identifying crevices and stagnant pockets needs hygienic-design expertise during assembly. Crevices harbor contamination that leads to failed batches and costly remediation.

rows.stk: Process engineers, system operators, batch technicians.
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of recipe steps omitted or placed out of order in the configured sequence
rows.imp_rat: Recipe steps omitted or out of order in the configured sequence can produce an incorrect batch, but as a pre-execution configuration error it ranks under the prior batch and is capped at 3.5.
rows.sat_rat: Sequence entry is typically manual with limited automated completeness checks, so omissions and ordering errors recur, leaving the need only partially served. Sequencing steps and re-verifying order by hand is a slow, iterative task. Getting the sequence right and complete leans on a process specialist who knows the intended procedure. Out-of-order or missing steps can ruin batches and consume rework resources.

rows.stk: Process engineers, system operators, batch technicians.
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of configured temperature, mixing, or ingredient-delivery setpoints outside validated operating limits
rows.imp_rat: Setpoints outside validated limits directly affect batch quality (severity) and are relevant to correct preparation, but this configuration error is capped below the higher-priority batch.
rows.sat_rat: Recipe systems increasingly support range checks against configured limits, so many out-of-range setpoints are caught, giving acceptable-to-good coverage. Checking each setpoint against validated limits adds time but is a defined task. Knowing the validated operating limits requires process engineering knowledge held by specialists. Out-of-limit setpoints can waste product and energy or damage equipment.

rows.stk: Process engineers, system operators, batch technicians.
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of configuring an obsolete recipe revision for production use
rows.imp_rat: Configuring an obsolete recipe revision is moderately relevant to producing a correct batch, and severity is meaningful since wrong formulation wastes product, but ranking caps this below the top batch.
rows.sat_rat: Version control exists in most electronic systems but manual selection still lets obsolete revisions slip in, so the need is only acceptably served. Verifying a revision is current takes a lookup against the recipe register, adding modest time. Distinguishing an obsolete revision requires disciplined change-control awareness by the configurer. Producing with an obsolete recipe can scrap an entire batch, a significant avoidable cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of batches confirmed at endpoint while defined criteria remain unmet
rows.imp_rat: Confirming batches at endpoint while criteria remain unmet (item 2) directly touches the release decision, which is fairly central, but the severity here is moderated since such false confirmations are usually caught downstream. Held to the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Recipe-enforced endpoint criteria reduce premature confirmation, yet partially manual endpoint sign-offs still let some batches through with unmet criteria. Automated endpoint checks confirm criteria without added review time. Configured endpoint logic reduces operator judgement about whether all criteria are met. Prematurely confirmed batches that later fail are expensive, but interlocked endpoints keep this infrequent.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of routing the prepared batch to the wrong intermediate vessel
rows.imp_rat: Routing a prepared batch to the wrong intermediate vessel (item 11) can cause mix-ups, giving moderate severity, but it is a handling/routing step of moderate centrality. Capped at 3.5.
rows.sat_rat: Interlocked transfer paths, tag-based valve verification and recipe-driven routing make correct-vessel delivery a well-established, mostly dependable practice with occasional gaps on manual overrides. Automated routing via configured recipes and interlocks executes vessel selection quickly once set up, adding little delay. Modern batch control systems with S88-style phase logic and valve interlocks reduce reliance on operator judgement for correct routing. A misrouted batch can scrap costly product, but interlocks and confirmation prompts make such events relatively uncommon in automated plants.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which critical process conditions deviate from approved limits during manual control
rows.imp_rat: Deviation of critical conditions during manual control (item 17) risks off-spec product, but manual-mode operation is a fallback scenario of moderate relevance. Bounded by the 3.5 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Without automated PID control in the loop, deviations under manual control are frequent and inconsistent, leaving this need poorly served. Manual control keeps the batch running but operator reaction time to correct drifting conditions is slow relative to automated loops. Manual control of critical process conditions leans entirely on scarce, attentive, well-trained operators, making it highly skill-dependent and error-prone. Manual excursions beyond approved limits routinely cause off-spec material and rework, driving avoidable cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required pump, valve, or agitator actions omitted or performed out of sequence during the batch step
rows.imp_rat: Omitting or misordering pump, valve, or agitator actions during a batch step directly affects batch correctness, but the ranking caps it below the prior batch's items.
rows.sat_rat: S88 phase logic and equipment interlocks make correctly sequenced actuation a mature, widely deployed practice, with residual risk mostly in manual or degraded modes. Automated phase sequencing executes pump, valve and agitator actions in the right order without slowing the batch. Recipe-driven sequence control removes the need for operators to remember or manually order each actuation, sharply lowering skill dependence. Omitted or out-of-order actions can ruin a batch, but sequence interlocks make such errors uncommon in automated systems.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of programmed ingredient-addition steps skipped during a batch run
rows.imp_rat: Skipping programmed ingredient-addition steps degrades batch quality with moderate severity, yet the combined ordering places it beneath higher-priority errors, holding it under 3.5.
rows.sat_rat: Recipe-managed batch execution with step confirmation and electronic records makes skipped ingredient additions an uncommon, well-controlled failure in automated plants. Programmed addition steps execute automatically in sequence, so no time is lost to operators tracking each step. Automated recipe execution enforces every ingredient-addition step, greatly reducing reliance on operator vigilance to avoid skips. A skipped addition can scrap a batch, but recipe enforcement and step confirmation make skipped steps rare, limiting cost exposure.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a verification sample changes composition through contamination before analysis
rows.imp_rat: Contamination of a verification sample before analysis (item 1) undermines batch confirmation, but sampling is a peripheral verification step relative to the core batching job; consequences are moderate since a bad sample typically triggers a re-test rather than a released defective batch. Capped at 3.5.
rows.sat_rat: Validated sample containers, chain-of-custody and prompt analysis keep pre-analysis composition change fairly uncommon, though handling and hold-time lapses still occur. Proper sample containers and prompt transfer to the lab add little time. Correct aseptic sample handling requires basic trained technique rather than scarce expertise. Contaminated verification samples trigger costly retesting but such events are individually infrequent.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of installing an incorrect filtration element in the batching system
rows.imp_rat: Installing an incorrect filtration element can compromise sterile assurance (severity), and filtration is relevant to preparation integrity, but this item sits below the higher batch and is capped.
rows.sat_rat: Part-number control, labeling, and kitting make incorrect filter installation relatively uncommon in disciplined operations. Selecting the correct filter is a quick step when part numbers are documented. Matching filter micron rating and type to the recipe requires modest training and reference documentation. Wrong filters waste product batches, but correct elements themselves are low cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of process material entering an unintended vessel or step during skid transfer
rows.imp_rat: Material entering an unintended vessel during skid transfer is relevant to correct processing, but ranking caps this batch; the cross-contamination consequence is real yet less central than the higher-batch items. Capped at 3.5 mean.
rows.sat_rat: Valve interlocks and phase logic largely prevent misrouting, but manual hose/jumper connections and complex manifolds still allow occasional wrong-path transfers. Automated valve sequencing routes transfers promptly without added delay. Interlocked valve logic reduces reliance on operators to remember correct routing paths. Misrouted material to the wrong vessel can cross-contaminate and scrap product at high cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage deviation of the delivered dose from the required dose
rows.imp_rat: Dose delivery deviation matters for product quality, but this error is peripheral to the skid's core operational job relative to the higher batch, and its severity is bounded. Capped at 3.5 mean.
rows.sat_rat: Mass-flow and gravimetric dosing with feedback control keep dose deviation tight, though pump wear and calibration drift cause periodic variation. Dosing pumps and flow control deliver at rate without meaningful time cost. Calibrated metering reduces the manual finesse needed to hit target dose. Overdosing wastes expensive additives and underdosing can fail the batch, so deviation carries cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that parameter adjustments push critical quality attributes outside approved limits across batches
rows.imp_rat: Parameter drift pushing CQAs outside limits across batches is relevant to quality, but the ranking caps this batch and consequences here are bounded relative to higher items. Capped at 3.5 mean.
rows.sat_rat: Change control, design-space definition, and automated setpoint limits keep most adjustments in bounds, though drift and undocumented tweaks occasionally push CQAs out. Parameter changes go through change control and requalification, which slows adjustments significantly. Setting parameters within CQA limits leans on process engineers' understanding of design space. Requalification and potential batch impact make errant adjustments expensive, driving cautious change management.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a defective protective function is recorded as passing during periodic testing
rows.imp_rat: A defective protective function recorded as passing carries real safety severity, but its relevance to the everyday job is peripheral and the ranking bounds this batch. Capped at 3.5 mean.
rows.sat_rat: Documented functional-test protocols catch most defective protective functions, but simulated-test shortcuts and improper test conditions can record a bad function as passing. Periodic testing of protective functions is scheduled and adds downtime for the test sequence. Correctly exercising interlocks and alarms requires trained technicians who understand the safety logic. Test labor and downtime cost money, but a false-pass on a safety function risks far larger losses.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of equipment damage during dismantling, handling, or transport
rows.imp_rat: Equipment damage during dismantling, handling, or transport is an off-line logistics error with low relevance to the processing job and moderate severity limited to repair cost and schedule impact.
rows.sat_rat: Established rigging, lifting-plan and transport practices handle most items well, but incidents still occur with awkward or corroded plant. Careful rigging and sequencing extend dismantling schedules but that trade is accepted. Skilled riggers and demolition crews reduce damage, though such crews are experienced and generally available. Equipment damaged during handling loses resale/scrap value and can incur repair or disposal costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of previously unidentified contaminated locations discovered after remediation begins
rows.imp_rat: Discovering previously unidentified contaminated locations after remediation begins is a scoping/ambiguity error affecting remediation completeness; peripheral to the plant's processing job with moderate rework consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Pre-work site characterization routinely under-samples, so unexpected hotspots are a chronic, recurring surprise during remediation. Discovering new contaminated spots mid-project causes major schedule slips, but the underlying failure is the surprise itself. Predicting hidden contamination demands specialist site-investigation and historical-process expertise that is frequently incomplete. Late discoveries blow budgets through unplanned excavation and disposal.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of removed safety components that belong to the general facility
rows.imp_rat: Removing general-facility safety components (rather than plant-specific ones) during decommissioning has low relevance to the core processing job, with moderate severity since it can strip protection from shared facility systems.
rows.sat_rat: Boundaries between plant and shared infrastructure are poorly documented, so mistaken removals recur despite marked-up drawings. Sorting which removed items belong to the general facility slows demolition slightly but is a secondary concern. Correctly attributing ownership of overlapping utility and safety systems requires facility knowledge that is often lost by the disposal stage. Wrongly removing shared facility equipment forces replacement and rework, imposing avoidable cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of plant-specific safety components left in place at site handover
rows.imp_rat: Plant-specific safety components left in place at site handover is a handover-administrative error with low relevance to the processing job, and severity is moderate only if residual components later create confusion or hazards.
rows.sat_rat: Handover checklists exist but as-built drawings are frequently outdated, so components are inconsistently identified and stragglers routinely surface after sign-off. Verifying every plant-specific safety component is removed adds walkdown time to handover, but timing is not the primary loss when one is missed. Distinguishing plant-specific safety devices from shared facility ones demands experienced commissioning/decommissioning engineers who are often not present at disposal. Leftover components are cheap in themselves but trigger costly re-mobilizations and disputed handovers.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that incoming ingredient temperature variation causes degradation before batching is complete
rows.imp_rat: Incoming ingredient temperature variation causing degradation before batching completes affects product quality moderately, but as an inbound-material condition it is somewhat tangential and ranking-capped.
rows.sat_rat: Jacketed temperature control and ingredient pre-conditioning are standard, but variation in incoming feed temperature still causes occasional degradation, leaving recurring gaps. Temperature-driven degradation is a race against batching duration, and status-quo control keeps most batches within acceptable time windows. Managing incoming ingredient temperature variation needs some operator attention to jacketed control and staging, a moderate skill load. Temperature-induced degradation spoils material, but pre-conditioning and jacketed vessels prevent most losses, keeping cost impact moderate.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of difference between the sample composition and the liquid at the designated sampling point
rows.imp_rat: A sample not matching the liquid at the sampling point (defect) affects quality-measurement fidelity and is moderately relevant, with severity bounded since it distorts test data rather than the batch itself.
rows.sat_rat: Well-placed sample points and good mixing yield representative samples in most cases, but stratification and dead legs still bias some draws. Drawing a representative sample and confirming it takes modest time. Proper sampling technique to get a representative draw requires trained personnel. Non-representative samples cause rework and material loss, a moderate recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the active batch cycle continues after the operator issues the pause command
rows.imp_rat: A batch cycle continuing after a pause command is a control-responsiveness fault with safety implications, but relative to core batching it ranks below the prior batch and stays under the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Batch control systems reliably honor pause/hold commands as a standard feature, so the cycle continuing past a pause is an uncommon failure. Pause commands act on the control system essentially immediately, so lag between command and cessation is negligible. The pause function is built into the control interface and requires no special skill to invoke correctly. A cycle continuing after a pause command could cause overfill or spoilage, but such command-execution failures are rare with tested control logic.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that pausing the batch cycle leaves the system outside safe pressure or temperature limits
rows.imp_rat: Pausing the batch leaving the system outside safe pressure or temperature limits carries genuine safety severity, but as a pause-state edge case it is bounded by the batch-2 ordering.
rows.sat_rat: Many systems have safe-hold logic, but not all pause points leave pressure and temperature within safe limits, so gaps remain for non-ideal pause moments. Reaching a safe held state after pause depends on how fast pressures and temperatures settle, which control logic manages reasonably quickly. Safe-state pausing is mostly designed into hold logic, but complex batches may require operator judgement to confirm safe conditions. An unsafe pause state risks equipment or batch damage, a real but infrequent cost given typical safe-hold design.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of resuming the interrupted batch from an incorrectly identified system state
rows.imp_rat: Resuming from an incorrectly identified system state (ambiguity) could produce an off-spec restart, giving moderate severity, but it applies only to interruption recovery and is bounded by the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Batch state tracking and hold/restart logic help, but incomplete state capture after an interruption still leads to resuming from a misidentified condition. Confirming the correct state before resuming adds restart delay. Judging the true system state before resuming an interrupted batch is a high-stakes skill-dependent decision. Resuming from a wrong state can spoil the batch, a large cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of deviation from approved batch-condition limits during the interruption
rows.imp_rat: Deviation from batch-condition limits during an interruption (item 16) can degrade quality, giving moderate severity, but interruption handling is an edge condition rather than the core batching flow. Capped at 3.5.
rows.sat_rat: Few systems have robust automated hold-state control for unplanned interruptions, so deviation from batch-condition limits during an interruption is a poorly served, recurring pain. During an interruption, keeping conditions within limits depends on how fast operators or systems react, and delays in response commonly let parameters drift. Holding a batch within approved limits mid-interruption demands experienced operators making judgement calls, so skill dependence is high. Deviations during interruptions frequently force reprocessing or scrapping, creating recurring material and investigation cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the approved interruption hold limit is exceeded before batch resumption
rows.imp_rat: Exceeding the approved interruption hold limit before batch resumption can compromise product integrity, but as a delay/hold-timing control it is peripheral to the core batching job and capped by the batch ordering.
rows.sat_rat: Hold timers and resumption procedures exist but are inconsistently enforced, so overruns before resumption still occur regularly. The error is interruption duration exceeding the approved hold limit before resumption, and restart delays are common and hard to bound. Timely, correct resumption needs experienced operators to safely restart from a held state, adding skill dependence. Interruptions that overrun the hold window force disposition of the batch, a recurring material cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that measurement faults are mistaken for actual batch conditions
rows.imp_rat: Mistaking measurement faults for actual batch conditions (item 4) can trigger wrong actions, giving it moderate severity, but it is a measurement-interpretation issue peripheral to the core batching job. Kept at or below 3.5.
rows.sat_rat: Sensor diagnostics and redundancy help flag faults, but single-sensor readings are still routinely mistaken for actual batch conditions. Diagnosing whether an alarm is a sensor fault or a real condition can consume investigation time. Distinguishing instrument faults from true batch deviations relies on experienced troubleshooting. Reacting to false faults or ignoring real ones drives unnecessary rework or spoilage costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time between onset of a critical batch anomaly and operator recognition
rows.imp_rat: Delay in operator recognition of a critical anomaly can let problems escalate, giving some severity, but the ranking caps the combined importance at or below 3.5.
rows.sat_rat: Alarm systems and trend displays help, but nuisance alarms and screen clutter mean critical events are still sometimes noticed slowly. Time-to-recognition of a critical anomaly depends heavily on alarm configuration and operator attention, and is often longer than it should be. Distinguishing a genuine critical anomaly from noise demands experienced operators, especially in busy periods. Late recognition can escalate to a scrapped batch, a substantial cost when it occurs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a critical alarm is obscured by simultaneous noncritical alarms
rows.imp_rat: A critical alarm obscured by noncritical alarms can delay urgent response, adding severity, but alarm management is a supporting concern kept below 3.5 by the ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Alarm rationalization and prioritization per ISA- and help, but many plants still run poorly rationalized systems where critical alarms get buried. Alarm flooding delays the moment an operator locates the one critical alarm among many. It takes trained judgement to triage a cascade of alarms and pick out the critical one under pressure. A missed critical alarm can cost an entire batch, but rationalization projects are cheap to specify.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the finished batch is transferred to the wrong downstream destination
rows.imp_rat: Transferring the finished batch to the wrong downstream destination (item 12) risks cross-contamination and moderate consequences, yet remains a downstream-routing concern relative to the core batching job. Held to the batch limit.
rows.sat_rat: Path verification, destination interlocks and electronic batch records make correct downstream transfer a mature practice, with residual risk mainly during manual reroutes or shared header lines. Automated transfer sequencing routes finished batches to the correct destination without meaningful added delay once paths are validated. Destination logic in the batch engine offloads the decision from the operator, lowering skill dependence for correct dispatch. Sending finished product to the wrong tank risks cross-contamination and loss of a full high-value batch, though such failures are infrequent under interlocked control.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the scaled batch quantity falls outside the system's validated operating range
rows.imp_rat: A scaled batch falling outside the validated operating range (instability) is fairly relevant to reliable batching and could cause off-spec product, but the ceiling caps it and validation limits are usually checked before scale-up.
rows.sat_rat: Recipe management with configured min/max charge limits catches most out-of-range scaling, but manual scale-ups and undocumented ranges still allow violations. Validated-range checks are near-instant when embedded in recipe management. Recognizing that a scaled quantity exceeds validated limits requires process and validation knowledge, especially outside automated recipe systems. A batch scaled outside the validated range risks scrap and rework, a significant cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the scaled batch exceeds validated sterile hold limits before filling
rows.imp_rat: Scaled batch exceeding validated sterile hold limits before filling risks contamination, which carries serious consequences, but relative to the batching CFJ it is a downstream hold-time concern ranked below the prior batch.
rows.sat_rat: Sterile hold-time excursions before filling remain a well-known, poorly buffered failure mode in aseptic operations despite validated limits, so the need is only partly served. The sterile hold clock is tight and any filling-line delay eats into it, making elapsed time a constant pressure. Managing scaled batches within validated sterile hold limits requires trained aseptic-process staff coordinating tightly, a high skill demand. Exceeding sterile hold limits forces rejection of an entire scaled, high-value sterile batch, an expensive and recurring loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of compromising sterile conditions while troubleshooting the batching fault
rows.imp_rat: Compromising sterile conditions while troubleshooting (contamination-risk) carries high severity because contamination can ruin a batch, but the ceiling caps it and this occurs only during fault handling, limiting relevance to the routine job.
rows.sat_rat: Closed systems, SIP and aseptic SOPs protect sterility, but any physical troubleshooting intervention still carries real contamination risk. Maintaining sterility during troubleshooting adds procedural steps and time. Preserving asepsis while intervening requires trained aseptic-technique discipline. A compromised sterile batch is a total loss plus potential CIP/SIP recovery cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the technician misidentifies the underlying cause of the batching fault
rows.imp_rat: Misidentifying the root cause of a batching fault (defect) has some relevance to restoring correct operation, with severity moderate since a wrong diagnosis prolongs downtime but is usually revisited.
rows.sat_rat: Diagnostics, historians and alarm logs help, but root-cause identification remains inconsistent and expertise-bound. Fault diagnosis can drag on when the cause is not obvious, extending downtime. Correctly identifying the underlying cause of a batching fault depends heavily on scarce, experienced troubleshooters. Misdiagnosis leads to wasted parts, repeat batches and prolonged downtime cost.
