Interpreting readings while accounting for known lag is a routine but experience-dependent operator skill. Lag itself carries little direct cost beyond the control quality it degrades.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of unavailable production capacity during formulation changeover
rows.imp_rat: Lost production capacity during formulation changeover is an efficiency delay that is relevant to throughput but has recoverable, non-catastrophic severity; kept at the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: SMED-style procedures and validated CIP cycles make changeover duration fairly repeatable though still a substantial gap. Formulation changeover downtime is a well-known lost-capacity window driven largely by cleaning and setup time. Efficient changeover depends on experienced crews executing CIP and setup correctly and quickly. Changeover consumes cleaning agents, water, energy and lost production time, a significant recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of liquid discarded during flushing and transition to the new formulation
rows.imp_rat: Liquid discarded during flushing and formulation transition is a waste/yield-efficiency error; peripheral to whether the plant does its core job and low severity since it is expected loss.
rows.sat_rat: Interface detection and CIP sequencing help, but transition loss remains large and variable in most plants. Flushing and transition routines are time-bounded but the discard volume itself is inherent to the changeover. Minimizing flush loss depends on operator skill in timing cut-points between product and flush. Significant saleable product is routinely lost to flush and transition, a well-known persistent give-away.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time required for the plant to stabilize at the requested throughput
rows.imp_rat: Time to stabilize at requested throughput touches production efficiency but is a startup transient, not central to the plant's ongoing job (moderate-low relevance); consequences are lost time and some off-spec product during ramp (moderate severity). Held to 3.0 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Automated setpoint ramping gives repeatable stabilization for common operating points but struggles with disturbances and grade-specific dynamics. PID control and cascade loops bring plants to requested throughput fairly quickly, though startup ramping and settling still consume noticeable time. Tuning loops for fast stabilization still leans on experienced control engineers, and poorly tuned loops oscillate. Extended stabilization wastes energy and off-spec product before steady state is reached.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of disruption across concurrent streams after an upset in one stream
rows.imp_rat: Cross-stream disruption after an upset propagates downtime, moderately relevant to overall plant stability, but the consequence is recoverable disruption rather than irreversible harm; capped at 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Interlocks and stream segregation limit some propagation but coupled utilities and shared resources still spread upsets unpredictably. An upset in one stream propagates disruption whose duration across concurrent streams is hard to bound. Containing cross-stream cascades demands skilled operators making fast isolation decisions. Cascading disruption wastes product and capacity across multiple streams at once.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of product or slurry remaining inside the tank after draining
rows.imp_rat: Product or slurry remaining after draining is a yield/waste concern (moderate relevance) with low severity since it is economic rather than catastrophic, staying well under the cap.
rows.sat_rat: Sloped bottoms and drain pumps reliably remove most liquid, though slurries and viscous product leave a stubborn heel. Gravity draining plus pumping heels is a routine, reasonably quick operation. Draining is a low-skill task using standard valves and pumps. Residual heels represent lost product and become hazardous-waste volume, but draining recovers the bulk economically.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of tank connections leaking after seal replacement
rows.imp_rat: Connections leaking after seal replacement is a rework/maintenance error with moderate consequences, but relevance is limited and leaks are typically detected quickly, keeping both proxies low.
rows.sat_rat: Proper gasket selection and controlled bolt-up make post-service leaks uncommon though re-torque is sometimes needed. Seal replacement and leak check are quick, routine tasks. Gasket/seal fitting is a common trade skill with well-known torque and alignment practices. Seals and gaskets are inexpensive, and rework cost is low.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of internal zones left untreated because they cannot be accessed during decontamination
rows.imp_rat: Untreated internal zones during decontamination can seed contamination, but this is a cleaning/access issue tangential to the batching CFJ and constrained by the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Standard CIP/SIP routines are designed for production, not disposal, so internal zones routinely go untreated with no reliable verification at end of life. Decontaminating dead legs and inaccessible internal zones adds significant time as crews improvise access or extend flush cycles. Identifying and reaching untreated internal zones on aging, undocumented systems demands scarce process and mechanical expertise. Disassembly, extra cleaning agents, and rework to reach hidden zones raise disposal cost materially.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of sterile fill-finish production disruption caused by system removal and handover
rows.imp_rat: Production disruption from system removal and handover (delay) is a transitional event not part of routine batching; its severity is bounded because it is a planned, scheduled downtime rather than an unexpected failure.
rows.sat_rat: Coordinated removal plans usually keep disruption bounded, though unforeseen tie-ins extend interruptions. System removal and handover directly consumes production time that planned-shutdown scheduling minimizes but cannot eliminate. Rigging and safe removal of large batching skids needs specialized crews. Downtime and specialist removal labor represent significant but planned costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of sterile fill-finish batching interruption during the transition
rows.imp_rat: Batching interruption during transition (delay) is a one-time changeover concern peripheral to the ongoing CFJ, with severity limited to planned lost-production time.
rows.sat_rat: Transition planning generally keeps interruptions predictable, with occasional overruns. Batching interruption during transition is scheduled into changeover windows to limit duration. Sequencing the transition to protect ongoing batches needs experienced production planners. Interrupted batching means idle lines, a real but anticipated cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of loose, degraded, or corroded batching-system connections remaining undetected after inspection
rows.imp_rat: Undetected loose or corroded connections after inspection can cause leaks, a moderately severe outcome, but this inspection-quality error is peripheral to the batching CFJ.
rows.sat_rat: Without instrumented connection monitoring, degraded or loose connections are commonly missed during walkdown inspections. Checking every connection is tedious and frequently rushed during turnarounds. Identifying loose or corroded connections relies on systematic technician attention that is easy to lapse. Undetected bad connections cause later leaks and failures, generating repeat callouts and downtime.

rows.stk: Instrumentation technicians, maintenance engineers, quality personnel.
rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of deviation from the specified tolerance after calibration
rows.imp_rat: Deviation from specified tolerance after calibration affects measurement accuracy, but it is a peripheral maintenance-quality issue with limited direct stakes for a single batch.
rows.sat_rat: Traceable reference standards and documented calibration procedures reliably bring devices well within tolerance in mature programs. Calibration procedures are well-defined and executed within scheduled service time. Standardized calibration methods and instruments reduce reliance on individual expertise. Calibration consumables and instrument time are modest and predictable.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that accessing tanks, piping, pumps, valves, mixers, sensors, or fittings for inspection causes damage or misalignment
rows.imp_rat: Damage or misalignment from accessing components for inspection (rework) is a maintenance-handling error peripheral to producing a batch, with consequences usually limited to repair rework.
rows.sat_rat: Procedures and access aids reduce incidents, but accidental damage or disturbed alignment during access still occurs with some regularity. Careful access to avoid damage slows the inspection but is factored into the plan. Accessing sensitive components without causing damage or misalignment relies on experienced, careful handling. Access-induced damage creates avoidable repair costs and additional downtime.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of residual process liquid remaining in tanks, vessels, and lines after drainage
rows.imp_rat: Residual liquid left after drainage relates to processing efficiency and changeover (moderate relevance) but consequences are usually minor yield loss or extra cleaning rather than catastrophic (low severity). Held to the 3.0 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Gravity draining and low-point drains handle most volume, but pockets in dead-legs and vessels routinely retain residue. Thorough draining takes time but is planned into decommissioning. Draining tanks and lines is standard operator work needing modest skill. Residual liquid left behind becomes hazardous waste, adding disposal cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of process-contact locations retaining detectable product traces after purging and cleaning
rows.imp_rat: Detectable product traces after cleaning touch on carryover risk (some relevance to the core job) but represent a residual cleaning-verification gap with manageable consequences here (moderate-low severity). Kept at or below the 3.0 cap.
rows.sat_rat: Purge-and-clean procedures are standard, yet detectable traces persist in complex geometry more often than desired. Repeat purging cycles to hit clean endpoints extend the schedule. Judging when process-contact surfaces are truly clean requires experienced cleaning technicians. Extra cleaning consumes solvent, water and disposal capacity.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required target-liquid property values that are missing, outdated, or inaccurate
rows.imp_rat: Missing or inaccurate target-liquid property values can cause off-spec output (severity) and is relevant to correct processing, but as a data-quality error it is typically catchable and correctable (relevance). Bounded by ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Property data is frequently incomplete, stale, or copied from similar products, so inaccurate inputs routinely corrupt downstream decisions. Chasing missing or outdated liquid property values causes real delays waiting on labs or suppliers. Knowing which property values matter and judging their currency requires process expertise not always available. Generating missing property data via testing can be costly and is often deferred.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of assigning an incorrect process-fit ranking when sterile fill-finish requirements are omitted
rows.imp_rat: Assigning an incorrect process-fit ranking when sterile fill-finish requirements are omitted is a selection/planning error, tangential to the running plant's core job (low-moderate relevance); a bad ranking could propagate wrong equipment choices (moderate severity). Held at 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Scoring matrices help, but when sterile criteria are omitted the ranking silently misdirects selection, a failure that often goes unnoticed until later. Re-ranking after catching omitted sterile requirements adds rework time to the evaluation. Correct process-fit ranking hinges on an expert weighting sterile requirements properly, and this judgement is error-prone without deep domain skill. Ranking is mostly an analytical exercise with little direct material cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of supplier compliance documents that do not correspond to the proposed plant configuration and intended sterile fill-finish process
rows.imp_rat: Supplier compliance documents mismatched to plant configuration create rework and audit exposure for sterile fill-finish, relevant administratively with moderate severity; held at the 3.0 cap.
rows.sat_rat: Suppliers often submit generic or template compliance docs that don't match the exact configuration, and this mismatch is commonly missed until audit. Matching each compliance document to the actual proposed configuration is a tedious, time-consuming reconciliation. Verifying that documents correspond to the specific configuration and process needs attentive, knowledgeable reviewers. Document reconciliation is labour cost rather than a major expenditure.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of batching tasks performed out of the required sequence during multi-operator production
rows.imp_rat: Out-of-sequence batching tasks in multi-operator production can cause errors, but they are usually recoverable procedural slips with moderate relevance to the core batching job.
rows.sat_rat: Electronic work instructions and sequence enforcement help, but human handoffs in multi-operator production still produce out-of-sequence execution with notable frequency. Out-of-sequence tasks across operators cause rework and waiting, adding avoidable time in multi-operator runs. Keeping the required task order across multiple operators depends heavily on procedural discipline and coordination skill. Sequence errors can spoil batches or require reprocessing, a recurring cost in busy multi-operator production.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of disagreement among process readings used to verify the batch endpoint
rows.imp_rat: Disagreement among readings used to verify endpoint complicates confirmation, but it is a bounded verification-step issue with limited consequence to the overall job.
rows.sat_rat: Multi-parameter endpoint checks and redundant sensors reduce disagreement, but calibration drift and single-point measurements still leave conflicting readings unresolved. Reconciling disagreeing readings at endpoint takes extra verification time. Judging which reading to trust when instruments disagree is a distinctly skill-dependent call. Redundant instrumentation to reduce disagreement adds capital cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that endpoint criteria cannot be matched unambiguously to the available process readings
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguous mapping of endpoint criteria to process readings (item 3) can delay confirmation, but this is largely a definitional/configuration concern with moderate relevance and modest, recoverable consequences. Within the 3.5 cap.
rows.sat_rat: Endpoint criteria often reference qualities not directly measured by installed sensors, leaving recurring interpretive ambiguity. Mapping endpoint criteria to available readings is quick when instrumentation is well-defined. Interpreting whether existing process readings unambiguously satisfy endpoint criteria demands engineering judgement. Ambiguity causes disputed dispositions and delays rather than large direct material cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of exceeding the allowable batch hold limit while completing the step manually
rows.imp_rat: Exceeding the allowable hold limit while completing a step manually (item 18) can compromise a batch, but it is a manual-fallback timing concern of moderate relevance and modest severity. Within cap.
rows.sat_rat: Timers and alarms exist to flag approaching hold limits, but manual completion means breaches still happen with real frequency. The core error is elapsed time exceeding a hold limit while a step is done manually, and manual execution is inherently slow and variable. Beating the hold clock manually requires practiced operators who work efficiently under pressure, so skill matters. Exceeding hold limits forces disposition or scrap of the batch, a recurring cost when steps run long.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time between recognizing a process deviation and completing the required setpoint correction
rows.imp_rat: Time between recognizing a process deviation and completing setpoint correction affects responsiveness, but modest delays rarely cause irreversible harm to the batch.
rows.sat_rat: Standard alarm-to-action workflows are well established, though inconsistent operator response and procedure gaps leave residual variability. Recognizing a deviation and executing the setpoint correction is fast on modern HMIs, though root-cause confirmation before acting adds delay. The correction magnitude depends on operator judgement, so response time varies with expertise. Prompt correction bounds off-spec production, a meaningful but not dominant saving.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of process-parameter oscillation caused by competing setpoint changes during an active batch
rows.imp_rat: Parameter oscillation from competing setpoint changes degrades batch stability, but the effect is typically transient and correctable, keeping both proxies modest.
rows.sat_rat: PID tuning, cascade control and interlock logic in standard DCS/PLC systems dampen most oscillation, though poorly tuned loops and manual overrides still cause recurring hunting. Modern batch controllers apply setpoint changes within seconds, so oscillation is not primarily a delay problem. Avoiding competing setpoint changes still leans on experienced operators who understand loop interactions and hold discipline during a batch. Oscillation wastes reagents and energy through overcorrection, but the recurring cost is modest relative to a lost batch.

rows.source_job: Coordinate skid with connected process units
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that material transfer starts before the receiving process unit is ready
rows.imp_rat: Starting material transfer before the receiving unit is ready can cause loss, but this sequencing error is bounded by the batch ranking with moderate consequences. Held at 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Where inter-unit permissives are configured premature transfer is well controlled, but manual coordination between skids still leaves gaps. Handshake/permissive checks add brief waits but prevent larger downstream delays. Automated readiness interlocks reduce reliance on operators to confirm the receiving unit manually. Starting transfer into an unready unit can spill or spoil material, wasting product.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of deviation between the as-maintained skid configuration and the approved baseline
rows.imp_rat: Deviation between as-maintained and approved baseline is a configuration-management error; moderately relevant to compliance and moderate consequences. Held at 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Change control and as-built records manage configuration reasonably well, yet undocumented field changes and drawing lag leave recurring mismatches with the baseline. Verifying as-maintained configuration against baseline adds documentation and walkdown time. Recognizing configuration drift requires knowledgeable personnel comparing against approved drawings. Configuration management tooling and audits cost money, but undetected drift causes costly deviations.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of incorrectly diagnosing the cause of an equipment fault
rows.imp_rat: Incorrect fault diagnosis prolongs recovery, but per the batch ceiling this diagnostic error ranks lower; consequences are extended downtime and misdirected repair effort rather than catastrophic loss.
rows.sat_rat: Diagnostic tools and procedures help, but complex multi-cause faults still frequently get misattributed. Misdiagnosis sends crews down wrong repair paths, wasting hours before the true cause is found. Correct fault diagnosis leans heavily on scarce experienced troubleshooters and their tacit knowledge of the plant. Wrong diagnoses waste replacement parts and labor on components that were not the problem.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of validation samples compromised during collection or handling
rows.imp_rat: Compromised validation samples affect verification confidence (moderate relevance/severity) but represent a rework-and-resample burden rather than direct product harm. Averaged at the 3.0 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Documented sampling SOPs reduce contamination, but human handling variability means a persistent fraction is still compromised. Recollecting compromised samples adds delay, but individual sampling is quick relative to the overall campaign. Aseptic, representative sampling depends heavily on operator technique and vigilance, which is a common source of avoidable error. Compromised samples waste reagents and analysis cost and may force repeat runs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of designated moving components receiving incompatible lubricant
rows.imp_rat: Incompatible lubricant on moving components can degrade bearings and contaminate product, but per the batch ceiling this maintenance-quality error ranks below higher-tier items; relevance is moderate and severity moderate-to-serious due to contamination risk, capped at 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Color-coding, dedicated equipment, and lube charts make incompatible-lubricant errors uncommon though not eliminated. Selecting the right lubricant is a quick lookup step with negligible time impact. Avoiding cross-contamination relies on labeling discipline and technician care rather than advanced skill. Wrong lubricant can ruin components and force costly rework, but incidents are relatively infrequent.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of designated moving components exceeding their specified lubrication interval
rows.imp_rat: Missing lubrication intervals on designated components risks wear and unplanned downtime, moderately relevant to keeping the plant running, with contained severity; held at the 3.0 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: CMMS-driven schedules make interval compliance well managed, though a fraction of points still slip past due dates under workload pressure. Scheduled lubrication is a short recurring task easily fit into planned routes. Adhering to intervals is a scheduling and compliance matter, requiring diligence more than expertise. Missed intervals accelerate wear, but the lubrication itself is inexpensive and well provisioned.

rows.stk: Maintenance engineers, plant operators, reliability teams.
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a plant health indicator misrepresents the actual equipment condition
rows.imp_rat: A misrepresenting health indicator can mask degradation, yet its impact on the core processing job is indirect; the error's consequence is delayed intervention, which is recoverable in most cases.
rows.sat_rat: Condition indicators are often indirect proxies with limited coverage, so misrepresentation of actual equipment state remains fairly common. A misleading health indicator delays correct action until the true condition is discovered the hard way. Interpreting whether an indicator reflects real condition demands engineering judgement about sensor placement and failure modes. False confidence or false alarms drive either unnecessary intervention or costly missed failures.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of the processed liquid becoming contaminated between plant discharge and receipt
rows.imp_rat: Contamination between plant discharge and receipt directly threatens product safety (high severity) and is fairly central to delivering usable liquid (relevance), yet the ranking constraint holds this item to a 3.0 maximum.
rows.sat_rat: Dedicated lines, tankers and cleaning regimes keep cross-contamination between discharge and receipt low. Contamination in transit rarely adds delay unless detected at receipt. Clean transfer practices are routine and need little specialist skill. Contaminated batches may be rejected, wasting product and transport cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of the processed liquid entering the wrong receiving system
rows.imp_rat: Processed liquid entering the wrong receiving system risks cross-contamination and loss (moderate severity) and relates to correct output routing (moderate relevance); capped at the 3.0 batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Line lockouts, valve tagging and transfer permits make wrong-system routing an uncommon event. Misrouting is quickly noticed but recovery adds little time when caught early. Correct routing depends on operator attention and clear labeling rather than scarce expertise. Sending liquid to the wrong system can contaminate a receiving stream, causing costly losses.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of transferred liquid that falls outside release specifications during transfer
rows.imp_rat: Transferred liquid falling outside release specifications during transfer affects product quality (moderate severity) and output correctness (moderate relevance), but is bounded by the batch's 3.0 importance ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Inline sampling and release testing catch most deviations, but spec excursions during transfer still recur. Out-of-spec transfers may require re-processing, extending the operation. Meeting release specs during transfer needs sampling and QC judgement. Off-spec liquid is downgraded or wasted, adding cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of unintended residual fluid trapped at piping connections after transfer or cleaning
rows.imp_rat: Residual fluid trapped at piping connections is relevant to cleaning and cross-contamination with moderate severity, but this hygienic-design detail ranks below the top batch.
rows.sat_rat: Hygienic connection design mitigates trapping, but field connections still trap fluid inconsistently, so the need is only partially served. Draining and verifying no trapped fluid at connections adds handling time. Recognizing and clearing dead-leg trapping requires hygienic-design and operator knowledge. Trapped residual fluid causes contamination and rework costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that obsolete or unapproved recipe versions are designated as current after import
rows.imp_rat: Designating obsolete recipe versions as current after import is relevant to formulation correctness with moderate severity, but as an import-administration error it ranks below the top batch.
rows.sat_rat: Import routines often don't enforce approval status automatically, so unapproved versions can be flagged current, leaving a recurring gap. Confirming the approval status of an imported version after import adds a verification step. It takes quality/change-control judgement to distinguish approved from unapproved versions during import. Designating an unapproved version as current risks scrapped batches and compliance costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that authorized users cannot perform assigned control-system tasks because required permissions are missing
rows.imp_rat: Missing permissions blocking authorized users (defect) is largely an access-administration issue peripheral to the batching CFJ, with modest consequences as it usually causes short workflow delays rather than batch loss.
rows.sat_rat: Role-based access provisioning is standard practice yet role definitions are frequently incomplete at preparation time, so authorized users still routinely hit missing-permission walls. Missing permissions surface as work stoppages that must be escalated and remediated, adding unpredictable delay during commissioning. Correctly mapping assigned control-system tasks to the exact permission set requires administrators who understand both the DCS role model and the operational duties, a combination that is often thin. Blocked authorized users idle expensive commissioning resources, but the incremental money lost per incident is usually modest.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percent of operating limits outside validated ranges for the handled liquid and sterile preparation process
rows.imp_rat: Operating limits outside validated ranges is relevant to process compliance with moderate severity, but overlaps other items and ranks below the prior batch, so it is capped.
rows.sat_rat: Alignment of configured limits to validated ranges relies on manual cross-referencing to validation documents, so mismatches persist and the need is poorly-to-acceptably served. Verifying that limits match validated ranges for a specific sterile process is a detailed, time-consuming review. This requires specialized sterile-process and validation expertise that is scarce. Limits outside validated ranges risk failed sterile batches with high scrap and reprocessing cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the combined capacity, flow, and timing limits make the required batch cycle infeasible
rows.imp_rat: Infeasible batch cycles from combined capacity/flow/timing limits are a design-phase constraint, largely resolved before production, keeping relevance to routine execution low.
rows.sat_rat: Established sizing calculations and simulation tools make most cycle-time bottlenecks visible before startup, though hidden interaction effects still occasionally render a required cycle infeasible. Capacity and flow feasibility checks during preparation are typically done up front through process calculations, so they rarely add delay once the design basis is set. Verifying that combined capacity, flow, and timing limits support the batch cycle demands experienced process engineers whose judgement is scarce and not fully proceduralized. A missed feasibility limit surfaces late and forces costly rework or equipment changes, but this is caught often enough that catastrophic overspend is uncommon.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the operator pauses the wrong batch cycle during an urgent intervention
rows.imp_rat: Pausing the wrong batch during an urgent intervention has moderate severity and some relevance to safe operation, but as an operator-action error it is capped below the higher batch.
rows.sat_rat: Unit-specific HMI displays and clear batch tagging reduce mis-selection, but crowded multi-unit screens still allow wrong-batch pauses during urgent intervention. Selecting the right cycle to pause under urgency can cost seconds of navigation, a minor delay in a crisis. Correctly identifying the target batch among several depends on operator familiarity with the interface and unit naming, a real skill demand under pressure. Pausing the wrong batch can disrupt or spoil an unrelated batch, an infrequent but costly mistake.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of uncertainty about whether the active batch cycle has fully paused
rows.imp_rat: Uncertainty about whether the batch cycle has fully paused is relevant to safe intervention and carries moderate severity, but it is a peripheral confirmation error ranked below the prior batch.
rows.sat_rat: Modern HMIs give explicit hold/paused state indication, making pause confirmation generally clear, though ambiguous or lagging indicators still occur in older or partial systems. State feedback appears on the HMI quickly after a pause, so operators are not left waiting long to know the status. Reading pause/hold status from the HMI requires minimal skill given standard status indicators. Uncertainty about pause state could prompt costly wrong actions, but clear status displays keep such errors limited.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of assigning the wrong initiating event when responses cascade through the batching system
rows.imp_rat: Assigning the wrong initiating event in a cascade (ambiguity) is a diagnostic-analysis error peripheral to the batching CFJ itself, with consequences mostly extended troubleshooting effort.
rows.sat_rat: Sequence-of-events recording and time-stamped logs help where present, but many systems lack the resolution to reliably isolate the first cause. Untangling a cascade to find the initiating event is time-consuming during a trip. Assigning the true first-cause among cascading responses is a demanding diagnostic skill few operators possess. Chasing the wrong initiating event wastes labor and can trigger unnecessary corrective spend.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of discrepancy between indicated batch status and the actual process state
rows.imp_rat: Discrepancy between indicated and actual batch status can mislead operators, but such gaps are often reconciled through checks, giving moderate-low relevance and severity.
rows.sat_rat: State machines and batch execution software (e.g. S88) track status well, yet sensor faults and unmodeled states still create discrepancies between indication and reality. Status updates on the HMI are near real-time, so lag is minor. Interpreting whether indicated status truly matches the physical state still requires process understanding. Acting on a false status can waste a batch, but the monitoring itself is inexpensive.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that required ingredients are unavailable in sufficient quantities for the enlarged batch
rows.imp_rat: Ingredient unavailability for an enlarged batch (delay) relates to scale-up logistics rather than core batching function, and the consequence is typically a schedule slip rather than a ruined product.
rows.sat_rat: ERP/MRP and inventory systems reliably flag shortages before batching, though manual dispensing errors and unlogged stock still occasionally cause surprises. Checking ingredient availability before scaling is a quick step in most planning workflows. Inventory checks are routine and require little specialized skill. Holding sufficient inventory for enlarged batches ties up working capital.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of the batch exceeding allowable hold limits before the batching fault is resolved
rows.imp_rat: The batch exceeding hold limits before a fault is resolved (item 20) can waste material, but it is a fault-recovery timing concern of moderate relevance and modest severity. Held to the 3.5 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Fault diagnosis and repair times vary wildly with no standard fast-recovery practice, so hold-limit breaches during faults are a frequent, poorly served pain. The clock keeps running while a batching fault is diagnosed and fixed, and fault resolution time is unpredictable and often long. Fast fault resolution requires scarce troubleshooting expertise, so the outcome hinges heavily on who is available. Unresolved faults that push a batch past hold limits routinely destroy batch value, a significant recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of incompatibilities between candidate-system controls and planned site interfaces and protocols
rows.imp_rat: Incompatibilities between candidate controls and site interfaces/protocols matter for integration but are a pre-deployment defect; severity is moderate as it causes rework rather than harm, held under the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Standard interface specifications and FAT checks catch many issues, but protocol and site-integration mismatches still recur. Verifying control-system compatibility with site interfaces requires coordination time that is often available. Automation and IT integration expertise is needed to identify control/protocol incompatibilities. Late-discovered incompatibilities drive integration rework costs, though upfront review is modest.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of safety-critical controls each operator misunderstands after training
rows.imp_rat: Safety-critical controls misunderstood after training raises real safety severity, and relevance is moderate to safe operation; the batch ordering nonetheless caps its mean at 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Competency assessments and refresher training catch many gaps, but some controls remain misunderstood after standard training rounds. Training and comprehension checks are scheduled activities that add planned time. Reducing misunderstanding hinges on trainer quality and material clarity, making this fundamentally a skill-transfer challenge. Misunderstood controls cost mostly in retraining and potential incident risk rather than direct spend.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of trained operators who execute safety-critical controls incorrectly during qualification
rows.imp_rat: Trained operators executing safety-critical controls incorrectly during qualification is relevant to safe operation and severity is elevated given safety stakes, but the batch ceiling caps the mean at 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Structured SOPs and qualification protocols reduce errors, yet a real fraction of operators still execute incorrectly on first qualification. Qualification testing takes scheduled time but is planned into the commissioning window. Correct execution of safety-critical controls depends directly on training quality and operator aptitude, the core variable this need targets. Failed qualifications require retraining and retesting, a recurring but manageable expense.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of misalignment between vessel, agitator, piping, and skid interfaces
rows.imp_rat: Interface misalignment between vessel/agitator/piping/skid is an installation defect with modest relevance and moderate severity from leaks or poor fit, but remains a lower-tier build-quality item.
rows.sat_rat: Machined interfaces and factory-skidded assemblies make good alignment repeatable, though field connections still show some deviation. Alignment and shimming is a familiar task but precise interface fit-up can consume meaningful setup time. Achieving tight interface alignment depends on skilled fitters using dial indicators and laser tools, so quality varies with crew expertise. Misalignment causes wear and rework, but standard shims, adjustable mounts, and skid pre-alignment limit the added cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of connecting vessel, agitator, piping, or skid modules in the wrong configuration
rows.imp_rat: Wrong configuration of vessel/agitator/piping/skid modules can compromise assembly, but it is an installation-phase rework error (modest relevance) with correctable, moderate consequences held under the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Assembly drawings, match-marks, and modular skid designs make correct configuration mostly reliable, with residual errors on complex custom builds. Assembling modules per assembly drawings is well-paced but reconfiguring a wrong build costs significant time. Correct skid and vessel configuration leans on experienced millwrights and clear drawings, so judgement gaps produce errors. Wrong configurations mean disassembly and re-fit labor, a real but bounded expense given standard build procedures.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of mismatch between facility utility characteristics and system connection requirements
rows.imp_rat: Mismatch between facility utilities and system connections is an installation defect with modest relevance and moderate severity from commissioning delays, capped for this batch.
rows.sat_rat: Site surveys and utility datasheets catch most mismatches, but incomplete facility data leaves recurring surprises at connection time. Confirming utility characteristics against requirements is done in engineering review, adding little to field time when done upfront. Matching voltage, pressure, and flow specs requires engineering diligence, and oversights slip through when documentation is incomplete. A utility mismatch can force costly retrofits or transformers, and such surprises are not rare on retrofit projects.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of utility lines connected to the wrong service or system connection point
rows.imp_rat: Mis-connected utility lines are an installation error peripheral to batch composition itself, with consequences generally caught during commissioning and of moderate severity. Below ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Standard tagging and checkout sheets cover most utility connections, but the volume and similarity of small lines leaves recurring gaps. Utility hookup checks are routine but the sheer number of small service lines can extend commissioning time. Distinguishing similar utility connection points needs attentive technicians, and fatigue or unclear labels drive occasional errors. A miswired utility can damage equipment or waste consumables, though catch rates during checkout keep major losses infrequent.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the control software is incompatible with dedicated computing infrastructure
rows.imp_rat: Control software incompatible with computing infrastructure has modest relevance to getting the system operational and moderate commissioning severity, held under the batch cap.
rows.sat_rat: Vendors typically ship validated hardware-software bundles, making incompatibility uncommon where standard packaged systems are used. Verifying software-hardware compatibility is a defined FAT/SAT step that rarely delays if hardware is pre-specified. It requires controls engineers who understand OS and driver requirements, a specialized but generally available skill set. An incompatibility discovered late means replacement hardware and delay, though vendor-specified bundles usually prevent this.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an interrupted installation leaves control software partially deployed
rows.imp_rat: A partially deployed control install from an interrupted installation blocks commissioning, moderately relevant; severity is bounded because it is typically detected and re-run before production, and the ceiling constrains it.
rows.sat_rat: Modern installers with rollback and validation help, but interrupted installs still occasionally leave systems in an ambiguous partial state. A partial install requires troubleshooting and reinstallation that can add unplanned time during commissioning. Recovering from a broken deployment needs skilled controls or IT staff who understand the install state, which not all sites have on hand. Partial deployments waste engineer time but rarely destroy expensive assets, keeping cost impact moderate.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of required batch data fields left blank in the production record
rows.imp_rat: Blank batch data fields undermine record completeness (modest relevance) and carry moderate regulatory-traceability severity, but as a documentation defect it stays under the batch cap.
rows.sat_rat: Mandatory-field enforcement in electronic batch records makes missing data one of the best-served needs, as records cannot be closed with required blanks. Electronic records prompt entries in workflow, adding negligible time. Guided electronic batch records require little skill to complete correctly since fields are structured and prompted. Blank fields cause review delays and rework of records rather than direct material loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required process parameters or cycle results omitted from the batch record
rows.imp_rat: Omitted process parameters or cycle results in the batch record hurt traceability (modest relevance) with moderate compliance severity, but ranks below the prior batch.
rows.sat_rat: Automated data acquisition tied to the batch record makes parameter omission uncommon where such systems are deployed, a well-served need with residual gaps on manual-entry points. Automated capture of process parameters logs data continuously without added operator time. Automatic data historian and EBR capture remove dependence on operators remembering to record cycle results. Omitted parameters trigger investigation and documentation rework costs more than material costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of trainee control inputs that move critical parameters outside approved limits
rows.imp_rat: Trainee inputs pushing parameters outside limits can affect batch quality (modest relevance) and cause moderate spoilage risk, but as a training-instability issue it stays within the lower ordering tier.
rows.sat_rat: Access controls, interlocks and alarm limits catch many out-of-limit inputs, but supervision-dependent gaps leave real residual risk. Trainee inputs happen in real time, but the error concerns correctness rather than speed. This need is fundamentally about compensating for low operator skill, and status quo relies on supervision plus system permission limits with mixed success. Trainee-caused excursions can spoil batches, adding rework and scrap cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of installing an incorrect consumable in the batch preparation system
rows.imp_rat: Installing an incorrect consumable has modest relevance to proper function and modest severity since errors are typically detected and swapped, remaining lower-priority.
rows.sat_rat: Labeling, kitting, and work instructions reduce wrong installs, but look-alike parts and manual handling still allow errors to reach the system. Installing the correct consumable is quick once selected, though verification adds small time. Physical installation of the wrong-but-fitting consumable happens when staff lack awareness of subtle spec differences. An incorrect installed consumable causes waste, contamination, or rework carrying real cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of selecting a replacement consumable that does not match the system specification
rows.imp_rat: Selecting an off-spec replacement consumable has modest relevance to correct operation and modest severity since mismatches are usually caught, keeping it in the lower tier.
rows.sat_rat: BOM references and CMMS parts links make correct selection common, but ambiguous specs, obsolete parts, and substitution decisions still cause mismatches. Looking up the correct consumable spec adds minor time, usually supported by parts catalogs. Matching a replacement to specification needs familiarity with the BOM, and generic-substitute temptation trips up less-informed staff. Correct parts management and stocking cost money, while wrong-spec purchases waste both the part and the rework.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of incorrectly identifying the system fault's root cause during diagnosis
rows.imp_rat: Incorrect root-cause diagnosis prolongs downtime and misdirects repairs, giving it modest relevance to keeping the system running and moderate severity, but capped as a lower-tier maintenance error.
rows.sat_rat: Diagnostic aids and alarm logs help, but multi-domain faults on integrated systems still produce frequent wrong root-cause conclusions. Root-cause diagnosis on complex batch systems can drag on through trial-and-error, extending downtime. Correct root-cause identification depends heavily on scarce, experienced troubleshooters, and less-skilled staff routinely fix symptoms rather than causes. Misdiagnosis wastes replacement parts and repeat labor, a recurring and often unbudgeted cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of misidentifying the source of a mechanical, electrical, or control fault
rows.imp_rat: Misidentifying a fault source overlaps with diagnosis rework, giving modest relevance to uptime and modest severity from misdirected repair effort within this lower-priority batch.
rows.sat_rat: Fault codes and diagnostics narrow the field, but overlapping symptoms across mechanical, electrical, and control layers still cause frequent misidentification. Isolating whether a fault is mechanical, electrical, or control-related consumes significant diagnostic time on interlinked systems. Correctly attributing the fault source across disciplines requires broad cross-domain expertise that few individual technicians hold. Misattributing the source leads to swapping the wrong subsystem and wasted parts and effort.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of recipe, configuration, or batch records omitted from the backup
rows.imp_rat: Records omitted from the backup undermine recoverability, which is meaningful but a supporting function; severity is real yet limited if originals persist, kept at the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Scheduled backups cover core databases well, but records stored outside the main system or added later are commonly omitted from the backup scope. Automated backup jobs capture data quickly once configured, minimizing ongoing time. Ensuring every recipe, config, and batch record is in scope requires knowing where all data lives, a skill gap when systems are distributed. Additional storage for complete backups is inexpensive relative to the loss of missing records.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that backed-up recipe and configuration data cannot be restored after data loss
rows.imp_rat: Failure to restore backed-up recipe/configuration data after loss is relevant to continuity, but capped by batch ranking; severity is notable since irrecoverable data forces reconstruction, though it is a recovery-edge case rather than the core prep job.
rows.sat_rat: Backups are commonly taken yet restore testing is frequently neglected, so undetected corruption or version incompatibility leaves many backups effectively unrecoverable. Backups run quickly, but restore verification is rarely done and only discovered as slow when disaster strikes. Successful restoration of recipe and configuration data often requires specialized vendor or controls knowledge not present on the maintenance team. Backup storage is cheap, but an unrestorable backup imposes catastrophic recovery costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required preventive maintenance activities omitted from the schedule
rows.imp_rat: Omitting required PM activities from the schedule is a maintenance-planning gap peripheral to the batch-prep CFJ, with consequences of gradual reliability loss that are moderate. Below ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: OEM manuals and CMMS templates capture standard PM tasks well, but activities not translated into the schedule during setup remain silently omitted. Building the PM task list is a one-time setup that saves time thereafter, but omissions surface only slowly. Deciding which PM activities the system actually requires demands OEM knowledge and engineering judgement that is a frequent gap. Thorough PM planning takes engineering effort upfront, and skipped tasks trade near-term savings for later failure cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that preventive maintenance is scheduled after the required service interval
rows.imp_rat: Late-scheduled preventive maintenance is an operational upkeep error only tangentially central to preparing a batch, with consequences of elevated failure risk that are moderate and recoverable. Below ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Widely deployed CMMS with due-date alerts reliably prevents most late scheduling, with lapses mainly from resource backlogs rather than the schedule itself. CMMS scheduling automatically triggers PM due dates, largely eliminating the delay that causes overdue service. Once a PM plan is configured, little skill is needed to follow the generated schedule, though setting correct intervals requires expertise. CMMS licensing and administration cost money but are standard, and on-time PM reduces expensive breakdown costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required validation, calibration, and compliance records omitted from the archive
rows.imp_rat: Validation/calibration/compliance records omitted from the archive carries regulatory weight, giving some severity, but is a documentation-support task less central than core prep; capped at 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Quality archiving practices exist under GxP but records fragmented across paper and systems still get omitted from the archive. Assembling validation and calibration records for archive is a lengthy manual gathering effort at closeout. Knowing the full set of required compliance records demands regulatory and quality expertise that is not always retained at disposal. Archiving costs are low but locating scattered historical records consumes labor.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required validation, calibration, or compliance records missing from the archive
rows.imp_rat: Validation/calibration/compliance records missing from the archive duplicates item 12's regulatory documentation concern; moderate stakes but ranked below prior batch, capped at 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Missing validation or compliance records in the archive remains a common deficiency found on audit after disposal. Compiling calibration and compliance records at end of life is a slow, records-chasing exercise. Determining which compliance records are mandatory requires quality-system knowledge that thins out during decommissioning. The cost is mainly labor hours rather than capital, but that labor is often underallocated.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the supplier rejects the equipment return because decontamination status is unverifiable
rows.imp_rat: Unverifiable decontamination status blocking equipment return is a peripheral logistics/rework issue (low relevance) with moderate consequences of delayed return and disputes (moderate severity), but does not derail batch preparation itself.
rows.sat_rat: Decontamination certificates exist as a practice but unverifiable or inconsistent documentation causes returns to be rejected fairly often. Generating decontamination certificates and documentation adds delay when supplier requirements are discovered late. Producing verifiable cleanliness evidence requires knowing supplier-specific and regulatory documentation standards that are easily misjudged. Failed returns trigger re-cleaning, re-shipping, and disposal costs that recur when documentation is incomplete.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required batch records, recipes, and process data omitted from the export
rows.imp_rat: Omitting required batch records/recipes/process data from the export affects data portability and compliance; relevance and severity are moderate but ranked below prior batch, so capped at 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Standard export procedures capture most records, though omissions from legacy or fragmented systems remain a recurring problem. Exporting batch records from modern systems is reasonably quick when tools and access are available. Complete data export depends on knowing where all records reside, requiring system familiarity that may be lost at end of life. Export tooling and archival storage are modest costs relative to the value of the records.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required batch records, recipes, or process data omitted from the export
rows.imp_rat: Duplicate-phrased export omission of required records affects data completeness; moderate relevance and severity, ranked lower than prior batch and capped at 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Records are usually exportable but omissions of ancillary recipes and process data still occur during rushed decommissioning. Data extraction is generally fast once the correct interfaces and credentials are located. Ensuring nothing is missed requires operators who understand the full recipe and record structure, expertise often departed by disposal. Export and storage costs are low, so the barrier is completeness rather than money.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of proprietary formula data remaining recoverable from system controllers after sanitization
rows.imp_rat: Recoverable proprietary formula data after sanitization is a data-security concern (low relevance to preparing batches) though breach consequences carry moderate severity, kept under the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Recoverable proprietary formula data routinely persists on controllers because sanitization is inconsistent and rarely verified. Proper controller sanitization is often skipped or rushed because it is treated as an afterthought at end of life. Effective data sanitization of embedded controllers requires IT/OT security skills rarely applied to disposed process equipment. Certified wiping or media destruction adds cost that is frequently unfunded for decommissioned gear.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of proprietary formula data remaining recoverable from controller storage after sanitization
rows.imp_rat: Residual recoverable formula data in controller storage is a security/decommissioning defect with low relevance to batch preparation and moderate IP-loss severity, held to the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Formula data remaining recoverable from controller storage is a chronic, poorly controlled gap in typical disposal practice. Thorough storage sanitization is time-consuming and commonly abbreviated during disposal. Ensuring data is unrecoverable from controller storage media demands specialized sanitization expertise seldom engaged. Physical media destruction or certified erasure is an added expense often avoided for scrapped equipment.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that selected configuration options and accessories are incompatible
rows.imp_rat: Incompatible selected configuration options and accessories cause integration rework, moderately relevant; severity is contained as a pre-purchase selection error, kept under the cap.
rows.sat_rat: Supplier configuration tools and quotes generally ensure selected options are compatible, with only occasional oversights. Checking option and accessory compatibility fits within normal vendor configuration review time. Vendor configurators and application engineers reduce the expertise buyers need to verify compatibility. Incompatible options cause rework costs, but vendor-guided selection largely prevents this cheaply.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the delivery route or site access cannot accommodate the system
rows.imp_rat: Delivery route or site access unable to accommodate the system is a logistics blocker, moderately relevant to deployment; severity is bounded as a one-time planning issue, held at the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Pre-delivery site and route surveys are standard practice and reliably prevent access surprises, making this among the best-served needs. Delivery route and access assessments are done quickly using dimensions and site drawings. Straightforward logistics and facilities checks require little specialized expertise. Access problems are expensive if missed, but rigging surveys are inexpensive and routine.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of batch preparation requirements lacking verifiable acceptance criteria
rows.imp_rat: Requirements lacking verifiable acceptance criteria create ambiguity with modest relevance to correct outcomes and modest severity from rework, kept within the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Many requirements are still specified qualitatively without measurable acceptance criteria, so this remains a common recurring weakness. Writing verifiable acceptance criteria for every requirement is laborious and often deprioritized under schedule pressure. Translating requirements into measurable, testable acceptance criteria demands strong validation and engineering skill. Unverifiable criteria cause disputed acceptance and rework costs, though drafting criteria is cheap.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that system utility demands exceed available site supply
rows.imp_rat: System utility demands exceeding site supply can stall operation, giving moderate relevance; severity is bounded as it is a planning-stage constraint usually caught before install, held under 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Vendor utility datasheets and site utility surveys make demand-versus-supply mismatches reliably caught in mature practice. Comparing utility demands against site supply is a quick, routine check early in acquisition. Basic facilities engineering knowledge suffices to compare utility loads to available supply. Utility shortfalls found late are costly, but datasheet-based checks are cheap and standard.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of discrepancy between actual and indicated agitator speed
rows.imp_rat: Agitator speed discrepancy affects mixing accuracy (relevance) but consequence is usually recoverable; kept low.
rows.sat_rat: Calibrated speed feedback keeps indicated and actual speed closely matched on most systems. Speed verification is quick and periodic. No special skill needed to trust a calibrated tachometer readout. Speed indication error rarely causes direct material loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of original information becoming unrecoverable during record correction
rows.imp_rat: Losing original information during correction harms record integrity (severity) but is peripheral to physical blending; low relevance.
rows.sat_rat: GMP correction conventions reliably preserve original information on paper records. Proper correction adds a few seconds per edit. Single-line-through correction is a trained GMP habit. Lost original data drives investigations, not material cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of disagreement among replicate concentration results used to assess the endpoint
rows.imp_rat: Replicate result disagreement affects endpoint confidence (relevance) but is usually resolvable; kept low.
rows.sat_rat: Validated methods usually give tight replicates, though sampling and assay variability still produce occasional spread. Disagreement among replicates triggers retesting that delays disposition. Interpreting replicate spread requires analytical judgement. Replicate disagreement drives extra testing cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of lingering doubt about aseptic integrity after an intervention near the compounding vessel
rows.imp_rat: Doubt about aseptic integrity after intervention affects confidence (severity) but is an assurance-ambiguity issue; low relevance.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of lingering doubt that the homogeneous endpoint was correctly recognized
rows.imp_rat: Lingering doubt about endpoint recognition can delay/misjudge disposition (severity) but is largely resolvable; low relevance.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of excursions outside approved compounding ranges that interrupt batch execution
rows.imp_rat: Excursions interrupting execution affect throughput (relevance) with recoverable consequence; kept low.
rows.sat_rat: Automated controls hold most parameters in range, but excursions still occasionally interrupt batches. Excursions that interrupt execution cause pauses that extend the batch window. Keeping conditions within range depends on control-loop tuning and operator attention. Interruptions idle the vessel and risk batch quality, incurring cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of blend-quality risk assessments based on incomplete, inaccurate, or untraceable evidence
rows.imp_rat: Risk assessments on incomplete evidence weaken judgment (severity) but are peripheral to the physical mixing job; low relevance.
rows.sat_rat: Risk assessments frequently rest on incomplete or untraceable evidence when source records are fragmented, a recurring weakness. Verifying evidence adequacy adds review time, not the core failure. Judging evidence completeness and traceability for risk assessment demands skilled reviewers. Mostly review labor cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of credible cross-contamination, bioburden, and cleaning-state hazards omitted from blend-quality risk evaluation
rows.imp_rat: Omitting cross-contamination hazards from evaluation weakens safety review (severity) but is an assessment-completeness issue; low relevance.
rows.sat_rat: Structured risk tools help, but omission of credible hazards persists because coverage depends on assessor thoroughness. Hazard identification is a front-loaded assessment activity, not a timing issue. Comprehensively identifying credible contamination and cleaning-state hazards depends on expert risk-assessment competence that varies widely. Risk assessment is staff-time cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unresolved conflicts between applied homogeneity criteria and approved batch specifications
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting homogeneity criteria vs specs can cause wrong acceptance (severity) but is a criteria-alignment ambiguity; low relevance.
rows.sat_rat: Specification management systems help align criteria and specs, but unreconciled conflicts still slip through during changes. Resolving criteria-versus-spec conflicts takes review time but the issue is the conflict itself. Detecting conflicts between applied criteria and approved specs requires knowledgeable quality reviewers. Mostly staff effort, minimal material cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of vessel, formulation, or blending-instruction changes implemented without validation impact approval
rows.imp_rat: Unapproved changes could invalidate the process (severity) but this is a change-control matter; low relevance to a given blend.
rows.sat_rat: Formal change-control systems make unapproved changes uncommon, though informal or emergency changes occasionally bypass validation impact review. Change-control approval adds lead time but the error is bypassing validation review, not slowness. Assessing validation impact of changes requires specialized judgment. Change control is administrative overhead already budgeted.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of vessel or measurement-instrument use beyond approved qualification or calibration due dates
rows.imp_rat: Using overdue-calibration instruments risks bad data (severity) but is an administrative-control matter; low relevance.
rows.sat_rat: Calibration/qualification scheduling systems with alerts make overdue use fairly well controlled, though lapses still occur under production pressure. Overdue qualification or calibration is an elapsed-time overrun, precisely a duration-control problem. Tracking due dates is procedural rather than skill-intensive. Calibration programs are budgeted, and overruns risk costly rework more than direct spend.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of batch-to-batch variation in agitation intensity under equivalent formulation and loading conditions
rows.imp_rat: Batch-to-batch agitation variation affects consistency (relevance) but individual consequence is modest; below cap.
rows.sat_rat: Modern VFD-controlled agitation delivers highly repeatable intensity under equivalent conditions. Verifying agitation repeatability adds minimal time with instrumented drives. Setting reproducible agitation uses standard control competence. Agitation variation risks inconsistent quality but little direct waste.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of agitation-output drift across repeated compounding cycles under equivalent setpoints
rows.imp_rat: Agitation-output drift across cycles matters for reproducibility, but a single cycle's impact is limited; held low.
rows.sat_rat: Agitation-output drift is well controlled by feedback drives and preventive maintenance, with rare surprises. Detecting agitation drift over cycles adds routine monitoring time. Interpreting drift trends uses standard maintenance skill. Drift can cause off-spec batches but is cheaply corrected.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of variability in cleaning results across formulations and production batches
rows.imp_rat: Cleaning-result variability affects cross-contamination risk (severity) but is only indirectly related to mixing; low relevance.
rows.sat_rat: Bracketing and worst-case approaches manage variability adequately, but multiproduct facilities still see inconsistent results when new or sticky formulations fall outside validated assumptions. Handling formulation-to-formulation variability can lengthen validation campaigns when new worst-cases must be bracketed. Establishing cleaning matrices and worst-case grouping demands significant specialist expertise and judgement that not every site has in depth. Additional matrix testing and revalidation for diverse formulations carries meaningful analytical and labor cost.

rows.stk: Process engineers, system operators, batch technicians.
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required recipe fields left blank before recipe release
rows.imp_rat: Required recipe fields left blank before release is a configuration-completeness gap with modest severity and limited centrality to the actual batching operation.
rows.sat_rat: Most modern recipe systems enforce mandatory-field checks at release, so this need is acceptably served though configuration gaps remain. Checking for blank required fields adds a review pass before release. Knowing which fields are truly required needs recipe-management familiarity but is fairly routine. Blank fields caught at release cost little; caught in production they cost batches.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of removed assemblies, single-use components, or spare parts becoming contaminated during storage
rows.imp_rat: Contamination of stored removed assemblies or single-use spares can compromise product if reintroduced, but this is a storage-handling error tangential to the operating CFJ and constrained below 2.5.
rows.sat_rat: Once a skid is retired, removed assemblies and single-use parts often sit in uncontrolled areas, so contamination during storage is a common and poorly guarded failure. Proper protective packaging and controlled storage add handling time that is often skipped under schedule pressure. Correct bagging, capping and storage-condition control require care but modest skill. Protective packaging, wrapping and controlled storage space add cost that is frequently underfunded for retired parts.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the replacement skid fails to maintain validated conditions after switchover
rows.imp_rat: A replacement skid failing to maintain validated conditions after switchover matters to continuity, but as a contingency-scenario error it ranks below the prior batch and is capped at 2.5.
rows.sat_rat: Change-control and commissioning/qualification practices are mature, yet subtle differences in the replacement skid still occasionally cause post-switchover deviations. Requalification and switchover verification of the replacement skid is a lengthy validation activity. Maintaining validated conditions across switchover requires specialized validation and process engineering expertise. Requalification protocols, testing and potential downtime carry substantial cost.

rows.source_job: Remove the skid from the facility
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of the skid falling during lifting or transport out of the facility
rows.imp_rat: A skid falling during lifting or transport is a physical safety/asset risk with high severity if it occurs, but it is peripheral to the CFJ of running validated bioprocess operations; capped by the batch ceiling at 2.5.
rows.sat_rat: Engineered lift plans, rated rigging and certified operators make dropped-load events rare, though awkward center-of-gravity skids still pose residual risk. Lift planning and rigging setup add time but are a normal part of the schedule. Certified riggers and lift planners are widely available and this is well-established competency. Crane hire and rigging gear are standard, budgeted removal costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of required sterile fill-finish skid capabilities left unassessed during supplier qualification
rows.imp_rat: Unassessed fill-finish capabilities during supplier qualification is an upstream procurement gap with low centrality to operating the skid and moderate downstream severity. Kept low.
rows.sat_rat: Formal supplier qualification and audit checklists are standard practice, but full coverage of all required capabilities is often incomplete. Supplier qualification protocols add structured time to the acquisition but are a routine gated activity. Mapping every required fill-finish capability against supplier claims needs experienced quality auditors who are available but stretched. Unassessed capabilities lead to gaps discovered post-award, driving rework costs, though audit practice mitigates the worst cases.

rows.stk: Engineering, procurement, and project management teams conducting vendor selection.
rows.source_job: Evaluate and benchmark candidate skid options
rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of defined project requirements with undetected gaps in candidate skid configurations
rows.imp_rat: Undetected requirement gaps in candidate configurations is a design/selection-phase defect with low centrality to operating the skid and moderate rework severity. Kept low.
rows.sat_rat: Requirement traceability is often done in spreadsheets with weak rigor, so undetected configuration gaps remain common. Cross-checking requirements against candidate configurations is a slow manual traceability exercise. Detecting undetected requirement gaps in configurations relies on meticulous engineering review that is error-prone and expertise-dependent. Undetected gaps translate to change orders and delays that inflate acquisition cost significantly.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of trained operators unable to perform required startup, shutdown, alarm-response, and cleaning procedures correctly
rows.imp_rat: Operators unable to perform startup, shutdown, alarm-response, and cleaning correctly touches daily operability, but this is a training-competency error ranked below the prior batch; both proxies stay under 2.5.
rows.sat_rat: Documented training with read-and-understand and practical assessments qualifies most operators, but real-world alarm-response and cleaning errors still recur under pressure. Operator training and qualification programs take substantial calendar time before a campaign can start. Competency depends on training quality and assessor judgement, and inconsistent operator proficiency is exactly the residual gap. Training hours and requalification carry recurring labor cost that is planned for.

rows.stk: Metrology, instrumentation, and quality teams executing the installation qualification.
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of skid instruments remaining outside specified calibration tolerance before qualification testing
rows.imp_rat: Instruments out of calibration tolerance before qualification is a pre-test administrative error, generally caught during qualification, so both relevance and severity are modest. Kept low.
rows.sat_rat: Established calibration programs with traceable standards and pre-qualification checks reliably keep instruments in tolerance, making this a well-solved need. Calibration is scheduled ahead of qualification and rarely gates the timeline when managed by a calibration program. Instrument calibration is a well-defined, standardized technician task supported by calibration management systems. Calibration labor and reference-standard costs are routine and predictable with no significant waste.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of trainee action driving skid parameters outside safe limits during supervised operation
rows.imp_rat: Trainee action pushing parameters outside safe limits during supervised operation carries safety risk, but supervision mitigates it and it is peripheral to the operational CFJ; capped at 2.5.
rows.sat_rat: Modern skids typically embed hardware interlocks, alarm limits and automated setpoint clamps that reliably stop most trainee-induced excursions before they reach unsafe limits, giving mature engineered protection layered under human oversight. Supervised training slows operation because a qualified operator must shadow the trainee in real time, but this is accepted overhead rather than a bottleneck for the need itself. The status quo leans heavily on the presence and vigilance of a skilled supervisor plus SOP checklists to catch a trainee before parameters drift, so the need is still fundamentally answered by scarce human attention rather than by the system preventing the error. Requiring a second experienced person to supervise every trainee session adds real labor cost, though it is a routine and budgeted part of onboarding.

rows.stk: Procurement managers, legal/commercial teams, capital project managers.
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of conflicts between the contract terms and sterile fill-finish compliance obligations
rows.imp_rat: Conflicts between contract terms and sterile fill-finish compliance obligations are a contractual/administrative error peripheral to plant processing; severity relates to compliance exposure but relevance to the core job is low.
rows.sat_rat: Standard contract review catches obvious conflicts, yet technical-compliance clauses are frequently misaligned with contract language, leaving recurring conflicts. Reconciling contract terms with sterile compliance obligations is a slow legal-technical cross-check. It requires combined legal and sterile-compliance expertise to spot conflicts, a rare pairing on acquisition teams. Legal review adds cost but is a normal line item in major plant contracts.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of instruments calibrated using incorrect range or tolerance requirements
rows.imp_rat: Instruments calibrated to wrong range or tolerance can misreport process values, moderately relevant but typically correctable, with contained severity; kept low under the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Documented calibration procedures and certificates are well established, so incorrect range/tolerance use is uncommon where procedures are followed. Calibration against documented ranges is a routine, planned bench-and-field activity. Selecting the correct range and tolerance depends on the technician reading the datasheet and calibration procedure correctly. Calibration equipment and time are standard instrumentation costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of scheduled production disruption caused by instruments remaining unavailable for calibration
rows.imp_rat: Instruments unavailable for calibration delays scheduled production, but the disruption is planned and recoverable; relevance to ongoing processing is modest and severity is low as no product or safety loss occurs.
rows.sat_rat: Pre-planned calibration scheduling and pre-calibrated instrument delivery reduce disruption, yet last-minute availability gaps still recur. Instruments waiting for calibration commonly hold up commissioning when calibration capacity or lab turnaround lags. Calibration itself is routine metrology work, but scheduling it against a moving commissioning plan needs coordination skill. Idle commissioning teams waiting on instruments are expensive, driving investment in on-site calibration.

rows.stmt: Minimize the time required for an operator to reach an emergency stop from a hazardous work position
rows.imp_rat: Time for an operator to reach an emergency stop matters for safety severity, but reachability is a peripheral ergonomic/layout detail relative to the core processing job (low relevance). Ranking caps it at 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Standards-driven e-stop layout reliably keeps stops within reach, though awkward maintenance positions can still leave gaps. E-stop placement rules explicitly minimize reach time from hazardous positions and are enforced by machinery safety standards. Positioning e-stops per ISO/EN reach requirements is codified and needs only standard safety-engineering competence. Additional e-stop devices are inexpensive relative to the harm they prevent.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of plant control devices connected to an incorrect designated network segment
rows.imp_rat: Control devices on an incorrect network segment can disrupt monitoring (severity), but it is a discrete commissioning error easily identified and remedied, and peripheral to the processing job (relevance).
rows.sat_rat: Network architecture drawings and address plans guide assignment, but incorrect segment connections still occur and are not always immediately obvious. Network segment verification is part of commissioning checks with defined effort. Assigning devices to correct segments requires networking-aware controls engineers. Reconfiguring a mis-segmented device is a low-material, labour-only fix.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the prepared floor area cannot support the equipment load during positioning
