rows.imp_rat: A floor area unable to support equipment load during positioning is a one-time installation risk (low relevance to running the plant) with potentially serious structural/safety consequences (moderate severity). Capped at 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Engineered floor-load calculations and structural certification make load failures during positioning quite rare. Floor-load verification is completed in the civil design phase and rarely delays positioning. Structural load assessment is standard civil engineering with well-established methods and codes. Floor failure under load is catastrophic, so load certification is routinely and cheaply obtained relative to the risk.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the electrical technician contacts an energized conductor while making plant power connections
rows.imp_rat: A technician contacting an energized conductor is a serious safety event (severity would be high) but the electrical-connection task is peripheral to the plant's core processing job (very low relevance). Ranking places it below prior batch, so capped at 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Established LOTO and verification-of-de-energization practices make energized contact rare, though human lapses still cause incidents. Lockout-tagout and permit-to-work procedures add time to connection tasks but are firmly embedded in the schedule. Safe isolation depends on trained, certified electricians consistently applying LOTO discipline, which remains the primary safeguard. The procedural controls cost little relative to the injury and liability they prevent.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a utility line is connected to the wrong plant interface during installation
rows.imp_rat: Connecting a utility line to the wrong interface is an installation-time misconnection (low relevance to steady-state job) with detectable, correctable consequences (moderate-low severity). Held at the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Clear tagging, color-coding and connection sign-off practice makes wrong-interface connections fairly uncommon, though ambiguous labeling still causes occasional misconnections. Tagging, labeling and verifying each utility connection point adds routine checking time. Following labeled P&IDs and tie-in schedules is straightforward for trained fitters and needs little specialist judgement. Labels and check sheets are cheap versus the rework of an incorrectly connected utility.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of leakage from a completed plant utility connection
rows.imp_rat: Leakage from a completed utility connection is a commissioning-quality error with modest relevance to the core processing job and typically low-severity consequences (containable leaks). Kept below 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Hydrostatic and pressure-decay testing reliably finds most utility leaks before service, making post-test leakage relatively uncommon. Pressure and leak testing every completed utility connection is time-consuming but routinely scheduled. Making and testing leak-tight utility joints is well-established fitter craft with clear acceptance criteria. Gaskets and test media are cheap relative to leak-driven downtime, and testing avoids larger losses.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a facility utility tie-in is incompatible with its corresponding plant interface at installation
rows.imp_rat: Utility tie-in incompatibility at installation is a one-time commissioning delay (low relevance to ongoing processing) with reworkable consequences (moderate-low severity). Kept below 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Drawing reviews and interface schedules catch most mismatches, but as-built deviations and spec ambiguities still produce recurring incompatibility surprises at installation. Verifying tie-in compatibility against drawings before connection adds checking time up front. Matching interface specs relies on engineering review that competent teams perform but that is error-prone under schedule pressure. An incompatible tie-in discovered late forces costly adapters, rework or reordering.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of instrument wiring terminations that do not match the instrument loop diagrams
rows.imp_rat: Instrument wiring terminations not matching loop diagrams are a commissioning defect that is largely caught during testing, so relevance and severity are modest; scored low.
rows.sat_rat: Point-to-point loop checks against approved loop diagrams are mature practice, so mismatched terminations are usually caught, though occasional errors persist. Loop checking against wiring diagrams is a scheduled, well-bounded task. Verifying terminations against loop diagrams is a documented technician task requiring care more than scarce skill. Labour for termination verification is a standard, predictable commissioning cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of instruments mounted at process locations or orientations inconsistent with the plant design
rows.imp_rat: Instruments mounted at inconsistent locations or orientations can degrade measurement, a design-conformance defect with modest relevance and severity; scored low under the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Installation details and hookup drawings are standard, so gross misorientation is usually avoided, though subtle inconsistencies still slip through. Verifying location and orientation against P&IDs and installation details is a bounded install-check task. Correct orientation for meters and transmitters requires knowledgeable installers who understand process measurement basics. Re-mounting a misplaced instrument is a modest but avoidable cost.

rows.stk: Quality managers, regulatory affairs teams, procurement engineers
rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of certifications whose scope excludes the intended sterile fill-finish application or jurisdiction
rows.imp_rat: Certifications whose scope excludes the intended application/jurisdiction is a compliance-fit defect with real severity if missed, but it is a verification gap peripheral to daily operation. Capped at 2.5.
rows.sat_rat: Certification bodies and standardized certificate formats make scope verification fairly reliable, so mismatches are caught more often than not. Checking certification scope against the intended application is a fairly quick document review. Regulatory affairs staff routinely read certificate scope, though jurisdictional nuances still require specialist reading. A scope mismatch discovered late forces costly recertification, but the check itself is cheap.

rows.stk: Process engineers, product development teams, procurement managers
rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which trial batch size and operating conditions differ from intended sterile fill-finish production
rows.imp_rat: Trial batch size/conditions differing from intended production introduces scale-up variability, relevant to qualification but a preparatory-phase error with recoverable consequences. Capped at 2.5.
rows.sat_rat: Trial batches almost always differ in size and conditions from real sterile fill-finish production, so trial success poorly predicts operational outcome. Arranging trial batches that mirror full production conditions adds significant lead time before acquisition can close. Designing representative trials for viscous aseptic product demands scarce process-development expertise. Full-scale representative trials are expensive, so purchasers accept scaled-down proxies that mislead.

rows.source_job: Position plant modules on prepared foundations
rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of deviation between the final module position and the approved installation layout
rows.imp_rat: Module position deviating from the approved layout is largely an installation/aesthetic-fit issue (low relevance) with modest operational consequences (low-moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: As-built survey verification against approved installation layouts is standard practice, so final module position deviations are typically small and reliably corrected. Module setting against approved layouts using survey control is a well-paced, planned lift-and-position operation. Rigging and precise positioning need trained crews, but marked benchmarks and survey tools reduce the intuition required. Repositioning a mis-set module is expensive, but survey-guided placement usually avoids that cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of documented plant components whose configuration differs from the physical installation
rows.imp_rat: Documented configuration diverging from physical installation is a documentation-accuracy defect; relevant to traceability but consequences are usually correctable and secondary to processing. Capped at 2.5.
rows.sat_rat: As-built and configuration records routinely diverge from the physical plant because updating documentation lags installation changes, making this a chronic, poorly served gap. Reconciling documentation with the physical installation is a slow, manual walk-down often deferred under schedule pressure. Detecting configuration mismatches requires diligent engineers cross-checking drawings against reality, a tedious task prone to oversight. Documentation drift is cheap to ignore now but costly later in maintenance and modification errors.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of residual viscous product left inside the line to solidify after shutdown
rows.imp_rat: Residual product solidifying after shutdown poses contamination and cleaning risk, giving some severity, but its ranking below the prior batch caps its importance here. Capped at 2.5.
rows.sat_rat: Unexpected shutdowns, trapped pockets and inadequate heat tracing make blockages a frequent and unpredictable failure mode, so the outcome remains inconsistent despite known workarounds. Flush and blow-out procedures at shutdown take time and are often rushed or skipped under production pressure, leaving material to set before the line can be fully cleared. Avoiding solidification depends heavily on operators knowing every dead leg, low point and cold spot and diligently purging or heat-tracing them, making it error-prone and highly attention-dependent. Solidified residue drives expensive teardown cleaning, solvent use, scrap and sometimes replaced piping, a costly recurring consequence that current practice only partly prevents.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of processed viscous product left in the plant after discharge
rows.imp_rat: Processed product left after discharge is a yield/waste loss that matters modestly for efficiency but has low-severity, recoverable consequences relative to the CFJ. Capped at 2.5.
rows.sat_rat: Recovery amounts vary batch-to-batch with viscosity, temperature and geometry, so the fraction left behind is only partly controllable and remains a recurring inconsistency. Established discharge practices like scraped-surface heaters, positive-displacement pumps and pigging clear most product within routine batch timing, though final drain-down still adds delay when material clings. Operators rely on learned judgement to know when a vessel or line is 'empty enough,' and complete recovery of the last cling film demands practiced technique rather than a foolproof procedure. Residual high-value viscous product left after discharge is a direct recurring yield loss that pigging and dead-leg minimization mitigate substantially but never eliminate, so real product is still routinely wasted.

rows.source_job: Adjust processing sequence for alternate formulation
rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which active sequence parameters deviate from approved values for the alternate formulation
rows.imp_rat: Sequence parameters deviating from approved values for an alternate formulation could affect quality, but this batch's ranking places it below higher-priority errors; kept at the ceiling. Capped at 2.5.
rows.sat_rat: Approved recipe versioning keeps active parameters aligned in managed systems, but manual edits and unmanaged alternates still allow occasional divergence. Recipe recall for an alternate formulation loads approved sequence parameters quickly without manual reconstruction. Version-controlled recipe management reduces the expertise needed to confirm the alternate formulation's parameters are correct. Deviating sequence parameters can spoil the alternate-formulation batch, a cost that recipe control largely prevents.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required preventive maintenance activities omitted from the plant schedule
rows.imp_rat: Preventive maintenance activities omitted from the schedule can erode reliability over time, but it is a planning-completeness defect ranked below the more critical batch above. Capped at 2.5.
rows.sat_rat: Widely deployed CMMS scheduling makes omitted preventive activities fairly uncommon, though backlog pressure still causes some deferrals. CMMS scheduling generates due-lists automatically, saving planning time. Executing scheduled PM needs routine competence rather than scarce expertise once the plan exists. Maintenance software and planner labor cost is modest against the value of not missing PMs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that backed-up configurations cannot be restored to their intended control assets
rows.imp_rat: Backed-up configurations that cannot be restored to control assets risks recovery failure, giving moderate severity, but it is a contingency-integrity concern ranked below the batch above. Capped at 2.5.
rows.sat_rat: Version-controlled backup regimes usually restore cleanly, but untested backups and version mismatches occasionally defeat restoration. Restoring a validated backup is fast when the backup and target asset are correctly matched. Ensuring a backup maps to its intended controller relies on controls-engineering discipline and version tracking. Backup infrastructure is low cost relative to the loss from an unrestorable configuration.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of critical component surfaces left uninspected because they are inaccessible or obscured
rows.imp_rat: Inaccessible surfaces left uninspected can hide defects, giving some relevance and severity, but as an inspection-coverage gap it sits below the batch above in importance. Capped at 2.5.
rows.sat_rat: Many critical surfaces stay uninspected because access is genuinely hard, leaving a chronic blind spot in condition assessment. Inspecting obscured surfaces often requires time-consuming disassembly or borescope setup. Reaching and correctly judging inaccessible surfaces demands experienced inspectors and specialized technique. Disassembly and inspection tooling for hidden areas add cost that tempts skipping them.

rows.source_job: Replace worn or consumed plant components
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of component replacements requiring reopening because of incorrect fit, alignment, or sealing
rows.imp_rat: Reopening component replacements for fit/alignment/sealing errors causes rework delays, but it is a maintenance-execution inefficiency with modest consequences relative to core production. Capped at 2.5.
rows.sat_rat: Procedures and torque specs help, but first-time-right assembly still varies with individual skill so reopening remains a frequent occurrence. Reopening for incorrect fit or alignment doubles the assembly time for that component. Correct fit, alignment and sealing on first assembly relies directly on technician craftsmanship and torque discipline. Rework consumes extra labor, gaskets and downtime.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that sensitive operational data remains recoverable after sanitization
rows.imp_rat: Recoverable sensitive data after sanitization is a data-security concern peripheral to running a viscous processing plant; severity is real for compliance but relevance to the CFJ is low. Capped at 2.5.
rows.sat_rat: Where a formal data-destruction policy applies erasure is verifiable, but on process plants recoverable data on legacy controllers and drives is often overlooked. Data sanitization of control systems and drives is often deferred and hurried at end of project. Secure erasure or destruction of storage media needs IT/OT security knowledge not always present in a demolition crew. Certified data destruction services add cost that is sometimes skipped to save money.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of tank acceptance criteria left unresolved in the final contract
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved acceptance criteria in the contract (ambiguity) is a peripheral administrative concern to the tank's core processing job; consequences are contractual friction rather than functional failure, but capped at 2.5 by batch ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Contracts often carry ambiguous or missing acceptance language because templates lag actual process needs, leaving recurring gaps at handover. Resolving open acceptance criteria drags out contract finalization when legal and engineering must iterate on wording. Drafting testable acceptance criteria for a sterile tank requires scarce combined process-engineering and contracting judgement. Unresolved criteria surface as disputes and warranty claims later, converting into avoidable cost during commissioning.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of discrepancies between the purchase order and the approved tank configuration
rows.imp_rat: Discrepancies between purchase order and approved configuration is a procurement/documentation error (low relevance to the operating job) with moderate downstream consequences, staying well under the cap.
rows.sat_rat: Standard procurement practice uses configuration sign-off and PO review checklists that catch most mismatches, though line-item errors still slip through on complex tank specs. PO reconciliation adds review cycles but rarely stalls acquisition for long since it happens in parallel with vendor negotiation. Catching PO-vs-configuration mismatches depends on an experienced buyer or engineer cross-checking documents, and that expertise is not always available. Discrepancies caught late can trigger change orders and rework charges, so the cost exposure is real but usually contained by pre-issue review.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unresolved supplier quality-system gaps against sterile fill-finish requirements
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved supplier quality-system gaps against sterile requirements carry compliance risk, but they are a supplier-qualification concern removed from the tank's active job and constrained to 2.5.
rows.sat_rat: Formal supplier qualification exists but coverage against sterile fill-finish specifics is uneven, so unresolved gaps regularly persist into award. Supplier quality audits and gap assessments are time-consuming and often bottleneck qualification before a PO can issue. Auditing a fabricator's quality system against fill-finish requirements needs trained quality auditors who are in short supply. Undetected quality-system gaps produce nonconforming tanks and costly requalification, a significant downstream expense.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of sterile-service requirements not satisfied in approved fabrication drawings
rows.imp_rat: Sterile-service requirements missing from approved drawings is a documentation-stage defect with downstream consequences, but it is upstream of active processing and constrained to the 2.5 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Drawing reviews against sterile requirements are inconsistent and checklist-dependent, so omissions in approved drawings remain a common failure. Verifying sterile-service features in fabrication drawings requires added drawing-review loops that extend the pre-fabrication window. It takes specialized hygienic-design expertise (drainability, crevice-free welds, surface finish) that few reviewers on a buying team truly possess. Missed sterile requirements caught after fabrication force expensive re-fabrication or field rework.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that transit damage remains undetected on received tank surfaces
rows.imp_rat: Undetected transit damage on received tank surfaces (unreliability) has moderate severity but is a receiving-inspection concern rather than a core processing driver; capped by the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Visual receiving inspection catches obvious damage but concealed or internal-surface damage frequently goes undetected until later. Receiving inspection adds time on delivery but is usually brief. Detecting subtle transit damage requires trained inspectors and sometimes NDE, expertise not always present at receiving. Missed damage leads to costly late discovery and warranty disputes.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of deviation between installed accessory positions and tank specifications
rows.imp_rat: Deviation of installed accessory positions from spec is a fabrication/installation defect with moderate rework consequences, but it is not central to the core processing job and is capped by the batch-4 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Drawing-based installation checks catch most deviations but tolerance interpretation and field fit-up still produce recurring minor mismatches. Verifying accessory positions is a straightforward walkdown of limited duration. Fitters using drawings can locate accessories with modest skill requirements. Minor mispositioning causes low-cost rework in most cases.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of trained operators unable to perform safe tank operation and cleaning procedures
rows.imp_rat: Operators unable to perform safe operation and cleaning is a training defect with some safety relevance, but for this batch it is ranked lower and kept at or under the 2.5 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Structured training and competency sign-offs exist but assessment rigor is inconsistent, leaving some operators unable to perform procedures safely. Operator training programs take significant lead time to develop and deliver. The need is fundamentally about building operator competence, and training quality and retention vary widely by site. Training consumes instructor and operator time but is a normal budgeted cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time between a tank alarm and the start of operator investigation
rows.imp_rat: A longer delay between a tank alarm and operator investigation is only moderately relevant to routine processing and its consequences vary; ranked below the prior batch, it is held at or under 2.5.
rows.sat_rat: Alarm delivery is generally dependable, but alarm flooding, acknowledgment fatigue and understaffed night shifts still occasionally stretch the time to first action. Modern SCADA/DCS alarms annunciate instantly and route to HMIs, radios and mobile alerts, sharply cutting the delay before an operator begins investigating. Prioritized, rationalized alarm systems reduce the interpretation skill needed, though nuisance-alarm floods can still confuse less experienced staff. Alarm and notification infrastructure is already standard on most process tanks, so no meaningful incremental cost blocks fast response.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percent of the tank-port cross-section obstructed after flushing
rows.imp_rat: Tank-port cross-section obstruction after flushing can affect flow, giving some relevance, but this item ranks below the prior batch and is held so the mean stays at or under 2.5.
rows.sat_rat: Standard flushing procedures exist but verification of residual obstruction is frequently visual and inconsistent, so partial blockage recurs and is a real recurring gap. Flushing cycles and inspection to confirm ports are clear can be time-consuming, often requiring repeat passes. Judging whether a port is adequately cleared often relies on operator experience rather than a hard measurable spec. Flushing consumes water, solvents and disposal capacity, and inefficient cycles waste those resources.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of external tank cleaning introducing contaminants into the sterile fill-finish area
rows.imp_rat: Contaminants introduced into a sterile fill-finish area during external cleaning can be severe, but this item sits below the prior batch in importance ordering; both proxies are held so the mean does not exceed 2.5.
rows.sat_rat: Barriers and procedures help, but external cleaning near classified areas remains a recurring contamination-ingress risk. Controlled external cleaning near sterile zones requires careful sequencing that adds time. Preventing cross-contamination into fill-finish areas depends on trained personnel following gowning and zoning discipline. Containment, controlled cleaning agents and area protection add cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of commissioning results invalidated by missing or expired calibration records
rows.imp_rat: Commissioning results invalidated by missing calibration records force rework of qualification, a documentation-driven error peripheral to steady-state processing with mainly schedule and cost consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Calibration management systems reliably flag missing or expired records, though manual paper trails at some sites still cause invalidations. Checking calibration records is fast when a calibration management system tracks due dates automatically. Record verification is administrative and needs little specialist skill once a tracking system exists. Invalidated commissioning results force costly rework, so calibration record management is standard investment.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of finished-liquid output interruption caused by unavailable downstream receiving capacity
rows.imp_rat: Output interruption from unavailable downstream receiving capacity is an external logistics constraint; peripheral to the plant's processing job with low severity as production simply pauses.
rows.sat_rat: Buffer/surge capacity smooths many mismatches but interruptions recur when downstream storage or logistics fill up. When downstream receiving capacity is unavailable, output interruption lasts as long as the bottleneck, and buffering only delays it. Managing surge tanks and diverting flow to avoid interruption relies on operator anticipation. Interruptions waste production capacity and can force product to be held or downgraded.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of production-start delay caused by unavailable or unreleased batch inputs
rows.imp_rat: Production-start delay from unreleased batch inputs is a supply-readiness error peripheral to the plant's processing function; severity is low as it only postpones start with no quality impact.
rows.sat_rat: ERP/MES release workflows make input availability more predictable, yet late QA release and supply gaps still routinely delay starts. Production-start delays from unreleased batch inputs are common and last until QA release or material arrival. Coordinating input readiness spans QA, warehouse and planning, requiring cross-functional attention rather than deep technical skill. Idle plant while waiting on inputs wastes labor and capacity but not material.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of residual liquid accumulation while its designated handling path is unavailable
rows.imp_rat: Residual liquid accumulation while its handling path is unavailable is a transient delay with modest centrality and recoverable consequences; kept low under the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Effluent and recovery systems handle normal loads but back up unpredictably when the designated path is down or full. When the residual handling path is unavailable, residual liquid accumulates until capacity frees up, and this wait is often uncontrolled. Deciding where to route accumulating residuals under pressure requires operator judgement. Accumulated residuals may need disposal or reprocessing, incurring waste and utility cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of sequencing mismatch between dependent concurrent streams
rows.imp_rat: Sequencing mismatch between concurrent streams can cause coordination faults, but it is a tunable control error with limited severity, mostly producing throughput inefficiency.
rows.sat_rat: Batch management systems enforce some sequencing but cross-stream dependencies still drift out of step under real conditions. Sequencing mismatch between dependent streams introduces waiting and rework that ripples through the schedule. Keeping dependent concurrent streams in step is a demanding coordination task that leans on planner and operator skill. Mismatched sequencing can spoil intermediates or force holding, wasting product and utilities.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of unavailable or stale process-variable readings while setpoint adjustment is required
rows.imp_rat: Stale or unavailable process-variable readings during setpoint adjustment impede control (moderate severity if setpoints drift) but is a transient instrumentation issue peripheral to the core job (low relevance). Held to 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Sensor dropout, comms faults and slow analyzers still intermittently deliver stale values at exactly the moment adjustment is needed. Modern DCS/SCADA polls process variables at high frequency so stale readings during setpoint adjustment are usually brief. Recognizing and compensating for a frozen or lagging reading during a manual adjustment still requires operator experience. Redundant instrumentation and communication buses that reduce staleness add capital cost but are standard in liquid plants.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of the specified process envelope left unevaluated for each candidate plant
rows.imp_rat: Leaving part of the process envelope unevaluated for candidate plants is relevant to selection due diligence, but it is an assessment-phase gap that ranks below core operational errors; consequences are recoverable through later evaluation. Capped at 2.5.
rows.sat_rat: Standard practice evaluates only nominal operating points, leaving large portions of the envelope unverified and outcomes uncertain. Evaluating the full process envelope for each candidate plant is time-intensive and usually truncated under acquisition deadlines. Mapping and probing the entire envelope demands deep process expertise, so purchasers cover only familiar operating points. Unevaluated envelope regions become expensive surprises during commissioning.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of installing a consumable element with an incorrect material, size, or rating
rows.imp_rat: Installing a consumable with wrong material, size, or rating is a defect that can degrade performance, but for this batch it ranks lower and both proxies are kept so the mean stays at 2.5 or below.
rows.sat_rat: BOM controls and spec labeling reduce mismatches, but manual selection still allows occasional incorrect elements. Verifying consumable material/size/rating is a brief check when parts are staged correctly. Correct part selection relies on technician attention and readable specs, an error-prone manual step. Wrong-part installs are cheap individually but costly if they cause failure or rework.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of tank fasteners remaining outside specified torque after tightening
rows.imp_rat: Fasteners left outside specified torque is a mechanical defect that could affect integrity, but it ranks below the prior batch and both proxies are held so the mean does not exceed 2.5.
rows.sat_rat: Calibrated torque tools and documented torque values are mature, widely deployed practice that keeps out-of-spec fasteners uncommon, though tool drift and human skips still cause occasional misses. Calibrated torque wrenches make tightening reasonably quick, though checking every fastener on a large tank adds appreciable time. Torque-to-spec is a well-documented routine that most maintenance technicians can execute with modest training using standard tools. Torque wrenches and periodic calibration are inexpensive relative to the cost of a leaking joint failure.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the return channel refuses the tank because contamination status is unverified
rows.imp_rat: A return-channel refusal over unverified contamination status is a logistics/reverse-flow error, peripheral to the tank doing its processing job, and held under 2.5 per ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Cleanliness certificates are standard practice, but mismatched or unverified documentation still causes receivers to reject tanks at the gate. Verification paperwork must clear before the tank ships, adding coordination delay. Producing acceptable contamination verification needs someone who understands the receiver's acceptance criteria. A refused tank means wasted transport and rescheduling costs.

rows.source_job: Obtain regulatory approval for tank disposal
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that tank disposal begins before required regulatory approval
rows.imp_rat: Disposal before regulatory approval is an end-of-life compliance error with real regulatory consequences, but it is remote from the tank's active processing job and ranked below the prior batch, so it stays at the 2.5 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Permit-tracking and hold-point procedures usually catch premature starts, though informal disposals occasionally jump the gate. Waiting on regulatory approval is a known scheduling gate that adds delay. Navigating permits requires compliance expertise but is a defined administrative competency. Permit fees and hold time carry cost, but proceeding early risks far larger fines.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of active sterile fill-finish production disrupted by disconnecting shared services
rows.imp_rat: Disrupting active fill-finish by disconnecting shared services has moderate severity, but this scheduling/coordination delay is peripheral to executing the batching job and capped by ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Lockout/isolation procedures reliably prevent cross-disruption when followed, though unexpected shared dependencies still surface. Disconnecting shared services during active production is the direct source of disruptive downtime that scheduling only partly contains. Planning safe isolation of shared utilities requires experienced facilities engineers. Lost sterile production hours during disconnection are costly but budgeted for planned shutdowns.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which residue or inaccessible surfaces obscure the physical condition of product-contact components
rows.imp_rat: Residue obscuring the condition of product-contact components hinders inspection with moderate severity, but this visibility error is peripheral to executing the batching job and ranked low.
rows.sat_rat: Residue and inaccessible geometry routinely hide true component condition, and no standard end-of-life inspection reliably overcomes this. Inspecting obscured product-contact surfaces requires slow disassembly and cleaning before condition can be judged. Interpreting residue-obscured surfaces for disposal decisions demands experienced inspectors. Extra teardown and borescope inspection to expose surfaces adds labor and tooling cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that updated control software fails required compliance validation
rows.imp_rat: Control software failing compliance validation after an update (item 7) is an administrative/rework concern with low relevance to producing a batch and modest severity since it is caught before use. Held low.
rows.sat_rat: Established CSV and change-control frameworks make compliance failure at release uncommon when procedures are followed correctly. Compliance validation cycles are long and formalized but scheduled deliberately into revalidation plans. GMP/CSV validation specialists are required to interpret regulatory requirements, a well-defined but expert-dependent role. Documentation and validation labor is costly yet a standard line item in regulated maintenance budgets.

rows.stk: Instrumentation technicians, maintenance engineers, quality personnel.
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the required reference standard is outside its certification validity period when calibration begins
rows.imp_rat: An expired reference standard at calibration start is an administrative traceability lapse; it is peripheral to running a batch and easily caught, so both proxies are low.
rows.sat_rat: Calibration management systems that flag expired standards make using an out-of-cert reference rare where such systems are deployed. Checking standard certification status is a quick pre-calibration verification step. Verifying certification validity is a simple procedural check requiring little specialized skill. Recertifying standards is a routine, budgeted expense with no surprise cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of leaving pump or agitator components misaligned or improperly secured after reassembly
rows.imp_rat: Misaligned or improperly secured pump/agitator components after reassembly (item 14) is a service-quality defect with low relevance to routine batching and moderate mechanical consequences. Under the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Laser alignment tools and checklists help, but misalignment or under-torqued components remain a frequent latent defect after reassembly. Alignment and securing checks add time but are part of standard reassembly procedures. Precise shaft/coupling alignment and correct fastener securing depend on experienced technicians and can be error-prone under pressure. Misalignment causes premature bearing/seal wear and repeat repairs, driving avoidable cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of serviced pumps, valves, and connections that leak after reassembly
rows.imp_rat: Leaks after servicing pumps, valves, and connections (item 9) are a maintenance-quality issue with low relevance to the batching job itself and modest, typically contained consequences. Kept low.
rows.sat_rat: Post-service pressure/leak tests catch many leaks, but reassembly leaks remain a common recurring finding requiring rework. Reassembly and subsequent leak testing extends downtime, though pressure-test procedures are routine. Correct torqueing, gasket seating, and connection makeup rely heavily on technician craftsmanship and attention. Rework from post-assembly leaks wastes fluid, labor, and repeat testing time.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of drainage stoppages caused by blocked or inaccessible flow paths
rows.imp_rat: Drainage stoppages from blocked flow paths are a localized delay with limited centrality to the core job and modest severity; scored low under the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Rodding, flushing and access planning resolve most blockages, though old plant with fouled lines still stalls drainage. Blocked or inaccessible flow paths halt drainage until cleared, and the wait is the primary loss. Clearing blockages needs practical troubleshooting rather than rare expertise. Stoppages extend labor hours and equipment rental cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of hazardous waste shipments rejected because shipment records are incomplete or inconsistent
rows.imp_rat: Rejected hazardous waste shipments due to incomplete records are an administrative/compliance error peripheral to the plant's core liquid-processing job (low relevance); consequences are logistical delays and re-documentation rather than product harm (moderate-low severity). Capped at 3.0 per batch ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Electronic manifest systems and pre-shipment checks have made record-driven rejections fairly infrequent though not eliminated. Incomplete manifests cause shipment holds and rescheduling. Preparing consistent shipping records needs trained waste coordinators but is a routine clerical competence. Rejected shipments incur demurrage and repeat handling costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of purge and cleaning delays caused by unavailable isolation, drainage, or utility access
rows.imp_rat: Purge and cleaning delays from unavailable isolation/utility access reduce availability but are a schedule inconvenience with limited severity; scored low.
rows.sat_rat: Permit-to-work and isolation planning cover the norm, but late or unavailable access still delays purge and cleaning. Cleaning waits on isolation, drainage or utility access, and that idle time is the loss. Arranging isolations and utilities is a planning task not requiring scarce skill. Delays keep crews and rented cleaning equipment idle at cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of recoverable plant materials rejected because of residual product or hazardous contamination
rows.imp_rat: Recoverable materials rejected due to residual product or hazardous contamination has moderate environmental/cost severity but low centrality to the processing job; kept low.
rows.sat_rat: Decontamination for recovery is inconsistent, so usable materials are often downgraded to waste despite marginal contamination. Deciding whether contaminated materials can be recovered adds handling time. Judging recoverability of residue-bearing materials needs specialist assessment. Recoverable materials rejected for contamination lose recycling value and add disposal cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of onsite storage while segregated materials await acceptance or collection
rows.imp_rat: Onsite storage delay for segregated materials awaiting acceptance is peripheral logistics rather than core liquid processing (low relevance); consequences are modest tie-up of space and minor scheduling friction (low severity). Capped at 3.0 per batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Storage itself is stable and controlled; the issue is duration rather than outcome failure. Segregated materials often sit awaiting collection because acceptance and haulier scheduling are slow. Managing storage logistics needs coordination skill but no rare expertise. Prolonged onsite storage ties up space and can incur interim storage compliance cost.

rows.stk: Plant managers, project engineers, compliance officers.
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that residual process material or contamination is omitted from the final plant-condition record before handover
rows.imp_rat: Omitting residual contamination from the plant-condition record is a documentation gap with possible downstream contamination consequences (severity), but recording it is peripheral to the plant actually processing liquid (relevance). Kept under 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Residual pockets are routinely missed or under-recorded, making omissions from the final record a common and hard-to-catch failure. Building a complete residual-material record requires walkdowns of every vessel and line, which is time-consuming and frequently truncated near handover. Recognizing where residuals hide in dead-legs, sumps and jackets takes experienced process operators whose knowledge is often already gone at disposal. Thorough residual surveys and sampling cost money that disposal budgets under-fund, encouraging omission.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of conflicting operator actions altering the same batch simultaneously
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting operator actions on the same batch can cause instability, but concurrent-edit control is a peripheral coordination error of limited central relevance, ranked low.
rows.sat_rat: Ownership/locking and access controls exist in many systems to prevent concurrent edits, but coordination gaps in multi-operator settings still let conflicting actions through. Conflicting simultaneous actions can cause rework or delays, but resolving them is usually quick once detected. Avoiding conflicts relies on operators coordinating verbally and following shift protocols, a coordination skill rather than a technical one. Conflicting changes to one batch can corrupt it and force disposition, an occasional but meaningful cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required process readings unavailable during endpoint verification
rows.imp_rat: Unavailable process readings during endpoint verification hamper confirmation, but missing data usually triggers a hold rather than causing harm, so both proxies stay low.
rows.sat_rat: Standard instrumentation covers most required readings, but sensor outages, fouling and gaps in coverage still leave some endpoint parameters unmeasured. Missing readings force waiting for manual samples, adding delay to endpoint verification. Working around an unavailable reading requires operator inference and experience. Backup lab analysis or extra sensors add moderate cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration that process readings lag behind the batch's actual condition during endpoint verification
rows.imp_rat: Reading lag behind actual condition during endpoint verification can slightly misjudge completion, but this is a narrow timing issue with limited stakes for the core job.
rows.sat_rat: Inline sensing largely eliminates lag where installed, but many endpoints still rely on delayed lab confirmation that lags actual condition. The core error is measurement lag itself, and inline sensors keep it short while lab methods stretch it to minutes or longer. Compensating mentally for known lag is an experience-based skill. Faster inline analytics cost more but reduce off-spec at endpoint.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of exceeding the approved hold limit while awaiting intermediate vessel availability
rows.imp_rat: Exceeding hold limit while awaiting vessel availability (item 19) is a scheduling/queueing delay with low relevance to the batching process and modest, recoverable consequences. Kept low.
rows.sat_rat: Vessel-availability bottlenecks are a chronic, poorly buffered constraint, so hold-limit breaches while waiting remain common. The error is waiting time for a vessel exceeding the hold limit, driven by scheduling and equipment availability that plants struggle to compress. Avoiding the breach depends on schedulers and operators coordinating vessel turnover, a moderate planning skill. Batches held too long awaiting a vessel get downgraded or scrapped, and idle equipment adds cost, making this a recurring loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of interrupted agitation while the ingredient-addition sequence is active
rows.imp_rat: Interrupted agitation during ingredient addition can cause mixing inhomogeneity, but its transient nature and modest severity keep it peripheral to the batching CFJ.
rows.sat_rat: Agitator-run interlocks tied to addition phases are common and reliable, though shorter unplanned agitation gaps still occur during upsets. The error is the duration agitation stays off during active addition, and automated interlocks that keep agitators running minimize that downtime well. Agitation-during-addition is usually enforced by interlock logic rather than operator attention, reducing skill dependence. Prolonged loss of agitation risks poor mixing and off-spec product, but interlocks keep such interruptions short and infrequent.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that verification results are biased by out-of-tolerance measurement references
rows.imp_rat: Verification bias from out-of-tolerance references (item 5) is a metrology-support concern with low relevance to executing the batch and moderate, correctable consequences. Well under the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Documented calibration and traceable references make out-of-tolerance reference bias uncommon, though drift between calibration intervals is a residual risk. Scheduled calibration is routine and does not disrupt normal batching time. Calibration programs offload the need for operators to judge reference accuracy. Calibration labor and standards are a predictable recurring cost far below the cost of biased releases.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of identifying the incorrect source of a batching-system fault
rows.imp_rat: Identifying the wrong source of a fault delays correct remediation, but fault diagnosis is a support activity of limited central relevance with moderate severity, ranked low.
rows.sat_rat: Diagnostics rely on technician reasoning and limited instrumentation, so misidentified fault sources are common and repeat repairs frequent. Fault diagnosis can consume extensive troubleshooting time when the true cause is elusive. Correctly diagnosing batching faults is deeply expertise-dependent and one of the hardest maintenance judgements. Misdiagnosis wastes labor and replaces good parts, a recurring and significant cost driver.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of additional batching-system components damaged during disassembly or repair
rows.imp_rat: Additional components damaged during disassembly or repair (defect) is a maintenance-collateral concern outside the core batching function, causing added cost and delay but not batch loss.
rows.sat_rat: Good procedures limit collateral damage, yet incidental damage to nearby components during disassembly remains a recurring occurrence. Careful disassembly slows work, and collateral damage triggers additional repair time. Avoiding collateral damage during teardown depends on technician care and familiarity with the equipment. Damaged adjacent components add unplanned parts and labor cost during service.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of fluid leaking from the filter housing after replacement
rows.imp_rat: Fluid leaking from a filter housing after replacement (item 13) is a maintenance-reliability issue with low relevance to batching and modest, containable consequences. Kept low.
rows.sat_rat: Correct seal seating and pressure verification handle most cases, though improperly seated O-rings still cause occasional leaks. Filter changeouts and post-change leak checks are quick, standardized tasks. Proper seating of housing seals is a routine skill most maintenance techs perform reliably. Leaks waste product and require cleanup, but housings are cheap and reseating is fast.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that skid and material states conflict with prerequisites for the selected recovery point
rows.imp_rat: Skid/material states conflicting with recovery-point prerequisites is a niche recovery-scenario error with low centrality to the core job and moderate severity. Kept low.
rows.sat_rat: Recovery-point state checking is weakly automated and prerequisite conflicts frequently surface, making this one of the least reliably served needs. Establishing a valid recovery state after an interruption often consumes significant troubleshooting time. Judging whether skid and material states are consistent for a chosen recovery point relies heavily on senior operator and engineer expertise. A wrong recovery decision can scrap in-process material, an expensive outcome.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that early component degradation remains hidden by normal skid operating variation
rows.imp_rat: Hidden early component degradation risks unexpected failure, but detection of it is a predictive-maintenance concern secondary to core operation and is capped by the ranking below 2.5.
rows.sat_rat: Most skids run reactive or fixed-interval maintenance with little predictive analytics, so incipient degradation routinely stays hidden until it causes a failure. Degradation is detected late, often only at failure, so timely warning is largely absent today. Spotting early degradation hidden in normal variation demands scarce reliability-engineering and data-analysis expertise rarely present at the skid. Undetected degradation leads to unplanned failures and batch loss, a costly recurring reality.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of validated recipes and control settings overwritten unintentionally during the software update
rows.imp_rat: Overwriting validated recipes during a software update could disrupt production, but it is an update-process error of moderate frequency ranked lower here; both proxies held under the 2.5 cap.
rows.sat_rat: Version control and pre-update backups help, but unintended overwrites of recipes and settings during updates remain a real and recurring failure mode. Software updates require staged testing and revalidation that consume substantial time. Preserving validated recipes through updates demands specialized automation engineers who understand overwrite risks. Rebuilding overwritten recipes and revalidating is costly, motivating careful update handling.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of skid instruments remaining outside specified tolerance after calibration
rows.imp_rat: Instruments still out of tolerance after calibration is a residual measurement-accuracy defect; relevance modest and severity limited since it is typically re-caught. Kept low.
rows.sat_rat: Mature calibration programs with traceable standards and as-found/as-left records make out-of-tolerance instruments after calibration rare and quickly caught. Calibration is scheduled and standardized, with reasonable turnaround per instrument. Calibration procedures and standards make this a well-defined, moderately skilled routine task. Reference standards and calibration labor are recurring but predictable and budgeted.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of the same equipment fault recurring after repair
rows.imp_rat: Recurring equipment faults after repair harm availability and maintenance cost (moderate severity) but repeated repair is a maintenance-effectiveness concern rather than core processing (low relevance). Held at 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Without consistent root-cause discipline, the same fault recurring after repair remains a common and frustrating outcome. Repeat failures reopen the maintenance cycle, adding unplanned downtime that a one-time fix would avoid. Durable repairs require root-cause skill that many maintenance teams lack, so symptomatic fixes dominate. Recurring faults multiply parts, labor, and lost-production costs on the same asset.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required validation records containing missing or inconsistent evidence
rows.imp_rat: Validation records with missing or inconsistent evidence is largely administrative/compliance rework (low relevance to physical processing) though it can jeopardize batch release (moderate severity). Under the 3.0 cap.
rows.sat_rat: Manual and hybrid record systems remain error-prone, so missing or inconsistent evidence is a recurring finding in audits. Chasing missing evidence and correcting records post-hoc extends closeout time considerably. Producing complete, consistent validation records requires disciplined documentation practice and reviewers who spot gaps. Incomplete records trigger costly re-work, investigations, or repeated qualification activities.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of deviation from the specified lubricant quantity for each designated moving component
rows.imp_rat: Deviation from specified lubricant quantity is a maintenance-detail error only loosely tied to the processing job; over/under-lubrication risks wear but consequences develop slowly and are correctable.
rows.sat_rat: Lube routes and specs are standard practice, but manual application still produces meaningful quantity deviation on individual points. Lubrication tasks are quick and routine, adding little to maintenance duration. Correct quantity relies on technician attention and following charts, which is basic but prone to over/under-application. Over-lubrication wastes lubricant and causes damage while under-lubrication shortens component life, both modest recurring costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that required traceable reference standards are unavailable or outside their validity period at scheduled calibration
rows.imp_rat: Unavailable or expired reference standards at calibration cause scheduling delays (low relevance to core processing) with recoverable consequences (moderate-low severity). Held to the cap.
rows.sat_rat: Metrology tracking systems and calendar controls make expired or missing standards uncommon, though lapses still occur under scheduling pressure. Reference standards are typically ordered and recertified ahead of scheduled dates, avoiding delay when managed well. Managing standard validity is a procedural tracking task requiring diligence more than scarce expertise. Maintaining certified standards and recertification carries a predictable recurring cost.

rows.stk: Maintenance engineers, plant operators, reliability teams.
rows.stmt: Minimize the time between the onset of equipment degradation and its recognition by monitoring personnel
rows.imp_rat: Slow recognition of degradation onset by personnel is a monitoring-latency error; relevance is moderate but severity is low because degradation is gradual and typically caught before failure.
rows.sat_rat: Where online condition monitoring exists detection is prompt, but many assets still rely on rounds that let degradation progress undetected. The gap between degradation onset and recognition is exactly the error, and periodic manual checks leave that window long. Early recognition depends on skilled analysts reading vibration or process trends, a capability not uniformly available. Continuous monitoring instrumentation to shorten the window carries meaningful capital and upkeep cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of corrosion on internal equipment surfaces and components during the shutdown
rows.imp_rat: Internal corrosion during shutdown can impair equipment integrity later, but it is a maintenance/preservation error with limited immediate relevance to running production and contained severity; scored low.
rows.sat_rat: Layup and desiccant practices are established, but incomplete coverage and humidity ingress still leave recurring corrosion on internal surfaces. Applying and later removing corrosion protection adds preparation and restart time to the shutdown. Effective preservation requires knowing which surfaces need protection and applying the right method, a moderately specialized task. Corrosion during idle periods forces expensive component replacement, while preservation consumables add moderate cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of seals and gaskets that crack or deform while the equipment is idle
rows.imp_rat: Seals and gaskets cracking while idle can cause future leaks, moderately relevant to integrity but a preservation-stage defect with contained severity; kept low.
rows.sat_rat: Preservation guidance exists but seals and gaskets still commonly dry out or deform while idle, producing startup leaks. Seal degradation is discovered at restart, causing leak-driven delays that could have been prevented. Preventing idle-state seal damage requires knowing correct storage and preservation, a modest specialist concern. Cracked seals mean replacement parts and leak cleanup, a moderate recurring cost after outages.

rows.stk: Procurement leads, process engineers, project managers.
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of overlooking critical sterile fill-finish requirements during proposal comparison
rows.imp_rat: Overlooking sterile fill-finish requirements during proposal comparison (defect) is a procurement-phase concern that is peripheral to executing the batching job, with consequences deferred and correctable before deployment.
rows.sat_rat: Requirement traceability matrices and comparison checklists help, but manual side-by-side review still routinely lets critical items go unnoticed. Thorough proposal comparison against a full requirement set is time-consuming when multiple bids are involved. Spotting an overlooked critical requirement across dense proposals depends on an experienced reviewer's attention and domain knowledge. Comparison effort is largely labor cost; missed requirements later become costly change orders.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of recipe components assigned to incorrect batching-system fields during import
rows.imp_rat: Recipe components mapped to incorrect fields during import is a data-import quality error; moderate severity but peripheral relevance to the physical batching job.
rows.sat_rat: Mapping is largely manual with weak automated validation, so wrong-field assignments slip through and are caught inconsistently, making this a chronic gap. Manual field-by-field mapping during import is slow and verification adds more time. Correct mapping requires someone who understands both the source recipe format and the batching system's field schema. Misassigned components can spoil a batch, wasting ingredients and batch time.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of imported recipe and product records with required formulation or product fields missing
rows.imp_rat: Missing required formulation fields in imported records is a data-quality error with limited direct relevance to the physical batching job and modest severity, ranked well below the top batch.
rows.sat_rat: Import validations and required-field rules catch many gaps, but incomplete source data still passes through, making this acceptably served. Scanning imported records for missing formulation fields adds a review pass. Identifying which formulation/product fields matter requires domain familiarity but is fairly standardized. Missing fields caught early are cheap; propagated to production they cost batches.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of ingredient-source routes with one or more required assets omitted
rows.imp_rat: Ingredient-source routes missing required assets create configuration gaps, but this is a setup/mapping error of limited severity and modest centrality to executing the batch.
rows.sat_rat: Today route definitions are often built from tribal knowledge and spreadsheets, so omissions recur and are only caught downstream, leaving this need poorly served. Assembling ingredient-source routes with all required assets is manually time-consuming and re-checking omissions adds further delay. Catching an omitted asset on a route depends heavily on an experienced engineer's familiarity with the plant layout and material flow. Missed assets discovered late force rework and can idle the line, incurring real cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of ingredient-source associations that conflict across piping, equipment, and control records
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting ingredient-source associations across records (item 15) is a data-consistency concern with low direct relevance to executing a batch and modest traceability consequences. Held low.
rows.sat_rat: With disconnected P&IDs, asset registers and control configs, conflicting source associations persist and are a chronic, poorly served gap. Reconciling conflicting source records across systems is a slow, mostly manual cross-check. Resolving conflicts between piping, equipment and control records demands expert knowledge of all three domains. Record reconciliation is labor cost more than material, but conflicts cause costly errors when unresolved.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of ingredient-source mappings left obsolete after design changes
rows.imp_rat: Obsolete ingredient-source mappings after design changes create rework, but this is a documentation-maintenance error peripheral to producing a correct batch.
rows.sat_rat: Change management frequently fails to purge obsolete source mappings, making this one of the least reliably served needs on the list. Obsolete mappings surface only during later troubleshooting, adding investigation time. Detecting stale mappings after design changes relies on diligent change-management discipline that is easily neglected. Maintaining current mappings is low direct cost but stale data drives expensive errors.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of operating limits based on ambiguous or mismatched units and reference bases
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguous units or reference bases in operating limits can cause misconfiguration, but this is a definitional/administrative error of limited central relevance, capped by ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Unit handling is largely manual with little automated dimensional checking, so ambiguous/mismatched bases are a chronic, poorly-served source of error. Reconciling units and reference bases across sources is tedious and slows configuration. Detecting ambiguous or mismatched units and reference bases demands careful expert scrutiny that few do consistently. Unit or reference-base errors can cause dosing errors that waste whole batches.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the operator cannot access the pause controls because of the current control mode or access authority
rows.imp_rat: An operator unable to access pause controls due to mode or authority has safety relevance, but this access-permission error is peripheral to the core batching job and ranked below the top batch.
rows.sat_rat: Role-based access and mode management are standard, but poorly configured authority levels occasionally leave the right operator unable to pause, a recurring edge case. When access is blocked by control mode or authority, resolving it costs time to switch modes or find authorized staff. The barrier is fundamentally about access rights and knowing the control-mode hierarchy, so it hinges on operator authorization and training. Inability to pause in time could allow a damaging excursion, but such lockout situations are relatively rare in well-configured systems.

rows.source_job: Establish operator access and permission levels
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of operator accounts assigned permissions beyond approved duties
rows.imp_rat: Operator accounts with permissions beyond approved duties is an access-governance defect with compliance/security stakes, but peripheral to the core batch-prep job; both proxies stay low under the cap.
rows.sat_rat: Role-based access control and audit reviews are standard, though residual over-provisioning persists from default or convenience settings. Configuring account permissions is a routine setup task that rarely adds significant schedule time. Proper role assignment needs administrators who understand both the process and the access model, and lapses occur when this is rushed. Over-permissioned accounts cost little directly but create audit and compliance exposure that can be expensive if found.

rows.source_job: Establish operator access and permission levels
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of operators assigned permissions beyond their approved duties
rows.imp_rat: Operators assigned permissions beyond approved duties mirrors item 6 as an access-control gap; peripheral to core prep with contained compliance/security severity, kept low.
rows.sat_rat: RBAC templates and qualification records make correct assignment fairly consistent, with recurring gaps from ad hoc access grants. Assigning operators to roles is quick within established access frameworks. Correct assignment depends on managers knowing each operator's approved duties, and errors arise when duties are poorly documented. Excess operator permissions carry compliance risk more than direct cost, kept in check by periodic access reviews.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required technical and commercial specifications missing from the quotation request
rows.imp_rat: Missing technical/commercial specs from a quotation request is a procurement-phase defect with low relevance to the batch job and modest downstream severity.
rows.sat_rat: RFQ templates and checklists cover most commercial and technical items, though specialized specs still get missed on occasion. Compiling complete technical and commercial specs for the RFQ takes coordinated effort that is usually scheduled. Ensuring completeness relies on experienced engineering and procurement collaboration. Missing specs yield poor quotes and re-quoting costs, while spec compilation is inexpensive.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required pharmaceutical quality and regulatory provisions missing from the final agreement
rows.imp_rat: Missing pharmaceutical quality/regulatory provisions in the final agreement is a contracting defect with low relevance to the physical batch job and modest severity.
rows.sat_rat: Mature pharma contracting practice and quality agreements cover most regulatory provisions, with only occasional gaps. Incorporating regulatory provisions into the final agreement fits within normal contracting timelines. Quality and regulatory affairs input is needed to ensure provisions are complete, requiring cross-functional expertise. Missing provisions risk compliance costs later, but drafting them costs little.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required validation and compliance deliverables omitted from the purchase agreement
rows.imp_rat: Omitting validation/compliance deliverables from a purchase agreement is an administrative contracting defect with low direct relevance to the batch job and modest downstream severity.
rows.sat_rat: Standard purchase-agreement templates for GMP equipment capture most validation deliverables, though specific items are occasionally omitted. Negotiating validation and compliance deliverables into contracts adds legal-technical review time that is generally allotted. Specifying required deliverables needs experienced quality and contracting staff working in tandem. Missing deliverables cause costly post-award disputes, but including them in the agreement is inexpensive.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which the actual agitation period deviates from the approved batch instruction
rows.imp_rat: Agitation period deviation from instruction is a procedural variance with limited direct harm to homogeneity.
rows.sat_rat: Documented mix times with timers keep actual agitation close to the instruction reliably. Timers and BR instructions govern agitation duration without added time. Following a specified mix time needs little skill. Deviating agitation time rarely wastes material directly.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of mismatch between recorded and observed vessel conditions
rows.imp_rat: Record-vs-observed mismatch is largely documentation integrity, only weakly tied to physical homogeneity.
rows.sat_rat: Manual recording still allows discrepancies between records and actual vessel state despite review steps. Reconciling records to observed conditions can slow batch review. Accurate observation and recording depend on operator diligence. Mismatches rarely cost material but drive investigation effort.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration between observing a compounding event and recording it
rows.imp_rat: Delay in recording an event is documentation timeliness, peripheral to producing homogeneous solution.
rows.sat_rat: Delayed entries introduce timestamp and recall errors into the record. Manual recording often lags the event, and contemporaneous entry is inconsistently achieved. Timely recording depends on operator discipline rather than skill. Recording lag costs little material but drives data-integrity findings.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of losing the current sequence position after an interruption
rows.imp_rat: Losing sequence position after interruption causes procedural confusion but is usually recoverable; low stakes.
rows.sat_rat: Paper checkmarks help but interruptions still risk losing the sequence position. Recovering sequence position after interruption can stall the operator. Re-establishing where you were relies on operator memory and record clarity. A lost position can cause omissions or duplications that scrap a batch.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of critical charging, agitation, temperature, and pH records unavailable for reconstructing the batch
rows.imp_rat: Unavailable records for reconstruction are a traceability gap remote from achieving homogeneity.
rows.sat_rat: Electronic batch records and data historians capture most critical parameters reliably today. Missing records for batch reconstruction extend investigations significantly. Reconstructing a batch from incomplete records demands expert effort. Unrecoverable batches from missing data are expensive losses.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of misclassifying observed solids by material identity, physical state, or source
rows.imp_rat: Misclassifying observed solids is a peripheral diagnostic error with limited direct effect on achieving blend homogeneity.
rows.sat_rat: Ad hoc visual identification is error-prone, and confirmatory analysis is not always performed promptly. Misclassifying observed solids leads to wrong corrective paths that waste investigation time. Correctly identifying solids by material, state, and source demands specialized analytical judgement. Misclassification can cause wrong disposition and material loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which concurrent deviations obscure the cause of incomplete dissolution
rows.imp_rat: Concurrent deviations obscuring cause is an investigative ambiguity, only indirectly tied to producing a homogeneous solution.
rows.sat_rat: Investigations rely on limited data, so concurrent deviations frequently obscure the true cause. Concurrent deviations tangle root-cause work, lengthening investigations. Disentangling overlapping causes of incomplete dissolution requires expert investigative skill. Prolonged unclear investigations delay disposition and risk batch loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of accepting a cause without evidence linking it to the observed incomplete dissolution
rows.imp_rat: Accepting an unsupported cause is an investigation-quality lapse, peripheral to the physical blending job.
rows.sat_rat: Without robust in-process evidence, root-cause conclusions are often weakly supported. Accepting an unsupported cause can short-circuit time but recurs and costs more later. Linking evidence to cause rigorously demands disciplined investigative expertise often in short supply. Wrong causes lead to ineffective fixes and repeat batch losses.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that confirmation of the dissolution failure cause exceeds the allowable batch hold window
rows.imp_rat: Cause-confirmation exceeding hold window is an investigation-timing issue with limited direct blend impact.
rows.sat_rat: Slow investigations frequently strain or exceed hold windows, making this a recurring risk. Failure-cause confirmation exceeding the hold window forces batch loss, a time-critical failure. Rapid, correct cause confirmation demands scarce investigative expertise. Blowing the hold window can scrap the batch entirely.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of proposed operating boundaries unsupported by representative batch evidence
rows.imp_rat: Unsupported operating boundaries are a validation-evidence gap only indirectly tied to blending.
rows.sat_rat: Regulatory expectation drives boundary justification, but boundaries backed by full batch evidence are often incomplete. Unsupported boundaries surface during audits or failures, causing rework delays. Generating representative boundary evidence requires strong development expertise. Unsupported boundaries risk batch failures and wasted development runs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of overlap between parameter measurement uncertainty and the proposed operating boundaries
rows.imp_rat: Measurement-uncertainty overlap with boundaries is a metrology-nuance issue, peripheral to the job.
rows.sat_rat: Uncertainty is rarely formally reconciled with operating boundaries, leaving a real gap. Measurement-uncertainty overlap with boundaries triggers extra confirmation that adds time. Quantifying measurement uncertainty against limits needs metrology expertise. Overlap can cause false accept/reject, wasting material.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time the compounding vessel waits for released components during charging
rows.imp_rat: Waiting for released components delays start but does not affect blend quality; low stakes.
rows.sat_rat: Scheduling and dispensing coordination usually align releases, but QC release delays still cause waits. Waiting for released components idles the vessel and delays the batch directly. Little operator skill affects this; it is a materials-logistics issue. Idle vessel time is lost capacity and cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of unplanned mixing interruptions before the target endpoint is reached
rows.imp_rat: Unplanned mixing interruptions delay endpoint but the job usually resumes; low stakes.
rows.sat_rat: Equipment reliability and procedures limit interruptions, but utility and mechanical stoppages still occur. Unplanned mixing interruptions directly delay reaching the endpoint. Recovering cleanly from interruptions takes operator judgement. Interruptions waste energy and vessel time and can compromise the batch.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time between blend readiness and initiation of endpoint confirmation
rows.imp_rat: Delay between blend readiness and endpoint confirmation is a timing lag with minor consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Scheduling usually initiates confirmation promptly, but lab handoff delays still occur. Delay between blend readiness and endpoint confirmation is direct idle batch time. Little special skill is involved; it depends on lab availability and coordination. Waiting to start confirmation idles the vessel, costing capacity.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of parameter or sequence discrepancies requiring clarification during batch execution
rows.imp_rat: Parameter/sequence discrepancies needing clarification cause minor interruptions; limited direct impact.
rows.sat_rat: Reviewed electronic records reduce discrepancies, but ambiguities requiring clarification still arise. Clarifying parameter or sequence discrepancies mid-batch stops execution and costs time. Resolving discrepancies requires experienced interpretation of the batch record. Discrepancy-driven holds waste labor and vessel time.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required source-volume agitation, geometry, temperature, and mixing values absent from controlled records
rows.imp_rat: Missing source values in controlled records is a documentation-completeness issue, indirect to blending.
rows.sat_rat: Source-scale mixing and geometry values are inconsistently captured in controlled records, leaving frequent gaps. Absent source-volume values force reconstruction or re-development, delaying scale-up work. Recovering or re-deriving missing scale-up values requires specialized engineering knowledge. Missing records can force costly repeat characterization runs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of the batch hold while endpoint results remain unavailable
rows.imp_rat: Batch hold awaiting endpoint results is a scheduling delay with little effect on blend quality.
rows.sat_rat: Off-line assay turnaround makes long hold waits routine rather than rare. Holding the batch while awaiting endpoint results is a major, common source of delay. Result turnaround depends on lab capacity, not executor skill. Extended holds tie up the vessel and risk material stability, adding cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of uncertainty about whether a mixing parameter remains within its approved range near a boundary
rows.imp_rat: Uncertainty about a parameter near a boundary is a transient interpretive doubt with minor consequence.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of doubt about consistency between executed and recorded mixing parameter values
rows.imp_rat: Doubt about executed-vs-recorded consistency is a records-assurance concern peripheral to the job.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of visible uncertainty about the batch endpoint while others await the disposition decision
rows.imp_rat: Visible uncertainty while others await disposition is largely a workflow-perception issue with minor consequence.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of disposition evidence not traceable to the correct batch, vessel run, or sample
rows.imp_rat: Untraceable disposition evidence is a traceability gap peripheral to the mixing outcome.
rows.sat_rat: Electronic traceability links evidence to batch and vessel run well where deployed, but paper-based or hybrid systems still lose linkage. Tracing evidence to the right run takes time but traceability is the target, not speed. Maintaining traceability requires disciplined record-keeping rather than deep expertise. LIMS/MES traceability systems have setup cost but low marginal cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of batch disposition delay caused by unavailable quality evidence
rows.imp_rat: Disposition delay from missing evidence is a workflow lag with little effect on blend quality.
rows.sat_rat: The eventual decision is usually correct once evidence arrives, so the failure is the delay itself rather than a wrong outcome.
