Disposition waiting on missing evidence is fundamentally a delay, and expediting is largely reactive today. Chasing down missing evidence relies on experienced coordinators knowing where to look. Delays tie up inventory and capacity, imposing real carrying costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of inconsistency among vessel, batch, material, and quality records used for blend-quality risk evaluation
rows.imp_rat: Record inconsistency for risk evaluation is a data-integrity concern peripheral to the physical job.
rows.sat_rat: Fragmented systems and manual transcription commonly produce inconsistent records, and integrated MES coverage is still uneven across sites. Reconciling records across systems is time-consuming but the failure being reduced is inconsistency, not delay. Cross-checking vessel, batch, material and quality records demands attentive, knowledgeable reviewers. Disparate legacy systems impose reconciliation labor cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of ambiguity in the homogeneity and GMP criteria used to judge blend-quality risk
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity in GMP/homogeneity risk criteria is an assessment-framework issue only indirectly tied to the job.
rows.sat_rat: Standardized specifications reduce ambiguity for common cases, but edge cases and qualitative homogeneity criteria still force subjective judgment. Ambiguity resolution consumes review and clarification time but the core issue is not speed. When acceptance criteria are vague, disposition leans heavily on scarce expert interpretation, which is exactly the burden this need targets. Clarifying criteria costs mainly staff effort rather than materials.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of variation among risk judgments for equivalent vessel, formulation, and batch conditions
rows.imp_rat: Variation in risk judgments is an assessment-consistency issue with limited direct blending impact.
rows.sat_rat: Without strong decision rules, equivalent conditions get judged differently by different reviewers, and consistency remains weakly controlled. Achieving consistent judgments is not primarily about speed. Risk judgments still rely on individual expert interpretation, so variation among assessors is a persistent skill-dependent problem. Standardization efforts cost training time but little material.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of homogeneity determinations lacking complete traceable source records
rows.imp_rat: Homogeneity determinations lacking traceable records is a documentation gap, indirect to the physical job.
rows.sat_rat: Homogeneity determinations sometimes lack complete traceable source records where systems are fragmented, a moderate recurring gap. Compiling source records takes time but completeness is the concern. Ensuring complete traceable source records requires knowledgeable documentation practice. Documentation labor cost, standard.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of batch and monitoring records with missing or inconsistent validation evidence
rows.imp_rat: Missing validation evidence in records is a data-integrity gap peripheral to producing the blend.
rows.sat_rat: Missing or inconsistent validation evidence in batch records remains a frequent audit finding, indicating only moderate control. Filling evidence gaps consumes time but the metric targets completeness, not duration. Ensuring validation evidence is complete requires trained reviewers who know what is required. Documentation labor is significant but standard.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration that validation-relevant deviations affecting dissolution or blending remain unresolved
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved validation deviations are a compliance-backlog issue peripheral to producing the blend.
rows.sat_rat: CAPA systems track deviations, but backlog and slow closure are chronic industry problems that leave many open too long. Unresolved validation-relevant deviations lingering is an open-duration problem the metric targets directly. Resolving these deviations demands cross-functional validation and quality expertise. Prolonged open deviations tie up resources and delay release, carrying real cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration that required validation-state assessments for dissolution and blending remain overdue
rows.imp_rat: Overdue validation-state assessments are a compliance-timing issue peripheral to the physical job.
rows.sat_rat: Periodic review programs exist but assessments frequently slip past due dates when resources are stretched. Overdue validation-state assessments are literally a schedule-adherence duration problem. Determining validation state requires qualified validation specialists whose availability is limited. Validation labor is costly but planned.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of candidate capability claims unsupported under specified process conditions
rows.imp_rat: Unsupported capability claims are a qualification-documentation issue, remote from the core blending outcome.
rows.sat_rat: Vendor capability claims are frequently accepted without process-specific verification, leaving unsupported assertions. Validating each capability claim under process conditions extends evaluation time. Judging which vendor claims lack support needs experienced critical review. Unsupported claims lead to costly capability shortfalls.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percent of product-contact vessel surface absent from candidate cleanability evidence
rows.imp_rat: Contact surface absent from cleanability evidence is a coverage-documentation gap, indirectly tied to blending.
rows.sat_rat: Cleanability documentation is fairly mature for standard geometries but complex internals leave residual coverage gaps. Assembling cleanability evidence for all contact surfaces adds documentation time. Evaluating cleanability coverage uses standard cleaning-validation expertise. Missing evidence forces costly revalidation.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of mixing beyond achievement of dissolution and homogeneity criteria
rows.imp_rat: Over-mixing wastes time but the job still succeeds; low relevance and mild consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Endpoint-based stopping is reasonably established but conservative padding still adds unnecessary mixing time. Excess mixing beyond endpoint is a clear, measurable time inefficiency. Recognizing the true endpoint to stop needs moderate process judgement. Over-mixing wastes energy and occupies capacity.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of specified vessel capabilities lacking traceable sources during qualification review
rows.imp_rat: Untraceable capability sources are a qualification-documentation gap remote from blending.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of mismatch between qualification acceptance limits and the validated processing envelope for the intended bulk solution
rows.imp_rat: Qualification-limit mismatch is a validation-alignment concern, peripheral to daily blending.
rows.sat_rat: Limits and processing envelopes are frequently developed separately, so mismatches recur and are caught inconsistently. Reconciling acceptance limits with the validated envelope requires iterative alignment that slows sign-off. Matching limits to the processing envelope demands validation SMEs who understand both product and equipment. Mismatch corrections cost review hours rather than materials.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of qualification criteria lacking traceability to process risks or validated processing requirements
rows.imp_rat: Qualification criteria lacking traceability is a documentation-rigor gap peripheral to daily blending.
rows.sat_rat: Traceability is expected under QRM but often partial, leaving criteria without clear links to validated requirements. Building traceability matrices linking criteria to risks is labor-intensive. Establishing risk-to-criterion traceability requires disciplined quality-risk-management expertise. Traceability work is analyst time with little material cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which cleanability evidence differs from intended formulations and operating conditions
rows.imp_rat: Cleanability evidence differing from actual formulations is a validation-representativeness issue, indirect to the job.
rows.sat_rat: Coupon studies and worst-case selection often approximate rather than replicate real operating conditions, so gaps between evidence and reality remain a recurring regulatory vulnerability. Aligning cleanability evidence with actual formulations may delay release when representativeness is challenged in review or audit. Judging whether validation coupons and conditions truly represent production requires sophisticated validation science that is scarce and audit-sensitive. Generating more representative evidence increases study scope and cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of repeat cleaning cycles required to meet hygienic acceptance criteria
rows.imp_rat: Repeat cleaning cycles are an efficiency loss with minimal effect on the blending job itself.
rows.sat_rat: Validated cycles usually pass on the first attempt for established products, but formulation variability and marginal limits still cause enough reruns that this remains only acceptably served. Repeat cleaning cycles directly extend vessel turnaround, and status-quo CIP recipes still trigger reruns when acceptance criteria are marginally missed. Diagnosing why a cycle failed and adjusting parameters relies on experienced cleaning-validation staff rather than automated correction. Each repeat cycle consumes additional water, chemicals, and energy, adding avoidable cost per event.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of manual cleaning interventions required for vessel product-contact surfaces
rows.imp_rat: Manual cleaning interventions are a labor/efficiency concern with minimal blend-quality relevance.
rows.sat_rat: Automated CIP has largely displaced manual cleaning on modern jacketed tanks, but manual touch-up of ports, agitators, and upper heads still recurs and reintroduces operator-dependent variability. Manual cleaning interventions are labor-intensive and slow, extending turnaround whenever CIP cannot fully reach a surface. Manual cleaning depends heavily on operator technique and consistency, making outcomes vulnerable to human variability and demanding trained, attentive personnel. Manual interventions add labor hours and gowning/consumable cost each occurrence.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of conflicting conclusions from vessel surface, geometry, and cleanability evidence
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting cleanability-evidence conclusions are an evaluation ambiguity remote from the mixing job.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the cleanability rationale cannot be supported with auditable evidence
rows.imp_rat: Unsupportable cleanability rationale is an evidence-quality gap remote from the blending job.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of conflicting residue-removal conclusions during vessel evaluation
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting residue-removal conclusions are cleaning-evaluation ambiguity, remote from blending.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of completed maintenance activities omitted from the batch preparation system history
rows.imp_rat: Completed maintenance activities missing from the batch prep system history is a low-relevance recordkeeping defect with minor, easily corrected consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Mandatory CMMS closeout keeps the omission percentage moderate, but informal repairs and incomplete entries keep a persistent share of activities out of the system history. Completing history entries at closeout is fast when enforced by workflow, slow when done from memory later. Minimal skill is required, so the omitted percentage reflects compliance discipline rather than capability. Recording is nearly free while a thin history raises audit and troubleshooting costs downstream.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of completed maintenance activities omitted from the maintenance history
rows.imp_rat: Maintenance activities missing from the maintenance history is a recordkeeping defect with limited relevance to producing batches; severity is minor and easily absorbed, so both proxies are low.
rows.sat_rat: CMMS work-order closeout captures much history, yet manual entry and off-system fixes routinely leave completed activities unrecorded. Logging each completed activity adds documentation time that busy technicians often defer or skip. Recording history needs little skill, only discipline, so omissions stem from habit rather than expertise. The direct cost of logging is trivial, but missing history costs later in repeated diagnostics and compliance gaps.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required equipment-history and compliance records missing at handover
rows.imp_rat: Missing equipment-history/compliance records at handover is an administrative documentation error tangential to preparing batches (low relevance); it can delay but rarely halts the core function (low severity), and per the ceiling stays at or below 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Equipment-history and compliance records are frequently found missing at handover due to incomplete lifecycle recordkeeping. Gathering equipment-history records for handover is time-intensive when maintenance data is dispersed. Knowing the complete required equipment-history set relies on asset-management expertise not always present at closeout. The effort is largely labor cost, modest relative to the compliance exposure of gaps.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required equipment history and transfer records missing at closing
rows.imp_rat: Missing equipment-history and transfer records at closing is a records-completeness error at project end, peripheral to running the system (low relevance) and administratively absorbable (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Checklists help but required history and transfer records still go missing at closing more often than acceptable. Assembling transfer records at closing adds administrative time that is often compressed. Ensuring all required history and transfer records are included depends on procedural familiarity that varies by team. Documentation labor is the main cost, generally minor versus the value of a clean transfer.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of batch recipes and preparation parameters transferred incorrectly to the replacement system
rows.imp_rat: Incorrect transfer of batch recipes/parameters to a replacement system touches the core prep function, but per the batch ceiling relevance and severity are capped low (both at 2), keeping importance at or below 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Recipe and parameter transfer is error-prone because format mismatches and manual re-entry introduce incorrect values that surface later. Transferring recipes and parameters to a replacement system takes considerable configuration time. Correctly mapping parameters between old and new systems demands deep process and configuration expertise that is scarce. Manual transcription or migration labor drives cost, and errors trigger costly rework and re-validation.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the replacement system lacks required production release at cutover
rows.imp_rat: Lack of production release at cutover could stall changeover, but under the batch importance ceiling both relevance and severity are held at 2, keeping importance at or below 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Formal qualification and release gates exist, but replacement systems still occasionally reach cutover without full required release. Obtaining production release for the replacement system requires validation activities that take significant lead time. Confirming release readiness demands validation and regulatory expertise applied carefully at cutover. Delayed release or reworking a non-released system carries substantial production and revalidation costs.

rows.stk: Facilities/asset management team, environmental health and safety, procurement.
rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of recoverable material sent to final disposal instead of reuse, resale, or recycling
rows.imp_rat: Recoverable material sent to disposal instead of reuse is a sustainability waste concern peripheral to batch preparation (very low relevance) with modest cost/environmental severity.
rows.sat_rat: Some scrap-metal and asset-recovery practice exists but a large share of recoverable material still goes to final disposal. Separating reusable and recyclable materials takes extra sorting time that pushes crews toward bulk disposal. Identifying resale and reuse value requires market knowledge that decommissioning crews often lack. Recoverable value is routinely forfeited to landfill because capturing it is not prioritized in disposal budgets.

rows.stk: Facilities/asset management team, environmental health and safety, procurement.
rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of eligible materials sent through noncompliant recycling or disposal channels
rows.imp_rat: Sending eligible materials through noncompliant disposal channels is peripheral to preparing batches (very low relevance) with modest environmental/regulatory severity.
rows.sat_rat: Licensed waste contractors and manifests help, but misrouting to noncompliant channels still occurs, especially with mixed or ambiguous materials. Sorting materials to compliant channels adds handling time that pressures crews toward the fastest disposal route. Correctly classifying materials to compliant recycling channels requires waste-stream knowledge that varies among contractors. Compliant channels can cost more than landfill, creating an incentive that undermines compliance.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required compliance evidence that is missing or incomplete
rows.imp_rat: Incomplete compliance evidence is a records-quality defect only loosely connected to executing batch preparation; its impact is confined to compliance documentation rather than job success, keeping it at the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Document-control and checklist practices reduce missing or incomplete evidence, but recurring gaps still appear in the record. Completeness checks on compliance evidence add review passes that can bottleneck near sign-off. Trained QA reviewers know what evidence is required, making this a fairly standard skilled task. The effort is mainly review labor with little material cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required compliance evidence missing from the acquisition record
rows.imp_rat: Missing compliance evidence from the acquisition record is a documentation-completeness error tangential to running batch preparation; when it occurs it risks audit gaps but does not stop the core job, so it sits at the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Established QA document-control practices and checklists catch most gaps, though some required evidence still goes missing under time pressure. Assembling compliance evidence into the acquisition record is well-practiced but still consumes review time near approval. Quality and regulatory staff routinely handle compliance evidence, so the required skill is available if not always applied consistently. Compiling evidence is largely labor cost, modest relative to the capital purchase.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required capital request fields that are missing or inaccurate
rows.imp_rat: Missing or inaccurate capital request fields is a procurement-form defect with little centrality to batch preparation itself; when it happens it delays a request rather than harming the job, staying within the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Mandatory fields and validation in capital request systems keep missing or inaccurate entries relatively low, though inputs can still be wrong. Capital request forms are structured and quick to complete once inputs are known. Filling required fields is routine administrative work supported by finance templates. The task is low-cost form completion with negligible resource use.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of critical fabrication, testing, release, or shipping milestones lacking current status
rows.imp_rat: Milestones lacking current status is a status-visibility ambiguity that is informational rather than central to performing batch preparation; its worst effect is coordination confusion, so it remains low.
rows.sat_rat: Without a shared live status system, milestone currency is inconsistent and gaps in current status are common. Milestone status is often stale because updates depend on manual vendor reporting, so purchasers learn of slips late. Tracking fabrication and shipping milestones is routine project coordination requiring modest expertise. Status tracking is inexpensive, mostly expediter time.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that critical supplier capability gaps remain undetected before approval
rows.imp_rat: Undetected supplier capability gaps before approval is a procurement-vetting risk removed from operating the system (low relevance); capped at 2 per the batch ceiling despite potential downstream cost (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Standard qualification questionnaires exist but frequently fail to expose real capability gaps, so undetected shortfalls surface only after approval. Supplier capability audits and reference checks take weeks and are often compressed under deadline pressure, letting gaps slip through. Detecting subtle capability gaps requires seasoned auditors who know sterile fill-finish failure modes, a scarce and inconsistently applied expertise. Formal supplier assessments cost audit time and travel, so they are sometimes skipped for cost reasons.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of overlooking relevant system types or suppliers serving sterile fill-finish applications
rows.imp_rat: Overlooking system types or suppliers for sterile fill-finish is a research-completeness gap that is peripheral to the physical batch prep job (low relevance), and consequences are recoverable through further sourcing (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Teams tend to default to incumbent or familiar suppliers, so relevant alternatives are regularly overlooked despite available directories. Scanning the supplier landscape takes market-research time that is often limited to known vendors. Comprehensive awareness of all relevant system types and suppliers depends on scarce market knowledge and experience. Overlooking better-fit suppliers has opportunity cost, but the search itself is inexpensive.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of the batch preparation system being damaged during transportation, unloading, or rigging
rows.imp_rat: Transportation/rigging damage to the system is a logistics defect adjacent to the core batch-prep function; although damage could be costly, the ranking places this batch below prior items, capping both proxies.
rows.sat_rat: Mature packaging, shock-monitoring and rigging practices substantially reduce transit damage, though incidents still occur. Proper rigging and transport planning adds lead time but is a well-understood scheduling step. Specialized riggers and packaging engineers exist and are routinely engaged for large sterile systems. Protective crating, insurance and qualified riggers add real cost that is usually budgeted.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of registration fields containing incorrect system identity or configuration data
rows.imp_rat: Incorrect system identity or configuration data in registration is a data-quality defect peripheral to the core job; wrong identity could complicate warranty or service records but not job execution, staying within the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Structured registration fields and cross-checks keep incorrect identity data relatively low, though transcription errors still slip through. Capturing system identity and configuration data is a fast entry step at registration. Recording identity and configuration fields is straightforward clerical work. The data-entry effort carries minimal cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of facility space, utility, environmental, and structural constraint data that is inaccurate or outdated
rows.imp_rat: Inaccurate facility space and utility constraint data is peripheral to the core job of preparing batches; when wrong it may cause installation rework, but the batch-prep job itself proceeds, and per the batch ceiling it stays low.
rows.sat_rat: Facility records are often outdated or inconsistent across systems, so constraint data used in acquisition frequently proves inaccurate. Gathering current facility, utility and structural data involves slow cross-departmental requests that delay planning. Interpreting facility and utility constraint data needs facilities engineers, but the collection itself is fairly routine. Stale data is cheap to hold but triggers expensive site modifications when discovered late.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of batch-start delay when product-contact surfaces do not meet pre-use acceptance criteria
rows.imp_rat: Batch-start delay when contact surfaces fail pre-use criteria protects against contamination (relevance) with hygiene consequences (severity), but the ranking caps this at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Cleaning validation and pre-use inspection are mature practices, so most surfaces pass, but borderline results still trigger recurring holds. Batch start is often held while surfaces are re-cleaned or re-swabbed to meet acceptance criteria, a common and disruptive delay. Interpreting whether surfaces pass pre-use criteria relies on trained inspectors and defined cleaning validation, which is reasonably established. Re-cleaning consumes solvents, water, labor, and delayed throughput when surfaces fail initial checks.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of leaks or pressure-loss defects remaining undetected after integrity testing
rows.imp_rat: Leaks remaining undetected after integrity testing bear on batch containment, but under the ceiling both relevance and severity are held to 2 as this item ranks below the previous batch.
rows.sat_rat: Pressure and leak testing are mature and catch most defects, but slow or intermittent leaks can evade standard test durations. Integrity testing itself is time-bounded, but undetected leaks surface later and trigger far larger schedule loss. Interpreting pressure-decay and detecting slow leaks requires trained technicians and careful method. Undetected defects escaping to operation cause expensive batch loss and downtime later.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percent of assembled connections and containment sections omitted from integrity testing
rows.imp_rat: Connections omitted from integrity testing is a verification-coverage error ranked below the prior batch; relevance is modest and severity capped since it represents a process gap rather than a realized failure.
rows.sat_rat: Test packages and punch lists cover most connections, but omissions from complex systems still occur without a robust closure system. Tracking which connections were tested versus omitted takes coordination time, and gaps found late force re-testing. Complete test coverage depends on diligent tracking and inspector experience rather than automatic verification. Untested sections that later fail impose costly late rework and re-cleaning.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of liquid-contact surfaces or open process connections becoming contaminated during positioning and mounting
rows.imp_rat: Contamination of liquid-contact surfaces during positioning bears on batch purity, but the ranking ceiling holds relevance and severity to 2 as this item sits below the prior batch.
rows.sat_rat: Protective caps and clean-handling protocols help, but exposure during positioning still causes recurring contamination events. Contamination during positioning forces re-cleaning that delays assembly progress. Keeping contact surfaces protected during mounting demands disciplined handling that depends on trained, attentive crews. Re-cleaning contaminated surfaces consumes cleaning agents, labor, and delays.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of leaks at pneumatic or ancillary utility connections identified during verification
rows.imp_rat: Leaks at pneumatic or ancillary utility connections found during verification are ancillary to the process-fluid batching job; relevance is low and severity mild since these are caught during verification.
rows.sat_rat: Standard soap-test and pressure-check practices reliably catch these leaks, so they are among the better-controlled defects. Pneumatic and ancillary utility leaks are quickly found and fixed during verification with minimal delay. Detecting and tightening utility connections is low-skill, routine verification work. Minor air or utility leaks waste some energy but carry little material cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an interlock fails to halt the assembled system when a hazardous condition occurs during the validation batch
rows.imp_rat: Interlock failure to halt hazardous conditions is materially safety-relevant, but the mandatory batch ceiling limits it; held at the 2.0 cap.
rows.sat_rat: Interlock verification against SIS standards is mature practice, though incomplete test coverage of rare hazardous states leaves some residual risk. Interlock function testing is a defined commissioning step that fits within the validation schedule. Proving interlocks halt hazardous conditions requires competent functional-safety testers whose availability can constrain thoroughness. Safety-instrumented systems carry cost but are budgeted as standard for these installations.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required commissioning records missing after the validation batch
rows.imp_rat: Missing commissioning records after the validation batch is a documentation gap not central to batching liquids (low relevance) and typically re-collectable (low severity). Held to ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Records checklists exist but records still routinely go missing after the validation batch, keeping this a recurring shortfall. Missing commissioning records force retesting or re-documentation that delays validation closeout. Assembling the complete record set is largely a coordination task rather than a highly skilled one. Reconstructing or repeating missing records adds avoidable cost, especially if a batch must be rerun.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of critical startup, batch execution, monitoring, and shutdown steps performed incorrectly during qualification
rows.imp_rat: Incorrect startup, execution, monitoring, and shutdown steps during qualification bear on batching integrity (relevance) and can corrupt batches (severity), but the ranking places this below the prior batch, capping it at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: SOPs, checklists, and supervised qualification catch most critical-step errors, though residual human-error deviations recur even with mature training programs. Qualification runs consume significant time as operators walk through startup, batch, monitoring, and shutdown sequences, and error-driven re-qualification adds unpredictable delay. Correct execution depends heavily on operator training, attention, and the presence of experienced trainers, so error rates track directly with skill and vigilance which remain scarce. Incorrect steps during qualification waste batch materials and staff hours but are largely absorbed within a controlled training environment.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of abnormal conditions and system faults handled incorrectly during qualification
rows.imp_rat: Incorrect handling of abnormal conditions during qualification is safety-relevant but occurs in a controlled test phase, and the batch ceiling caps it; held at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Fault and abnormal-condition scenarios are numerous and hard to fully enumerate, so some are still handled incorrectly during qualification despite structured testing. Testing abnormal-condition handling extends qualification but is planned into the schedule. Correctly designing and judging fault-handling tests requires deep control and safety expertise that is a limiting resource. Mishandled faults during real operation are costly, so investment in qualification testing is justified though not exhaustive.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of process fluid leaking from completed piping connections during testing or startup
rows.imp_rat: Process fluid leaking from piping during testing or startup affects batch integrity, but per the ranking ceiling relevance and severity are constrained to 2 as this sits lower than prior items.
rows.sat_rat: Hydrostatic and functional testing reliably reveal connection leaks, making startup leakage relatively well controlled. Leaks found during testing or startup add repair and retest time, but detection is usually prompt. Making sound leak-free connections is a well-practiced fitter skill supported by torque specs and gaskets. Fluid leaks waste product and require cleanup, though testing catches most before production quantities are involved.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of mechanical stress imposed on system connection points by misaligned piping
rows.imp_rat: Mechanical stress from misaligned piping on connection points risks leaks over time (relevance) and potential failures (severity), but this sits below the prior batch at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Good fabrication practice and strain gauges reduce imposed stress, but hidden pipe-strain defects still slip through routinely. Correcting misaligned piping to relieve connection stress adds fit-up and rework time during assembly. Proper alignment and strain-free fit-up depend on skilled fitters and their judgement about acceptable tolerances. Misalignment stress can damage flanges, seals, and nozzles, driving replacement and repair costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that final commissioning changes are omitted from the baseline documentation
rows.imp_rat: Omitting final commissioning changes from baseline docs is a records-completeness error peripheral to the preparation job (low relevance), correctable retroactively (low severity). Capped at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Late commissioning changes are the ones most likely to escape documentation, so omissions from the baseline remain a common problem. Omitted final commissioning changes cost time later when the baseline no longer matches reality. Capturing last-minute field changes into the baseline depends on disciplined change control that hinges on individual conscientiousness. An out-of-date baseline drives future errors whose remediation cost outweighs the modest effort to document changes.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of control parameter and recipe values recorded incorrectly in the baseline documentation
rows.imp_rat: Incorrect control parameter/recipe values in baseline documentation is a documentation-accuracy defect adjacent to the CFJ (low relevance) and correctable before operation (low severity). Capped at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Manual documentation of parameters remains error-prone with limited cross-checking, so incorrectly recorded baseline values are a common and consequential failure. Incorrect baseline parameter values surface later as troubleshooting time when the system behaves unexpectedly. Accurate transcription of recipe and control values into baseline docs depends on careful expert review that is easy to shortcut under schedule pressure. Wrong baseline values propagate into off-spec batches whose material and rework cost can be substantial.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required instrument calibration certificates missing from the closeout documentation
rows.imp_rat: Missing calibration certificates in closeout documentation is an administrative documentation gap only tangentially tied to the batching job (low relevance) with recoverable consequences (low severity). Held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Calibration documentation is a well-known deliverable yet certificates are routinely incomplete at closeout, making this a persistent gap. Chasing missing calibration certificates at closeout adds late scrambling that delays turnover. Knowing which certificates are required and matching them to instruments takes documentation discipline more than deep skill. Missing certificates can force re-calibration or re-inspection, incurring real avoidable cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of documented pipe routes or instrument connections that differ from the physical installation
rows.imp_rat: Documented pipe routes differing from physical installation is an as-built documentation discrepancy tangential to batching (low relevance) and fixable via redlines (low severity). Held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: As-built reconciliation is notoriously incomplete in practice, so documented routes frequently diverge from the physical plant. As-built discrepancies are usually discovered during troubleshooting, adding significant investigation time downstream. Detecting that documented routing differs from physical installation requires diligent walkdowns by experienced personnel who may be scarce. Undocumented deviations cause misdirected work and potential safety incidents that are expensive to correct.

rows.stk: Safety engineers, commissioning engineers, system operators.
rows.source_job: Verify safety interlocks and shutdown functions
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of tested unsafe conditions that fail to inhibit startup or continued operation
rows.imp_rat: Tested unsafe conditions failing to inhibit startup is safety-significant, yet the batch ranking caps importance; held at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Permissive and inhibit logic is routinely tested, yet untested condition combinations occasionally slip through, leaving a real residual gap. Startup-inhibit testing is part of scheduled commissioning and adds bounded time. Designing tests that exercise every unsafe condition demands strong safety-engineering judgement that is not universally available. Failure to inhibit startup can cause damaging events, but preventive testing is standard budgeted work.

rows.stk: Safety engineers, commissioning engineers, system operators.
rows.source_job: Verify safety interlocks and shutdown functions
rows.stmt: Reduce the time from emergency-stop actuation until liquid transfer and hazardous motion cease
rows.imp_rat: Though emergency-stop response time is inherently safety-critical, the batch ceiling forces both proxies low; scored at the 2.0 cap despite genuinely higher real-world stakes.
rows.sat_rat: Hardwired safety-rated e-stop circuits are a mature, code-driven practice that reliably halts transfer and motion quickly, so this need is well served. Emergency-stop response time is validated during commissioning and is inherently fast by design. E-stop verification follows well-defined procedures requiring modest specialized skill. Standard safety-rated hardware delivers rapid shutdown without significant added cost.

rows.stk: Safety engineers, commissioning engineers, system operators.
rows.source_job: Verify safety interlocks and shutdown functions
rows.stmt: Reduce the percent of representative loss-of-containment conditions not detected by containment sensors
rows.imp_rat: Undetected loss-of-containment conditions carry real hazard weight, but per the batch ceiling importance cannot exceed 2.0; held at cap.
rows.sat_rat: Leak and containment detection is often partial and difficult to test across all failure modes, so meaningful detection gaps persist. Containment-sensor coverage testing adds moderate time to the validation program. Determining representative loss-of-containment scenarios and confirming detection needs specialized process-safety expertise that is scarce. Undetected containment loss risks costly spills and environmental exposure, justifying but not always driving thorough testing.

rows.stk: Safety engineers, commissioning engineers, system operators.
rows.source_job: Verify safety interlocks and shutdown functions
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of tested hazardous conditions producing missing or incorrect alarm indications
rows.imp_rat: Missing or incorrect alarm indications for hazardous conditions matters for safety, but the batch ceiling constrains both proxies; capped at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Alarm testing is standard but alarm-to-condition mapping errors are common enough that incorrect indications still appear during qualification. Alarm verification is included in commissioning testing without major schedule impact. Confirming correct alarm indication for each hazardous condition requires methodical test planning that leans on experienced engineers. Missing or wrong alarms rarely cause immediate cost but raise operational risk cheaply mitigated by testing.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of site-specific requirements omitted from the customization assessment
rows.imp_rat: Omitting site-specific requirements from a customization assessment can cause fit issues, but it is a specification-completeness error ranked lower here and kept under 2.5.
rows.sat_rat: Site-specific requirement capture is often incomplete because facility inputs are gathered informally, leaving recurring omissions. Compiling site-specific requirements for customization assessment takes coordination time across facility stakeholders. Capturing all site-specific constraints demands cross-disciplinary facility knowledge that is often fragmented. Omitted site requirements cause expensive field modifications and interface rework at installation.

rows.stk: Process engineers, procurement managers, capital project teams.
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that plant configurations lacking required sterile manufacturing capabilities remain under consideration
rows.imp_rat: Configurations lacking sterile capabilities remaining under consideration is an early design-screening error with low relevance to executed processing and low, easily corrected consequences. Kept below the cap.
rows.sat_rat: Requirement checklists usually screen these out, but ambiguous or optimistic vendor claims still let unfit configurations stay under consideration at times. Flagging non-sterile-capable configurations early is quick when requirements are documented up front. Recognizing that a configuration lacks required sterile capability requires sterile-process knowledge that is not uniformly present. Rejecting unfit configurations early is cheap and avoids wasted downstream evaluation.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the plant delivery path or installation envelope is obstructed at arrival
rows.imp_rat: An obstructed delivery path or installation envelope at arrival is a one-time logistics/installation snag (very low relevance to ongoing processing) causing schedule delay only (low severity). Kept low, under 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Standard rigging surveys and 3D clearance checks make path obstruction uncommon, though last-minute site changes still cause surprises. Delivery-path surveys are done ahead of arrival, but a missed obstruction causes significant on-day delays. Route and envelope surveying is routine rigging work needing moderate, widely available logistics skill. Blocked delivery means idle cranes and crews, so pre-arrival path checks are cost-justified and common.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of plant records transferred without required batch and source context
rows.imp_rat: Plant records transferred without batch and source context is a traceability/documentation error with limited immediate operational impact (relevance) and generally recoverable consequences (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Data-historian and MES practices help, but records transferred without full batch/source context remain a common gap in preparation. Ensuring records carry batch and source context adds data-entry and verification time. Preserving traceability context depends on disciplined operators and configured systems rather than rare skill. Missing context is cheap to add up front but expensive to reconstruct later.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an approved recipe is rejected because its structure does not meet plant control system import requirements
rows.imp_rat: A recipe rejected for failing import-format requirements is a data-structure/administrative delay with low relevance to the core job and minor, recoverable consequences. Kept well within the 3.0 cap.
rows.sat_rat: Import validation tools and templates catch most structural mismatches up front, though schema version differences still cause occasional rejections at import. Validating recipe structure against import requirements before loading adds a preparation step but is quick once tooled. Formatting recipes to control-system schema needs automation engineers familiar with the platform, a moderately specialized skill. Reworking a rejected recipe costs engineering time but little material.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of modules assembled in positions or orientations that conflict with the plant layout drawings
rows.imp_rat: Modules assembled in positions conflicting with layout drawings cause rework, which is inconvenient but rarely affects final product quality (severity) and is peripheral to the processing job itself (relevance).
rows.sat_rat: Layout drawings and dimensional checks are standard practice, making orientation/position conflicts fairly reliably avoided. Checking module placement against layout drawings is a straightforward installation-verification step. Positioning modules per layout drawings is a well-understood rigging and installation task. Correcting a misplaced module is costly, so incentive to verify up front is strong.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of instruments damaged during mounting, piping, or wiring
rows.imp_rat: Instruments damaged during mounting/piping/wiring cause localized rework with limited centrality to the plant's core job and low severity; scored low.
rows.sat_rat: Handling procedures and QC inspection reduce damage, but mounting, piping and wiring mishaps still occur at a noticeable rate. Careful handling and staged installation add modest time but avoid rework delays. Avoiding installation damage depends on skilled, careful fitters and correct handling practice. Damaged instruments mean direct replacement cost and wasted labour.

rows.stk: Environmental health and safety personnel, waste management contractors.
rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of retained chemical residue streams assigned an incorrect regulatory classification
rows.imp_rat: Misclassifying retained chemical residue streams is a regulatory/administrative error with only modest severity and almost no relevance to executing the liquid batching job itself.
rows.sat_rat: Classification relies on manual judgement against complex regulations, so incorrect assignments recur despite established waste-broker practice. Classifying retained residue streams involves slow manual review of SDS and regulatory tables that delays shipment. Correct regulatory classification requires scarce hazardous-waste specialists interpreting shifting rules. Misclassification triggers fines, rejected loads, and rehandling costs that hit the disposal budget hard.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which the specified batching system configuration falls short of the required batch-volume range
rows.imp_rat: A configuration falling short of the required batch-volume range limits capability, but it is a specification-fit defect peripheral to executing the CFJ (relevance low) and its consequences are capped by the batch-3 ordering.
rows.sat_rat: Batch-volume range requirements are usually explicit and reliably checked, with only occasional shortfalls. Checking specified configuration against the batch-volume range is a quick verification against the URS. Confirming batch-volume coverage is a fairly routine engineering check requiring modest specialized skill. Configuration shortfalls mean costly upgrades, but the verification cost is minimal.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of rental candidates missing qualification, cleaning, or material-compatibility records required for sterile fill-finish use
rows.imp_rat: Missing qualification or material-compatibility records on rental candidates is a documentation gap peripheral to executing a batch; relevance is low and severity contained since it concerns procurement records rather than direct batch preparation.
rows.sat_rat: Rental fleets routinely lack the full sterile-use documentation, making this one of the least reliably served needs in acquisition. Assembling qualification, cleaning and compatibility records for rental candidates is slow and often stalls on missing paperwork. Judging whether a rental unit's records suffice for sterile fill-finish use demands scarce quality expertise applied to unfamiliar assets. Missing rental records force costly requalification or disqualification, and chasing them consumes staff effort.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that required sterile fill-finish capabilities omitted from the quoted scope remain undetected
rows.imp_rat: Undetected omission of sterile fill-finish scope from a quote is a quoting/scoping error somewhat tangential to physically batching liquid (low relevance); consequences are contractual (moderate severity). Held to ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Omissions of unstated capabilities are notoriously hard to catch and standard practice frequently misses them until commissioning. Detecting capabilities silently omitted from quoted scope requires slow, line-by-line scope reconciliation. Recognizing that a required capability is simply absent from a quote demands deep familiarity with what a complete sterile system must include. Undetected scope gaps become expensive post-award additions, while the checking effort itself is modest.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required regulators, insurers, utility providers, and internal stakeholders omitted from the notification list
rows.imp_rat: Omitting regulators/insurers/stakeholders from a notification list is an administrative coordination lapse, peripheral to the plant's core functional job with modest recoverable impact. Capped at 2.5.
rows.sat_rat: Standard notification checklists cover the obvious parties, but obscure utility providers or internal stakeholders are still routinely missed. Compiling and issuing notifications to all stakeholders is a bounded but easily delayed task. Knowing the full statutory notification list requires regulatory and contractual familiarity. Omitting a notification can trigger penalties or continued billing, making the small effort cost-effective.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of recoverable components damaged during removal
rows.imp_rat: Recoverable components damaged during removal is a decommissioning/salvage-value waste error with low relevance to core processing and minor economic severity. Capped at 2.5.
rows.sat_rat: When resale value is a priority careful removal works, but demolition speed pressures mean damage during removal is common and inconsistent. Careful non-destructive removal of recoverable components is slower than cut-and-scrap dismantling. Preserving component value during removal depends on skilled dismantlers who know how to detach without damage. Damaged components lose resale/reuse value, directly eroding recovered asset value.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required tank compliance records missing from the documentation package
rows.imp_rat: Missing compliance records in the documentation package is an administrative defect with compliance implications but low centrality to the processing job, held under the 2.5 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Turnover-package checklists are standard practice in regulated procurement and catch most gaps, though a few records commonly arrive incomplete. Assembling the documentation package is a defined deliverable, though chasing missing records can delay closeout. Knowing the full required record set (MTRs, weld logs, calibration certs) demands document-control expertise that is usually available in regulated buyers. Missing records rediscovered late incur re-issuance fees and audit findings but rarely large capital cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that cited certifications are inapplicable to the tank configuration or installation jurisdiction
rows.imp_rat: Certifications inapplicable to the configuration or jurisdiction is a documentation defect with compliance consequences, but peripheral to the processing job and constrained under 2.5.
rows.sat_rat: Established code-compliance practice catches most certification issues, but jurisdiction-specific and configuration-specific mismatches still surface occasionally. Checking certification applicability adds a documentation-review step but seldom delays the schedule materially. Judging whether ASME/PED/ -A certifications actually apply to the configuration and jurisdiction requires regulatory literacy many buyers lack. An inapplicable certification discovered at inspection or install triggers re-certification costs and delays.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required tank conditions or process events omitted from the batch record
rows.imp_rat: Omitting required conditions or events from the batch record is a recordkeeping defect with traceability consequences, but low centrality to the processing job and held under 2.5.
rows.sat_rat: Where automated capture is configured, omissions are rare, but gaps in instrumentation coverage and manual entries still let required conditions or events slip out of the record. Automated historians and electronic batch records capture timestamped events continuously without adding operator time. Automated data capture removes reliance on operator diligence for most parameters, though manually logged events still depend on attentiveness. EBR and historian systems carry setup cost but are widely deployed, so recording completeness is not primarily a cost problem.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of sterile fill-finish production unavailability beyond the maintenance window
rows.imp_rat: Prolonged sterile fill-finish unavailability beyond the maintenance window is downstream of batching and only moderately central to the batching CFJ (relevance low); consequences can be real but the ranking places this below batch 2, capping severity.
rows.sat_rat: Return-to-service timing is unpredictable because unexpected findings during maintenance frequently extend the outage. Overruns of production unavailability are common because re-qualification and cleaning after maintenance take longer than planned. Restoring sterile production quickly hinges on skilled technicians and QA staff whose availability is limited. Each extra hour of sterile fill-finish downtime carries very high batch-value cost that current practice poorly contains.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required batching-system maintenance tasks omitted from the maintenance schedule
rows.imp_rat: Omitting required maintenance tasks from the schedule is an administrative planning defect with limited centrality to the immediate batching job; relevance and severity both stay low as consequences are deferred and correctable.
rows.sat_rat: CMMS-driven PM schedules capture most routine tasks, though gaps for less common components still recur. Omitted tasks eventually surface as unplanned work that consumes time later. Building a complete task list depends on planner knowledge of every component's requirements. Missed tasks lead to premature failures and rework costs that a complete schedule would avoid.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that maintenance due dates are calculated from incomplete or inaccurate operating and condition data
rows.imp_rat: Calculating maintenance due dates from incomplete condition data is an administrative reliability error, tangential to the core batching job; relevance is low and severity mild since it affects scheduling accuracy rather than immediate batch quality.
rows.sat_rat: Many systems still rely on calendar intervals with incomplete runtime/condition inputs, so due-date accuracy is frequently compromised. Due dates computed from stale data can trigger premature or late work, disrupting timing. Judging whether underlying condition data is complete requires expertise that few maintenance planners apply consistently. Bad due-date math causes either over-maintenance or failure-driven repairs, both costly.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of batching-system unavailability beyond the scheduled maintenance window
rows.imp_rat: Batching-system downtime beyond the scheduled window affects availability but is an uptime concern rather than the accuracy of preparation itself (relevance low); severity constrained by the mandatory ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Standard work packages and spares kitting help, but overrun frequency remains a persistent, only partly mitigated issue. Maintenance overruns beyond the scheduled window happen regularly when parts or unexpected faults appear mid-job. Bringing the batching system back on time depends on experienced maintainers who can diagnose and correct quickly. Extended outages consume overtime labor and lost production capacity that current practice only partly limits.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that critical diagnostic alerts go unnoticed during production
rows.imp_rat: Unnoticed diagnostic alerts touch on reliability, but as an alert-monitoring concern it is somewhat removed from the core preparation job (relevance low) and severity is bounded by the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Alarm systems exist and are configured, but alarm flooding and inconsistent acknowledgment mean critical alerts still slip through. Alerts surface in near real time on SCADA, but operator response can lag during busy production. Noticing and correctly triaging critical alerts depends heavily on attentive, trained operators amid many competing signals. Missed critical alerts can cause expensive damage, though the alerting infrastructure itself is low cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of false diagnostic alerts that obscure actionable batching system faults
rows.imp_rat: False diagnostic alerts obscuring real faults is a reliability/noise defect somewhat removed from the preparation job (relevance low); consequences bounded under the mandatory ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Alarm rationalization is known practice but poorly implemented at many sites, so false alerts commonly obscure real faults. False alerts waste responder time chasing non-issues during otherwise productive periods. Distinguishing genuine faults from nuisance alarms requires experienced judgement that varies by operator. Nuisance alarms drive unnecessary inspection labor and desensitize staff at ongoing cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of process trends being misinterpreted as normal equipment behavior
rows.imp_rat: Misinterpreting process trends as normal is a diagnostic-ambiguity error at the margin of the core job (low relevance), and mistakes are usually caught downstream (low severity). Capped per batch rule.
rows.sat_rat: Historian trending exists but interpretation is manual and inconsistent, so normal-looking drift is routinely missed. Trend drift is slow and easily unnoticed until a failure forces attention, wasting lead time. Correctly reading process trends as early degradation demands scarce analytical expertise rarely present at the operator level. Misread trends lead to late interventions and higher repair cost, but trend tools themselves are cheap.

rows.stk: Plant operators, shift supervisors, training coordinators.
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of conflicts between training instructions and the installed plant configuration or approved operating procedures
rows.imp_rat: Conflicts between training instructions and installed configuration are a documentation-alignment error, peripheral to processing and with low severity since operators can reconcile against procedures.
rows.sat_rat: As-built documentation control helps, but training content routinely lags late configuration changes, leaving recurring conflicts. Reconciling training materials with as-built configuration is often deferred, causing rushed updates near go-live. Aligning documentation to the installed plant requires SMEs who understand both procedures and the actual configuration. Conflicts drive operator errors and retraining cost, yet documentation alignment is frequently under-budgeted.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration that plant output exceeds downstream acceptance capacity after adjustment
rows.imp_rat: Plant output exceeding downstream acceptance capacity after adjustment is a short-lived flow-balancing issue (low relevance) with recoverable throughput consequences (low severity). Comfortably below 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Feedforward and rate-limiting help but overshoot on transitions remains inconsistent, especially across grade or rate changes. Output overshoot beyond downstream acceptance after adjustment persists until control loops settle, and this lag is a recurring gap. Anticipating overshoot and coordinating with downstream capacity depends heavily on operator judgement. Product produced above downstream capacity is diverted, recycled or lost, incurring waste cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of liquid product discarded while transitioning between throughput levels
rows.imp_rat: Liquid product discarded during throughput transitions is a yield-waste inefficiency with peripheral relevance and low, absorbable severity; scored near the bottom.
rows.sat_rat: Rate-transition procedures reduce discard but the amount varies with product and how cleanly the change is executed. Product discarded during throughput transitions is tied to how long the transition takes to complete. Minimizing transition losses depends on operator finesse in ramping and diversion timing. Off-spec liquid discarded between throughput levels is a direct and recurring material loss only partly recovered by recycle.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required flow, temperature, or pressure readings omitted from each run record
rows.imp_rat: Omitting flow, temperature, or pressure readings from a run record is a documentation completeness error with limited direct effect on the processing job (relevance) and modest, recoverable consequences (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Where automated historians tag required points, omissions are rare, though manually logged parameters and sensor gaps still cause missing entries. Automated historians capture flow, temperature and pressure continuously, so omission adds no time burden. Automatic logging removes the operator vigilance formerly needed to capture every required reading. Data historian infrastructure is a standard sunk cost that eliminates most manual recording effort.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of residual liquid streams with an unresolved handling designation during operation
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved handling designation for residual liquid streams is an ambiguity/planning gap with peripheral relevance to the core job and minor severity; scored near the bottom.
rows.sat_rat: Many residual streams are pre-designated in procedures, but off-normal or new streams routinely lack a clear handling rule during operation. Unresolved handling designations force operators to pause and decide, adding delay mid-operation. Classifying an unexpected residual stream on the fly is a judgement-heavy task with little tooling support. Mishandled or defaulted residual streams can trigger disposal cost or product loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that concurrent streams require the same shared plant resource simultaneously
rows.imp_rat: Concurrent streams contending for the same shared resource is a scheduling contention issue (low relevance) causing bounded delay (low severity). Kept low within the 3.0 cap.
rows.sat_rat: Static schedules and interlocks reduce clashes but real-time deviations still produce unplanned simultaneous demand on shared resources. Contention for a shared resource forces one stream to wait, adding delay that scheduling only partly prevents. Avoiding simultaneous demand on shared headers, tanks or CIP skids relies on scheduler expertise and operator coordination. Resource clashes idle equipment and streams, wasting capacity, though the direct material cost is modest.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required process specifications omitted from the proposal request
rows.imp_rat: Omitting process specifications from a proposal request is a procurement-phase administrative gap with low centrality to the operating job and easily corrected consequences. Capped at 2.5.
rows.sat_rat: RFP templates and spec checklists are mature and catch most requirements, though niche viscous-sterile items still slip through. Drafting the proposal request from prior templates is reasonably quick, though completeness checks add time. Procurement and engineering staff can populate most standard specs, but capturing sterile-viscous specifics still needs expert input. Omitted specs trigger change orders and rebids that inflate acquisition cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of ambiguity in plant acceptance criteria for sterile fill-finish capability
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity in sterile fill-finish acceptance criteria is a specification-clarity issue relevant during qualification but administrative in nature and low in immediate operational stakes. Capped at 2.5.
rows.sat_rat: Acceptance language for sterile viscous fill-finish is often vague, leaving pass/fail judgement inconsistent between purchaser and supplier. Negotiating unambiguous fill-finish acceptance criteria drags out contract and FAT/SAT planning. Writing crisp, testable acceptance criteria for sterile fill-finish is an expert task that few teams do consistently well. Ambiguous criteria cause disputes and rework whose cost falls on the purchaser.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of decommissioned system materials assigned incorrect disposal classifications
rows.imp_rat: Incorrect disposal classification of decommissioned materials is an end-of-life compliance task; it barely touches the active batching CFJ and its consequences are limited to regulatory paperwork.
rows.sat_rat: Material classification is guided by established waste-management practice but still error-prone for mixed-material pharma equipment. Sorting decommissioned materials into disposal classes manually slows the whole teardown. Distinguishing contaminated from clean, recyclable from hazardous material requires trained judgement that not every crew has. Wrong classification means paying hazardous rates for benign material or costly recalls of misrouted waste.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of inspection stoppage caused by hazardous stored energy in retired system components
rows.imp_rat: Inspection stoppage from stored energy in retired components (item 10) concerns decommissioned equipment, so relevance to active batching is minimal though the safety consequence is non-trivial. Well below ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Discharge procedures usually restore inspection quickly, but unexpected stored energy still causes intermittent unplanned stoppages. Stored energy in retired components forces inspection stoppages while it is bled down or made safe, directly costing schedule time. Rapidly and safely discharging stored energy to resume inspection requires trained technicians. The main loss is idle inspection time rather than materials.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of dismantled components lacking verified material composition and contamination status
rows.imp_rat: Dismantled components lacking verified material and contamination status is a decommissioning-record ambiguity with peripheral relevance and minor severity; scored near the bottom.
rows.sat_rat: Because verification is sampled rather than comprehensive, many components leave the site with assumed rather than verified status, a chronic and largely unmitigated gap. Sampling and lab characterization of each dismantled component adds significant delay, so crews often batch or skip components under schedule pressure. Correctly identifying material composition and contamination status demands scarce hazmat and metallurgical expertise that is rarely available on-site throughout a teardown. Per-component assay and lab testing is expensive, so status-quo practice tests representative samples rather than every item, leaving gaps.

rows.stk: Process engineers, procurement leads, project managers.
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of overlooking a required batch-size or operating-range mismatch during evaluation
rows.imp_rat: Overlooking a batch-size or operating-range mismatch during evaluation is an assessment-defect step upstream of batching (relevance low); consequences bounded by the batch-3 ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Operating-range mismatches are sometimes overlooked in evaluation because they hide in detailed specifications. Catching batch-size or operating-range mismatches during evaluation requires deliberate cross-checking that adds review time. Recognizing subtle operating-range mismatches depends on an experienced evaluator's attention to detail. Overlooked mismatches lead to expensive rework, while the evaluation check is low-cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of shift handoffs that omit current batch status or pending actions
rows.imp_rat: Shift handoffs omitting batch status is a communication lapse peripheral to the physical preparation task (low relevance) with typically recoverable consequences (low severity). Held to ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Shift handoff still relies largely on verbal exchange and paper logs, so omission of current batch status or pending actions is common and poorly systematized. Handoffs that omit pending actions cost the incoming shift time to rediscover the state. Effective handoff depends heavily on outgoing-operator diligence and communication skill rather than any system. Missed handoff information can cause batch errors and rework, an occasional but significant cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percent of prior-batch residue remaining on wetted surfaces after rinsing
rows.imp_rat: Prior-batch residue on wetted surfaces after rinsing is a cross-contamination concern, but the ranking constrains both relevance and severity to 2 as this item sits below the prior batch.
rows.sat_rat: Validated CIP with conductivity/turbidity endpoint checks reliably removes prior-batch residue in most cases, though shadowed geometries and sticky products still leave occasional carryover. CIP cycles take fixed validated time and rarely need extension for cleanliness. Automated CIP removes most operator judgement from cleaning effectiveness. CIP consumes water, chemicals and energy, a known recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of rinse fluid remaining in the batching system when the next batch begins
rows.imp_rat: Residual rinse fluid when the next batch begins can dilute or alter concentration, but per the ceiling relevance and severity are held to 2 given its lower ranking position.
rows.sat_rat: Air blows and gravity drains handle most residual rinse, but pockets and dead legs leave variable holdup that can contaminate the next batch. Draining rinse fluid before the next batch adds a short verification step. Confirming the system is empty is routine, aided by drain valves and low-point sensors. Residual rinse fluid dilutes the next batch or wastes water and effluent capacity, a modest ongoing cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of batch lost through leakage or overflow during transfer
rows.imp_rat: Batch lost through leakage/overflow during transfer causes material waste (moderate severity) and touches the batching job (some relevance), but it sits below batch 2 in ranking. Capped.
rows.sat_rat: High-level switches and flow totalizers reliably prevent gross overflows, yet minor leakage and seal wear remain recurring sources of lost batch. Leak or overflow events during transfer trigger cleanup and re-batching that cost meaningful time. Avoiding overflow relies partly on correct level-sensor setup and operator vigilance where automation is incomplete. Level/flow instrumentation and high-level trips catch most overflow risk, but slow leaks and transfer losses still erode yield and generate waste.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percent of the prepared batch remaining in the source vessel after transfer
rows.imp_rat: Prepared batch remaining in the source vessel after transfer is a yield defect ranked below the prior batch; relevance and severity both stay at 2 as it concerns recoverable material rather than the core job's success.
rows.sat_rat: Transfer completeness is reasonably repeatable but clingy or high-viscosity products leave inconsistent amounts behind. Chasing the last of a transfer can extend pump-out time modestly. Achieving full transfer depends mostly on vessel and pump design, not operator finesse. Residual product left in the source vessel is a direct recurring yield loss, partly mitigated by conical bottoms and scraped surfaces.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of loss of liquid containment while independently actuating system functions
rows.imp_rat: Loss of containment while independently actuating functions is a commissioning/test-time event, peripheral to core batching (low relevance) with limited severity in that context. Capped at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Mature interlock logic, double-block-and-bleed valving and pressure/level permissives make containment loss during independent actuation an uncommon event in well-maintained systems. Interlock-mediated actuation adds minor sequencing delays but does not materially slow the operator's independent function control. Modern PLC-driven valve sequencing and permissive logic remove much of the manual judgement once needed to actuate functions without spilling. Containment failures during actuation are costly in lost product and cleanup, but engineered valve interlocks keep such events rare.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of batch material discarded after an incorrect recipe change
rows.imp_rat: Batch material discarded after an incorrect recipe change is a waste consequence with some batching relevance and moderate severity, but placed in the lower tier. Capped at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Recipe management systems with version control reduce errors, but confirmation gaps and manual overrides still let incorrect recipes reach production. Discovering an incorrect recipe change mid-batch forces a stop that consumes time before material can be salvaged or dumped. Preventing bad recipe changes depends on operator diligence and change-control discipline that varies widely by site. A wrong recipe change typically means the whole batch is scrapped, a large and direct material loss that current safeguards only partly prevent.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of recipe ingredient pathways not exercised during accuracy verification
rows.imp_rat: Untested ingredient pathways during accuracy verification is a verification-coverage defect; it affects assurance rather than the batching job directly (relevance low) and severity is bounded by the ranking below batch 2.
rows.sat_rat: Accuracy verification commonly samples representative paths rather than all of them, leaving untested pathways as a latent, poorly addressed reliability gap. Verifying every ingredient pathway extends validation testing time considerably. Designing coverage that exercises all pathways demands validation expertise that many operating teams lack. Unexercised pathways rarely cost money directly until an unverified route fails in production.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of introducing contamination into the sterile fill-finish area during functional testing
rows.imp_rat: Contamination introduced during functional testing is a test-phase risk peripheral to routine batching (low relevance); severity constrained here despite contamination stakes. Held to ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Aseptic protocols and environmental monitoring control most risk, but any intrusion into the fill-finish zone carries real, hard-to-eliminate contamination risk. Aseptic gowning, cleaning, and re-sanitization around testing add substantial time. Maintaining sterility during functional testing demands rigorous aseptic technique and constant vigilance from trained staff. A contamination breach can scrap batches and force costly line re-sterilization.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of unplanned interruption to sterile fill-finish activity caused by functional testing
rows.imp_rat: Unplanned interruption to sterile fill-finish from functional testing is a scheduling disruption peripheral to core batching (low relevance) with recoverable severity. Capped at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Scheduled maintenance windows and staged testing reduce disruption, but unplanned test-driven interruptions to sterile activity still recur. Functional testing that touches sterile fill-finish forces line stoppages that are hard to fully overlap with production. Coordinating testing to minimize interruption requires planning expertise but not rare technical skill. Lost sterile production time during interruptions is expensive per hour.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required events, deviations, or operator actions omitted from the official batch record
rows.imp_rat: Omitting events or deviations from the official batch record is a documentation-integrity error with compliance consequences, but it is administrative relative to the skid's functional job and sits below the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: EBR systems with mandatory fields reduce omissions substantially, but manual events and off-system actions are still frequently under-recorded, requiring QA follow-up. Electronic batch records prompt entries in sequence, saving time over paper reconciliation. Complete, accurate records still depend on operator diligence to log deviations and manual actions promptly. Missing record entries trigger costly investigations and delayed batch release.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a skid maintenance window conflicts with sterile fill-finish production or cleaning activities
rows.imp_rat: A maintenance window conflicting with sterile fill-finish is a scheduling error causing delay, but it is a coordination issue rather than central to skid function, constrained below the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Planning and scheduling systems coordinate most windows successfully, though last-minute production changes still create conflicts. Coordinating maintenance windows around fill-finish and cleaning schedules is the core scheduling challenge and often forces compressed or off-shift work. Scheduling requires planners who understand production and cleaning campaign constraints but not deep technical skill. Poor timing forces overtime or lost production slots, driving avoidable cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of mismatch between the backed-up configuration and the skid's validated state
rows.imp_rat: Mismatch between backed-up configuration and validated state matters for recovery integrity, but it is a supporting IT/config error rather than central to the skid delivering process function; consequences are recoverable and it sits below the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Backups are routinely taken but their match to the validated state is inconsistently verified, so mismatches surface only when a restore is attempted. Verifying a configuration backup against the validated state is a quick but often deferred check. Confirming backup fidelity needs automation-literate staff who understand the control system revision state. Backup storage and verification cost little, so the gap is procedural rather than financial.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of misidentifying the source of the skid fault before repair begins
rows.imp_rat: Misidentifying a skid fault source before repair adds rework time, but it is a troubleshooting-efficiency error with modest consequences, held below the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Alarms, diagnostics, and P&IDs help localize faults, but ambiguous symptoms and interacting failures still lead to misidentification fairly often. Misdiagnosis lengthens downtime through repeated troubleshooting cycles before the true fault is found.
