Accurate fault isolation depends heavily on experienced troubleshooters and remains highly expertise-dependent. Wrong diagnoses waste parts and labor and prolong lost production time.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of alignment drift after process loads are restored
rows.imp_rat: Alignment drift after process loads are restored is a minor mechanical error with limited direct impact on the core processing job (relevance) and typically low, gradual consequences (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Hot/cold offset practices are mature but drift after loads are restored still occurs where offsets were estimated rather than measured. Re-checking alignment after loads return requires a return visit that extends commissioning. Accounting for thermal and load-induced growth demands experienced alignment specialists who understand offset targets. Post-load misalignment drives premature bearing and coupling wear with real replacement costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of processed liquid lost through leakage or spillage during transfer
rows.imp_rat: Product lost through leakage or spillage during transfer is mostly a yield/waste concern (low relevance to core processing) with limited, recoverable consequences (low severity). Well within the 3.0 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Proper couplings, drip trays and containment keep transfer losses low, though minor leaks still occur. Cleaning up spills interrupts transfer briefly. Leak-tight connection making is standard operator competence. Leaked or spilled product is directly lost material plus cleanup expense.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of transfer stoppages caused by unavailable receiving capacity
rows.imp_rat: Transfer stoppages from unavailable receiving capacity are a material-handling bottleneck largely outside the processing core; consequences are minor throughput delays easily absorbed.
rows.sat_rat: Scheduling and surge storage mitigate most gaps, but capacity mismatches still cause stoppages. When receiving capacity is unavailable transfers stall, and this waiting is the core loss. Coordinating receiving capacity is a scheduling task requiring no rare skill. Stoppages idle crews and equipment, driving standby cost.

rows.stk: Process engineers, materials engineers, procurement leads.
rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of wetted components whose material identity is unverified
rows.imp_rat: Unverified wetted-component material identity risks compatibility and contamination issues (relevance) with quality consequences (severity), but the ceiling keeps it at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Material certificates cover many components but positive verification of every wetted part is inconsistently done, leaving unverified items. Verifying material identity via mill certs and testing adds documentation and lab time. Confirming actual wetted-component material identity needs metallurgical or PMI expertise beyond routine review. Material testing and traceability add cost, and unverified materials risk expensive failures.

rows.stk: Process engineers, materials engineers, procurement leads.
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of intended fluid chemistry and process requirements missing from the compatibility evaluation basis
rows.imp_rat: Missing fluid chemistry and process requirements from the compatibility basis can lead to material failures (relevance) and contamination (severity), but this batch is capped at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Compatibility evaluation bases are frequently incomplete because some process conditions get overlooked, a recurring reliability gap. Compiling all fluid chemistry and process requirements into the compatibility basis is an upfront effort that adds time. Knowing which chemistry and process conditions must be captured requires experienced process and materials specialists. Missing requirements in the basis cause costly compatibility failures, though compilation cost is modest.

rows.stk: Procurement leads, quality engineers, regulatory affairs personnel.
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that submitted certifications are expired, invalid, or outside the proposed batching system's scope
rows.imp_rat: Expired or out-of-scope certifications are a procurement/compliance-vetting error only loosely connected to running a batch (low relevance) and correctable before use (low severity). Capped at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Certificate expiry and scope checks are well-defined and reliably performed, though scope-mismatch subtleties occasionally slip by. Verifying certification validity and scope is a fast lookup against issuing bodies. Checking certificate currency and scope requires only modest training once the requirements are known. Certification verification is cheap and prevents costly acceptance of invalid credentials.

rows.stk: Procurement leads, quality engineers, regulatory affairs personnel.
rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of mismatch between compliance evidence and the proposed batching system configuration
rows.imp_rat: Mismatch between compliance evidence and system configuration is a documentation-alignment issue rather than a batching-function failure (low relevance), reconcilable without harm (low severity). Held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Evidence often pertains to similar-but-not-identical configurations, and this mismatch is only partially caught in practice. Matching compliance evidence to the exact proposed configuration adds a reconciliation step to review. Detecting that evidence covers a different configuration than proposed takes an attentive, knowledgeable reviewer. Configuration-evidence mismatches cause costly requalification, but checking is inexpensive.

rows.stk: Process engineers, project managers, procurement leads.
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of critical sterile fill-finish requirements omitted from the requirements baseline
rows.imp_rat: Missing sterile requirements in the baseline is a documentation error with downstream but indirect impact on the batching CFJ (low relevance) and manageable consequences (low severity). Capped at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: URS templates and regulatory checklists exist, but incomplete baselines remain a common root cause of downstream problems. Building a complete requirements baseline is an upfront effort that adds time before RFQ release. Assembling a complete critical-requirement set relies heavily on experienced engineers and quality staff to recall all applicable sterile requirements. Baseline development is mostly staff time, but omitted requirements drive costly late redesign.

rows.stk: Process engineers, project managers, procurement leads.
rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of mismatch between specified batching capacity and projected production demand
rows.imp_rat: Mismatch between batching capacity and projected demand is a planning-level defect that affects throughput but not the core act of preparing a batch correctly (relevance low); severity is bounded and ranked below the prior batch.
rows.sat_rat: Demand-to-capacity sizing is well-established practice, though uncertain demand forecasts still leave residual mismatch risk. Capacity sizing against demand projections is a routine calculation that adds little time. Matching batching capacity to demand uses standard process-engineering sizing methods that most engineers handle. Mismatched capacity wastes capital or forces re-procurement, but the analysis itself is inexpensive.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of fluid leaking from inlet, outlet, or recirculation piping joints during transfer
rows.imp_rat: Fluid leaking from piping joints during transfer touches batch delivery, yet the ranking places it below the prior batch so relevance and severity are both held at 2.
rows.sat_rat: Proven jointing methods and post-assembly testing make transfer-path leakage uncommon in mature installations. Joint leaks during transfer cause interruptions, but well-made connections rarely require in-service repair. Assembling leak-tight inlet, outlet, and recirculation joints is standard competent-fitter work with defined torque and gasket practice. In-service joint leaks waste process fluid and cleanup effort, though these are infrequent after testing.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of import delay caused by source records that cannot be interpreted
rows.imp_rat: Import delay from uninterpretable source records is peripheral to physically batching and preparing liquids (low relevance), and its consequences are a recoverable data-ingest slowdown (low severity). Capped at 2.0 per batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Import tooling handles clean records well but has weak tolerance for malformed inputs, so unparseable records reliably trigger delay. Uninterpretable source records force manual re-keying or clarification loops that directly extend import duration. Reconstructing meaning from malformed source data relies on people who know the legacy formats, a dependency that slows things when they are unavailable. Import delays consume labor but rarely destroy material, keeping direct cost moderate.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of assigning recipe-editing permissions beyond approved responsibilities
rows.imp_rat: Over-broad recipe-editing permissions is an access-control defect with some relevance to recipe integrity and moderate severity, but ranked below batch 2. Held to ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Least-privilege intent is widely accepted but in practice permissions are often granted generously to avoid rework, so scope creep on recipe editing remains a recurring gap. Restricting recipe-editing rights is a configuration step that adds little time when role templates exist. Deciding who legitimately needs recipe-edit authority requires governance judgement that is often delegated to overburdened administrators without full context. Over-broad edit rights create latent compliance and product-quality exposure that is expensive if exploited, though direct cost at setup is negligible.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of batching beyond the allowable processing window while the operator evaluates and responds to unexpected ingredient properties
rows.imp_rat: Batching beyond the allowable window while evaluating unexpected ingredient properties threatens the batch (relevance) and can force rework (severity), but it remains below the prior batch at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: There is little automated real-time detection of anomalous ingredient properties, so outcomes depend on human vigilance and vary widely. When an ingredient behaves unexpectedly the operator must pause and evaluate, and the batch frequently drifts past its processing window before a response is decided. Judging off-spec ingredient behavior demands seasoned process knowledge that is scarce, so the response is often slow and hesitant. Extended out-of-window holds risk scrapping the batch, a significant material and rework cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the designated sampling point is inaccessible or obstructed when the sample is required
rows.imp_rat: An inaccessible sampling point delays sample draw but rarely halts batching itself; relevance is low and severity limited to minor delay. Capped per batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Dedicated sampling valves and cleanable ports make points accessible most of the time, though fouling and poor design still cause occasional obstruction. An obstructed sample point delays the draw until cleared. Clearing or working around a blocked point needs some operator know-how but is generally routine. Redundant or cleanable sample points add modest design cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of an uncontrolled liquid release during the sample draw
rows.imp_rat: Uncontrolled liquid release during sampling poses some safety/hygiene risk (severity), but it is a peripheral event to the core batching job (low relevance). Capped at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Closed sampling valves and containment devices reliably prevent uncontrolled release, with residual risk mainly from seal failures or manual sample ports. Closed-loop and aseptic sampling devices make the draw quick. Using sealed sampling valves properly requires basic trained handling, not deep expertise. Closed sampling systems and single-use assemblies add cost but prevent loss and cleanup.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time the interrupted batch waits for required utilities after resumption is initiated
rows.imp_rat: The delay in an interrupted batch waiting for utilities after resumption is a peripheral timing concern for liquid batching; relevance is low since the core job proceeds regardless, and severity is minor as it merely extends recovery time without compromising product.
rows.sat_rat: Utility headers are generally kept ready so resumption is fairly predictable, with occasional waits when shared utilities are contended. After resumption is initiated, waiting for utilities like steam, chilled water or clean air to reach setpoint adds a modest recurring delay. Re-establishing utilities is a largely automated or routine operator step needing little expertise. Standby utility conditioning consumes energy but is a normal operating cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of batch-affecting manual interventions absent from the operational record
rows.imp_rat: Manual interventions missing from the operational record is a recordkeeping defect peripheral to executing preparation (relevance low); consequences bounded by the batch-3 ordering.
rows.sat_rat: Manual interventions are chronically under-recorded because logging still relies on operators remembering to note actions the control system never sees, making this a persistent weak spot. Capturing manual interventions in real time adds documentation burden that operators often defer. Complete intervention logging depends heavily on disciplined operator behavior and judgement about what to record. Missing intervention records rarely cost material but create expensive compliance and investigation gaps.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the batching cycle initiates before the operator's intentional start command
rows.imp_rat: A premature batch cycle start before the operator command is a control-timing error with some job relevance and safety severity, but the ranking places it below batch 2. Held to ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Deliberate two-step start confirmations and command latching are deeply embedded in control practice, so unintended cycle initiation before an operator command is very rare. Requiring an explicit start command imposes negligible time cost on the operator. Standard start-permissive and confirmation logic means no special skill is needed to prevent premature initiation. An unintended early start could waste a batch, but the safeguard is cheap and effectively universal.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that unresolved process or safety interlocks block cycle initiation
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved interlocks blocking cycle initiation causes startup delay tied to batching (some relevance) but consequences are recoverable delay (low severity). Capped at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Interlocks reliably do their protective job, but false or opaque blocks that stop legitimate starts remain a recurring nuisance that standard HMIs only partially clarify. Unresolved interlocks routinely stall cycle start until an operator hunts down the blocking condition, adding real delay. Diagnosing which interlock is unsatisfied often demands experienced operators or engineers because HMI messaging is frequently cryptic. Blocked starts waste labor and vessel availability but rarely destroy material outright.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of unavailable or frozen live process data during a batching cycle
rows.imp_rat: Frozen or unavailable live process data blinds operators mid-cycle (relevance) risking off-spec batches (severity), but this batch sits below the prior tier so it stays at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Watchdog timers and heartbeat monitoring make prolonged data loss rare, though legacy networks and single-point communication links still occasionally stall live values. Modern SCADA/PLC systems with redundant I/O and buffered historians keep live data refreshing in near real time, so frozen-data episodes are brief and uncommon on well-maintained lines. Recovering from a data freeze usually requires only a routine operator refresh or automatic failover rather than specialist intervention. Redundant controllers and historians carry capital cost but are standard on new batching installs, so the incremental cost of avoiding freezes is largely already sunk.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of gaps in active batch monitoring during operator handovers
rows.imp_rat: Monitoring gaps at operator handovers can let excursions go undetected (relevance) with quality consequences (severity), yet the ordering ceiling holds this at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Automated alarms and trends provide baseline continuity across handovers, but active human attention still lapses during the overlap, leaving intermittent monitoring gaps. Monitoring gaps during handover are brief but can delay response to an evolving condition. Continuous coverage depends on staffing discipline and operator conscientiousness during the overlap. A monitoring gap that misses an excursion can cost a batch, though such events are occasional.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of finished batch released outside the intended transfer path during discharge
rows.imp_rat: Finished batch discharged outside the intended path is a waste/misroute defect with some batching relevance and moderate severity, but constrained to the ceiling for this tier.
rows.sat_rat: Automated route selection and interlocked transfer valves make wrong-path discharge uncommon, though manual valve lineups and cross-connection errors still occur. Misrouted discharge forces rerouting and rework that costs time, but correctly configured valve trains discharge without delay. Correct routing depends on valve-lineup verification that still leans on operator attention in manually configured plants. Product sent down the wrong path is often unrecoverable, making misrouting a direct material loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of finished batch remaining in the batching system after discharge
rows.imp_rat: Finished batch remaining in the system after discharge is a yield-loss waste error, moderately relevant but capped at 2 per the ranking, with severity limited to recoverable material loss.
rows.sat_rat: Well-designed discharge geometry gives fairly consistent residuals, though viscosity and foaming cause batch-to-batch variation. Draining residual heel adds a little discharge time but is not a major duration driver. Emptying the vessel fully is largely a matter of equipment design rather than operator skill. Sloped, drainable tank designs recover most product but a measurable heel of finished batch is still routinely lost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of process data being linked to an incorrect batch identifier for a production run
rows.imp_rat: Linking process data to the wrong batch identifier is a data-association ambiguity; traceability matters but it is not the act of batching (relevance low) and severity is held to the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: MES-driven batch-ID assignment and scanning make linking data to the wrong batch rare, though manual entry points remain a residual failure mode. Correct batch-ID linkage happens automatically at run start, adding no operator time. Barcode/automated batch-ID assignment removes most reliance on operators to key the right identifier. A mislinked batch can trigger costly investigations, but automated ID handling makes this uncommon.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required process data absent from the batch record for a production run
rows.imp_rat: Absent process data in the batch record affects traceability reporting rather than the core preparation function (low relevance), and gaps are typically reconstructable (low severity). Capped at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Electronic batch record systems enforce most required fields at capture, substantially reducing missing data, though manual and off-system entries still leave gaps. Chasing down missing required data before record release adds review time. Determining completeness against the required data set needs trained review staff. Incomplete batch records can delay release and tie up inventory, an indirect but real cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of a stored batch record becoming inaccessible when needed for traceability or reporting
rows.imp_rat: An inaccessible stored batch record impairs later reporting, not the act of batching liquids (low relevance), and retrieval failures are usually recoverable via backups (low severity). Held to ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Standard database storage with backups and redundancy makes records reliably accessible, so inaccessibility mainly arises from rare archival or migration failures. Retrieving a stored record is normally near-instant, but recovery after a system fault can be slow. Routine retrieval needs little skill, though restoring from backup requires IT expertise. Backup infrastructure carries ongoing cost but averts far larger losses from lost records.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that assessed interfaces differ from the system configuration being acquired
rows.imp_rat: Assessed interfaces differing from the acquired configuration is a specification-matching detail marginal to the CFJ (low relevance); mismatches are usually caught and corrected (low severity), capped at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Configurations drift during negotiation and documents are rarely re-baselined, so assessed interfaces commonly diverge from what is actually acquired. Verifying interfaces against the exact configuration being bought adds review cycles that slow specification sign-off. Matching assessed interfaces to the acquired system takes detailed engineering judgement about utilities, controls and mechanical ties. Interface mismatches are cheap to note on paper but costly to fix if carried into procurement.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which trial materials and operating conditions differ from intended sterile fill-finish conditions
rows.imp_rat: Trial materials/conditions differing from intended sterile conditions affects validation fidelity but sits below the prior batch in importance, so relevance and severity are held at 2 to stay at or below the 2.0 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Trials at vendor sites routinely use surrogate materials and non-representative conditions, so the outcome poorly predicts real production. Arranging trials that mimic true sterile fill-finish conditions is time-consuming and often abbreviated to hit schedule. Designing representative trial conditions demands deep aseptic process expertise that is thinly staffed at the acquisition stage. Full-fidelity trials with real materials and sterile conditions are expensive, driving substitution of cheaper surrogates.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of mismatch between trial conditions and sterile fill-finish production conditions
rows.imp_rat: Mismatch between trial and production conditions is a validation-representativeness concern; under the batch ceiling both proxies are held at 2, keeping importance at or below 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Persistent mismatches between trial and actual sterile production conditions leave performance uncertain until the system is installed. Reconciling trial versus production conditions requires extra iterations that lengthen acceptance testing. Judging whether trial and production conditions truly match needs experienced sterile process specialists. Closing the condition gap means costly on-site or full-scale runs that budgets often will not cover.

rows.stk: Receiving/logistics staff, procurement team, site installation coordinator.
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that transit damage remains undetected before shipment acceptance
rows.imp_rat: Undetected transit damage before acceptance is an inspection-gap defect peripheral to batch preparation execution; consequences could surface later but the batch ceiling holds both proxies at the maximum allowed.
rows.sat_rat: Shock/tilt indicators and receiving inspection catch much damage, but concealed damage still sometimes escapes acceptance. Incoming inspection on receipt is a defined step but can be rushed to keep installation on schedule. Detecting concealed transit damage needs experienced inspectors who know what to check on complex systems. Inspection is modest labor cost and shock indicators are inexpensive.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of deviation from level after the batch preparation system is anchored
rows.imp_rat: Level deviation after anchoring is a physical-setup detail with limited centrality to batch preparation itself (low relevance); typical deviations are correctable and low-consequence (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Grouting, anchor procedures, and re-verification make post-anchor level deviation infrequent, though anchor settling can still cause minor drift. Final level checks after anchoring are quick and integrated into the setting procedure. Verifying and correcting post-anchor level is standard millwright work requiring no scarce expertise. Post-anchor re-leveling is cheap unless anchors must be redone, which is uncommon with proper technique.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of deviation from required level tolerance after anchoring
rows.imp_rat: Deviation from level tolerance after anchoring is an installation-quality detail marginal to the CFJ (low relevance); minor level errors are usually correctable during commissioning (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Precision levels, laser instruments, and adjustable feet make meeting level tolerance a mature, highly repeatable practice. Leveling during anchoring is a fast, routine task with common tools and rarely delays installation. Achieving level tolerance uses well-understood techniques any competent millwright applies with precision instruments. Shims and leveling hardware are inexpensive and re-leveling costs little, so the financial impact is minor.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required operator or maintenance access paths blocked by the final system position
rows.imp_rat: Blocked operator/maintenance access paths from final positioning is a spatial-arrangement error peripheral to batch prep (low relevance) and typically resolvable by repositioning (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Layout reviews and clearance standards catch many conflicts, but blocked access paths remain a common residual problem at final positioning. Blocked access paths found after installation force rework that can significantly disrupt the schedule. Preventing blocked paths depends on planners anticipating maintenance and operator movement, which requires experienced foresight. Moving equipment to clear an access path is expensive, and this correction recurs often enough to matter on dense installations.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree to which the final position violates workflow or maintenance clearances
rows.imp_rat: Final position violating workflow/maintenance clearances is a layout-ergonomics issue marginal to producing batches (low relevance); it causes inconvenience but is reworkable (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: 3D layout models and clearance checks help, but field reality and last-minute changes still produce recurring clearance violations. Clearance issues discovered after placement can trigger time-consuming repositioning of heavy equipment. Preserving clearances relies on planners reading layout drawings correctly, and oversights occur when layouts are done under pressure. Repositioning to recover clearance is costly in rigging and downtime, and such rework is not rare on congested sites.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of undissolved active or excipient particles remaining at the blend endpoint
rows.imp_rat: Undissolved particles at endpoint directly defeats the dissolve job (some relevance/severity), but the mandated ceiling caps it below the prior batch.
rows.sat_rat: Validated dissolution plus inspection usually leaves few undissolved particles. Confirming full dissolution takes inspection time. Detecting residual particles depends on operator observation. Undissolved particles risk rejection or filtration cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of active ingredient degraded during blending
rows.imp_rat: Active-ingredient degradation during blending harms potency (some severity), but the ranking keeps this item's proxies low.
rows.sat_rat: Validated gentle conditions keep active degradation during blending low. Limiting blend time protects the active but is set by validation. Managing shear/temperature to protect active needs know-how. Degraded active is a direct potency loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of blending required to meet bulk-solution homogeneity criteria
rows.imp_rat: Blending duration to meet homogeneity relates to the core job (some relevance) but is a throughput concern with minor consequence, capped by the ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Validated blend times reliably achieve homogeneity even if lengthy. Homogeneity blending time is set by validation and can be long. Optimizing blend time safely needs process knowledge. Extra blend time consumes energy and occupancy.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of component material not transferred into the vessel during charging
rows.imp_rat: Untransferred component material during charging can affect blend composition (some relevance) but is largely a yield-loss waste with minor consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Transfer losses are consistent but only partly recoverable by rinsing. Rinsing to complete transfer adds time. Quantitative transfer technique reduces losses. Component not transferred is a direct yield loss on every charge.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of unplanned agitator stoppages during compounding
rows.imp_rat: Unplanned agitator stoppages affect blending (some relevance) but this reliability item is ranked below the prior batch, keeping proxies low.
rows.sat_rat: Maintained drives run steadily, making unplanned agitator stoppages uncommon. Unplanned stoppages force restart delays. Preventing stoppages depends on maintenance, not runtime skill. Stoppages risk re-blend costs and downtime.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of charged solids remaining undissolved at the dissolution endpoint
rows.imp_rat: Solids undissolved at endpoint directly undermines dissolution (some relevance/severity), but the mandated ceiling caps this item below the prior batch.
rows.sat_rat: Validated dissolution reliably leaves little undissolved solid for most formulations. Confirming full dissolution at endpoint adds inspection time. Judging complete dissolution relies on operator observation. Undissolved solids risk rejection or filtration loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of active or excipient material degraded during dissolution
rows.imp_rat: Material degradation during dissolution affects product quality (some severity), yet the mandate holds this item below the prior batch.
rows.sat_rat: Validated temperature/time windows keep dissolution degradation low. Controlling dissolution conditions to protect material adds no direct time. Protecting sensitive materials needs process knowledge. Degradation during dissolution is a direct potency loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration that liquid temperature remains outside the validated dissolution range
rows.imp_rat: Temperature outside the validated range during dissolution touches the core job (some relevance) but is ranked and capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Controlled jacket loops quickly return temperature to the dissolution range. Time with temperature off dissolution range extends processing. Automated jacket control needs little skill to correct. Off-range temperature slows dissolution, costing time.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration that liquid pH remains outside the validated dissolution range
rows.imp_rat: pH outside the validated range affects dissolution (some relevance) but this duration item is ranked below the prior batch, keeping proxies low.
rows.sat_rat: Manual pH adjustment leaves solutions off-range for variable periods. Time spent with pH out of range extends dissolution. pH adjustment requires trained titration judgement. Prolonged off-range pH risks degradation loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of charged solids retained on vessel surfaces instead of entering the liquid medium
rows.imp_rat: Solids retained on surfaces rather than dissolving affects yield/composition (some relevance) but is chiefly a waste item; ranked and capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Wall retention varies with charging method and is only partly controlled. Rinsing down wall-retained solids adds time. Preventing wall caking needs careful charging technique. Solids stuck on walls instead of dissolving is a direct yield loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the time required to reach the confirmed dissolution endpoint
rows.imp_rat: Time to reach dissolution endpoint touches the core job (some relevance) but is a throughput matter with minor consequence; ranking caps it below the prior batch.
rows.sat_rat: Validated conditions make reaching a confirmed endpoint dependable if slow. Dissolution endpoint is reached only after validated mixing time, which can be lengthy. Speeding dissolution safely needs process knowledge of temperature and shear. Longer dissolution consumes energy and occupancy time.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of dissolved components that precipitate before bulk-solution transfer
rows.imp_rat: Precipitation before transfer defeats homogeneity (some relevance/severity), yet the ranking holds this instability item below the prior batch.
rows.sat_rat: Controlled temperature and hold times keep pre-transfer precipitation uncommon. Prompt transfer limits precipitation time. Preventing precipitation needs formulation knowledge. Precipitation is a direct potency and yield loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of charged components recorded with an incorrect identity, lot, or quantity
rows.imp_rat: Wrong identity/lot/quantity recorded is a serious traceability/composition error (some severity), but the ranking caps this item low.
rows.sat_rat: Manual transcription still yields identity/lot/quantity recording errors. Recording identity/lot/quantity accurately adds documentation time. Accurate recording depends on operator diligence. Recording errors drive investigations more than material loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of process excursions or interventions omitted from the batch record
rows.imp_rat: Excursions omitted from the batch record is a documentation-completeness/compliance concern with some severity but low relevance to the physical blend; capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Under event pressure, excursions still get omitted from the record. Documenting interventions adds time during events. Capturing events completely relies on operator discipline. Omitted events drive costly investigations.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of batch components added out of the specified sequence
rows.imp_rat: Out-of-sequence additions can affect dissolution/quality (some severity), yet ranked below the prior batch so proxies stay low.
rows.sat_rat: Sequenced records reduce but manual execution still allows sequence errors. Sequence verification adds brief checks. Following addition sequence needs operator attention. Out-of-sequence addition can scrap a batch.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of specified components omitted during vessel charging
rows.imp_rat: Omitting specified components directly corrupts the formulation (some relevance/severity), yet the ranking holds this item below the prior batch.
rows.sat_rat: Checkoff records reduce omissions but they still occur occasionally. Verifying all components added costs brief checks. Tracking completeness relies on operator attention. An omitted component can scrap the batch.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of batch components added more than once during vessel charging
rows.imp_rat: Double-added components corrupt formulation (some relevance/severity), but the mandated ceiling holds this defect item below the prior batch.
rows.sat_rat: Checkoff records reduce but do not eliminate duplicate additions. Verifying no double-add costs brief checks. Tracking additions relies on operator attention and record marks. A double addition can scrap the batch.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of temperature variation within the bulk solution during compounding
rows.imp_rat: Temperature variation during compounding relates to the dissolution job (some relevance) but this item is ranked below the prior batch, so proxies stay low.
rows.sat_rat: Jacketed vessels with agitation reliably minimize internal temperature variation. Jacket circulation and agitation reach uniform temperature with modest time. Little skill needed with jacketed circulation and mixing. Temperature gradients rarely waste material.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration that the batch remains outside the required temperature range
rows.imp_rat: Batch outside required temperature range relates to the blend (some relevance) but this duration item is ranked below the prior batch, so proxies remain low.
rows.sat_rat: Jacketed control reliably keeps batch temperature in range with short excursions. Time out of required temperature range is minimized by responsive control. Little operator skill needed with automated control. Off-range temperature risks degradation and rework.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of ambiguity in vessel pressure, temperature, agitation, or contents information
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity in pressure/temperature/agitation/contents info touches process control (some relevance) but is largely an information-clarity concern; ceiling caps it low.
rows.sat_rat: Instrumentation gives generally clear readings but gaps in resolution and display remain. Interpreting ambiguous readings can slow decisions. Resolving unclear indications requires experience. Ambiguity rarely wastes material directly.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of dissolution beyond the validated expectation
rows.imp_rat: Dissolution running beyond validated expectation relates to the core job (some relevance) but is chiefly a timing deviation with modest consequence; ceiling applies.
rows.sat_rat: Dissolution duration varies with material lots, causing occasional overruns. Dissolution running beyond expectation directly extends the batch. Managing conditions to hit expected dissolution time needs know-how. Extended dissolution wastes energy and time.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of batch material discarded because of contamination introduced at the vessel
rows.imp_rat: Discarding contaminated material has real cost and quality stakes (some severity), but the ranking mandate holds this waste item below the previous batch.
rows.sat_rat: Cleaning validation and closed processing keep such discards uncommon. Discarding and re-cleaning after contamination costs time. Preventing vessel-introduced contamination needs disciplined technique. Discarded contaminated batch material is a large direct loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of batch material rendered unusable by incorrect component quantities
rows.imp_rat: Material lost to incorrect quantities affects formulation correctness (some relevance) but the ceiling caps this waste item low regardless.
rows.sat_rat: Weighing checks make quantity-driven scrap uncommon. Reworking or discarding after quantity errors costs time. Accurate quantities depend on careful weighing. Material rendered unusable by wrong quantities is a direct loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of manual handling events that introduce operator-borne contamination into the batch
rows.imp_rat: Operator-borne contamination from manual handling has real quality stakes (some severity) but is held below the prior batch by the mandated ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Manual handling events remain a leading, only partly controlled contamination source. Careful handling to avoid operator-borne contamination adds time. Aseptic manual handling depends heavily on operator discipline. Operator-borne contamination can scrap a batch.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of batch exposure to the surrounding environment during charging or sampling
rows.imp_rat: Environmental exposure duration during charging/sampling is a contamination-risk driver (some severity) but held below the prior batch by the mandated ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Open charging/sampling exposure windows persist despite good practice. Faster, closed operations shorten environmental exposure but open steps still take time. Minimizing exposure duration needs efficient technique. Exposure risks contamination scrap, not direct spend.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that vessel intervention introduces contamination into the bulk solution
rows.imp_rat: Contamination from intervention threatens batch integrity (some severity), but per the mandated ceiling this item ranks below the prior batch so relevance and severity stay low.
rows.sat_rat: Any vessel opening for intervention raises contamination risk despite controls. Aseptic intervention procedures add time. Contamination-free intervention demands strong aseptic discipline. Intervention contamination can scrap the batch.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the excursion cause is misidentified before recovery action
rows.imp_rat: Misidentifying an excursion cause is a post-hoc investigation concern, peripheral to actually dissolving and blending components (low relevance); wrong recovery action can extend impact (modest severity), but ranking caps it below the prior batch.
rows.sat_rat: Cause identification is often guesswork in the moment, so misidentification is common. Diagnosing the excursion cause before acting takes time. Correct root-cause identification under time pressure is highly skill-dependent. Misdiagnosis leads to wrong action and possible batch loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required excursion actions or observations omitted or recorded inaccurately
rows.imp_rat: Omitted/inaccurate excursion actions is a record-integrity concern with compliance stakes (some severity) but peripheral to blending; capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Excursion documentation is often incomplete or inaccurate in the moment. Recording required excursion actions accurately adds time. Knowing and recording required actions needs training. Inaccurate records drive rework and investigations.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of material containers with missing or unreadable identity details at verification
rows.imp_rat: Unreadable container identity at verification risks mischarging (some severity) but is chiefly a documentation concern; ranked and capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Container labeling standards make missing/unreadable identity details uncommon. Chasing missing label details delays verification. Reading labels needs minimal skill. Unreadable labels cause holds, not direct material loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of material inputs with conflicting identity or lot details across labels and records
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting identity/lot details across records risks mischarging (some severity) but is chiefly a documentation concern; ranked and capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Label-to-record reconciliation reduces but does not eliminate identity conflicts. Reconciling conflicting label/record details takes time. Spotting label-record mismatches needs careful attention. Conflicts drive investigation and potential rework.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration the bulk solution remains outside validated compounding limits during the disruption
rows.imp_rat: Solution outside validated limits during disruption relates to the job (some relevance) but this duration item is ranked and capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Control systems and procedures usually pull conditions back promptly, but response duration remains variable. The longer the solution stays outside limits, the greater the delay to recovery; response speed varies. Rapid containment depends on experienced operators recognizing and acting on the excursion. Extended time out of limits raises batch-loss cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the rate of batches released despite evidence of unmet homogeneity or GMP criteria
rows.imp_rat: Releasing batches despite unmet homogeneity/GMP evidence is a serious disposition failure (severity), but it sits below the previous batch, so proxies remain capped at low values.
rows.sat_rat: Strong disposition gates and independent QA release make releasing against clear adverse evidence a rare, well-guarded failure. This is about wrong release decisions, not timing. Preventing release despite adverse evidence relies on disciplined disposition judgment. A wrongful release risks recall and remediation costs, but occurrence is rare.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of vessel, material, formulation, or control changes and deviations implemented without blend-quality risk evaluation
rows.imp_rat: Change control without risk evaluation is an administrative-governance error only loosely tied to blending the current batch (low relevance); consequences can be broad but the ranking caps it here.
rows.sat_rat: Change-control triggers usually mandate risk evaluation, but some changes and deviations still bypass it in practice. Risk evaluation adds lead time to changes but the error is skipping it. Deciding which changes and deviations warrant blend-quality risk evaluation needs expert judgment. Assessment effort is administrative cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the rate of nonconforming batches classified as meeting homogeneity specifications
rows.imp_rat: False acceptance of nonconforming batches carries patient-safety severity, but the ranking mandate places it below the prior batch so proxies are held low.
rows.sat_rat: Multiple test points and specification gates keep false-accept rates low, making this a comparatively well-guarded failure though never fully eliminable. Escaped nonconformances are about decision correctness, not timing. Detecting a nonconforming batch that looks conforming requires acute analytical and interpretive skill. An escaped nonconforming batch risks major recall and safety costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of vessel capability specified beyond defined formulation and process requirements
rows.imp_rat: Over-specified vessel capability is a design-economy issue, essentially irrelevant to achieving homogeneity.
rows.sat_rat: Over-specification does not harm outcomes but represents avoidable resource waste that is inconsistently caught. Trimming over-specification adds minor review time but avoids later delays. Distinguishing needed from excess capability requires value-engineering judgement. Capability specified beyond requirements directly wastes capital and operating cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of unnecessary restrictiveness in specified mixing requirements
rows.imp_rat: Over-restrictive mixing requirements cause mild inefficiency; minimal relevance to job success.
rows.sat_rat: Excess restrictiveness rarely causes failures but drives unnecessary cost that reviews only partly catch. Relaxing over-restrictive requirements takes modest review effort. Judging appropriate requirement tightness needs experienced specification skill. Overly restrictive requirements inflate cost and narrow vendor options.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that product or cleaning-agent residues remain above defined acceptance limits at worst-case vessel locations after cleaning
rows.imp_rat: Residues above limits at worst-case locations is a real contamination risk (some severity) but held below the prior batch by the mandated ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Cleaning validation with worst-case sampling is standard, but residues at hidden locations still occasionally exceed limits. Worst-case location residue testing extends cleaning validation. Identifying worst-case locations requires cleaning-validation expertise. Swab and rinse analytics add cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that microbial or endotoxin contamination persists in the vessel after cleaning
rows.imp_rat: Persisting microbial/endotoxin contamination has serious safety stakes (some severity), but the mandate caps this item below the prior batch.
rows.sat_rat: Sanitization validation reliably controls microbial persistence in most qualified vessels. Microbial and endotoxin verification requires incubation and testing time. Micro assessment relies on microbiology expertise that is generally available. Endotoxin and bioburden testing add cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the selected residue challenge understates the hardest-to-clean production residue
rows.imp_rat: Understated residue challenge weakens cleaning validation (some severity) but is a qualification-design concern remote from the blend; ranked and capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Worst-case selection is often subjective, so understated challenges recur and undermine cleaning claims. Selecting a defensible worst-case residue challenge adds study-design time. Choosing the hardest-to-clean residue is a judgement-heavy task dependent on scarce cleaning experts. Additional challenge studies consume material and analytics.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that compiled qualification evidence misrepresents the proposed vessel configuration or intended operating envelope
rows.imp_rat: Misrepresenting qualification evidence is a documentation-integrity error peripheral to the blend job (low relevance) though compliance-consequential; ranking caps it low.
rows.sat_rat: Change control and as-built verification usually keep evidence aligned, though configuration drift occasionally slips through. Verifying that evidence reflects the actual configuration adds review cycles. Detecting misrepresentation of configuration requires knowledgeable reviewers cross-checking drawings and data. Verification is low material cost but consumes expert attention.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of credible contamination pathways at product-contact interfaces unaddressed by qualification evidence
rows.imp_rat: Unaddressed contamination pathways is a qualification-coverage concern with some safety severity but low relevance to the physical blend; capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Contamination-pathway analysis is inconsistently thorough, leaving credible pathways unaddressed. Mapping contamination pathways adds risk-assessment time. Identifying credible pathways at interfaces requires contamination-control expertise. Assessment is labor cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of mismatch between compatibility evidence and the actual bulk solution composition or intended exposure conditions
rows.imp_rat: Mismatch between compatibility evidence and actual solution touches material-suitability risk (some severity), but the ranking mandate caps this item low.
rows.sat_rat: Vendor compatibility data is often generic, so mismatches with the actual solution and exposure conditions recur. Matching compatibility evidence to actual composition and exposure conditions requires added assessment cycles. Judging relevance of compatibility data to specific formulations needs materials-science expertise. Extended compatibility testing can add material and lab cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of contaminants leached from product-contact materials into the bulk solution
rows.imp_rat: Leachables from contact materials into the solution is a real safety risk (some severity), but held below the prior batch by the mandated ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Leaching from qualified stainless is usually low, but full leachables characterization is often incomplete for the specific solution. Leachables studies require extended exposure and analytical time. Leachables assessment demands specialized extractables/leachables expertise. E&L studies are analytically expensive.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of chemical degradation of product-contact materials caused by bulk-solution exposure
rows.imp_rat: Chemical degradation of contact materials threatens integrity/contamination (some severity), yet the ranking keeps this item's proxies low.
rows.sat_rat: Corrosion-resistant material selection generally prevents degradation, keeping this reasonably well served. Assessing chemical degradation adds exposure-study time. Predicting material degradation needs corrosion/materials expertise. Degradation testing consumes material.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of active ingredient lost through interaction with product-contact surfaces
rows.imp_rat: Active-ingredient loss to contact surfaces affects potency (some relevance) but this defect is ranked below the prior batch, keeping proxies low.
rows.sat_rat: Active loss to surfaces is often assumed negligible rather than verified, leaving occasional surprises. Quantifying active adsorption loss adds analytical study time. Assessing surface adsorption of active needs analytical expertise. Adsorption studies consume active material.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of bulk-solution degradation attributable to contact with vessel surfaces
rows.imp_rat: Solution degradation from vessel-surface contact affects quality (some relevance) but is ranked and capped low by the mandate.
rows.sat_rat: Inert surface selection generally limits solution degradation, keeping this moderately well served. Assessing surface-contact degradation requires stability study time. Linking degradation to vessel contact needs stability expertise. Stability testing consumes material.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of swelling or embrittlement in product-contact elastomers under intended solution, cleaning, and sterilization exposure
rows.imp_rat: Elastomer swelling/embrittlement risks seal failure/contamination (some severity) but is a material-durability concern with low blend relevance; capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Elastomer degradation under combined exposures is frequently under-characterized, leaving recurring risk. Elastomer aging studies require long or accelerated exposure time. Assessing swelling/embrittlement needs polymer expertise. Elastomer testing consumes material and lab cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of credible sterile-boundary and product-contact contamination pathways absent from qualification criteria
rows.imp_rat: Contamination pathways absent from qualification criteria is a coverage-gap concern with some safety severity but low blend relevance; capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Sterile-boundary pathways are often incompletely reflected in qualification criteria, a recurring gap. Covering sterile-boundary pathways in criteria extends protocol development. Sterile-boundary risk analysis needs aseptic-processing expertise. Mostly review labor cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of undissolved material remaining after the intended dissolution cycle
rows.imp_rat: Undissolved material after the cycle directly undermines the dissolve-and-blend job (some relevance/severity), but the mandated ceiling caps it below the prior batch.
rows.sat_rat: Visual and analytical dissolution checks are well established, making undissolved material a relatively uncommon miss. Confirming complete dissolution across cycles adds observation and sampling time. Assessing dissolution completeness draws on process knowledge but is supported by clear endpoints. Repeat verification consumes some material.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of bulk-solution concentration variation across intended formulations and batch volumes
rows.imp_rat: Concentration variation across formulations relates to homogeneity (some relevance) but as ranked below the prior batch its proxies stay low.
rows.sat_rat: Assay-based concentration control is mature, so variation within intended ranges is generally contained. Characterizing concentration variation across formulations lengthens development testing. Understanding concentration drivers needs formulation expertise. Multi-formulation testing consumes material.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of spatial and temporal temperature variation during dissolution and blending
rows.imp_rat: Spatial/temporal temperature variation relates to dissolution quality (some relevance) but the ceiling caps this instability item low, with modest consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Multi-point temperature mapping is a standard qualification practice with generally reliable results. Spatial and temporal mapping over cycles extends test time. Analyzing temporal temperature profiles needs standard thermal-validation skills. Instrumentation and logging incur modest cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that vessel geometry retains bulk solution or cleaning residue after drainage
rows.imp_rat: Geometry retaining solution/residue after drainage is a cleanability concern with some contamination severity but low relevance to the blend; capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Sanitary design guidelines reduce retention risk but geometry-driven holdup still surfaces in complex vessels. Assessing geometry-related retention adds inspection and study time. Evaluating drainage and dead-legs needs sanitary-design expertise. Riboflavin/coverage studies add modest cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that wetted materials or surface finishes interact adversely with the bulk solution
rows.imp_rat: Adverse wetted-material interaction with the solution risks contamination/degradation (some severity), yet the ranking keeps this item below the prior batch.
rows.sat_rat: Standard 316L and finish selection reduces adverse interactions, but solution-specific effects still slip through generic data. Confirming surface interactions across conditions adds assessment time. Evaluating adverse material interactions needs materials expertise. Interaction testing incurs lab cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of losing sterile vessel-boundary integrity under intended compounding and transfer conditions
rows.imp_rat: Loss of sterile-boundary integrity is a serious contamination risk (some severity), but the ranking holds this item below the prior batch.
rows.sat_rat: Sterile-boundary integrity testing is established, keeping loss of integrity relatively uncommon. Verifying sterile-boundary integrity adds integrity-test time. Integrity testing needs aseptic and mechanical expertise. Integrity testing incurs some cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of critical sensing and control attributes failing required range, accuracy, response, or location criteria
rows.imp_rat: Sensing/control attributes failing range/accuracy affects process control (some severity), but the mandate caps this item below the prior batch.
rows.sat_rat: Instrument qualification is a mature discipline, so failing sensing attributes are relatively uncommon. Verifying sensing/control attributes adds instrument-qualification time. Assessing range, accuracy, and response needs instrumentation expertise. Instrument testing incurs modest cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that vessel mixing introduces solution-damaging shear or aeration under intended operating conditions
rows.imp_rat: Shear/aeration damaging the solution can compromise product quality (some severity), but the mandated ceiling caps this item below the prior batch.
rows.sat_rat: Shear and aeration effects are often under-characterized in vessel qualification, leaving real recurring risk. Characterizing shear and aeration effects requires added studies. Predicting shear or aeration damage demands specialized mixing and product-stability expertise. Shear/aeration studies consume material and analytics.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that jacketed heat-transfer capacity fails to achieve required heating or cooling conditions across intended batches
rows.imp_rat: Jacket heat-transfer failing required heating/cooling directly threatens dissolution (some severity), yet the ranking mandate holds this item below the prior batch.
rows.sat_rat: Heat-transfer capacity is typically well specified and tested, so failures to reach conditions are uncommon. Verifying heat-transfer capacity across batches adds testing time. Assessing jacket capacity needs thermal-engineering judgement. Heating/cooling trials consume utilities and time.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that product-contact surfaces retain residues or bioburden after required cleaning and sterilization
rows.imp_rat: Retained residues/bioburden after cleaning threatens batch safety (some severity), yet the ranking keeps this item's proxies low.
rows.sat_rat: Cleaning and sterilization validation is mature, keeping residue/bioburden retention relatively uncommon. Post-cleaning and sterilization verification adds cycle time. Assessing residue/bioburden retention needs cleaning and micro expertise. Micro and residue testing incur recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of bulk-solution temperature nonuniformity during dissolution and blending
rows.imp_rat: Temperature nonuniformity during dissolution touches the core job (some relevance) but is capped low by the ranking, with modest consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Jacketed-vessel temperature control and mapping are mature, keeping nonuniformity generally well managed. Temperature mapping studies add qualification duration. Interpreting thermal uniformity requires thermal-mapping competence that is reasonably available. Mapping uses probes and logging equipment at modest cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of intended formulations or cleaning agents incompatible with vessel product-contact materials
rows.imp_rat: Formulations/cleaners incompatible with contact materials is a material-selection concern with some quality consequence, but ranked and capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Published compatibility references make cleaner/material incompatibilities generally well identified. Compatibility screening across formulations and cleaners adds review time. Compatibility matrices rely on materials knowledge that is fairly well codified. Screening is mostly desk review with limited testing cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of ambiguity in the contract's acceptance obligations for sterility-critical batching performance
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity in contract acceptance obligations for sterility-critical performance is administrative to the functional batching job (relevance) though it carries compliance stakes (severity); held at 2.0 by ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Acceptance clauses for sterility performance are frequently vague, leaving recurring ambiguity that surfaces at handover. Negotiating unambiguous acceptance obligations extends contracting discussions. Drafting clear, testable acceptance criteria for sterility-critical performance requires both legal and technical expertise that rarely align. Ambiguous acceptance terms lead to costly disputes, while precise drafting adds only modest legal cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of required skid lifecycle records unavailable for archiving at decommissioning
rows.imp_rat: Unavailable lifecycle records at decommissioning is an end-of-life archiving error with minor consequences, peripheral to the CFJ and held below 2.5.
rows.sat_rat: Records fragmented across paper, legacy systems and personal files mean required archives are routinely incomplete at decommissioning, a persistent unsolved gap. Hunting for scattered lifecycle records at end-of-life consumes significant unplanned time. Knowing which records exist and where they live often depends on tacit knowledge of departing staff. Record retrieval and reconstruction effort adds labor cost, usually modest.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the purchase order authorizes a skid configuration different from the approved selection
rows.imp_rat: A purchase order authorizing the wrong skid configuration is a procurement error catchable before delivery; peripheral to the CFJ and low-stakes, kept under 2.5.
rows.sat_rat: Procurement approval gates and configuration sign-offs make PO-to-selection mismatches uncommon under standard controls. PO issuance against the approved selection is a quick administrative step in the procurement workflow. Matching PO configuration to the approved selection is a clerical cross-check requiring modest skill. An authorization mismatch is costly but rare given procurement approval controls.

rows.stk: Procurement and engineering teams managing the vendor selection process.
rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of solicitation requirements that are incomplete or internally conflicting
rows.imp_rat: Incomplete or conflicting solicitation requirements is a procurement-document error, largely peripheral to the skid performing its functional job, with modest downstream consequences well under the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Templates help, but conflicting and incomplete solicitation requirements remain a frequent recurring problem. Reviewing solicitation documents for completeness and conflicts adds time that is often compressed under bid schedules. Producing internally consistent, complete solicitations requires disciplined technical-writing and review skill that is unevenly applied. Incomplete or conflicting solicitations drive costly clarification cycles and scope disputes.

rows.stk: Quality assurance and regulatory affairs personnel supporting the acquisition.
rows.source_job: Evaluate regulatory compliance of candidate skid
rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required compliance evidence missing from the supplier submission
rows.imp_rat: Missing compliance evidence in a supplier submission is a documentation-completeness error at procurement, tangential to skid function and low-stakes, kept under the cap.
rows.sat_rat: Compliance documentation checklists are standard, but gaps in supplier submissions still occur regularly and require follow-up. Chasing missing compliance evidence adds review cycles but is a routine part of document control. Knowing which compliance evidence is required and checking submissions needs quality expertise that is generally available. Missing evidence found late causes qualification delays and rework costs, partly mitigated by document checklists.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of service, monitoring, or software contracts remaining active after the intended termination date
rows.imp_rat: Contracts active past their termination date is a procurement housekeeping waste error, peripheral to batch preparation function; the consequence is limited to recurring cost leakage, so both proxies remain low.
rows.sat_rat: With calendar reminders and asset/contract management systems increasingly common, hitting the intended termination date is reasonably consistent though lapses still occur where such tools are absent. Missing the intended termination date is inherently a timing miss, but what the organization loses is the money for the overrun period rather than the delay per se. Timing a cancellation to a target date is routine administrative work needing minimal expertise. The primary loss is continued payment beyond the intended end date, and modern contract-management and renewal-alert tools handle date-driven termination fairly well when adopted.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of service, monitoring, or software contracts remaining active after disposal
rows.imp_rat: Service/software contracts lingering after disposal is an administrative billing error with essentially no bearing on the core batch preparation job; consequences are modest wasted spend, keeping both proxies low.
rows.sat_rat: Whether every contract is actually terminated is inconsistent when tracking depends on memory or scattered procurement files, leaving residual active agreements. Contracts can linger past disposal simply because cancellation is deprioritized, so time-to-cancel is a contributing factor but not the loss itself. Cancelling service, monitoring or software contracts is administratively simple and requires little specialized skill. The dominant loss is paying for services on equipment that no longer exists, a direct and recurring financial waste that contract inventories only partly catch.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of affected stakeholder groups omitted from the batch preparation system retirement notification
rows.imp_rat: Omitting stakeholder groups from a retirement notification is a communication-completeness administrative task unrelated to running batch prep (very low relevance) with easily remediable consequences (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: The failure is an omitted group producing an incomplete notification; retirement communications are usually driven by informal distribution lists with no systematic stakeholder mapping, so omissions recur and the need is served only modestly. Identifying every affected group takes coordination time, but the harm from a missed group is not primarily a delay. Knowing which stakeholder groups (operators, regulators, service partners, downstream users) must be notified requires institutional knowledge that varies widely by organization. Omissions rarely cost much money directly, so cost is a minor lens for this need.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of affected stakeholders omitted from the retirement notification
rows.imp_rat: Omitting affected stakeholders from the retirement notification is a low-centrality administrative notification error (very low relevance) whose consequences are minor and correctable (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: The core error is individual recipients dropped from the notification; contact lists are often stale and manually maintained, so consistent full coverage is not assured and the need is only acceptably served. Reaching all individual stakeholders adds lead time, but timeliness is not what fails when someone is missed. Maintaining an accurate roster of individual affected stakeholders needs diligent record-keeping rather than deep expertise, lowering the skill barrier somewhat. The cost of contacting stakeholders is negligible, so cost barely bears on this need.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required take-back records rejected as incomplete or inconsistent
rows.imp_rat: Take-back records rejected as incomplete is an end-of-life documentation rework issue essentially unrelated to preparing batches (very low relevance) and low-consequence (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: The dominant loss is inconsistent, incomplete records that get bounced back; today's manual reconciliation and generic spreadsheets leave frequent gaps, so this need is only partially served. Assembling take-back records at end-of-life is slow because data is scattered across procurement, maintenance and decommissioning logs, but time is not the primary loss when a record is rejected. Compiling complete, consistent take-back documentation for regulated batch systems demands staff who understand the applicable WEEE/RCRA-style reporting rules, and that expertise is scarce at the disposal stage. Rejected records trigger rework and occasionally fines, but the direct monetary hit is modest relative to the compliance disruption.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of take-back documentation being rejected as incomplete or inaccurate
rows.imp_rat: Take-back documentation rejected as incomplete/inaccurate is a retirement-phase administrative rework far from batch preparation (very low relevance) with minor, absorbable consequences (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: The essence of this need is documentation being accepted first time; standard practice relies on ad hoc checklists and human review, so rejections for inaccuracy remain common and the need is poorly served. Documentation cycles stretch when submissions are returned for correction, but the delay is a symptom rather than the core failure here. Accurate take-back documentation still leans heavily on knowledgeable EHS/compliance personnel who know what each authority and take-back scheme requires. Resubmission and correction consume labor and can incur penalties, yet the resource waste is secondary to the accuracy problem.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of obtained vendor references lacking comparable sterile fill-finish conditions
rows.imp_rat: Vendor references lacking comparable sterile conditions is a supplier-vetting nuance remote from the batch prep job (very low relevance); it slightly weakens selection confidence without causing direct harm (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Vendors frequently provide references from non-comparable applications, so obtaining truly comparable sterile fill-finish references remains a chronic weak point. Finding references under comparable sterile fill-finish conditions takes extensive outreach that is often shortcut. Assessing condition-comparability requires sterile-processing expertise to know what counts as comparable. Non-comparable references waste evaluation effort and risk expensive misjudgement.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of references lacking verifiable evidence from comparable sterile fill-finish installations
rows.imp_rat: References lacking verifiable evidence from comparable installations is a procurement due-diligence concern far from executing batch prep (very low relevance); poor references risk vendor selection but not immediate job failure (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Vendors often supply references without verifiable, documented evidence from truly comparable sterile installations, leaving this poorly served. Obtaining and verifying reference evidence from comparable installations is slow and dependent on vendor cooperation. Judging whether reference evidence is genuinely comparable and verifiable requires experienced evaluators. Reference checking consumes modest effort but weak references risk costly poor selection.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required justification, specification, or quotation documents missing from the approval package
rows.imp_rat: Missing justification or quotation documents in an approval package is an administrative delay error remote from the core batch-prep job; the consequence is a slower approval cycle, so both proxies are minimal.
rows.sat_rat: Standard capital-approval templates and checklists make missing documents relatively uncommon and easy to catch. Approval-package assembly is a defined workflow, though chasing missing quotations or specs can still cause delay. Procurement staff routinely know which justification and quotation documents are needed, a well-established competence. Package preparation is administrative labor with minimal direct cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration between a manufacturing or shipment schedule deviation and its disclosure to the purchaser
rows.imp_rat: Delay in disclosing a schedule deviation to the purchaser is a communication-timeliness error tangential to the core job; its consequence is eroded trust rather than functional failure, keeping both proxies low.
rows.sat_rat: There is no enforced real-time deviation-alert mechanism, so disclosure timing is inconsistent and often lags the actual schedule slip. The delay itself is the error, and status-quo reliance on periodic manual updates means deviations often reach the purchaser late. Prompt disclosure depends on vendor project managers' diligence rather than any special skill. Faster disclosure costs little beyond communication discipline.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of relevant sterile batch preparation configurations omitted from research findings
rows.imp_rat: Omitting relevant configurations from research findings is an upstream information-gathering gap distant from the physical CFJ (very low relevance); it may need supplemental research but causes no operational harm (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: There is no standard checklist guaranteeing all relevant configurations surface, so findings routinely omit options and the outcome varies with who did the search. Research into the full range of sterile batch prep configurations is slow and iterative, drawing out the acquisition timeline as teams chase vendor literature and reference sites. Comprehensive coverage of relevant configurations depends heavily on scarce process engineers who know what to look for, so omissions track expert availability. Missed configurations rarely cost money at this stage but drive expensive rework or wrong purchases later.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of mismatch between the configurations and supply scopes used for comparison
rows.imp_rat: Mismatch between compared configurations and supply scopes is a procurement-comparison variability error remote from batch preparation itself; it may skew a purchase decision but does not affect job execution, so proxies stay minimal.
rows.sat_rat: Vendors quote inconsistent scopes and there is no standard normalization, so comparison bases frequently mismatch. Normalizing competing bids to a common scope adds analysis time during evaluation. Aligning configurations and supply scopes for fair comparison takes experienced procurement and engineering judgement. Scope mismatches drive costly change orders if not caught, though the comparison work itself is cheap.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of registration fields containing inaccurate or inconsistent system information
rows.imp_rat: Inaccurate registration fields is a records-data defect only loosely tied to running batch preparation; the consequence is minor data cleanup, holding both proxies at the low end of the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Asset registers and validation rules keep most fields consistent, though inaccurate or inconsistent entries still recur across systems. Registration data entry is quick but reconciling inconsistencies across systems takes some time. Entering asset registration data is routine clerical work with low skill demand. Registration is negligible administrative cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of ambiguity in specified test pressures and acceptance criteria for the assembled system
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity in test pressures and acceptance criteria for the assembled system affects commissioning safety (relevance) with potential pressure-safety stakes (severity); held at 2.0 by ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Specifications are often incomplete or inconsistently transcribed, so ambiguity in test criteria recurs and undermines confidence in results. Clarifying ambiguous test pressures or acceptance criteria stalls testing while teams seek engineering confirmation. Resolving ambiguity demands engineering judgement and reference to codes that not all field staff command. Delayed or repeated tests due to unclear criteria waste labor and can require re-work of test setups.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required operator or maintenance access clearances obstructed by mounted components
rows.imp_rat: Obstructed operator or maintenance access clearances is an ergonomic/layout defect with minimal centrality to preparing a batch; relevance is very low and severity minor and correctable.
rows.sat_rat: 3D models and layout reviews catch most clearance conflicts, but field obstructions still appear where reviews are incomplete. Discovering blocked access clearances during operation forces retrofits that consume time, though early layout review often prevents them. Anticipating maintenance and operator clearances during mounting requires experienced layout judgement. Obstructed clearances discovered late drive costly relocation of mounted components.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of conflicts between training instructions, approved procedures, and the current batching system configuration
rows.imp_rat: Conflicts between training, procedures, and system configuration can cause operator errors (relevance) but usually surface benignly (severity); the ceiling holds it at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Document control systems exist but lag equipment change, so mismatches between instructions, approved procedures, and current configuration recur frequently. Discovering conflicts between training material, procedures, and actual configuration mid-qualification forces stoppages and rework that consume time. Reconciling these conflicts demands experienced judgement to know which source is authoritative, placing burden on scarce senior staff.
