Document reconciliation and re-issuance carry administrative cost but little direct material waste.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of misalignment among vessels, rotating equipment, and dispensing hardware after assembly
rows.imp_rat: Misalignment among vessels, rotating equipment, and dispensing hardware after assembly affects reliability (relevance) but is usually caught at commissioning (severity); held at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Standard alignment procedures and tooling handle most cases well, though field misalignment still recurs and drives vibration issues. Re-aligning vessels, rotating equipment, and dispensing hardware after assembly consumes measurable setup time. Precision alignment of rotating equipment requires trained millwrights and laser or dial-indicator technique. Residual misalignment accelerates bearing and seal wear, imposing downstream maintenance cost.

rows.stk: Safety engineers, commissioning engineers, system operators.
rows.source_job: Verify safety interlocks and shutdown functions
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of safety functions with conflicting acceptance criteria before startup
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting acceptance criteria for safety functions is a specification-ambiguity issue resolvable before startup (low relevance) and detectable in review (low severity). Below ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Acceptance criteria are drawn from multiple documents that are not always harmonized, so conflicts surface with some regularity before startup. Reconciling conflicting acceptance criteria before startup consumes review time when discrepancies are found late. Spotting conflicts among safety-function acceptance criteria demands cross-disciplinary safety review that few individuals can perform alone. Conflicting criteria can force retesting or delayed startup, incurring schedule cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of shortlisted liquid processing plant suppliers that fail minimum delivery capability criteria
rows.imp_rat: Shortlisted suppliers failing delivery-capability criteria is a procurement-screening concern only marginally central to the plant's core processing job (low relevance); consequences surface early and are recoverable (low-moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Prequalification and reference checks screen out many weak suppliers, but some that fail delivery capability still reach the shortlist. Vetting delivery capability during shortlisting adds evaluation time but is not primarily a speed problem. Assessing true delivery capability requires experienced buyers who can see past optimistic supplier claims. Shortlisting an incapable supplier risks costly re-tendering, though that is a downstream cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of disagreement among procurement, engineering, and quality reviewers over whether suppliers meet minimum qualification criteria
rows.imp_rat: Reviewer disagreement over supplier qualification is a procurement-administrative friction that is peripheral to running a processing plant (low relevance); when it occurs it delays paperwork but rarely harms product or safety (low severity). Ceiling-bound to 1.5.
rows.sat_rat: Prequalification questionnaires and approved-vendor lists give a shared baseline, yet subjective weighting of minimum criteria still yields recurring reviewer disagreement. Cross-functional supplier qualification reviews take time to convene and reconcile, adding to schedule. Reaching agreement leans heavily on the judgement of senior procurement, engineering, and quality specialists who weigh criteria differently. Disagreement itself consumes review effort but does not directly overspend project funds.

rows.stk: Process engineers, procurement managers, capital project teams.
rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of plant configurations assessed against ambiguous sterile fill-finish project requirements
rows.imp_rat: Assessing configurations against ambiguous sterile fill-finish requirements has quality relevance, but this batch ranks below prior items, limiting both proxies.
rows.sat_rat: Sterile project requirements are frequently ambiguous at assessment time, so configurations are routinely judged against shifting targets, a persistent reliability gap. Assessing against ambiguous requirements wastes time in clarification loops with stakeholders. Detecting and resolving requirement ambiguity demands experienced requirements engineers often not embedded in the team. Rework from ambiguous requirements drives avoidable cost but the assessment itself is cheap.

rows.stk: Procurement managers, site engineers, quality inspectors.
rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of specification deviations in delivered equipment that remain undetected before acceptance
rows.imp_rat: Undetected specification deviations before acceptance is a QC-detection gap at commissioning, one step from ongoing processing (low relevance) and often caught later (low severity per ranking). Held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: FATs and inspection plans catch many deviations, but coverage is incomplete and a real fraction still slip through to acceptance. Factory and pre-delivery inspections take time to schedule and execute against the acquisition timeline. Detecting specification deviations reliably requires skilled inspectors and reviewers who are in short supply. Undetected deviations that surface after acceptance are expensive to remediate, so early detection protects spend.

rows.stk: Procurement managers, legal/commercial teams, capital project managers.
rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of ambiguity in the agreed scope boundaries, supplier exclusions, and interface responsibilities
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguous scope boundaries and supplier exclusions is a contracting-stage ambiguity, low in relevance to running the plant and moderate in downstream dispute severity.
rows.sat_rat: Despite standard specifications and scope-of-supply documents, interface and exclusion gaps routinely remain and produce disputes after award. Negotiating and clarifying scope boundaries is time-consuming, but the core failure is residual ambiguity rather than duration. Drafting unambiguous scope splits and interface responsibilities requires scarce experienced contract and systems engineers. Scope ambiguity surfaces later as costly change orders and disputed extras that are hard to control upfront.

rows.stk: Procurement managers, legal/commercial teams, capital project managers.
rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of commercial exposure from obligations not included in the agreed acquisition price
rows.imp_rat: Commercial exposure from obligations excluded from the acquisition price is a financial/contractual error only tangential to plant processing (low relevance), with consequences confined to cost overruns (low-moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Structured commercial reviews reduce surprises, but hidden or assumed obligations still frequently emerge as unpriced claims. Identifying out-of-scope obligations takes negotiation time but the core loss is financial exposure. Spotting excluded obligations before signing requires experienced commercial staff who read contracts carefully. Obligations left outside the agreed price become unbudgeted extras, the direct commercial exposure this need targets.

rows.stk: Procurement managers, legal/commercial teams, capital project managers.
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that supplier delivery commitments conflict with project commissioning dependencies
rows.imp_rat: Supplier delivery conflicts with commissioning dependencies is a project-schedule risk, marginal to steady-state processing (low relevance) and usually re-planned (low severity). Capped at 1.5.
rows.sat_rat: Project scheduling tools and milestone contracts help align dates, yet supplier commitments and commissioning dependencies still collide fairly often. The whole need is about supplier delivery dates aligning with commissioning windows, making schedule the primary concern. Building a realistic integrated schedule requires experienced planners who understand both supplier lead times and commissioning logic. Delivery conflicts drive standby and rescheduling costs, but the felt loss first appears as schedule slip.

rows.stk: Procurement managers, legal/commercial teams, capital project managers.
rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of critical plant acceptance criteria excluded from warranty coverage
rows.imp_rat: Acceptance criteria excluded from warranty coverage is a contractual-risk gap, peripheral to day-to-day processing (low relevance) with financial rather than functional consequences (low severity). Capped at 1.5.
rows.sat_rat: Standard warranty clauses exist but they frequently fail to cover all plant-critical acceptance criteria, leaving recurring coverage holes. Aligning acceptance criteria with warranty terms adds negotiation time but that is not the central loss. Mapping critical acceptance criteria into warranty language needs skilled contract and quality staff who are often not fully coordinated. Gaps between acceptance criteria and warranty coverage create significant unbudgeted repair exposure later.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required utility connections unavailable or outside specified conditions when installation begins
rows.imp_rat: Utility connections unavailable at installation start causes schedule delay rather than core processing failure; relevance is moderate-to-low and severity is limited, constrained under the 1.5 cap.
rows.sat_rat: Pre-install utility readiness checks are standard practice, but mismatches between as-supplied and specified conditions still recur enough to leave real gaps. Utilities not ready or out of spec at install start stalls crews and is a frequent cause of preparation delay. Verifying utility availability against spec is routine facilities work requiring modest expertise. Idle labor and temporary utility hookups add cost when connections are not ready on time.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required site approvals or safety clearances incomplete when installation begins
rows.imp_rat: Incomplete site approvals when installation begins can stall work, but the ranking places this below the prior batch, capping both proxies.
rows.sat_rat: Mature permit-to-work and readiness-review checklists on regulated liquid plants catch most incomplete clearances, though occasional gaps still surface. Chasing missing approvals just before install start is a common source of schedule slip when clearances lag the work front. Tracking which permits and clearances are outstanding needs experienced coordinators but is not deeply technical. Standing crews idle awaiting clearances wastes money, though this is usually contained by planning.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of anchoring delay caused by foundation concrete being below required strength at the scheduled start
rows.imp_rat: Anchoring delay from under-strength foundation concrete is a narrow one-time construction sequencing issue (very low relevance to processing) causing only schedule slip (low severity). Well under the 3.0 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Standard cylinder-break testing and cure planning make below-strength anchoring predictable and largely avoidable, but weather and schedule compression still cause delays. Concrete strength gain is governed by cure time that cannot be rushed, though schedule buffers and rapid-set mixes mitigate delays. Concrete testing and cure scheduling are routine civil practices requiring modest specialist input. Anchoring delays idle installation crews, so accelerated concrete and cure monitoring are cost-justified.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of dosing setpoints configured outside approved formulation ranges
rows.imp_rat: Dosing setpoints outside approved formulation ranges could affect product quality, but this item is ranked below the prior batch so relevance stays low; severity of any single misconfiguration is contained. Capped at 1.5 mean.
rows.sat_rat: Formulation range checks are common practice, though manual configuration still lets some setpoints land outside approved ranges. Verifying dosing setpoints against approved ranges is a quick check when procedures exist. Confirming setpoints against approved formulation needs a competent process engineer, not rare skill. Out-of-range dosing scraps batches, but the check itself costs little.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of temporary safety bypasses left active when the plant is released for operation
rows.imp_rat: Leaving safety bypasses active at release is a serious defect in principle, but the ranking caps it below the prior batch; relevance here is bounded and severity constrained by release checks.
rows.sat_rat: Bypass registers and pre-startup safety reviews are established controls, so active bypasses left at release are uncommon where discipline holds. Removing temporary bypasses is a quick close-out step when tracked properly. Identifying and clearing bypasses requires disciplined technicians rather than deep expertise. A forgotten bypass carries huge incident cost but the removal effort is trivial.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of connected plant control devices unable to exchange required control, monitoring, diagnostic, or production data
rows.imp_rat: Devices unable to exchange control/monitoring data touches integration reliability, but the ranking places it below prior items, so relevance is low and severity capped at ceiling 1.5.
rows.sat_rat: Loop checks and comms testing are standard, yet heterogeneous device integration still leaves a meaningful fraction unable to exchange all required data initially. Diagnosing devices that cannot exchange required data consumes significant integration time. Resolving fieldbus and protocol interoperability issues requires specialized controls integrators. Failed data exchange forces added engineering hours and sometimes hardware swaps.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required sequence steps with ambiguous or conflicting requirements
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguous or conflicting sequence-step requirements affect execution clarity, but the batch ranks below prior items, limiting both proxies.
rows.sat_rat: Requirements management is still largely document- and email-driven, so conflicting or vague sequence steps routinely slip through and corrupt commissioning outcomes. Resolving ambiguous or conflicting sequence steps forces stop-and-clarify cycles during preparation that slow the whole ramp-up. Untangling conflicting requirements leans heavily on scarce senior engineers who carry undocumented tribal knowledge of the plant's intent. Ambiguity discovered late triggers costly rework and change orders, but the direct spend is secondary to the outcome uncertainty.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of imported recipe parameter values that differ from the approved source values
rows.imp_rat: Imported recipe parameters differing from approved values could impact a batch, but its ranking below the prior batch fixes relevance low; the error is typically caught and corrected, keeping severity low.
rows.sat_rat: Recipe import with checksum or side-by-side verification is increasingly standard, so divergences from approved source values are moderately well caught. Verifying imported recipe values against source is fast when done digitally. The comparison is largely clerical and needs limited specialized skill. A wrong imported value can ruin a run, but the verification effort is minimal.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required recipe steps, phases, or parameters omitted during import
rows.imp_rat: Omitting recipe steps or parameters during import affects process fidelity, but the ranking places this below prior items, keeping both proxies minimal.
rows.sat_rat: Import tools and checksums help, but omitted steps, phases or parameters during import still occur and depend on manual verification to catch. Verifying complete recipe import adds a reconciliation step against the source master. Confirming no steps or phases were dropped requires familiarity with the full recipe structure. Fixing an incomplete import early is cheap versus rerunning a batch.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required operating parameters left unset before the planned production run
rows.imp_rat: Operating parameters left unset before a production run can disrupt output, but this item sits below the prior batch, capping both proxies.
rows.sat_rat: Checklists and setpoint templates help, but required parameters left unset before a run remain a recurring gap without strong system enforcement. Confirming all required parameters are set before a run adds a verification pass. Knowing which parameters must be set relies on operator and engineer familiarity with the recipe. An unset parameter caught in preparation costs little; missed it can spoil a run.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of set parameter values that change before the planned production run begins
rows.imp_rat: Set parameters drifting before a run starts can cause off-spec output, but the error is typically detected at run start; relevance is limited and severity is low given pre-run checks.
rows.sat_rat: Parameter change control is often loosely enforced during preparation, so values quietly shifting before the run is a recurring reliability weakness. Re-verifying parameters that drifted before the run consumes scarce pre-run time. Detecting silent parameter changes requires attentive engineers who know the approved baseline. A run started on changed parameters can scrap product, but the tracking effort itself is cheap.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unsafe conditions linked to the wrong protective responses for the installed equipment arrangement
rows.imp_rat: Wrong protective responses for equipment arrangement touches safety, but the ranking places it below prior batch items, so relevance is treated as low and severity capped despite its potential; held at ceiling 1.5.
rows.sat_rat: Protection settings often assume the design arrangement rather than the actual field build, leaving mismatches that surface as unsafe conditions. Matching protective responses to the as-installed arrangement requires study time that pressures the schedule. Correctly mapping protective responses to hazards demands scarce functional-safety expertise. Wrong protection settings are cheap to fix but catastrophic if left, so the risk not spend dominates.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required process-circuit sections omitted from integrity testing
rows.imp_rat: Omitting process-circuit sections from integrity testing could leave latent leak risk, but as a commissioning-check error it is ranked below the prior batch (low relevance); the ceiling holds severity to a low-moderate value.
rows.sat_rat: Integrity test protocols exist and are widely used, but complex circuit topologies still leave some sections inadvertently omitted from testing. Pressure and integrity testing every circuit section is time-consuming during preparation. Planning full test coverage of process circuits needs experienced piping/commissioning engineers. Omitted sections that later leak cause costly rework, but testing itself is modest cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of plant power conductors terminated at incorrect terminals
rows.imp_rat: Power conductors terminated at incorrect terminals can cause faults, but as a construction-phase defect it is peripheral to the ongoing processing job (low relevance); the batch ceiling limits the otherwise-real severity to a low-moderate value.
rows.sat_rat: Point-to-point continuity checking against wiring schedules is mature practice, so incorrectly terminated conductors are usually caught before energization. Point-to-point termination checks add time but are routinely built into the schedule. Correct termination and verification is standard electrician work with established checking. Wrong terminations cause rework but are cheap to correct if caught during checkout.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unresolved conflicts or omissions in electrical specifications needed to complete plant power connections
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved electrical spec conflicts blocking power connections is a commissioning ambiguity; low centrality to steady-state processing with moderate, resolvable severity.
rows.sat_rat: Electrical documentation is frequently issued with unresolved conflicts or omissions, so problems commonly surface only at connection time. Resolving electrical spec conflicts before power connection often causes waiting and clarification delays. Reconciling conflicting electrical drawings demands qualified electrical engineers in short supply. Late electrical rework is costly, though catching conflicts early is inexpensive.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of sampled product-contact locations exceeding microbial acceptance limits before first production use
rows.imp_rat: Product-contact locations exceeding microbial limits before first use is a contamination-risk concern with real stakes, but the batch-4 ranking forces combined importance at or below 1.5; proxies pinned low.
rows.sat_rat: Validated cleaning plus swab/rinse sampling is standard in liquid plants, yet occasional locations still exceed limits and require repeat cycles. Microbial sampling requires incubation lag that delays confirmation of product-contact acceptance before first use. Aseptic sampling and interpreting microbial results need qualified QC microbiology staff. Failed swabs mean re-cleaning and re-testing, wasting time and consumables but bounded cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of completed maintenance actions omitted from the plant maintenance history
rows.imp_rat: Missing completed maintenance actions from the history is an administrative record-keeping gap only tangential to the core processing job (low relevance); consequences are contained to traceability lapses (modest severity).
rows.sat_rat: Electronic work-order closure captures most completed actions, yet informal or rushed jobs still escape the maintenance history. Logging completed actions in a CMMS is quick but adds a step technicians sometimes defer. Recording history needs no special skill, just consistent documentation discipline. Recordkeeping cost is negligible while missing history can be costly for audits and trend analysis.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required batch, validation, and maintenance records omitted from the retired-plant archive
rows.imp_rat: Omitting batch, validation, and maintenance records from a retired-plant archive is peripheral to running a viscous product processing plant (low relevance), and though lost records can hamper future audits (moderate severity), the ceiling caps this at 1.5.
rows.sat_rat: Records scattered across paper and legacy systems are frequently incomplete, so omissions from the retired-plant archive are common. Assembling batch, validation, and maintenance records into an archive is a lengthy retrospective effort. Knowing which records are legally required for retention needs quality/regulatory expertise. Archiving is relatively cheap, but missing records can force costly reconstruction or compliance exposure.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required recipes, batch records, and control configurations omitted from the export
rows.imp_rat: Omitting recipes, batch records, or control configurations from an export could impair replication of processes (some severity), but it is a data-transfer defect peripheral to running the scope (low relevance) and ranks below the prior batch.
rows.sat_rat: Legacy and proprietary control systems make complete configuration export unreliable, and partial exports are common. Exporting recipes, batch records, and control configurations is a discrete but often rushed end-of-life task. Extracting proprietary control-system configurations requires automation/system expertise that may already have left the site. Export tooling is low cost, but lost recipes can carry high replacement value if the process is redeployed.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of recoverable metals, motors, and electronic boards sent to landfill
rows.imp_rat: Recoverable metals and boards sent to landfill is an end-of-life sustainability waste with minimal relevance to the plant's core functional job and low operational severity. Capped at 2.5.
rows.sat_rat: Established scrap and e-waste recycling channels reliably absorb metals and motors, though mixed or bonded assemblies still slip to landfill. Sorting metals, motors, and boards for recycling adds sorting time at the waste stage. Basic material segregation for scrap is well understood and needs little specialist skill. Scrap-metal markets give strong economic incentive to divert metals and motors from landfill, capturing recoverable value.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required manuals, drawings, or certificates omitted from the transferred documentation package
rows.imp_rat: Omitting manuals, drawings, or certificates from a transfer package is a documentation-handoff defect largely outside the core processing job (low relevance), with recoverable consequences (modest severity), held under the 1.5 cap.
rows.sat_rat: As-built drawings and certificates for aging plants are often outdated or missing, so transferred packages routinely have gaps. Gathering manuals, drawings, and certificates into a transfer package is time-consuming at handover. Identifying the complete required documentation set relies on document-control familiarity with the plant. Missing documentation is inexpensive to omit now but costly to the receiving party later.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of equipment items whose recorded disposition differs from the actual final disposition
rows.imp_rat: A recorded-versus-actual equipment disposition discrepancy is an asset-tracking bookkeeping error only marginally tied to the processing job (low relevance), with limited downstream harm (modest severity).
rows.sat_rat: Asset registers are seldom updated in real time as equipment is scrapped, sold, or relocated, so recorded and actual dispositions frequently diverge. Reconciling recorded disposition against actual final disposition requires ongoing tracking through a chaotic dismantling phase. Maintaining accurate disposition records demands disciplined asset-tracking practice that is often lacking. Discrepancies undermine audit and asset accounting but the tracking effort itself is low cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of tank options missing critical sterile-service specifications
rows.imp_rat: Tank options missing sterile-service specs create selection ambiguity, yet this is a pre-purchase information gap with modest downstream stakes; capped at 1.5.
rows.sat_rat: Bid-tabulation practice normalizes options but sterile-spec completeness across options is inconsistently verified, leaving recurring gaps. Confirming that every option carries its sterile-service spec adds comparison effort during bid evaluation. Detecting which options lack critical sterile specs requires detailed technical comparison skill across vendor quotes. Selecting an under-specified option leads to change costs, though usually smaller than full rework.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of the processing tank being damaged during transport or rigging
rows.imp_rat: Tank damage during transport or rigging can be costly, yet within this ordering it is ranked below batch 4 and the mandatory ceiling limits both proxies.
rows.sat_rat: Mature logistics practice with bracing, insurance, and qualified riggers keeps transport/rigging damage relatively uncommon. Transport and rigging planning is routine and rarely the pacing item in acquisition. Specialized riggers and heavy-haul carriers bring mature expertise, reducing dependence on the buyer's own skill. Transit damage is expensive when it occurs, but insurance and specialized packaging cap the exposure.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time between fabrication schedule slippage and its visibility to the project manager
rows.imp_rat: Delay in surfacing fabrication schedule slippage affects project visibility but is upstream and recoverable; ranked low and held under the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Progress reporting is inconsistent and often optimistic, so surprises at delivery remain common despite periodic status calls. Schedule slippage often becomes visible to the PM only at the next milestone review, so the reporting lag is chronically long. Extracting real fabrication status demands a diligent expediter who knows how to read a shop's true progress versus its claims. Late visibility forces expedited shipping and downstream schedule crashing, adding avoidable cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of submitting the tank warranty registration after the eligibility deadline
rows.imp_rat: Submitting warranty registration after the eligibility deadline forfeits coverage, a modest financial consequence far from core function; capped by the batch-5 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Standard project closeout checklists and vendor-driven reminders make missed registration deadlines uncommon. Warranty registration is a short, well-bounded task usually completed near delivery without meaningful delay. Registration is clerical and requires almost no specialized skill. Missing the deadline forfeits warranty value, but the mitigation cost is negligible when tracked.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of process-media constituents and cleaning residues omitted from the compatibility assessment
rows.imp_rat: Omitting media constituents or cleaning residues from a compatibility assessment could allow corrosion, but this item sits low in the ordering and the ceiling holds relevance and severity minimal.
rows.sat_rat: Compatibility assessments routinely miss trace constituents and cleaning agents, making this a recurring and poorly controlled failure mode. Compiling a full media-and-cleaning-residue compatibility assessment is slow when data must be gathered from multiple process owners. Complete compatibility assessment requires materials and process-chemistry expertise that is scarce and easily overlooks minor constituents. Omitted constituents cause corrosion or contamination failures whose remediation cost is very high.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of critical sterile-process requirements missing from the quotation request
rows.imp_rat: Missing sterile-process requirements from a quotation request affects procurement accuracy but is upstream and correctable; ranked below prior batch and capped at 1.5.
rows.sat_rat: RFQ templates help but are frequently incomplete for sterile specifics, so critical requirements are commonly left out of the initial request. Building a complete RFQ takes time, and omissions found later force re-quoting rounds that add delay. Specifying all critical sterile-process requirements up front requires experienced specifiers who understand hygienic design end to end. Requirements missing from the RFQ resurface as scope changes with premium pricing after award.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of mismatch between recorded and actual installation-space and access-route dimensions
rows.imp_rat: Mismatch between recorded and actual installation-space dimensions can cause install rework, but it is a data-accuracy issue ranked below the prior batch and capped.
rows.sat_rat: Site surveys and 3D scans are increasingly standard and catch most issues, but reliance on stale drawings still produces mismatches. Field-verifying install-space and access-route dimensions takes coordinated site visits that can extend planning. Accurate as-built measurement and rigging-path assessment needs surveying and rigging know-how not always on the buying team. A dimensional mismatch discovered at delivery causes very costly rework, storage, or return of an oversized tank.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of as-built tank details differing from the installed configuration
rows.imp_rat: As-built details differing from installed configuration is a documentation-accuracy defect of low relevance to active processing and modest severity; kept well under the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: As-built accuracy is a well-known chronic weakness where field changes routinely go unrecorded, leaving documentation that diverges from the installed configuration. Updating as-built documentation is often deferred to the end and squeezed by schedule pressure. Redlining and drawing updates require draftsmen and engineers whose time is often reprioritized to commissioning tasks. As-built updating is low direct cost but chronically underfunded relative to its downstream value.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of completed tank maintenance events lacking a corresponding record
rows.imp_rat: Missing maintenance records for completed events is a documentation defect with limited direct impact on tank function; consequences are recordkeeping only, kept under the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: CMMS work-order closeout enforces some record capture, yet manual entry and after-the-fact logging mean a meaningful fraction of events still go unrecorded or under-documented. Logging completed work adds a documentation step that technicians often defer under time pressure. Recording an event is low-skill, but doing it completely and accurately depends on discipline more than expertise. Digital record capture is cheap, but missing records create downstream compliance and rework costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that preventive maintenance is scheduled after the tank service deadline
rows.imp_rat: Scheduling preventive maintenance after the service deadline can shorten tank uptime, but it is a schedule-administrative error with modest consequences, ranked below the prior batch and capped at 1.5.
rows.sat_rat: Computerized maintenance management systems reliably generate due-date alerts and are widely deployed, though backlog, resource constraints and override still let some PMs slip past the deadline. CMMS scheduling triggers work orders automatically, saving planners the time of manual tracking. Setting up service intervals requires some planning judgement but day-to-day scheduling is largely automated. Scheduling software is a modest fixed cost, and catching PM before the deadline avoids costlier reactive repairs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that scheduled batching-system maintenance conflicts with sterile fill-finish production windows
rows.imp_rat: Maintenance conflicting with production windows is a scheduling-coordination issue only loosely tied to the batching CFJ (very low relevance); severity limited to lost windows. Held to ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Shared calendars and CMMS scheduling reduce collisions, yet last-minute production changes still cause recurring conflicts that people routinely work around. Coordinating maintenance around fill-finish windows relies on planning lead time that is often compressed by shifting production schedules. Effective deconfliction depends on experienced planners who understand both production and maintenance calendars, a scarce combination. Conflicts trigger costly rescheduling and idle production time, but the planning itself is inexpensive.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of scheduled batching-system maintenance work exceeding the available outage capacity
rows.imp_rat: Scheduled maintenance work exceeding outage capacity is a resource-scheduling variability issue peripheral to preparing batches (relevance low), with low-consequence impact holding severity down.
rows.sat_rat: Some sites use capacity planning tools, but mismatches between planned work and available outage time recur often. Scheduled maintenance workload frequently exceeds the outage window because task duration estimates are optimistic. Right-sizing the work against outage capacity requires planning expertise that many sites lack. Overloaded outages force deferrals or overtime, adding cost that better scoping could avoid.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percent of product-contact surface area missed during initial cleaning and sanitization
rows.imp_rat: Missed product-contact surface during cleaning bears on hygiene, but the combined ranking places it below prior items, so relevance is scored low and severity capped at ceiling 1.5.
rows.sat_rat: CIP coverage studies and riboflavin testing are standard, but shadowed or dead-leg areas still get missed enough to leave recurring gaps. Confirming full surface coverage during cleaning adds verification time to the preparation window. Achieving and confirming complete CIP coverage needs trained cleaning-validation staff. Missed areas force re-cleaning cycles that waste chemicals, water and time.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of safety-critical functions that fail functional tests before plant handover
rows.imp_rat: Safety-critical functions failing tests before handover has genuine stakes, but the batch-4 ceiling mandates combined importance at or below 1.5; relevance and severity are constrained accordingly.
rows.sat_rat: Formal pre-handover functional test protocols are well established for safety-critical functions, so undetected failures at handover are relatively uncommon. Functional safety testing before handover is scheduled work that adds time but is planned for. Executing and interpreting safety function tests requires trained commissioning technicians, a skill that is available but not abundant. Fixing a failed safety function late is costly, though pre-handover testing is a modest line item.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of specified performance acceptance criteria the installed plant fails during performance verification
rows.imp_rat: Failing performance acceptance criteria at verification is a commissioning-stage defect; important in principle but ranked low here, so relevance is limited and severity is caught before operation.
rows.sat_rat: Structured PQ/performance verification against defined acceptance criteria is mature practice, so outright failures are moderately well contained though not rare. Performance verification is a defined test phase that consumes scheduled time. Running and judging performance acceptance tests needs experienced commissioning engineers. Failing acceptance criteria forces expensive rework and retesting late in the project.

rows.stk: Plant operators, shift supervisors, training coordinators.
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of safety-, sterility-, or quality-critical operating scenarios omitted from operator training
rows.imp_rat: Omitting safety- or sterility-critical scenarios from operator training has real stakes, yet the batch-4 ranking forces its combined importance at or below 1.5; proxies are pinned low accordingly.
rows.sat_rat: Training programs commonly emphasize normal operation and omit rare critical scenarios, so coverage of the edge cases that matter most is inconsistent. Building scenario coverage into operator training takes time few projects protect during the compressed pre-production window. Anticipating every safety-, sterility- and quality-critical scenario depends on rare trainers who deeply understand both the process and its failure modes. Under-trained operators cause downstream deviations and rework, but the training itself is not the main expense.

rows.stk: Plant operators, shift supervisors, training coordinators.
rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of assigned plant operators who miss required training before pre-production
rows.imp_rat: Operators missing pre-production training is a readiness-delay error, indirectly tied to processing (low relevance) and usually resolved before go-live (low severity). Capped at 1.5.
rows.sat_rat: LMS-based training matrices and attendance tracking are mature, so operators missing required training before pre-production is fairly well controlled. Scheduling operators through required training before pre-production is a planning task that fits the timeline. The need is fundamentally about ensuring operators acquire required competency, the core skill-readiness concern. A missed operator can delay startup, but tracking attendance is inexpensive.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of sterility-control conditions reported incorrectly during shift handover
rows.imp_rat: Incorrectly reported sterility conditions at handover is a contamination concern, yet ranked below the prior batch; relevance is bounded and severity constrained since downstream checks often intercept it.
rows.sat_rat: Sterility-control status is often communicated informally or through partially completed logs, so incorrect reporting at shift change remains a recurring, poorly mitigated failure mode. Verbal handover of sterility conditions is quick but that brevity is exactly what causes omission of nuance. Correctly conveying sterility-control status leans heavily on the outgoing operator's experience and attention, with high variance between skilled and junior staff. A misreported sterility condition can cause batch loss or recall, making the cost of error high while the handover process itself is cheap.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of active alarms, deviations, or temporary operating restrictions omitted from shift handover
rows.imp_rat: Omitting active alarms or restrictions from handover could hide risks, but the ranking caps it; relevance is moderate and severity typically mitigated by redundant records.
rows.sat_rat: Handover checklists and alarm-summary screens exist but are inconsistently used, so active alarms, open deviations and temporary restrictions are still routinely dropped between shifts. Reviewing all active alarms and deviations at handover takes time that rushed shift changes often compress. Judging which alarms and restrictions matter for handover relies on operator experience rather than a systematic prompt. Overlooked deviations or restrictions can escalate into equipment or batch damage, though the handover itself adds little direct cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of conflict between the outgoing operator's account and current plant records
rows.imp_rat: Conflict between operator account and plant records is an ambiguity error at handover; peripheral to core processing and with modest, reconcilable severity.
rows.sat_rat: Because handovers blend informal recollection with formal records and the two are rarely cross-checked systematically, contradictions between operator accounts and plant data persist as a common source of confusion. Reconciling an operator's verbal account against plant records adds time that is often skipped under shift pressure. Spotting conflicts between a narrative account and system data requires an experienced operator who knows what the records should show. Acting on a mistaken account rather than actual plant state can trigger costly interventions, though the conflict check itself is inexpensive.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that processing or output data are linked to the wrong run, batch, or processing stage
rows.imp_rat: Linking data to the wrong run or batch has traceability consequences, but the ranking places this below the prior batch, capping severity and relevance.
rows.sat_rat: MES batch context reduces misassociation but manual entries, concurrent batches and stage transitions still occasionally link data to the wrong run. Correct batch/stage linkage happens automatically at capture, adding no time when the system is configured right. Auto-association reduces reliance on operators to correctly tag data, though setup and exception handling need care. Mislinked data cause investigation and rework cost when they occur.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of mismatch between peer plant conditions and the purchaser's sterile viscous-product requirements
rows.imp_rat: A mismatch between peer plant conditions and a purchaser's sterile viscous-product requirements could compromise product sterility (some severity) but is a transactional/comparison error peripheral to operating the scope (low relevance), and it is ranked below the prior batch.
rows.sat_rat: Truly comparable sterile viscous-product reference plants are rare, so peer comparisons routinely carry hidden mismatches. Sourcing and visiting comparable peer plants is slow and often infeasible within the acquisition window. Judging whether a peer plant's conditions truly match sterile viscous requirements needs experienced eyes. Reference-site visits cost travel and time, and mismatches missed there cost far more later.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required financial and technical justification omitted from the capital request
rows.imp_rat: Omitting financial/technical justification from a capital request is an internal approval-process defect distant from actual processing (low relevance); worst case is a delayed or rejected request (modest severity).
rows.sat_rat: Mature capital-appropriation processes and templates make required justification fairly complete, with occasional technical gaps. Capital request templates speed assembly of the financial and technical justification. Finance and engineering can populate standard justification, though technical depth still needs expert input. Omitted justification elements delay or shrink funding approval, but standard formats capture most cost drivers.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of discrepancy between registered warranty coverage and contracted warranty coverage
rows.imp_rat: A discrepancy between registered and contracted warranty coverage is an administrative paperwork error distant from operation (low relevance), with limited financial exposure (modest severity).
rows.sat_rat: Standard warranty registration and contract records usually align, though occasional clerical discrepancies persist. Reconciling registered versus contracted warranty is a quick document comparison. Warranty registration checks are routine clerical work needing little expertise. A coverage discrepancy risks uncovered repair costs, but the reconciliation itself is cheap.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of viable plant configurations omitted from the market review
rows.imp_rat: Omitting viable plant configurations from a market review is a procurement-research gap distant from operation (low relevance); it may lead to a suboptimal choice but is recoverable (modest severity).
rows.sat_rat: Market reviews rely on known vendors and personal networks, so viable configurations are inconsistently captured. A thorough market review of viable configurations takes considerable research time. Knowing the full field of viable viscous-sterile plant configurations depends on scarce market and technical knowledge. Omitting viable configurations forgoes better-value options, an opportunity cost rarely quantified.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of qualification and regulatory compliance expenditure omitted from the ownership-cost estimate
rows.imp_rat: Leaving qualification and compliance expenditure out of an ownership-cost estimate is a budgeting error peripheral to plant operation (low relevance); it may cause cost surprises but not functional failure (modest severity).
rows.sat_rat: TCO models exist but seldom fully capture sterile-plant qualification, so cost estimates are inconsistently complete. Building qualification and compliance cost into TCO estimates lengthens the capital-approval cycle. Estimating validation and regulatory expenditure accurately requires specialists who understand the qualification burden. Qualification and regulatory spend is frequently underestimated or omitted, so true ownership cost is understated.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of supplied documentation that does not match the installed plant configuration
rows.imp_rat: Documentation not matching the installed configuration is a records-accuracy defect only loosely tied to operation (low relevance), causing confusion during maintenance rather than failure (modest severity).
rows.sat_rat: As-built documentation frequently lags configuration changes, so mismatches are a common, poorly-controlled failure. Cross-checking documentation against as-built configuration is tedious and time-consuming. Verifying doc-to-build consistency needs staff who understand both the design intent and the physical plant. Documentation mismatches cause validation delays and rework costs downstream.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the purchaser and supplier sign different versions of the purchase contract
rows.imp_rat: Signing different versions of a purchase contract is a contract-administration defect outside the processing job (low relevance); it risks disputes but is legally recoverable (modest severity).
rows.sat_rat: Document-management and e-signature controls make signing mismatched versions uncommon in modern procurement. Contract execution is a fast administrative step once terms are agreed. Legal and procurement staff routinely control contract versioning with little specialist burden. A version mismatch is rare and its resolution cost is modest relative to the project.

rows.stk: Procurement managers, project engineers, logistics coordinators
rows.source_job: Coordinate delivery logistics and track progress
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of plant components arriving outside the required installation sequence
rows.imp_rat: Components arriving outside required installation sequence can delay commissioning (moderate severity) but is a logistics-timing error peripheral to the processing job (low relevance), held under the cap.
rows.sat_rat: Project scheduling and delivery-sequencing tools handle most cases, though multi-vendor plants still see sequence slips. Out-of-sequence component arrival directly stalls installation and idles crews. Coordinating delivery sequence is a logistics-planning skill widely held by project managers. Sequence errors cause storage, rehandling and idle-labour costs during installation.

rows.stk: Site engineers, project managers, receiving inspection teams
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of delivered configuration deviations not identified during receiving inspection
rows.imp_rat: Failing to catch delivered configuration deviations at receiving could allow wrong equipment into service (some severity), but this inspection defect is peripheral to operating the scope (low relevance) and ranks below the prior batch.
rows.sat_rat: Checklists and packing documentation catch many deviations, but subtle configuration differences on complex plants still escape. Receiving inspection against configuration lists is a bounded, schedulable activity. Inspectors need moderate training to verify configuration against specs, generally available in-house. Unidentified deviations cause rework whose cost is far higher than the inspection.

rows.stk: Facilities engineers, project engineers, site planners
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of plant footprint or service-clearance conflicts missed during site assessment
rows.imp_rat: Missing footprint or service-clearance conflicts during site assessment can cause install rework (moderate severity) but is a pre-project planning error tangential to ongoing operation (low relevance), capped at 1.5.
rows.sat_rat: 3D layout models and standardized clearance checks catch most footprint conflicts, though service-access details occasionally slip. Site assessments with modern layout tools are reasonably quick to perform. Facilities engineers routinely check footprint and clearances, a well-understood competency. A missed clearance conflict is costly to remedy after delivery, but the assessment itself is inexpensive.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the backup does not represent the current approved tank configuration
rows.imp_rat: A backup not matching the current approved configuration risks restore errors, but it is a data-management concern peripheral to daily processing; held to the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Backups are frequently taken on a schedule disconnected from approved configuration changes, so stale or non-current backups are a common, poorly mitigated pain. Refreshing a backup to match the current configuration is quick if triggered, but it is rarely tied to change events so it lags. Recognizing that a configuration change invalidates the last backup requires attentive change-management discipline that is often lacking. Storage for backups is cheap, so the cost barrier is low, but the failure cost of restoring a stale configuration is high.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of tank components routed to incompatible recycling streams
rows.imp_rat: Routing components to incompatible recycling streams (waste) is an end-of-life environmental error with minimal relevance to processing and low severity; well below the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Scrap dealers and material markings help, but mixed materials and coatings are still routinely routed to the wrong stream. Segregating components into recycling streams adds sorting time at teardown. Identifying alloys, linings and coatings correctly requires material knowledge that varies by crew. Mis-routed components lose recovery value or contaminate recycling loads, a direct cost hit.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required final inspection findings left undocumented at end of service
rows.imp_rat: Undocumented final inspection findings at end of service is a low-relevance recordkeeping defect with limited direct consequence to processing; well below the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Checklists and inspection forms are common, yet findings still get partially captured or lost under time pressure at retirement. Documenting inspection findings is a modest end-of-service administrative task. Recording findings needs an inspector's judgement but is procedurally straightforward. Poor documentation is cheap in the moment but costs later in disputes and rework.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that archived tank records become unavailable during the required retention period
rows.imp_rat: Archived records becoming unavailable during retention risks compliance gaps, but it is an archival concern far from active processing; kept under the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Retention policies exist, but format obsolescence, orphaned files and unindexed archives still make records unavailable when later needed. Archiving is a one-time action that does not slow disposal. Proper records management needs archival discipline more than technical skill. Digital archiving is inexpensive, though long retention and migration carry modest cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of reusable tank accessories damaged during removal
rows.imp_rat: Damaging reusable tank accessories during removal is peripheral to the core processing job and its consequences are minor cost/rework; capped by the batch-5 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: When salvage is prioritized it works, but disposal-mode teardown often treats accessories as scrap and damages them. Careful removal of accessories slows teardown compared to demolition. Salvaging reusable fittings intact takes deliberate, experienced handling. Damaged accessories forfeit resale or reuse value, the core loss this need addresses.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of proprietary recipes, formulations, and configuration data that remains recoverable from onboard storage after removal
rows.imp_rat: Recipe data recoverable from onboard storage after removal is a data-security/decommissioning defect unrelated to executing a batch (relevance minimal); IP exposure severity is capped by the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: There is no standard verified-erasure practice for control systems, so recoverable proprietary data routinely remains on removed hardware. Thorough wiping of onboard storage is time-consuming and frequently omitted before scrapping. Securely purging recipes and configs from embedded controllers demands specialized knowledge of the control system. Certified data destruction services cost money that disposal budgets seldom allocate.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of proprietary process data being exposed to unauthorized parties during removal or disposition
rows.imp_rat: Exposure of proprietary process data during disposition is an end-of-life data-security concern almost unrelated to the batching CFJ (very low relevance); severity limited within this ranked tier. Held to ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Disposal workflows rarely include a reliable data-security step, so exposure of proprietary process data during removal is a largely unmitigated risk. Verifying no proprietary data leaves with removed equipment adds review time that is often skipped under schedule pressure. Knowing where proprietary data resides across controllers and drives requires IT/OT security expertise rarely present at disposal. A data exposure incident carries large IP-loss and liability costs.

rows.stk: Process engineers, procurement leads, project managers.
rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of critical sterile fill-finish specifications evaluated without supporting supplier evidence
rows.imp_rat: Specs evaluated without supplier evidence is a procurement-evidence gap tangential to physically batching liquid (very low relevance) with procedural severity. Capped.
rows.sat_rat: Critical specs are still commonly evaluated on vendor claims without traceable evidence, a recurring reliability weakness. Verifying evidence behind each critical specification is a slow document-intensive task. Distinguishing evidence-backed specs from asserted ones requires experienced technical reviewers. The review labor is significant and unverified specs cause expensive later deviations.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of required automation programs, process recipes, historian records, and configuration files omitted from the preserved export
rows.imp_rat: Omitting automation programs or historian records from a preserved export is a data-preservation defect; low centrality to ongoing processing and severity limited to lost archives.
rows.sat_rat: Without a rigorous inventory of what must be exported, portions of automation and configuration data are routinely omitted. Exporting all programs, recipes, historians and configs is time-consuming across heterogeneous systems. Comprehensive export requires engineers fluent in each vendor platform, a scarce combination. Full-scope export tooling and effort are costly and often scoped down.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that unavailable access credentials or decryption information prevents extraction from source control systems
rows.imp_rat: Missing credentials blocking extraction is a data-migration delay, peripheral to the core processing job and with recoverable, contained severity.
rows.sat_rat: Missing credentials or keys frequently block extraction outright, with few reliable status-quo fallbacks at end of life. Recovering lost credentials or decryption keys can stall extraction for long periods. Circumventing or recovering access to legacy systems demands rare IT/OT security expertise. Vendor callouts and forensic recovery to regain access are costly and unplanned.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that source control systems are decommissioned before all required data is extracted
rows.imp_rat: Decommissioning source control systems before extracting data risks data loss, but this is a transitional/administrative concern peripheral to ongoing liquid processing, so relevance is low and severity is contained to lost records.
rows.sat_rat: Source systems are sometimes powered down or scrapped before full extraction, an irreversible and recurring failure. Extracting all required data before decommissioning source systems takes time that is often squeezed at shutdown. Knowing what data exists and how to pull it from legacy control systems needs specialist controls engineers. Data extraction effort competes for budget with physical demolition and is under-resourced.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that proprietary process data, credentials, or confidential configurations remain recoverable after sanitization
rows.imp_rat: Recoverable proprietary data after sanitization is a data-security defect, peripheral to the processing job with contained confidentiality-exposure severity in this ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Proprietary data often remains recoverable because status-quo wiping is inconsistent and rarely verified for OT devices. Verified sanitization adds time beyond a simple delete or reformat. Ensuring data is truly unrecoverable needs security-savvy staff who understand media-specific wiping. Certified sanitization tools and services cost money that disposal projects may skip.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of retained digital systems unintentionally altered during sanitization
rows.imp_rat: Unintended alteration of retained digital systems during sanitization is a narrow IT-hygiene concern only loosely tied to the plant's core processing job (low relevance), and typically recoverable from backups (low severity). Held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Retained systems are occasionally altered during sanitization when isolation is imperfect, though the risk is moderate. Carefully isolating retained systems during sanitization slows the process. Avoiding accidental changes to retained systems requires careful configuration expertise. Rework from unintended alterations is costly but the preventive effort is modest.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unstable structures, unguarded openings, or sharp remnants left unidentified before handover
rows.imp_rat: Unidentified unstable structures or sharp remnants before handover pose physical hazard, but the ranking places this below the prior batch, capping severity and relevance.
rows.sat_rat: Mature construction/demolition safety inspection regimes catch most physical hazards, though transient ones during active teardown still slip through. Safety walkdowns for physical hazards are quick relative to material testing and are usually scheduled before handover. Spotting unstable structures and sharp remnants is a well-established competency for demolition safety officers. Hazard identification is a low-cost visual activity already built into standard demolition safety practice.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of safety-critical plant areas that remain inaccessible during the final inspection
rows.imp_rat: Inaccessible safety-critical areas during final inspection has some safety relevance, but the ranking constrains it below prior items, capping both proxies.
rows.sat_rat: A meaningful fraction of safety-critical areas remain inaccessible at final inspection, leaving verification incomplete despite good intentions. Gaining access to sealed or hazardous areas for inspection often requires clearing and staging work that delays the final inspection. Deciding how to safely access contaminated or unstable areas needs specialist rigging and confined-space expertise. Providing access (scaffolding, remote tooling, decontamination) is costly, so some areas are left uninspected.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of safety-critical conditions that cannot be verified because decommissioning or utility-isolation records are missing or conflicting
rows.imp_rat: Unverifiable safety conditions from missing isolation records carry safety weight, but the ranking places this below prior items, holding both proxies low.
rows.sat_rat: Missing or conflicting isolation records are endemic, leaving safety-critical conditions unverifiable — a chronic pain with poor status-quo mitigation. Chasing down missing or conflicting isolation records consumes time crews rarely have at closeout. Interpreting fragmentary decommissioning and isolation records to confirm a safe state requires deep system knowledge that is often lost. Reconstructing missing records after the fact is expensive and frequently deferred.

rows.stk: Plant managers, compliance officers, records management teams.
rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of required batch, quality, compliance, and lifecycle records omitted from the archive
rows.imp_rat: Omitting required records from the archive is a compliance-documentation error, peripheral to the operational job (low relevance); consequences carry some regulatory weight but sit below the prior batch, so severity stays low-moderate.
rows.sat_rat: Because records are scattered across generations of media and departing personnel, omissions are common and frequently only discovered when a record is later demanded, so completeness is only partially assured. Assembling a complete records archive at decommissioning is a slow, manual reconciliation across decades of paper and disparate systems, so completeness suffers under schedule pressure. Knowing which batch, quality, compliance, and lifecycle records are legally required demands scarce documentation and QA expertise that plants losing staff at closure often lack. Chasing down and digitizing legacy records is labor-intensive but rarely the budget-breaker of a decommissioning project.

rows.stk: Plant managers, compliance officers, records management teams.
rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of archived records that are corrupted or unreadable after plant retirement
rows.imp_rat: Corrupted archived records after plant retirement is a post-lifecycle administrative error, peripheral to processing (low relevance), with consequences limited to records access rather than operations (low-moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Established archival and backup practice keeps most records readable, but format obsolescence and media decay still cause losses over time. Archive integrity checks are quick relative to the retention timeline. Maintaining readable long-term archives requires records-management expertise that is inconsistently applied. Durable archival storage and periodic integrity checks carry ongoing cost often neglected after retirement.

rows.stk: Plant managers, compliance officers, records management teams.
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that authorized custodians are unable to access archived records after decommissioning
rows.imp_rat: Custodians unable to access archived records after decommissioning is a post-lifecycle access issue, peripheral to the operational job (low relevance); consequences are compliance-oriented and bounded (low-moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Media degradation, format obsolescence, and lost credentials make future access uncertain, a well-known chronic pain that standard archival practice only partly mitigates. Retrieving archived records years after decommissioning can take days or weeks when media formats and systems have been retired. Accessing legacy formats often requires specialized IT knowledge and defunct software that few remaining custodians retain. Maintaining readable long-term archives and migration paths costs money that is often deprioritized once a plant closes.

rows.stk: Plant managers, project engineers, compliance officers.
rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of plant areas, systems, and utility connections omitted from the final-condition documentation
rows.imp_rat: Missing plant areas or utility connections from final-condition documentation is a records-completeness defect with limited direct relevance to product processing and mild severity, being correctable. Held at or below the 1.5 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Utility connections and minor systems are commonly omitted from final-condition documentation because there is no systematic completeness check. Documenting every area, system and utility connection is laborious and often compressed at project end. Ensuring completeness requires someone who knows the full plant scope, and such people are scarce at disposal. Comprehensive as-disposed documentation is a cost that projects routinely trim.

rows.stk: Plant managers, project engineers, compliance officers.
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of discrepancies between recorded decommissioned states and actual plant states at handover
rows.imp_rat: Discrepancies between recorded and actual decommissioned states at handover carry some safety consequence, but this item ranks lower than the prior batch, so both relevance and severity are held low.
rows.sat_rat: Records and field reality drift apart during dismantling and status-quo change control is weak, so discrepancies at handover are frequent. Reconciling recorded states against actual field states requires repeat walkdowns that lag behind fast-moving demolition work. Judging whether an as-left state matches the record needs experienced inspectors familiar with the original plant configuration. Verification walkdowns and re-surveys add labor cost that projects try to minimize.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that critical supplier capability gaps remain undetected during validation
rows.imp_rat: Undetected supplier capability gaps during validation can propagate defects, but the ranking places this below the prior batch; relevance to the plant's core processing job is indirect and severity is moderate at worst.
rows.sat_rat: Even with validation protocols, critical supplier capability gaps often surface only after contract, making this an unreliably served need. Detecting capability gaps during validation depends on audits and trials that extend the timeline. Uncovering hidden supplier capability gaps requires seasoned auditors who can probe beyond stated claims. Supplier audits and validation testing are costly and sometimes trimmed, letting gaps go undetected.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of capability requirements interpreted differently by procurement, engineering, and quality reviewers
rows.imp_rat: Differing interpretation of capability requirements among reviewers is a pre-procurement documentation ambiguity, marginal to actual processing (low relevance) and self-correcting in review (low severity). Capped at 1.5.
rows.sat_rat: Requirement specs and evaluation matrices help, but ambiguous wording still leads different functions to score the same capability inconsistently. Divergent interpretations trigger rework and clarification loops that lengthen the evaluation period. Consistent reading of capability requirements depends on experienced reviewers sharing tacit understanding that is rarely codified. Misinterpretation mainly wastes review effort rather than committing project money at this point.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of mismatch between supplier-declared engineering and fabrication capacity and capacity available for the project schedule
rows.imp_rat: Mismatch between declared and available supplier capacity affects scheduling of a project rather than ongoing plant operation (low relevance), with consequences that are manageable through replanning (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Suppliers routinely overstate available capacity and buyers have weak means to verify it, so this mismatch recurs frequently. Verifying real available capacity extends evaluation timelines when suppliers are slow to disclose loading. Judging the gap between declared and truly available capacity demands seasoned assessors who can interrogate supplier workload. Capacity mismatches lead to delay penalties and expediting costs downstream rather than upfront spend.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unresolved mismatches between proposed plant connections and site interfaces at the compatibility decision
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved mismatches between proposed plant connections and site interfaces at the compatibility decision matter to integration, but the ranking places this below the prior batch, capping its centrality and consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Established P&ID and interface-matrix practices catch most connection mismatches, though some are still discovered at installation. Interface reconciliation is time-bounded when site drawings and plant connection specs are both available. Matching connections to site utilities needs engineering competence that is usually present in plant projects. Correcting interface mismatches late is costly, but the check itself is inexpensive.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the purchase commitment is issued without required internal authorization
rows.imp_rat: Issuing a purchase commitment without authorization is a procurement-governance defect, peripheral to plant processing with contained financial/compliance severity.
rows.sat_rat: Mature ERP purchase-approval workflows and delegation-of-authority matrices make unauthorized commitments rare in established organizations. Formal approval routing adds some cycle time, but electronic workflow systems have made sign-offs reasonably fast. Approval gates are procedural and require little specialized skill once the authority matrix is defined. Delegation-of-authority controls directly prevent unauthorized financial commitments, protecting spend well.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of financial exposure beyond the approved price created by the purchase commitment
rows.imp_rat: Financial exposure beyond the approved price from a purchase commitment is a budgetary/contractual error peripheral to processing (low relevance), with consequences limited to cost impact (low-moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Purchase controls and firm-price terms limit overruns reasonably well, though variations and escalation clauses still create some exposure. Financial approval and price-cap controls add minor review time. Capping exposure to the approved price is procedural once commercial terms are set, needing modest specialist input. Fixed-price contracts and approved budgets directly constrain financial exposure beyond the sanctioned amount.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required configuration evidence unavailable before the acquisition decision
rows.imp_rat: Missing configuration evidence before an acquisition decision is a procurement-stage delay error with low centrality to running the plant and only moderate downstream consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Status quo relies on ad hoc document requests and supplier promises, so a meaningful fraction of configuration evidence is still unverified when the decision is made. Chasing missing configuration evidence late in the acquisition cycle stretches decision timelines, but the delay is a symptom rather than the primary loss here. Determining which configuration evidence is required demands experienced engineers who often carry that knowledge informally rather than in a standardized checklist. Missing evidence rarely shows up as direct spend until a wrong commitment is made, so cost impact is indirect at this stage.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of mandatory scope, sterile-processing, validation, delivery, or commercial requirements omitted from the quotation request
rows.imp_rat: Omitting mandatory scope or sterile-processing requirements from a quotation request is a procurement-stage administrative gap; relevance to actually running liquid processing is low and severity is minor since it is caught and corrected upstream. Capped per batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Templated RFQs cover common items, yet sterile-processing, validation, or commercial requirements are still frequently omitted, causing recurring downstream gaps. Building a complete quotation request is time-consuming when many requirement categories must be covered. Ensuring nothing mandatory is omitted relies on experienced buyers and engineers knowing the full requirement set. Omissions cause costly change orders later, but drafting the RFQ is inexpensive.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required sterile fill-finish quality and validation deliverables omitted from the purchase order
rows.imp_rat: Omitted quality/validation deliverables from a purchase order is a documentation-completeness error remote from the batching CFJ (very low relevance); consequences are contractual (low severity). Capped.
rows.sat_rat: Standard PO templates and deliverable checklists capture most items, but occasional omissions still slip into contracts. Ensuring all quality and validation deliverables reach the PO adds a checklist step at contracting. Knowing the full set of required validation deliverables depends on procurement staff versed in GMP acquisition. Omitted deliverables force costly retroactive negotiation, though the check itself is cheap.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of production delays caused by operator access conflicts
rows.imp_rat: Production delays from operator access conflicts are a peripheral scheduling nuisance with very low relevance to the core batching job and minor, recoverable severity.
rows.sat_rat: Role-based HMI access and shift scheduling largely prevent conflicts, though poorly staffed shifts still create bottlenecks. Operator access conflicts cause occasional short queuing delays when multiple tasks compete for the same panel or resource. Resolving the conflict is a coordination matter needing no special skill. The lost time is minor and rarely causes material loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of production interruption while switching the batching system to the next recipe
rows.imp_rat: Interruption while switching recipes is a routine changeover delay—some relevance to throughput but trivial, recoverable severity. Held to ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Automated recipe download makes the control-side switch reliable, but the physical cleaning and changeover remain the dominant, only partially optimized delay. Recipe changeover still involves cleaning, revalidation and setup that create significant production interruption between products. Efficient changeovers depend on skilled operators executing CIP and setup sequences correctly. Downtime during switchover ties up capital-intensive vessels and consumes cleaning resources.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of recipe targets or tolerance limits that conflict across verification records
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting recipe targets across verification records is a documentation-consistency ambiguity, only loosely tied to actually batching liquid (relevance low), with modest consequences that keep severity minimal.
rows.sat_rat: Where a single validated recipe master feeds all records conflicts are rare, but siloed spreadsheets and duplicated spec entries still let inconsistent limits persist. Reconciling conflicting targets across verification records is a slow manual review when discrepancies surface. Detecting conflicting tolerance limits requires trained QA reviewers who understand the recipe specification hierarchy. Conflicts caught late can invalidate batches, but the direct cost is mostly rework of documentation.

rows.stk: Procurement leads, quality engineers, regulatory affairs personnel.
rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of applicable sterile fill-finish requirements unsupported by supplier evidence
rows.imp_rat: Requirements unsupported by supplier evidence is an administrative evidence gap barely central to actually batching liquid (very low relevance); consequences are procedural (low severity). Capped.
rows.sat_rat: Suppliers routinely leave requirements without evidence and reviewers often accept assertions, so this gap persists frequently. Chasing supporting evidence for each applicable requirement extends evaluation cycles considerably. Judging whether supplier evidence actually substantiates a requirement takes trained assessors who can evaluate documentation quality. Evidence gathering and review is labor-intensive, and unsupported requirements risk costly compliance failures.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of control-system accounts issued with shared or non-unique credentials
rows.imp_rat: Shared or non-unique control-system credentials is an access-governance ambiguity peripheral to the CFJ (relevance minimal); security consequences exist but severity is held under the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Modern control systems and audit expectations push strongly toward unique accounts, so shared credentials are increasingly rare though legacy shortcuts persist during rushed commissioning. Enforcing unique credentials during provisioning adds minimal time when identity systems are in place. Avoiding shared credentials is a well-understood administrative discipline requiring little specialized skill. Shared credentials create audit and traceability exposure whose cost is largely deferred rather than immediate.
