rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of process control or batch data being overwritten before backup completion
rows.imp_rat: Batch data overwritten before backup completion is a data-loss risk, but as a lower-ranked batch-4 item it is capped; relevance and severity are set low under the 1.5 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Modern historians buffer and confirm backup completion before purging, so overwrite-before-backup is largely controlled, though high-throughput data bursts and storage-full conditions still occasionally cause premature overwrite. Backup windows are scheduled around production cycles, and overwrite protection rarely delays operators noticeably. Configuring buffering, retention and backup-completion locks requires specialist IT/OT skill but is a one-time setup rather than a recurring operator burden. Redundant storage and buffering to hold data until backup completes adds hardware cost that plants generally absorb as standard infrastructure.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of backed-up process control or batch records being altered or deleted without authorization
rows.imp_rat: Unauthorized alteration of backed-up batch records is a data-integrity concern, but as a batch-4 item it is ranked below prior items and capped; relevance and severity are set low to respect the 1.5 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Immutable audit trails, write-once storage and privileged-access management make unauthorized alteration or deletion rare where validated systems are deployed, though insider misuse and misconfiguration remain residual risks. Access-control checks add negligible time since authentication is transparent to authorized users during normal operation. GMP-compliant historians and role-based access controls are standard in regulated liquid-processing plants, requiring little operator skill to enforce once configured by IT/validation staff. Part -compliant systems with audit trails carry meaningful licensing and validation cost but are treated as a mandatory baseline in pharma and food plants.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that required parts or consumables are unavailable when scheduled service begins
rows.imp_rat: Unavailable parts when service begins delays maintenance, but relevance to the core processing job is indirect and the severity is a schedule slip rather than harm.
rows.sat_rat: CMMS-driven kitting and min/max stocking are standard and mostly work, yet stockouts of long-lead or rarely-used parts still occur. A missing part at service start stalls the whole job until it is sourced, extending downtime. Accurate spares provisioning needs planners with strong equipment and BOM knowledge. Kitting reduces waste but excess safety stock ties up capital, so the tradeoff is imperfect.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of sterile fill-finish production downtime beyond the approved shutdown window
rows.imp_rat: Sterile fill-finish downtime beyond the approved shutdown window matters for throughput, but per the mandatory batch-4 ceiling its combined importance cannot exceed 1.5; relevance and severity are pinned low accordingly.
rows.sat_rat: Turnaround planning tools exist and mostly work, but scope discovery and requalification delays still push finishes past the approved window. Planned shutdown windows are scheduled but overruns beyond the approved window are a frequent occurrence when work uncovers surprises. Keeping within window relies on experienced planners and crews who can sequence and troubleshoot fast. Every hour past the approved window is expensive lost sterile production plus overtime labor.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of repair delay caused by unavailable required components
rows.imp_rat: Repair delay from unavailable components extends downtime, but relevance to the core job is indirect and severity is a schedule cost rather than harm.
rows.sat_rat: Spares strategies help, but long-lead and obsolete parts routinely leave repairs waiting. When a required component is unavailable, repair sits idle for the full procurement or fabrication lead time. Expediting or improvising a substitute demands experienced maintenance and procurement judgement. Emergency freight and expediting premiums make these delays costly to shorten.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of sterile fill-finish production interruption beyond the planned maintenance window
rows.imp_rat: Production interruption beyond the planned maintenance window affects output timing but is ranked below the previous batch and capped; relevance and severity are held low per the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Mature planned-maintenance practice covers the routine cases, yet real execution variance keeps overruns a recurring gap. Planned maintenance windows are defined but interruptions commonly bleed past them when tasks run long. Staying inside the planned window depends on skilled schedulers and technicians executing to plan. Overrun beyond the planned window converts into lost fill-finish revenue and premium labor cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of alignment shift while the coupled equipment is being secured
rows.imp_rat: Alignment shift while securing coupled equipment affects mechanical integrity, yet the ranking constrains it below prior items, keeping relevance and severity minimal.
rows.sat_rat: Laser alignment tools and staged tightening make soft-foot and securing shift well controlled by competent crews, though it still happens. Rechecking after tightening adds a modest step but is routinely built into alignment procedures. Minimizing bolt-tightening-induced shift depends on technician technique and torque sequencing skill. Alignment shift on securing causes accelerated wear, but the corrective effort is comparatively cheap.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of applying incorrect alignment tolerances because configuration information conflicts
rows.imp_rat: Applying incorrect alignment tolerances due to conflicting configuration data affects assembly quality, yet the ceiling holds relevance and severity low.
rows.sat_rat: Fragmented, out-of-date, or conflicting configuration records remain common, so wrong tolerances get applied with disturbing frequency. Resolving conflicting tolerance data mid-job stalls the alignment until the correct spec is confirmed. Judging which of several conflicting configuration sources is authoritative requires experienced engineering judgement. Applying a wrong tolerance can force rework or cause wear-related failures, incurring downstream cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of plant unavailability caused by seized, obstructed, or inaccessible consumables during replacement
rows.imp_rat: Downtime from seized consumables during replacement is a maintenance-delay error; relevance to core processing is indirect and severity is a bounded availability loss.
rows.sat_rat: Good design-for-maintenance and preventive replacement mitigate this, but corrosion and fouling still cause unpredictable seizures. Seized or inaccessible consumables force extra extraction effort that lengthens the replacement outage. Freeing stuck components without collateral damage relies on hands-on technician skill and the right tools. Extra labor and occasional collateral part damage add modest cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of sterile fill-finish production unavailability beyond the scheduled maintenance window
rows.imp_rat: Sterile production unavailability beyond the scheduled maintenance window is a throughput delay ranked below prior items; its combined importance is capped at 1.5, so proxies stay low.
rows.sat_rat: Standard scheduling handles most jobs, but requalification and unexpected findings still cause overruns. Scheduled maintenance durations are estimated but unavailability regularly extends past the scheduled window. Meeting the scheduled window leans on experienced crews and coordinators. Time beyond schedule is directly costly in idle capacity and labor.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required preventive maintenance activities omitted from the plant schedule
rows.imp_rat: Omitted preventive maintenance items matter, but as a scheduling-completeness error they are one step removed from the immediate processing job (low relevance) and usually absorbed before failure (low severity here per ranking). Capped at 1.5.
rows.sat_rat: CMMS auto-generation of PM work orders is mature and standard, so wholesale omissions are now uncommon in well-configured systems. A dropped PM task adds no immediate time and often goes unnoticed until failure. Building a complete PM schedule needs reliability-engineering knowledge, but once set it runs with modest skill. Omitted PM saves nothing and risks costly failures, though the scheduling itself is inexpensive.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of maintenance activities scheduled after their required service deadlines
rows.imp_rat: Maintenance scheduled past service deadlines is a timing-compliance error, indirectly related to processing continuity (low relevance) and usually caught before failure (low severity per ranking). Capped at 1.5.
rows.sat_rat: CMMS due-date tracking and overdue reporting are widely used, so most tasks hit their windows, with a persistent overdue tail. Late scheduling compresses time and can force rushed execution, but the deadline miss itself is a discrete event. Prioritizing against deadlines needs planner judgement to balance production and compliance. Overdue PMs raise failure risk and possible regulatory cost, though scheduling adds little expense.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that scheduled maintenance overlaps sterile production commitments
rows.imp_rat: Maintenance scheduling overlapping sterile production commitments is a coordination-instability issue ranked below prior items; capped at 1.5, so relevance and severity stay low.
rows.sat_rat: Integrated production/CMMS scheduling reduces clashes in well-run plants, though siloed calendars still let overlaps slip through periodically. Detecting a clash late forces last-minute rescheduling that wastes coordination time. Avoiding overlap depends on planners who understand both production commitments and maintenance needs, a coordination skill in short supply. An overlap that halts a sterile run is expensive, but the scheduling itself costs little.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of maintenance activities assigned to windows lacking required shutdown and contamination-control prerequisites
rows.imp_rat: Assigning maintenance to windows lacking contamination-control prerequisites carries contamination risk, yet the ranking caps it here; relevance to daily processing is modest and consequences are usually caught before harm.
rows.sat_rat: Prerequisite checks in permit and work-order systems are inconsistent, so tasks still land in windows lacking their controls. Discovering missing shutdown or contamination-control prerequisites at the window forces delay while they are arranged. Knowing which prerequisites each task needs requires experienced planners familiar with sterile-area constraints. Aborting or reworking an improperly prepared window wastes labor and mobilization cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of residual liquid product remaining in pipework and vessels after purging and flushing
rows.imp_rat: Residual liquid after purging affects yield and cross-contamination, but the ranking sets it below prior batch items so relevance is low and severity capped at ceiling 1.5.
rows.sat_rat: Validated CIP/flush recipes and drainability design control this well, though dead legs and complex geometries still trap residue. Extra purge cycles to clear residual product lengthen the changeover but are procedurally bounded. Effective flush design and endpoint judgement require process knowledge of dead legs and drainability. Residual product left behind is wasted material and drives more flushing medium use.

rows.stk: Maintenance engineers, plant operators, reliability teams.
rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of plant health indicator data unavailability during plant operation
rows.imp_rat: Plant health indicator data unavailability during operation touches monitoring, but its position below the prior batch keeps relevance low; brief unavailability is usually absorbed without incident, so severity is low.
rows.sat_rat: Historians and condition-monitoring are widely deployed, but sensor dropouts and unmonitored assets still leave data unavailable at times. Gaps in health-indicator data create blind windows during operation that delay detection of developing faults. Diagnosing during data gaps forces reliance on expert intuition instead of instruments. Sensor and historian infrastructure to close gaps carries capital and maintenance cost.

rows.stk: Maintenance engineers, plant operators, reliability teams.
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of false plant health alerts that trigger unnecessary maintenance action
rows.imp_rat: False plant health alerts triggering unnecessary maintenance is an efficiency nuisance rather than a core-job blocker (low relevance), with low, largely wasted-effort consequences (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Alarm thresholds are commonly poorly tuned, so nuisance alerts and alarm floods remain a chronic, widely-felt problem. False alerts waste technician time investigating non-issues and can interrupt production needlessly. Distinguishing false from real alerts demands skilled analysts to tune thresholds and interpret signals. Unnecessary maintenance actions triggered by false alerts waste labor and parts.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of stored equipment components that sustain mechanical damage during the outage
rows.imp_rat: Mechanical damage to stored equipment during an outage is peripheral to running liquid processing operations (low relevance) and its consequences are limited to a subset of idle assets (moderate-low severity). The batch ceiling caps it regardless.
rows.sat_rat: Standard storage, tagging and preservation procedures prevent most damage, though handling and environment lapses still occur occasionally. Damage discovered on reinstall causes rework delay, but storage itself takes no active time. Proper preservation and handling of stored components requires disciplined technician practice. Damaged stored parts mean replacement cost and wasted preservation effort.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of critical operational records missing from the deviation interval
rows.imp_rat: Missing critical records in a deviation interval is a documentation-completeness defect that does not alter the batch itself (relevance minimal); severity capped despite audit implications.
rows.sat_rat: Automated historians capture most process data, but critical manual and lab records still go missing from deviation intervals with troubling frequency. Assembling complete records for a deviation interval is time-consuming when data is scattered. Knowing which critical records are required demands quality expertise applied case by case. Missing critical records can force batch rejection, an expensive outcome the status quo only partly prevents.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of timestamp misalignment across ingredient delivery, vessel, control, and equipment records
rows.imp_rat: Timestamp misalignment across delivery, vessel, control, and equipment records is a data-reconciliation ambiguity tangential to the CFJ (relevance minimal); severity limited and ranked below the prior batch.
rows.sat_rat: Independent PLCs, MES and lab systems with unsynchronized clocks routinely drift, and network time synchronization is inconsistently deployed, leaving cross-record misalignment a common problem. Aligning timestamps across separate systems during a review is a slow, manual correlation task. Cross-system reconciliation demands staff who understand each system's clock and logging behavior. Misalignment across records mainly costs investigation effort rather than material.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of ingredient loading interruptions caused by an intake blockage
rows.imp_rat: Intake blockages interrupting ingredient loading add delay (relevance) but are typically recoverable (severity); the ceiling keeps it at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Strainers, level sensors and flow monitoring catch some blockages but sticky or agglomerating ingredients still cause unpredictable intake stoppages. Intake blockages typically require manual clearing that stalls loading for a noticeable stretch of the cycle. Clearing a blockage is mostly a manual, moderately-skilled task but locating the cause can require experience. Downtime and occasional wasted partially-loaded material impose real but bounded cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of finished-batch hold while the downstream destination is unavailable
rows.imp_rat: Finished-batch hold for an unavailable downstream destination is a logistics delay peripheral to the batching job (relevance), with modest risk to product if held too long (severity); capped at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Surge/hold vessels are standard practice and predictably absorb short downstream stalls, though extended outages still back up the line. Finished-batch holds when downstream is busy are common and can idle a batch for extended periods until capacity frees up. Managing the hold is a routine scheduling task requiring little specialized skill. Holding tanks and buffer capacity exist to absorb this but tie up vessel capacity and energy for temperature maintenance.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of mismatch between recorded timestamps and actual production events
rows.imp_rat: Timestamp-vs-event mismatch is a record-integrity defect that does not affect the physical batching job (relevance minimal); consequences for data trust exist but are capped under the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Automated data historians timestamp events consistently at the source, so recorded-versus-actual mismatch is generally small where a synchronized control system logs directly. Timestamp mismatches surface during review and cost investigation time to reconcile. Interpreting timestamp drift requires some analyst familiarity but is not highly specialized. Misaligned timestamps rarely destroy material but can cost compliance effort to explain.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time from production-run completion until the complete batch record is available for traceability and reporting
rows.imp_rat: Time until the complete batch record is available is a documentation-latency concern peripheral to the CFJ (low relevance) with minor, recoverable impact (low severity). Below ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: The record does become complete reliably; the issue is how long completion takes rather than whether it happens. Electronic batch records shorten availability lag, but manual review, sign-offs and lab result integration still delay completion by hours to days. Timely record closure depends on reviewers being available and skilled at resolving exceptions quickly. Delayed record availability can hold up product release and cash flow, a real carrying cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of ambiguity in the alarm and batch-state indications available to the technician
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguous alarm and batch-state indications slow correct technician response (relevance), though consequences are more nuisance-level (severity); capped at 2.0 by the ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Alarm rationalization standards (ISA- exist but are inconsistently applied, so alarm flooding and vague batch-state indications remain a widespread recurring source of confusion. Ambiguous alarms force technicians to spend extra time cross-checking screens and logs before acting, but this diagnosis is usually minutes not hours. Interpreting cryptic or flooding alarms leans heavily on experienced operators' tacit knowledge, and less-skilled staff routinely misread batch state. The cost impact is indirect, showing up as occasional wrong responses rather than direct spend.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of charging delays caused by unavailable released batch components
rows.imp_rat: Charging delays from unavailable components is an upstream scheduling issue, marginal to blending and low in consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Component availability timing is variable and unpredictable. Charging delays from unavailable released components are a recurring wait. Availability depends on supply/QA, not operator skill. Delays waste labor and vessel occupancy.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of ambiguities or conflicts in the endpoint acceptance criteria
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguities in endpoint acceptance criteria is a specification-clarity concern peripheral to the physical blend and low in immediate consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Endpoint criteria are usually defined but occasional ambiguities persist. Resolving criteria ambiguity mid-batch causes delay. Interpreting ambiguous acceptance criteria needs experienced judgement. Acting on ambiguous criteria risks batch decisions and loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time spent waiting for required endpoint evidence before confirming the batch endpoint
rows.imp_rat: Waiting for endpoint evidence is a timing delay, only loosely tied to confirming blend completion; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Endpoint evidence turnaround is variable, making confirmation timing unpredictable. Waiting for endpoint evidence before confirming is a frequent delay. Evidence turnaround depends on lab/sampling, not operator skill. Waiting ties up the vessel and labor.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of blending after the defined endpoint has been reached
rows.imp_rat: Over-blending past the endpoint is a minor waste/energy item; it does not jeopardize homogeneity and carries low consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Defined mix times keep post-endpoint blending fairly controlled. Stopping promptly at endpoint requires timely detection to avoid over-blending. Recognizing the endpoint to stop needs operator judgement. Over-blending wastes energy and vessel time.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time an agitation excursion persists before operator response begins
rows.imp_rat: Agitation excursion persisting before response is a response-timing concern; ranked below the prior batch, so proxies remain low.
rows.sat_rat: Alarms on speed/load make agitation excursion recognition fairly prompt. Time from agitation excursion to response start depends on detection speed. Recognizing an agitation excursion needs operator vigilance. Delayed response risks blend defects and rework.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required processing entries left blank during compounding
rows.imp_rat: Blank required processing entries is a record-completeness concern peripheral to the physical blend and low in immediate impact.
rows.sat_rat: Required fields are still left blank during busy compounding despite review. Completing all fields adds documentation time. Filling required entries relies on operator diligence. Blank entries drive investigations, not material loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of entries missing required performer attribution or verification
rows.imp_rat: Missing performer attribution/verification is a record-completeness concern, marginal to executing the blend and low in direct consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Entries still get made without required performer attribution or verification. Adding signatures/verifications takes documentation time. Attribution discipline is a trained GMP habit. Missing attribution drives review rework, not material loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of conflicts in batch-record timestamps or event sequences
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting timestamps/event sequences is a record-integrity ambiguity, peripheral to executing the blend and low in immediate consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Manual timestamping frequently produces sequence conflicts in records. Reconciling timestamp conflicts slows record review. Consistent time recording depends on discipline. Timestamp conflicts cost review effort, not material.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of batch-record entries that cannot be interpreted unambiguously
rows.imp_rat: Uninterpretable batch-record entries is a documentation-clarity concern, marginal to the physical blend and low in immediate consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Handwritten records still produce entries that cannot be interpreted unambiguously. Deciphering unclear entries slows review. Legible, unambiguous recording depends on operator care. Uninterpretable entries drive investigations, not material loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of waiting for a required component at its scheduled sequence step
rows.imp_rat: Waiting for a component at its sequence step is a scheduling delay upstream of blending with low direct consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Just-in-time staging is imperfect, so step waits recur. Waiting for a component at its sequence step stalls the batch. Availability is logistics-driven, not skill-driven. Sequence-step waiting wastes vessel and labor time.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time between temperature-excursion onset and operator recognition
rows.imp_rat: Time to operator recognition of a temperature excursion is a detection-latency concern peripheral to the blend and low in direct impact.
rows.sat_rat: Continuous temperature monitoring with alarms makes recognition prompt and reliable. Time from temperature excursion onset to recognition depends on monitoring. Little skill needed when temperature alarms are present. Delayed recognition risks thermal degradation loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of temperature excursions caused by repeated thermal cycling
rows.imp_rat: Temperature excursions from repeated thermal cycling is a stability concern, marginal to a single blend and low in immediate consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Tuned control loops largely prevent oscillatory thermal cycling. Avoiding thermal cycling may require slower ramping. Managing setpoints to avoid cycling needs some control knowledge. Repeated cycling wastes energy and can degrade product.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time compounding start is delayed because the vessel is unavailable
rows.imp_rat: Delayed compounding start from vessel unavailability is only peripherally related to the CFJ of dissolving/blending; capped at 1.0 by batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Vessel turnaround and cleaning timing make availability variable. Delayed start from vessel unavailability is a recurring scheduling loss. Vessel availability is scheduling-driven, not skill-driven. Start delays waste labor and campaign time.

rows.stmt: Minimize the time spent waiting for approved components before charging can begin
rows.imp_rat: Waiting for approved components before charging is a scheduling delay upstream of blending, marginal to the job and low in consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Release timing is variable and often causes unpredictable charging delays. Waiting for released components before charging is a common scheduling delay. No operator skill affects material release timing. Idle waiting consumes labor and vessel occupancy cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of component charging beyond the planned charging interval
rows.imp_rat: Charging beyond the planned interval is a timing-efficiency concern, marginal to blend quality and low in consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Manual charging pace varies, so overruns occur intermittently. Charging beyond planned interval extends the operation. Efficient charging technique keeps duration down. Overrun charging costs labor and occupancy.

rows.stmt: Minimize the time spent waiting for required in-process results before batch completion
rows.imp_rat: Waiting for in-process results is a throughput timing issue not affecting blend success and with minor, recoverable consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Lab turnaround is variable, making completion timing unpredictable. Waiting for in-process lab results before completion is a frequent bottleneck. Result turnaround depends on the lab, not operator skill. Waiting ties up the vessel and labor.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of completed-batch hold in the vessel before transfer
rows.imp_rat: Completed-batch hold in the vessel is a throughput/scheduling delay with negligible effect on blend success and low consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Validated hold limits keep holds within bounds reliably. Completed-batch hold before transfer adds waiting governed by scheduling. Hold duration is scheduling-driven, not skill-driven. Extended hold ties up the vessel.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time spent reconciling execution discrepancies before vessel release
rows.imp_rat: Reconciling execution discrepancies before release is documentation rework, not core to producing the blend; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Manual records generate discrepancies that consistently require time-consuming reconciliation. Reconciling discrepancies before release is a known review bottleneck. Resolving execution discrepancies needs experienced review judgement. Reconciliation time delays release and ties up resources.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of the batch-parameter excursion after recognition
rows.imp_rat: Duration of a parameter excursion after recognition is a response-timing concern; while excursions matter, this timing item is ranked and capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Manual recovery makes excursion clearance times variable. Excursion duration after recognition depends on speed of corrective action. Fast effective correction relies on operator skill. Prolonged excursion risks degradation and material loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of a restored parameter drifting outside its permitted range before compounding resumes
rows.imp_rat: A restored parameter drifting before resumption is a recovery-stability concern, peripheral to the main blend and low in direct consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Restored parameters often drift again before resumption under manual control. Waiting to confirm a restored parameter is stable adds time. Stabilizing a parameter after restoration needs skilled tuning. Re-drift forces repeated correction and possible loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of doubt that vessel-batch entries are complete and defensible
rows.imp_rat: Doubt that batch entries are complete is record-quality uncertainty, remote from the CFJ; capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of uncertainty about which vessel observation to record when indications conflict
rows.imp_rat: Uncertainty over which observation to record is a documentation-decision issue, peripheral to blending; capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of anxiety spikes while awaiting evidence for a vessel-batch entry
rows.imp_rat: Anxiety spikes awaiting entry evidence is an emotional-timing concern, peripheral to blending; capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of feeling exposed after discovering an omission or correction in the batch record
rows.imp_rat: Feeling exposed after a record omission is an emotional/documentation concern, low relevance to blending; capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of unresolved doubt about homogeneity and parameter conformance at the endpoint decision
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved doubt about homogeneity at endpoint is closer to the CFJ but as framed is a decision-confidence concern; capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of visible uncertainty while others await endpoint direction
rows.imp_rat: Visible uncertainty while others await endpoint direction is a perception/timing concern, peripheral to blending; capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of anxiety while awaiting final evidence needed to confirm the endpoint
rows.imp_rat: Anxiety while awaiting endpoint evidence is an emotional-timing concern, low relevance to homogeneity; capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of second-guessing the endpoint decision after the batch is released
rows.imp_rat: Second-guessing endpoint decision after release is a post-hoc anxiety concern, peripheral to the CFJ; capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of doubt about aseptic-boundary integrity during vessel handling
rows.imp_rat: Doubt about aseptic-boundary integrity during handling is a contamination-perception concern, tangential to blending; capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of visible uncertainty about whether a vessel-handling step remained controlled
rows.imp_rat: Visible uncertainty about a controlled handling step is a documentation-perception concern, tangential to the CFJ; capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of doubt caused by conflicting sterile-handling instructions during charging or blending
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting sterile-handling instructions during charging is an ambiguity concern, somewhat near execution but capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of anxiety spikes while awaiting clearance for a questionable handling step
rows.imp_rat: Anxiety spikes awaiting clearance for a handling step is an emotional-timing concern, peripheral to blending; capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that reviewers attribute an audit finding to the compounder's inaccurate batch documentation
rows.imp_rat: Attributing an audit finding to inaccurate documentation is a records-blame error tangential to the mixing task (low relevance), with reputational fallout kept low by the cap (low severity).

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of credibility loss when vessel-compounding entries are judged noncontemporaneous
rows.imp_rat: Credibility loss over noncontemporaneous entries is a documentation-timing error tangential to homogeneity (low relevance), with reputational fallout kept low by the cap (low severity).

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of compounding decisions being second-guessed because batch records appear inconsistent
rows.imp_rat: Decisions second-guessed over inconsistent records is a documentation-trust error tangential to producing homogeneous solution (low relevance); consequences are review friction, ranking-capped (low severity).

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of correction requests that reinforce a careless-documentation reputation
rows.imp_rat: Correction requests reinforcing a careless reputation is a documentation-perception error tangential to blending (low relevance), with reputational fallout held low by the cap (low severity).

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that reviewers attribute a batch deviation or rejection to the operator's compounding execution
rows.imp_rat: Reviewers attributing a deviation to operator execution is a blame-attribution error tangential to the blend outcome (low relevance), with reputational-only consequences bounded by the cap (low severity).

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of operator credibility lost when bulk-solution quality is questioned at handoff
rows.imp_rat: Operator credibility lost when bulk quality questioned is reputational, tangential to blending; capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of the operator being second-guessed during charging, dissolution, or blending decisions
rows.imp_rat: An operator second-guessed during charging/dissolution is a confidence error only loosely tied to the outcome (low relevance); its consequence is hesitation, kept low by the cap (low severity).

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of the operator being bypassed for critical compounding batches because of perceived inconsistency
rows.imp_rat: An operator being bypassed for perceived inconsistency is a personnel-trust error remote from the blend job itself (low relevance); its impact is career-perception only and ranking-capped (low severity).

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of dread triggered by unexplained contamination signals during charging or mixing
rows.imp_rat: Dread from unexplained contamination signals is an emotional-response item, peripheral to the core job and low in direct consequence.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of anxiety while awaiting clarification of a suspected bioburden or foreign-material event
rows.imp_rat: Duration of anxiety awaiting bioburden clarification is an experiential need with negligible bearing on blend success and trivial consequence.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of feeling exposed when unable to justify a mixing parameter change during batch review
rows.imp_rat: Feeling exposed over justifying a parameter change is an interpersonal-review concern remote from blending homogeneity; ranking caps it at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of anxiety triggered while awaiting evidence needed to judge batch homogeneity
rows.imp_rat: Operator anxiety while awaiting evidence is an emotional/experiential need with negligible bearing on whether components dissolve and blend and trivial consequence.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a sequence or parameter deviation is attributed to operator indiscipline during batch review
rows.imp_rat: Attribution of a deviation to operator indiscipline is a blame concern, tangential to blending; capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of credibility lost with Quality Assurance because of gaps in the compounding batch record
rows.imp_rat: Credibility lost with QA over record gaps is reputational, remote from the mixing job; capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of being bypassed for high-scrutiny compounding batches because of perceived execution unreliability
rows.imp_rat: Being bypassed for high-scrutiny batches is a reputational/staffing concern, remote from the CFJ; capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a dissolution or homogeneity deviation is attributed to the operator's mixing decisions during batch review
rows.imp_rat: Attribution of a deviation to operator decisions is a blame/review concern, peripheral to the CFJ; capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of credibility lost with production and quality leadership after a batch contradicts the operator's assurances
rows.imp_rat: Credibility lost after a batch contradicts assurances is reputational, remote from the mixing job itself; capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency with which leadership second-guesses the operator's judgment about mixing parameters and addition sequence
rows.imp_rat: Leadership second-guessing operator judgment is reputational, remote from producing homogeneity; capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency with which leadership bypasses the operator when defining mixing parameters and addition sequence for later batches
rows.imp_rat: Leadership bypassing operator on parameters is reputational, remote from producing homogeneity; capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of investigations reopened because root-cause evidence was insufficient
rows.imp_rat: Investigations reopened for insufficient evidence is a rework error tangential to the blend job (low relevance); its consequence is redone effort, held low by the cap (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Evidence standards reduce reopenings, but insufficient initial evidence still triggers them periodically. Reopened investigations add substantial rework time. Sufficient first-pass evidence gathering depends on investigator skill. Reopening consumes duplicate investigative resources.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that approved corrective action fails to address the confirmed root cause
rows.imp_rat: Corrective action failing to address root cause is a CAPA-quality error tangential to the mixing task (low relevance), with recurrence consequences kept low by the cap (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: CAPA processes aim for root-cause alignment, but corrective actions still sometimes fail to address the real cause. An ineffective corrective action means recurrence, costing more time later. Matching corrective action to true root cause depends on skilled investigators. Ineffective corrective action leads to repeat losses.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time the scheduled batch waits for released ingredients
rows.imp_rat: Batch waiting for released ingredients is an upstream scheduling delay with negligible effect on blend success and low consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Release delays recur due to QC turnaround, making this a familiar bottleneck. Batches routinely wait for released ingredients, a common source of delay. Coordinating release timing is a planning and QA-interface skill. Waiting for release idles the vessel, wasting capacity.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time the vessel remains unavailable beyond its scheduled handoff
rows.imp_rat: Vessel unavailable beyond handoff is scheduling delay, peripheral to producing homogeneous solution; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Handoffs usually occur near schedule, but overruns from prior steps propagate into late availability. Vessel unavailability beyond scheduled handoff directly delays downstream batches. Timely handoff depends on coordination discipline more than technical skill. Late handoff idles subsequent scheduled capacity.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time from scheduled in-process sampling to availability of results needed to proceed
rows.imp_rat: Time from sampling to results availability is a throughput-timing concern peripheral to executing the blend and low in direct impact.
rows.sat_rat: Sampling and assay workflows reliably produce results, but the wait itself persistently stalls progression. Waiting for in-process sample results to proceed is a well-known delay, gated by lab turnaround. The delay is driven by lab throughput, not operator skill. rows.imp_rat: Uncertainty duration while awaiting the vessel recommendation is a decision-timing concern, marginal to executing the blend and low in consequence.

rows.imp_rat: Inability to defend the homogeneity recommendation is a communication/defensibility concern, marginal to executing the blend and low in consequence.

rows.imp_rat: Unresolved doubt about contamination during evaluation is evaluation-phase uncertainty, tangential to blending execution; capped at 1.0.

rows.imp_rat: Confidence erosion from conflicting cleanability claims is an evaluation-ambiguity concern, marginal to blending and low in consequence.

rows.imp_rat: Defending the recommendation against contamination concerns is evaluation advocacy, peripheral to blending; capped at 1.0.

rows.imp_rat: Doubt about scientific rationale surviving validation is a qualification-confidence ambiguity, remote from blending and low in immediate consequence.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of inconsistent interpretations of vessel acceptance criteria by qualification and validation reviewers
rows.imp_rat: Inconsistent reviewer interpretation of acceptance criteria is a qualification-process ambiguity, remote from executing the blend and low in operational consequence.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of being unable to answer an unanticipated challenge to vessel specification rationale
rows.imp_rat: Being unable to answer a spec-rationale challenge is a review-anxiety concern, remote from execution; capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a homogeneity failure is attributed to the specifier's vessel requirements by engineering and quality leadership
rows.imp_rat: Homogeneity failure attributed to specifier's requirements is a blame concern, peripheral to execution; capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of engineering and quality leadership second-guessing the specifier's mixing and thermal evaluation criteria
rows.imp_rat: Leadership second-guessing evaluation criteria is reputational, remote from mixing execution; capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the specifier is bypassed when mixing, heating, cooling, or agitation requirements are changed or waived
rows.imp_rat: Specifier bypassed on requirement changes is reputational, remote from producing homogeneity; capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of credibility lost when vessel recommendations differ across comparable formulations without scientific justification
rows.imp_rat: Credibility lost from unjustified varying recommendations is reputational, remote from mixing execution; capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that QA and validation reviewers judge vessel capability recommendations scientifically unjustified
rows.imp_rat: Reviewers judging capability unjustified is a qualification-defensibility concern remote from the blend and low in operational consequence.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of inspection-readiness findings attributed to untraceable vessel specification rationale
rows.imp_rat: Inspection findings from untraceable specification rationale is an administrative traceability concern, marginal to blending and low in immediate impact.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of the evaluator being bypassed in final vessel specification decisions because stakeholders distrust the evaluation criteria
rows.imp_rat: Evaluator bypassed in spec decisions is reputational, remote from producing the blend; capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of vessel recommendations being reversed under qualification scrutiny because specification rationale is inadequate
rows.imp_rat: Reversed vessel recommendations under qualification scrutiny is a rework/documentation concern far from executing the blend and low in operational consequence.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of variation in residual levels across repeated vessel cleaning cycles
rows.imp_rat: Variation in residual levels across cleaning cycles is a cleaning-consistency concern, marginal to executing the blend and low in immediate consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Three-cycle cleaning validation is standard, giving reasonable control though variation still occurs. Repeated cleaning-cycle testing extends validation. Interpreting residual variation needs cleaning-validation competence. Multiple cycles consume analytical resources.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of liquid retained in vessel low points and internal features after drainage
rows.imp_rat: Liquid retained in low points after drainage is a residue/cleaning concern, peripheral to the blend job and low in direct consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Sloped, drainable designs are standard, keeping retained liquid generally low but not eliminated. Measuring drainage holdup adds testing time. Quantifying low-point retention uses standard drainability methods. Drainage testing is low material cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of product-contact locations inaccessible for post-cleaning assessment
rows.imp_rat: Inaccessible contact locations for post-cleaning assessment is a design/inspectability concern remote from blending and low in immediate impact.
rows.sat_rat: Sanitary design minimizes inaccessible locations, though a few product-contact points typically remain hard to assess. Identifying inaccessible locations adds inspection planning time. Recognizing inaccessible points needs inspection and design familiarity. Boroscope or teardown inspection adds some cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree to which cleanability evidence departs from intended vessel geometry, residue load, and production conditions
rows.imp_rat: Cleanability evidence departing from real geometry/conditions is a qualification-fidelity concern, peripheral to blending and low in immediate impact.
rows.sat_rat: Generic cleanability data commonly departs from actual vessel geometry and production conditions, a persistent gap. Producing geometry- and condition-specific cleanability evidence takes substantial study time. Adapting evidence to actual geometry and residue load requires cleaning-validation expertise. Representative worst-case studies consume material and analytical cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that cleaning conditions required to meet acceptance limits exceed vessel or facility constraints
rows.imp_rat: Cleaning conditions exceeding facility constraints is a cleanability-feasibility concern remote from the blend job and low in immediate consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Cleaning feasibility is usually checked but constraint conflicts still surface late in qualification. Reconciling cleaning conditions with facility constraints adds planning time. Judging feasibility against constraints needs facility and cleaning knowledge. Infeasible conditions can force costly facility changes.

rows.stmt: Reduce the rate of product-contact surface or seal degradation under repeated cleaning exposure
rows.imp_rat: Surface/seal degradation under repeated cleaning is a durability concern, marginal to a single blend and low in immediate consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Seal degradation under repeated cleaning is only partially assessed, so failures still emerge in service. Repeated-cleaning degradation testing extends validation time. Evaluating seal/surface wear needs materials-durability expertise. Durability studies consume material.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required cleanability evidence missing from the vessel suitability assessment
rows.imp_rat: Missing cleanability evidence in the suitability assessment is a documentation-gap concern, remote from executing the blend and low in direct consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Cleanability assessment is a standard step but required evidence is frequently incomplete for new geometries. Assembling cleanability evidence extends the suitability assessment timeline. Determining sufficient cleanability evidence relies on cleaning-validation specialists. Cleaning studies consume solvents, swabs, and analytical resources.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of qualification requirements lacking documented supporting evidence
rows.imp_rat: Qualification requirements lacking supporting evidence is a documentation-completeness gap, remote from the blend job with low direct consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Requirement-evidence mapping is standard practice and catches most gaps, though some requirements remain undocumented. Gathering supporting evidence for each requirement is slow when documents span multiple sources. Recognizing adequate supporting evidence relies on GMP-literate reviewers with checklists. Evidence gathering is coordination cost, not material spend.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of qualification requirements without a verifiable link to supporting records
rows.imp_rat: Requirements without verifiable links to records is a documentation-traceability gap, remote from the blend and low in direct consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Traceability matrices are standard but manual maintenance leaves some links unverifiable, especially in large packages. Linking requirements to records manually is a tedious, time-consuming reconciliation. Maintaining verifiable requirement-record links needs disciplined documentation practice. Mostly administrative labor cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of contradiction among conclusions in compiled qualification records
rows.imp_rat: Contradiction among qualification-record conclusions is documentation ambiguity, peripheral to mixing; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Consolidated review usually surfaces contradictions, but some inconsistencies persist in large packages. Reconciling contradictory conclusions across records adds review time. Detecting contradictions needs experienced cross-document review. Review labor cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of qualification claims unverifiable against source records
rows.imp_rat: Unverifiable qualification claims against source records is an integrity/documentation concern peripheral to blending with low immediate impact.
rows.sat_rat: Source verification during review catches many issues, but unverifiable claims still surface in audits. Verifying claims against source records is slow, document-by-document work. Auditing claims to source requires meticulous QA reviewers. Verification is labor-intensive but not material-heavy.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unresolved deviations in records supporting vessel qualification
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved deviations in qualification records are documentation defects, tangential to the mixing job; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Deviation management systems reliably track and close most deviations before recommendation. Resolving record deviations extends qualification closure time. Deviation resolution needs QA judgement that is generally available. Deviation handling is labor cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of delay in receiving qualification evidence needed for the vessel recommendation
rows.imp_rat: Delay receiving qualification evidence is a timing concern, peripheral to producing homogeneity; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Evidence delivery timing is unpredictable and frequently late, poorly served by current practice. Waiting on vendor and internal qualification evidence is a chronic, hard-to-compress delay. Expediting evidence needs coordination skill but not deep technical expertise. Delays carry schedule and carrying costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of product-contact components omitted from the compatibility assessment
rows.imp_rat: Contact components omitted from compatibility assessment is a documentation-completeness gap, remote from executing the blend and low in direct impact.
rows.sat_rat: BOM-driven compatibility reviews are standard but minor components are still occasionally omitted. Cataloguing all product-contact components takes methodical time. Ensuring completeness needs component-level familiarity with the vessel BOM. Component review is administrative cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of change in product-contact surface compatibility after repeated processing and sanitation exposure
rows.imp_rat: Compatibility change after repeated processing is a long-term material-stability concern, marginal to a single blend and low in immediate consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Repeated-exposure compatibility change is rarely fully characterized, leaving a persistent gap. Assessing compatibility change over repeated exposure requires long-term or accelerated studies. Predicting aging effects needs specialized materials-degradation expertise. Aging studies consume material and extended lab time.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unresolved discrepancies in product-contact material identity and composition records
rows.imp_rat: Discrepancies in material identity records are documentation ambiguity, tangential to blending; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Material certificates and MTRs usually resolve identity, though discrepancies occasionally remain unresolved. Reconciling material identity records adds documentation-review time. Verifying material identity needs QA and materials familiarity. Mostly administrative cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of compatibility variation across material lots and fabricated surface conditions
rows.imp_rat: Compatibility variation across lots/surfaces is a material-variability concern remote from the current blend and low in direct impact.
rows.sat_rat: Lot and surface-condition variation is seldom explicitly qualified, so this remains poorly served. Testing across material lots and surface conditions adds substantial time. Judging lot-to-lot and finish variation needs deep materials expertise. Multi-lot testing is material- and cost-intensive.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of qualification acceptance criteria lacking objective pass-or-fail definitions
rows.imp_rat: Acceptance criteria lacking objective pass/fail definitions is a qualification-clarity issue peripheral to blending and low in consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Objective acceptance criteria are the norm in good protocols, yet ambiguous criteria still appear and cause disputes. Writing objective pass/fail criteria upfront takes time but is a one-time protocol effort. Converting vague requirements into objective criteria demands experienced validation authors, a recurring skill dependency. This is protocol-authoring labor with negligible material cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of cleaning, sterilization, and residue-control requirements lacking explicit acceptance limits
rows.imp_rat: Cleaning/residue requirements lacking acceptance limits is a specification-clarity gap peripheral to the blend and low in direct consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Acceptance limits are standard in cleaning validation, though some requirements still lack explicit limits. Defining explicit acceptance limits for all cleaning/sterilization requirements takes protocol time. Setting residue and sterilization limits needs validation and toxicology input. Limit derivation is analyst labor.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of critical vessel measurements lacking requirements for accuracy, calibration status, or record integrity
rows.imp_rat: Measurements lacking accuracy/calibration requirements is a metrology-specification gap, peripheral to the blend and low in immediate consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Calibration and data-integrity requirements are well established, so critical measurements rarely lack them. Documenting accuracy, calibration, and data-integrity requirements adds setup time. Specifying metrology and data-integrity requirements needs calibration expertise. Calibration programs carry ongoing cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of unresolved conflict among quality, validation, engineering, production, and user requirements
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved conflict among stakeholder requirements is a requirements-alignment issue, remote from blending; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Cross-functional review forums exist but unresolved requirement conflicts commonly linger into recommendation. Resolving cross-functional requirement conflicts is time-consuming negotiation. Reconciling competing stakeholder requirements demands strong facilitation and technical judgement. Conflict resolution is meeting and labor cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of qualification-criteria rework caused by requirement changes after vessel evaluation begins
rows.imp_rat: Qualification-criteria rework from requirement changes is a process-rework concern, peripheral to the CFJ; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Change control limits uncontrolled changes but post-start requirement shifts still trigger recurring rework. Late requirement changes force rework that directly extends the evaluation timeline. Managing change impact needs experienced change-control judgement. Rework consumes labor and can waste completed studies.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of mismatch between the evaluated vessel configuration and the configuration available for purchase
rows.imp_rat: Configuration mismatch between evaluated and purchasable vessel is a procurement discrepancy, low relevance to homogeneity; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Change control usually keeps configurations aligned, though vendor spec drift occasionally causes mismatch. Confirming evaluated-versus-purchasable configuration adds verification time. Detecting configuration mismatch needs procurement and technical coordination. Mismatch can cause costly reprocurement.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of qualification delay caused by conflicts with facility constraints or adjacent processing stages
rows.imp_rat: Qualification delay from facility conflicts is a scheduling concern, remote from the mixing job; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Facility-driven qualification delays are common and only partially mitigated by scheduling practice. Facility and adjacent-stage conflicts routinely delay qualification scheduling. Coordinating around facility constraints needs planning skill. Delays incur schedule cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that vessel ranking changes when unverified evaluation assumptions are corrected
rows.imp_rat: Vessel ranking changing when assumptions corrected is an evaluation-robustness concern, remote from mixing; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Unverified assumptions are seldom stress-tested, so ranking instability is a real, under-addressed risk. Testing sensitivity of ranking to assumptions adds analysis time. Assessing how corrected assumptions shift rankings needs analytical rigor rarely applied. Sensitivity analysis is labor cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of doubt that the recommended vessel will pass qualification at final signoff
rows.imp_rat: Doubt the vessel passes qualification is an evaluation-confidence concern, tangential to blending; capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of confidence erosion when qualification criteria and vessel evidence support conflicting conclusions
rows.imp_rat: Confidence erosion when criteria and evidence conflict is an emotional-decision error not central to producing solution (low relevance); consequences are personal hesitation, ranking-capped (low severity).

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of anxiety while evidence needed to defend qualification readiness remains unresolved
rows.imp_rat: Duration of anxiety while defense evidence is unresolved is a personal-stress error unrelated to blending (low relevance), with emotional consequences bounded by the cap (low severity).

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of uncertainty when reviewers challenge evidence for reproducible dissolution and blending
rows.imp_rat: Uncertainty when reviewers challenge reproducibility evidence is a review-anxiety concern, tangential to the CFJ; capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of calm erosion when vessel qualification records conflict with supporting claims
rows.imp_rat: Calm erosion from conflicting qualification records is an emotional-documentation concern, tangential to the CFJ; capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of anxiety while gaps in documented qualification evidence await clarification
rows.imp_rat: Anxiety while qualification-evidence gaps await clarification is a personal-stress error unrelated to blending (low relevance), with emotional consequences ranking-capped (low severity).

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of alarm when an unexamined vessel constraint emerges near purchase approval
rows.imp_rat: Alarm when an unexamined constraint emerges is a decision-anxiety error unrelated to blending (low relevance); consequences are emotional and ranking-capped (low severity).

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of doubt in the vessel recommendation when technical evidence conflicts
rows.imp_rat: Doubt in a recommendation amid conflicting evidence is a decision-confidence error not central to the mixing job (low relevance); its consequence is hesitation, held low by the cap (low severity).

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of feeling exposed when challenged on an unresolved vessel requirement during purchase review
rows.imp_rat: Feeling exposed on an unresolved requirement is a personal-anxiety error unrelated to producing solution (low relevance); consequences are emotional-only and capped (low severity).

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of anxiety spikes when unresolved qualification questions surface before purchase approval
rows.imp_rat: Anxiety spikes over unresolved qualification questions is a personal-stress error unrelated to the blend outcome (low relevance); consequences are emotional-only and capped (low severity).

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a regulatory contamination or compliance finding is attributed to the purchase influencer's vessel recommendation
rows.imp_rat: Regulatory finding attributed to the influencer's recommendation is a blame/liability concern, remote from mixing; capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of credibility lost with quality reviewers when the vessel evaluation rationale is challenged
rows.imp_rat: Credibility lost when the evaluation rationale is challenged is a reputational error outside the mixing function (low relevance), with consequences limited to standing and capped (low severity).

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency with which validation stakeholders second-guess the purchase influencer's judgment on reproducible dissolution and blending
rows.imp_rat: Validation stakeholders second-guessing dissolution/blending judgment is an influence error tangential to the actual blend (low relevance), with credibility consequences ranking-capped (low severity).

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency with which project stakeholders perceive inconsistency in the purchase influencer's qualification rationale
rows.imp_rat: Stakeholders perceiving inconsistency in the influencer's rationale is an influence-credibility error unrelated to the blend task (low relevance); consequences are perception-only and capped (low severity).

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that quality or validation leadership attributes a vessel qualification failure to the advisor's recommendation
rows.imp_rat: Qualification failure attributed to the advisor is a blame concern, remote from producing homogeneity; capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of credibility lost with quality and validation leadership when a recommended vessel causes process-validation rework
rows.imp_rat: Credibility lost when vessel causes validation rework is reputational, remote from the CFJ; capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency with which the advisor is bypassed in subsequent vessel decisions after qualification evidence challenges a recommendation
rows.imp_rat: The advisor bypassed after a challenged recommendation is a role-continuity error unrelated to the mixing task (low relevance); consequences are standing-only and capped (low severity).

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency with which quality and validation leadership challenges the advisor's criteria as unfit for qualification or process-validation needs
rows.imp_rat: Quality leadership challenging the advisor's criteria is an advisory-credibility error only tangential to the mixing job (low relevance), with consequences confined to influence loss and capped by ranking (low severity).

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of water, cleaning agent, and energy consumed during validated vessel cleaning
rows.imp_rat: Water/cleaning-agent/energy consumption is a resource-efficiency concern, not central to homogeneity; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Consumption is consistent and predictable, but conservative overuse to guarantee cleanliness means efficiency is rarely optimized. Utility consumption is a resource issue rather than a time issue, with negligible schedule impact. Optimizing water and chemical use requires some engineering effort but no scarce expertise once recipes are set. Validated cycles are typically designed for assurance rather than minimal utility use, so water, CIP chemicals, and energy are often consumed conservatively beyond strict need.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of operator labor required for vessel cleaning
rows.imp_rat: Operator labor for cleaning is a labor-efficiency matter, not central to dissolution; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Reduced operator involvement also lowers human-error variability, making automated cleaning both cheaper and more consistent in labor terms. Automated CIP already removes most cleaning labor time from the operator's day for standard cycles. Where labor remains it is manual and technique-dependent, but CIP has reduced reliance on skilled cleaning effort substantially. Automation has cut recurring operator labor cost markedly, leaving mainly residual manual touch-points and oversight.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of vessel unavailability during cleaning and release activities
rows.imp_rat: Vessel unavailability during cleaning/release is throughput timing, peripheral to the CFJ; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Turnaround times are fairly predictable under validated procedures, but analytical release lead times keep total unavailability longer than desired. Cleaning plus sampling, analysis, and release commonly hold vessels out of service for extended periods, the core loss this need targets. Scheduling and release decisions need coordination expertise but are routine for planning staff. Downtime represents significant opportunity cost in capacity, though it is expected and planned for.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of unresolved contamination-control uncertainty at specification sign-off
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved contamination uncertainty at sign-off is an evaluation-phase concern, tangential to blending; capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of feeling exposed when the vessel specification's cross-contamination safeguards cannot be explained
rows.imp_rat: Feeling exposed over unexplained cross-contamination safeguards is a review-anxiety concern, tangential to the CFJ; capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of anxiety while awaiting evidence needed to resolve vessel contamination-control concerns
rows.imp_rat: Anxiety awaiting contamination-control evidence is an emotional-timing concern, peripheral to the CFJ; capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which the vessel recommendation lacks traceability to qualification criteria
rows.imp_rat: Recommendation lacking traceability to qualification criteria is a documentation-traceability concern peripheral to blending and low in direct impact.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of conflicting interpretations of vessel cleanability requirements during audit review
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting cleanability interpretations at audit is documentation ambiguity, tangential to the CFJ; capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of visible uncertainty when an auditor challenges the vessel-selection rationale
rows.imp_rat: Visible uncertainty when auditor challenges rationale is a review-anxiety concern, tangential to the mixing job; capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of lingering doubt that completed vessel cleaning left residue behind
rows.imp_rat: Lingering doubt about cleaning residue is a contamination-perception concern, tangential to blending; capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of anxiety while awaiting evidence that vessel cleaning met residue limits
rows.imp_rat: Anxiety awaiting residue-limit evidence is an emotional-timing concern, low relevance to homogeneity; capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of fearing that the vessel recommendation must be reopened after an apparent cleaning pass
rows.imp_rat: Fear the recommendation must be reopened after a cleaning pass is an emotional-rework concern, tangential to the CFJ; capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a formal compliance finding is attributed to the specifier's vessel evaluation criteria
rows.imp_rat: Compliance finding attributed to evaluation criteria is a blame concern, tangential to mixing; capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of vessel-recommendation challenges caused by an incomplete qualification rationale
rows.imp_rat: Recommendation challenges from incomplete rationale is a documentation-completeness concern peripheral to the blend and low in operational impact.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that engineering or procurement bypasses the specifier because their compliance judgment is not trusted
rows.imp_rat: Engineering bypassing the specifier over trust is an influence error unrelated to producing homogeneous solution (low relevance), with consequences limited to role standing and capped (low severity).

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of credibility lost with validation and manufacturing stakeholders through inconsistent vessel-selection rationale
rows.imp_rat: Credibility lost from inconsistent selection rationale is reputational, remote from the mixing job; capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a sterile-product contamination finding is attributed to the vessel recommendation before quality and manufacturing leadership
rows.imp_rat: Attribution of a sterile contamination finding to the recommendation is a blame/liability concern, peripheral to execution; capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of credibility lost with quality leadership when the vessel cleanability rationale fails scrutiny
rows.imp_rat: Credibility lost when cleanability rationale fails is reputational, remote from producing homogeneity; capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency with which the specifier is excluded from future vessel decisions after manufacturing leadership disputes a prior recommendation
rows.imp_rat: Excluding the specifier from future decisions is a role-continuity error unrelated to the blend job (low relevance), with career-standing consequences bounded by the cap (low severity).

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that public blame for a missed sterile-readiness commitment is assigned to the specifier's vessel criteria
rows.imp_rat: Public blame for a missed sterile-readiness commitment is a reputational/political error, not central to homogeneous blending (low relevance); its consequences are indirect and the cap fixes it low (low severity).

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of acquisition, integration, qualification, operating, and retirement costs omitted from the compounding vessel financial case
rows.imp_rat: Omitted lifecycle costs from the financial case is a costing completeness issue unrelated to blending; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Without a standard TCO template most cases capture only capital outlay, making omissions the norm rather than the exception. Building a full lifecycle cost picture is slow because operating and retirement costs must be estimated from scattered sources. Total-cost-of-ownership modelling requires financial and engineering expertise that most purchase executors lean on others to supply. Lifecycle costs like qualification, energy, maintenance, and decommissioning are frequently omitted, so the financial case understates true spend.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of compounding vessel financial assumptions not traceable to verifiable source evidence
rows.imp_rat: Financial assumptions not traceable to evidence is a business-case defect, remote from the CFJ; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Most financial cases lack formal source-linking, so traceability gaps are widespread and rarely audited before approval. Tracing each assumption back to source evidence is laborious and slows case preparation. Establishing evidentiary traceability requires analytical rigor not consistently applied by procurement staff. Untraceable assumptions carry no direct spend but can conceal costly errors.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of mismatch among commercial scopes used to compare compounding vessel acquisition options
rows.imp_rat: Mismatch among commercial comparison scopes is a procurement-analysis error remote from the physical job (low relevance), with consequences limited to evaluation clarity and capped (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Scope normalization is attempted routinely but supplier proposals differ enough that mismatches still slip through. Normalizing scopes across options adds comparison effort but is a familiar procurement step. Building apples-to-apples comparisons is a standard sourcing competency the executor applies. Mismatched scopes can hide the true cost differences between options, distorting the award decision.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of economic benefit attributed to vessel throughput, yield, labor, and downtime without supportable evidence
rows.imp_rat: Unsupported economic-benefit attribution is a financial-justification error remote from the blend job (low relevance); its consequence is credibility of the case, ranking-capped (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Benefit claims are commonly asserted without supporting evidence, making this a persistent weakness in typical cases. Substantiating benefit claims adds analysis time that is frequently under-resourced. Evidencing throughput, yield, labor, and downtime benefits requires operational analytics skill outside procurement. Overstated benefits can justify overspending on the vessel relative to real returns.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of errors in calculations connecting compounding vessel costs, cash impacts, and approval criteria
rows.imp_rat: Errors in cost/cash/approval calculations are a finance-accuracy error remote from the dissolve/blend job (low relevance); consequences are budgeting errors, held low by the cap (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Spreadsheet models and finance review catch many errors, though manual linkages still allow occasional mistakes. Checking calculation linkages is fairly quick with spreadsheet-based cases. Basic financial arithmetic connecting costs to approval criteria is within the executor's competence. Calculation errors can misstate cash impact and lead to wrong funding decisions.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unresolved conflicts among batch-demand, vessel-utilization, installation-schedule, and funding inputs
rows.imp_rat: Conflicts among demand/utilization/schedule/funding inputs are a coordination error outside the physical blending task (low relevance); the fallout is administrative and ranking-capped (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: These inputs are usually owned by different functions and rarely reconciled systematically, so conflicts persist. Resolving conflicts among demand, utilization, schedule, and funding inputs extends preparation time. Aligning these interdependent inputs requires integrative planning expertise beyond core procurement. Unresolved conflicts risk sizing or timing errors that carry cost consequences.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of financial exposure from sterile batch loss, contamination, qualification failure, and production interruption left unquantified
rows.imp_rat: Unquantified financial exposure from batch loss is a costing concern, remote from the mixing job; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Financial exposure from sterile failures is almost never quantified in typical cases, leaving a chronic blind spot. Quantifying risk exposure adds significant lead time and is often skipped under schedule pressure. Modelling exposure from batch loss, contamination, and qualification failure requires risk-quantification expertise well outside the procurement role. Unquantified exposures leave large potential financial losses invisible in the case.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of divergence between financial-case commercial inputs and terms available when the compounding vessel is contracted
rows.imp_rat: Divergence between financial-case inputs and contract terms is a commercial-variance concern, remote from the CFJ; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Terms often shift between case-building and contracting with no formal refresh step, so divergence is common. Reconciling case inputs with live contracting terms adds a re-verification step near authorization. rows.stmt: Reduce the amount by which financing charges exceed the approved economic threshold for vessel acquisition
rows.imp_rat: Financing charges exceeding threshold is a finance-variance concern, remote from blending; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Standard finance review keeps financing costs within threshold fairly reliably. Financing terms are usually set upfront so timing rarely drives this error. Evaluating financing charges against hurdle rates is core finance skill readily available. Financing charges exceeding the economic threshold represent avoidable excess cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that committed capital is unavailable when contractual vessel payments are due
rows.imp_rat: Capital unavailable when payments are due is a finance-timing concern, peripheral to blending; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Established capital-commitment and milestone-tracking practices make funds reliably available when payments fall due, with only occasional reprioritization risk. Treasury cash-flow scheduling generally aligns fund release with payment milestones on a predictable timeline. Managing committed-capital availability is routine treasury practice requiring modest specialist finance skill. Funds set aside for contractual payments are normally ring-fenced, limiting cost surprises.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of conflicting capital figures across vessel funding, authorization, and procurement documents
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting capital figures across documents is a financial-consistency error peripheral to mixing (low relevance), with reconciliation consequences kept low by the cap (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Multiple document owners and versions make figure conflicts a frequent, poorly-controlled error.
