Reconciling conflicting capital figures across documents takes time that pressured cycles compress. Cross-document reconciliation needs finance and procurement diligence more than rare skill. Conflicting figures can cause over-commitment or misdirected payments.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of financing conditions that conflict with vessel procurement and qualification obligations
rows.imp_rat: Financing conditions conflicting with procurement obligations is a contractual-consistency error remote from the blend job (low relevance); consequences are compliance friction, capped low (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Financing and procurement terms are usually reconciled, so conflicts are relatively infrequent. Financing conditions are set upfront so timing rarely drives conflicts. Spotting financing conditions that clash with procurement obligations needs treasury-legal coordination skill. Conflicting conditions can force costly renegotiation or penalties.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that committed vessel capital is withdrawn before purchase contract execution
rows.imp_rat: Capital withdrawn before contract execution is a finance-stability concern, peripheral to the CFJ; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Capital-approval locks are fairly durable but reprioritization or cash constraints occasionally rescind funds before signing. The window between approval and contract execution is usually short enough to limit withdrawal exposure. Protecting committed capital through to contract needs governance discipline more than scarce skill. A withdrawn commitment forces re-authorization and can raise cost through lost pricing.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of feeling personally exposed when the vessel authorization rationale faces quality or regulatory scrutiny
rows.imp_rat: Feeling exposed under regulatory scrutiny of the rationale is a personal-stress error remote from the blend job (low relevance); consequences are emotional-only and capped (low severity).

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of uncertainty that vessel supplier-selection evidence will withstand quality and regulatory scrutiny
rows.imp_rat: Uncertainty that selection evidence withstands scrutiny is a procurement-defense error peripheral to the blend job (low relevance); consequences are audit exposure, held low by the cap (low severity).

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of doubt that vessel contract terms adequately support quality and regulatory obligations
rows.imp_rat: Doubt that contract terms support obligations is a contractual-clarity error remote from the blend job (low relevance), with compliance consequences held low by the cap (low severity).

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of uncertainty that funded vessel capacity will deliver the throughput and availability promised in the investment case
rows.imp_rat: Uncertainty that capacity delivers promised throughput is a performance-assurance error peripheral to the blend job (low relevance); its consequence is delivery risk, ranking-capped (low severity).

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of doubt caused by conflicting evidence that funded vessel capacity can meet sterile fill-finish commitments
rows.imp_rat: Doubt that capacity meets sterile commitments is a planning-confidence error peripheral to the blend job (low relevance), with delivery-risk consequences held low by the cap (low severity).

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of occasions when the purchase executor feels exposed by unanswered questions about the vessel-capacity commitment
rows.imp_rat: Occasions feeling exposed on the capacity commitment is a personal-anxiety error remote from mixing (low relevance); consequences are emotional-only and held low by the cap (low severity).

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of uncertainty that the vessel commitment fits the approved capital envelope
rows.imp_rat: Uncertainty about fitting the capital envelope is a budgeting concern, peripheral to the CFJ; capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of anxiety while unresolved vessel cost inputs block release of capital funds
rows.imp_rat: Anxiety while cost inputs block fund release is a personal-stress error remote from mixing (low relevance); consequences are emotional-only and held low by the cap (low severity).

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of feeling exposed by unanswered questions about costs omitted from the vessel commitment
rows.imp_rat: Feeling exposed on omitted costs is a personal-anxiety error remote from the blend job (low relevance); consequences are emotional-only and bounded by the cap (low severity).

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that senior leadership attributes a vessel budget breach to the purchase executor's lack of capital discipline
rows.imp_rat: A budget breach attributed to lack of discipline is a blame error remote from mixing (low relevance); consequences are reputational and ranking-capped (low severity).

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of credibility lost with senior leadership and finance when the vessel investment's commercial value cannot be defended
rows.imp_rat: Credibility lost when commercial value can't be defended is a reputational error remote from the blend job (low relevance), with standing consequences ranking-capped (low severity).

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that senior leadership and finance perceive the vessel authorization as supplier preference rather than defensible value
rows.imp_rat: Perception of supplier preference over value is a procurement-fairness error remote from the blend job (low relevance), with perception consequences bounded by the cap (low severity).

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that failure to deliver promised vessel outcomes is publicly attributed to the purchase executor
rows.imp_rat: Public attribution of failure to the executor is a blame error remote from mixing (low relevance); consequences are reputational and ranking-capped (low severity).

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of credibility lost with finance when realized vessel value falls short of the authorized financial case
rows.imp_rat: Credibility lost when realized value falls short is a financial-performance error peripheral to mixing (low relevance); consequences are reputational and bounded by the cap (low severity).

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of later sterile fill-finish capital decisions in which the purchase executor's judgment is second-guessed because of the vessel investment
rows.imp_rat: Judgment second-guessed in later capital decisions is a career-continuity error remote from mixing (low relevance); consequences are standing-only and bounded by the cap (low severity).

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of the batch hold while awaiting quality verification results
rows.plain: Spend less time keeping a medicine batch on hold while waiting for quality test results. This helps the batch be released or addressed sooner.
rows.imp_rat: Batch hold awaiting quality verification is an inherent gating delay (relevance) with minimal downside since it protects quality (severity); held at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Off-line lab testing is reliable in accuracy but the turnaround is inconsistent and rarely fast enough to avoid the hold. Waiting for lab QC results routinely holds batches for tens of minutes to hours, a chronic bottleneck between preparation and release. The delay stems from lab analytical work requiring skilled chemists, and inline alternatives demanding specialized validation. Held batches tie up capital and vessel capacity, and lab testing carries direct labor and reagent cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of operator exposure to hazardous ingredient material during loading
rows.plain: Protect workers from breathing in or touching dangerous ingredient material while loading it. This helps prevent harm to their health.
rows.imp_rat: Operator exposure to hazardous ingredient during loading is a safety concern with serious potential consequences, yet the batch-4 ranking caps importance at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Closed-loop charging, dust containment and PPE protocols substantially reduce exposure, though manual charging of hazardous ingredients still exposes operators when engineering controls are absent. Safe loading procedures add some handling time but this is not the primary concern. Proper use of PPE and containment requires training and discipline that varies across operators. Closed transfer systems and containment equipment carry capital cost but are increasingly standard.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of waiting for the batching system to accept the start command after all start conditions are satisfied
rows.plain: Have the mixing system begin its work quickly once every required condition is ready. This avoids wasting time after the operator has given the start instruction.
rows.imp_rat: Waiting for the system to accept a start command after conditions are met is a minor latency nuisance—low relevance to the CFJ and trivial severity. Capped at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Acceptance is consistent and predictable, though older or heavily loaded control networks occasionally add erratic delay. Once all conditions are met, modern controllers accept and act on the start command within seconds, though scan cycles and handshake delays introduce a noticeable lag on some legacy systems. No operator skill affects this acceptance latency; it is a function of the control architecture. The short acceptance delay wastes little more than a few seconds of operator attention per batch.

rows.stk: Process engineers, procurement leads, project managers.
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of supplier specifications presented on incompatible operating bases or units
rows.plain: Avoid comparing supplier information when it uses different conditions or measurement units. This makes it possible to compare the options fairly and accurately.
rows.imp_rat: Specs presented on incompatible units create comparison ambiguity in sourcing, remote from actual preparation work (low relevance); ceiling keeps both proxies at 1.
rows.sat_rat: There is no enforced standard, so suppliers present specs on divergent bases and errors slip through unless an expert catches them. Normalizing specs onto common units and bases is a slow manual conversion step during evaluation. Detecting and correcting incompatible operating bases (flow, concentration, temperature references) demands scarce process-engineering judgement. The effort is labor, not direct spend, though mis-normalized comparisons can drive costly wrong selections.

rows.stk: Process engineers, materials engineers, procurement leads.
rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of ambiguity in each candidate system's allowable temperature, pressure, and chemical exposure limits
rows.plain: Clearly state the highest and lowest temperatures, pressures, and chemical exposures each possible system can safely handle. This helps the buyer choose equipment that will work safely in the intended setting.
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity in candidate-system exposure limits is an evaluation-phase clarity issue (relevance 1) with deferred consequences (severity 1); capped at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Standard datasheets and P&ID/spec templates cover common cases, but ambiguous or incomplete limit statements still slip through and cause recurring late surprises. Extracting and confirming temperature, pressure, and chemical exposure limits from vendor datasheets takes moderate time but is a routine part of technical evaluation cycles. Interpreting rating envelopes and chemical compatibility charts demands experienced process/materials engineers who are not always available in-house. The clarification work adds modest engineering cost, far below the cost of a mis-rated system discovered later.

rows.stk: Process engineers, materials engineers, procurement leads.
rows.stmt: Minimize the time spent waiting for missing supplier compatibility information during candidate evaluation
rows.plain: Get the missing information needed to check whether the supplier’s option will work with the existing process and materials. This keeps the evaluation from being held up.
rows.imp_rat: Waiting for missing compatibility info during evaluation is a minor procurement delay with modest impact on the CFJ; the ceiling constrains it to 1.
rows.sat_rat: Suppliers provide compatibility data inconsistently, so the gap and wait recur across evaluations. Candidate evaluation stalls while buyers wait for suppliers to send compatibility information not provided upfront. Identifying which compatibility data is missing requires technical evaluation skill on the buyer side. The wait costs schedule, not direct money.

rows.stk: Procurement leads, quality engineers, regulatory affairs personnel.
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of material contradictions among compliance documents, certifications, and supplier claims
rows.plain: Make sure compliance documents, certificates, and supplier statements do not significantly contradict one another. Consistent evidence makes it easier to judge whether the product or supplier meets the required rules.
rows.imp_rat: Contradictions among compliance documents is a documentation-reconciliation issue (relevance 1) with indirect consequences (severity 1); capped at the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Contradictions among documents and claims are common and only diligent manual reconciliation catches them, leaving residual gaps. Cross-checking compliance documents, certifications, and supplier claims for contradictions is painstaking and slow. Detecting material contradictions across documents requires meticulous quality-assurance review skill. Contradictions found after purchase can trigger costly requalification, but review cost itself is modest.

rows.stk: Procurement leads, quality engineers, regulatory affairs personnel.
rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of the compliance-gate delay caused by late supplier clarifications
rows.plain: Spend less time waiting at the required compliance checkpoint because the supplier provides explanations late. This helps the work move forward sooner.
rows.imp_rat: Compliance-gate delay from late supplier clarifications is a supplier-process error tangential to batching; consequences are schedule slippage. Ceiling applies.
rows.sat_rat: Late supplier clarifications happen unpredictably because there is no upfront compliance data package standard, keeping the gate uncertain. Compliance gates stall while buyers wait for suppliers to clarify certifications and code compliance, often over multiple slow email exchanges. Framing the right compliance questions demands regulatory literacy that only specialized quality or compliance staff possess. The delay carries schedule cost more than direct monetary waste for this need.

rows.stk: Facilities engineers, process engineers, project managers.
rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of required candidate-system dimensional, utility, and connectivity specifications that are missing or unverified during technical evaluation
rows.plain: Check and confirm that a possible replacement system will fit the site, have the needed services, and connect properly before choosing it.
rows.imp_rat: Missing candidate-system specs during technical evaluation is a selection-phase gap (relevance 1) with contained consequences (severity 1); held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Candidate spec sheets are frequently incomplete or unverified, and standard evaluation only partly closes these gaps. Collecting and verifying dimensional, utility, and connectivity specs during evaluation is a lengthy data-gathering exercise. Confirming completeness of technical specs requires engineering literacy across mechanical and controls domains. Missing verified specs cause costly rework at install, so gaps have downstream financial impact.

rows.stk: Facilities engineers, process engineers, project managers.
rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of discrepancy between documented site constraints and actual installation conditions
rows.plain: Make sure the written description of the site matches the conditions that are actually there, so the equipment will fit and work properly.
rows.imp_rat: Discrepancy between documented and actual site constraints is an installation-planning error, low relevance to core batching output; severity held at 1 under the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: As-built documentation is often outdated or incomplete, so gaps between documented and actual conditions remain a persistent problem. Reconciling documented versus actual site constraints requires site visits and measurement that add schedule. Detecting subtle discrepancies needs experienced field engineers who can read both drawings and as-built conditions. Undiscovered discrepancies drive costly field rework and change orders.

rows.stk: Facilities engineers, process engineers, project managers.
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required site utilities whose available capacity or quality falls below candidate-system requirements
rows.plain: Make sure the site’s water, power, air, and other needed services have enough capacity and suitable quality for the equipment. This helps prevent operating problems after installation.
rows.imp_rat: Inadequate utility capacity or quality can hinder operation, yet the ranking places it lower so both proxies stay at the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Utility capacity/quality assessments are a mature part of acquisition and reliably catch most shortfalls before purchase. Comparing candidate utility demands to available site capacity is a well-established, relatively quick engineering check. Utility load matching is routine facilities-engineering work with widely available competence. Utility shortfalls discovered late require expensive upgrades, so upfront checks are cost-protective.

rows.stk: Facilities engineers, process engineers, project managers.
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the candidate system or its transport modules cannot traverse site access paths or fit within the installation envelope
rows.plain: The proposed system and the parts used to move it should fit through the site’s entrances and travel routes and fit in the planned installation space. This prevents the equipment from being impossible to deliver or install.
rows.imp_rat: Transport/installation-envelope fit is a logistics precondition, peripheral to the core batching-and-preparation function; the batch ceiling caps it at 1.0 regardless, and its consequences are recoverable with rerouting.
rows.sat_rat: Route surveys and layout drawings catch many issues, but envelope and access-path clashes still surface at delivery with some regularity. Verifying access-path and envelope fit is a discrete pre-delivery task that fits the planning window when done early. 3D fit and transport-module routing checks need experienced logistics/layout engineers not always engaged early enough. A late-discovered fit failure forces costly rework, disassembly, or facility modification.

rows.stk: Facilities engineers, process engineers, project managers.
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required process, control, and data connections that are incompatible with site interfaces
rows.plain: Make sure the equipment’s needed processes, controls, and information connections work with the systems already at the site, so everything can connect and operate together.
rows.imp_rat: Site-interface-incompatible connections affect installation, but this item sits lower in importance ordering so both proxies are held at the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Connection incompatibilities are common because interface specs are exchanged informally, and standard practice only partly prevents them. Mapping required control and data connections to site interfaces involves lengthy coordination with automation teams. Verifying interface compatibility (protocols, I/O, historian links) demands specialized controls-engineering skill. Interface incompatibilities force integration rework, a notable unplanned cost.

rows.stk: Process engineers, project managers, procurement leads.
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unresolved conflicts among quality, production, engineering, and procurement requirements
rows.plain: Resolve disagreements among quality, production, engineering, and purchasing needs when describing the system. This helps prevent conflicting instructions and costly changes later.
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved conflicts among quality/production/engineering/procurement requirements is a cross-functional alignment error tangential to the core job; consequences are decision gridlock. Held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: There is rarely a formal requirements-conflict review, so contradictions between quality, production, engineering, and procurement slip through. Unresolved conflicts among functional requirements force iterative reconciliation that extends the definition phase. Detecting and resolving cross-functional requirement conflicts demands an experienced systems or lead engineer. Late conflict discovery can force costly change orders, but the felt loss is defect-like inconsistency.

rows.stk: Process engineers, project managers, procurement leads.
rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of ambiguity in requirements for material transfers, utilities, cleaning, and environmental interfaces
rows.plain: Clearly state what is needed for moving materials, supplying services such as water or electricity, cleaning, and connecting to the surrounding environment. This prevents important requirements from being missed.
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity in transfer/utility/cleaning interface requirements is relevant to smooth batching-system setup, but the batch-4 ceiling caps it; capped at 1/1.
rows.sat_rat: URS templates and datasheets help, but CIP/SIP, drainage, and environmental interface details are frequently under-specified and generate recurring change orders. Nailing down transfer, utility, cleaning, and environmental interface requirements requires several vendor exchanges that stretch the acquisition timeline. It takes cross-disciplinary judgement (process, utilities, sanitation) that few individual reviewers hold, so gaps persist. Ambiguity here is cheap to leave in the spec but expensive to resolve after award, and status quo often defers the cost downstream.

rows.stk: Process engineers, project managers, procurement leads.
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that specified requirements conflict with available space, utilities, or material-flow constraints
rows.plain: Make sure the requested system features fit the available room, power and other services, and the way materials move through the site.
rows.imp_rat: Spec conflicts with space/utility constraints is a design-fit error caught at planning, peripheral to batching operation (low relevance); cap holds severity at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Site fit reviews and layout drawings catch most conflicts, though mismatches still emerge during installation planning. Checking specs against space, utilities, and material-flow constraints via site surveys takes time but is a standard pre-order step. This requires facilities and layout engineering judgement that most sites can access internally or via vendors. Late-discovered spatial or utility conflicts cause costly redesign, so early checks save money.

rows.stk: Procurement leads, process engineers, project managers.
rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of ambiguity created by inconsistent scopes and assumptions across supplier proposals
rows.plain: Make the different supplier offers clear and consistent about what work is included and what each supplier assumes. This makes the offers fair and easy to compare.
rows.imp_rat: Inconsistent scopes across proposals create evaluation ambiguity in procurement, not central to batching (low relevance); ceiling forces both proxies to 1.
rows.sat_rat: Free-form proposal formats make inconsistent assumptions the norm, and status-quo scope-matrix practice only partially tames the ambiguity. Aligning inconsistent scopes and assumptions across proposals extends the evaluation timeline substantially. Building a like-for-like comparison from heterogeneous proposals hinges on experienced technical evaluators, a scarce resource. Ambiguity leads to scope-gap change orders that cost money downstream, though not visible at bid stage.

rows.stk: Procurement leads, process engineers, project managers.
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required technical documents missing from supplier proposals
rows.plain: Reduce the number of supplier proposals that are missing required technical documents. This makes it easier to compare what each supplier is offering.
rows.imp_rat: Missing technical documents in proposals is a procurement-evaluation gap peripheral to the batching job (low relevance); the mandatory ceiling holds both proxies at 1.
rows.sat_rat: A documented RFQ checklist helps, but suppliers commonly omit or partially supply required technical deliverables, so gaps recur. Chasing missing technical documents from suppliers adds iteration cycles before evaluation can close. Knowing which technical documents are mandatory for sterile batching systems requires engineering domain knowledge not always present in procurement. Missing documents rarely cost money directly but delay and re-work consume resources.

rows.stk: Procurement leads, process engineers, project managers.
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of conflicting specifications, pricing, or terms within supplier submissions
rows.plain: Make sure each supplier’s offer gives consistent information about what is included, how much it costs, and the terms. This makes it easier to compare offers fairly.
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting specs/pricing in supplier submissions is a procurement-admin error peripheral to the physical CFJ of batching and preparing liquids; the ranking caps it low and its consequences are absorbable through clarification.
rows.sat_rat: Bid-tab comparison is standard practice yet manual, so conflicting details within a submission are caught only when reviewers are thorough. Spotting internal contradictions in supplier submissions during bid review adds checking time before award. Cross-checking specs, pricing, and terms for consistency relies on a careful buyer's diligence rather than automation. Undetected conflicting terms can produce unexpected charges, but the primary error is the inconsistency itself.

rows.stk: Procurement leads, process engineers, project managers.
rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of acquisition cost omitted from proposal pricing
rows.plain: Leave out as little of the purchase cost as possible from the prices shown in the sellers' offers.
rows.imp_rat: Acquisition cost omitted from proposal pricing is a commercial gap, yet this item sits lower in ordering so both proxies remain at the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Proposals commonly omit or footnote costs, and buyers only partly reconcile them before committing. Extracting the full acquisition cost from a proposal requires parsing exclusions and assumptions over time. Recognizing hidden or unpriced items in proposals needs a skilled estimator familiar with vendor tactics. Costs left out of proposal pricing become surprise spend, directly defeating budget accuracy.

rows.stk: Receiving personnel, project managers, commissioning engineers.
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of missing ordered components incorrectly recorded as received during inspection
rows.plain: Make sure every part that was ordered is actually present before marking it as received. This prevents missing parts from being overlooked.
rows.imp_rat: Missing components misrecorded as received is a receiving-inspection error, peripheral to batching function; severity capped at 1 per batch position.
rows.sat_rat: Packing-list and ERP receiving checks catch most shortfalls, though hurried or partial inspections still misrecord missing components. Line-item receiving reconciliation is a routine step that adds little time. Checking packing lists against received items is a low-skill clerical task. Recording a missing item as received causes downstream cost, but reconciliation itself is cheap.

rows.stk: Receiving personnel, project managers, commissioning engineers.
rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of inconsistency between delivered-system identifiers and approved order documentation
rows.plain: Make sure the equipment delivered has the same identifying information as the equipment approved in the order. This helps confirm that the correct equipment arrived.
rows.imp_rat: Inconsistency between delivered identifiers and order documentation is a procurement-record defect, peripheral to the batching job; capped at 1.0 by batch position.
rows.sat_rat: Standard document-control and tag-number practices keep identifier inconsistencies fairly low, with only sporadic mismatches. Cross-checking delivered identifiers against approved order documents is a fast confirmation step. The comparison is straightforward and requires little specialized skill. The check is inexpensive relative to the cost of accepting mislabeled equipment.

rows.stk: Receiving personnel, project managers, commissioning engineers.
rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of inspection delay caused by inaccessible or unsafe inspection areas after delivery
rows.plain: Spend less time waiting to inspect delivered equipment when the inspection areas are hard to reach or not safe to enter. This helps staff check the equipment safely and sooner.
rows.imp_rat: Inspection delay from inaccessible areas after delivery is a site-access error remote from the core batching function; consequences are delayed acceptance. Ceiling applies.
rows.sat_rat: Receiving-area preparation is a fairly established practice, so severe access blocks are occasional rather than chronic. Inspection is delayed when delivered equipment sits in areas that must first be made safe or accessible, adding days to acceptance. Judging access and safety needs a competent inspector or HSE officer, a role generally available at receiving sites. Re-scheduling inspectors and idle equipment adds some cost but is not the primary loss.

rows.stk: Receiving personnel, project managers, commissioning engineers.
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of transit damage remaining undetected before delivery acceptance
rows.plain: Make sure any damage from shipping is found before the delivered equipment is formally accepted. This protects the recipient from accepting equipment that arrived damaged.
rows.imp_rat: Undetected transit damage before acceptance can cause later failures, yet the batch-4 ceiling forces relevance and severity to 1/1.
rows.sat_rat: Shock/tilt indicators and visual checks reliably catch gross damage, but concealed damage to internal components still escapes acceptance. Receiving inspection for transit damage is a standard step that fits within normal delivery-acceptance timing. Spotting external transit damage needs only moderate inspector skill, though hidden internal damage requires more expertise. Inspection labor is low-cost and far cheaper than accepting a damaged unit, though hidden damage claims can be expensive.

rows.stk: Receiving personnel, project managers, commissioning engineers.
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of contamination remaining undetected on delivered system surfaces during receiving inspection
rows.plain: Make sure unwanted material left on the delivered equipment is noticed during the incoming inspection. This helps prevent dirty equipment from being accepted and used.
rows.imp_rat: Undetected contamination on delivered surfaces matters for product safety, but per the mandatory ceiling this batch cannot exceed 1.0; capped at 1/1.
rows.sat_rat: Visual and wipe checks catch obvious fouling, yet subtle internal or microbiological contamination often goes undetected until commissioning. Surface contamination checks add inspection time at receiving but are usually scheduled into the acceptance window. Detecting residual contamination reliably requires trained QC inspectors and sometimes swab/analytical methods not always on hand at receiving. Visual inspection is cheap, but missed contamination triggers costly rework or requalification.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of introducing contamination into the sterile-product fluid pathway while making piping connections
rows.plain: Keep harmful or unwanted material out of the path that carries the sterile product while the pipes are joined. This helps keep the product safe from contamination.
rows.imp_rat: Contamination introduced into the sterile fluid pathway while making connections is genuinely serious, but the batch-4 ceiling limits both proxies to 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Aseptic connectors and sanitization protocols exist, but maintaining sterility while making connections remains one of the least reliably controlled risks in this stage. Any suspected contamination of the sterile fluid path while connecting forces re-sterilization cycles that heavily delay startup. Aseptic connection technique demands high skill and rigorous discipline, and errors are easy to make manually. Compromised sterile pathways can scrap batches and force costly re-sterilization.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of damaging batching-system nozzles or piping seals while aligning and securing the piping
rows.plain: Avoid damaging the machine's pipe ends or seals while lining up and fastening the pipes. This helps prevent leaks and equipment problems.
rows.imp_rat: Damaging nozzles or piping seals during install is a defect concern, but the batch ceiling constrains both proxies to 1.
rows.sat_rat: Flange alignment procedures and torque specs help, but reliance on manual finesse means seal and nozzle damage during fit-up still occurs with some regularity. Careful alignment and controlled tightening slows piping work but avoids the far larger delay of replacing a damaged nozzle or seal. Avoiding nozzle overload and seal damage depends heavily on experienced fitters using proper alignment technique and torque control, making this strongly skill-bound. A damaged nozzle or crushed seal is costly to replace and can require refabrication, but incidents are individually contained.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of account setup stalled by incomplete or conflicting access approvals
rows.plain: Set up a person’s account quickly instead of letting missing or conflicting approvals hold it up. This allows the person to begin their authorized work sooner.
rows.imp_rat: Account setup stalled by access-approval conflicts is an administrative onboarding delay largely disconnected from batching liquid (relevance minimal), with trivial consequences for the CFJ.
rows.sat_rat: Approval workflows exist but are often manual and email-driven, so conflicts and incompleteness recur and reliably cause delay. Account setup stalled by incomplete or conflicting approvals is a chronic bottleneck where users wait days for sign-offs to reconcile. Resolving conflicting approvals depends on coordinators chasing the right authorities rather than any specialized technical skill. Stalled setup keeps commissioning teams idle and extends schedule, a real but bounded cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of newly established accounts with credentials that fail at initial sign-in
rows.plain: Make sure newly created user accounts work the first time the users sign in. This lets people begin their work without access problems.
rows.imp_rat: Credentials failing at first sign-in is a minor account-provisioning reliability nuisance with negligible bearing on preparing batches (both proxies minimal).
rows.sat_rat: Provisioning-then-verify practices catch most bad credentials, so initial sign-in failures are common annoyances but rarely persistent problems. A credential that fails at first sign-in causes a short reset cycle rather than a major delay. Diagnosing and reissuing a failed credential is routine help-desk work needing little expertise. The cost of a failed initial login is trivial, limited to a brief support interaction.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of ingredient sources that cannot be uniquely identified from project records and physical connections
rows.plain: Make sure every ingredient source can be identified as one specific source using the project records and the actual connections.
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguous ingredient-source identification is a documentation traceability gap, indirect to the running job (low relevance); ceiling sets severity to 1.
rows.sat_rat: Tag numbering, P&IDs and line-list documentation exist as standard practice, but gaps between physical connections and records still leave ambiguous sources fairly often. Tracing ambiguous ingredient sources back through records and connections is a slow, manual reconciliation task when tagging is incomplete. Correct identification depends on diligent labeling discipline and someone who knows the P&IDs, so it is vulnerable to human oversight. Mislabeled or unidentifiable sources risk wasted batches and rework, but the direct cost of tagging itself is low.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required equipment history and qualification records missing or inaccurate at handover
rows.plain: Make sure the equipment’s use history and testing records are complete and accurate when it is handed over. This helps the new owner understand the equipment’s condition and past compliance.
rows.imp_rat: Missing equipment history and qualification records at handover reduces documentation quality, but relevance and severity stay at the batch-5 cap.
rows.sat_rat: Equipment files are routinely incomplete or outdated at end of life, so missing or inaccurate records at handover are a frequent recurring gap. Locating and verifying equipment history and qualification records at handover often causes last-minute delays. Judging which qualification records are complete and accurate requires engineering and validation expertise. Re-creating missing qualification documentation is costly but occasional.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that equipment is incompatible with the receiving facility's space, utilities, or sterile production requirements
rows.plain: Make sure the equipment will fit the new facility, work with its basic services, and meet its clean-production needs.
rows.imp_rat: Equipment incompatible with facility space, utilities, or sterile requirements can block installation (relevance) with expensive rework (severity), but capped at 1.0 by the batch ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Site-acceptance surveys reduce but do not eliminate fit surprises, especially for sterile requirements. Verifying receiving-facility compatibility can be done ahead of time but is often rushed. Assessing space, utility, and sterile-production fit requires cross-disciplinary engineering judgement. Late incompatibility discoveries force expensive rework or re-siting.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of postponed handover caused by unresolved ownership, regulatory, or cross-border approvals
rows.plain: Avoid delaying the handover of equipment because no one has settled who owns it, whether the transfer follows required rules, or whether it may cross a border. This helps the equipment reach its new owner sooner.
rows.imp_rat: Postponed handover from unresolved approvals is impactful, yet the batch-5 ceiling forces relevance and severity to 1.
rows.sat_rat: Because approvals depend on external authorities on their own timelines, delays are frequent and largely outside the team's control, making this poorly served. Handovers stalled on unresolved ownership, regulatory, or cross-border approvals routinely drag on for months with little acceleration available. Untangling multi-jurisdiction approval dependencies demands scarce coordination expertise spanning legal, regulatory, and trade domains. Postponed handover accrues carrying, security, and standby costs on an asset generating no value.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required regulatory and environmental authorities that do not receive a decommissioning notice
rows.plain: Make sure every government office responsible for safety or the environment receives notice that the equipment is being taken out of service, so required authorities are informed.
rows.imp_rat: Failing to notify required regulatory authorities of decommissioning is a compliance step at end-of-life, tangential to core processing (low relevance) though missed notices carry regulatory risk; ceiling caps at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Standard notification checklists make missed authorities fairly rare. Notifying all relevant authorities is a routine administrative task with defined timelines. Maintaining the authority distribution list requires regulatory familiarity but is well-documented. Notification is low-cost administrative work.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of submitting a required decommissioning notice after its mandated deadline
rows.plain: Avoid sending a required shutdown notice after the official deadline. Sending it on time helps meet legal requirements.
rows.imp_rat: Submitting a decommissioning notice past its deadline is an administrative timeliness error at plant retirement, marginal to core processing (low relevance), with regulatory but recoverable consequences; ceiling holds at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Deadline-tracking practices are mature, making late submissions uncommon when followed. Meeting mandated notice deadlines is a scheduling discipline supported by regulatory calendars. Tracking deadlines needs modest regulatory administrative skill. Late notices risk penalties but tracking is cheap.

rows.stmt: Reduce the rate of decommissioning notices rejected for missing required plant-retirement details
rows.plain: Fewer notices about retiring plant equipment should be rejected because they leave out required details. This helps regulatory authorities receive complete information the first time.
rows.imp_rat: Decommissioning notices rejected for missing retirement details create rework in an end-of-life administrative process, peripheral to core processing (low relevance) with correctable consequences; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Notice completeness varies and rejections for missing plant-retirement details still occur regularly. Rejections for missing details force rework cycles that extend the timeline. Preparing complete retirement details requires knowledge of each authority's requirements. Resubmission consumes staff time but limited direct cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of inconsistency in plant-retirement details reported to different authorities
rows.plain: Make sure all authorities receive the same accurate information about closing or retiring the plant. This helps prevent conflicting official records.
rows.imp_rat: Inconsistent plant-retirement details reported to different authorities is an end-of-life reporting ambiguity, marginal to core processing (low relevance) with recoverable compliance consequences; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: With no single source of truth feeding multiple regulators, discrepancies in retirement details recur and are caught inconsistently, leaving the need poorly served. Compiling separate retirement filings for each authority is time-consuming when data must be re-keyed per template. Ensuring consistency across filings depends on diligent coordinators cross-checking submissions rather than any systemic control. Inconsistencies discovered later cause corrective refiling costs and potential penalties, but base filing cost is modest.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that receipt of a required decommissioning notice cannot be demonstrated
rows.plain: Make it unlikely that there is no proof that the required notice about taking equipment out of service was received. This helps show that the proper authorities were notified.
rows.imp_rat: Inability to demonstrate receipt of a decommissioning notice is a records-evidence concern at retirement, marginal to core processing (low relevance) with limited direct consequence; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Registered-delivery and acknowledgement practices are mature and usually demonstrable, but gaps in tracking distributed or informal notices still occasionally leave receipt unprovable. Certified mail, courier receipts, and email read-receipts deliver proof of notice quickly, so timing is rarely the bottleneck. Legal and regulatory-affairs staff routinely handle statutory notice service, though correctly identifying every required recipient still demands specialist judgement. Proof-of-delivery mechanisms are cheap relative to plant decommissioning budgets, adding negligible expense.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of sterile fill-finish production interruption during cutover
rows.plain: Spend less time stopping germ-free medicine production while switching from the old equipment or process to its replacement. This helps the new setup begin work sooner.
rows.imp_rat: Sterile fill-finish production interruption during cutover directly halts output (relevance) with supply consequences (severity), but the batch ceiling caps it at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Interruptions are largely unavoidable but their duration varies with planning quality. Minimizing production interruption is the central scheduling challenge of cutover and interruptions routinely occur. Sequencing to compress downtime requires seasoned transition planners. Lost production during interruption is a large direct cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of sterile product becoming contaminated during the transition
rows.plain: Avoid allowing the sterile product to become dirty or unsafe while changing to the replacement. Keeping it clean protects its quality and safety.
rows.imp_rat: Sterile product contamination during transition is a serious quality failure (severity) central to sterile production (relevance), yet the batch ceiling mandates a 1.0 cap.
rows.sat_rat: Sterile transitions carry inherent high contamination risk that standard practice mitigates only partially. Maintaining sterile boundaries during transition adds time for controlled procedures. Preventing contamination in transit demands highly specialized aseptic and cleaning expertise. Containment, cleaning, and re-validation to avoid contamination are costly.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of critical process parameters, control limits, or batch records transferred incorrectly or omitted during cutover
rows.plain: Avoid transferring important process settings, operating limits, or production records incorrectly or leaving them out during the changeover. Accurate transfer helps the replacement system run consistently and safely.
rows.imp_rat: Critical process parameters or batch records transferred incorrectly during cutover threatens product quality (relevance/severity), yet the mandatory ceiling holds it at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Manual or semi-automated transfer of critical parameters is error-prone and a recurring source of deviations. Transferring parameters and batch records correctly takes verification time that is often compressed. Accurate parameter transfer demands validation and process expertise to check each transfer. Errors caught late trigger costly rework or batch loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of replacement plant output shortfall relative to sterile fill-finish demand after cutover
rows.plain: Make sure the replacement plant can produce enough sterile, ready-to-fill product after the changeover, so demand is not left unmet.
rows.imp_rat: A replacement plant output shortfall against sterile fill-finish demand directly threatens supply (relevance/severity), yet the ceiling limits this to 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Output shortfalls after cutover are a common and material risk despite transition planning. Ramping replacement plant output to meet demand takes extended qualification time. Forecasting and closing output gaps requires experienced production planning. Building inventory buffers or dual-running to cover shortfall is expensive.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of required materials, qualified personnel, or utilities being unavailable at cutover
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that needed supplies, trained people, or basic services are unavailable when work moves to the replacement equipment. This helps the changeover happen without disruption.
rows.imp_rat: Unavailable materials, personnel, or utilities at cutover directly stalls plant startup (relevance), and the consequences of a missed cutover window are costly (severity); yet the batch ceiling caps this at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Despite project planning tools, resource unavailability at cutover remains a common execution risk. Coordinating materials, crews, and utilities at cutover involves lead times that are frequently underestimated, causing delays. Effective resource scheduling relies on experienced project management that is available but not always applied. Standby crews and expedited materials to cover shortfalls drive real cost overruns.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of exported control-system files that are corrupted or unreadable
rows.plain: Reduce the share of exported files that control the equipment that are damaged or cannot be opened. This helps preserve the information needed to operate or review the system.
rows.imp_rat: Corrupted exported control-system files is a data-integrity error, peripheral to active processing (low relevance) and recoverable from source (low severity). Held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Standard verification catches most corrupted exports, though unverified transfers still occasionally yield unreadable files. Verifying export integrity adds a checking step that is fairly quick with checksums. Basic integrity verification is a routine IT competency widely available. Checksum verification and re-export cost little.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of version inconsistency among preserved automation programs, process recipes, historian data, and configuration files
rows.plain: Keep the saved control programs, production instructions, recorded operating data, and setup files on matching versions, so they work together and describe the same system.
rows.imp_rat: Version inconsistency among preserved programs and recipes risks data integrity, but relevance and severity remain at the batch-5 minimum.
rows.sat_rat: Poor lifecycle version management means preserved digital assets are frequently inconsistent, a chronic and under-addressed problem. Reconciling versions across automation programs, recipes, historian data, and configs is a painstaking manual effort. Identifying the authoritative version among many demands deep control-systems and process expertise. Version control tooling exists but establishing it retroactively at disposal is expensive.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of reusable motors and instruments damaged during removal or handling
rows.plain: Make sure reusable motors and measuring devices are not damaged while being removed or moved. This matters so they can be used again instead of being discarded.
rows.imp_rat: Damage to reusable motors and instruments during removal is an asset-handling defect of low centrality to the processing job and limited severity; kept low per the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Even with skilled crews, handling damage during removal is common, so outcomes vary widely by team competence. Careful removal of reusable motors and instruments takes longer than demolition-grade tear-out, creating schedule tension. Damage-free removal depends heavily on skilled millwrights and technicians handling equipment correctly. Salvage value gives a clear cost incentive to handle equipment well, funding some careful practice.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of retained structures or services damaged during plant dismantling
rows.plain: Reduce the number of building parts or site services meant to remain that are damaged while the plant is taken apart. This helps preserve what is still needed after the work is finished.
rows.imp_rat: Damage to retained structures during dismantling is a decommissioning defect of low relevance to ongoing processing and modest severity; held at the floor within the 1.5 cap.
rows.sat_rat: Standard demarcation and protection practices help, but accidental damage to retained structures during dismantling still occurs regularly. Protecting retained structures slows dismantling as crews work carefully around them. Avoiding collateral damage to retained services requires experienced operators reading drawings correctly. Damage to retained assets carries direct repair cost, motivating protective practice.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of dismantled tanks or structural sections being unable to pass through planned site removal routes
rows.plain: Make sure tanks and building sections taken apart will fit through the planned ways out of the site. This helps prevent delays and the need for extra dismantling.
rows.imp_rat: Dismantled sections unable to pass removal routes causes rework, but relevance and severity are capped by the batch-5 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Established demolition planning and 3D survey practice make removal-route failures uncommon when done competently. Planning removal routes and segmenting large sections is done during pre-demolition surveys with adequate lead time. Rigging and demolition engineers routinely calculate section sizes against egress routes as standard practice. Extra cutting or route modification adds cost but is normally anticipated in demolition estimates.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of in-scope controllers and storage devices omitted from sanitization
rows.plain: Make sure every included control device and data-storage device has its stored information safely erased, so no included device is overlooked.
rows.imp_rat: Controllers omitted from sanitization scope is a coverage-completeness ambiguity, marginal to core processing (low relevance) and typically remediable on discovery (low severity). Ceiling-bound.
rows.sat_rat: Devices are routinely omitted from sanitization because the asset inventory is incomplete or out of date. Building and working through a full device inventory for sanitization takes time under closeout pressure. Knowing every in-scope controller and storage device requires thorough asset knowledge that is often incomplete. Complete inventory and sanitization of all devices costs more than a partial pass.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time controllers and storage devices remain inaccessible for sanitization
rows.plain: Spend less time waiting for control units and storage devices to become available for secure cleaning of their stored information. This helps remove information before the devices are reused or discarded.
rows.imp_rat: Time controllers remain inaccessible during sanitization is a scheduling-delay error, peripheral to the processing job (low relevance) and typically brief (low severity). Capped at 1.5.
rows.sat_rat: Delays are common but generally recoverable, so the impact is felt as schedule drag rather than outright failure. Devices frequently sit waiting on access, power, or credentials before they can be sanitized, extending the disposal timeline. Reducing this wait depends on coordinators who understand device dependencies and access paths. Prolonged inaccessibility ties up storage and staging resources at some cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of sanitized controllers and storage devices lacking verifiable completion records
rows.plain: Make sure cleaned control computers and storage devices have records proving the cleaning was completed. This provides evidence that stored information was properly removed or handled.
rows.imp_rat: Missing completion records for sanitized controllers is a recordkeeping gap, tangential to the core processing job (low relevance) with mostly audit-trail consequences (low severity). Held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Many sanitized devices end up without verifiable completion records because certification is not systematically enforced. Generating and filing completion records for each device adds administrative time. Producing verifiable sanitization certificates needs disciplined process knowledge often lacking on-site. Formal record-keeping and verification tools add modest cost frequently deprioritized.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that undisclosed liens or third-party interests prevent clear plant title from passing
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that hidden debts or other people's ownership claims make it impossible to transfer the factory equipment with clear ownership. This helps the buyer receive undisputed ownership.
rows.imp_rat: Undisclosed liens preventing clear title is severe for ownership transfer, yet the mandatory ceiling forces relevance and severity to 1 for this lower-ranked batch.
rows.sat_rat: Formal searches and title insurance catch most encumbrances, yet undisclosed or unregistered third-party interests in complex plant assets still occasionally slip through. Title and lien searches through registries take days to weeks but are a well-understood scheduled step. Real-estate and secured-lending counsel routinely run encumbrance searches, though industrial-asset liens can require specialist tracing. Title searches, escrow, and title insurance add cost but are standard and predictable transaction expenses.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of plant components omitted from the executed transfer instruments
rows.plain: List every plant component in the documents used to transfer ownership. This makes clear exactly what equipment and parts were handed over.
rows.imp_rat: Components omitted from executed transfer instruments affects legal completeness, yet the ceiling forces relevance and severity to 1.
rows.sat_rat: Incomplete or stale asset inventories mean components are regularly omitted from executed instruments, and there is no robust standard catch for this. Building a complete component schedule for transfer instruments takes significant lead time when inventories are outdated. Determining which components, spares, and embedded assets belong in the instrument requires engineering and legal collaboration that is often thin. Omitted components create later true-up costs or orphaned assets, though the drafting itself is inexpensive.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that transfer instruments are executed without valid signatory authority
rows.plain: Make sure the people signing the transfer have official permission to do so. This helps ensure the ownership change is legally valid.
rows.imp_rat: Transfer instruments without valid signatory authority is a serious defect, but the mandatory ceiling limits relevance and severity to 1.
rows.sat_rat: Mature corporate-authorization practices and closing checklists make invalid-signatory execution uncommon, though cross-border and subsidiary structures occasionally create authority gaps. Verifying signatory authority via board resolutions and delegations is a quick standard closing checklist item. Transaction counsel routinely validate authority through corporate records, a well-established competency. Authority verification adds minimal cost through legal review already budgeted for closing.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of plant asset identifiers that conflict across executed ownership records
rows.plain: Make sure each plant asset has the same identifying number in all ownership records. Matching records help prevent confusion about which equipment was transferred.
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting asset identifiers across ownership records create traceability risk, but the ceiling requires relevance and severity capped at 1 here.
rows.sat_rat: Legacy plant registers, tag renumbering, and disparate systems routinely produce conflicting identifiers with no automated reconciliation, so errors recur often. Reconciling asset identifiers across ownership records is a slow manual cross-referencing task at closing. Matching tag numbers, asset-register IDs, and legal descriptions demands attentive staff familiar with the plant's own inventory conventions. Identifier mismatches are cheap to fix if caught early but costly if they surface post-transfer as disputed assets.

rows.stmt: Minimize the time between the agreed ownership-transfer date and legal completion
rows.plain: Complete the legal transfer as soon as the agreed transfer date arrives. This makes the change in ownership official without unnecessary delay.
rows.imp_rat: Time between agreed transfer date and legal completion matters, but the mandatory ceiling limits relevance and severity to 1.
rows.sat_rat: Established closing processes usually bring completion within a reasonable window, though outstanding conditions can still extend it unpredictably. The lag between agreed transfer date and legal completion is often compressed by escrow and conditions-precedent structures but still runs weeks. Closing coordinators and counsel manage completion timelines competently using standard closing mechanics. Extended completion periods add limited incremental cost when interim arrangements are in place.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a required permit or approving authority is omitted from the decommissioning plan
rows.plain: Make sure the disposal plan includes every permit and every official approval that is required. This helps the equipment be taken out of service legally and properly.
rows.imp_rat: Omitting a permit or approving authority from the decommissioning plan is a peripheral end-of-life administrative task relative to the plant's core processing job (low relevance), and while missing approvals can carry regulatory consequences (some severity), the batch ceiling caps this at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Regulatory checklists and consultants make omissions relatively uncommon though not eliminated. Identifying all required permits and authorities early is feasible with adequate planning time. Comprehensive permit identification requires specialized regulatory affairs knowledge. Missing a permit causes costly stoppages, but permit research itself is inexpensive.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of permit applications returned because plant decommissioning information is incomplete or inconsistent
rows.plain: Provide complete and consistent information about the plant being taken out of use when applying for disposal approval. This prevents the application from being sent back for correction.
rows.imp_rat: Permit applications returned for incomplete decommissioning information cause administrative rework at end-of-life, marginal to core processing (low relevance) with recoverable delay consequences; ceiling holds at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Inconsistent or incomplete applications are a common cause of returns despite review practices. Returned applications trigger correction and resubmission delays. Assembling consistent, complete decommissioning information needs regulatory drafting expertise. Rework consumes professional time but modest direct cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of regulatory reviews before plant decommissioning approvals are issued
rows.plain: Spend less time waiting for government reviews before getting approval to shut down the plant. This helps the plant closure begin as planned.
rows.imp_rat: Duration of regulatory reviews before decommissioning approvals is an end-of-life timing concern peripheral to core processing (low relevance) with delay-only consequences; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Review timelines are unpredictable and variable across agencies, a persistent frustration. Regulatory review durations are largely outside the applicant's control and often lengthy. Little skill on the applicant side shortens review, though quality submissions help. Extended reviews delay project close-out, carrying holding costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that late hazardous-material or contamination findings require changes to submitted permit scopes
rows.plain: Find hazardous or contaminating materials early, so the disposal permits do not need to be changed after they are submitted.
rows.imp_rat: Late hazardous-material findings forcing permit-scope changes is decommissioning rework, peripheral to core processing (low relevance) though contamination findings carry some risk; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Hidden contamination in aging plants is frequently discovered late despite surveys, making scope changes a persistent risk. Late hazmat or contamination findings force permit-scope changes that add substantial delay. Thorough upfront site characterization requires specialized environmental assessment expertise. Rework of permit scopes and unplanned remediation is very costly.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of conflict among environmental, demolition, and waste-disposal approval conditions
rows.plain: Make sure the environmental, removal, and waste-disposal approval conditions do not contradict one another. This helps the project meet all requirements without conflicting instructions.
rows.imp_rat: Conflict among environmental, demolition, and waste-disposal approval conditions is a decommissioning-coordination ambiguity distant from core processing (low relevance) with correctable consequences; ceiling holds at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Because agencies issue conditions independently with no unified review, latent conflicts frequently surface only during execution, making this a chronic and poorly served pain. Reconciling conflicting conditions across separate environmental, demolition, and waste permits typically triggers slow back-and-forth with multiple agencies. Resolving inter-agency condition conflicts requires scarce senior environmental and permitting expertise that most retirement teams lack in-house. Conflicting approval conditions drive rework, consultant fees, and standby costs on demolition contractors.

rows.stk: Plant managers, compliance officers, records management teams.
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of archived records missing the identifiers needed to associate them with the correct batch or plant configuration
rows.plain: Make sure stored records include the information needed to link them to the correct production batch or plant setup. This prevents records from becoming impossible to identify correctly.
rows.imp_rat: Archived records missing batch/plant identifiers hampers traceability, but relevance to running the plant's core processing job is low, and severity is bounded per the batch-5 ceiling below prior items.
rows.sat_rat: Inconsistent historical indexing means many archived records lack traceable identifiers, undermining their evidentiary value. Reconstructing missing batch or configuration identifiers after the fact is time-consuming detective work. Correctly linking records to plant configurations requires institutional memory that erodes rapidly at closure. Metadata tagging is relatively cheap when done at source but expensive to retrofit onto legacy archives.

rows.stk: Plant managers, compliance officers, records management teams.
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of archived record classes assigned retention periods that conflict with applicable regulatory, legal, or organizational obligations
rows.plain: Make sure the required storage time for each type of stored record does not conflict with any relevant government rule, law, or organizational requirement. This prevents records from being deleted too soon or kept inappropriately long.
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting retention periods on archived record classes is a compliance record defect with low core-job relevance and severity held at the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Retention schedules exist as standard practice but conflicting obligations and misclassification produce recurring errors. Determining correct retention periods for each record class is a slow legal-regulatory research task. Mapping records to overlapping regulatory, legal, and organizational retention obligations demands specialized records-management and legal expertise. Over-retaining as a safety margin wastes storage money, while under-retaining risks fines.

rows.stk: Plant managers, project engineers, compliance officers.
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the plant condition changes after documentation and before handover
rows.plain: Make sure the plant stays in the same condition after it is documented and until it is handed over. This prevents disagreements about what condition it was actually in.
rows.imp_rat: Plant condition changing after documentation before handover is an instability risk, but the ceiling caps relevance and severity at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Idle plants degrade, get scavenged, or shift condition, and with only periodic snapshots the documented state frequently diverges from handover reality. The gap between documentation and handover can stretch for a decommissioned plant, widening the window for condition change. Detecting deterioration, theft, or unauthorized change in an idle plant requires periodic condition surveys by qualified inspectors. Interim security, preservation, and re-inspection carry ongoing cost that is easily under-provisioned for an asset being retired.

rows.stk: Plant managers, project engineers, compliance officers.
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of documented conditions lacking traceable evidence or required approval at closure
rows.plain: Make sure every condition recorded at the end has supporting evidence that can be traced back to its source and has any needed approval. This makes the final plant record trustworthy.
rows.imp_rat: Documented conditions lacking traceable evidence at closure is a documentation-ambiguity defect of low relevance to the processing job and low severity; kept at the floor within the 1.5 cap.
rows.sat_rat: Documented conditions frequently lack traceable backup or sign-off, so closure records are often not fully defensible. Gathering traceable evidence and approvals for each documented condition slows the closure process. Assembling defensible evidence chains requires document-control discipline not always present on demolition teams. Formal evidence and approval workflows add administrative cost that lean disposal projects resist.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of missing contractual cancellation deadlines that trigger renewals or termination charges
rows.plain: Cancel plant services before the contract deadlines that would cause automatic renewals or extra cancellation fees. This helps avoid unwanted costs and commitments.
rows.imp_rat: Missing cancellation deadlines triggering charges is financially relevant, but the batch-5 ceiling forces relevance and severity to 1.
rows.sat_rat: Manual calendar tracking of dispersed deadlines fails often, so missed cancellation windows recur even where some CLM tooling exists. Cancellation deadlines are often discovered late, leaving too little time to act before auto-renewal windows close. Tracking heterogeneous notice-period clauses across many contracts requires diligent contract administration that is frequently under-resourced at retirement. Missed deadlines directly trigger renewals and termination charges, a recurring and avoidable financial leak.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of cancellation delays caused by incomplete authorization or contract documentation
rows.plain: Avoid long cancellation delays when the required approval or contract papers are incomplete. This helps plant services end when intended.
rows.imp_rat: Cancellation delays from incomplete authorization documentation are administrative and remote from the plant's core processing job (low relevance), with easily absorbed consequences (low severity). Capped at 1.0 by the batch ordering.
rows.sat_rat: Missing or misfiled documentation frequently holds up cancellations, and reconstruction is inconsistent, leaving the need only weakly served. Cancellations stall when required authorization or contract documentation is incomplete, adding avoidable delay to each termination. Gathering authorizations and locating executed contract copies needs administrative persistence rather than deep expertise. Delayed cancellations extend billing periods, adding modest but real ongoing cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of terminating agreements before decommissioning and compliance dependencies are complete
rows.plain: Avoid ending agreements before all shutdown work and required checks are complete. Services may still be needed until those responsibilities are finished.
rows.imp_rat: Terminating agreements before decommissioning dependencies complete is risky, but the ceiling caps relevance and severity at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Without a linked register tying agreements to decommissioning dependencies, premature terminations happen and are only caught reactively, leaving the need poorly served. Sequencing agreement termination after decommissioning completes lengthens the overall wind-down timeline. Mapping which agreements are dependencies for compliance and decommissioning requires careful cross-functional analysis often missing. Premature termination can strand plants without required services, triggering emergency re-contracting at premium cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of service, licensing, and support charges billed after agreed cancellation dates
rows.plain: Prevent service, software-use, and support bills from continuing after the agreed cancellation date. This avoids paying for services that should have ended.
rows.imp_rat: Charges billed after cancellation dates are a billing/waste issue with no bearing on plant processing performance (low relevance) and small, refundable financial amounts (low severity). Mandatory cap keeps it at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Vendor billing systems lag cancellations and reconciliation is imperfect, so post-cancellation charges recur and only some are recovered. Disputing and clawing back post-cancellation charges takes weeks of billing-cycle back-and-forth with vendors. Reconciling invoices against cancellation dates is routine accounts-payable work but requires attentive matching to catch overbilling. Charges billed after agreed cancellation dates represent direct leakage that is often paid before being caught and recovered.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of active service, licensing, and support agreements omitted from cancellation at plant retirement
rows.plain: When the plant closes or retires its operations, every current service, software-use, and support agreement should be found and canceled when appropriate. This helps avoid ongoing charges for services no longer needed.
rows.imp_rat: Agreements omitted from cancellation at retirement waste money, but the ordering constraint holds relevance and severity at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Incomplete contract inventories mean agreements are routinely overlooked at retirement, and there is no reliable standard to guarantee full coverage. Assembling a complete list of active service, licensing, and support agreements is slow when contracts are scattered across owners and departments. Identifying every live agreement tied to a plant demands institutional knowledge that erodes as staff depart during wind-down. Agreements omitted from cancellation continue billing, wasting money on a plant no longer operating.

rows.stmt: Minimize the time between requesting the plant documentation package and receiving it complete
rows.plain: Provide the complete set of plant documents as soon as it is requested. This lets the requester use the information without waiting for missing parts.
rows.imp_rat: Delay in receiving a complete documentation package slows the job, but the ranking constraint holds both proxies at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Suppliers commonly deliver documentation late and in installments, so complete-package timeliness is a recurring pain. The lag between requesting and receiving a complete documentation package is often long and unpredictable. Expediting document delivery needs persistent follow-up skill but little technical expertise. Documentation delays stall qualification and delay production startup, carrying opportunity cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percent of required plant documents, drawings, and manuals missing from the received package
rows.plain: Receive fewer plant documents, drawings, and instruction books with important items missing. A complete package gives people the information needed to operate, maintain, and understand the plant.
rows.imp_rat: Missing plant documents and manuals hurt operability, yet batch-5 placement forces both proxies to 1.
rows.sat_rat: Turnover document packages for plants are chronically incomplete at handover despite documentation checklists. Confirming completeness of the document package requires cross-checking against required lists, adding receiving effort. Knowing which drawings and manuals are contractually required needs experienced documentation control. Missing documents delay commissioning and can force costly reconstruction or re-issue.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of received plant documents with conflicting revision status
rows.plain: Fewer received facility documents should disagree about which version is current. This helps workers use the right information.
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting revision status among documents causes ambiguity, but the ordering caps both proxies at 1 for this batch.
rows.sat_rat: Revision-control discipline varies widely by supplier, so conflicting revision statuses appear frequently in received packages. Resolving conflicting revisions requires back-and-forth clarification that consumes time. Spotting revision conflicts across a large document set demands disciplined document control expertise. Working from wrong revisions risks costly rework during installation and qualification.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percent of received plant documentation files that cannot be opened or read
rows.plain: Receive fewer plant documents that cannot be opened or read. Usable files ensure people can access the information they need.
rows.imp_rat: Unreadable documentation files are a usability defect, yet batch-5 placement forces both proxies to 1.
rows.sat_rat: Standard file formats and digital transfer make unreadable files uncommon and easily remedied, so this need is largely well served. Discovering an unreadable file is instant and re-requesting it is quick. Opening files needs no special skill, only common software. Corrupt or unreadable files cost little beyond a re-transmission request.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percent of received documents lacking identifiers needed for cataloging
rows.plain: Receive fewer documents without the names, numbers, or other details needed to organize them. Clear identifying details make the documents easier to find and manage.
rows.imp_rat: Documents lacking cataloging identifiers is a records-management rework issue far from processing operations (low relevance) with trivial consequences (low severity). Batch ceiling limits it to 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: A meaningful share of received documents still arrive without required cataloging identifiers because supplier submittals are inconsistently controlled. Chasing missing identifiers before cataloging delays document filing until the gap is closed. Recognizing which identifiers are required for cataloging depends on a document controller's familiarity with the scheme. Missing identifiers waste handling effort but seldom cause direct financial loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time suppliers take to provide complete capability evidence
rows.plain: Spend less time waiting for suppliers to provide complete proof that they can do the required work. This helps evaluate their ability sooner.
rows.imp_rat: Supplier slowness providing capability evidence is peripheral to the core job, and the ceiling caps both proxies at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Initial submissions are frequently incomplete and require re-requests, making the arrival of a full set unreliable. Suppliers typically take days to weeks assembling capability packs because requests trigger internal document hunts with no standing dossier ready. Compiling complete evidence leans on supplier proposal specialists whose availability varies, slowing responses when they are stretched.
