The repeated back-and-forth consumes both buyer and supplier labor but rarely represents a large hard-dollar outlay.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of supplier references that do not represent comparable sterile fill-finish projects
rows.plain: Use customer references from projects that are truly similar in size, type, and sterile production work. This gives a more reliable picture of what the supplier can do.
rows.imp_rat: Supplier references not representing comparable sterile fill-finish projects weakens vendor vetting, a procurement-stage concern only loosely tied to the CFJ (low relevance) with limited direct consequence; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Suppliers self-select flattering references and comparability is hard to confirm, so non-representative references frequently slip through. Verifying that references genuinely match sterile fill-finish scope requires slow follow-up calls and site visits. Judging comparability of a reference project needs sterile-processing domain expertise that few buyers possess. The verification effort is modest in money terms but reference site visits add travel cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required site dimensions and clearances assessed from inaccurate or incomplete records
rows.plain: Use complete and accurate information when checking the site’s measurements and the space needed around the equipment. This helps prevent choosing equipment that will not fit or cannot be used safely.
rows.imp_rat: Site dimensions assessed from inaccurate records risk fit problems (relevance) and rework (severity), but ranked below the prior batch and capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: As-built drawings and laser scans help, but many sites still rely on outdated or incomplete dimensional records, so errors persist. Re-measuring or verifying site dimensions adds field time when records are suspect. Reliable dimensional assessment needs surveyors and engineers who know which clearances matter. Acting on inaccurate records causes expensive fit-up problems during installation.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time spent waiting for verified site utility capacity and quality data during the compatibility assessment
rows.plain: Spend less time waiting for confirmed information about the site’s available services and their condition. This helps determine sooner whether the equipment will work properly at that location.
rows.imp_rat: Waiting for verified utility capacity data during compatibility assessment is a delay of low core-job relevance, severity held at the ranking floor.
rows.sat_rat: Verified utility data is often incomplete or slow to obtain, so assessments proceed on provisional figures more than they should. The need is explicitly about waiting for verified site utility capacity and quality data, so delay is the core loss. Obtaining and validating utility data leans on scarce facilities engineers whose availability gates the assessment. Waiting itself carries little direct cost beyond the risk of decisions on stale data.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of overlooking access-route obstructions or handling-clearance constraints before approving the site
rows.plain: Check for blocked paths and enough room to move and handle the equipment before approving the site. This helps prevent delivery, installation, and safety problems.
rows.imp_rat: Overlooking access-route obstructions before site approval risks handling failures (relevance) and delays (severity), but it is a pre-approval check ranked low and capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Transport and access studies catch many constraints, but obstructions and handling clearances are still occasionally missed before site approval. Access-route surveys add scheduling time before site approval. Spotting handling-clearance and obstruction constraints requires experienced logistics and rigging planners. An overlooked obstruction forces expensive re-routing or crane changes during delivery.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that site environmental and contamination-control conditions are characterized incorrectly
rows.plain: Make sure the site’s surroundings and cleanliness controls are understood correctly before deciding whether the system will work there.
rows.imp_rat: Incorrectly characterizing site contamination-control conditions risks sterile product integrity (relevance/severity), yet the mandatory ceiling holds it at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Site surveys and standards give a basis, but environmental and contamination conditions are still sometimes assessed incorrectly for liquid processing needs. Characterizing environmental and contamination conditions can require surveys that add lead time. Correct characterization of contamination-control conditions requires specialist environmental and cleanroom expertise. Mischaracterization drives costly containment retrofits after the fact.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of discrepancies between the purchase commitment and the approved plant scope or configuration
rows.plain: Make sure the purchase commitment matches the plant work and setup that were approved. This prevents ordering something different from what the plant needs.
rows.imp_rat: Discrepancies between purchase commitment and approved plant scope are commercial/administrative, so both relevance and severity stay low under the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Standard purchase-order review against approved scope catches most discrepancies, though subtle configuration changes still occasionally slip through. Comparing the purchase commitment to approved scope is a defined step that adds moderate time. It requires careful contract-versus-spec reading, a competency generally available in procurement. Scope-discrepancy checks are low cost relative to catching errors after purchase.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time between final internal approval and supplier receipt of the formal plant order
rows.plain: There should be less time between the last internal approval and the supplier receiving the official order for facility work or equipment. This helps the supplier begin sooner.
rows.imp_rat: Delay between approval and supplier receipt of the order is an order-handling latency distant from processing liquid product (low relevance) with minor schedule impact (low severity). Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Approval-to-transmission handoffs are largely systematized, though occasional manual sign-off queues still cause sporadic delays before the supplier receives the order. Once internal approval clears, formal orders are usually transmitted within a day or two via established PO systems and EDI. Order issuance is a routine clerical task requiring little specialized skill in a mature procurement function. Any lag here rarely adds material cost beyond minor administrative time.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of conflicting provisions across the purchase commitment and its contractual or technical attachments
rows.plain: Make sure the order and its attached documents do not give different instructions or promises. This helps the buyer and seller agree on exactly what is being purchased.
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting provisions across purchase commitment and attachments are contractual/administrative, so both relevance and severity remain low under the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Multiple attachments commonly contain conflicting provisions, and while structured review catches many, inconsistencies regularly survive into signed agreements. Cross-checking a contract against all technical attachments is slow given document volume. Spotting conflicting provisions across attachments requires meticulous, experienced review. The review is labour cost, minor against contract value.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of mismatch among production assumptions used to project capacity for competing plant configurations
rows.plain: Use the same production assumptions when estimating how much different factory layouts can produce, so the layouts can be compared fairly.
rows.imp_rat: Mismatched capacity projection assumptions is planning ambiguity of low relevance to the running plant, with severity held at the ranking-imposed floor.
rows.sat_rat: Suppliers use differing production bases and buyers rarely fully normalize them, so capacity projections across configurations often diverge. Aligning production assumptions across competing configurations adds analysis time to the evaluation. Projecting comparable capacity requires process engineers to harmonize disparate supplier throughput assumptions. Assumption mismatch can lead to buying under- or over-sized capacity, an expensive misjudgement downstream.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which configuration utility requirements are understated relative to facility constraints
rows.plain: Make sure the stated needs for electricity, water, air, and other services match what the facility can actually provide.
rows.imp_rat: Understated utility requirements is a design defect with low relevance to ongoing processing and severity pinned to the batch minimum.
rows.sat_rat: Utility requirement schedules exist but are frequently understated relative to real facility capacity, so shortfalls recur. Reconciling utility requirements against facility limits takes coordination time between disciplines. Correctly stating utility demands versus constraints depends on skilled utility and process engineers who are often engaged late. Understated utility needs cause expensive late upgrades to power, water, or steam systems.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of classifying a plant configuration as footprint-compatible without sufficient space for material flow and operator access
rows.plain: Do not decide that a factory layout will fit just because the equipment physically fits in the available area. There must also be enough room for moving materials and for workers to reach and operate the equipment.
rows.imp_rat: Misclassifying footprint compatibility is a design defect with low relevance to processing operations and severity held at the ranking floor.
rows.sat_rat: 2D/3D layout tools support footprint checks, but access and flow clearances are still sometimes underestimated, misclassifying configurations as fitting. Layout and flow checks add time but the primary concern is a correct compatibility verdict. Judging adequate space for material flow and operator access requires experienced layout and process engineers. A wrong footprint call forces costly rework or facility modification later.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of inconsistency in scope boundaries and proposal assumptions across request documents
rows.plain: Make sure all request documents describe the same work and rely on the same assumptions. This helps suppliers prepare comparable price offers.
rows.imp_rat: Inconsistent scope boundaries and proposal assumptions across request documents is a procurement-documentation ambiguity, tangential to the operating CFJ (low relevance) with correctable consequences; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Request packages are typically assembled by multiple authors under time pressure, so scope and assumption inconsistencies are common and only partially controlled. Reconciling scope boundaries across multiple request documents adds review cycles before issue. Detecting inconsistent assumptions across a document set demands a meticulous lead engineer with contract literacy. Inconsistencies cause costly rework and change orders once bids are compared.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of shortlisted suppliers whose designated contacts do not receive the complete quotation request
rows.plain: Make sure every chosen supplier’s contact person receives the full request for a price and proposal. This gives suppliers all the information they need to respond correctly.
rows.imp_rat: Quotation requests failing to reach supplier contacts is a sourcing-communication error only loosely tied to the CFJ (low relevance), and a missed contact is recoverable by resending (low severity). Ceiling holds it at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Stale distribution lists and wrong-person emails cause a recurring minority of requests to miss their intended designated contacts. Distribution of quotation requests is near-instant by email, so timing is rarely the bottleneck for this need. Maintaining accurate contact lists requires diligent record-keeping that many buying teams handle inconsistently. A missed contact costs little directly but can forfeit a competitive quote, an indirect and infrequent loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a shortlisted supplier cannot prepare a compliant proposal by the submission deadline because of an insufficient response window
rows.plain: Give a potential supplier enough time to prepare a proposal that meets the requirements before the deadline. This allows the supplier to submit a complete offer for consideration.
rows.imp_rat: An insufficient response window for supplier proposals is a procurement-timing concern peripheral to actually processing liquid product (low relevance), and a missed proposal merely narrows the supplier pool without lasting harm (low severity). Batch ceiling caps it at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: When windows are adequate the outcome is dependable, but ad hoc scheduling means some suppliers predictably cannot deliver compliant proposals in time. Standard RFP practice sets explicit submission windows, though these are often compressed when upstream shortlisting runs late, squeezing supplier prep time. Skilled procurement leads know to build in adequate response windows, but junior buyers under pressure sometimes set unrealistic deadlines. A rushed window occasionally forces suppliers to no-bid, reducing competition and driving prices up, a modest indirect cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of material changes to plant requirements or proposal instructions after the quotation request is issued
rows.plain: Avoid changing the factory’s requirements or instructions after suppliers have been asked for prices. This helps suppliers prepare comparable and accurate offers.
rows.imp_rat: Material changes to requirements after a quotation request is issued destabilize sourcing, a procurement-phase concern distant from the running-plant CFJ (low relevance) with manageable consequences; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Change-control processes exist but requirements churn after RFQ issue remains a frequent, poorly contained problem in capital projects. Late requirement changes force re-quoting cycles that stretch the acquisition timeline. Freezing requirements correctly upfront takes disciplined requirements engineering that many teams lack. Post-issue changes trigger price escalations and administrative rework.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of applicable jurisdictional or industry requirements omitted from the compliance review
rows.plain: Make sure the review covers every relevant government rule and industry requirement. This helps the organization avoid breaking rules or missing required protections.
rows.imp_rat: Jurisdictional requirements omitted from the compliance review is a review-completeness defect of low direct relevance to the processing job and limited severity; kept low per the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Requirements are frequently missed when scope spans multiple jurisdictions, so omissions in the compliance review remain a recurring problem. Assembling the full applicable jurisdictional requirement set can be slow across multiple regions. Knowing every applicable jurisdictional and industry requirement demands specialized regulatory expertise that is scarce and the main failure point. Regulatory expertise or consultants add cost, but this is budgeted on major acquisitions.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of required supplier compliance evidence remaining unavailable before purchase commitment
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that required proof of a supplier’s compliance with rules is still missing before the purchase is approved. This helps ensure the supplier meets the company’s requirements.
rows.imp_rat: Supplier compliance evidence unavailable before purchase commitment is a procurement due-diligence delay with low centrality to processing and low severity; held at the minimum under the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Gate reviews usually require evidence before commitment, but some required documents are still outstanding at signing, a common residual risk. Collecting compliance evidence before commitment involves supplier lead times that can delay the decision. Knowing which evidence is required and adequate needs compliance expertise present on most regulated projects. Gathering evidence is mostly a process cost, not a major expense.

rows.stk: Process engineers, procurement leads, project managers.
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that candidate throughput is overstated by excluding cleaning, changeover, and planned downtime
rows.plain: Make sure the expected output includes time for cleaning, switching between products or tasks, and planned stoppages. Otherwise, the actual output may be less than promised.
rows.imp_rat: Overstated candidate throughput from excluding cleaning and downtime is a planning-accuracy error remote from running a batch (relevance) with low operational harm (severity); capped at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Vendors routinely quote ideal throughput and buyers seldom systematically adjust for real downtime, making this a chronic under-caught error. Scrutinizing throughput claims for excluded downtime requires digging into vendor assumptions, slowing evaluation. Recognizing that quoted throughput omits cleaning, changeover and downtime demands experienced operations judgement. Overstated throughput leads to costly under-capacity, while the scrutiny cost is minor.

rows.stk: Process engineers, procurement leads, project managers.
rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of inconsistency in how engineering, procurement, and project stakeholders interpret production requirements
rows.plain: Make sure the engineering, purchasing, and project teams all understand the production needs in the same way before choosing equipment.
rows.imp_rat: Inconsistent interpretation of production requirements across stakeholders is a requirements-alignment error indirectly related to the CFJ; consequences are rework. Ceiling applies.
rows.sat_rat: Requirements are often captured in prose without controlled definitions, so inconsistent interpretation persists as a recurring gap. Differing interpretations of production requirements trigger clarification loops that delay specification finalization. Translating production needs into unambiguous purchase specs requires process-engineering skill that is unevenly available. Misinterpretation risks buying the wrong configuration, but the immediate loss is inconsistency rather than money.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required tank history records missing at ownership transfer
rows.imp_rat: Missing tank history records at ownership transfer is an administrative handover gap with minimal bearing on the core processing job; lowest tier, capped.
rows.sat_rat: Tank history is often fragmented across paper and systems, so required records are commonly missing at ownership transfer. Gathering scattered history records at transfer is a slow, manual assembly task. Locating decades-old vessel records requires familiarity with legacy filing that few retain. Missing records force reconstruction or revaluation, an avoidable cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of applicable vessel records remaining active after deregistration
rows.imp_rat: Vessel records remaining active after deregistration is a registry-cleanup defect with negligible functional consequence; lowest importance tier.
rows.sat_rat: Without a linked asset register, vessel records frequently stay active in some systems long after the tank is gone. Deregistration filings are quick but often deferred after physical disposal. Knowing which registries and jurisdictions apply needs compliance-specific expertise. Lingering active records incur ongoing fees and inspection obligations.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required stakeholders omitted from the tank retirement notification
rows.imp_rat: Omitting stakeholders from a tank retirement notification is a purely administrative communication gap with trivial functional consequences; ranked lowest tier and capped.
rows.sat_rat: Notification lists exist but are frequently incomplete, so required stakeholders are routinely omitted. Issuing retirement notifications is quick once the distribution list is known. Knowing every required stakeholder demands institutional knowledge that is often held by few people. Missing a notification is nearly free to send but costly if it triggers compliance gaps.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of the monitoring gap before tank retirement activities are complete
rows.imp_rat: A monitoring gap before retirement completion is an end-of-life administrative timing issue with trivial consequence; lowest tier, capped.
rows.sat_rat: Scheduled decommissioning plans keep most gaps bounded, but sequencing delays still leave intervals where monitoring lapses before retirement is truly done. The window between monitoring cessation and completed retirement is the very delay this need targets, and it is often longer than planned. Closing out monitoring cleanly needs coordination rather than deep expertise. A prolonged monitoring gap ties up resources and extends oversight cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time the processing-tank purchase request waits for required approver decisions
rows.imp_rat: A purchase request waiting on approver decisions is a procurement workflow delay with minor, correctable impact; lowest importance tier.
rows.sat_rat: Electronic approval workflows with routing and reminders are widely deployed and make cycle time fairly predictable, though bottlenecks at senior approvers persist. Approval waits are the core error here and remain a common source of delay despite workflow tools. Little specialized skill is needed; the delay is about approver availability and process routing. Approval delays chiefly cost schedule float rather than direct money, though they can push into price-escalation windows.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of return delay caused by misaligned site access, pickup, and receiving availability
rows.plain: Spend less time waiting to return equipment because the site, pickup service, and receiving location are not ready at the same time. Better coordination helps the equipment get back sooner.
rows.imp_rat: Return delay from misaligned access/pickup/receiving is a reverse-logistics scheduling error peripheral to batching function; consequences are timeline delays. Ceiling applies.
rows.sat_rat: Standard logistics scheduling handles most returns, though occasional misalignment still causes avoidable delays. Return delays from misaligned site access, pickup, and receiving windows are common but usually resolved within days. Coordinating logistics is routine scheduling work needing little specialized skill. Delays incur modest demurrage or storage costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required decommissioning records left incomplete or inconsistent at project closure
rows.plain: Complete and keep consistent all records needed when the system is taken out of use at the end of the project. This prevents confusion about what was done and leaves a reliable closing record.
rows.imp_rat: Incomplete decommissioning records at closure is a paperwork-consistency error largely peripheral to liquid batching with modest impact; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Checklists and closeout procedures exist but consistency is poor because verification is manual and frequently rushed at project end. Compiling complete decommissioning records at closure is slow and often deferred until teams are already dispersed. Knowing exactly which records are required and cross-checking them demands experienced project-quality personnel who are often unavailable at closeout. Incomplete records force costly reconstruction, re-inspection, or regulatory follow-up later.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of decontamination records missing evidence required for disposal approval
rows.plain: Make sure the cleaning records include all proof needed to approve disposal. Without this evidence, the material may not be cleared for disposal.
rows.imp_rat: Decontamination records missing disposal-approval evidence is a recordkeeping gap peripheral to the batching job with contained consequences; held to 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Decontamination records are often incomplete for end-of-life equipment because cleaning was not documented for disposal purposes, so gaps recur. Compiling decontamination evidence for disposal approval is a slow records-gathering effort. Knowing which evidence satisfies disposal reviewers requires quality and regulatory experience. Missing records force re-decontamination or re-testing, adding cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of historical batch data and recipe records omitted from the export
rows.plain: Make sure exported records include all past production-group information and written machine instructions, so the history is not missing important details.
rows.imp_rat: Omitted historical batch/recipe data in export undermines record completeness, but it sits below the prior batch and is capped at 1/1.
rows.sat_rat: Export scoping often misses archived or ancillary datasets, so partial omissions recur despite decent tooling. Bulk exporting historical data is reasonably fast when scoped properly. Ensuring completeness of the export requires knowledge of where all records reside across systems. Omitted records may require costly retrieval from decommissioned systems later.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that batch records are corrupted during extraction or transfer
rows.plain: Make sure production records are not damaged or changed while they are being copied out or moved. The records must remain usable afterward.
rows.imp_rat: Corrupted batch records during extraction threaten data integrity, but this item ranks below the prior batch and is capped at 1/1.
rows.sat_rat: Standard database export and checksum verification make outright corruption uncommon, though legacy formats occasionally cause issues. Batch record extraction is generally quick with vendor export tools. Running exports needs IT/automation support familiar with the historian or MES, a moderate skill. Corrupted records force costly recovery efforts, but corruption is relatively infrequent.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of exported batch histories missing links to their recipe versions and audit trails
rows.plain: Make sure exported production records stay linked to the correct recipe version and history of changes. These links help people understand how each batch was made and what was changed.
rows.imp_rat: Exported histories missing recipe/audit links weaken traceability, yet ranking below prior batch forces a cap at 1/1.
rows.sat_rat: Exports commonly flatten data and sever links between batches, recipe versions, and audit trails, leaving this poorly served. Re-linking batch histories to recipe versions and audit trails manually is time-consuming when links break on export. Preserving relational links requires data-migration expertise in the specific system architecture. Broken links drive expensive reconstruction and compliance risk.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that exported records cannot be opened or interpreted after the control platform is retired
rows.plain: Make sure the transferred production records can still be opened and understood after the machine’s control software is retired.
rows.imp_rat: Inability to open exported records after platform retirement is an archival/end-of-life concern with low relevance to running batching operations; severity capped at 1 under the batch ordering.
rows.sat_rat: Proprietary, undocumented export formats routinely become unreadable once the platform is retired, and this is a chronic, largely unmitigated failure in the disposal stage. Data migration from a retired control platform is rarely planned ahead, so teams scramble at end-of-life with no time budget for format conversion. Interpreting proprietary export formats after the platform is gone demands scarce specialist knowledge that usually leaves with the vendor or the departed engineer. Recovering or re-hosting unreadable records later is far more expensive than exporting to open formats upfront, but that spend is rarely provisioned.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of misalignment between export verification and decommissioning schedules
rows.plain: Make sure the timing for checking exported batches matches the timing for taking the related equipment out of service, so records are checked before they are no longer available.
rows.imp_rat: Misalignment between export verification and decommissioning schedules is an end-of-life coordination error peripheral to batching; consequences are compliance delay. Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Export control processes exist but are often coordinated separately from decommissioning, so schedule misalignment recurs. Aligning export verification with the decommissioning schedule can introduce timing gaps that delay shipment. Export compliance verification requires specialized trade-compliance expertise that is not always embedded in decommissioning teams. Misalignment risks holds, penalties, or storage costs on export-bound equipment.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of required batch, audit, or service data unintentionally erased while removing proprietary data
rows.plain: Avoid accidentally deleting important production, inspection, or service records while removing the company's private information. This matters because the needed records must still be available afterward.
rows.imp_rat: Erasing required batch or audit data during proprietary-data removal risks compliance loss, but it is a decommissioning-data error ranked below prior items (low relevance); ceiling caps both proxies at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Sanitizing proprietary data without destroying retained records is poorly tooled, so unintended erasure is a real and inadequately mitigated risk. Careful selective deletion is slower than bulk wiping, creating pressure that raises accidental-erasure risk. Separating proprietary data from records that must be retained requires data-classification expertise rarely present at disposal. Unintentionally erased batch or audit data can be irrecoverable, forcing costly reconstruction or compliance exposure.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of transfer or scrapping delays caused by unresolved deletion and retention requirements
rows.plain: Shorten the delay in transferring or scrapping equipment while people are still deciding what information must be deleted and what must be kept. This helps the equipment move on sooner while handling information properly.
rows.imp_rat: Transfer or scrapping delays from unresolved retention rules are end-of-life timing errors remote from batching (low relevance); ceiling caps both proxies at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Retention and deletion requirements are frequently unclear at disposal, so delays from unresolved questions are predictable and recurring. Unresolved deletion and retention questions stall transfer or scrapping until legal and compliance sign off, a common bottleneck. Resolving retention-versus-deletion conflicts needs records-management and legal judgement that disposal teams lack. Delays incur storage and standby costs while assets await disposition clearance.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of removed proprietary data being repopulated on the controller before transfer, resale, or scrapping
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that deleted company information is restored to the machine before it is transferred, sold, or discarded. This helps keep private information from being exposed.
rows.imp_rat: Repopulation of proprietary data on the controller before transfer or scrapping is a data-hygiene/end-of-life concern peripheral to preparing liquid batches, with contained consequences; ceiling of 1.0 applies.
rows.sat_rat: Without chain-of-custody controls, wiped controllers can be re-imaged from backups, and this risk is rarely reliably managed. Confirming data is not restored before transfer adds a verification step often bypassed. Preventing repopulation from backups requires understanding of the system's data architecture that few disposal crews possess. Repopulated data undermining a resale or scrap plan can incur IP and remediation costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of recovered components that retain unacceptable process residues
rows.plain: Make sure recovered parts do not still have unacceptable leftover material or chemicals on them. Clean parts are safer and more suitable for reuse.
rows.imp_rat: Recovered components retaining residues is a decommissioning/recycling concern removed from active batching (low relevance); ceiling holds severity at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Cleaning at disposal is not held to production-grade verification, so recovered components frequently retain unacceptable residues. Achieving and verifying clean recovered components extends teardown time. Removing residues from complex product-contact geometry requires skilled cleaning technicians. Components retaining residue are downgraded or scrapped, wasting recovery value.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of salvageable pumps, valves, sensors, and vessels damaged during extraction
rows.plain: Avoid damaging pumps, valves, sensors, and tanks that could still be used while removing them. This preserves useful equipment and reduces replacement costs.
rows.imp_rat: Damaging salvageable pumps/valves during extraction is an asset-recovery error peripheral to the batching function with limited severity; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Without standardized salvage-extraction procedures, damage rates vary greatly and are commonly underestimated. Gentle extraction of pumps, valves, and sensors is slower than demolition-style removal. Preserving salvageable precision components demands skilled technicians familiar with each device's fragility. Damage during extraction directly reduces resale or reuse recovery value, a real financial loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a recovered component has undetected degradation
rows.plain: Make sure a part recovered for reuse does not have hidden wear or damage. This helps prevent an unsafe or unreliable part from being used again.
rows.imp_rat: Undetected degradation in a recovered component (relevance 1) sits below the prior batch and is capped; while a hidden fault could cause a later batch failure (severity 1), the ranking places it at the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: There is little standard practice to systematically detect hidden degradation on retired components, so this remains a chronic blind spot. Inspecting recovered components for degradation is time-consuming and often skipped under disposal timelines. Detecting undetected degradation requires metallurgical or NDT expertise rarely applied at disposal. Undetected degradation mostly matters if components are reused, but assessment cost is often deemed not worthwhile at end of life.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of recovered components with missing identity or service-history records
rows.plain: Fewer recovered parts should be missing records showing what they are or how they were used and maintained. This information helps determine whether they can be safely reused.
rows.imp_rat: Missing identity/service-history on recovered components matters to asset recovery admin but is peripheral to preparing and batching liquid; consequences are traceability gaps, not batch failure. Ceiling applies.
rows.sat_rat: Records are routinely lost between CMMS, paper files, and removed tags, so missing-identity recovery is chronic and largely unsolved. Locating and attaching service history to each recovered component is slow manual archaeology. Reconstructing missing identity and maintenance records needs experienced staff who recall the equipment. Components lacking provenance fetch far less on resale and may be scrapped, a clear cost loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that recyclable materials are rejected because of residual pharmaceutical or cleaning substances
rows.plain: Make sure recyclable materials are free of leftover medicines or cleaning products before sending them for recycling. This prevents the materials from being rejected or creating a safety problem.
rows.imp_rat: Rejection of recyclables for residues is an end-of-life waste concern outside the core batching job (low relevance); ceiling holds severity at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Pre-recycling decontamination is inconsistent for pharma equipment, so residual substances still cause recycler rejections fairly often. Rejected recyclables must be re-cleaned and re-submitted, adding turnaround time. Achieving recycler-acceptable residue levels requires cleaning know-how applied to end-of-life parts. Rejected recyclables revert to costly hazardous disposal, raising expense.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of system materials assigned to incompatible recycling streams
rows.plain: Send each system material to the correct recycling category rather than putting it in the wrong one. This helps the materials get recycled properly and avoids contamination.
rows.imp_rat: Materials assigned to incompatible recycling streams is a disposal-classification error (relevance 1) with compliance-adjacent but low functional stakes (severity 1); held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Without reliable composition data, mixed and composite parts are frequently assigned to wrong streams, leaving recurring gaps. Sorting materials to correct streams adds sorting time that is sometimes shortcut. Correctly identifying and separating mixed-material assemblies requires material knowledge that on-site crews often lack. Misrouted materials lose recycling value and can incur contamination penalties.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of components whose material composition cannot be verified before routing
rows.plain: Make sure the material each component is made from can be confirmed before it is sent for recycling. This helps ensure materials are handled and recycled correctly.
rows.imp_rat: Unverifiable material composition before routing is a disposal/routing concern (relevance 1) with limited batching consequences (severity 1); held at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Original material certifications are often lost by disposal, so many components cannot be verified and get routed on assumption. Verifying material composition before routing is slow when documentation is missing and testing is needed. Alloy or polymer identification requires XRF tools or lab analysis and trained interpretation not typically on hand. Composition testing or conservative disposal to avoid uncertainty adds cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that applicable recycling channels refuse separated system materials
rows.plain: Make sure the separated materials from the system are accepted by the appropriate recycling services. This prevents recyclable materials from being refused or sent to the wrong place.
rows.imp_rat: Recycling channels refusing separated materials is an end-of-life disposal issue (relevance 1) far from active batching, with low operational stakes (severity 1); held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Established recycling channels for common metals and plastics accept most materials, though contamination or specialty alloys occasionally trigger refusal. Confirming recycler acceptance can require lead time to arrange, occasionally delaying material routing. Waste brokers and recyclers guide acceptance, reducing the skill burden on the facility. Rejected materials incur return, re-handling, or landfill fees, a moderate recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration separated recyclable materials remain onsite awaiting transfer
rows.plain: Move separated recyclable materials offsite sooner. This keeps storage areas from becoming crowded while the materials wait for pickup.
rows.imp_rat: Recyclable materials awaiting transfer onsite is a waste-handling delay error remote from liquid batching; consequences are storage congestion. Held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Established recycling and scrap-hauler arrangements make transfer fairly consistent with only minor delays. Recyclable materials sometimes linger onsite awaiting haulers but pickup is generally arranged within normal cycles. Managing recyclable transfer is routine waste-handling work requiring little expertise. Onsite storage of recyclables carries low cost and materials retain scrap value.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of legal ownership of the batching system remaining disputed after transfer
rows.plain: Make clear who legally owns the mixing system after it is handed over. This prevents later arguments about ownership and responsibility.
rows.imp_rat: Disputed legal ownership after transfer is a contractual/title error peripheral to the batching system's function; consequences are legal exposure but not operational failure. Capped at 1.0 by ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Bills of sale, asset transfer agreements, and title documentation make clean ownership transfer the norm, with disputes rare. Legal ownership transfer paperwork can lag but is typically completed around the transfer event. Clearing title requires legal and asset-management input that is generally available for major disposals. Disputed ownership can expose the firm to liability or lost salvage value, though this is uncommon.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required validation, maintenance, and safety records that are missing or inconsistent at handover
rows.plain: Make sure the new owner receives complete and consistent records about testing, upkeep, and safety. This matters so they can operate and care for the system safely after the handover.
rows.imp_rat: Missing validation/maintenance/safety records at handover is administrative documentation, peripheral to producing batches; severity kept at 1 per the mandatory batch cap.
rows.sat_rat: Handover record sets are commonly incomplete or inconsistent because record-keeping over a system's life is fragmented across systems and people, with only partial checklists to catch gaps. Chasing down scattered validation, maintenance, and safety records at handover consistently delays closeout because they were never consolidated in advance. Knowing which records are required and where they live relies on institutional memory that is often gone by decommissioning time. Reconstructing missing records or re-doing lost documentation adds avoidable labor and rework cost at handover.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of batching system components damaged during removal and relocation
rows.plain: Avoid damaging parts of the material-mixing system when it is taken apart and moved. This helps keep the equipment usable and reduces repair or replacement work.
rows.imp_rat: Components damaged during removal and relocation is a handling error tangential to the batching CFJ itself, with recoverable impact; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Rigging plans and lifting procedures reduce damage, but shared-line congestion and rushed schedules still cause frequent incidents. Careful disassembly and rigging for relocation extends teardown timelines. Safe removal and handling of batching components relies on experienced riggers and millwrights. Damaged components during relocation directly erode salvage or reuse value.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of disruption to adjacent active equipment caused by disconnecting a shared interface
rows.plain: Disconnect a shared connection without interrupting nearby equipment that is still operating. This lets the work continue while other equipment remains in use.
rows.imp_rat: Disruption to adjacent equipment from disconnecting a shared interface is a decommissioning-phase error peripheral to the batching CFJ; consequences are localized downtime. Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Isolation planning and blinding procedures exist but shared-interface surprises still cause unplanned adjacent-equipment downtime. Disconnecting shared interfaces often forces extended shutdowns of adjacent equipment that are hard to minimize. Isolating shared utilities without disrupting live equipment requires skilled process and safety engineers. Disruption to active adjacent equipment can halt production, imposing significant cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of damaging facility-side piping or fittings while separating connections
rows.plain: Avoid damaging the site's pipes or connecting parts while taking the connections apart. This helps keep the site's piping usable and leak-free.
rows.imp_rat: Damaging facility piping during disconnection is a defect risk, but the mandatory ceiling holds relevance and severity at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Standard lockout, isolation, and mechanical disconnection practices make facility-side damage uncommon when procedures are followed. Careful disconnection to protect facility piping adds modest time but is a routine planned activity. Skilled millwrights and pipefitters generally know how to isolate and separate connections without damaging facility-side fittings. Occasional damage to facility piping incurs repair cost but is usually contained.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of process parameters transferred to the replacement system that differ from approved settings
rows.plain: Make sure the operating settings moved to the replacement system are the same as the approved settings, so the process works as intended.
rows.imp_rat: Transferred parameters differing from approved settings could affect quality, but the batch-4 ceiling forces both proxies to 1.
rows.sat_rat: Change-control and validation practice during system replacement reliably reconciles most parameters, though manual re-entry still introduces occasional deviations. Transferring approved parameters to a replacement system is a defined data-migration task with predictable duration. Verifying transferred settings against approved values requires trained validation engineers. Parameter deviations can spoil batches on the new system, an avoidable cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of production batches without a clear system assignment during the transition
rows.plain: Fewer production batches should be left without a clear connection to the system that made them during the changeover. This makes each batch easier to identify and track.
rows.imp_rat: Batches without clear system assignment during transition touches production but as a transitional admin gap; the ranking places it below higher batches, so relevance and severity stay at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Transition planning tools exist but batch-to-system mapping gaps still occur during cutover, leaving some batches unassigned. Reassigning production batches during transition can stall if system ownership is unclear. Correct batch reassignment requires production planners who understand both old and new system capabilities. Misassigned batches risk scrapped or delayed product, a direct production cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of operational responsibilities without clear ownership after handover
rows.plain: Make sure every ongoing work duty has a clearly named owner after the handover. This prevents important duties from being left undone.
rows.imp_rat: Operational responsibilities lacking ownership after handover is a governance/handover error with only indirect bearing on batching function; consequences are coordination confusion. Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Handover often relies on informal RACI or meeting notes, leaving responsibilities ambiguous and frequently orphaned. Clarifying ownership of residual responsibilities during handover often drags on past the transition. Defining who owns each remaining operational duty needs managerial judgement across functions. Ownership gaps create low direct cost but risk costly lapses in safety or compliance duties.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of major components or subsystems omitted from the final condition record
rows.plain: Record the condition of every major part of the system at the end, so problems are not overlooked and others know what they are receiving.
rows.imp_rat: Components omitted from the final condition record is an end-of-life documentation gap, low relevance to producing batches; forced to 1.0 by the ordering.
rows.sat_rat: Final condition records are typically checklist-driven yet still omit minor or hidden subsystems because coverage is not systematically enforced. Walking down every component for the final condition record takes significant time that decommissioning schedules often compress. Knowing which subsystems and components must be captured requires competent asset knowledge, not just a walkthrough. Thorough condition recording costs inspection labor but avoids downstream disputes over omitted assets.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that operational condition cannot be verified after supporting utilities are disconnected
rows.plain: Make it unlikely that the machine’s working condition cannot be checked after its supporting equipment and connections are turned off or removed. This helps create a reliable record of its final condition.
rows.imp_rat: Inability to verify operational condition after utilities are disconnected relates to decommissioning (relevance 1) and its consequences are contained (severity 1); capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Standard practice does not capture verified operational state before de-energizing, so condition frequently cannot be confirmed afterward. Once utilities are disconnected there is a narrow window and verifying operational condition afterward is often impractical, causing delays if attempted. Confirming operational condition post-disconnection needs test-rig setups and technicians familiar with the system. Re-energizing or rigging temporary utilities to verify condition is costly and seldom justified at disposal.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of condition entries associated with the wrong component or subsystem identifier
rows.plain: Make sure each condition note is linked to the correct part or section of the system. This prevents people from making decisions based on information about the wrong item.
rows.imp_rat: Condition entries tied to the wrong identifier is a data-mapping error with low centrality to preparing liquids and minor consequence; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Manual, spreadsheet-based condition logging with degraded or removed tags makes wrong-component entries a common, poorly caught error. Correctly matching condition entries to component IDs slows the assessment step when tags are missing. Accurate association demands personnel who know the system's tagging and P&ID conventions. Misassigned condition data causes downstream valuation errors and rework costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of sterility-critical interlocks, alarms, or sequence steps that behave incorrectly after transfer
rows.plain: Avoid having important safety connections, warnings, or operating steps work incorrectly after the control settings are moved to another system. This helps the equipment keep operating safely.
rows.imp_rat: Sterility-critical interlocks behaving incorrectly after transfer is a contamination risk, yet the batch ceiling requires both proxies remain at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Transferred sterility interlocks are frequently mis-mapped and current requalification testing catches only a fraction, leaving this poorly served. Verifying interlock and alarm behavior after transfer adds substantial testing time that is often compressed under restart pressure. Validating sterility-critical sequence logic requires specialized validation and controls expertise that few staff possess. An interlock behaving incorrectly can scrap sterile batches, making the cost of failure very high.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of required control logic, parameters, and automation settings absent from the replacement configuration at cutover
rows.plain: Put all needed operating instructions, settings, and automatic controls into the replacement system before it is put into use, so it behaves as expected.
rows.imp_rat: Missing control logic and automation settings at cutover is relevant to correct batching operation, but the batch ceiling caps this item; severity of an incomplete replacement configuration is constrained here.
rows.sat_rat: Status quo relies on manual configuration inventories and spreadsheets that routinely omit undocumented tweaks, so undetected gaps at cutover remain common. Configuration transfer at cutover is a slow, manual reconciliation that stretches decommissioning timelines when logic is discovered missing late. Correctly capturing every control parameter and automation setting demands scarce automation engineers who understand both the retiring and replacement systems. Missed logic surfaces as rework and post-cutover firefighting, driving up commissioning cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of selecting a superseded or unapproved retiring-system configuration as the migration source
rows.plain: Avoid choosing an outdated or unapproved setup from the system being replaced as the starting point for the move. Using the wrong setup could cause errors in the new system.
rows.imp_rat: Selecting a superseded configuration as migration source is a data-migration risk at retirement, peripheral to active batching; ceiling holds both proxies at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Weak or ambiguous version control on retiring systems makes selecting a superseded configuration a real and recurring risk with few safeguards. Verifying which configuration is the approved current one adds time when revision histories are unclear. Distinguishing an approved baseline from a superseded one demands configuration-management expertise that is often thin at disposal. Migrating from the wrong source configuration causes costly rework or invalid replacement builds.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of transferred configuration elements lacking traceability to an approved source record required for release
rows.plain: Make sure every moved setting can be traced back to an approved record before the system is put into use. This shows where each setting came from and supports safe approval.
rows.imp_rat: Configuration elements lacking traceability to approved source records affect release integrity, but ranking places it below prior items; capped at 1/1.
rows.sat_rat: Legacy systems often lack complete approved source records, so a meaningful share of configuration elements cannot be traced for release. Tracing each transferred configuration element to an approved source record is a slow, document-intensive effort. Establishing traceability for release requires quality and validation specialists who understand data integrity requirements. Gaps in traceability trigger costly investigations and delay release.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of sterile fill-finish production restart delay caused by configuration migration at cutover
rows.plain: Spend less time waiting to restart germ-free medicine production while the old equipment settings are moved to the replacement. This helps the replacement start with the right settings sooner.
rows.imp_rat: Restart delay from configuration migration affects throughput, but this item is capped below the prior batch, keeping both proxies at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Restart timing is unpredictable because configuration issues surface only during requalification, so schedules routinely slip. Restart delay from configuration migration is the core error and current practice offers little to shorten the migration-driven downtime. Speeding restart depends on experienced migration teams working in parallel, a scarce capability. Idle sterile fill-finish lines are enormously expensive per day, so delays translate directly into lost production value.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required disposal approvals or notifications omitted from the submission plan
rows.plain: Include every required approval and notice for disposing of the equipment in the submission plan. This helps avoid delays or violations during disposal.
rows.imp_rat: Disposal approvals omitted from the submission plan is a compliance-paperwork gap peripheral to batching with contained consequences; ceiling of 1.0 enforced.
rows.sat_rat: Established EHS checklists cover common approvals but omissions still occur for unusual materials or jurisdictions. Building the full list of required approvals and notifications takes time researching applicable jurisdictions. Identifying every required approval demands regulatory affairs expertise that varies by site. Omitted notifications risk penalties and rework costing time and money.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that approval applications are rejected for incomplete or inaccurate system-disposal information
rows.plain: Make sure disposal applications contain complete and accurate information about the system being discarded. This helps prevent the applications from being rejected.
rows.imp_rat: Disposal-approval rejections for incomplete information are administrative end-of-life paperwork, peripheral to the CFJ of batching liquid product (low relevance); consequences are delay rather than harm and this batch is capped at 1.0 (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Standard application templates reduce errors but incomplete or inaccurate system information still triggers periodic rejections. Correcting rejected applications and resubmitting lengthens the disposal timeline. Accurate system-disposal applications require experienced regulatory preparers. Rejections cause delay-driven storage and re-filing costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of regulatory review beyond the planned decommissioning start date
rows.plain: Spend less time waiting for government approval to dispose of equipment after the planned shutdown date. This helps the shutdown stay on schedule.
rows.imp_rat: Regulatory-review delay past decommissioning start is a schedule/compliance issue at end-of-life, low relevance to core batching; ceiling forces both proxies to 1.
rows.sat_rat: Review durations are highly variable and unpredictable, making schedule slippage the norm rather than the exception. Regulatory review of decommissioning routinely overruns planned start dates because agency timelines are outside the operator's control. Skilled regulatory-affairs staff can shorten review, but such expertise is scarce and unevenly available. Extended review keeps assets and staff in limbo, accruing standby and holding costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of conflict between environmental and safety authorities over applicable disposal requirements
rows.plain: Resolve differences between environmental and safety officials about how the waste must be disposed of. This prevents delays and helps ensure the disposal is accepted by all authorities.
rows.imp_rat: Conflict between authorities over disposal requirements is a regulatory-coordination concern at end-of-life, peripheral to batching; held at 1.0 by the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Jurisdictional overlap between environmental and safety authorities produces genuine conflicting requirements with no reliable arbitration mechanism, a chronic pain. Resolving conflicts between environmental and safety authorities drags out approvals unpredictably. Reconciling competing regulatory regimes requires specialized legal and compliance judgement that few disposal teams possess. Prolonged inter-agency conflict inflates consulting and delay costs with no clear owner.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an approval or permit expires before disposal activities begin
rows.plain: Make sure the required approval or permit is still valid when disposal begins. This prevents disposal work from starting without permission.
rows.imp_rat: An expiring disposal permit is a decommissioning scheduling issue distant from the batching CFJ (low relevance); ceiling holds severity at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Calendar and compliance-tracking tools reliably flag expirations for most sites, though coordination lapses occasionally let permits lapse. Permit expiry before disposal begins is fundamentally a timing/scheduling failure between approval and execution. Tracking permit validity windows needs organized regulatory administration. An expired permit forces costly re-application and idle-asset storage.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of unclear operational control ownership during the shift transition
rows.plain: Shorten the time when it is unclear who is responsible for controlling operations during a shift change. Clear responsibility helps the work continue safely and smoothly.
rows.imp_rat: Unclear control ownership during shift transitions is a transient handoff-ambiguity error, peripheral to the plant's core function (low relevance) and generally short-lived without lasting consequence (low severity). Ceiling-bound.
rows.sat_rat: Overlap periods and verbal 'you have control' conventions cover many transitions, but the absence of a formal, logged ownership transfer leaves recurring short windows of ambiguous responsibility. Control-ownership ambiguity typically lasts only minutes but recurs at nearly every transition when no explicit handoff moment is defined. Clear ownership depends on operators' shared understanding of shift protocol rather than any enforced mechanism. Brief ownership gaps rarely cost money unless an event occurs precisely during the transition, so exposure is intermittent.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the incoming shift is unavailable at the scheduled handover
rows.plain: Make sure the workers taking over the shift will be available at the agreed handover time. This prevents gaps in responsibility and important information.
rows.imp_rat: An incoming shift being unavailable at handover is a scheduling-delay error, low in relevance to the core processing job and low in severity given short-term coverage options.
rows.sat_rat: Established rostering, attendance rules and overlap windows make scheduled-handover unavailability infrequent, with absenteeism and staffing shortages the main residual causes. Fixed shift schedules and overlap periods mean the incoming shift is present on time in the large majority of transitions. Ensuring coverage is a supervisory scheduling task requiring little specialized skill. Overtime or callout costs arise when relief is late or absent, but these are contained by standard rostering and on-call arrangements.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration between observing processing data and documenting them in the run record
rows.plain: Write down production measurements and observations as soon as they are seen. This helps keep an accurate record of what happened during the production run.
rows.imp_rat: The delay between observing and documenting processing data is largely administrative to the CFJ, and the ranking ceiling keeps both relevance and severity at the floor.
rows.sat_rat: Automatic timestamped capture is highly consistent where deployed, with delay only for the residual manual-entry fields. Automated data acquisition documents processing data essentially in real time, collapsing the observe-to-record lag. Electronic records remove the transcription skill and diligence formerly required to record promptly. MES/historian systems that timestamp automatically are widely deployed and cheap to run once installed.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of changes to run records lacking operator, timestamp, or reason attribution
rows.plain: Keep people from changing production records without saying who made the change, when it happened, or why. This makes the records trustworthy and easier to check.
rows.imp_rat: Missing attribution on run-record changes is a data-integrity administrative concern, and the ranking ceiling keeps relevance and severity at the floor.
rows.sat_rat: Part -style audit trails make unattributed record changes rare where enforced, though paper and legacy systems still leave gaps. Electronic audit trails attach attribution instantly with no added operator time. Systems enforce operator, timestamp and reason capture so it no longer depends on individual diligence. Compliant electronic-record systems carry validation cost but are standard in regulated liquid processing.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of setpoint changes omitted from operator handoffs during a production run
rows.plain: Make sure all changes to the target operating settings are passed on when operators change shifts during production. Missing a change could cause the process to run incorrectly.
rows.imp_rat: Omitted setpoint changes at handoff is a communication-ambiguity error confined to shift transitions (low relevance) and generally correctable mid-run (low severity). Held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Although modern DCS/historian systems log setpoint changes, handover communication of them still relies on manual recall, so changes made mid-run are regularly omitted from operator-to-operator handoffs. Recounting every setpoint change made during a run lengthens handover and is frequently truncated under time pressure. Remembering and communicating all setpoint adjustments depends on operator diligence and recall rather than an automatic capture mechanism. An unreported setpoint change can push a batch off-spec and waste material, while the handoff itself costs nothing.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of completed maintenance, calibration, or cleaning events omitted from skid history
rows.imp_rat: Omitting maintenance or calibration events from skid history degrades traceability, but this error is capped at 1 on both proxies by the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: CMMS and electronic logbooks capture most events reliably, but manual entry lapses and offline tasks still leave occasional gaps in skid history. Logging maintenance events adds minor documentation time to each task. Recording events is a low-skill compliance activity supported by CMMS workflows. Record-keeping systems are already in place, so incremental cost is negligible.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a critical replacement component is unavailable when skid maintenance is required
rows.imp_rat: Unavailable replacement components delay maintenance, but per the batch-5 ceiling this ranks below prior items; relevance to skid operation and severity of downtime are both capped at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Min/max stocking and criticality analysis cover common spares, but long-lead or obsolete components periodically prove unavailable when needed. A missing critical spare can extend downtime dramatically while a part is sourced. Spare-parts planning is an inventory-management discipline needing forecasting rather than technical skill. Balancing carrying cost of critical spares against stockout risk is the central tension, and long-lead specialized parts strain budgets.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of external skid surfaces retaining dust, spill residue, or contamination after cleaning
rows.imp_rat: Residual external contamination after cleaning poses risk, but as a lower-ranked item both relevance and severity must stay at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Routine cleaning SOPs and visual checks handle external surfaces well, with residual dust or spill residue being uncommon and easily corrected. External surface cleaning is quick and easily fit into routine maintenance. Wipe-down of external surfaces is a low-skill, well-understood task. Cleaning supplies and labor for external surfaces are minimal.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required final-condition and disposition details omitted from the skid record
rows.imp_rat: Omitted final-condition and disposition details weaken the skid record, but this documentation error is capped at 1 for both relevance and severity under the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Closeout records are frequently rushed or partially filled, leaving disposition and final-condition details missing from the skid record with no strong enforcement. Completing final-condition and disposition fields is a brief closeout task when done. Documenting disposition needs only basic competence, though completeness depends on diligence. Recording detail costs essentially nothing beyond staff time.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of required quality, regulatory, and operations stakeholders omitted from the skid-retirement notification
rows.imp_rat: Omitting stakeholders from a skid-retirement notification is a purely administrative communication error with minimal impact on the CFJ, scoring at the low end of the range.
rows.sat_rat: Retirement notifications rely on informal or partial distribution lists, so omitting a required quality or regulatory stakeholder is a recurring failure. Notification is a quick administrative step once the stakeholder list is known. Little specialized skill is needed, but knowing every required recipient depends on organizational awareness. Notification carries negligible direct cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of eligible skid material discarded with nonrecyclable waste
rows.imp_rat: Discarding recyclable skid material with nonrecyclable waste is a peripheral sustainability error; both relevance and severity are held at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Absent a standard segregation process, the share of eligible material diverted from waste is inconsistent and largely dependent on individual initiative. Sorting materials for recycling adds handling time that projects under pressure often skip. Identifying recyclable alloys and segregating streams requires some materials knowledge not always present. Recoverable stainless and metals are routinely landfilled, forfeiting salvage value and incurring avoidable disposal fees.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of skid scope and qualification-support inclusions unresolved before purchase-order issuance
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved scope and qualification inclusions before PO issuance create ambiguity, yet the ranking caps both relevance and severity at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Scope-of-supply matrices exist but unresolved inclusions at PO issuance remain a common source of disputes. Resolving scope and qualification-support inclusions before PO adds negotiation time that is often rushed. Defining qualification-support scope boundaries needs experienced commissioning/qualification input that is not always engaged early. Unresolved scope inclusions become expensive post-award claims and change orders.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a transit exception affecting skid arrival goes unreported to the project manager
rows.imp_rat: An unreported transit exception delays response, but this coordination error ranks below prior items so both proxies are capped at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Carrier tracking and defined escalation paths make unreported transit exceptions relatively uncommon. Transit exception reporting is quick once carrier tracking and notification channels are in place. Escalating a transit exception to the PM is a low-skill communication task. An unreported exception can be costly, but tracking and carrier notifications keep this rare.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of qualified biopharmaceutical skid vendors omitted from the market landscape
rows.imp_rat: Omitting qualified vendors from the market landscape narrows sourcing options, but the ranking holds both relevance and severity at 1.
rows.sat_rat: The qualified biopharma skid vendor market is narrow and well-known, so omitting a genuinely qualified vendor is fairly unlikely. Building a market landscape of qualified vendors takes research time but is a one-time upfront effort. Identifying qualified biopharma skid vendors uses established industry networks and directories requiring moderate market knowledge. Missing viable vendors forgoes competitive savings, but the known vendor pool is relatively small and well-documented.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that skid utility demand exceeds available facility capacity in intended operating states
rows.imp_rat: Utility demand exceeding facility capacity could halt operations, yet the ranking places this error below the previous batch, so both relevance and severity are held at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Utility load assessment is established engineering practice, but mismatches in specific operating states still slip through. Utility demand-versus-capacity checks are a defined engineering task but can lag in the acquisition timeline. Utility balance calculations across operating states need competent process/utility engineers who are usually available. Undersized utilities discovered late force costly facility upgrades, though standard load calcs reduce frequency.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of skid damage during loading, transit, or placement at the receiving facility
rows.imp_rat: Skid damage during loading or transit is costly, but as a lower-ranked item both relevance and severity are capped at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Mature crating, rigging, and transport practices make skid damage during handling relatively uncommon though not eliminated. Rigging and transit planning add lead time but are handled by established logistics practice. Safe loading and placement rely on experienced riggers and logistics specialists who are readily contracted. Transit damage is expensive when it happens, but proper crating and insurance limit exposure.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of required manuals, certificates, and test reports missing from the turnover package
rows.imp_rat: Missing manuals and certificates from the turnover package hinders handover, yet both relevance and severity remain at 1 given the ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Documentation deliverable lists are standard, but incomplete turnover packages remain a frequent late-stage annoyance. Verifying the turnover documentation package is a defined receiving/closeout step but tedious. Checking manuals, certificates, and test reports against a required list is a checklist task needing modest expertise. Missing turnover documents delay qualification and incur retrieval costs, mitigated by contractual deliverable lists.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of critical skid components omitted from supplier spare-parts availability information
rows.imp_rat: Critical components omitted from spare-parts availability info affects planning, yet both relevance and severity stay at 1 under the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Recommended spare-parts lists are commonly requested, though critical-component completeness is inconsistent across suppliers. Requesting spare-parts lists from suppliers is a routine procurement task with modest turnaround. Identifying critical components needing spares requires maintenance/reliability judgement that is generally available. Omitted spares information risks costly downtime later, partly offset by standard recommended-spares requests.

rows.stk: Procurement, engineering, and quality personnel responsible for goods receipt.
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of transit damage remaining undetected during skid receipt inspection
rows.imp_rat: Undetected transit damage during receipt inspection risks later failure, but the batch ceiling holds both relevance and severity at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Standard receiving inspection plus shock/tilt indicators catch most damage, but concealed damage still occasionally goes undetected. Receipt inspection is a quick scheduled step at delivery. Detecting concealed transit damage requires careful inspection skill and sometimes specialists beyond a visual check. Undetected damage found later voids claims and drives costly repairs, though shock indicators reduce this.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of plant-specific licenses, monitoring services, or support subscriptions left active after contract cancellation
rows.imp_rat: Leaving licenses or subscriptions active after cancellation is a minor recurring-cost waste, trivial in consequence (low severity) and unrelated to the core processing job (low relevance).
rows.sat_rat: Without a consolidated contract register, orphaned subscriptions routinely stay active and continue billing after shutdown. Cancelling licenses, monitoring, and subscriptions is quick per item but easily forgotten in the closeout rush. Identifying every plant-specific subscription needs procurement and contract knowledge but little technical skill. Leftover active subscriptions bleed recurring money, and this is exactly the waste the need targets.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of residual asset value lost in the completed plant sale
rows.imp_rat: Residual asset value lost in a plant sale is a financial recovery waste that neither drives nor blocks the core processing job (low relevance) and has bounded monetary consequences (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Used-plant markets and auction channels exist but valuations are variable and outcomes uncertain, so value recovery is inconsistent. Marketing and selling a decommissioned plant or its assets takes considerable time, and rushed sales depress value. Maximizing sale value needs specialist asset brokers and market knowledge not held in-house. Residual asset value lost in the sale is a direct financial loss, and undervalued or scrapped-instead-of-sold plant is common.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the planned site access route cannot accommodate the batching system's dimensions or weight
rows.plain: Make sure the route into the site has enough space and can support the batching system's weight. This helps prevent problems when bringing the equipment to the site.
rows.imp_rat: Site-access route accommodating dimensions/weight is a delivery-phase constraint, not central to the batching CFJ; consequences are logistical and the ceiling holds it at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Established route-survey practice makes dimension/weight refusals fairly rare, though bridge and clearance limits occasionally get missed. A site access route survey is a quick step relative to the overall acquisition schedule. Transport route assessment is a routine competency for heavy-haul logistics providers. Route surveys are inexpensive compared with the cost of a stranded oversized load.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which available transport and rigging capacity falls below the batching system's size and weight requirements
rows.plain: Make sure the available vehicles, lifting equipment, and handling support can safely move the production system, given its size and weight.
rows.imp_rat: Insufficient transport/rigging capacity is a one-time logistics risk with low centrality to the batching function; ranking forces relevance and severity to 1.
rows.sat_rat: Weight/dimension data plus standard capacity checks make undersized rigging uncommon when specs are accurate. Matching rigging and transport capacity to load is a standard pre-mobilization calculation that rarely delays anything. Riggers routinely compute capacity against equipment size and weight as core competence. Upsizing cranes/transport when needed is a known, budgetable cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of delivery delay caused by conflicting availability among the site, carrier, and rigging contractor
rows.plain: Coordinate the location, transport company, and lifting contractor so the delivery does not have to wait. This helps the equipment arrive when everyone is ready to receive and move it.
rows.imp_rat: Delivery delay from conflicting site/carrier/rigging availability is a logistics-scheduling error remote from the core batching job; consequences are timeline slippage. Ceiling applies.
rows.sat_rat: Without shared scheduling tools, conflicting availability recurs unpredictably and confirmations often unravel at the last minute. Aligning site readiness, carrier slots, and rigging crews is a manual phone-and-email scheduling exercise that frequently stalls deliveries for days or weeks.
