Coordinating three independent parties requires a skilled logistics coordinator who anticipates dependencies, and such staff are thin on the ground for one-off system deliveries. Delays incur standby, storage, and re-mobilization charges, but the cost is secondary to the schedule hit felt first.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of batching system damage during loading, transport, unloading, or rigging
rows.plain: Protect the mixing system from damage while it is loaded, moved, unloaded, or lifted into place. Damage during delivery could delay installation or make the system unsafe to use.
rows.imp_rat: Physical damage during loading/rigging can impair the batching system, but the mandatory ceiling caps relevance and severity at 1/1.
rows.sat_rat: Mature rigging practice and packaging standards make handling damage uncommon, though tight sites and lifts still cause occasional incidents. Proper rigging and transport planning adds lead time but is a well-established logistics step. Specialized riggers and heavy-haul crews with the needed competence are commercially available for this class of equipment. Qualified rigging and protective packaging cost money, but far less than damaging a fabricated skid.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that delivery is held because required transport permits or site-entry clearances are missing
rows.plain: Make sure the delivery is not delayed because required travel permissions or permission to enter the site are missing.
rows.imp_rat: Delivery held for missing transport permits is a logistics-timing error tangential to the batching function, and its consequences are recoverable; capped at 1.0 by ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Established freight/logistics practice covers most permit needs, but oversized or hazardous batching-skid moves still occasionally get held. Arranging transport permits and site-entry clearances is time-sensitive and can hold a delivery if started late. Logistics coordinators and freight forwarders handle permits competently as a routine function. Missing clearances cause demurrage and rescheduling costs, though these are usually contained.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that material financial instability in a prospective supplier remains undetected before approval
rows.plain: Check whether a possible supplier is financially stable before approving it, so financial trouble does not disrupt the purchase.
rows.imp_rat: Undetected supplier financial instability is a procurement-risk error peripheral to batching operation, with delayed rather than acute consequences; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Credit-report and financial-screening practice is well established, catching most material instability though private-firm opacity leaves gaps. Financial checks via credit reports are quick to obtain within the evaluation window. Interpreting financial-stability signals needs some finance literacy but is supported by standard rating services. Supplier failure post-award is very costly, which the check aims to preempt.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which a prospective supplier's manufacturing capability claims are unsupported for the required batching systems
rows.plain: Make sure a possible supplier can prove its claims about making the required batch-production systems.
rows.imp_rat: Unsupported supplier capability claims is a vetting concern only indirectly tied to the batching CFJ, with limited direct severity; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Capability claims are self-asserted and rarely independently validated, so unsupported claims frequently go undetected. Verifying manufacturing capability claims requires audits or visits that add weeks to acquisition. Assessing whether a supplier can actually build the required batching systems needs experienced technical due-diligence capability. Capability shortfalls surface as costly delays or defects later, not at claim stage.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required supplier qualification evidence that is missing, outdated, or unverifiable during assessment
rows.plain: Make sure suppliers provide complete, current, and checkable proof that they meet the required standards. This matters so the business can confidently decide whether a supplier is suitable.
rows.imp_rat: Missing or outdated supplier qualification evidence is an administrative assessment defect with low centrality to batching operation and modest consequence; ceiling of 1.0 enforced.
rows.sat_rat: Supplier qualification packages commonly arrive with expired certs or gaps, and while checklists catch many, missing or unverifiable evidence still slips through regularly. Collecting and cross-checking supplier qualification evidence is slow, with back-and-forth requests stretching assessment timelines. Recognizing outdated or unverifiable certificates requires trained quality-audit judgement that is unevenly available on procurement teams. Supplier audits and document reviews consume auditor hours but are a budgeted, expected acquisition expense.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of conflicting assessments of the same prospective supplier against organisational procurement criteria
rows.plain: Make sure different people reach the same conclusion when judging a possible supplier against the organisation’s buying rules. This supports fair and reliable supplier decisions.
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting supplier assessments against procurement criteria is a vendor-evaluation error distant from the batching CFJ; consequences are inconsistent sourcing. Held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Standardized scorecards exist in mature procurement organizations but are inconsistently used, so conflicting assessments of the same supplier still recur. Reconciling divergent supplier assessments consumes extra review meetings, slowing the qualification step. Consistent scoring against procurement criteria depends on evaluator experience, and differing judgement is the root cause of conflicts. The rework of re-assessing suppliers wastes staff effort but little hard money.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the purchase order specifies a system configuration different from the approved configuration
rows.plain: Make sure the purchase order describes the same system setup that was approved. This helps prevent the wrong equipment or features from being ordered.
rows.imp_rat: A purchase order specifying a configuration different from the approved one is a procurement-accuracy defect with recoverable consequences and modest centrality; held to 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: PO-to-approved-config discrepancies happen when specs evolve between approval and ordering, and only disciplined change control reliably prevents them. Reconciling the PO against the approved configuration adds a review step but is usually quick when a spec baseline exists. Matching PO line items to an approved configuration is a clerical-plus-technical check that moderately skilled buyers handle. Configuration mismatches on the PO can trigger costly change orders, so errors here carry financial weight.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time between final supplier selection and purchase order issuance
rows.plain: There should be less time between choosing the final supplier and sending the official purchase order. This helps the supplier start providing the requested system sooner.
rows.imp_rat: Time between selection and PO issuance is a purely administrative procurement delay with negligible bearing on preparing liquids; ranking fixes it at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Mature ERP and procurement workflows make the selection-to-PO handoff dependable, with residual delay mostly from final contract-term negotiation rather than process failure. Once a supplier is selected, issuing the PO is a fairly routine, well-templated procurement step that typically closes within days for established vendors. PO issuance follows standard purchasing procedures that clerks and procurement staff execute without specialized expertise. The clerical effort to convert a selection into a PO is low-cost and rarely a source of material overspend.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of discrepancies between the purchase order and the approved supplier offer for price, currency, taxes, and payment terms
rows.plain: Make sure the order matches the supplier's approved offer for the price, type of money used, taxes, and payment schedule. This prevents unexpected costs or payment disputes.
rows.imp_rat: PO-versus-offer discrepancies on price, currency, and terms are commercial-document errors peripheral to preparing liquids (low relevance); batch ranking forces both proxies to 1.
rows.sat_rat: Mature three-way-match and PO-approval practice makes commercial-term discrepancies uncommon, though manual re-keying still lets a few through. Cross-checking a PO against the accepted offer takes little time when ERP fields pre-populate from the quote. Standard purchasing-clerk competence covers matching price, currency, tax, and payment terms against an offer document. Errors here directly cost money, but modern PO templates and approval workflows catch most before commitment.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unresolved contractual conflicts between the purchase order and the final supplier agreement
rows.plain: Resolve any contract differences between the purchase order and the final agreement with the supplier. This helps ensure both sides are committed to the same deal.
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved PO-versus-agreement conflicts are contractual ambiguity errors distant from the batching function (low relevance); batch-5 cap keeps both proxies at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Standard T&Cs and supplier counter-terms frequently coexist unreconciled, and battle-of-the-forms conflicts routinely persist into execution. Reconciling PO terms with a negotiated final agreement drags out when legal review cycles between parties. Resolving contractual conflicts needs legal and contracts expertise that many acquisition teams lack in-house for specialized equipment. Unresolved conflicts surface as disputes or change orders that cost money later, though not immediately visible at PO stage.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of delivered-system identifiers rejected as invalid or mismatched
rows.plain: Make sure the system’s identifying information is correct and matches the delivery, so the system can be registered without errors.
rows.imp_rat: Rejected delivered-system identifiers is a receiving/labeling defect, low centrality to the batching CFJ; severity capped at 1 by batch ordering.
rows.sat_rat: Tagging and ERP matching make outright invalid identifiers relatively uncommon, though occasional label errors still cause rejections. Verifying identifiers at receiving is quick when barcodes or tag numbers are used. Matching identifiers to order documentation is a routine clerical activity. Identifier verification is cheap and prevents costly acceptance of wrong items.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that required delivery-acceptance evidence is unavailable when registration is submitted
rows.plain: Make sure all required proof that the delivery was accepted is available before registering the system. This prevents the registration from being delayed or rejected.
rows.imp_rat: Missing delivery-acceptance evidence at registration is a documentation-sequencing error with low centrality to batching and limited severity; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Acceptance-evidence retention is a fairly established GDP practice, though handoffs between receiving and regulatory occasionally drop records. Assembling delivery-acceptance evidence at registration time can delay submission if not captured proactively. Standard QA/documentation staff know to retain acceptance records, requiring modest specialized skill. Missing evidence rarely costs money directly but risks registration rework.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required registration fields with conflicting owner or installation-site details
rows.plain: Make sure the owner and installation location are recorded consistently, so the system registration does not contain conflicting information.
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting owner/site details in registration fields is a clerical data-entry error tangential to the batching job with minor severity; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Registration forms are structured and reviewed, so conflicting details are relatively infrequent though not eliminated. Reconciling conflicting owner and site details for registration adds minor rework time when caught. Basic administrative diligence catches most owner/site field conflicts. Conflicts carry negligible direct cost, only correction effort.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of the gap between delivery acceptance and manufacturer confirmation of registration
rows.plain: Spend less time waiting between accepting the delivered system and the maker confirming that it has been officially recorded. This helps the system become fully recognized sooner.
rows.imp_rat: The gap between delivery acceptance and manufacturer registration confirmation is an administrative delay peripheral to batching, with mild impact; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Confirmation timing is unpredictable and outside the buyer's control, so the gap varies widely with no reliable standard. The gap between acceptance and manufacturer registration confirmation is often long and dependent on the manufacturer's own cadence. Little specialized skill closes this gap; it is largely administrative follow-through. The delay ties up capital in an unregistered asset but incurs little direct spend.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of reference requests unanswered before the supplier selection decision
rows.plain: Get answers to all requested user references before choosing a supplier. This gives the buyer useful feedback from people who have already used the supplier.
rows.imp_rat: Unanswered reference requests before selection is a due-diligence gap peripheral to the batching job (low relevance); the mandatory ceiling holds both proxies at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Reference outreach is ad hoc and low-response, so a meaningful share stay unanswered when the selection is made. Reference responses often lag or never arrive before the decision deadline, forcing choices on incomplete input. Getting references answered is more about persistence and relationships than specialized skill. Unanswered references carry little direct cost, only decision risk.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of reference users whose operating settings are not comparable to the intended sterile fill-finish environment
rows.plain: Make sure reference users work in conditions similar to the planned sterile drug-filling operation. Their experience is more useful when their equipment and work setting are comparable.
rows.imp_rat: Reference users whose operating settings differ from the sterile fill-finish environment is a benchmarking-comparability issue with low relevance to batching the liquid itself, and its consequences are indirect; capped at 1.0 by batch-4 ranking.
rows.sat_rat: References are routinely offered from dissimilar (non-sterile or lab-scale) installations, so buyers frequently act on non-comparable evidence with no standard way to verify comparability. Chasing down reference-site operating conditions comparable to a sterile fill-finish context adds weeks of site visits and questionnaires during acquisition. Judging whether a reference user's environment truly matches sterile fill-finish demands scarce GMP process expertise that few buying teams possess. The status quo relies on travel and consultant time to vet references, but the direct spend is modest relative to the risk.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which reference accounts are biased by supplier screening or participation
rows.plain: Make sure other customers’ opinions are not shaped only by which customers the supplier chose or allowed to take part.
rows.imp_rat: Reference-account bias from supplier screening is a peripheral procurement-vetting concern with low relevance to actually batching and preparing liquids, and consequences are mild; capped at 1.0 by batch ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Suppliers almost always hand-pick favorable references, and buyers have no established mechanism to counter this selection bias. Screening for reference bias is rarely done, so it adds no time but also yields no assurance. Recognizing that supplier-curated references are skewed requires evaluator sophistication that is inconsistently present. Biased references impose no direct cost but raise the risk of a costly wrong pick.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of decision-critical operating detail withheld because of confidentiality restrictions
rows.plain: Get as much important information about how the equipment operates as possible, even when people cannot share some details because they are confidential.
rows.imp_rat: Operating detail withheld due to confidentiality is an information-transfer friction with low centrality to batching and modest impact; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Suppliers routinely withhold operating detail citing confidentiality, and buyers lack reliable leverage to obtain it pre-award. NDA negotiation to unlock withheld detail slows the information-gathering phase. Extracting decision-critical detail past confidentiality barriers requires skilled negotiation and technical questioning. Confidentiality limits impose no direct cost but risk under-informed, costlier decisions.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of evaluation criteria with conflicting peer accounts of system performance in sterile fill-finish use
rows.plain: Make sure people giving references agree about how well the system performs when used to fill and seal sterile products. Conflicting accounts make it harder to judge whether the system is suitable.
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting peer accounts on sterile fill-finish performance is an evaluation-ambiguity error in sourcing, not central to batching itself (low relevance); ceiling holds both proxies at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Peer accounts of performance in this specialized use frequently conflict, and there is no standard method to reconcile them. Reconciling conflicting peer accounts requires extra follow-up interviews that stretch evaluation. Judging which conflicting reference account to trust for sterile fill-finish performance needs deep domain familiarity. The cost is mainly evaluation effort and selection risk rather than direct spend.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the supplier's committed completion date falls after the required site-delivery date
rows.plain: Make sure the supplier can finish the order early enough for it to arrive at the work site by the required date. This helps keep the work on schedule.
rows.imp_rat: A supplier completion date slipping past required site delivery is a scheduling error tangential to liquid batching function (low relevance); mandatory batch-5 ceiling keeps both proxies at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Delivery-date commitments in this niche are notoriously variable because engineered batching systems have long, uncertain fabrication cycles that standard PO terms poorly enforce. Suppliers routinely quote optimistic lead times and the status quo relies on manual schedule tracking, so committed dates slipping past required delivery is a chronic, late-discovered problem. Experienced procurement staff can probe for realistic delivery commitments, but this judgement is scarce and inconsistently applied across buyers. Late commitments trigger expedite fees, penalties, or idle downstream resources, but the direct monetary hit is secondary to the schedule slip itself.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that critical custom components are unavailable when manufacturing must begin
rows.plain: Make sure important specially made parts will be available when manufacturing needs to start.
rows.imp_rat: Unavailable custom components at manufacturing start is a supply-chain risk upstream of operation; despite real stakes, the batch ordering caps importance at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Custom long-lead items frequently slip, and status quo scheduling and expediting only partly protect the manufacturing start date. Long lead times on critical custom components mean availability gaps at manufacturing start are common and hard to compress. Managing custom-component procurement schedules requires skilled supply-chain planners who are often overloaded. Expediting or holding buffer stock for custom items is costly, and shortfalls halt production expensively.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of specified configuration requirements omitted from the supplier's commitment
rows.plain: Make sure the supplier agrees to every required setup or feature. This prevents important requirements from being left out of the supplier's promise.
rows.imp_rat: Configuration requirements omitted from a supplier commitment is a contractual-completeness error, indirect to the CFJ (low relevance); cap sets severity to 1.
rows.sat_rat: Suppliers omit or reinterpret specified requirements in their commitments, and matrix-based traceability is inconsistently applied. Cross-checking each specified requirement against the supplier's written commitment is a slow line-by-line exercise. Detecting silently dropped requirements in a supplier's response takes an experienced technical reviewer. Requirements the supplier never committed to become paid change orders later, a real financial exposure.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of waiting for the supplier to provide a firm manufacturing and component-availability commitment
rows.plain: Get a definite promise from the supplier about when the item can be made and whether its parts are available. This helps the buyer know when the order can realistically be delivered.
rows.imp_rat: Waiting for a firm manufacturing/availability commitment is a pre-purchase delay with limited relevance to preparing liquid batches and easily-absorbed consequences; held at the cap.
rows.sat_rat: Firm availability commitments are frequently provisional given component shortages, so certainty remains low. Waiting for a supplier's firm manufacturing and component-availability commitment can stretch out due to their own supply-chain checks. Extracting a firm commitment takes a buyer who knows how to press for confirmed lead times, a moderately specialized negotiation skill. The delay defers the schedule more than it wastes money directly.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that confirmed component availability changes before purchase terms are finalized
rows.plain: Make sure the parts that are currently available will still be available when the purchase agreement is completed. A change could delay the order.
rows.imp_rat: The error of component availability changing before purchase terms finalize is a procurement-timing concern peripheral to actually batching liquids (low relevance); per the batch-5 ceiling and its ranking below prior items, both proxies are capped at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Supplier quotes and lead-time confirmations are only weakly binding, so confirmed availability frequently shifts before finalization. Confirmed availability can change during the window before purchase terms close, and re-checking lags real supplier status. Keeping availability confirmations current demands diligent buyer follow-up that is easy to let lapse. A reversed availability commitment forces costly resourcing or expediting after terms were set.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of batching devices that lose communication with the control platform after the update
rows.plain: Keep mixing equipment from losing its connection to the control system after the software is updated. This helps the equipment continue communicating and operating as expected.
rows.imp_rat: Devices losing communication after an update affects control, but this batch's ranking mandates relevance and severity at 1 regardless of intrinsic stakes.
rows.sat_rat: Staged rollouts and post-update verification help, but device dropouts after firmware/software updates still occur with some regularity. Devices dropping communication after updates trigger time-consuming re-commissioning. Restoring lost device communication requires controls engineers familiar with the platform. Lost communication causes downtime and troubleshooting labor cost after each update.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that required personnel, parts, or approvals are unavailable at the scheduled maintenance start
rows.plain: Make sure the needed workers, replacement parts, and approvals are ready when planned maintenance is due to begin. This helps the maintenance work start on time.
rows.imp_rat: Personnel, parts, or approvals missing at maintenance start is a scheduling delay only tangential to the core batching job, and its impact is recoverable; held to 1.0 by the batch ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Work-order kitting and readiness reviews are common practice, yet parts stockouts and pending approvals still stall starts. Missing parts or approvals at start time cause immediate delays and idle crews. Coordinating personnel, parts, and approvals demands attentive planning across multiple functions. Standing down a crew for missing items is expensive and largely preventable with kitting.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of completed inspections, calibrations, replacements, repairs, updates, and service events omitted from the batching-system history
rows.plain: Fewer completed checks, adjustments, part changes, repairs, updates, and other service work should be missing from the production system's history. This provides a complete record of what was done.
rows.imp_rat: Omitted service events from history impair traceability, yet the ranking caps both proxies for this batch.
rows.sat_rat: Manual, memory-dependent logging routinely leaves inspections and minor repairs unrecorded, leaving the batching-system history systematically incomplete. Reconstructing omitted events later consumes investigation time, but the primary damage is the incomplete history itself. Complete logging depends on technicians remembering to record every event, a discipline that varies widely by individual and shift. Omitted service events cause duplicated work and premature part replacement, modestly inflating maintenance spend.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of maintenance records containing incorrect inspection findings, calibration results, replacement details, repair details, update details, or service dates
rows.plain: Keep maintenance records accurate about inspection results, equipment checks, replaced parts, repairs, changes, and service dates. Reliable details help workers know the machine’s true condition and service history.
rows.imp_rat: Incorrect maintenance records affect traceability, but this item ranks below the prior batch so relevance and severity are capped.
rows.sat_rat: Manual and paper-based record entry remains common, so incorrect findings, dates, and details recur despite QA review. Wrong record entries surface later as time lost reconciling history during audits or repeat work. Accurate record entry depends on diligent technicians transcribing findings correctly. Bad records cause rework and compliance risk cost, though entry itself is inexpensive.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of calibration and service records missing required certificates, approvals, or sign-offs
rows.plain: Fewer adjustment and repair records should be missing required certificates, approvals, or signatures. This helps show that the work was properly completed and accepted.
rows.imp_rat: Missing certificates and sign-offs on records is a compliance gap, but the batch ceiling constrains relevance and severity here.
rows.sat_rat: Paper forms and disconnected CMMS entries mean required approvals routinely slip through, and audits frequently surface records with absent or unsigned certificates. Chasing missing sign-offs after the fact adds days to audit prep, but the delay is a symptom of the gap rather than the core loss. Ensuring every certificate and approval is attached demands knowledgeable technicians and QA reviewers who know which documents each regulated activity requires. Missing certificates trigger costly re-work, repeat calibrations, and audit findings, though the direct spend is modest compared to compliance exposure.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of maintenance events represented by conflicting records in the batching-system history
rows.plain: Make sure the batching system does not show conflicting versions of the same maintenance event. One consistent record prevents confusion about what work happened.
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting maintenance records create ambiguity, but the mandatory ceiling holds both proxies at 1 for this batch.
rows.sat_rat: Duplicate systems (paper plus CMMS plus operator notes) regularly produce disagreeing entries, and there is little automated reconciliation to catch them. Resolving which conflicting record is correct wastes troubleshooting time, though the delay follows from the ambiguity. Sorting out contradictory entries requires experienced staff who can judge which source to trust, a scarce and error-prone task. Conflicting records occasionally drive wrong parts orders or unnecessary interventions, adding some avoidable cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time between completion of a maintenance activity and its entry into the batching-system history
rows.plain: There should be less time between finishing maintenance work and recording it in the production system’s history. This keeps the work record up to date.
rows.imp_rat: Delay in logging maintenance into the batching-system history is administrative (relevance 1) and rarely affects live batching output (severity 1); capped at the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Delayed entry increases the chance details are forgotten or transcribed wrong, but modern mobile CMMS terminals help where deployed. Records are often batched and entered at shift end or later, so lag between completing work and logging it remains routinely long. Prompt entry needs no special expertise, just discipline, so skill is not the limiting factor here. The delay itself costs little directly, mostly manifesting as stale data used for later decisions.

rows.source_job: Back up control and recipe data
rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of required batch recipes, calibration records, controller settings, user configurations, and event logs omitted from each backup
rows.plain: Include all needed production instructions, equipment adjustment records, operating settings, user choices, and activity histories in every backup. This helps restore operations if the original information is lost.
rows.imp_rat: Omitted recipes, calibration records, and logs from backups matter for recoverability, yet this item ranks below the prior batch so both proxies stay at the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Manual or partially scripted backups routinely omit some recipes, logs, or configs, so completeness is frequently incomplete. Discovering omitted items during recovery forces time-consuming manual reconstruction. Knowing every artifact that must be captured requires deep system knowledge. Incomplete backups drive costly rebuild effort when restoration is needed.

rows.source_job: Back up control and recipe data
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that exported backup files are corrupted or unreadable
rows.plain: Make sure saved copies of the machine’s operating settings and product instructions are complete and can be opened.
rows.imp_rat: Corrupted backup files are a data-integrity issue; the mandated batch cap holds relevance and severity at 1 regardless of intrinsic stakes.
rows.sat_rat: Verification and checksum practices exist but are inconsistently applied, so undetected corrupt backups still surface at recovery. A corrupt backup discovered at recovery time causes severe delay when it is too late to remake it. Verifying backup integrity requires disciplined procedures that not all teams follow. Corrupt backups can force full reconfiguration at very high cost.

rows.source_job: Back up control and recipe data
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that related recipes, calibration records, controller settings, and user configurations are captured from inconsistent system states
rows.plain: Make sure recipes, measurement records, machine settings, and user preferences are saved from one consistent version of the system. This helps prevent missing or conflicting information during recovery or transfer.
rows.imp_rat: Capturing records from inconsistent system states is a backup-integrity nuance; the mandated batch cap keeps relevance and severity at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Backups are often taken piecemeal from a running system, so snapshot consistency across all configuration types is frequently unverified. Capturing configurations from inconsistent states forces later reconciliation that costs time. Ensuring a coherent snapshot across recipes, calibration, and settings demands methodical expertise. Inconsistent captures risk faulty restores and rework at real cost.

rows.source_job: Back up control and recipe data
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of unauthorized access to sensitive process data during export and storage
rows.plain: Protect sensitive information about how the process is run when it is copied and stored. This prevents people without permission from accessing it.
rows.imp_rat: Unauthorized access to exported process data is a data-security concern tangential to physically batching liquids (low relevance); while a breach can matter, the ranking caps this batch at 1.0 so severity is held to 1.
rows.sat_rat: Regulated GMP environments commonly enforce access controls and audit trails, so unauthorized access during export is relatively well contained. Security controls rarely delay routine data export once configured. Proper secure export and storage requires IT/security expertise not always embedded in maintenance teams. Encryption and access controls are low incremental cost once in place.

rows.source_job: Back up control and recipe data
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that completed backups cannot be located or retrieved when recovery is required
rows.plain: Make sure saved copies of control and recipe information can be found and opened when needed to restore it. A backup is not useful if no one can locate or retrieve it.
rows.imp_rat: Un-retrievable backups matter for recovery, but the mandatory batch-4 cap forces relevance and severity to 1 as it ranks below prior items.
rows.sat_rat: Structured backup repositories and retention policies exist, yet inconsistent labeling means some backups still cannot be found when needed. Hunting for a needed backup during recovery adds critical delay when systems are down. Locating the correct backup version relies on organized administrators following naming conventions. Failed retrieval prolongs outages at high cost, though storage itself is cheap.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of required wiring and terminal connection points that remain inaccessible or obscured during inspection
rows.plain: Make all needed wires and connection points visible and reachable during inspection, so workers can check them properly and find problems.
rows.imp_rat: Obscured wiring and terminal connection points during inspection is a maintainability/access defect of modest relevance and severity; batch ceiling of 1.0 applies.
rows.sat_rat: Many panels and conduits leave terminal points obscured, so a meaningful percentage remain uninspectable without rework. Obscured wiring points require extra time to expose during inspection. Tracing and exposing terminal connections safely requires qualified electricians. Panel access and re-terminating adds modest labor cost when connections are hidden.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of batching-system electrical components misidentified because records conflict with installed wiring
rows.plain: Avoid wrongly identifying electrical parts because the paperwork does not match the wires and parts that are actually installed. Accurate identification helps people inspect, repair, and operate the system safely.
rows.imp_rat: Misidentified electrical components from conflicting records is a records-vs-reality defect of limited direct relevance to batching output; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: As-built documentation is chronically out of date at many sites, so electrical components are frequently misidentified against records. Misidentified components from outdated drawings waste time troubleshooting the wrong part. Resolving record-versus-reality conflicts leans on experienced electricians who can read installed wiring. Wrong-part orders and misdiagnosis drive avoidable material and labor cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required tank, piping, pump, valve, mixer, sensor, and fitting inspection points inaccessible at the scheduled interval
rows.plain: Reduce the number of equipment inspection spots that workers cannot reach when they are due to be checked. This helps them find wear or leaks on time.
rows.imp_rat: Inaccessible inspection points at scheduled intervals is a maintainability delay of limited relevance to producing a batch and modest severity; held to 1.0 by ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Established access plans and sample-point design cover most points, though some tanks, valves, and fittings remain hard to reach at interval. Inaccessible inspection points force disassembly that adds time to the outage. Reaching obscured points often needs skilled riggers or specialized access procedures. Access difficulties add labor and scaffolding costs but are partly designed around.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of disagreement between maintenance technicians and process engineers about whether observed deterioration requires corrective action
rows.plain: Make sure maintenance workers and process specialists agree on whether signs of wear or leaks need to be fixed. This helps ensure problems are neither ignored nor treated unnecessarily.
rows.imp_rat: Disagreement between technicians and engineers on deterioration is a judgment-alignment issue (relevance 1) with indirect impact on batching (severity 1); capped at the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Without agreed criteria and trend data, the same condition gets judged differently across people, producing inconsistent maintenance decisions. Debating whether deterioration warrants action can stall decisions, but the delay is secondary to the disagreement itself. The disagreement stems from differing expert judgement between technicians and engineers with no shared objective threshold, a fundamentally skill/judgement-bound problem. Wrong calls either over-maintain (wasted parts and labor) or under-maintain (failures), so the cost swings both ways.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of batching-system unavailability caused by sealing-component replacement
rows.plain: Keep the equipment that prepares products in batches from being unavailable for long while worn sealing parts are replaced. This helps prevent leaks and lets production restart sooner.
rows.imp_rat: Downtime from sealing-component replacement touches availability (relevance 1) but the batch ceiling holds both proxies at 1 (severity 1).
rows.sat_rat: Seal replacement is a well-understood, standardized procedure with predictable outcomes and mature kits available. Seal replacement typically requires draining, disassembly, and re-sanitization, keeping the system down for a predictable but significant interval. Seal changes are routine mechanical work most maintenance techs perform competently with standard procedures. Seals are inexpensive consumables, so the cost impact is minor relative to the lost production time.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of equipment condition indicator unavailability during production
rows.plain: Spend less time without information showing whether the equipment is working properly during production. This helps staff notice problems and respond sooner.
rows.imp_rat: Condition-indicator unavailability during production affects monitoring (relevance 1) but the mean must stay at the batch ceiling despite some risk (severity 1).
rows.sat_rat: Condition monitoring exists on modern systems but sensor dropouts, network gaps, and unmonitored components leave recurring blind spots during production. Gaps in condition indicators leave technicians blind for periods, delaying detection of developing faults. Interpreting and restoring condition monitoring requires instrumentation expertise that is not always on hand. Indicator outages risk undetected degradation leading to expensive failures, though the monitoring hardware itself is a modest spend.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percent of component runtime records containing inaccurate values during production
rows.plain: Record fewer incorrect readings about how long machine parts have been running during production. Accurate records help workers understand the machine’s condition and maintenance needs.
rows.imp_rat: Inaccurate component runtime records mildly affect maintenance planning (relevance 1) but do not directly stop batching (severity 1); held at the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Automated PLC/SCADA runtime counters are common and fairly accurate, though sensor drift and manual overrides still corrupt values. Correcting inaccurate runtime values after the fact takes some effort but is not the main harm. Detecting and fixing bad runtime readings requires technicians who understand sensor behavior and expected duty cycles. Inaccurate runtime data drives mis-timed maintenance, wasting parts or causing premature failures with real cost impact.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of corrosion-prone batching-system surfaces left unprotected during extended inactivity
rows.plain: Protect surfaces that can easily rust on the mixing system while it is not being used for a long time, so they do not deteriorate.
rows.imp_rat: Unprotected corrosion-prone surfaces during lay-up is a preservation issue during inactivity, marginal to active batching; capped at 1.0 by batch position.
rows.sat_rat: Established preservation practices exist and work when applied, but coverage of hard-to-reach or overlooked surfaces during extended inactivity is inconsistent, leaving recurring gaps. Applying preservation and lay-up protection is a well-understood procedure that takes predictable time when scheduled. Corrosion preservation of wetted surfaces is standard trade knowledge, though correct selection of inhibitors and coatings still needs some competence. Preservation materials and labor are modest relative to the cost of corrosion-driven scrap, so the economics favor doing it.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of drain, isolation, or preservation states recorded incorrectly at lay-up handoff
rows.plain: Make sure the records correctly show whether drains were emptied, equipment was separated or secured, and the equipment was protected for storage. This helps prevent damage while the equipment is not being used.
rows.imp_rat: Incorrectly recorded drain/isolation/preservation states at lay-up is an end-of-service recordkeeping detail, peripheral to the CFJ and capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Lay-up state logging depends on manual tagging and paper or spreadsheet capture that is error-prone, so incorrect drain/isolation/preservation records are a common recurring failure. Recording drain, isolation, and preservation states at lay-up is quick when a form exists but is often deferred until details are forgotten. Correctly capturing each state requires the operator to understand the system's isolation logic, which varies by installation. Errors here cost little to record correctly but can trigger expensive rework or damage if a state is misread later.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required operations, quality, and safety stakeholders omitted from the system-removal notice
rows.plain: Make sure the notice about removing the system reaches everyone responsible for operations, quality, and safety. This gives all affected people time to prepare and respond.
rows.imp_rat: Omitting stakeholders from the removal notice is a notification-completeness error (relevance 1) with coordination consequences (severity 1); held at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Change-control and MOC processes typically enumerate required stakeholders, though informal removals sometimes miss functions. Compiling the full distribution list takes little time when a stakeholder register exists. Knowing which operations, quality, and safety roles must be notified requires organizational familiarity more than technical skill. Omitting a stakeholder is cheap to prevent but can cause costly downstream disruption.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that stakeholders receive the notice too late to prepare for the system removal
rows.plain: Make sure the notice arrives early enough for everyone affected to prepare for the system’s removal.
rows.imp_rat: Late notice for system removal is a communication-timing concern (relevance 1) with modest consequences (severity 1); capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Planned shutdowns follow project timelines with scheduled notifications, though ad hoc removals still occasionally surprise stakeholders. The core error is late notice, and standard project scheduling with advance decommissioning plans usually gives stakeholders reasonable lead time. Issuing timely notices is a routine coordination task requiring little special skill. Late notice can force rushed workarounds, but the notification itself is inexpensive.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of critical removal details missing from the notice
rows.plain: Avoid leaving important information about removing the equipment out of the notice. Complete instructions help everyone affected understand what will be removed and what they need to do.
rows.imp_rat: Missing critical removal details in a notice can cause rework during decommissioning, but this administrative error ranks below the prior batch and is capped at 1/1.
rows.sat_rat: Standardized MOC notice templates capture most critical details, but freeform notices routinely omit specifics. Assembling complete removal details is quick when a template drives the notice. Populating critical details needs someone who understands the removal scope, a modest expertise requirement. Missing details cause avoidable rework and coordination cost during removal.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of inconsistency in removal dates, scope, or responsibilities communicated across operations, quality, and safety teams
rows.plain: Make sure the operations, quality, and safety teams receive the same information about when equipment will be removed, what the work includes, and who is responsible.
rows.imp_rat: Inconsistency in removal dates/scope across teams is a decommissioning coordination error only loosely tied to the CFJ; consequences are communication friction. Held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Communication commonly relies on email and siloed schedules, so inconsistencies across teams are frequent and hard to catch. Reconciling removal dates and scope across teams consumes repeated meetings and delays alignment. Coordinating operations, quality, and safety requires practiced cross-functional facilitation. Inconsistent information causes rework and standby costs when teams act on conflicting plans.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that stakeholder receipt and understanding remain unconfirmed before removal begins
rows.plain: Make sure everyone affected has received and understood the notice before removal starts. This helps them prepare and avoid surprises.
rows.imp_rat: Unconfirmed stakeholder receipt before removal is a coordination unreliability; consequences are recoverable and it sits below the prior batch, capped at 1/1.
rows.sat_rat: Most notices assume receipt via email without documented acknowledgment, so genuine confirmation of understanding is frequently unverified. Chasing confirmations of receipt and understanding adds coordination time before removal can proceed. Confirming understanding is administrative rather than skill-intensive. Unconfirmed understanding is cheap to close but expensive if it leads to unsafe or disruptive removal.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the batching system is routed to the wrong destination because return-channel instructions conflict
rows.plain: The batching system should be sent to the correct destination, even if different return instructions are provided. This prevents the equipment from being returned to the wrong place.
rows.imp_rat: Misrouting the system due to conflicting return-channel instructions is a reverse-logistics error remote from the core preparation job (low relevance); the batch ceiling holds both proxies at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Return channels for retired systems are often documented inconsistently across contracts and vendors, so wrong-destination routing happens with partial safeguards only. Sorting out conflicting return-channel instructions delays dispatch while destinations are confirmed. Interpreting which return instruction governs requires logistics and contract familiarity that varies by team. Misrouting incurs re-shipping, storage, and penalty costs that are wholly avoidable.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the return is refused because eligibility or authorization records are incomplete
rows.plain: The equipment should only be returned when the records show that it is allowed and properly authorized for return. This prevents the return from being refused.
rows.imp_rat: Return refusal from incomplete authorization records is an end-of-life administrative error not central to batching (low relevance); the ceiling caps both proxies at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Eligibility and authorization records are frequently incomplete because they are assembled reactively, so refusals recur despite established return processes. A refused return forces re-submission cycles that stall the disposal timeline. Assembling complete eligibility and authorization records demands knowledge of the specific return program's rules. Refusals cause repeated logistics and storage costs while paperwork is corrected.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of damage to the batching system during disconnection, removal, or transit
rows.plain: Prevent the batching system from being damaged while it is disconnected, removed, or transported. This helps keep the equipment usable when it is returned.
rows.imp_rat: Damage during removal or transit affects asset integrity, yet the ranking places this below the prior batch, capping proxies at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Established rigging and crating practices keep gross damage uncommon, though minor damage during disconnection is routine. Protecting the system during removal and transit adds handling time but is normal rigging work. Experienced riggers and movers handle skid-mounted systems competently, though attention to fragile instrumentation varies. Since the batching system is being disposed of, transit damage is less costly unless resale or recycling value is impaired.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required supplier certifications that are expired, invalid, or unverifiable
rows.plain: Make sure all supplier certificates required by law or company rules are current, genuine, and possible to verify. This helps confirm that the supplier still meets the requirements.
rows.imp_rat: Expired or unverifiable supplier certifications is a supplier-qualification defect with low direct relevance to running the plant and modest severity; held at the floor within the cap.
rows.sat_rat: Certification checking is a mature practice with online verification, so expired or invalid certs are usually caught, though unverifiable niche certs occasionally remain. Verifying certifications is fairly fast when accreditation registries are accessible. Checking certification validity is a routine task requiring modest skill. Verification is low cost and often free via public registries.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of purchased licenses, warranties, and service entitlements omitted during registration of the liquid product processing plant
rows.plain: Fewer purchased software permissions, guarantees, and support rights should be missed when registering the facility that processes liquid products. This helps ensure the facility receives everything it has paid for.
rows.imp_rat: Omitting purchased licenses, warranties, and entitlements during registration is an administrative asset-tracking defect, peripheral to the operating CFJ (low relevance) with modest recoverable consequences; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Contract and asset-management systems capture most entitlements, but scattered documentation still leads to occasional omissions. Registering licenses, warranties and entitlements is a discrete clerical task done reasonably promptly. The task is largely administrative and needs modest skill once documentation exists. Omitted entitlements waste money already paid for support and coverage.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of purchased entitlements being assigned to the wrong plant, site, or legal entity
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that purchased usage rights are assigned to the wrong factory, location, or company. This helps the intended site use what it bought.
rows.imp_rat: Assigning purchased entitlements to the wrong plant, site, or entity is a registration/administrative error, tangential to the CFJ (low relevance) with correctable consequences; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: ERP and asset registers reduce misassignment, though multi-site and multi-entity structures still cause periodic errors. Assigning entitlements to the correct entity is quick when master data is in order. Correct assignment needs care but little specialist skill given standard asset registers. Misassigned entitlements can forfeit paid coverage or complicate tax and warranty claims.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of submitted plant identifiers that conflict with supplier records
rows.plain: Avoid submitting facility identification details that do not match the supplier’s records. This helps activate the facility’s approved access or services correctly.
rows.imp_rat: Plant identifiers conflicting with supplier records is a data-reconciliation ambiguity peripheral to the CFJ (low relevance) and easily corrected clerically (low severity). Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Identifier mismatches occur at a modest steady rate from transcription and versioning errors, caught by routine reconciliation but not eliminated. Resolving an identifier conflict against supplier records adds a correction cycle but is not the primary time sink. Matching identifiers is a clerical check that most receiving staff perform competently with reference tables. Conflicts cause minor rework and occasional misallocation but little hard cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time from complete plant registration submission until purchased entitlements become available
rows.plain: Spend less time between completing a plant registration and making the purchased services available. This lets the plant use what it paid for sooner.
rows.imp_rat: Delay from registration submission to entitlement availability is an administrative provisioning lag distant from the core job (low relevance) with modest impact (low severity). Batch cap keeps it at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Activation timelines are inconsistent across suppliers, though most eventually complete without loss of entitlement. Entitlement activation after registration can take days to weeks depending on the supplier's manual back-office processing. The registration submission itself is low-skill, but resolving activation holdups requires knowing whom to escalate to. Delayed entitlements rarely cost money directly unless a service need arises during the gap.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of mismatch between activated warranty or service coverage and purchased terms
rows.plain: Make sure the support and warranty coverage that is turned on matches what was actually bought, so the plant receives the protection it paid for.
rows.imp_rat: Mismatch between activated and purchased warranty coverage is a post-sale entitlement error only loosely relevant to processing operations (low relevance) and resolvable through correction (low severity). Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Warranty activation frequently drifts from purchased terms because registration and contract data are entered separately and rarely cross-checked. Reconciling activated coverage against purchased terms usually happens well after the fact rather than promptly. Catching coverage mismatches requires someone who reads warranty terms carefully against the order, a detail-oriented task often neglected. Coverage gaps can leave the buyer paying for repairs that should have been covered, a real financial exposure.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of shortlisted liquid processing plant suppliers that fail minimum financial capability criteria
rows.plain: Choose suppliers that have enough money and financial strength to do the work. This reduces the risk of selecting a supplier that cannot complete the project.
rows.imp_rat: Shortlisted suppliers failing financial-capability criteria is a procurement screening concern, peripheral to the operating plant's CFJ (low relevance) with manageable consequences; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Established supplier-qualification and credit-rating practices catch most weak suppliers, though private-company opacity leaves residual gaps. Financial screening can be done quickly once credit reports and statements are gathered. Standard financial-vetting frameworks let procurement staff apply minimum criteria without deep specialist skill. Credit checks and financial due-diligence services are inexpensive relative to plant value.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of shortlist decision delays caused by late supplier capability evidence
rows.plain: Spend less time choosing the best suppliers when information showing what they can do arrives late. Earlier information helps qualified suppliers be selected sooner.
rows.imp_rat: Shortlist delays from late capability evidence are largely administrative, and the ranking holds both proxies at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Evidence arrives inconsistently and in varying formats, so the completeness of the shortlist basis is unpredictable from one procurement to the next. Shortlist decisions routinely stall for weeks while buyers chase late capability evidence through email and phone follow-ups with no enforced turnaround. Experienced procurement staff can partly anticipate which suppliers lag and prompt them early, but this depends on scarce institutional memory. Delays inflate carrying cost and staff hours spent re-soliciting, but the direct monetary loss is secondary to the schedule slip felt first.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of inconsistency in scope boundaries and financial assumptions used to calculate candidate plant costs
rows.plain: Use the same definition of what is included and the same money-related assumptions when estimating the cost of each possible plant. This makes the estimates fair to compare.
rows.imp_rat: Inconsistent scope/financial assumptions in plant cost estimates is planning ambiguity with low relevance to processing operations and capped severity per the ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Bid normalization templates exist but are inconsistently applied, so candidate cost comparisons often rest on mismatched assumptions. Reconciling cost bases across candidates is a laborious step that slows comparison. Ensuring consistent scope and financial assumptions across bids demands skilled estimators who normalize submissions. Inconsistent cost bases produce misleading comparisons that can drive a more expensive selection.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of installation and commissioning expense left unbudgeted because sterile-site integration, qualification, or production-interruption requirements were overlooked
rows.plain: Include all costs for installation and getting the equipment ready to use, including connecting it to the sterile production area, checking that it works properly, and handling any production stoppage.
rows.imp_rat: Unbudgeted installation and commissioning expense from overlooked integration or qualification requirements is a project-budgeting defect, loosely tied to the CFJ (low relevance) with cost-overrun but recoverable consequences; ceiling holds at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Because qualification and interruption impacts are project-specific and hard to foresee, unbudgeted surprises recur despite experienced teams' best efforts. Scoping site-integration and qualification budgets thoroughly takes time rarely allocated before order. Estimating sterile-site integration, qualification and production-interruption costs demands rare combined GMP and engineering expertise. These overlooked installation and commissioning costs are a major source of budget overrun on sterile plant projects.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of deviation between projected energy expense and energy expense under the expected production profile and utility tariffs
rows.plain: Make the predicted energy cost match the energy cost the machine is likely to use during normal production and under the local energy prices. This makes the total cost estimate more reliable.
rows.imp_rat: Deviation between projected and actual energy expense affects cost forecasting (relevance) with modest financial consequence (severity), and is capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Vendor efficiency data and simulation tools give a defensible baseline, but real tariff volatility and off-design operation cause persistent deviation. Running energy models against tariffs and production profiles takes engineering effort but is achievable within the acquisition window. Reasonable energy projections require process and utility engineers who exist but must interpret uncertain future tariffs. Energy is a major recurring operating cost, and deviations between projected and actual spend directly erode the business case.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that lifecycle maintenance expense is underestimated because parts, labor, service, or replacement needs are omitted
rows.plain: Include all expected costs for parts, workers, repairs, services, and replacements, so the long-term cost is not estimated too low.
rows.imp_rat: Underestimating lifecycle maintenance expense from omitted needs skews budgets (relevance) with financial impact (severity), but it is a planning estimate capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Standard practice leans on optimistic vendor maintenance schedules, so systematic underestimation recurs with few checks to catch omissions. Building a complete lifecycle maintenance forecast is slow and often deferred under acquisition deadlines. Anticipating every part, labor, service and replacement stream for a complex processing plant requires deep maintenance and reliability expertise rarely present at buy time. Omitted maintenance items are the classic hidden overspend that inflates true ownership cost long after the deal closes.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of candidate ownership-cost inputs that lack supporting evidence
rows.plain: Fewer estimated costs of owning and using the equipment should be based on unsupported information. This helps people compare options using trustworthy cost estimates.
rows.imp_rat: Ownership-cost inputs lacking supporting evidence weakens financial estimates (relevance) with modest downstream risk (severity), and is capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Today's total-cost-of-ownership models are built largely on vendor estimates and analogies, so a large share of inputs lack traceable evidence and the practice to enforce sourcing is immature. Chasing evidence for each cost input adds weeks of back-and-forth with suppliers and internal finance during acquisition. Distinguishing an evidenced cost input from an assumed one demands experienced cost engineers who are scarce on capital-plant teams. Unsupported cost inputs routinely drive material budget errors that surface only after purchase commitment.

rows.stk: Process engineers, procurement managers, capital project teams.
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of qualified sterile fill-finish plant suppliers omitted from the market landscape
rows.plain: Fewer approved suppliers of facilities that fill products under germ-free conditions should be left out of the search. This helps ensure all suitable options are considered.
rows.imp_rat: Omitting qualified suppliers from the market landscape narrows options during sourcing, a procurement-scope concern distant from the running-plant CFJ (low relevance) with modest consequence; ceiling holds at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: In a niche market, informal networks and trade directories miss capable entrants, so omissions are a recurring known gap. Building a full market landscape of qualified suppliers is a time-consuming search across a niche field. Knowing the full population of credible sterile fill-finish plant builders requires specialist market knowledge concentrated in a few consultants. Missing a qualified supplier costs competitiveness rather than direct outlay.

rows.stk: Process engineers, procurement managers, capital project teams.
rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of researched options with missing or noncomparable capability, throughput, and commercial information
rows.plain: Make sure each researched plant or supplier option has complete information that can be compared fairly, including what it can do, how much it can produce, and its cost details. This matters so the best option can be chosen using reliable information.
rows.imp_rat: Missing or noncomparable capability and commercial information during option research affects procurement clarity (limited relevance to the running job) with modest, correctable consequences; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Suppliers report specs inconsistently and RFI responses arrive in different formats, so gaps and non-comparable entries are common despite structured templates. Normalizing capability and commercial data across options is a lengthy manual reconciliation exercise. It takes an experienced evaluator to spot when throughput or capability figures are stated on non-comparable bases. The effort is mostly analyst time rather than direct material spend.

rows.stk: Process engineers, procurement managers, capital project teams.
rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which supplier availability and plant configuration information is outdated when formal evaluation begins
rows.plain: Keep information about available suppliers and possible factory setups up to date before the formal review starts. This helps people compare current options and make a sound choice.
rows.imp_rat: Outdated supplier availability and configuration data at evaluation is a pre-purchase information error marginal to the operating job (low relevance) and correctable before commitment (low severity). Batch ceiling limits it to 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Plant configuration and availability data captured at shortlisting is often weeks old by formal evaluation, making the evaluation basis untrustworthy. Refreshing availability and configuration data before evaluation adds delay because it triggers a fresh round of supplier queries. Judging which configuration details have gone stale requires technical procurement expertise that is not always present. Evaluating on outdated data risks costly rework or misaligned specifications, though the immediate spend is modest.

rows.stk: Procurement managers, site engineers, quality inspectors.
rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of delivered items with transit damage that are accepted without detection
rows.plain: Make sure damage caused during shipping is noticed before delivered items are accepted, so problems can be reported and addressed.
rows.imp_rat: Undetected transit damage on accepted items carries cost, but the ordering places it below prior batch, capping both proxies at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Standard receiving inspection catches obvious damage but concealed defects routinely pass undetected. Thorough transit-damage inspection slows receiving, so it is often abbreviated under pressure. Detecting concealed transit damage on complex equipment demands trained inspectors and vigilant attention that is often unavailable. Undetected damage accepted at receipt shifts repair and claim costs onto the buyer.

rows.stk: Procurement managers, site engineers, quality inspectors.
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of accepting a consignment with missing bill-of-materials items
rows.plain: Make sure the delivered equipment includes every part listed for it before accepting the shipment. Missing parts could prevent the equipment from being used as intended.
rows.imp_rat: Accepting a consignment with missing BOM items is a real defect, but the batch-5 ranking constraint holds both proxies at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Packing-list checks help, but for complex multi-crate plant deliveries missing items are still accepted more often than desired. Verifying a consignment against the full BOM at receipt takes time, especially for large plant shipments. Detecting missing BOM items requires familiarity with the equipment scope that receiving staff may lack. Accepting incomplete consignments causes downstream shortage costs and re-shipment expense.

rows.stk: Procurement managers, site engineers, quality inspectors.
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of conflicts between the purchase order and bill of materials that prevent a conformance decision
rows.plain: Make sure the order matches the list of parts received so staff can decide whether the delivery is correct. This prevents disagreements about whether anything is missing or wrong.
rows.imp_rat: PO/BOM conflicts blocking a conformance decision matter to acceptance, but the batch-5 ceiling caps this below prior items; relevance and severity both held at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Structured PO-to-BOM matching in procurement systems catches most conflicts, making blocking conformance disputes relatively uncommon. Reconciling PO against BOM at receipt is a routine check completed within normal receiving cycles. Line-by-line conformance checking is a well-defined task needing modest procurement skill. Unresolved conflicts delay payment and can incur demurrage or dispute costs.

rows.stk: Procurement managers, site engineers, quality inspectors.
rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of inspection delay caused by inaccessible identification and inspection points
rows.plain: Spend less time waiting to inspect delivered equipment because its labels or inspection points are difficult to reach. This helps staff check the equipment sooner.
rows.imp_rat: Inspection delays from inaccessible identification points are a logistical inconvenience marginal to processing liquid product (low relevance) and merely lengthen inspection time (low severity). Ceiling holds at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Access adequacy varies by plant design and staging, so inspection delays from this cause are intermittent but recurring. Inspections stall when identification and inspection points are hard to reach, adding hours or a return visit. Working around inaccessible points relies on inspector ingenuity and equipment familiarity. Repeat inspection trips and standby time add tangible cost when access is blocked.

rows.stk: Procurement managers, project managers, site logistics coordinators.
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that reported fabrication progress differs from actual build status
rows.plain: Make sure updates about how much of the equipment has been built match its actual condition. This helps the team plan accurately and spot delays.
rows.imp_rat: Reported fabrication progress diverging from actual build status is a build-phase reporting defect not central to running the plant (low relevance) and typically caught at inspection (low severity). Ranking caps it at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Self-reported fabrication status is notoriously optimistic and rarely independently verified, so reported and actual status diverge often. Discovering that reported progress diverges from actual build status usually surfaces late, only at inspections or missed dates. Detecting overstated progress demands experienced expeditors who can read a shop floor, a scarce and costly capability. Unexpected build shortfalls trigger expensive schedule recovery and idle downstream resources.

rows.stk: Procurement managers, project managers, site logistics coordinators.
rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of missing fabrication milestone updates during the plant build
rows.plain: Make sure updates about important building and manufacturing steps are not missed while the plant is being built. This helps staff know whether the delivery is on schedule.
rows.imp_rat: Missing fabrication milestone updates during the build is a tracking-visibility gap peripheral to the CFJ (low relevance) whose consequences are limited to reduced oversight (low severity). Held at 1.0 by the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Reporting cadence varies widely by supplier and project, so milestone updates are frequently skipped during the build. Milestone updates often arrive late or only when prompted, delaying the buyer's visibility into the build. Keeping updates flowing depends on dedicated expediting staff who must persistently chase the fabricator. Missing updates cause reactive expediting effort and occasional costly surprises rather than direct overspend.

rows.stk: Procurement managers, project managers, site logistics coordinators.
rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of shipment status gaps during transit handoffs
rows.plain: Keep shipment information up to date when one delivery company or location hands the shipment to another. This helps people know where the plant’s delivery is during transit.
rows.imp_rat: Shipment status gaps during transit handoffs is a tracking-visibility issue peripheral to plant processing (low relevance) and resolved once the shipment resurfaces (low severity). Capped at 1.0 by the ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Handoffs between carriers and modes routinely create tracking blind spots because systems do not seamlessly hand off status data. Status visibility can go dark for hours or days precisely at transit handoffs when custody changes carriers. Filling status gaps requires a logistics coordinator who knows which party to call at each handoff. Lost visibility rarely costs money directly unless a delay or loss goes undetected.

rows.stk: Procurement managers, project managers, site logistics coordinators.
rows.stmt: Minimize the time between a change in expected site arrival and its communication to site coordinators
rows.plain: Tell the people coordinating the site as soon as the expected arrival time changes. This helps them adjust their plans quickly.
rows.imp_rat: Delay in communicating changed site arrival to coordinators is a logistics-notification error only loosely tied to the core job (low relevance) with recoverable scheduling impact (low severity). Held at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Notification of arrival changes is ad hoc and easily dropped, so site coordinators are frequently informed late. Changes in expected site arrival often reach site coordinators late because communication relies on manual relay from logistics to the field. Timely relay depends on a coordinator actively monitoring and pushing updates rather than any specialized skill. Late notice can cause wasted crane and crew standby at site, a genuine cost exposure.

rows.stk: Procurement managers, project managers, site logistics coordinators.
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of plant consignments that cannot be matched to the ordered plant
rows.plain: Fewer delivered facility components should be impossible to match with what was ordered. This helps ensure the right items arrive and can be used as planned.
rows.imp_rat: Unmatched consignments impede reconciliation, but per the ordering this sits below batch 4, so both proxies stay at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Barcoding, packing lists and receiving procedures make consignment-to-order matching a largely solved, high-reliability activity. Matching consignments to orders is fast where labeling and packing lists are standard. Goods-receipt matching is a routine warehouse task requiring little specialist judgement. Unmatched consignments tie up capital and incur storage and handling costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time integration work waits for required access permissions and interface details
rows.plain: Spend less time waiting for permission to access plant systems and for the information needed to connect to them. This helps the systems work together sooner.
rows.imp_rat: Integration work waiting for permissions and interface details is a delay with relevance and severity capped at the batch-5 minimum.
rows.sat_rat: Missing or ambiguous interface details cause rework when assumptions prove wrong, and no widespread practice guarantees timely, complete information handover. Integration work routinely idles while waiting for access permissions, credentials, and interface documentation from third parties or plant owners, a delay that standard scheduling rarely eliminates. Chasing permissions and interface details is administrative coordination rather than a skill-limited task, though knowing whom to ask helps. Waiting integrators still accrue labour cost, but the direct financial waste is secondary to the elapsed delay.

rows.stmt: Reduce the rate of communication dropouts between connected control devices during project commissioning
rows.plain: Make communication interruptions between connected control devices less frequent while the project is being started and checked. This helps the devices work together reliably.
rows.imp_rat: Communication dropouts during commissioning cause instability, but both relevance and severity are capped by the batch-5 ceiling below prior items.
rows.sat_rat: Despite mature diagnostic tools and shielded/terminated wiring standards, dropouts remain a stubborn, hard-to-fully-eliminate class of commissioning failures caused by grounding, EMI, and configuration mismatches. Diagnosing intermittent communication dropouts during commissioning consumes significant time as technicians must observe, log, and reproduce failures that appear sporadically. Isolating dropout causes across fieldbus, Ethernet, and controller layers demands scarce network-and-controls specialists who understand both the protocol stack and the physical wiring. Dropouts drive expensive recommissioning cycles and occasional hardware swaps, but the direct material cost is modest compared with schedule impact.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time plant control devices remain unconnected because network assignment and connection requirements are missing or conflicting
rows.plain: Spend less time leaving plant control devices disconnected because their network settings and connection instructions are missing or disagree with each other. Clear requirements help the devices connect correctly.
rows.imp_rat: Control devices left unconnected due to missing network assignment info delays commissioning (relevance) and can idle equipment (severity), but ranked below the prior batch and capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Address planning and network design documents exist as standard practice, yet conflicts and gaps regularly surface at commissioning because plans are not fully validated against the field. Devices sit unconnected while network addresses, VLAN assignments, and connection specs are clarified or resolved, adding avoidable delay to commissioning. Resolving conflicting network assignments needs knowledgeable network engineers, but the core issue is missing or contradictory documentation rather than raw expertise. The idle-device delay carries labour and schedule cost more than material waste.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of sterility-critical parameters set outside validated limits for the planned production run
