rows.plain: Keep the settings that protect product sterility within the safe ranges already shown to work for the planned production run. This helps prevent the product from becoming contaminated.
rows.imp_rat: Sterility-critical parameters set outside validated limits is a contamination risk, yet the mandatory ceiling caps both relevance and severity at the batch-5 low.
rows.sat_rat: Validated-limit checks exist but the sheer number of sterility-critical parameters and manual setup mean some still get set outside limits. Verifying every sterility-critical parameter against validated limits adds meaningful pre-run time. Confirming sterility-critical settings demands scarce sterility-assurance and validation expertise. A parameter outside validated limits risks scrapping sterile product, dwarfing the low verification cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time the pipefitting technician waits for specified compatible fittings, valves, or flexible connections
rows.plain: Provide the correct matching connectors, shutoff parts, and flexible tubing quickly so the pipes can be connected.
rows.imp_rat: A pipefitter waiting for compatible fittings delays installation work (relevance) and adds labor cost (severity), but this is a localized delay capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Piping isometrics and material takeoffs are mature practice that usually get the right parts on site, so mismatches are less chronic than for control devices though still recurring. Pipefitting technicians wait for specified compatible fittings, valves, and flexible connections when supply or specification mismatches occur, stalling piping progress. The waiting is a supply-chain matter, not a limit on the fitter's skill, though a skilled fitter may improvise temporary workarounds. Idle skilled labour and expedited parts add cost, but the primary loss is the delay to the fitting task.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of routed plant power cables with insulation or conductor damage
rows.plain: Make sure the power cables that are installed have no damage to their protective covering or inner wires. This matters so electricity can be connected safely and reliably.
rows.imp_rat: Damaged power cable insulation is a safety concern, but under the batch-5 ceiling both relevance and severity for this scope's core job are capped at the minimum.
rows.sat_rat: Insulation-resistance testing before energization is a mature, standard step, so damaged conductors slip through only occasionally. Insulation testing of routed cables is quick and routinely scheduled. Megger and continuity testing is standard electrician competency, widely available. Damaged cable found late is costly to replace, but pre-energization testing is cheap.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time installation is stalled by unavailable or incompatible instruments, fittings, or cables
rows.plain: Spend less time waiting for the right tools, parts, and cables to be available and fit properly. This helps installation work continue without avoidable stops.
rows.imp_rat: Installation stalled by unavailable or incompatible parts is a delay, yet its relevance and severity are held to the batch-5 cap.
rows.sat_rat: BOM checks and vendor lead-time tracking catch many mismatches, yet late discoveries during install remain common because specs and deliveries are imperfectly reconciled. Installation is frequently stalled for days or weeks when a specified instrument, fitting, or cable turns out to be unavailable or incompatible, and expediting or re-sourcing is slow. Anticipating incompatibilities requires experienced procurement and engineering judgement, but the stall itself is more a logistics than a skill problem. Rush orders, substitute parts, and idle crews add real cost, though the dominant loss felt first is lost schedule time.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of contamination being introduced into sterile product-contact paths during integrity testing
rows.plain: Keep germs or other unwanted material from getting into the parts that touch the product while checking that the system is properly sealed. This helps protect the product from becoming unsafe.
rows.imp_rat: Contamination of sterile paths during integrity testing could in principle be serious, but the mandatory batch-4 ceiling forces relevance and severity to 1 since it ranks below all prior items.
rows.sat_rat: Closed single-use assemblies, sterile connectors and validated test protocols substantially reduce ingress risk, yet occasional breach events at manual connection points remain the recurring gap. Sterile integrity testing already follows defined SOPs that pace the work predictably, though aseptic technique adds setup and hold time that cannot be rushed. Preventing contamination during integrity testing depends heavily on trained aseptic operators and correct gowning/connection discipline, so outcomes still hinge on scarce human vigilance. Single-use sterile connectors, filters and clean consumables add recurring cost but are routinely budgeted as part of validated practice.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of component rupture or uncontrolled pressure release during integrity testing
rows.plain: Make sure the equipment does not break or release pressure suddenly while it is being checked for leaks and strength. An uncontrolled release could injure people or damage the system.
rows.imp_rat: Component rupture during integrity testing is a safety event, yet the batch-4 ceiling forces both proxies to 1 as it ranks below all previously scored items.
rows.sat_rat: Pressure relief devices, rated components and automated ramp control make uncontrolled rupture during testing an uncommon event where standard practice is followed. Pressure-hold and diffusion tests are quick, standardized procedures that rarely extend the preparation timeline. Automated integrity testers with preset pressure limits reduce reliance on operator judgement, though rig-up still needs a competent technician. Relief valves, rated tubing and calibrated testers are modest, well-established investments relative to the rupture hazard they prevent.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the installation foundation cannot safely support the batching system loads
rows.plain: Make sure the floor or base under the mixing system is strong and safe enough to carry its weight and operating loads. This helps prevent movement, damage, or collapse.
rows.imp_rat: An unsafe foundation for system loads is a stability concern, but the mandatory ceiling holds both proxies at 1 for this batch.
rows.sat_rat: Engineered foundation design with documented load data is a well-established practice, making an inadequate foundation an uncommon failure when the design basis is correct. Foundation adequacy is established during design and site prep well before installation, so it rarely delays the preparation stage itself. Structural and civil engineers size foundations against known loads using mature methods, reducing dependence on field judgement. Foundations and load verification are a planned capital cost, and over-design margins are standard rather than a surprise expense.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of tanks, pumps, and metering hardware positioned outside specified locations or orientations
rows.plain: Place tanks, pumps, and measuring equipment in the required positions and directions. This helps the equipment work correctly and safely.
rows.imp_rat: Tanks, pumps, and metering hardware placed outside specified locations is an install-layout error tangential to running a batch (relevance) with low direct harm (severity); capped at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Dimensioned drawings and installation checks make correct positioning the norm, with only occasional orientation errors remaining. Repositioning mislocated tanks, pumps, or metering hardware adds rework time but is usually caught early. Following layout drawings to place equipment correctly is routine work for competent installers. Relocating heavy hardware after the fact carries rigging and labor cost when errors go uncaught.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of component mounting interfaces being misaligned with prepared foundation anchors
rows.plain: Make sure the equipment’s attachment points line up with the anchors already set in the foundation. Misalignment could make installation difficult or leave the equipment unstable.
rows.imp_rat: Misaligned mounting interfaces cause rework, yet the ranking places this item lower so both proxies remain at the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Anchor-bolt templates and as-built surveys reduce misalignment, but concrete pour tolerances and template errors still routinely produce mounting-interface mismatches. Anchor misalignment is typically caught at set-down and forces time-consuming re-drilling or shimming on the critical path. Accurate anchor placement and correction relies on skilled millwrights and surveyors working from templates. Rework of misplaced anchors means chipping concrete, epoxy anchors and lost labor, a moderate but common expense.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of personnel injury or batching-system damage during initial energization
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that people get hurt or the mixing equipment is damaged when power and other services are turned on for the first time. This helps make the startup safe.
rows.imp_rat: Personnel injury during energization is inherently high-stakes, but the batch-4 ceiling explicitly caps both proxies at 1 for all items here.
rows.sat_rat: LOTO, pre-energization inspections and staged power-up are mature practices that make injury or equipment damage at first power infrequent, though wiring or setpoint errors still occasionally slip through. Energization is gated by pre-power checklists and lockout/tagout steps that add controlled time but keep the sequence orderly. Safe first energization leans on qualified electricians and commissioning engineers whose judgement remains essential to catch wiring errors. The cost is mainly labor for pre-checks plus occasional damaged components, which is contained by disciplined commissioning.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of deviation of connected utility supplies from specified voltage, pressure, flow, and quality requirements
rows.plain: Make sure the incoming electricity and other services meet the required levels for power, pressure, amount, and cleanliness. This helps the machine run safely and reliably.
rows.imp_rat: Utility supply deviations affect operation, yet the batch ceiling requires both proxies to be held at 1 for this lower-ranked set.
rows.sat_rat: Utility acceptance checks and site-readiness protocols catch most deviations before startup, though marginal or fluctuating plant supplies remain a recurring source of trouble. Utility verification is a defined pre-start step, but chasing out-of-spec supplies can cause real delays when plant utilities fall short. Interpreting voltage, pressure, flow and quality readings against spec requires competent commissioning staff but is well-documented routine work. Metering, regulators and filtration to correct supplies add cost, and undersized utilities can force expensive remediation.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of hook-up delay caused by unavailable required utility sources or inaccessible connection points
rows.plain: Spend less time waiting to connect power and other needed services when the sources or connection points cannot be reached. This helps the equipment be set up sooner.
rows.imp_rat: Hook-up delays from unavailable utilities or inaccessible connections are setup logistics peripheral to batching (relevance) with low harm (severity); capped at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Utility readiness depends on upstream coordination that is inconsistently managed, so gaps surface repeatedly at hook-up. Hook-up frequently stalls when required utilities or accessible connection points are not ready, a recurring source of startup delay. Identifying and routing utilities takes moderate craft skill but is within normal maintenance capability. Waiting crews and idle equipment during utility unavailability impose labor and schedule costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of operator qualification delays caused by unavailable prerequisites, trainers, or batching system access
rows.plain: Avoid delays in preparing workers to run the system when required materials, instructors, or system access are missing. This lets operators become ready to work sooner.
rows.imp_rat: Operator qualification delays from missing prerequisites are a scheduling issue tangential to executing a batch (relevance) with low direct harm (severity); capped at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Scheduling and resource-tracking practices are ad hoc, so prerequisite and access conflicts recur unpredictably despite planning. Qualification delays from unavailable trainers, prerequisites, or blocked system access are a chronic scheduling pain that routinely stretches operator readiness timelines. Availability of qualified trainers is itself a scarce-skill bottleneck, and no standard practice reliably decouples qualification from that dependency. Idle operators and stalled equipment during these delays impose real labor and opportunity costs on the preparation stage.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of structural supports or modules shifting or collapsing during mechanical assembly
rows.plain: Avoid allowing supports or parts of the system to move or fall during assembly. This helps prevent injuries and damage to the system.
rows.imp_rat: Structural supports shifting during assembly is a safety concern, but the batch ranking caps both proxies at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Lift plans, temporary supports and sequenced assembly are standard rigging practice that make shifting or collapse during assembly uncommon, though setup errors occasionally cause instability. Temporary bracing and staged lifting add setup time but keep assembly moving safely without major stoppages. Safe assembly relies on competent riggers and lift planning, keeping the task dependent on experienced crews. Cribbing, bracing and rigging gear are modest recurring costs against the severe expense of a collapse.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of particulate or microbial contamination introduced into product-contact areas during assembly
rows.plain: Keep dust and germs from getting onto surfaces that will touch the product while the parts are being put together. This helps protect the product from contamination.
rows.imp_rat: Particulate or microbial contamination in product-contact areas during assembly is genuinely consequential, but the mandatory batch-4 ceiling limits both proxies to 1.0 regardless.
rows.sat_rat: Controlled-environment assembly and flushing reduce contamination, but introduction during hands-on assembly remains a persistent, hard-to-eliminate risk. Particulate or microbial contamination found later triggers re-cleaning and re-testing that lengthens the stage. Preventing ingress during assembly requires trained cleanroom discipline and constant vigilance that is hard to sustain. Contamination remediation wastes materials and can scrap affected components.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of sterile-contact surfaces or seals damaged during handling and assembly
rows.plain: Avoid damaging surfaces or seals that must stay free of germs while putting the system parts together. This helps prevent contamination.
rows.imp_rat: Damaged sterile-contact surfaces or seals during handling is a quality-affecting defect, but batch-4 positioning caps both proxies at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Handling procedures and protective packaging help, but seal and surface damage during assembly still recurs with manual handling. Replacing seals or surfaces damaged in handling adds procurement and rework time. Avoiding damage to sterile-contact surfaces and seals depends heavily on careful, skilled handling technique. Damaged sterile components are often scrapped and replaced, an outright material loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of supplied modules with mechanical interfaces that do not mate to adjoining modules as specified
rows.plain: Avoid receiving system parts whose connecting pieces do not fit together as planned. This helps the system go together and work properly.
rows.imp_rat: Non-mating mechanical module interfaces cause assembly problems, but the ranking places this below the prior batch, capping relevance and severity.
rows.sat_rat: Dimensional drawings and factory acceptance testing reduce mismatch risk, but tolerance stack-up and vendor coordination gaps still produce interfaces that do not mate as specified. Interface mismatches discovered at assembly cause significant on-site delay while parts are reworked or refabricated. Skilled fitters can adapt many minor mismatches in the field, so the burden falls on craft labor when interfaces do not mate cleanly. Non-mating modules drive costly field rework, custom spools and shipping of replacements.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of foreign material entering product-contact piping during installation
rows.plain: Keep dust, debris, and other unwanted material out of the product-carrying pipes while they are being installed. This helps protect the product from contamination.
rows.imp_rat: Foreign material entering product-contact piping during installation is a real contamination risk, but the mandatory batch-4 ceiling limits both proxies to 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: End caps, flushing, and inspection reduce ingress, but foreign material still enters product-contact piping during installation with some regularity. Foreign material discovered in piping forces disassembly, flushing, and re-inspection that delay startup. Preventing debris ingress relies on disciplined capping and housekeeping practice that demands sustained attention. Foreign-material removal and re-flushing waste cleaning resources and time.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of ambiguity in the documented access permission structures
rows.plain: Clearly record who is allowed to access the system and what each person is allowed to do. This helps prevent unauthorized changes or use.
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity in documented access permission structures is an administrative governance concern with minimal centrality to batching liquid (relevance minimal) and low direct consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Access permission documents are commonly informal or inconsistent at preparation time, so ambiguity is a routine source of misconfiguration rather than an occasional one. Ambiguous access documentation slows every downstream provisioning decision as people stop to interpret intent. Interpreting a vague permission structure demands experienced staff who can infer what was meant, offloading effort onto scarce judgement. Ambiguity causes rework and misconfiguration whose costs accumulate quietly over the project.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of batching system unavailability caused by between-batch rinsing
rows.plain: Keep the equipment that prepares products in batches from being unavailable for long while it is rinsed between batches. This helps the next batch start sooner.
rows.imp_rat: Between-batch rinsing downtime is a throughput-relevant delay with mild consequences, and the batch-4 ranking caps its importance at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: CIP cycle times are predictable and consistent, but the total downtime remains substantial and hard to compress without risking cleanliness. Between-batch rinsing is a significant, recurring source of system unavailability that directly eats into productive time. Rinsing is largely automated so it demands little skill, but sequencing it around production requires planning. The unavailability represents lost production capacity plus rinse consumables.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of rinse-fluid leaks or spills during between-batch rinsing
rows.plain: Avoid spilling or leaking rinse liquid while cleaning between production runs. This helps keep people, equipment, and the work area safe and clean.
rows.imp_rat: Rinse-fluid leaks or spills during rinsing is a waste/housekeeping error of low centrality and minor consequence; ceiling of 1.0 applies.
rows.sat_rat: Closed CIP loops, gasketed connections and drip trays make spills fairly uncommon, with leaks mainly from aging seals or misaligned hoses. Cleaning up an occasional rinse spill adds minor unplanned time. Preventing leaks is mostly about equipment integrity, not operator skill. Leaks waste rinse fluid and chemicals and create effluent handling cost, though volumes are usually small.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time the batching system waits for a required replacement component after diagnosis
rows.plain: Get the needed replacement part quickly after finding the problem, so the batching system can be repaired sooner.
rows.imp_rat: Waiting for a replacement component after diagnosis affects repair turnaround (relevance 1) but is capped by the ranking (severity 1).
rows.sat_rat: Standard practice of stocking common parts covers routine cases, but non-stocked or long-lead items reliably cause waits. Post-diagnosis waiting for a component is a direct, often multi-day delay when the part is not local, dominating repair duration. The wait reflects supply logistics, not technician skill, once the diagnosis is made. Expedited shipping to shorten the wait carries premium cost that plants sometimes pay reluctantly.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of batching system downtime caused by an unavailable replacement element
rows.plain: Keep the equipment that prepares products in batches from being out of service for long when a replacement filter is not available. This helps work resume sooner when the filter needs changing.
rows.imp_rat: Downtime from an unavailable replacement element does touch availability (relevance 1), yet the ranking caps this batch, so both proxies stay at 1 (severity 1).
rows.sat_rat: Spare-parts management via CMMS min/max levels is mature, yet critical-part stockouts still cause avoidable extended downtime. When a spare is not on the shelf, downtime extends directly by the procurement and delivery interval, a frequent and painful delay. Avoiding stockouts is largely an inventory-planning task rather than a technical-skill limitation. Stocking every possible spare is costly, so plants under-stock and eat downtime, a genuine cost tradeoff.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of process control and batch records omitted from backups
rows.plain: Fewer production instructions and batch records should be left out of backup copies. This helps prevent important production information from being lost.
rows.imp_rat: Batch records omitted from backups is a records-completeness defect with low centrality to active processing and modest severity; held at the floor under the cap.
rows.sat_rat: Scheduled backups capture most records reliably, but newly added tags, disconnected islanded systems and manual paper batch records frequently fall outside automated scope, leaving recurring omission gaps. Automated backup jobs run in the background without consuming operator time. Ensuring all tags and record streams are enrolled in backup scope demands configuration diligence that can lapse when new equipment or points are added. Comprehensive backup coverage costs little incrementally once the historian and storage exist.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of backed-up process control and batch records that are corrupted or unreadable
rows.plain: Fewer saved production instructions and records should be damaged or impossible to open. This helps the production process be reviewed or restored when needed.
rows.imp_rat: Corrupted or unreadable backed-up records is a data-integrity defect of low centrality to active processing and modest severity; held at the floor under the mandatory ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Checksums and RAID/replication catch most corruption, but silent bit-rot, untested backups and media degradation mean unreadable records are discovered only at restore time more often than plants would like. Verification and checksum routines run automatically and do not slow production. Detecting and remediating corruption depends on IT staff configuring integrity checks and periodic restore tests, a skill routinely available in validated environments. Verification, redundant copies and periodic restore drills add modest ongoing cost that regulated plants budget for.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of backed-up records with missing or mismatched batch identifiers and timestamps
rows.plain: Make sure saved copies of records have the correct production-batch numbers and times. This helps people match each record to the right production activity.
rows.imp_rat: Backed-up records with mismatched batch identifiers are a data-hygiene issue, so both relevance and severity stay low under the ranking ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Integrated MES/ERP linkage keeps identifiers consistent in most runs, but backlogs and manual reconciliation still produce mismatches. Reconciling backed-up records with missing or mismatched IDs is a recurring after-the-fact time cost. Resolving mismatched batch identifiers and timestamps requires knowledgeable review of what actually happened. Record backlog reconciliation consumes labor and can delay batch release, adding cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time the technician waits for equipment to be released for scheduled service
rows.plain: The technician should be able to start planned equipment service without waiting for the equipment to be made available. This helps routine work stay on schedule.
rows.imp_rat: Waiting for equipment release for service causes delay, but its relevance and severity to the core processing job are pinned to the batch-5 minimum.
rows.sat_rat: Permit-to-work and handover procedures are standard, but coordination delays between production and maintenance still leave technicians waiting regularly. Technicians frequently wait for equipment isolation, decontamination and permit release before starting, a direct idle delay. Executing release steps requires operators and safety staff to perform lockout and decon correctly. Waiting technician time is paid but unproductive, adding labor cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that incomplete upgrade records block release of the plant module for sterile production
rows.plain: Make sure all records of the upgrades are complete, so the production unit can be approved for making germ-free products.
rows.imp_rat: Incomplete upgrade records blocking sterile-production release is a documentation gate; the ranking ceiling holds relevance and severity at the floor.
rows.sat_rat: Paper and hybrid change-control systems in most GMP plants still leave gaps that surface only at release, so incomplete-record holds remain a recurring deviation. Chasing missing upgrade sign-offs before a batch release routinely adds hours or days of hold time while documentation gaps are reconstructed. Verifying complete upgrade records depends on experienced QA and validation staff who know what a defensible release package must contain. The paperwork itself is cheap, but a blocked module ties up costly sterile capacity while records are chased.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of plant unavailability while sanitization validation results remain pending
rows.plain: Avoid keeping the plant unavailable while waiting for cleaning checks to be confirmed. This helps the facility return to work sooner after cleaning.
rows.imp_rat: Plant unavailability while sanitization validation is pending is a scheduling delay, and the ceiling keeps relevance and severity at the floor.
rows.sat_rat: The wait itself is predictable, but rapid-micro methods are not yet widely deployed so the delay is rarely compressed. Microbiological sanitization validation results depend on incubation and lab turnaround, so the plant sits idle for a largely fixed and often multi-day period. Interpreting and releasing on validation results needs qualified micro/QA staff, a normal but constraining dependency. Idle sterile lines during the pending window carry high opportunity cost that current scheduling does little to recover.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of inspection delays caused by inaccessible vessel, piping, or component surfaces
rows.plain: Spend less time waiting to check tanks, pipes, and parts for wear or damage because their surfaces are difficult to reach. This helps staff find problems sooner.
rows.imp_rat: Inspection delays from inaccessible surfaces slow maintenance, yet core-job relevance and severity are held to the mandated batch-5 minimum.
rows.sat_rat: Even with access, hidden surfaces are inspected incompletely, so the true condition is often uncertain until something is opened up. Gaining access to internal vessel, buried piping, or lagged component surfaces still requires scaffolding, insulation removal, vessel entry permits and confined-space preparation that routinely stretch shutdown inspection windows by days. Positioning access and rigging demands experienced scaffolders and permit-issuing authorities, but the bottleneck is the sequential physical preparation rather than scarce judgement. Scaffold hire, insulation stripping and reinstatement, and extended crew standby impose large recurring costs every outage.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of inspection findings assigned to the wrong vessel, pipework section, or component
rows.plain: Make sure each inspection problem is linked to the correct vessel, pipe section, or part. This helps the right problem get fixed in the right place.
rows.imp_rat: Inspection findings assigned to the wrong vessel or pipework misdirects remediation (relevance) with safety risk (severity), but ranked below prior batch and capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Manual location referencing against isometrics and tag lists leaves recurring misassignments, though inspection data management systems reduce them where used. Misassigned findings require rework to trace and reassign, adding delay to defect resolution. Correctly attributing a finding to the right pipework spool or vessel section relies on the inspector's spatial knowledge of the plant. A finding on the wrong component can leave the real defect unaddressed until failure, an expensive outcome.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of completed maintenance activities omitted from the plant history
rows.plain: Make sure every finished maintenance job is added to the plant’s history. This helps people know what work was done and plan future upkeep.
rows.imp_rat: Completed maintenance omitted from plant history leaves gaps in records (relevance) with compliance risk (severity), but it is administrative and capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Work-order close-out in CMMS captures most planned jobs, but ad-hoc and break-in maintenance frequently go unrecorded, leaving history incomplete. Omitted activities surface only later, forcing time-consuming reconstruction of what was done. Ensuring every completed job is logged depends on workforce discipline rather than any specialized skill. Missing history distorts reliability analysis and warranty claims, causing avoidable spend.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of maintenance entries containing inaccurate service or repair details
rows.plain: Keep the maintenance history accurate about the service and repairs that were done. This helps future workers know what work the machine actually received.
rows.imp_rat: Inaccurate service details in maintenance entries degrade record reliability (relevance) and can mislead future repairs (severity), yet remain administrative and capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Free-text, end-of-shift data entry means transcription and recall errors persist, degrading the accuracy of the maintenance record. Correcting inaccurate service details later consumes engineering time during audits and failure investigations. Accurate recording depends on the technician correctly interpreting and transcribing what was actually done, an error-prone manual finesse task. Wrong repair details cause repeat failures and mis-ordered spares, wasting materials and labour.

rows.stmt: Minimize the time between completion of a maintenance activity and its entry in the plant history
rows.plain: Enter completed upkeep work in the plant's history as soon as possible. This helps keep the plant's records current.
rows.imp_rat: Lag between completing maintenance and logging it introduces temporary record gaps (relevance) with low direct harm (severity), and is capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: The longer the entry lag, the more detail is lost or misremembered, undermining record accuracy. Data entry is typically batched at shift-end or later, so a lag of hours to days between completion and record entry is normal. The delay is a workflow and habit issue, not a matter of scarce expertise. Delayed entries occasionally cause duplicate dispatch or planning on stale data, a modest recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of maintenance records linked to the wrong plant component
rows.plain: Link each maintenance record to the correct part of the plant. This prevents work on one part from being mistaken for work on another.
rows.imp_rat: Maintenance records linked to the wrong component corrupts traceability (relevance) and can misguide interventions (severity), but is administrative and capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Barcode/QR asset tagging and CMMS dropdowns reduce mislinking where implemented, but ambiguous tag naming and manual selection still cause wrong-component entries. Finding the correct tag when a record is misfiled wastes searching time during later work planning. Selecting the right component from thousands of similar tags relies on the technician's familiarity with the asset hierarchy. Misallocated records skew maintenance cost tracking and can trigger work on the wrong asset.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of maintenance entries lacking required inspection or repair evidence
rows.plain: Make sure every maintenance entry includes the required proof of inspections or repairs. This shows that the work was properly checked and completed.
rows.imp_rat: Missing inspection or repair evidence in maintenance entries weakens compliance traceability (relevance) and can cause audit findings (severity), but it is administrative relative to the plant's core job and capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: CMMS mandatory attachments help, but evidence capture still depends on human follow-through so entries lacking required proof remain common. Attaching inspection or repair evidence to a work order is quick when a CMMS supports it, but manual photo and report uploads are often deferred and forgotten. Knowing what evidence a given task requires depends on the individual technician's diligence and procedural knowledge rather than an enforced check. Missing evidence forces re-inspection or re-opening of work, and in regulated plants can invalidate an entire maintenance campaign.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that qualified maintenance personnel are unavailable for assigned activities
rows.plain: Make it unlikely that people with the needed maintenance skills are unavailable for the work they were assigned. This helps ensure the planned maintenance can be completed.
rows.imp_rat: Unavailability of qualified maintenance personnel delays activities, yet its relevance to the core processing job and severity are held at the mandated batch-5 low.
rows.sat_rat: Skills matrices and scheduling help match qualified staff to tasks, but absences and niche-certification gaps still leave activities uncovered. If a qualified person is unavailable the task waits, adding delay to the outage. The whole need is about scarce qualified/certified personnel, and specialist availability is a persistent constraint in sterile plants. Cross-training and contractor coverage cost money and premium rates for scarce skills.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of flushing medium consumed before release criteria are met
rows.plain: Use as little cleaning liquid or gas as possible before the pipes are clean enough to release. This saves materials while still meeting the required cleanliness level.
rows.imp_rat: Excess flushing medium consumed is a resource-waste error, tangential to processing output (low relevance) with modest cost impact (low severity). Ceiling-bound.
rows.sat_rat: Validated endpoints (conductivity, TOC) make release consistent, but they are typically tuned for safety margin rather than minimal consumption. Running flush until release criteria are met adds cycle time that current recipes only partly optimize. Setting efficient flush endpoints needs experienced process engineers. Flushing medium (WFI, solvents) is a real consumable expense that conservative recipes tend to over-consume.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of calibration records missing required as-found, as-left, reference traceability, or authorization evidence
rows.plain: Make sure each measurement-check record includes the instrument’s condition before and after the check, proof that the reference was reliable, and approval from the responsible person. This provides evidence that the check was done properly.
rows.imp_rat: Calibration records missing traceability evidence is a documentation defect of low core-job relevance with severity held at the floor.
rows.sat_rat: Where computerized calibration management with mandatory fields is deployed, missing traceability or authorization evidence is largely designed out, though paper-based sites still see recurring gaps. Calibration management systems auto-prompt required fields, cutting the time chasing missing as-found/as-left data, though manual back-entry still lags. Modern calibration software enforces traceability entry so technicians need less procedural memory, though understanding reference standards still requires training. Non-compliant records trigger costly recalibration or audit findings, but software validation limits the frequency of that rework.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of production interruption caused by instrument isolation, calibration, and return to service
rows.plain: Keep production from stopping longer than necessary while measuring devices are taken out, checked, and put back into use. This helps the process restart sooner and keeps measurements reliable.
rows.imp_rat: Production interruption from instrument isolation and calibration touches operational uptime but is a narrow maintenance activity relative to the CFJ (low relevance), with recoverable short-term downtime consequences; ceiling holds it at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Mature isolation procedures and redundant/bypass arrangements make outcomes consistent, but the interruption duration itself remains largely unavoidable. Instrument isolation, calibration and return-to-service still require staged permit, bypass and proof-testing steps that interrupt production for measurable periods. Safe isolation and return of instruments demands qualified technicians and controlled procedures, but the constraint is procedural duration rather than skill scarcity. Production downtime during calibration carries high opportunity cost, only partly mitigated by scheduling during planned outages.

rows.stk: Maintenance engineers, plant operators, reliability teams.
rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of disagreement among maintenance engineers, plant operators, and reliability teams about the condition indicated for the same equipment
rows.plain: Make sure maintenance workers, machine operators, and equipment-health teams agree on what condition the same equipment is in. This supports a shared and accurate view of problems.
rows.imp_rat: Disagreement about equipment condition among teams is coordination ambiguity of low core-job relevance, severity capped per the ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Without a shared calibrated condition standard, the same equipment is genuinely labelled differently by different teams, so the recorded condition is inconsistent and untrustworthy. Reconciling conflicting condition opinions across engineers, operators and reliability teams drags out review meetings and delays repair decisions. Consistent condition assessment depends heavily on experienced judgement interpreting vibration, thickness and process data that different specialists weigh differently. Disagreement drives either premature intervention or deferred repair, both of which waste money through unnecessary work or unplanned failure.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of ambiguity in preservation requirements or completion records for idle equipment
rows.plain: Clearly state how unused equipment must be protected and what records must be completed while it is not being used. This helps keep the equipment in known condition and shows what was done to preserve it.
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity in preservation requirements for idle equipment is a documentation-clarity issue far from the core processing job (low relevance) with easily absorbed consequences (low severity). Ceiling applies.
rows.sat_rat: Preservation records are often informal or inconsistent, so ambiguity about idle-equipment status remains a common gap. Ambiguous preservation records cost time to interpret or re-verify before equipment can be trusted for use. Resolving ambiguous idle-equipment status leans on experienced staff who remember what was done. Ambiguity is cheap to create but can trigger costly re-preservation or scrap decisions.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that decommissioning activities begin before review findings and protective requirements are finalized
rows.plain: Make sure equipment is not taken out of service before the safety review is complete and all needed protections are decided.
rows.imp_rat: Decommissioning beginning before findings are finalized is an end-of-life sequencing risk, marginal to batching operations; held at 1.0 under the mandatory ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Hold-point and permit-to-work practices exist to gate decommissioning, but schedule pressure and informal starts still cause premature activity in many cases. Waiting for review findings before starting can delay work, creating pressure to begin prematurely. Judging when protective requirements are truly finalized requires regulatory and safety expertise that is not always on hand. Starting before finalization risks abortive work and remediation costs that dwarf the delay avoided.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of system records that are missing or inconsistent with the end-of-life configuration
rows.plain: Avoid missing records or records that do not match the system’s final setup. This helps people identify remaining safety concerns accurately.
rows.imp_rat: Records inconsistent with end-of-life configuration is a decommissioning documentation error, minimally relevant to the batching job; held at 1.0 by the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Configuration records commonly drift from reality over a system's life and are seldom fully reconciled at end-of-life, so inconsistencies are frequent. Reconciling records to the actual end-of-life configuration is time-consuming because as-built drift accumulates over years. Detecting divergence between documented and actual configuration requires experienced eyes familiar with the specific system's modification history. Field verification to correct configuration records adds labor cost that is rarely budgeted at disposal.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of identification markings that remain legible after removal or obliteration
rows.plain: Make sure old identification marks are truly unreadable after they are removed, so the equipment cannot be mistaken for something else.
rows.imp_rat: Legibility of markings after obliteration is a decommissioning-marking detail with low relevance to preparing liquids and slight consequences; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Without a defined legibility standard or check, partial or still-readable markings routinely remain, so outcome consistency is only moderate. Obliterating markings by grinding or painting is a quick step within teardown. Obliteration requires little expertise, just adherence to a defacement standard. Consumables and labor for obliteration are inexpensive.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required regulatory or safety markings removed unintentionally
rows.plain: Prevent legally required or safety labels from being removed by mistake. These markings help people identify the equipment and handle it safely.
rows.imp_rat: Unintended removal of regulatory markings occurs during decommissioning, distant from the batching CFJ, with limited consequence; capped at 1.0 by ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Ad hoc marking practices and unclear retain-lists make unintended removal a recurring and poorly controlled error. Distinguishing markings to keep versus remove slows crews who must pause and verify each one. Correctly identifying which regulatory or safety markings must be preserved demands trained judgement that decommissioning crews often lack. Accidental removal of required markings triggers re-labeling and compliance rework costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of damaging system surfaces or components while stripping markings
rows.plain: Remove labels or markings without scratching or otherwise harming the equipment. This helps keep the equipment in good working condition.
rows.imp_rat: Damaging surfaces while stripping markings is a decommissioning-handling error peripheral to preparing liquids, with recoverable consequences; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Outcomes vary with operator care since there is little standardized non-damaging removal procedure. Careful stripping to avoid surface damage slows the work versus aggressive removal. Avoiding damage to sensitive product-contact or cleanroom surfaces while stripping markings requires skilled, attentive technicians. Surface damage during disposal matters less for scrap but can devalue salvageable or refurbishable components.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of removed tags and plates remaining recoverable from the waste stream
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that removed labels and nameplates can still be recovered from the waste. This helps prevent the equipment from being identified after disposal.
rows.imp_rat: Recoverable tags and plates in the waste stream is an end-of-life disposal concern far from the batching CFJ, with minor consequences; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Practice varies widely and recoverable tags frequently slip through because destruction is rarely verified downstream. Destroying or defacing tags adds only minor time to the disposal workflow. Physical destruction of plates is low-skill work needing basic tools and simple instructions. Grinding, shredding, or scrapping tags is cheap, though verifying destruction in the waste stream adds modest expense.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of adhesive residue or label debris left on cleanroom-facing or product-contact surfaces
rows.plain: Leave as little glue, ink, or label material as possible on surfaces that face the clean work area or touch the product. This helps keep those surfaces clean and safe.
rows.imp_rat: Adhesive residue on product-contact surfaces touches contamination but sits below the batch above in ranking, so relevance and severity are held at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Established cleaning practices handle most residue, but stubborn adhesive on critical surfaces still leaves recurring gaps. Residue cleanup is a defined but time-consuming step in surface reconditioning. Solvent selection and cleanroom-grade cleaning to remove adhesive need moderately trained cleaning staff. Cleaning agents and labor add modest cost, greater if product-contact validation is involved.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of agreements supporting active batching systems terminated by mistake during decommissioning
rows.plain: Avoid accidentally ending agreements that are still needed by systems that are actively combining or preparing batches during equipment removal. This keeps those systems working while they are still needed.
rows.imp_rat: Accidentally terminating agreements for still-active systems during decommissioning could disrupt operations, but it is a lifecycle-admin error below prior items (low relevance); ceiling caps both proxies at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Poor asset-to-contract linkage occasionally causes wrong terminations, but consequences are usually caught and corrected, limiting frequency. Verifying which agreements belong to which system before terminating adds modest time. Distinguishing agreements supporting active versus retired systems requires accurate asset-to-contract mapping expertise. Erroneously terminating a live-system agreement can trigger reinstatement fees and service gaps.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of linked service, calibration, maintenance, and consumable supply arrangements left active after system retirement
rows.plain: Make sure all connected service, testing, repair, and supply arrangements are ended when the system is retired. This prevents payments and deliveries from continuing after they are no longer needed.
rows.imp_rat: Leaving service and consumable arrangements active after retirement is a wind-down housekeeping error, not job-critical to batching (low relevance); ceiling holds both proxies at 1.
rows.sat_rat: There is rarely a single register linking arrangements to assets, so dangling active contracts are a frequent oversight. Identifying and cancelling every linked arrangement takes time but is not the primary loss. Tracking down all service, calibration, and consumable contracts tied to a system needs procurement knowledge that is often incomplete. Arrangements left active after retirement bleed recurring fees for services no longer needed, a direct and common waste.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of required decommissioning support unavailability after premature agreement termination
rows.plain: Keep the help needed to shut down the equipment available for longer if connected services end earlier than planned. This helps prevent delays during the shutdown.
rows.imp_rat: Decommissioning-support unavailability after premature termination is an end-of-life timing error not central to batching (low relevance); batch ranking caps both proxies at 1.
rows.sat_rat: When premature termination occurs the resulting support gap is somewhat predictable and usually recoverable, so the exposure is bounded. Restoring support after a mistaken termination introduces lead-time gaps until service is re-established. Re-establishing terminated support requires vendor-relationship handling that most teams can manage. Support unavailability may force costly interim workarounds, but this is secondary to the downtime itself.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of avoidable termination fees incurred when contractual notice deadlines are missed
rows.plain: Avoid unnecessary fees for ending services when required notice dates are missed. This reduces the cost of stopping services that are no longer needed.
rows.imp_rat: Avoidable termination fees from missed notice deadlines are a financial-admin error peripheral to batching function (low relevance); batch-5 cap holds both proxies at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Contract-management systems flag many notice dates, but obscure or one-off agreements still slip through, so fees recur occasionally. Missing a notice deadline does not itself slow work; it triggers a fee. Tracking contractual notice windows is a clerical discipline, not a scarce skill, though it still needs diligence. Avoidable termination fees from missed notice deadlines are a direct financial loss that calendaring practices partly but imperfectly prevent.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of billing and termination disputes caused by missing or conflicting supplier confirmations
rows.plain: Resolve charges and cancellation disagreements quickly when suppliers have not confirmed the change or have given conflicting answers. This helps make sure linked services end correctly.
rows.imp_rat: Billing/termination disputes from missing supplier confirmations is a contract-admin error peripheral to liquid batching; consequences are financial disputes. Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Contract and PO records help, but missing or conflicting supplier confirmations still make disputes a recurring drag. Billing and termination disputes with suppliers can drag on for weeks awaiting confirmations. Resolving them needs procurement staff familiar with contract terms, a moderately available skill. Prolonged disputes tie up payments and incur administrative and possible penalty costs.

rows.stk: Quality assurance, compliance officers, records management.
rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required lifecycle, maintenance, and disposal compliance records missing or incomplete when the retired batching system archive is finalized
rows.plain: Make sure the retired mixing system’s records are complete, including its use, maintenance, and proper disposal. This provides a reliable history and shows that required rules were followed.
rows.imp_rat: Missing lifecycle/disposal compliance records at archive finalization affects compliance standing, but ranking caps it at 1/1.
rows.sat_rat: Records are typically scattered and maintenance/disposal documentation is often incomplete by end of life, so archives finalize with gaps. Gathering lifecycle, maintenance, and disposal compliance records into a final archive is a slow document-hunt across many sources. Knowing which compliance records are mandated requires regulatory and quality expertise. Incomplete archives risk regulatory findings whose remediation cost is high.

rows.stk: Quality assurance, compliance officers, records management.
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that archived records become unreadable or inaccessible during the mandated retention period
rows.plain: Make sure stored records remain readable and available throughout the required storage period. This prevents important information from being lost when it is needed later.
rows.imp_rat: Archived records becoming unreadable during retention threatens long-term compliance, but the batch-4 ceiling caps relevance and severity at 1/1.
rows.sat_rat: Archived records commonly sit in proprietary or aging formats and media, so unreadability during long retention periods is a real recurring risk. Ensuring long-term readability is not a timed activity but future retrieval can be very slow when formats are obsolete. Preserving accessibility over decades requires digital-preservation expertise rarely applied at disposal. Migrating archives to durable formats and maintaining retrieval systems is an ongoing cost often underfunded.

rows.stk: Quality assurance, compliance officers, records management.
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of archived records attributed to the wrong retired batching system or component
rows.plain: Keep stored records from being linked to the wrong retired mixing system or part. This helps preserve an accurate history of retired equipment.
rows.imp_rat: Misattributing archived records to the wrong retired system is a records-labeling error peripheral to the batching job (low relevance); batch-5 ceiling caps both proxies at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Weak asset-tagging discipline at archiving causes records to be linked to the wrong system or component often enough to remain a recurring gap. Attributing records to the correct retired asset adds minor indexing time. Correct attribution depends on accurate asset identifiers, which requires disciplined metadata practice more than scarce expertise. Misattributed archives cost little upfront but cause expensive retrieval failures during later audits.

rows.stk: Quality assurance, compliance officers, records management.
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of archived records assigned an incorrect retention or disposition requirement
rows.plain: Keep stored records from being given the wrong rule for how long to keep them or when to dispose of them. This helps ensure records are kept and destroyed at the right times.
rows.imp_rat: Incorrect retention/disposition on archived records is a records-management error distant from batching function (low relevance); batch ranking caps both proxies at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Retention schedules exist in mature organizations but are applied inconsistently, so misclassified archived records occur with moderate frequency. Correctly classifying retention at archiving is fast when a schedule exists but slow when it must be researched. Assigning the right retention or disposition requires records-schedule knowledge that varies widely across organizations. Wrong retention leads to either premature destruction risk or needless long-term storage cost.

rows.stk: Quality assurance, compliance officers, records management.
rows.stmt: Reduce the time required records remain unarchived after the decommissioning archive deadline
rows.plain: Spend less time leaving required records unfiled after the deadline for storing them has passed. Filing them promptly keeps important history available and organized.
rows.imp_rat: Records lingering unarchived past a decommissioning deadline is an administrative closure error with minimal bearing on the CFJ and only minor consequences; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Reminder and workflow tools make timely archiving fairly consistent, though gaps recur when project teams disband before closeout. Document management systems and EDMS workflows exist to archive decommissioning records promptly, but manual handoffs and end-of-project fatigue routinely push archiving past deadlines. Archiving is largely clerical once systems are in place, requiring little scarce expertise, so the skill barrier is modest. Late archiving carries low direct cost but can trigger audit-preparation rework that consumes staff time.

rows.stk: Environmental health and safety personnel, waste management contractors.
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of a residue shipment being rejected because required regulatory documentation is incomplete or inconsistent
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that a shipment of leftover hazardous material is refused because required legal papers are missing or do not match. This helps the material reach an approved disposal destination.
rows.imp_rat: Residue shipment rejection over incomplete regulatory paperwork is a documentation/logistics matter peripheral to the batching CFJ, with recoverable consequences; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Waste-broker manifest practices catch most gaps, but incomplete or inconsistent documentation still causes periodic rejections. Assembling complete residue-shipment documentation is slow manual paperwork that can delay pickup. Complete manifests require regulatory documentation expertise from EHS staff. A rejected shipment means return freight and re-handling costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required components or options omitted from the batching system order specification
rows.plain: Make sure the order form for the production system includes all the parts and choices that are needed. This helps prevent delays or an unusable system.
rows.imp_rat: Omitting components from the order specification is a procurement-completeness error, only indirectly tied to running batches (low relevance); the cap keeps severity at 1 for this batch.
rows.sat_rat: Order specs routinely miss options because requirements are dispersed across stakeholders, and no standard practice guarantees completeness. Building a complete component/option list against system requirements is time-intensive detail work during specification. Knowing every needed option (valves, sensors, CIP/SIP features) for a batching system requires strong process-engineering expertise. Omitted components surface as retrofit or change-order costs, so gaps here directly waste money.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of discrepancies between specified facility interfaces and installation-site conditions
rows.plain: Make sure the system design matches the conditions at the place where it will be installed. This helps prevent problems during setup.
rows.imp_rat: Discrepancies between specified facility interfaces and site conditions cause rework at install but are not central to ongoing batching; batch ordering caps both proxies at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Interface schedules and walkdowns catch many issues, but discrepancies between spec and actual utility tie-ins still recur frequently. Field verification of facility interfaces takes coordinated site walkdowns that extend the acquisition timeline. Cross-checking specified interfaces against site conditions demands multidiscipline field expertise that is often thinly spread. Interface mismatches found at installation cause expensive rework and delays.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unresolved conflicts among process, quality, engineering, and procurement requirements in the order specification
rows.plain: Resolve disagreements among process, quality, engineering, and purchasing needs before writing the equipment order. This helps ensure the supplier receives one clear set of instructions.
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved requirement conflicts in the spec are a pre-project coordination error, peripheral to actually batching liquids (low relevance); cap sets severity to 1.
rows.sat_rat: Conflicting requirements frequently persist into the final spec because there is no robust mechanism to surface and reconcile them. Reconciling conflicting process, quality, engineering, and procurement inputs requires drawn-out cross-functional meetings. Resolving these conflicts demands seasoned cross-disciplinary judgement that few individuals hold, making it acutely skill-bound. Unresolved conflicts baked into an order cause expensive rework, but the immediate loss is coordination effort.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the capital request is rejected because its financial and production justification is insufficient
rows.plain: The request to buy the equipment should clearly explain its cost and how it will support production. This improves the chance that the purchase will be approved.
rows.imp_rat: A capital request rejected for insufficient justification is a funding-process error (relevance 1) with delayed rather than acute consequences (severity 1); capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Justifications are rejected inconsistently depending on reviewer expectations, and no standard template guarantees approval. Preparing a defensible capital justification (NPV, capacity, compliance case) takes considerable preparation time. Crafting a compelling financial and production justification demands scarce business-case and finance skill. Rejected requests waste sunk preparation effort but the direct money loss is indirect.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time the purchase request remains pending with required approvers
rows.plain: The purchase request should be approved quickly by everyone who needs to review it. This helps the buyer get what is needed without unnecessary delay.
rows.imp_rat: A purchase request pending with approvers is an internal approval delay peripheral to the CFJ, with absorbable consequences; the batch ceiling holds it at minimum.
rows.sat_rat: Electronic approval routing has improved consistency, yet absent approvers, missing documentation, and escalation for high-value spend still cause recurring, variable pending times. Purchase requests for capital liquid-batching equipment often stall in multi-level approval queues, waiting days to weeks on budget and engineering sign-offs. Navigating approval chains efficiently depends on a requester knowing whom to prompt and how to package justification, a soft skill unevenly distributed. Approval delays tie up needed capital timing but do not themselves consume significant money beyond opportunity cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required quality, validation, or supplier-qualification documents missing from the approval package
rows.plain: Include all needed quality, testing, and supplier records in the approval package, so the purchase can be reviewed and approved with enough evidence.
rows.imp_rat: Missing quality or supplier-qualification documents from the approval package is a paperwork defect only administratively tied to batching, with recoverable impact; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Mature validation-master-plan and DQ checklists catch most missing documents, but late-discovered gaps in approval packages still occur. Assembling the approval package against a document checklist is time-consuming but a well-rehearsed routine in regulated buying. Standard QA document lists reduce reliance on individual expertise, though interpreting completeness still needs some quality knowledge. Document assembly is largely labor cost absorbed within existing quality functions.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of disagreement among engineering, quality, finance, and procurement stakeholders on purchase requirements
rows.plain: Make sure engineering, quality, finance, and purchasing agree on what the purchase must include. This reduces the chance of approving equipment that does not meet important needs.
rows.imp_rat: Stakeholder disagreement on purchase requirements is a procurement-alignment error tangential to the core job; consequences are specification churn. Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Without a single controlled requirements document, stakeholders continue to hold conflicting expectations that surface late. Reconciling stakeholder disagreement on requirements adds cross-functional meeting cycles before a purchase can proceed. Facilitating consensus among engineering, quality, finance, and procurement takes a skilled requirements owner who is often not designated. Disagreement mainly costs coordination effort rather than direct spend.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount by which the evaluated acquisition cost exceeds the proposed capital budget
rows.plain: Keep the full expected purchase cost within the approved amount for new equipment or other long-term purchases. This makes it easier to get approval.
rows.imp_rat: Acquisition cost exceeding the proposed capital budget is a procurement/budgeting concern (relevance 1) with financial rather than functional consequences (severity 1); held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Budget overruns are frequently discovered late because early cost estimates are optimistic, though comparison itself is reliable. Comparing evaluated acquisition cost to the capital budget is a quick, routine finance step. Budget-versus-quote comparison requires only modest financial skill and is widely practiced. This need is precisely about overspend against budget, and status quo estimating leaves recurring gaps.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of purchaser payment due before agreed delivery and acceptance obligations are fulfilled
rows.plain: Avoid paying before the supplier has met the agreed delivery and acceptance requirements. This protects the buyer from paying for equipment that has not yet been properly delivered or approved.
rows.imp_rat: Minimizing purchaser payment before delivery/acceptance is a commercial payment-terms concern peripheral to the batching system's core functional job; consequences are financial timing issues, not job failure. Capped by batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Delivery/acceptance-linked payment terms are common practice, though vendors with leverage still extract sizeable upfront payments. Negotiating milestone-linked payment schedules is a normal, bounded part of contracting time. Standard contract negotiation skill covers structuring payments against delivery and acceptance. Progress payments before acceptance tie up cash and expose the buyer, but milestone terms limit most exposure.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percent of critical system components and performance obligations excluded from warranty coverage
rows.plain: Reduce the share of important system parts and promised performance that the warranty does not cover. This gives the buyer broader protection if something fails or does not perform as promised.
rows.imp_rat: Warranty exclusions affect risk exposure, but this item sits below the prior batch, capping both proxies at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Standard contract review catches obvious exclusions, but subtle carve-outs of critical components still slip past. Reviewing warranty terms for excluded components and obligations is a bounded contract-review task. Interpreting warranty exclusions blends legal and technical judgement that procurement typically handles adequately. Uncovered warranty gaps expose the buyer to future repair costs, making this a direct financial concern.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of required project cost omitted from the agreed purchase price
rows.plain: Make sure the agreed purchase price includes every project cost that is needed. This prevents unexpected charges later.
rows.imp_rat: Cost omitted from the purchase price is a commercial issue, but this item ranks lower so both proxies stay at the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Total-cost accounting is inconsistently done, so agreed prices routinely exclude significant real project costs. Building a complete project cost picture beyond purchase price takes iterative estimating effort. Identifying all cost elements (install, validation, integration) requires experienced project-cost estimating. Costs omitted from the agreed price are the very overruns this need targets, and the status quo underestimates them frequently.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of contract execution delay caused by unresolved purchaser and supplier approvals
rows.plain: Avoid delaying the purchase because the buyer and supplier have not finished approving it. This helps the agreement be signed and carried out sooner.
rows.imp_rat: Contract execution delay from unresolved approvals is a procurement-process error peripheral to liquid batching operation; consequences are administrative delay. Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Approval workflows are semi-formalized but lack enforced routing, so stalls on unresolved sign-offs remain common. Contract execution routinely waits on sequential purchaser and supplier sign-offs that pass slowly through email and legal queues. Resolving approval holdups needs someone who knows the approval hierarchy and can chase the right authority, a coordination skill unevenly present. Delayed execution can push pricing validity and mobilization costs upward, though the direct spend impact is modest.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of finding no rental or lease arrangement that meets the required batching capacity, sterile-processing requirements, and production window
rows.plain: Find a rental or lease option that can handle the needed amount of production, meet cleanliness requirements, and be available during the planned production period. This helps ensure the temporary equipment will actually meet the project's needs.
rows.imp_rat: Finding no suitable rental/lease arrangement is a sourcing-availability error tangential to the batching CFJ and ranked below the prior batch; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: The rental market for sterile-capable batching systems is thin, so often no arrangement meeting all requirements exists at all. Searching for a suitable rental/lease arrangement can consume long, often fruitless effort. Locating a lessor matching sterile-processing and capacity needs requires niche market knowledge few possess. Failing to find a lease forces costly purchase or delay, a significant resource impact.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount by which the total rental or lease expense exceeds the project budget because required charges were omitted
rows.plain: Include every required charge so the total rental or lease cost stays as close as possible to, and no higher than, the project budget.
rows.imp_rat: Rental/lease expense exceeding budget from omitted charges is a procurement-budgeting error, tangential to the CFJ of liquid preparation; consequences are cost overruns, not operational failure. Held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: There is no standardized total-cost-of-rental template in typical practice, so whether all charges are captured varies widely from deal to deal. Budget reconciliation of rental line items happens late in the procurement cycle, so omitted charges surface only after quotes are compared, extending review loops. Catching every ancillary charge (installation, demurrage, insurance, decommissioning) depends on an experienced buyer's judgement, and that expertise is scarce and inconsistently applied. Status-quo quote checklists routinely miss embedded rental fees, so projects regularly discover overspend against budget after commitment with no reliable safeguard.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of accepting lease terms that conflict with the trial schedule, decontamination obligations, or return conditions
rows.plain: Choose rental terms that fit the testing timetable, cleaning responsibilities, and requirements for returning the equipment. Conflicting terms could cause delays, extra work, or unexpected costs.
rows.imp_rat: Accepting conflicting lease terms is a contractual-negotiation error only indirectly relevant to batching, with manageable consequences; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Lease/rental arrangements for sterile batching gear are non-standard, so conflicting terms are common and inconsistently caught. Reviewing lease terms against trial schedule and return conditions adds contract-review time. Spotting conflicts between lease terms and decontamination/return obligations demands both legal and process expertise. Bad lease terms create liabilities and penalties that hit the budget later.

rows.stmt: Minimize the time required to obtain the technical, commercial, and availability information needed to approve a rental arrangement
rows.plain: Gather the price, practical details, and availability needed to approve renting something as quickly as possible. This helps the buyer choose and approve a rental without delay.
rows.imp_rat: Time to gather info to approve a rental arrangement is administrative and peripheral to the core batching job, with low-stakes consequences; ranking fixes it at minimal.
rows.sat_rat: Rental info requests lack a standard data package, so the completeness of what suppliers return is inconsistent. Gathering the technical, commercial, and availability data to approve a rental arrangement involves multiple supplier round-trips that slow approval. Knowing exactly what information a rental approval requires depends on procurement experience with leasing. The information-gathering delay is a time cost more than a monetary one.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of critical batching or control functions that fail during the demonstration
rows.plain: Avoid having important mixing, measuring, or control functions fail during the demonstration. The demonstration should show that the system can perform its essential jobs.
rows.imp_rat: Critical batching/control functions failing during demonstration is central to the CFJ, yet the mandatory ceiling forces a 1/1 cap.
rows.sat_rat: Pre-FAT checkout and structured test scripts keep most core functions working, though software/control glitches during demos remain common. Demonstration functional failures cause reruns that eat schedule, but staged FAT dry-runs limit this. Vendor commissioning engineers competent to exercise control and batching functions are generally available for planned demos. A failed function during demo is comparatively cheap to fix on the vendor floor versus after installation.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of purchase-critical acceptance criteria left without observable evidence after the demonstration
rows.plain: Leave fewer important purchase requirements without clear proof after the equipment demonstration. This helps confirm that the equipment actually meets what was promised.
rows.imp_rat: Acceptance criteria without observable evidence after demonstration risks unverified purchase, but the ceiling caps this at 1/1.
rows.sat_rat: FAT protocols cover many criteria, but purchase-critical ones frequently end up asserted rather than demonstrated, leaving evidence gaps. Building observable evidence for every acceptance criterion during a demo lengthens the FAT/SAT event considerably. Designing test protocols that produce witnessable evidence for each criterion requires experienced commissioning/validation staff often in short supply. Thorough evidence capture adds test cost, but unproven criteria create expensive post-award disputes.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the scheduled demonstration is postponed because required materials, utilities, records, or qualified personnel are unavailable
rows.plain: The demonstration should take place as planned, with all needed supplies, services, records, and trained people available. This prevents the demonstration from being delayed.
rows.imp_rat: A postponed demonstration due to unavailable materials/personnel is a schedule delay with recoverable consequences, capped at 1/1 by the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Readiness checklists exist but are inconsistently enforced, so demos are frequently postponed for one missing prerequisite. When materials, utilities, records, or qualified people are missing, the demo slips and reschedules cost weeks. Coordinating all readiness prerequisites demands strong project coordination skill that is often overstretched. Postponed demos waste mobilized labor and travel, a recurring avoidable cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of restrictions on observation, component access, or operator interaction during the demonstration
rows.plain: Keep the system open for viewing, hands-on access, and operator participation during the demonstration. This lets people properly examine and try the system.
rows.imp_rat: Restrictions on observation/access during a demonstration is a pre-purchase evaluation nuisance far from the core batching job, with minor consequences; capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Standard FAT/SAT protocols exist and give reasonably consistent observation opportunities, but component-level access and free operator interaction are commonly curtailed by vendors protecting proprietary configurations. Factory acceptance tests and demonstrations of batching systems are typically time-boxed, with observation and operator interaction limited to scheduled windows that constrain how long buyers can examine the system. Getting meaningful hands-on access during a demo depends on the buyer's technical team knowing what to ask for and how to negotiate participation into the test protocol. Restricted demonstration access is a modest cost concern, though repeat site visits or re-tests to compensate for limited access add travel and time expense.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the quotation scope differs from the selected liquid batching and preparation system configuration
rows.plain: Make sure the price offer covers the exact liquid mixing and preparation system that was chosen. This helps prevent missing equipment, features, or costs.
rows.imp_rat: Quotation-vs-configuration mismatch is a sales/spec administrative error tangential to batching (low relevance); cap sets severity to 1.
rows.sat_rat: Quote scope commonly drifts from the selected configuration, and only careful comparison catches it, so residual errors persist. Confirming quotation scope matches the selected configuration adds clarification rounds before order placement. Aligning quote scope to chosen configuration needs moderate technical literacy about the system architecture. Scope-vs-configuration mismatches translate directly into unplanned cost once discovered.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required commercial and delivery details omitted from the quotation
rows.plain: Include all important business and delivery details in the supplier's price proposal. This helps the buyer compare the offer and understand exactly what the supplier is proposing.
rows.imp_rat: Omitted commercial/delivery details in a quotation is a paperwork gap far removed from actually batching liquids, and any consequence is a follow-up request; ceiling holds it at minimal.
