rows.sat_rat: RFQ templates and quote checklists are common practice, so completeness is reasonably reliable though gaps still appear. Missing commercial or delivery details in quotes trigger follow-up requests that delay evaluation. Knowing which details are mandatory relies on buyer experience and completeness checklists. Omitted details can hide costs but the immediate impact is incomplete information rather than waste.

rows.stmt: Minimize the time from submitting a complete quotation request to receiving the supplier quotation
rows.plain: Get the supplier’s price offer as quickly as possible after sending a complete request. This helps the buyer decide and move forward sooner.
rows.imp_rat: Time to receive a supplier quotation is a procurement-cycle delay only loosely tied to the CFJ, with mild rework consequences; the batch ceiling keeps it at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Reputable suppliers give reasonably predictable turnaround, though response times still vary widely across vendors. The elapsed time from a complete RFQ to receiving a quotation is largely set by supplier internal turnaround and is often long. Little buyer skill affects this once the request is complete; the wait is external. Slow quoting delays the project but has minimal direct cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the supplier cannot access configuration specifications or request attachments
rows.plain: Make sure the supplier can see the detailed requirements and files needed to prepare a price quote. This helps them quote the right product or service.
rows.imp_rat: Supplier inability to access config specs/attachments is an access hiccup remote from the liquid-preparation job, with easily-resolved consequences; capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Email and shared portals make document delivery fairly reliable, though format or access issues still occasionally block suppliers. When suppliers cannot open configuration specs or attachments, resending files adds a short delay. Handling file formats and portal access is routine administrative work requiring little specialized skill. Access failures cost negligible money.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the quotation expires before procurement review is completed
rows.plain: Make sure the supplier's price offer remains valid until the purchasing review is finished. Otherwise, the supplier may require a new offer or different terms.
rows.imp_rat: Quotation expiring before procurement review is a scheduling nuisance with low centrality to the liquid-preparation job, and re-quoting mitigates any harm; capped at minimum.
rows.sat_rat: Validity dates are visible on quotes and often tracked, so expiry before review is a manageable and moderately reliable concern. Quotes carry validity windows and procurement review sometimes runs past them, forcing re-quotes and lost time. Tracking expiry dates against review progress is a routine administrative task requiring little specialized skill. Expiry can force re-pricing at higher rates, a modest cost exposure.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of quoted substitutions whose equivalence to acquisition requirements cannot be verified
rows.plain: Fewer alternative products should be quoted when no one can confirm that they meet the buying requirements. This helps prevent the purchase of something that does not do the job needed.
rows.imp_rat: Unverifiable quoted substitutions is a procurement equivalence concern (relevance 1) with limited direct batching impact (severity 1); capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Suppliers substitute components and equivalence often cannot be verified upfront, so risk carries into the order. Verifying equivalence of quoted substitutions requires additional technical evaluation cycles. Judging whether a substituted component meets acquisition requirements needs deep material/process expertise. Unverified substitutions can fail later, incurring replacement and downtime costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of ambiguity in responsibility for utility, controls, and facility interfaces
rows.plain: Clearly assign who is responsible for electricity and other services, control systems, and connections to the building. This prevents gaps between the equipment and the facility.
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguous responsibility for utility/controls/facility interfaces risks handoff gaps, yet its centrality to the batching CFJ is low here and it is capped at 1/1.
rows.sat_rat: Battery-limit and responsibility matrices exist but are inconsistently applied, so interface ownership gaps remain a chronic source of disputes. Sorting out who owns utility, controls, and facility interfaces drags through multiple meetings and revised scope-split documents. Clear responsibility allocation needs experienced project managers who understand both vendor and site scope, a scarce competency. Undefined boundaries routinely produce unbudgeted scope gaps that someone must fund late in the project.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required qualification and compliance documentation deliverables missing from the quoted scope
rows.plain: Make sure the price proposal includes all required proof that the equipment meets standards and has been properly tested, so the buyer knows what is included.
rows.imp_rat: Qualification/compliance deliverables missing from the quoted scope is a procurement-documentation defect of low centrality and recoverable severity; held to 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Suppliers frequently under-scope compliance documentation in quotes, and there is no consistent practice ensuring it is fully captured upfront. Verifying that quoted scope includes all qualification and compliance deliverables requires iterative clarification cycles with suppliers. Spotting quietly omitted IQ/OQ/PQ or FAT/SAT deliverables in a quote demands experienced technical-quality reviewers. Omitted deliverables later billed as extras create real unbudgeted cost, which the status quo poorly prevents.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that commercial exclusions or optional components are mistaken for included scope
rows.plain: Make sure items left out of the price and optional parts are not mistaken for things that are included. This prevents unexpected costs and missing equipment.
rows.imp_rat: Mistaking exclusions for included scope is a proposal-interpretation error peripheral to preparing liquids (low relevance); batch-5 ceiling holds both proxies at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Suppliers bury exclusions and options inconsistently, so misread scope is a recurring, hard-to-prevent error under current practice. Teasing exclusions and options out of proposals adds review time as clarifications are requested. Spotting that a listed option or exclusion is not in base scope requires attentive, experienced reading of fine print. Mistaking exclusions for included scope produces surprise add-on costs, a frequent painful outcome.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the system shipment becomes untraceable because its tracking identifiers are missing or inconsistent
rows.plain: Make sure the shipment has complete and matching identification numbers so it can be followed during delivery. This helps prevent the shipment from becoming impossible to locate.
rows.imp_rat: Untraceable shipment from missing tracking IDs is a logistics-tracking error peripheral to batching liquids, and consequences are recoverable; ranking holds it at minimum.
rows.sat_rat: Carrier tracking numbers and BOLs make most shipments traceable, so missing or inconsistent identifiers are relatively rare. An untraceable shipment triggers investigative delays while identifiers are reconstructed. Managing tracking identifiers is standardized carrier practice needing little special skill. Lost or delayed shipments can incur real cost, but occurrences are infrequent.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration that the shipment status remains outdated after a transit event
rows.plain: Update the shipment status soon after something happens during transport. This helps people see where the shipment stands without relying on old information.
rows.imp_rat: Stale shipment status after a transit event is a minor tracking latency issue distant from the liquid-preparation job and low-consequence; ceiling applies.
rows.sat_rat: Standard carrier tracking refreshes status reasonably but with lags, so outdated status after a transit event remains a common minor gap. Shipment status often lags real transit events because updates post in batches rather than in real time. Reading tracking updates requires no special skill. Stale status has little direct cost impact.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of discrepancy between the reported arrival estimate and the actual arrival of the system shipment
rows.plain: Make the reported delivery date for the system match the date it actually arrives. This helps the receiving team plan for the shipment.
rows.imp_rat: Discrepancy between estimated and actual arrival is a delivery-forecast error with peripheral relevance to batching and easily-planned-around consequences; held at cap.
rows.sat_rat: For engineered liquid-handling systems the promised-versus-actual arrival gap is chronically wide because fabrication, custom instrumentation, and freight of tanks and skids introduce unpredictable delays that current tracking rarely reconciles. Vendors quote lead times at PO but the actual arrival of custom-fabricated batching skids routinely slips weeks past the reported estimate. Interpreting a vendor's shipping estimate and chasing realistic dates requires an experienced buyer who knows which suppliers habitually over-promise. Late or misforecast arrivals force costly expediting, rescheduled crews, and idle installation resources that inflate the true acquisition cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of split-shipment consignments with unidentified contents or receiving destinations
rows.plain: Clearly identify what is in each shipment sent separately and where it must be delivered. This helps receiving staff send every part to the right place.
rows.imp_rat: Split-shipment consignments with unidentified contents is a shipping-logistics error, marginal to core batching, and capped at 1.0 in this batch.
rows.sat_rat: Shipment manifests and tracking help, but split shipments with ambiguous contents or destinations remain a recurring receiving headache. Tracking split shipments adds coordination time across multiple consignments. Reconciling multi-consignment deliveries needs attentive logistics/receiving staff but not specialized expertise. Mis-routed or unidentified consignments cause storage and expediting costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a route change or special-handling event affecting the system shipment goes unreported
rows.plain: Make sure changes to the delivery route or special handling of the shipment are reported. This helps the recipient know where the system is and about any delivery risks.
rows.imp_rat: Unreported route/special-handling events are a shipment-visibility gap only tangentially relevant to the CFJ, with limited direct consequence; capped at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Proactive exception reporting for oversized system shipments is uneven across carriers, so events routinely go unreported until arrival. Unreported route changes or special-handling events surface late, delaying reaction and inspection planning. Interpreting handling exceptions needs a logistics coordinator paying active attention to carrier updates. Missed handling events risk damage costs but are not primarily a spend issue.
